topics.title: Introduction to Corrosion and Historical Background
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Corrosion and Historical Background # MODULE 1: INTRODUCTION TO CORROSION AND HISTORICAL BACKGROUND ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Corrosion According to the **NACE International (formerly National Association of Corrosion Engineers)** and **ASTM G15 Standard**, corrosion is defined as: > *"The deterioration of a material, usually a metal, that results from a chemical or electrochemical reaction with its environment."* A broader engineering definition (as per **Fontana's Corrosion Engineering**, universally adopted in KTU curricula) states: > *"Corrosion is the destructive attack of a metal by chemical or electrochemical reaction with the environment."* The **ISO 8044:2024** international standard further classifies corrosion as the *"physico-chemical interaction between a metal and its environment, which results in changes in the properties of the metal, and which may lead to significant impairment of the function of the metal, the environment, or the technical system of which these form a part."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** > Corrosion is fundamentally an **electrochemical process** for metals in aqueous/electrolytic environments and a **direct chemical process** in dry/gaseous environments. Both pathways lead to the metal reverting back to its thermodynamically stable oxide/sulphide/carbonate form (its ore state). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Return to Nature" Intuition Think of corrosion as the metal's **involuntary journey back home to the soil**. When we mine iron ore (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄) from the earth, we apply enormous amounts of energy in a blast furnace to separate the pure iron from oxygen: $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \xrightarrow{\Delta H = + \text{high}} 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$$ The pure iron we extract is in a **thermodynamically unstable (high energy) state**. Nature dislikes this imbalance. The moment iron is exposed to moisture and oxygen, it begins to "want" to recombine with oxygen to form rust (Fe₂O₃) again — the same state it was in the ore. > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a ball resting on a hilltop. It has high potential energy. Roll it down the hill (give it some initial disturbance), and it rolls to the bottom valley (lowest energy state) and stays there permanently. The ball (iron) on the hilltop is like freshly refined metal. Corrosion is the act of rolling it back down to the valley (oxide ore). ### 1.3 Essential Standard Metrics & Constants The following fundamental constants and economic metrics are universally applied in KTU Board questions: | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Corrosion Rate (CR)** | **mm/year (mmpy)** or **mils per year (mpy)** | Standard penetration depth rate | | **Current Density (i)** | **A/cm²** or **µA/cm²** | Rate of electrochemical attack | | **Faraday's Constant (F)** | **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol) | Charge per mole of electrons | | **Metal Density (ρ)** | g/cm³ | Required for rate calculations | | **Equivalent Weight (EW)** | dimensionless | Atomic mass / valence change | | **Standard Electrode Potential** | **Volts (V) vs. SHE** | Tendency to corrode | > [!NOTE] > **1 mil = 0.001 inch = 0.0254 mm**. Board questions often mix units; always convert mpy → mmpy using the factor: **1 mpy = 0.0254 mmpy**. ### 1.4 Historical Background — A Timeline of Corrosion Understanding The history of corrosion is intertwined with the history of metallurgy itself. **Era 1 — Ancient Civilizations (Pre-1000 BC to 500 AD):** - **Egyptians (~3000 BC):** First recorded metallurgists; used gold (noble, non-corroding) extensively. They had no concept of corrosion control but exploited "corrosion-resistant" metals. - **Romans (~100 BC – 200 AD):** Used **lead (Pb) aqueducts and water pipes**. They observed "plumbism" (lead poisoning) but did not connect it to metal-environment interaction. The Latin word *"plumbum"* gave us the symbol **Pb**. - **Iron Pillar of Delhi, India (~400 AD):** A 7.0 m, 6-tonne wrought iron pillar commissioned by **King Chandragupta II** has resisted corrosion for over **1,600 years**. Modern analysis (by IIT Kanpur, 2002) revealed a high-phosphorus iron composition (0.15% P) that forms a protective crystalline "misawite" (δ-FeOOH) layer. This is a celebrated case study in passive film science. **Era 2 — Middle Ages to Industrial Revolution (500 AD – 1800 AD):** - Alchemists observed metal tarnishing but attributed it to "phlogiston" (a hypothetical fire-like element). - No scientific understanding of corrosion mechanism. **Era 3 — Birth of Electrochemistry (1830 – 1900):** - **1834 — Michael Faraday** formulated the laws of electrolysis (Faraday's Laws), laying the quantitative foundation. - **1838 — Christian Friedrich Schönbein and Humphry Davy** investigated galvanic corrosion. - **Sir Humphry Davy (1824)** proposed sacrificial zinc anodes to protect copper sheathing on Royal Navy wooden warships — the **birth of cathodic protection**, still used today on pipelines and ship hulls. - **1837 — De La Rive** explained atmospheric corrosion of zinc. - **1888 — Nernst** developed the Nernst equation linking electrode potential to ion concentration — the cornerstone of corrosion thermodynamics. **Era 4 — 20th Century — The Modern Corrosion Era (1900 – 2000):** - **1924 — U.R. Evans** published *"The Corrosion of Metals"* — the first systematic textbook on corrosion science. - **1943 — NACE International (National Association of Corrosion Engineers)** was founded in Houston, Texas, USA. NACE is now the global authority on corrosion standards. - **1957 — Fontana & Greene** published *"Corrosion Engineering"*, the most widely used textbook globally. - **1960s–1980s:** Development of **stainless steels** (already discovered by Harry Brearley in 1913), advanced polymer coatings, cathodic protection standards, and corrosion inhibitors. **Era 5 — 21st Century — Smart Corrosion Management (2000 – Present):** - **Smart coatings** with self-healing microcapsules. - **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** for in-situ corrosion monitoring. - **AI/Machine Learning** for predicting corrosion rates and remaining useful life. - **Green corrosion inhibitors** derived from plant extracts (sustainable chemistry). - **NACE merged with SSPC (2018)** to form **AMPP (Association for Materials Protection and Performance)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Metals Corrode — The Thermodynamic Driving Force Every metal exists in nature in a thermodynamically stable state as an **ore** (oxide, sulphide, carbonate). Refining the metal requires energy input, putting the refined metal in a higher-energy, unstable state. Corrosion is the spontaneous reversal of this refining process. The driving force is quantified by the **Change in Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG)**: $$\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$$ Where: - **n** = number of electrons transferred per mole - **F** = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - **E_cell** = cell potential (V) > [!NOTE] > If **ΔG < 0**, the corrosion reaction is **spontaneous** (will occur naturally). If **ΔG > 0**, the reaction is non-spontaneous and requires energy input (like electroplating, which is the *opposite* of corrosion). ### 2.2 Classification of Corrosion (Overview) The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes **8 major forms of corrosion** under Module 1 / Module 2: 1. **Uniform / General Corrosion** — Even attack over the entire exposed surface (e.g., rusting of steel bridge). 2. **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** — When two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in an electrolyte (e.g., steel bolt in copper plate). 3. **Crevice Corrosion** — Localized attack in stagnant micro-environments (e.g., under gaskets, bolt heads). 4. **Pitting Corrosion** — Localized formation of small, deep cavities (e.g., stainless steel in chloride solutions). 5. **Intergranular Corrosion** — Attack along grain boundaries (e.g., sensitized stainless steel welds). 6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — Combined action of tensile stress and corrosive environment (e.g., brass in ammonia — "season cracking"). 7. **Erosion Corrosion** — Accelerated attack due to mechanical wear + chemical action (e.g., impingement in pipe bends). 8. **Microbial Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** — Attack accelerated by bacteria/fungi (e.g., sulfate-reducing bacteria in oil pipelines). ### 2.3 Economic Impact of Corrosion Corrosion imposes a massive economic burden globally: | Region / Authority | Estimated Annual Cost | % of GDP | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **USA (NACE, 2016 IMPACT Study)** | **$2.5 Trillion** | **~6.0% of GDP** | | **India (NACE India estimate)** | **~$100 Billion (₹8 lakh crore approx.)** | **~4–5% of GDP** | | **Global Total** | **>$3.3 Trillion** | **~3.4% of global GDP** | | **Avoidable Portion (with proper control)** | **~25–35%** | Savings potential | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite:** The NACE IMPACT study finding that **15–35% of corrosion costs are avoidable** through existing control technologies is a frequently asked 3-mark question. ### 2.4 The Cost Components of Corrosion The total economic loss has four components (per Hoar Committee Report, UK, 1971 — still the standard reference): 1. **Replacement of structures and equipment** (largest share). 2. **Cost of preventive maintenance (painting, cathodic protection).** 3. **Loss of efficiency** (e.g., fouled heat exchangers, scaled boilers). 4. **Loss of product** (e.g., leakages in oil pipelines). ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description | KTU Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy & cell potential | Predict spontaneity | | 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10}[Q]$ | **Nernst Equation** (at 25 °C) | Electrode potential calculations | | 3 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \times i \times \text{EW}}{d}$ | Weight-loss rate in mpy | $i$ in A/ft², $d$ in g/cm³ | | 4 | $\text{CR (mmpy)} = \dfrac{0.00327 \times i \times \text{EW}}{d}$ | Penetration rate in mm/year | $i$ in µA/cm², $d$ in g/cm³ | | 5 | $\text{EW} = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n \text{ (valence change)}}$ | Equivalent weight | Faraday law calculation | | 6 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot \text{EW}}{F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved | Faraday's First Law | | 7 | $W = \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F}$ | Equivalent weight from formula | Chemical equivalent | | 8 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Galvanic series cell EMF | Galvanic corrosion | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unit conversion factors for corrosion rate:** > - **1 mpy = 0.0254 mmpy** > - **1 g/m²·day (gmd) = 0.04 mpy (for steel, ρ ≈ 7.86 g/cm³)** > - **1 A/m² = 0.01 mA/ft²** ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Corrosion control is not an academic exercise — it underpins critical infrastructure: - **Oil & Gas:** Internal pipeline corrosion (CO₂, H₂S, "sour service") costs the industry > **$1.4 billion/year** in failures. - **Marine:** Saltwater accelerates corrosion 5–10×; ship hulls require cathodic protection + antifouling coatings. - **Aerospace:** Stress corrosion cracking in aluminum alloys caused Aloha Airlines Flight 243 incident (1988). - **Nuclear:** Corrosion of reactor pressure tubes (e.g., Monel 400 in CANDU reactors) led to decades-long research. - **Reinforced Concrete:** Chloride-induced rebar corrosion is the #1 cause of bridge deterioration worldwide. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples Since this is an introductory topic, the mathematical rigor centres on the **Nernst Equation** (the foundational equation for all corrosion electrochemistry) and a **corrosion rate calculation** — both are KTU board favourites. ### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation **Starting Point:** The change in Gibbs free energy for an electrochemical reaction is related to the cell potential: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 1:** Substitute the relationship $\Delta G = -nFE$ into both sides of the equation: $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 2:** Divide both sides by $-nF$ to isolate $E$: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 3:** Convert natural logarithm ($\ln$) to base-10 logarithm ($\log_{10}$) using the identity $\ln Q = 2.303 \log_{10} Q$: $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$ **Step 4:** Substitute standard values at $T = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (25 °C): - $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ - $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}$ $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485} = 0.0592 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5:** Final Nernst Equation at 25 °C: $$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q}$$ This equation is the **thermodynamic backbone** of corrosion prediction. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Corrosion Rate Calculation (Board Pattern) **Problem (Typical KTU Pattern):** A structural steel component undergoes uniform corrosion in seawater. After 30 days of exposure, the weight loss is found to be **485 mg** from an exposed area of **10 cm²**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(a) mdd (mg/dm²·day)** and **(b) mmpy**. Density of steel, $\rho = 7.86 \text{ g/cm}^3$. **Solution:** **Part (a): Corrosion Rate in mdd (mg/dm²·day)** **Step 1:** Convert area from cm² to dm²: $$1 \text{ dm}^2 = 100 \text{ cm}^2 \Rightarrow 10 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.10 \text{ dm}^2$$ **Step 2:** Apply the formula: $$\text{CR (mdd)} = \frac{\text{Weight loss (mg)}}{\text{Area (dm}^2) \times \text{Time (days)}}$$ $$\text{CR} = \frac{485 \text{ mg}}{0.10 \text{ dm}^2 \times 30 \text{ days}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CR} = 161.67 \text{ mdd}}$$ **Part (b): Corrosion Rate in mmpy (mm/year)** **Step 1:** Use the penetration rate formula: $$\text{CR (mmpy)} = \frac{\text{CR (mdd)} \times 24 \times 365}{1000 \times \rho \times 10^4}$$ Wait — for clean derivation, use the **Faraday-equivalent conversion**: $$\text{CR (mmpy)} = \frac{0.0365 \times \Delta W}{A \times t \times \rho}$$ Where: - $\Delta W$ = weight loss in mg - $A$ = area in cm² - $t$ = time in days - $\rho$ = density in g/cm³ **Step 2:** Plug in the values: $$\text{CR (mmpy)} = \frac{0.0365 \times 485}{10 \times 30 \times 7.86}$$ $$\text{CR (mmpy)} = \frac{17.7025}{2358}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CR} = 7.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mmpy} \approx 0.0075 \text{ mmpy}}$$ **Interpretation:** A corrosion rate of **0.0075 mmpy** is considered **excellent** corrosion resistance (typically < 0.05 mmpy is "outstanding"). This would qualify for use in marine service with adequate protective coatings. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Corrosion Resistance vs. Practical Implication | Corrosion Rate (mmpy) | Practical Engineering Implication | | :--- | :--- | | $< 0.0254$ (1 mpy) | Outstanding — suitable for critical service | | $0.0254$ – $0.127$ (1–5 mpy) | Excellent — general industrial use | | $0.127$ – $0.254$ (5–10 mpy) | Good — typical for coated carbon steel | | $0.254$ – $0.508$ (10–20 mpy) | Fair — requires monitoring | | $> 0.508$ (> 20 mpy) | Poor — replacement or redesign needed | > [!NOTE] > The threshold **0.127 mmpy (5 mpy)** is the **NACE "acceptable" limit** for most refinery and process plant equipment. Values above this trigger mandatory mitigation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — The "Big Picture" of Corrosion Science This Mermaid block presents the **hierarchical framework** that maps the entire discipline of corrosion as it appears in the KTU syllabus. It functions as a **functional architecture flow** of how the topic is structured. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["CORROSION SCIENCE

All four must be present simultaneously — remove any one, and corrosion stops. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ANO ["ANODE SITE - Oxidation"] A["Metal M dissolves
M to M_positive_plus_n e_negative"] end subgraph CAT ["CATHODE SITE - Reduction"] B["Electron consuming reaction
Example O2_plus_2H2O_plus_4e_to_4OH_negative"] end subgraph EL ["ELECTROLYTE - Ionic Conductor"] C["Aqueous medium
Contains dissolved ions
Completes ionic circuit"] end subgraph EM ["METAL PATH - Electronic Conductor"] D["Bulk metal
Carries electrons
from anode to cathode"] end A -- electrons flow --> EM EM -- electrons flow --> B B -- cations migrate --> C C -- anions migrate --> A classDef anodeStyle fill:#ffe1e1,color:#7a0000,stroke:#a00000,stroke-width:2px classDef cathodeStyle fill:#e1f0ff,color:#003366,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px classDef electrolyteStyle fill:#fff7e1,color:#7a5a00,stroke:#7a5a00,stroke-width:2px classDef metalStyle fill:#e8e8e8,color:#000000,stroke:#404040,stroke-width:2px class A anodeStyle class B cathodeStyle class C electrolyteStyle class D metalStyle ``` ### 4.3 Historical Timeline — Corrosion Science Milestones ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["3000 BC

Smart Coatings
and AI Monitoring"]:::future T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4 --> T5 --> T6 --> T7 --> T8 --> T9 classDef ancient fill:#f5e6d3,color:#5a3a00,stroke:#5a3a00,stroke-width:1px classDef early fill:#d4e6f1,color:#1a3a5a,stroke:#1a3a5a,stroke-width:1px classDef modern fill:#d5f5e3,color:#0a4a1a,stroke:#0a4a1a,stroke-width:1px classDef future fill:#fadbd8,color:#7a1a0a,stroke:#7a1a0a,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]** Define corrosion. List the eight major forms of corrosion recognized by NACE. **Model Answer (3 Marks — Strict Rubric):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Corrosion is the *destructive and unintentional attack of a metal due to its chemical or electrochemical reaction with the surrounding environment*, resulting in the conversion of the metal into its thermodynamically stable oxide, hydroxide, sulphide, or salt form. **Eight Forms (½ Mark each for naming, 1 Mark for any 4 brief descriptions):** 1. **Uniform (General) Corrosion** — Even attack over the entire exposed surface. 2. **Galvanic (Bimetallic) Corrosion** — Two dissimilar metals in electrical contact in an electrolyte. 3. **Crevice Corrosion** — Localized attack under gaskets, washers, or deposits. 4. **Pitting Corrosion** — Localized formation of small deep cavities on the surface. 5. **Intergranular Corrosion** — Preferential attack along grain boundaries. 6. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — Combined action of tensile stress and corrosive medium. 7. **Erosion Corrosion** — Combined mechanical wear and electrochemical attack. 8. **Microbial Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** — Accelerated attack by microorganisms. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]** State the Faraday's laws of electrolysis. What is Faraday's constant? State its value and significance. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Faraday's First Law (1 Mark):** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity (charge) passed through the electrolyte. $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot \text{EW}}{F}$$ **Faraday's Second Law (1 Mark):** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights. $$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2}$$ **Faraday's Constant (1 Mark):** Faraday's constant **F** is the total electric charge carried by **one mole of electrons** (or one equivalent of substance). It equals **Avogadro's number × charge of one electron**: $$F = N_A \times e = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C/mol}$$ $$\boxed{F = 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol} \approx 96{,}500 \text{ C/mol}}$$ > **Significance:** Used in all quantitative corrosion rate calculations and Tafel extrapolation. It is the conversion bridge between electrical current and mass of metal dissolved. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > In Part B questions, students typically lose **2 to 4 marks** because they: > 1. Forget to write the **Nernst equation in logarithmic form** at 25 °C (the **0.0592 factor** is mandatory). > 2. Do not **state units** in the final answer (always write mmpy, mpy, V vs SHE, etc.). > 3. Confuse the **sign convention** in galvanic series calculations (always: $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$). > 4. Fail to **interpret the result** — just writing a number without stating whether the corrosion is acceptable/unacceptable loses 1 mark. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** Define corrosion with reference to NACE/ISO standards. Discuss in detail the **historical development** of corrosion science from ancient civilizations to the modern era. Highlight at least **four milestone discoveries**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A piece of zinc (atomic weight 65.4, valence 2, density 7.13 g/cm³) is subjected to a corrosion current of **0.5 A** over a surface area of **100 cm²** for **24 hours**. Calculate: - (i) The mass of zinc dissolved. - (ii) The corrosion rate in **mmpy**. - (iii) Comment on the severity of corrosion. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Definition (2 Marks)** - NACE definition (1 Mark) - Fontana definition + ISO 8044 mention (1 Mark) **Step 2: Historical Development (5 Marks — 1.25 Marks per milestone)** | Era | Milestone | Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Ancient (400 AD) | Delhi Iron Pillar | First evidence of **passive film protection** (high-phosphorus δ-FeOOH layer) | | 1824 | Humphry Davy's **Cathodic Protection** of copper-sheathed ships | First engineered corrosion control method; still used today | | 1834 | **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis** | Quantitative foundation for all corrosion rate calculations | | 1888 | **Nernst Equation** | Thermodynamic prediction of electrode potential | | 1943 | **NACE founded** | Global standardization; introduced industry-wide corrosion standards | **Concluding line (to fetch the last 0.5 mark):** Modern era emphasizes AI-based monitoring, green inhibitors, and nano-coatings. --- **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Identify Parameters (1 Mark)** - $I = 0.5 \text{ A}$, $t = 24 \text{ h} = 86{,}400 \text{ s}$ - $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2$, $\rho = 7.13 \text{ g/cm}^3$ - Atomic weight = 65.4, valence $n = 2$ **Step 2: Equivalent Weight (1 Mark)** $$\text{EW} = \frac{65.4}{2} = 32.7$$ **Step 3: Mass Dissolved Using Faraday's First Law (2 Marks)** $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot \text{EW}}{F} = \frac{0.5 \times 86{,}400 \times 32.7}{96{,}485}$$ $$m = \frac{1{,}412{,}640}{96{,}485} = 14.64 \text{ g}$$ $$\boxed{m = 14.64 \text{ g}}$$ **Step 4: Corrosion Rate in mmpy (2 Marks)** Current density: $$i = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 5000 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$ $$\text{CR (mmpy)} = \frac{0.00327 \times i \times \text{EW}}{\rho} = \frac{0.00327 \times 5000 \times 32.7}{7.13}$$ $$\text{CR (mmpy)} = \frac{534.7}{7.13} = 75.0 \text{ mmpy}$$ $$\boxed{\text{CR} \approx 75 \text{ mmpy}}$$ **Step 5: Interpretation (1 Mark)** A corrosion rate of **75 mmpy is in the "Severe" category** (above the 20 mpy threshold). This zinc will lose ~7.5% of its thickness per year. For most engineering applications, this is **unacceptable**; protective coatings or cathodic protection are mandatory. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Explain the **economic impact of corrosion** globally and in India. Discuss the four components of corrosion cost as per the **Hoar Committee Report**. Why is corrosion engineering considered a critical discipline in modern industry? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **electrochemical mechanism of corrosion**. State and explain the **Nernst Equation** with a suitable derivation. Apply the Nernst equation to find the electrode potential of a **Cu/Cu²⁺ half-cell** at 25 °C when the Cu²⁺ concentration is **0.01 M** (Standard potential $E^{\circ} = +0.34 \text{ V}$). **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Global Economic Data (2 Marks)** - USA: $2.5 trillion/year (~6% of GDP) — NACE IMPACT Study 2016 - Global: >$3.3 trillion/year (~3.4% of global GDP) - **15–35% of these costs are avoidable** through existing control methods **Step 2: India-Specific Data (1 Mark)** - ~$100 billion/year (~4–5% of GDP) - Refineries, fertilizers, marine, and infrastructure sectors are most affected **Step 3: Hoar Committee Four Components (3 Marks)** 1. **Replacement costs** — Plant, machinery, structures that fail due to corrosion (largest share) 2. **Corrosion control costs** — Paints, cathodic protection, inhibitors, additional alloy cost 3. **Loss of efficiency** — Scaled boilers, fouled heat exchangers, increased pumping costs 4. **Loss of product** — Leakages in pipelines, tanks; contamination in food/pharma **Step 4: Critical Discipline Argument (1 Mark)** Corrosion engineering directly impacts: - **Safety** (e.g., pipeline explosions, bridge collapses) - **Economy** (asset preservation, GDP protection) - **Environment** (preventing toxic leaks) - **Sustainability** (resource conservation) --- **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1: Electrochemical Mechanism Diagram + Description (3 Marks)** The four essential components (also see SECTION 4.2): - **Anode** — Oxidation: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$ (metal dissolves) - **Cathode** — Reduction: e.g., $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ (in neutral/alkaline) OR $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$ (in acid) - **Electrolyte** — Ionic conduction path - **Metallic connection** — Electronic conduction path The anode and cathode can be located on the same metal surface (micro-galvanic cells) or on two different metals (macro-galvanic cells). **Step 2: Nernst Equation — Derivation (2 Marks)** Starting from $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ and using $\Delta G = -nFE$: $$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$ $$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$ At 25 °C: $$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q}$$ **Step 3: Numerical Application (2 Marks)** For $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$: - $E^{\circ} = +0.34 \text{ V}$, $n = 2$, $[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.01 \text{ M}$ $$E = 0.34 - \frac{0.0592}{2} \log_{10}(0.01)$$ $$E = 0.34 - 0.0296 \times (-2)$$ $$E = 0.34 + 0.0592 = 0.3992 \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{E = +0.3992 \text{ V vs SHE}}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** When $[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ is low, the reduction tendency decreases (potential becomes slightly more positive? No — wait, the standard result is: lower ion concentration → less driving force for reduction → more positive potential is the actual direction here because we are at the cathode. The student should state that the equilibrium potential is **0.399 V**, slightly more positive than the standard 0.34 V, because the reaction quotient is in the denominator). > [!NOTE] > **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting that the Nernst equation for a reduction half-cell has the **products/reactants** ratio. For $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$, $Q = 1/[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ (since solid Cu has activity = 1). Always double-check the reaction quotient! --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **Definition (verbatim):** Corrosion = Deterioration of metal by chemical/electrochemical reaction with environment. (1 mark fetch line) - **ASTM G15** is the standard terminology reference. - **Four components essential for wet corrosion:** Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte, Metallic connection. Removing any one stops corrosion. - **Iron Pillar of Delhi (400 AD):** 1600+ years corrosion-free. High-phosphorus passive film (δ-FeOOH misawite). Classic example of natural passivity. - **Davy (1824):** Cathodic protection invented. - **Faraday (1834):** Quantitative laws — $m = \frac{It \cdot EW}{F}$ - **NACE (1943):** Global authority on corrosion. - **AMPP (2018):** NACE + SSPC merger. - **Global cost:** $3.3 Trillion/year (~3.4% of global GDP). - **Avoidable portion:** 15–35% (most-cited KTU statistic). - **Hoar Committee components:** Replacement + Control + Efficiency loss + Product loss. - **Nernst equation at 25 °C:** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log_{10} Q$ — memorize with units and conditions. - **Faraday's constant:** 96,485 C/mol — always use this exact value in calculations. - **Corrosion rate formulas to remember:** - $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \times i \times \text{EW}}{d}$ (with $i$ in A/ft²) - $\text{CR (mmpy)} = \dfrac{0.00327 \times i \times \text{EW}}{d}$ (with $i$ in µA/cm²) - **Acceptable corrosion rate:** < 0.127 mmpy (5 mpy) per NACE for refinery equipment. - **Conversion:** 1 mpy = 0.0254 mmpy. - **8 forms of corrosion:** Uniform, Galvanic, Crevice, Pitting, Intergranular, SCC, Erosion, MIC. (Remember all 8 for 3-mark questions.) - **Always state units** and **interpret the result** in numerical problems — these are the two easiest ways to gain partial marks. - **Sign convention:** $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$; spontaneous corrosion has $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$.

topics.title: Definitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definitions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Corrosion — The Central Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):** > **Corrosion** is the **irreversible deterioration** of a metal or alloy caused by an **electrochemical reaction** with its surrounding environment (typically moisture, oxygen, acids, salts, or industrial chemicals), leading to the formation of metallic compounds such as **oxides, hydroxides, sulfides, or carbonates**. The word *corrosion* originates from the Latin **"corrodere"**, which literally means **"to gnaw away"** — a perfect description of how corrosion gradually eats into metals. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍎 > Think of corrosion as a **"rusty iron nail"** left in a damp corner of your house. The shiny iron surface slowly turns reddish-brown and crumbles. In chemical terms, the iron (Fe) is reacting with oxygen (O₂) and water (H₂O) in the air to form **iron(III) oxide-hydroxide (FeOOH)**, commonly known as **rust**. The metal is being **chemically eaten** by the environment. ### Standard Metrics Used in Corrosion Science - **Corrosion Rate (CR):** measured in **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimeters per year (mm/yr)**. - **Electrode Potential (E):** measured in **Volts (V)** versus **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**. - **Current Density (i):** measured in **A/cm²** or **µA/cm²**. --- ## 1.2 Essential Corrosion Vocabulary (KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlights) | Term | Formal Definition (KTU 2024) | |---|---| | **Anode** | The electrode where **oxidation** occurs; it is the metal that **loses electrons** and **dissolves** (corrodes). | | **Cathode** | The electrode where **reduction** occurs; it is **protected** and **does not corrode**. | | **Electrolyte** | An ionic conducting medium (aqueous solution, soil moisture, or molten salt) that allows ion flow between anode and cathode. | | **Oxidation** | Loss of electrons; metal atoms release electrons and become **cations** (M → M^n+ + ne⁻). | | **Reduction** | Gain of electrons; species (O₂, H⁺, H₂O) accept electrons at the cathode. | | **Electrochemical Cell** | A system where **chemical energy is converted into electrical energy** via redox reactions (e.g., Daniell cell). | | **Galvanic Cell** | A spontaneous electrochemical cell formed when **two dissimilar metals** are electrically connected in an electrolyte. | | **Electrolytic Cell** | A non-spontaneous electrochemical cell driven by an **external DC power source** (used in cathodic protection). | | **Passivity** | The state in which a metal becomes **thermodynamically unstable but kinetically inert** due to a thin, adherent, protective oxide film (e.g., Al, Ti, Cr, stainless steels). | | **Pitting Corrosion** | A **highly localized** form of corrosion producing small cavities or "pits" on an otherwise passivated surface. | | **Galvanic Corrosion** | Accelerated corrosion of the **more active metal** when it is electrically coupled to a **nobler metal** in a common electrolyte. | | **Faraday's Constant (F)** | **96,485 C/mol** — the electric charge carried by **one mole of electrons**. | | **Corrosion Current (I_corr)** | The rate of electron flow (in Amperes) associated with the corrosion reaction at the corrosion potential. | | **Corrosion Potential (E_corr)** | The mixed potential of a corroding metal measured against a reference electrode when **no external current is applied**. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between **electrochemical cell** and **electrolytic cell** for 3 marks. Memorize the direction of spontaneity: **Galvanic = spontaneous**, **Electrolytic = non-spontaneous (needs external power)**. --- ## 1.3 Faraday's Two Laws — The Quantitative Foundation of Corrosion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis:** > The **mass (m)** of a substance liberated at an electrode is **directly proportional** to the **quantity of charge (Q)** passed through the electrolyte. > $$ m \propto Q $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Faraday's Second Law of Electrolysis:** > When the **same quantity of electricity** is passed through different electrolytes in series, the **masses of substances liberated are proportional** to their **equivalent weights**. ### Conceptual Analogy 🔋 > Imagine water flowing through a pipe. The **total volume of water (Q = I × t)** is like the **total charge** passed. The **amount of dissolved metal carried away** (m) is the corrosion. More charge flowing → more metal dissolved → faster corrosion. Faraday's laws are the **"flow meter"** of corrosion science. --- ## 1.4 Corrosion Rate — The Practitioner's Metric > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** Corrosion Rate (CR) is the **amount of metal lost per unit area per unit time**, typically expressed in **mpy (mils per year)** in US units or **mm/yr** in SI units. A mil is **1/1000 of an inch** (i.e., **25.4 µm**). A corrosion rate of **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr**. > [!NOTE] > **Quick Reference:** > - **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr** > - **1 g/(m²·day) = 0.00144 mpy** (approximate) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Logical Flow of Electrochemical Corrosion Corrosion is fundamentally a **two-half-cell process** occurring simultaneously on the same metal surface (or between two metals). The sequence is: 1. **Anodic Reaction (Oxidation):** Metal atoms at the anodic site release electrons and dissolve into the electrolyte as cations. $$\text{M (s)} \longrightarrow \text{M}^{n+} \text{(aq)} + n e^-$$ 2. **Electron Transport:** The released electrons flow through the metal (or external wire) to the **cathodic site**. 3. **Cathodic Reaction (Reduction):** A species from the electrolyte (commonly O₂, H⁺, or H₂O) consumes the electrons. - In **aerated neutral solutions:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ - In **acidic solutions:** $\text{2H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \uparrow$ 4. **Ionic Migration:** Cations move toward the cathode through the electrolyte; anions move toward the anode, **closing the circuit**. 5. **Product Formation:** Dissolved metal ions combine with hydroxyl ions to form insoluble corrosion products (rust, patina, etc.). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter?** Without the **electrolyte path**, the circuit is incomplete and corrosion **stops**. This is the foundation of **cathodic protection** — break the electrolyte continuity or force all surfaces to be cathodes. --- ## 2.2 Derivation of Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law The mass of metal lost during corrosion can be calculated using Faraday's First Law. - **Quantity of Charge:** $Q = I \cdot t$ - **Mass of Metal Dissolved:** $m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ Where: - $I$ = corrosion current in **Amperes** - $t$ = time in **seconds** - $M$ = atomic weight of metal (g/mol) - $n$ = valence (number of electrons transferred per atom) - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** ### 2.2.1 Conversion to Corrosion Rate in mpy $$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$ Where: - $I$ = current in **Amperes** - $M$ = atomic weight - $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³) - $A$ = exposed area in **inches²** - **534** is a unit-conversion constant (= $0.129 \times 10^6 / 24 \times 365$) ### 2.2.2 SI Version — Corrosion Rate in mm/yr $$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$ Where: - $I$ = current in **µA** - $A$ = area in **cm²** - $\rho$ = density in **g/cm³** - $M$ = atomic weight - **87.6** is the SI unit-conversion constant --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **directly usable in KTU board examinations**. Practice substituting units carefully. | # | Formula | Parameter Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge = Current × Time | C = A·s | | 2 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved (Faraday) | grams | | 3 | $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Faraday's constant | C/mol | | 4 | Equivalent weight $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Molar mass per electron | g/eq | | 5 | CR (mpy) $= \dfrac{534 \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | US-units corrosion rate | mpy | | 6 | CR (mm/yr) $= \dfrac{87.6 \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A}$ | SI-units corrosion rate | mm/yr | | 7 | 1 mpy $= 0.0254$ mm/yr | Conversion | — | | 8 | 1 g/(m²·day) $\approx 1.44$ mpy | Conversion | — | | 9 | Tafel Equation: $\eta = a + b \log(i)$ | Overpotential vs log current density | V vs A/cm² | | 10 | Nernst Equation: $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log(Q)$ | Electrode potential under non-std conditions | Volts | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** The constant changes with the unit system! > - Use **534** for **mpy** (with area in in², current in A). > - Use **87.6** for **mm/yr** (with area in cm², current in µA). > Mixing the two = **automatic zero**. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These definitions and formulas are the **backbone** of: - **Pipeline Protection:** Calculating the current required for **cathodic protection** of buried oil/gas pipelines using the mpy formula. - **Marine Engineering:** Predicting service life of ship hulls, offshore platforms, and propellers in seawater. - **Chemical Plant Design:** Material selection for storage tanks, reactors, and heat exchangers handling acids, alkalis, and salts. - **Aerospace:** Estimating the lifetime of aluminum airframe components under atmospheric exposure. - **Boiler & Cooling Water Treatment:** Sizing the sacrificial anode mass (zinc, magnesium) needed to protect steel heat-exchanger tubes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Faraday's Law → Corrosion Rate (mpy) > [!NOTE] > This derivation is the **most repeated numerical question** in KTU Corrosion Control exams. Practice it until it becomes second nature. ### Step 1 — Start with Faraday's First Law The mass of metal dissolved at the anode is directly proportional to the total charge passed. $$m \propto Q$$ Introducing the proportionality constant based on the **equivalent weight (E = M/n)** and **Faraday's constant (F)**: $$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ ### Step 2 — Convert Mass Loss to Depth Loss (Penetration) The thickness loss (depth of corrosion) is: $$d = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A}$$ Where $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³) and $A$ = exposed area. Substituting $m$: $$d = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert Seconds to Years To express rate per year, replace $t$ with the number of seconds in one year: $$t = 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 3.1536 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$$ Substituting: $$\text{CR} = \frac{I \cdot (3.1536 \times 10^7) \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert cm to mils (1 cm = 393.7 mils ≈ 1000/2.54) Since 1 inch = 2.54 cm, we have 1 cm = 1/2.54 inch = 1000/2.54 mils = 393.7 mils. $$\text{CR} = \frac{I \cdot (3.1536 \times 10^7) \cdot M \cdot 393.7}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$ ### Step 5 — Simplify the Numerical Constant Plug in $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol and compute: $$K = \frac{3.1536 \times 10^7 \times 393.7}{96{,}485} \approx 128.7$$ But the empirical constant published in corrosion handbooks is **534** because the formula uses $I$ in **Amperes** and $A$ in **inches²** but $\rho$ in **g/cm³**, requiring an extra unit conversion. The final accepted form is: $$\boxed{\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot I \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A}}$$ ### Step 6 — Derivation of SI Version (mm/yr) For SI units, time is in years, area in cm², and current in µA. Following identical logic with $I$ in µA ($10^{-6}$ A) and 1 year = $3.1536 \times 10^7$ s, but with cm units: $$\boxed{\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{87.6 \cdot I_{\mu A} \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho \cdot A_{cm^2}}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A steel plate (Fe, $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³) of area **50 cm²** experiences a corrosion current of **150 µA**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(a) mm/yr** and **(b) mpy**. ### Solution **Part (a):** Using the mm/yr formula. $$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 150 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.87 \times 50}$$ Let us compute the numerator and denominator separately: - **Numerator:** $87.6 \times 150 = 13{,}140$; then $13{,}140 \times 55.85 = 733{,}869$ - **Denominator:** $2 \times 7.87 = 15.74$; then $15.74 \times 50 = 787$ $$\text{CR} = \frac{733{,}869}{787} \approx 932.5 \text{ mm/yr}$$ **Part (b):** Convert mm/yr to mpy using 1 mm = 39.37 mils: $$\text{CR (mpy)} = 932.5 \times 39.37 \approx 36{,}710 \text{ mpy}$$ That is **extraordinarily high** — characteristic of **severe localized or pitting corrosion**. For comparison, a CR > 50 mpy is generally considered **"unacceptable"** for structural steel. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Unit? | Scenario | Recommended CR Unit | Reason | |---|---|---| | US-based oil & gas industry | **mpy** | NACE / API standards use mpy | | European / Indian academic exam (KTU) | **mm/yr** | SI is preferred | | Comparing metal loss in lab coupons | **g/(m²·day)** or **mg/(dm²·day)** | Easy gravimetric calculation | | Pitting penetration rate | **mm/yr** with PREN notation | Pitting is depth-based | | Atmospheric exposure (long term) | **µm/yr** | Thin films, low rates | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Corrosion Cell > [!NOTE] > This block diagram captures the **interactions** between the four mandatory components of any corrosion cell. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ANODE - METAL DISSOLUTION
Oxidation: M to Mn plus plus ne] --> B[METALLIC PATH
Electron Flow e] B --> C[CATHODE - REDUCTION SITE
O2 plus H2O plus 4e to 4OH] C --> D[ELECTROLYTE
Ionic Conductor - Soil or Water] D --> A A --> E[CORROSION PRODUCT
Rust or Patina] E --> F[Environment] style A fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#B30000,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFE699,stroke:#B38600,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#C2F0C2,stroke:#009933,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Concept Map — Classification of Corrosion ```mermaid flowchart TB R[CORROSION] R --> R1[UNIFORM CORROSION] R --> R2[LOCALIZED CORROSION] R --> R3[DRY OR CHEMICAL CORROSION] R2 --> R2A[PITTING] R2 --> R2B[CREVICE] R2 --> R2C[GALVANIC] R2 --> R2D[INTERGRANULAR] R2 --> R2E[STRESS CORROSION CRACKING] R2 --> R2F[EROSION CORROSION] R3 --> R3A[OXIDATION AT HIGH TEMP] R3 --> R3B[CARBURIZATION] style R fill:#FFD966,stroke:#7F6000,stroke-width:3px style R1 fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:1px style R2 fill:#F4CCCC,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px style R3 fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture — Faraday to Corrosion Rate Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR step1[Faraday First Law m equals Q times M over nF] --> step2[Substitute Q equals I times t] step2 --> step3[Convert Mass to Depth using density and area] step3 --> step4[Convert Time to Year 3.1536 times 10 to power 7 s] step4 --> step5[Convert cm to mils or mm to maintain SI or US units] step5 --> step6[Final Formula CR equals 534 I M over n rho A for mpy] style step1 fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#674EA7,stroke-width:2px style step6 fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#38761D,stroke-width:3px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define corrosion. Differentiate between a galvanic cell and an electrolytic cell.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Corrosion (1 mark):** Corrosion is the irreversible deterioration of a metal or alloy by an electrochemical reaction with its environment, producing oxides, hydroxides, or salts of the metal. - **Galvanic cell (1 mark):** A spontaneous electrochemical cell that converts chemical energy into electrical energy; e.g., Daniell cell (Zn–Cu). - **Electrolytic cell (1 mark):** A non-spontaneous cell requiring an external DC source to drive the redox reaction; e.g., electroplating, cathodic protection. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024] **State Faraday's First and Second Laws of Electrolysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **First Law (1.5 marks):** The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed through the electrolyte. $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ - **Second Law (1.5 marks):** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights. $m_1 / m_2 = E_1 / E_2$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Define the following terms with one example each: **(7 marks)** 1. Anode and Cathode 2. Passivity 3. Galvanic corrosion **(b)** A mild steel component ($M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³) of exposed area **100 cm²** is observed to lose **0.6 g** in **30 days** under uniform corrosion. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(i) mdd**, **(ii) mm/yr**, and **(iii) mpy**. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question A **Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks):** 1. **Anode (1 mark):** The electrode at which oxidation occurs. Example: In Zn–Cu cell, Zn is the anode ($Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$). **Cathode (1 mark):** The electrode at which reduction occurs. Example: In Zn–Cu cell, Cu is the cathode ($Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$). 2. **Passivity (2 marks):** A state in which a metal becomes thermodynamically unstable (reactive) but kinetically inert due to a thin, adherent, and protective oxide film on its surface. Examples: Aluminum, Titanium, Chromium, and Stainless Steels form passive oxide layers. 3. **Galvanic Corrosion (2 marks):** Accelerated corrosion of the more active (anodic) metal when it is electrically coupled to a nobler (cathodic) metal in a common electrolyte. Example: Steel rivets in a copper plate corrode rapidly in seawater. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** $m = 0.6$ g, $t = 30$ days, $A = 100$ cm² = $0.01$ m². **(i) Corrosion rate in mdd (mg/dm²·day):** First, convert units: - $m = 0.6$ g = $600$ mg - $A = 100$ cm² = $1$ dm² $$\text{CR (mdd)} = \frac{600 \text{ mg}}{1 \text{ dm}^2 \times 30 \text{ days}} = 20 \text{ mdd}$$ **(ii) Corrosion rate in mm/yr:** Using Faraday's relation to find equivalent current, then applying the mm/yr formula. Alternatively, convert mdd directly: $$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{\text{CR (mdd)} \times 365}{10 \times \rho} = \frac{20 \times 365}{10 \times 7.87} = \frac{7300}{78.7} \approx 92.76 \text{ mm/yr}$$ **(iii) Corrosion rate in mpy:** Using 1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy: $$\text{CR (mpy)} = 92.76 \times 39.37 \approx 3652 \text{ mpy}$$ This extremely high rate indicates the component is **in a highly aggressive environment** and requires immediate **cathodic protection or replacement**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Define the following terms: **(7 marks)** 1. Standard electrode potential 2. Reference electrode 3. Nernst equation 4. Tafel equation **(b)** A copper pipe ($M = 63.5$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 8.96$ g/cm³) of area **250 cm²** carries a corrosion current of **80 µA**. Calculate the corrosion rate in **(i) mm/yr** and **(ii) mpy**. Comment on the suitability of the pipe for domestic water supply. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question B **Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks):** 1. **Standard Electrode Potential (2 marks):** The potential of an electrode measured against the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) at standard conditions (1 M concentration, 1 atm pressure, 25 °C). Convention: A more positive E° means more noble (cathodic). 2. **Reference Electrode (2 marks):** An electrode with a stable, reproducible, and well-known potential used to measure the potential of another electrode. Examples: Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE), Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE), Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl). 3. **Nernst Equation (1.5 marks):** $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10}(Q)$ at 25 °C. It relates the electrode potential to the concentration of ionic species. 4. **Tafel Equation (1.5 marks):** $\eta = a + b \log(i)$. It is an empirical relation between the overpotential ($\eta$) and the current density ($i$) in electrochemical kinetics. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) CR in mm/yr:** $$\text{CR} = \frac{87.6 \times 80 \times 63.5}{2 \times 8.96 \times 250} = \frac{445{,}128}{4480} \approx 99.36 \text{ mm/yr}$$ **(ii) CR in mpy:** $$\text{CR (mpy)} = 99.36 \times 39.37 \approx 3911 \text{ mpy}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** A CR of ~99 mm/yr (3911 mpy) is **catastrophically high** — far exceeding the acceptable limit of < 20 mpy for domestic water pipes. The pipe is **unsuitable** without protective coating or cathodic protection. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > - **Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Using the wrong conversion constant (534 vs 87.6) when switching between mpy and mm/yr. **Always check units first.** > - **Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert area or current to the correct units before substituting. > - **Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Confusing **anode with cathode**. Mnemonic: **"A = Anode = Away"** (electrons go away from anode). > - **Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Not writing units in the final answer. Always state **mm/yr** or **mpy** explicitly. > - **Mistake 5 (–2 marks):** Mixing up $E^\circ$ values in the galvanic series with actual field potentials — always clarify **standard** vs **measured** conditions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Definitions):** - **Corrosion** = irreversible electrochemical deterioration of a metal. - **Anode** = site of oxidation (metal dissolves, electrons released). - **Cathode** = site of reduction (protected, electrons consumed). - **Electrolyte** = ionic conductor that completes the circuit. - **Galvanic Cell** = spontaneous; **Electrolytic Cell** = needs external DC power. - **Passivity** = kinetically inert state due to a protective oxide film (Al, Ti, Cr, SS). - **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$. - **Faraday's Second Law:** $m_1 / m_2 = E_1 / E_2$. - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (memorize the value). - **CR (mpy) formula** uses constant **534**; **CR (mm/yr)** uses constant **87.6**. - **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr**; **1 mm/yr = 39.37 mpy**. - **Acceptable corrosion rate** for structural steel: **< 20 mpy (0.5 mm/yr)**. - **Galvanic corrosion** = dissimilar metals coupled in electrolyte; the **more active** one corrodes. - **Standard conditions** for E°: 1 M, 1 atm, 25 °C, vs SHE. - **Reference electrodes** to remember: SHE, SCE, Ag/AgCl. - **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ - (0.0591 / n) \log Q$ at 25 °C. - **Tafel Equation:** $\eta = a + b \log i$ — relates overpotential to current density. - **Rust** = hydrated Fe₂O₃ / FeOOH — the most common corrosion product of steel. - **Patina** = greenish layer on copper (basic copper carbonate) — actually **protective**. > **End of Module 1 — Definitions Notes | PEICT866 Corrosion Control**

topics.title: Corrosive Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Corrosive Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Corrosive Environment** is any naturally occurring or artificially created surrounding medium (gas, liquid, soil, or biological system) whose chemical, electrochemical, thermal, or biological characteristics are capable of initiating and sustaining the degradation of engineering materials — particularly metals and alloys — through electrochemical, chemical, or microbiologically influenced reactions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, a corrosive environment is characterized by three essential thermodynamic/kinetic parameters:
1. **Electrolyte availability** (water, moisture, or ionic conducting medium)
2. **Oxidizing species** (dissolved $O_2$, $H^+$, $Cl^-$, $SO_2$, $H_2S$)
3. **Sufficient thermal/activation energy** to overcome the activation barrier of the anodic dissolution reaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A "corrosive environment" is NOT limited to strong acids. Even atmospheric moisture with dissolved $CO_2$ (forming weak carbonic acid, pH ≈ 5.6) is classified as a corrosive environment, because it provides both the electrolyte and the cathodic reactant.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Rust Bucket" Mental Model

Imagine you left a brand-new iron nail on your kitchen counter for six months. Nothing spills on it. No acid is poured. Yet, by the end of the monsoon season, it turns brown and flaky. **Why?**

The kitchen air, although invisible, is not empty — it is a **gaseous electrolyte**. It carries:
- Water vapor (humidity)
- Dissolved $O_2$ (≈ 21% of air)
- Dissolved $CO_2$ (forming $H_2CO_3$)
- Trace $NaCl$ or $SO_2$ (from cooking, coastal areas, or industrial zones)

The nail is a **micro-galvanic cell**: at one microscopic spot iron oxidizes (anode), at another spot oxygen reduces (cathode), and the thin moisture film acts as the **electrolyte highway** carrying ions between them.

> **The Environment is the unseen electrolyte.** The metal doesn't need to be "dipped" — it just needs to be *exposed*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Callout

> [!NOTE]
> - **Electrolyte:** A medium containing mobile ions capable of carrying current (e.g., salt water, humid air film, soil moisture).
> - **Anodic site:** Where metal loses electrons (oxidation, $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$).
> - **Cathodic site:** Where a reduction reaction occurs (e.g., $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$).
> - **Aggressivity:** The relative severity of a corrosive environment, often quantified by mass loss (mpy or mm/yr).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Corrosive Environments

Corrosive environments are systematically classified into **five major categories** under the KTU 2024 framework:

#### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Corrosion
- **Medium:** Humid air with reactive gaseous pollutants
- **Mechanism:** Electrochemical reaction under thin electrolyte film (Type I, II, or III wetting)
- **Key Drivers:** Relative Humidity (RH), $SO_2$, $Cl^-$, $NO_x$, temperature cycling
- **Critical Insight:** Atmospheric corrosion rate accelerates sharply when RH > **Critical Humidity (≈ 60–80%)**.

#### 2.1.2 Marine / Seawater Environment
- **Medium:** ≈ 3.5% NaCl solution, high $Cl^-$ activity
- **pH range:** 7.5 – 8.3 (slightly alkaline, well-buffered)
- **Key Driver:** $Cl^-$ ions penetrate passive films, causing pitting and crevice corrosion.
- **Aggressivity Index:** Very High — Reynolds number, temperature, and dissolved $O_2$ all matter.

#### 2.1.3 Industrial / Chemical Plant Environment
- **Media:** Acids ($HCl$, $H_2SO_4$, $HNO_3$), alkalis ($NaOH$), organic solvents
- **Key Drivers:** pH, oxidizing/reducing nature, concentration, temperature
- **Hazards:** General (uniform) corrosion, intergranular corrosion, stress corrosion cracking (SCC)

#### 2.1.4 Soil / Underground Environment
- **Medium:** Heterogeneous mixture of minerals, organic matter, water, dissolved salts
- **Key Drivers:** Soil resistivity ($\Omega \cdot cm$), moisture content, pH, redox potential, bacterial activity (e.g., **Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria — SRB**)
- **Notable:** Stray current from DC traction systems or pipelines causes severe localized corrosion.

#### 2.1.5 High-Temperature / Gaseous Environment
- **Mechanism:** Chemical (not electrochemical) oxidation
- **Key Reaction:** $M + \tfrac{n}{2}O_2 \rightarrow M_nO_n$ (e.g., oxide scale formation on boilers, jet engines)
- **Driver:** Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) determines whether the scale is protective.

### 2.2 Factors Governing Environment Aggressivity

| **Factor** | **Effect on Corrosion** | **Typical Critical Value** |
|---|---|---|
| Relative Humidity (RH) | Exponential rise in rate above threshold | RH > 60–80% |
| Temperature | Arrhenius-type acceleration | +10 °C ≈ doubles rate |
| pH | Acidic media favor $H^+$ reduction | pH < 4 aggressive to steel |
| Dissolved $O_2$ | Cathodic depolarizer | > 5 ppm in water |
| $Cl^-$ concentration | Localized attack, pitting | > 50 ppm in fresh water |
| $SO_2$ in air | Forms $H_2SO_3$ / $H_2SO_4$ | > 0.1 ppm |
| Flow velocity | Erosion-corrosion threshold | > 1–2 m/s for Cu-alloys |
| Microbial activity | MIC, tubercles | SRB in anaerobic soil |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | Equilibrium potential of half-cell |
| Simplified Nernst (25 °C) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Standard lab/board problems |
| Faraday's Law of Corrosion | $w = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass loss from current |
| Corrosion Rate (mpy) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | W in mg, D in g/cm³, A in in², t in hr |
| Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) | $\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | SI metric form |
| Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}$ | Protective vs. non-protective scale |
| Current Density (Tafel) | $\eta = \beta \log \left( \dfrac{i}{i_0} \right)$ | Activation overpotential |
| Soil Resistivity | $\rho = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{L}$ | Soil corrosivity classification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the **simplified Nernst constant = 0.0591 V** at 25 °C (= 298 K). The 0.0591 comes from $\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}$ at 298 K. Always specify n (number of electrons transferred).

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Materials Selection:** Choosing correct alloys (e.g., duplex SS for marine, Inconel for high-temp).
- **Cathodic Protection Design:** Requires soil resistivity, water chemistry data.
- **Failure Analysis:** Identifying whether a failure was due to SCC, pitting, or MIC.
- **Coating & Inhibitor Selection:** Matching inhibitor chemistry to environment (e.g., $NaNO_2$ for neutral, amines for acid).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Simplified Nernst Equation for a Corrosive Half-Cell

**Starting Premise:** Consider a generalized corrosion half-cell reaction at equilibrium:

$$
M^{n+}_{aq} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons M_{(s)}
$$

The Gibbs free energy change under non-standard conditions relates to the reaction quotient $Q$ via:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

We know that $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$. Substituting both:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

Divide both sides by $-nF$:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Step 1 — Convert natural log to base-10 log** using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute standard values at 25 °C (T = 298 K):**
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$
- $2.303 \cdot R \cdot T = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298$

Let us evaluate this product step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2.303 \times 8.314 &= 19.147 \;\text{J/(mol·K)} \\
19.147 \times 298 &= 5705.8 \;\text{J/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by F (96485 C/mol):**

$$
\frac{5705.8}{96485} = 0.05913 \text{ V} \approx 0.0591 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Final simplified Nernst Equation at 25 °C:**

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}
$$

> For the metal deposition half-cell, $[\text{Red}] = 1$ (solid) and $[\text{Ox}] = [M^{n+}]$, so the equation further reduces to:

$$
E_{M^{n+}/M} = E^{\circ}_{M^{n+}/M} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} [M^{n+}]
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)

**Physical Premise:** When a metal oxidizes, the volume of the oxide formed compared to the parent metal volume determines whether the oxide layer protects the underlying metal.

**Volume of metal consumed per mole:**

$$
V_{metal} = \frac{M_{metal}}{\rho_{metal}}
$$

**Volume of oxide formed per mole of metal** (with n metal atoms per oxide formula):

$$
V_{oxide} = \frac{M_{oxide}}{n \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

**Ratio:**

$$
\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}} = \frac{M_{oxide} \cdot \rho_{metal}}{n \cdot M_{metal} \cdot \rho_{oxide}}
$$

**Interpretation rule:**
- $\text{PBR} < 1$: Oxide is porous, non-protective (e.g., Mg, alkali metals)
- $1 < \text{PBR} < 2$: Protective, adherent scale (e.g., Al, Cr, SS)
- $\text{PBR} > 2$: Scale spalls off due to compressive stress (e.g., Fe — reason rust flakes off)

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Typical Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $w$ | Mass of metal lost | grams (g) or mg |
| $I$ | Corrosion current | Amperes (A) or mA |
| $t$ | Exposure time | seconds (s) or hours |
| $M$ | Molar mass of metal | g/mol |
| $n$ | Electrons transferred | dimensionless |
| $F$ | Faraday's constant | 96485 C/mol |
| $D$ | Density of metal | g/cm³ |
| $A$ | Exposed surface area | cm² or in² |

**Sample Numerical Demonstration (Iron in acid):**

Given: $I = 0.1$ mA, $t = 24$ h, Fe $\rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$, $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $D_{Fe} = 7.87$ g/cm³, $A = 10$ cm².

**Step 1:** Convert charge $Q = I \cdot t$:

$$
Q = 0.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 8.64 \text{ C}
$$

**Step 2:** Moles of electrons = $Q/F$:

$$
n_{e^-} = \frac{8.64}{96485} = 8.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Moles of Fe dissolved = $n_{e^-}/2$ (since n = 2):

$$
n_{Fe} = 4.48 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass loss:

$$
w = 4.48 \times 10^{-5} \times 55.85 = 2.50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 2.50 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 5:** Corrosion rate in mpy (W in mg, t in hr, A in in², D in g/cm³). Convert 10 cm² → 1.55 in²:

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{534 \times 2.50}{7.87 \times 1.55 \times 24} = 4.56 \text{ mpy}
$$

This is a *moderate* corrosion rate — typical of mild steel in dilute acid.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Corrosive Environments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corrosive Environment] --> B[Atmospheric]
    A --> C[Marine]
    A --> D[Industrial]
    A --> E[Soil]
    A --> F[High Temperature]

    B --> B1[RH above 60 percent]
    B --> B2[SO2 and NOx pollutants]
    B --> B3[Chloride from coastal air]

    C --> C1[Seawater 3.5 percent NaCl]
    C --> C2[Submerged structures]
    C --> C3[Splash zone aeration cell]

    D --> D1[Acid media pH less than 4]
    D --> D2[Alkaline media]
    D --> D3[Organic solvents]

    E --> E1[Soil resistivity]
    E --> E2[SRB microbiological activity]
    E --> E3[Stray DC currents]

    F --> F1[Oxidation scaling]
    F --> F2[Carburization]
    F --> F3[Sulfidation]

    B1 --> G[Thin electrolyte film mechanism]
    C1 --> G
    D1 --> H[Electrochemical aqueous cell]
    E1 --> H
    F1 --> I[Direct chemical attack]

    G --> J[Material degradation outcome]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Atmospheric Corrosion Mechanism (Electrochemical Cell in a Water Film)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MetalSurface[Metal Surface with Thin Moisture Film]
        A1[Anodic site M dissolves to Mn+] -->|Electrons travel through metal| A2[Cathodic site O2 reduces to OH-]
    end

    A2 -->|OH- ions migrate| A3[Metal hydroxide forms FeOH2]
    A1 -->|Mn+ ions migrate| A3
    A3 --> A4[Further oxidation to Fe2O3 rust]

    Pollutants[SO2 and Cl- in air] -->|Dissolve into film| MetalSurface
```

### 4.3 Pourbaix Diagram — pH–Potential Map (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Pourbaix Diagram for Fe-H2O at 25 C] --> P2[Vertical axis E in V vs SHE]
    P1 --> P3[Horizontal axis pH 0 to 14]

    P2 --> P4[Upper region immunity of Fe2+ and Fe3+]
    P2 --> P5[Middle region passive Fe2O3 and Fe3O4]
    P2 --> P6[Lower region immune Fe metal]

    P3 --> P7[Acidic pH less than 4 soluble Fe2+]
    P3 --> P8[Neutral pH 4 to 9 passive film]
    P3 --> P9[Alkaline pH greater than 9 soluble HFeO2-]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Tip for KTU Board:** A Pourbaix diagram is a **thermodynamic** map, not a kinetic one. It tells you *whether* corrosion is possible, not *how fast* it occurs. Always overlay kinetic data (Tafel slopes, $i_{corr}$) for engineering decisions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. Define a "corrosive environment" and list the four essential conditions required for corrosion to occur in such an environment. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
A corrosive environment is any surrounding medium (gaseous, liquid, or soil) whose physicochemical characteristics promote the degradation of engineering materials. The **four essential conditions** (often called the "corrosion cell quartet") are:
1. Presence of an **anode** (metal undergoing oxidation)
2. Presence of a **cathode** (site for reduction reaction)
3. **Electrical continuity** between anode and cathode (through the metal)
4. **Electrolyte path** between anode and cathode (ionic conduction)

If any one of these is missing, corrosion halts — this is the basis of all corrosion prevention methods (e.g., insulating coating breaks the electrolyte path).

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1(b)]**

**Q2. State and explain the Pilling–Bedworth Ratio. Why does iron rust flake off while aluminum oxide remains adherent? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR) is the ratio of the volume of oxide formed to the volume of parent metal consumed during oxidation.

$$
\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{oxide}}{V_{metal}}
$$

- For **iron**, $\text{PBR} \approx 2.1$ — oxide volume exceeds metal volume, generating compressive stress that causes the scale to spall. Hence, rust flakes.
- For **aluminum**, $\text{PBR} \approx 1.28$ — lies in the protective range (1–2), the oxide film is adherent and self-healing.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Q2]** *(Module Internal Choice Pattern)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Classify the various types of corrosive environments encountered in engineering practice. For each, identify the dominant corrosion mechanism and one representative failure case. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Environment** | **Dominant Mechanism** | **Representative Failure** |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric | Thin-film electrochemical | Rusting of iron bridges near coast |
| Marine | $Cl^-$-induced pitting | Pitting of 304 SS in seawater |
| Industrial (acidic) | Uniform + SCC | Stress corrosion cracking of brass in $NH_3$ |
| Soil | Differential aeration + MIC | Pipeline leaks from SRB |
| High-temperature | Chemical oxidation | Boiler tube scaling |
| Biological (MIC) | Cathodic depolarization | Aluminum fuel tank perforation |

Each environment is characterized by its own electrolyte chemistry, oxygen availability, and pollutant profile. Industrial environments with strong acids tend to cause uniform corrosion, while chloride-rich marine environments favor localized pitting.

**(b) Discuss the role of dissolved oxygen, pH, temperature, and chloride ions in determining the aggressivity of an aqueous corrosive environment. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Dissolved Oxygen ($DO$):** Acts as the primary cathodic depolarizer. In neutral/alkaline water: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$. Higher DO → higher $i_{corr}$ until it saturates. In hot water, DO decreases but kinetics accelerate, often with a net increase in corrosion.

2. **pH:**
   - For Fe, Zn, Mg: corrosion rate is high in acid (pH < 4), drops in near-neutral region, and rises again in strong alkali (pH > 10) due to formation of soluble ferrates.
   - For amphoteric metals (Al, Zn, Pb), both acid and alkali are aggressive.
   - Pourbaix diagrams map these regimes precisely.

3. **Temperature:** Arrhenius-type — rate roughly doubles per 10 °C rise in activation-controlled regimes. However, in open systems, oxygen solubility decreases, sometimes giving a maximum at ≈ 80 °C.

4. **Chloride Ions ($Cl^-$):** Small, aggressive anion that penetrates passive films. Critical in causing **pitting corrosion** and **crevice corrosion** of stainless steels. A $Cl^-/OH^-$ ratio > 0.3 typically initiates pitting in 304 SS.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice)*

**(a) Derive the Nernst equation for the half-cell $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons M$. How is it applied to predict the corrosion tendency of a metal in a solution of known ion concentration? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start from the relation between Gibbs free energy and the reaction quotient:

$$
\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$
-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q
$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to base-10 logarithm and substitute $T = 298$ K:

$$
E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10}[M^{n+}]
$$

**Application:** If $[M^{n+}] = 1$ M, $E = E^{\circ}$ (standard potential). If $[M^{n+}] < 1$ M, the metal is *less noble* (more negative E) and more likely to corrode. This is used in constructing **Pourbaix diagrams** and predicting galvanic series behavior.

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the mechanism of atmospheric corrosion of iron. Explain why corrosion is accelerated in coastal and industrial atmospheres. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mechanism (refer to Section 4.2):**
Iron surface exposed to humid air develops a thin moisture film (Type III wetting) that becomes the electrolyte. Micro-galvanic cells form — anodic dissolution $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ and cathodic reduction $\tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2OH^-$. The $Fe^{2+}$ and $OH^-$ combine, then oxidize further to hydrated $Fe_2O_3$ (rust).

**Acceleration in Coastal Areas:** Sea-spray aerosol deposits $Cl^-$ ions on the metal. $Cl^-$ breaks down the protective film, causes pitting, and increases electrical conductivity of the moisture film.

**Acceleration in Industrial Areas:** Combustion of fossil fuels releases $SO_2$, which dissolves in the moisture film to form sulfurous/sulfuric acid, lowering the pH and increasing $H^+$ availability for cathodic reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing "corrosive environment" with "corrosive agent."** The environment is the *medium*, the agent is the *species* (e.g., $Cl^-$, $O_2$). Students often mix the two and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the third and fourth conditions** of the corrosion cell (electrical continuity and electrolyte path). Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Writing PBR as a ratio of masses** instead of volumes — this is a 1-mark error that appears almost every year.
> 4. **Quoting the Nernst equation with $\log_e$ instead of $\log_{10}$** in numerical problems. Always convert.
> 5. **Stating "pH < 7 is corrosive"** — this is wrong! Some metals (Cu) are attacked by *alkaline* solutions. Always specify metal–environment compatibility.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Corrosive Environment**

- [x] A corrosive environment is a **medium** (not a chemical), characterized by electrolyte + oxidizer + activation energy.
- [x] The **four conditions** for corrosion: anode, cathode, metallic path, electrolyte path.
- [x] Five major environments: **Atmospheric, Marine, Industrial, Soil, High-temperature**.
- [x] **Critical Humidity** ≈ 60–80% RH marks the onset of significant atmospheric corrosion.
- [x] **$Cl^-$** → pitting & crevice attack; **$SO_2$** → acid rain attack; **SRB** → MIC in soils.
- [x] **Nernst equation** simplified constant at 25 °C: **0.0591 V**.
- [x] **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio** is a *volume* ratio, not mass.
  - PBR < 1 → non-protective; 1–2 → protective; > 2 → spalling.
- [x] **Pourbaix diagram** = thermodynamic map (E vs pH); not kinetic.
- [x] Corrosion rate units: **mpy** (US) and **mm/yr** (SI).
- [x] Faraday's Law: $w = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — fundamental to all lab calculations.
- [x] Dissolved $O_2$ is the **cathodic depolarizer** in neutral/alkaline aqueous corrosion.
- [x] Amphoteric metals (Al, Zn, Pb) corrode in **both** acid and alkali.
- [x] Soil resistivity, moisture, and bacterial activity are the **big three** for underground corrosion.
- [x] Stray currents from DC traction or welding cause **severe localized** soil corrosion.
- [x] Remember $F = 96485$ C/mol and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) — examiners may not provide them.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway:** Identifying the environment is **70% of corrosion control** — the rest is materials selection and protection strategy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consequences of Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Consequences of Corrosion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Consequences of Corrosion** refer to the cumulative, multi-dimensional adverse effects—both tangible and intangible—that result from the unwanted degradation of metals and alloys through electrochemical, chemical, or microbiological interaction with their environment. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEICT866), these consequences are systematically classified into **direct consequences** (immediate material loss, equipment failure, plant shutdown) and **indirect consequences** (economic loss due to production downtime, environmental contamination, safety hazards, and loss of product purity). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"Corrosion, though often perceived merely as surface degradation, is a multi-faceted phenomenon resulting in direct material loss, indirect economic losses, safety hazards, environmental pollution, and contamination of products. A thorough understanding of these consequences is essential to justify corrosion control investments."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the **human body**. When you catch a disease, you don't only suffer from the *direct symptom* (say, a fever). You also lose working days, spend money on medicines, feel fatigued, and the disease may have long-term effects on your organs. The disease itself is analogous to the **corrosion process**, while the fever is the **direct consequence**, and the lost wages, medical bills, and organ damage are the **indirect consequences**. Similarly, when a pipe carrying sulphuric acid corrodes in a chemical plant: - **Direct consequence** → A hole forms in the pipe (material loss). - **Indirect consequences** → Acid leaks, the plant must shut down for repairs, workers might be exposed, nearby soil and groundwater get contaminated, and the company loses revenue. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU Examiners:** > Examiners often expect students to mention that the **indirect economic losses typically exceed the direct material losses by a factor of 3 to 5 times** (industry estimate). Memorize this ratio—it is a high-yield factoid. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Consequences Corrosion engineers worldwide use these standard metrics (often appearing in KTU numerical problems): - **mm/year (mpy)** — Corrosion rate measurement. - **USD per square meter per year** — Cost of corrosion per unit area. - **Percentage of GDP** — National economic burden. - **MTPA (Million Tonnes Per Annum)** — Material loss in developing nations. > [!TIP] > The total global cost of corrosion is estimated at **~3–4% of the world's GDP**, a figure often cited in KTU viva voce questions. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Corrosion Consequences The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to classify the consequences into **six well-defined categories**. Each category has been explained below with operational logic and engineering utility. ### 2.1.1 Direct Material Loss This is the most obvious consequence: the metal itself is consumed, converted into oxides, hydroxides, or salts. - **Mechanism:** Metal atoms lose electrons (anodic reaction) and dissolve into the electrolyte. - **Engineering Impact:** Reduces wall thickness, cross-sectional area, and load-bearing capacity. - **Example:** Rebar corrosion in concrete bridges reduces the effective diameter of steel, weakening the structure. ### 2.1.2 Plant Shutdown and Loss of Production When a critical component (heat exchanger, condenser, pipeline) fails due to corrosion, the entire plant must be brought offline for repair or replacement. - **Why it matters:** Industrial plants have tightly coupled processes; one failed heat exchanger can halt the whole plant. - **Real-world example:** A crude oil refinery shutdown can cost **$500,000 to $1,000,000 per day** in lost revenue. ### 2.1.3 Loss of Product and Contamination Corrosion products (metal ions, oxides) can leach into the process stream, contaminating the product. - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Even trace metal contamination (ppb levels) can ruin a batch. - **Food Industry:** Tin or iron leaching into canned food is a serious health hazard. - **Electronics Industry:** Copper ion contamination in printed circuit board etching baths ruins production. ### 2.1.4 Safety Hazards and Human Risk Corrosion-induced failures can lead to catastrophic accidents: -
