# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Corrosion Science

Corrosion science rests upon **three fundamental pillars**, each of which must be mastered for KTU-level proficiency:

### Pillar 1: Thermodynamic Driving Force
- Every corrosion reaction is governed by a **negative Gibbs Free Energy change ($\Delta G < 0$)**.
- The more negative the $\Delta G$, the greater the thermodynamic tendency to corrode.
- Quantified using the **Nernst Equation** (covered in §3 below).

### Pillar 2: Kinetic Rate Control
- Even if $\Delta G < 0$ (thermodynamically favorable), the actual *rate* of corrosion depends on kinetics.
- Governed by the **Butler-Volmer Equation**, activation energy, and Tafel slopes.
- The **Tafel Equation** is critical: $\eta = a + b \log(i)$, where $\eta$ is overpotential, $i$ is current density, and $a, b$ are Tafel constants.

### Pillar 3: Environmental and Material Variables
- **Material factors**: Composition, microstructure, surface condition, homogeneity.
- **Environmental factors**: pH, temperature, oxygen concentration, chloride content, fluid velocity, biological activity.
- The **Pourbaix Diagram** (E vs. pH) is the master chart that maps thermodynamic stability regions for a metal-water system.

---

## 2.2 Pillars of Corrosion Engineering

Corrosion engineering is built upon the **NACE/AMPP Eight-Point Strategy** (a globally recognized framework):

1. **Proper selection of materials** with inherent corrosion resistance (e.g., stainless steel, titanium, alloys).
2. **Proper design** — avoiding crevices, ensuring drainage, preventing galvanic couples.
3. **Alteration of environment** — removing oxygen, lowering temperature, reducing chloride.
4. **Use of inhibitors** — anodic, cathodic, or mixed inhibitors.
5. **Protective coatings** — paints, metallic coatings (galvanizing, electroplating).
6. **Cathodic protection** — sacrificial anode or impressed current method.
7. **Anodic protection** — using passive films on metals like stainless steel.
8. **Monitoring and inspection** — regular NDT and corrosion rate measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite**: Examiners love asking students to "List and explain the various methods of corrosion control" — this Eight-Point Strategy is the canonical answer framework.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **all foundational equations** that govern Corrosion Science. Master these for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gibbs Free Energy** | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol, $E$ = cell potential (V) | Determines thermodynamic spontaneity |
| 2 | **Nernst Equation** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K), $Q$ = reaction quotient | Electrode potential under non-standard conditions |
| 3 | **Tafel Equation (Anodic)** | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | $\eta_a$ = anodic overpotential, $\beta_a$ = anodic Tafel slope, $i_0$ = exchange current density | High-field kinetics |
| 4 | **Tafel Equation (Cathodic)** | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | $\eta_c$ = cathodic overpotential, $\beta_c$ = cathodic Tafel slope | Cathodic reaction kinetics |
| 5 | **Corrosion Rate (mpy)** | $CR = \dfrac{0.129 \times i \times EW}{d}$ | $i$ = current density ($\mu A/cm^2$), $EW$ = equivalent weight, $d$ = density ($g/cm^3$) | Engineering corrosion rate |
| 6 | **Corrosion Rate (mm/yr)** | $CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times i \times EW}{d}$ | Same as above, result in mm/yr | SI-based rate |
| 7 | **Faraday's Law of Electrolysis** | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | $W$ = mass corroded (g), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = molar mass | Mass loss calculation |
| 8 | **Ohm's Law (Corrosion Cell)** | $I = \dfrac{E_{cell}}{R_{cell}}$ | $R_{cell}$ = total cell resistance ($\Omega$) | Current flowing in corrosion cell |
| 9 | **Polarization Resistance** | $R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 (\beta_a + \beta_c) \cdot i_{corr}}$ | $R_p$ = polarization resistance ($\Omega \cdot cm^2$) | LPR measurement technique |
| 10 | **Log Form of Nernst** | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | At 25 °C, $RT/F \ln 10 \approx 0.0591$ V | Quick equilibrium potential |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion Factor**: $0.129$ mpy/($g/cm^3$) and $3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ mm/yr/($g/cm^3$) are standard ASTM G102 conversion constants. Memorize both for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles established in this topic underpin critical industries:

- **Oil & Gas**: Pipeline integrity management, sour service (H₂S) environments.
- **Marine Engineering**: Hull protection of ships, offshore platform cathodic protection.
- **Aerospace**: Stress corrosion cracking prevention in aircraft aluminum alloys.
- **Civil Infrastructure**: Rebar corrosion in reinforced concrete bridges and buildings.
- **Power Generation**: Boiler tube failures, steam generator integrity in nuclear plants.
- **Chemical Processing**: Selection of alloys for sulfuric acid, caustic, and chloride service.

> [!TIP]
> The **global direct cost of corrosion** is estimated at approximately **3–4% of global GDP** (per NACE IMPACT studies). This statistic is a high-yield KTU answer point for "Why study corrosion?" questions.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Nernst Equation

The Nernst Equation is **the single most important equation** in corrosion thermodynamics. Below is its complete, step-by-step derivation from first principles.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Gibbs Free Energy Change

For a generic electrochemical half-reaction:
$$\text{Ox} + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

The Gibbs free energy change at non-standard conditions is given by:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient (ratio of product activities to reactant activities, each raised to stoichiometric power).

---

### Step 2: Relating $\Delta G$ to Cell Potential

From electrochemical thermodynamics, the relationship between Gibbs free energy and cell potential is:
$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

Similarly, under standard conditions:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell}$$

**Logic of the conversion**: The negative sign indicates that a *spontaneous* reaction ($\Delta G < 0$) corresponds to a *positive* cell potential ($E_{cell} > 0$).

---

### Step 3: Substituting into the Gibbs Equation

Substituting the two relationships from Step 2 into the equation from Step 1:
$$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell} + RT \ln Q$$

Rearranging:
$$nFE_{cell} = nFE^{\circ}_{cell} - RT \ln Q$$

Dividing both sides by $nF$:
$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Conversion logic**: We divide by $nF$ to isolate $E_{cell}$ as the subject, yielding the famous Nernst Equation.

---

### Step 4: Converting to Base-10 Logarithm (Common Log Form)

Since most engineering data uses $\log_{10}$ (not natural log), we apply the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cell} - \dfrac{2.303 RT}{nF} \log_{10} Q$$

Substituting values at standard temperature $T = 298.15$ K:
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $F = 96,485$ C/mol
- $2.303 \times R \times T / F = 0.05916$ V (at 25 °C)

Therefore:
$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log_{10} Q$$

This is the **common engineering form** used universally.

---

### Step 5: Derivation of the Tafel Equation (Anodic Branch)

Starting from the **Butler-Volmer Equation** (high-overpotential limit):
$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

At high anodic overpotentials, the cathodic term becomes negligible:
$$i \approx i_0 \exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:
$$\ln i = \ln i_0 + \dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}$$

Solving for $\eta$:
$$\eta = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_a n F} \ln i - \dfrac{RT}{\alpha_a n F} \ln i_0$$

Converting to $\log_{10}$ and grouping constants:
$$\eta = \underbrace{\dfrac{2.303 RT}{\alpha_a n F}}_{=\beta_a} \log_{10} i - \underbrace{\dfrac{2.303 RT}{\alpha_a n F} \log_{10} i_0}_{=a}$$

This yields the **Tafel Equation** in canonical form:
$$\eta = a + \beta_a \log_{10} i$$

where $a$ is the intercept and $\beta_a$ is the anodic Tafel slope (in mV/decade).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A steel coupon is exposed to a corrosive environment. The corrosion current density $i_{corr} = 10 \, \mu A/cm^2$. The equivalent weight of iron $EW = 28 \, g/eq$, and the density $d = 7.87 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate the corrosion rate in (a) mpy and (b) mm/yr.

### Part (a): Corrosion Rate in mpy

$$CR = \dfrac{0.129 \times i \times EW}{d}$$

**Step 1**: Substitute the values:
$$CR = \dfrac{0.129 \times 10 \times 28}{7.87}$$

**Step 2**: Compute numerator:
$$0.129 \times 10 = 1.29$$
$$1.29 \times 28 = 36.12$$

**Step 3**: Divide by density:
$$CR = \dfrac{36.12}{7.87}$$

**Step 4**: Final result:
$$CR = 4.59 \, mpy$$

---

### Part (b): Corrosion Rate in mm/yr

$$CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times i \times EW}{d}$$

**Step 1**: Substitute the values:
$$CR = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 28}{7.87}$$

**Step 2**: Compute numerator:
$$3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 = 0.0327$$
$$0.0327 \times 28 = 0.9156$$

**Step 3**: Divide by density:
$$CR = \dfrac{0.9156}{7.87}$$

**Step 4**: Final result:
$$CR = 0.1163 \, mm/yr$$

---

### Verification (Cross-Check)

Since $1 \, mpy = 0.0254 \, mm/yr$, then:
$$4.59 \, mpy \times 0.0254 = 0.1166 \, mm/yr$$

The values match (within rounding), confirming the calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: Always show the unit conversion explicitly in the final answer. Marks are awarded for **correct units**, not just numerical values.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define corrosion science and corrosion engineering. Differentiate between them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

**Corrosion Science** is the study of the fundamental mechanisms, thermodynamics, and kinetics governing the degradation of materials due to chemical or electrochemical reactions with their environment. It is concerned with the **"why"** behind corrosion phenomena — for example, why iron rusts in water, what is the driving force ($\Delta G$), and what controls the rate (Tafel kinetics).

**Corrosion Engineering** is the applied discipline that uses the principles of corrosion science to **prevent or control** the destructive effects of corrosion in practical engineering systems. It focuses on the **"how"** — selecting corrosion-resistant alloys, designing proper drainage, applying cathodic protection, using inhibitors, and installing protective coatings.

| Aspect | Corrosion Science | Corrosion Engineering |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Theory and mechanism | Application and mitigation |
| **Question Answered** | Why does corrosion occur? | How do we control it? |
| **Discipline** | Electrochemistry, materials science | Design, materials selection, maintenance |
| **Output** | Equations, models, understanding | Solutions, specifications, systems |

**Awarding Logic**: 1 mark for each correct definition + 1 mark for the differentiation table.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Q: List any six methods of corrosion control.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

The six principal methods of corrosion control (per NACE/AMPP framework) are:

1. **Proper material selection** (e.g., stainless steel, titanium, corrosion-resistant alloys).
2. **Proper engineering design** (avoiding crevices, ensuring drainage, preventing galvanic couples).
3. **Environmental modification** (de-aeration, pH control, reducing chloride concentration).
4. **Use of corrosion inhibitors** (anodic, cathodic, or mixed-type).
5. **Protective coatings** (organic paints, metallic coatings like galvanizing, electroplating).
6. **Cathodic protection** (sacrificial anode method or impressed current method).

**Supplementary methods** (for full credit): anodic protection and routine monitoring/inspection.

**Awarding Logic**: ½ mark for each correct method × 6 = 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern mandates an **internal choice**: Students answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** (both 14 marks), each split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the various forms of corrosion with appropriate diagrams. Explain the mechanisms of uniform, galvanic, pitting, crevice, and intergranular corrosion. **[7 Marks]**

**Q: (b)** A structural steel beam (composition: 0.2% C, balance Fe) is exposed to a marine atmosphere. The corrosion current density measured is $25 \, \mu A/cm^2$. The equivalent weight of iron is 28 g/eq and density is $7.87 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate:
  (i) The corrosion rate in mpy.
  (ii) The corrosion rate in mm/yr.
  (iii) The mass of iron lost per square meter of surface in one year. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a): Forms of Corrosion [7 Marks]

The major forms of corrosion are:

**1. Uniform (General) Corrosion [1 mark]**
- Mechanism: Uniform electrochemical attack across the entire exposed surface.
- Example: Atmospheric rusting of steel, acidic attack on carbon steel tanks.
- Most predictable form; design allowance (corrosion allowance) is straightforward.

**2. Galvanic (Two-Metal) Corrosion [1 mark]**
- Mechanism: Occurs when two dissimilar metals are electrically coupled in an electrolyte. The more anodic metal (lower electrode potential) corrodes preferentially.
- Example: Steel rivets in copper sheets, brass fittings on iron pipes.
- Driven by the difference in standard electrode potentials.

**3. Pitting Corrosion [1.5 marks]**
- Mechanism: Localized attack producing small holes (pits) that penetrate deeply. Occurs due to breakdown of passive films (e.g., Cl⁻ ions attacking stainless steel).
- Example: Stainless steel tanks in chloride environments.
- Especially dangerous because failure occurs with minimal mass loss.

**4. Crevice Corrosion [1.5 marks]**
- Mechanism: Localized attack within crevices, gaskets, lap joints, or under deposits where stagnant electrolyte accumulates. Differential aeration cell forms.
- Example: Bolted joints, tube-tubesheet connections in heat exchangers.

**5. Intergranular Corrosion [1.5 marks]**
- Mechanism: Preferential attack along grain boundaries, often due to chromium carbide precipitation (sensitization) in stainless steels heated to 500–800 °C.
- Example: Weld decay in austenitic stainless steels (e.g., 304).
- Prevention: Use low-carbon grades (304L, 316L) or stabilized grades (321, 347).

**6. Other Forms (Brief mention) [0.5 marks]**
- Erosion-corrosion, stress corrosion cracking (SCC), hydrogen embrittlement, fatigue corrosion.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b): Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given**: $i_{corr} = 25 \, \mu A/cm^2$, $EW = 28$ g/eq, $d = 7.87$ g/cm³.

**Part (i): Corrosion Rate in mpy**

Using the formula:
$$CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{0.129 \times i \times EW}{d}$$

Substituting:
$$CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{0.129 \times 25 \times 28}{7.87}$$

Step-by-step calculation:
- $0.129 \times 25 = 3.225$
- $3.225 \times 28 = 90.30$
- $90.30 \div 7.87 = 11.47 \, mpy$

**Final Answer (i)**: $\boxed{CR = 11.47 \, mpy}$ **[2 Marks]**

**Part (ii): Corrosion Rate in mm/yr**

Using the formula:
$$CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times i \times EW}{d}$$

Substituting:
$$CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 28}{7.87}$$

Step-by-step calculation:
- $3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 0.08175$
- $0.08175 \times 28 = 2.289$
- $2.289 \div 7.87 = 0.2908 \, mm/yr$

**Final Answer (ii)**: $\boxed{CR = 0.291 \, mm/yr}$ **[2 Marks]**

**Part (iii): Mass Loss per m² per Year**

Using Faraday's Law:
$$W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 1**: Convert current density to current per m²:
$$i = 25 \, \mu A/cm^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \, A/cm^2$$

Per m² ($1 m^2 = 10,000 \, cm^2$):
$$I = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 10,000 = 0.25 \, A/m^2$$

**Step 2**: Time in seconds for one year:
$$t = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.1536 \times 10^7 \, s/yr$$

**Step 3**: Apply Faraday's Law (with $M = EW = 28$ g, $n = 1$ for Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, equivalent weight already accounts for n):
$$W = \dfrac{0.25 \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 \times 28}{96,485}$$

**Step 4**: Compute numerator:
$$0.25 \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 = 7.884 \times 10^6$$
$$7.884 \times 10^6 \times 28 = 2.2075 \times 10^8$$

**Step 5**: Divide by $F$:
$$W = \dfrac{2.2075 \times 10^8}{96,485} = 2288 \, g/m^2/yr = 2.288 \, kg/m^2/yr$$

**Final Answer (iii)**: $\boxed{W = 2.288 \, kg/m^2/yr}$ **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, explain the Pourbaix diagram for the iron-water system. Discuss the regions of immunity, passivity, and corrosion. How is this diagram useful in corrosion engineering? **[7 Marks]**

**Q: (b)** The standard electrode potential of copper is $+0.34$ V vs. SHE and that of zinc is $-0.76$ V vs. SHE. When a copper-zinc galvanic couple is formed in seawater (assume standard conditions):
  (i) Identify the anode and cathode.
  (ii) Calculate the cell potential ($E_{cell}$).
  (iii) Calculate the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$) per mole of electrons transferred.
  (iv) Comment on the spontaneity of the reaction. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a): Pourbaix Diagram [7 Marks]

**Introduction [1 mark]**: The Pourbaix diagram (also called the E-pH diagram) is a thermodynamic map that plots electrode potential (E) on the y-axis against pH on the x-axis for a metal-water system at 25 °C. It identifies regions where a metal is thermodynamically stable.

**Three Principal Regions for Iron [3 marks]**:

1. **Immunity Region** (low E, broad pH range): Iron is thermodynamically stable as the metal Fe. Corrosion does not occur. Cathodic protection operates in this region.
2. **Corrosion Region** (high E, low pH and very high pH): Iron dissolves as Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺ (acidic conditions) or as HFeO₂⁻ (alkaline conditions).
3. **Passivity Region** (high E, near-neutral pH): Iron forms stable oxide/hydroxide films (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄, Fe(OH)₃) that act as protective barriers.

**Diagrammatic Representation (Textual Sketch) [2 marks]**:

```
   E (V)
    +1.5 |  P    P     P          P = Passivity
         |  
    +0.5 |  C  P  P   C          C = Corrosion
         |
     0.0 |-----------------------------  (H₂/H⁺ line)
         | I  I    I  I
    -0.5 |  I       I               I = Immunity
         |     I
    -1.0 |________________________________________
              0    4    8    12    14    pH
```

**Engineering Utility [1 mark]**: Pourbaix diagrams guide:
- Selection of cathodic protection potentials.
- Choice of operating pH ranges.
- Identification of conditions for spontaneous passivation (e.g., stainless steel, aluminum).

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b): Galvanic Cell Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given**: $E^{\circ}_{Cu} = +0.34$ V, $E^{\circ}_{Zn} = -0.76$ V.

**Part (i): Identify Anode and Cathode** **[1 Mark]**

- Anode (oxidation): **Zinc** (more negative potential → greater tendency to oxidize).
- Cathode (reduction): **Copper** (more positive potential → greater tendency to reduce).

**Part (ii): Cell Potential** **[2 Marks]**

$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}$$
$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{Cu} - E^{\circ}_{Zn}$$
$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = 0.34 - (-0.76)$$
$$E^{\circ}_{cell} = 0.34 + 0.76 = \boxed{1.10 \, V}$$

**Part (iii): Gibbs Free Energy Change** **[2.5 Marks]**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{cell}$$

For the overall reaction $\text{Zn} + \text{Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + \text{Cu}$, $n = 2$.

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -2 \times 96,485 \times 1.10$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -212,267 \, J/mol$$
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \boxed{-212.27 \, kJ/mol}$$

**Part (iv): Spontaneity** **[1.5 Marks]**

Since $\Delta G^{\circ} = -212.27$ kJ/mol is **highly negative**, the galvanic reaction is **strongly spontaneous** under standard conditions. This explains why zinc corrodes sacrificially when coupled to copper in marine environments — the basis for the **sacrificial anode cathodic protection method**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on This Topic**:
> 
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Cell Potential**: Students often compute $E_{cell}$ as $E_{anode} - E_{cathode}$ (incorrect). The correct convention is **$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$**. Wrong sign = 2 marks lost.
> 
> 2. **Unit Errors in Faraday's Law**: Forgetting to convert current density from $\mu A/cm^2$ to $A/m^2$ before applying Faraday's Law leads to mass loss values off by factors of $10^4$. Always check units first.
> 
> 3. **Confusing "Corrosion" with "Passivation" in Pourbaix Diagrams**: Students incorrectly label passivity regions as corrosion. Remember: passivity = protective film, corrosion = active dissolution.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the Tafel Slope Justification**: When asked to "derive the Tafel equation," many students skip the Butler-Volmer origin and jump straight to the log-linear form. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for missing the starting equation.
> 
> 5. **Equating "Corrosion Science" and "Corrosion Engineering"**: A 3-mark KTU question explicitly tests the difference. Writing them as synonyms loses the differentiation mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist** (last-minute exam prep):

- [ ] **Corrosion Science** = study of mechanism, thermodynamics, kinetics. **Corrosion Engineering** = application of control methods.
- [ ] **Three Pillars of Corrosion Science**: Thermodynamics ($\Delta G$, $E$), Kinetics (Tafel, $i_{corr}$), Environment-Material interaction.
- [ ] **Nernst Equation** (must memorize): $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ at 25 °C.
- [ ] **Faraday's Constant**: $F = 96,485$ C/mol.
- [ ] **CR Formulas**: 0.129 constant (mpy) and $3.27 \times 10^{-3}$ constant (mm/yr).
- [ ] **Pourbaix Diagram Zones**: Immunity (lower E), Passivation (mid E, mid pH), Corrosion (high E or extreme pH).
- [ ] **Eight Methods of Corrosion Control**: Material selection, Design, Environment modification, Inhibitors, Coatings, Cathodic protection, Anodic protection, Monitoring.
- [ ] **Cathode has higher $E^{\circ}$**, **Anode has lower $E^{\circ}$** in a galvanic couple.
- [ ] **$\Delta G < 0$** ⇒ reaction is spontaneous (corrosion occurs).
- [ ] **$E_{cell} > 0$** ⇒ spontaneous electrochemical cell.
- [ ] Standard temperature reference is **25 °C (298.15 K)**.
- [ ] **Units to remember**: mpy, mm/yr, $\mu A/cm^2$, $g/cm^3$, V vs. SHE.
- [ ] **Corrosion cost** is approximately 3–4% of global GDP (high-yield answer point).
- [ ] **Tafel equation**: $\eta = a + b \log i$; higher $b$ ⇒ slower kinetics.
- [ ] **Polarization Resistance (LPR)**: Inverse relation with $i_{corr}$ — used in field instruments.

---

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes: Corrosion Science and Corrosion Engineering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Aspects of Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional Aspects of Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Functional Aspects of Corrosion** refer to the practical engineering consequences and design considerations that arise when metallic (and sometimes non-metallic) materials interact with their service environment. Unlike the *scientific aspects* (thermodynamics, kinetics, mechanisms) which explain *why* corrosion occurs, the functional aspects address *how corrosion impacts* the **serviceability**, **safety**, **economics**, **reliability**, and **aesthetic value** of engineering components and structures during their operational lifetime.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic bridges the gap between theoretical electrochemistry and practical plant/structural design decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Functional aspects encompass — (i) Economic considerations, (ii) Safety and health hazards, (iii) Environmental concerns, (iv) Performance degradation, (v) Design allowances, and (vi) Material selection philosophy. Each is a high-yield area for ESE questions.

## 1.2 The Six Pillars of Functional Corrosion

| Pillar | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|
| **Economic** | Direct loss (replacement) + Indirect loss (downtime, contamination) |
| **Safety** | Catastrophic failure of pressure vessels, bridges, pipelines |
| **Environmental** | Leakage of toxic/hazardous chemicals into soil/water |
| **Performance** | Loss of efficiency in heat exchangers, turbines, pumps |
| **Aesthetic** | Tarnishing, staining of architectural components |
| **Design** | Corrosion allowance, selection of materials, protective schemes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Silent Tax"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of corrosion as a *silent tax* levied on every metal structure in service. Imagine owning a brand-new car. You don't see the steel undercarriage being eaten away by road salt every monsoon, but over 10 years the chassis thins, weld joints weaken, and exhaust hangers snap. The car still "runs," but its **functional life, resale value, safety margin, and aesthetics** have all degraded. That silent, invisible degradation is exactly what the *functional aspects of corrosion* quantify and manage. Engineers don't just ask *"Will it corrode?"* — they ask *"How fast, what is the cost, who is at risk, and what should we do at the drawing-board stage?"*

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Density of Iron (Fe): 7.87 g/cm³** — used universally in corrosion rate calculations for steels.
- **Density of Copper (Cu): 8.96 g/cm³**
- **Density of Aluminium (Al): 2.70 g/cm³**
- **Faraday's Constant (F): 96,485 C/mol (≈ 96,500 C/mol)** — used in electrochemical mass-loss calculations.
- **Design Life of Industrial Plants: 20–30 years** (typical assumption in chemical/process industries).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Economic Aspects of Corrosion

Corrosion cost is bifurcated into two well-defined categories in industrial economics:

### 2.1.1 Direct Costs
- Replacement of corroded equipment (pipes, tanks, roofing, structural members).
- Application of protective coatings (paint, galvanizing, plating).
- Cathodic protection systems (sacrificial anodes, impressed current).
- Use of corrosion-resistant alloys (stainless steel, Inconel, Monel) instead of carbon steel.

### 2.1.2 Indirect Costs (often 3–5 times the direct cost!)
- **Plant shutdown** losses (e.g., refinery shutdown can cost ₹50 lakh–₹1 crore per day).
- **Loss of product** from leaking tanks/pipelines.
- **Contamination** of product (e.g., rust in food, pharma, or dye chemicals).
- **Loss of efficiency** in heat exchangers due to fouling + corrosion scale.
- **Over-design** costs — using thicker sections than structurally required.
- **Insurance premiums** for failure-prone installations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classic Data Point:** A widely cited NACE/Battelle study estimates the annual global cost of corrosion at **~3–4 % of GDP**. For India, this translates to several lakh crore rupees per year — making corrosion a boardroom-level concern, not just a metallurgy curiosity.

## 2.2 Safety & Health Aspects

Corrosion failures can be **catastrophic and irreversible**:

- **Pressure vessels & boilers:** Localized pitting or wall thinning can lead to explosions.
- **Aircraft:** Stress-corrosion cracking (SCC) of aluminium alloys (Al 7075) led to early Aloha Airlines Flight 243 incident (1988).
- **Bridges:** Failure of suspender ropes (e.g., Morbi bridge-type incidents) due to atmospheric + stress corrosion.
- **Pipelines:** Rupture of gas pipelines causing fires (e.g., GAIL pipeline explosions).
- **Toxic release:** Corrosion-induced leaks in ammonia, chlorine, or hydrocarbon service.

## 2.3 Environmental Aspects

- **Soil contamination** from leaking underground petroleum storage tanks (USTs).
- **Water pollution** from corroded condenser tubes in power plants.
- **Atmospheric emissions** of toxic metal oxides during high-temperature oxidation.
- **Spent acid disposal** from pickling/descaling operations.

## 2.4 Performance Degradation

Corrosion products (rust, scales) act as **insulating layers**, drastically reducing:
- Heat-transfer coefficients in heat exchangers (up to **40–60 %** drop).
- Flow area in pipelines (fouling factor increases pumping cost).
- Electrical conductivity in copper conductors (tarnish layer).

## 2.5 Design Considerations — Corrosion Allowance

A **Corrosion Allowance (CA)** is the *extra wall thickness* deliberately provided at the design stage to compensate for material loss due to uniform corrosion over the design life.

$$\text{Corrosion Allowance} = \text{Corrosion Rate (mm/yr)} \times \text{Design Life (years)}$$

The actual design thickness then becomes:

$$t_{\text{design}} = t_{\text{mechanical}} + t_{\text{corrosion}} + t_{\text{mill tolerance}}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \dfrac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Corrosion rate in mils per year | mpy |
| 2 | $CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \dfrac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$ | Corrosion rate in mm per year | mm/yr |
| 3 | $CR \;(\text{gmd}) = \dfrac{W}{A \times T}$ | Mass-loss rate | g/(m²·day) |
| 4 | $W = \dfrac{I \times t \times M}{n \times F}$ | Faraday's law — mass of metal dissolved | grams |
| 5 | $CA = CR \times L$ | Corrosion allowance (extra thickness) | mm |
| 6 | $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density from measured current | A/cm² |
| 7 | $r = \dfrac{i \times M}{n \times F \times D} \times 10^{4}$ | Penetration rate from current density | mm/yr |

**Where:**
- $W$ = mass loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = exposed area (in² for mpy, cm² for mm/yr), $T$ = exposure time (hours)
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol), $n$ = electrons transferred, $F$ = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
- $L$ = design life (years)

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

The functional aspects framework is **directly applied** by:
- **Pressure vessel designers** (ASME Section VIII mandates corrosion allowance).
- **Pipeline engineers** (API 570 inspection codes).
- **Marine architects** (choosing cupronickel vs. aluminium bronze for seawater service).
- **Refinery process engineers** (selecting carbon steel with inhibitors vs. stainless steel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Corrosion Rate (mpy) Formula

### Step 1 — Start from Faraday's Law
The mass of metal dissolved by anodic dissolution is:

$$W_{\text{grams}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

If we want mass in **milligrams**:

$$W_{\text{mg}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M \times 1000}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Mass Loss per Unit Area per Unit Time
Divide by Area ($A$) and Time ($T$):

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{W}{A \cdot T} \;\; \left[\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{cm}^{2}\cdot\text{hr}}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Express as Penetration Depth (mils per year)
Penetration depth $p$ is related to mass loss through density:

$$p = \frac{W}{D \cdot A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{mpy} = \frac{p \;\; (\text{mils})}{\text{time} \;\;(\text{yr})}$$

Combining all conversions (cm → inches, hours → year, g → mils using 1 mil = 0.001 inch = 0.00254 cm), the empirical constant becomes **534** for mpy and **87.6** for mm/yr.

$$\boxed{\;CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **534** emerges from the conversion chain: 
> $$\frac{1000 \,\text{mg/g} \times 24 \,\text{hr/day} \times 365 \,\text{days/yr}}{2.54 \,\text{cm/in} \times 1000 \,\text{mils/in} \times \rho_{\text{factor}}} \approx 534$$
> 
> The **87.6** constant similarly yields mm/yr when area is in cm².

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

### Problem Statement
A mild steel coupon of dimensions 5 cm × 2 cm × 0.5 cm was exposed to seawater for 30 days. After cleaning, the weight loss was found to be **285 mg**. Density of steel = **7.87 g/cm³**. Calculate:
1. Corrosion rate in **mpy**.
2. Corrosion rate in **mm/yr**.
3. Corrosion allowance for a **15-year design life** of a structural column.
4. State whether the steel is suitable for the application (use NACE classification).

### Step 1 — Identify Data
- Area exposed (assuming both sides of 5 × 2 face): $A = 2 \times (5 \times 2) = 20$ cm² (using major faces for simplicity). For two-sided exposure: $A = 20$ cm².
- Time: $T = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hours.
- $W = 285$ mg, $D = 7.87$ g/cm³.

### Step 2 — Corrosion Rate in mpy

$$CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

Substituting:

$$CR = \frac{534 \times 285}{7.87 \times 20 \times 720}$$

$$CR = \frac{152{,}190}{113{,}328}$$

$$\boxed{\;CR \approx 1.34 \;\; \text{mpy}\;}$$

### Step 3 — Corrosion Rate in mm/yr

$$CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

$$CR = \frac{87.6 \times 285}{7.87 \times 20 \times 720}$$

$$CR = \frac{24{,}966}{113{,}328}$$

$$\boxed{\;CR \approx 0.22 \;\; \text{mm/yr}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Corrosion Allowance for 15 Years

$$CA = CR \times L = 0.22 \times 15$$

$$\boxed{\;CA = 3.30 \;\; \text{mm}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret via NACE Classification

| Corrosion Rate (mpy) | Class | Remark |
|---|---|---|
| < 1 | Excellent | Suitable for critical service |
| 1 – 5 | Good | Generally acceptable |
| 5 – 10 | Fair | Use with caution |
| 10 – 50 | Poor | Requires protection |
| > 50 | Severe | Not suitable |

Our value of **1.34 mpy → "Good" category** — steel is acceptable but protective coating is recommended for 15-year service life with 3.30 mm extra thickness.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choice of Functional Response

| Observation | Probable Corrosion Type | Functional Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform metal loss | General corrosion | Corrosion allowance, coatings |
| Deep isolated pits | Pitting corrosion | Cathodic protection, inhibitors |
| Cracking under tensile load | SCC | Stress relief, change environment |
| Wear + corrosion | Erosion-corrosion | Increase velocity, harder alloy |
| Grain boundary attack | Intergranular | Stabilized grades (321 SS) |
| Cracks at weldments | Knife-line attack | Use low-carbon grades (304L) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Aspects — Hierarchy of Decision-Making

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the interrelationships between the various functional considerations that govern a typical industrial design choice:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Environment Identified] --> B[Predict Corrosion Mechanism]
    B --> C{Severity Assessment}
    C -->|Low < 1 mpy| D[Use Carbon Steel]
    C -->|Moderate 1 to 10 mpy| E[Carbon Steel plus Protection]
    C -->|High > 10 mpy| F[Use CRA or Alloy]
    E --> E1[Apply Coating System]
    E --> E2[Add Inhibitor Dosing]
    E --> E3[Install Cathodic Protection]
    E --> E4[Add Corrosion Allowance]
    D --> G[Economic Evaluation]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Safety Check}
    H -->|Pass| I[Final Design Approved]
    H -->|Fail| J[Re-engineer or Upgrade]
    J --> B
    I --> K[Document for Inspection Plan]
    G --> L[Environmental Compliance]
    L --> I
```

## 4.2 Corrosion Rate Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Exposure Test Setup] --> B[Measure Weight Loss W mg]
    B --> C[Note Area A cm squared]
    B --> D[Note Time T hr]
    C --> E[Apply Formula]
    D --> E
    E --> F[CR in mpy]
    E --> G[CR in mm per yr]
    F --> H[Classify per NACE]
    G --> I[Calculate Allowance]
    I --> J[Add to Design Thickness]
    H --> K{Decision}
    K -->|Acceptable| L[Approve Material]
    K -->|Not Acceptable| M[Select Alternative Material]
```

## 4.3 Interconnection of Functional Aspects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Economic Cost] --> P5[Material Selection]
    P2[Safety Risk] --> P5
    P3[Environmental Impact] --> P5
    P4[Performance Loss] --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Corrosion Allowance Provision]
    P5 --> P7[Protective Strategy Choice]
    P5 --> P8[Inspection and Maintenance Plan]
    P6 --> Q[Final Engineering Decision]
    P7 --> Q
    P8 --> Q
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a *decision node*. The arrows indicate information flow, not physical movement. In an actual KTU board answer, a clear hand-drawn version of this hierarchy (with labelled boxes) carries **2–3 bonus marks** for structured presentation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define corrosion allowance. Why is it provided in the design of chemical process equipment? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
Corrosion allowance is the *extra thickness* of metal deliberately incorporated into the wall of a process vessel, pipeline, or structural member to compensate for the metal expected to be lost due to uniform (general) corrosion over the equipment's design life.

**Mathematically:** 
$$CA = CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) \times \text{Design Life} \;(\text{yr})$$

**Why it is provided:**
1. To ensure the vessel/pipe has adequate *mechanical strength* even after the expected metal loss.
2. To provide a safety margin against *unexpected corrosion rates*.
3. To extend the *inspection interval* — the equipment remains safe till the next scheduled shutdown.
4. It is a *cost-effective* alternative to using a more expensive corrosion-resistant alloy (CRA) for moderately corrosive services.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six functional aspects of corrosion. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The six functional aspects are:
1. **Economic aspects** — direct (replacement, coatings) and indirect (downtime, contamination) losses.
2. **Safety aspects** — risk of catastrophic failure (boiler explosion, bridge collapse).
3. **Environmental aspects** — soil, water, and air pollution from leaking systems.
4. **Performance aspects** — efficiency loss in heat exchangers, pumps, turbines.
5. **Aesthetic aspects** — tarnishing, staining of architectural and decorative components.
6. **Design aspects** — material selection, corrosion allowance, protective schemes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ONE)

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Discuss the economic aspects of corrosion with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Economic aspects of corrosion are classified into **direct costs** and **indirect costs**.

**A. Direct Costs** (visible, easily quantified):
- **Replacement of corroded parts:** Heat exchanger tubes, boiler tubes, roof sheeting in chemical plants must be replaced periodically. Example: Replacement of overhead tank roofs every 5–7 years in coastal Kerala due to atmospheric corrosion.
- **Protective coatings:** Painting, galvanizing, electroplating, powder coating — recurring expenses for industrial plants.
- **Corrosion-resistant alloys (CRAs):** Using SS-304, SS-316, Monel, Inconel instead of carbon steel raises initial cost by 3–10 times.
- **Cathodic protection:** Impressed-current CP systems cost ₹2–5 lakh per km of pipeline; sacrificial anodes need periodic replacement.

**B. Indirect Costs** (hidden, often 3–5× direct cost):
- **Plant shutdown:** A refinery shut down due to a corroded overhead condenser can lose ₹50 lakh–₹1 crore per day in lost production.
- **Loss of product:** Leaking petroleum storage tanks lose valuable crude/diesel; contaminated product must be re-processed.
- **Contamination:** Rust particles in pharmaceutical, food, or dye products make the entire batch unusable (e.g., a 10,000-litre batch of dye rejected due to iron contamination).
- **Loss of efficiency:** Rust scale on condenser tubes drops heat-transfer coefficient by 40–60 %, increasing fuel consumption.
- **Over-design:** Adding 6 mm corrosion allowance instead of using a 4 mm extra plate multiplies material cost across thousands of pipes.
- **Insurance and legal costs:** Failure-related lawsuits and higher premiums.

**Conclusion:** Corrosion costs the global economy **3–4 % of GDP annually**. A holistic corrosion management programme (material selection + design + protection + monitoring) typically yields 15:1 to 30:1 return on investment.

---

**(b) Explain the safety and environmental aspects of corrosion. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**A. Safety Aspects — Catastrophic Failures:**

Corrosion is the *root cause* of many industrial and structural disasters:

| Industry | Failure Mode | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure vessels / boilers | Wall thinning, pitting | Explosion, loss of life |
| Aircraft | SCC in Al 7075 fuselage | In-flight failure (Aloha 1988) |
| Bridges | Corrosion of suspender cables | Collapse |
| LPG / gas pipelines | External + internal corrosion | Fire, blast |
| Nuclear plants | SCC in steam generator tubes | Radioactive release |

**Safety Codes:** ASME, API, IS 2825 mandate periodic inspection, corrosion allowance, and NDT (ultrasonic thickness gauging, radiography) specifically to detect corrosion-driven wall thinning before catastrophic failure.

**Human Loss vs. Cost:** A single refinery explosion can cause 10–50 fatalities — making corrosion fundamentally a *public safety* issue, not merely an engineering economics problem.

**B. Environmental Aspects:**

- **Soil contamination:** Underground petroleum tanks corroding release hydrocarbons into groundwater — a major concern at old fuel stations in India.
- **Marine pollution:** Corroded ballast tanks of ships leak oil into oceans.
- **Air emissions:** High-temperature oxidation of alloys in furnaces releases metal oxides.
- **Toxic releases:** Ammonia, chlorine, and acid leaks from corroded storage tanks can poison agricultural land and waterways.
- **Pickling waste:** Spent acid from descaling operations (FeSO₄, FeCl₃) requires careful disposal.

**Regulatory Compliance:** Indian factories must comply with the **Factories Act, EPA 1986**, and **PESO** (for petroleum), all of which require documented corrosion management.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for corrosion rate in mpy and mm/year from Faraday's law. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis:**
The mass of metal $W$ dissolved at an anode by a current $I$ for time $t$ is:

$$W_{\text{grams}} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s), $M$ = atomic mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Step 2 — Express mass in mg and time in hours:**
With $W$ in mg and $t$ in hours, $W$ (mg) = $\dfrac{I \times t \times M \times 1000}{n \times F}$ — but for uniform corrosion rate from weight-loss method, we use empirical constants derived as follows.

**Step 3 — Penetration Depth:**
Penetration $p$ is related to mass loss through density:

$$p = \frac{W}{D \cdot A} \;\; \text{(cm)}$$

where $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = exposed area.

**Step 4 — Convert to mils per year (mpy):**
1 cm = 0.3937 inch = 393.7 mils (since 1 inch = 1000 mils and 1 inch = 2.54 cm, so 1 cm = 1000/2.54 = 393.7 mils).
1 year = 365 × 24 = 8760 hours.

Therefore:

$$CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \frac{W \;(\text{mg}) \times 393.7 \;\text{mils/cm} \times 8760 \;\text{hr/yr}}{D \;(\text{g/cm}^3) \times A \;(\text{cm}^2) \times T \;(\text{hr}) \times 1000 \;(\text{mg/g})}$$

$$\boxed{\;CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mm per year:**
1 cm = 10 mm, so mils-to-mm conversion gives:

$$CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{W \times 10 \times 8760}{D \times A \times T \times 1000} = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}$$

$$\boxed{\;CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T}\;}$$

**Verification of Constants:**
- $\dfrac{393.7 \times 8760}{1000} = 3449$, but dividing by additional unit-conversion factors yields the standard constants **534** and **87.6**.
- Empirical constants incorporate $A$ in in² (for mpy) or cm² (for mm/yr).

---

**(b) A mild steel plate of exposed area 50 cm² was immersed in 3 % NaCl solution for 50 hours. Weight loss was 220 mg. Density of steel = 7.87 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) corrosion rate in mpy, (ii) corrosion rate in mm/yr, and (iii) the required corrosion allowance for 20-year design life. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $W = 220$ mg, $A = 50$ cm², $T = 50$ hr, $D = 7.87$ g/cm³, $L = 20$ yr.

**Step 1 — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$CR \;(\text{mpy}) = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times T} = \frac{534 \times 220}{7.87 \times 50 \times 50}$$

$$CR = \frac{117{,}480}{19{,}675} = \boxed{5.97 \;\; \text{mpy}}$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr:**

$$CR \;(\text{mm/yr}) = \frac{87.6 \times W}{D \times A \times T} = \frac{87.6 \times 220}{7.87 \times 50 \times 50}$$

$$CR = \frac{19{,}272}{19{,}675} = \boxed{0.98 \;\; \text{mm/yr}}$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion allowance for 20-year life:**

$$CA = CR \times L = 0.98 \times 20 = \boxed{19.6 \;\; \text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark bonus):**
- 5.97 mpy falls in the **"Fair"** NACE category (5–10 mpy) → mild steel in 3 % NaCl requires protection.
- 19.6 mm is a *substantial* allowance — economically, it is wiser to substitute a corrosion-resistant material (e.g., SS-316L) than to provide such a thick allowance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Most Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing up units of area** (cm² vs. in²) — the constants 534 and 87.6 demand specific units. Using the wrong constant with wrong area units is a *full-mark killer*.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert time from days to hours** when the exposure is given in days — this leads to an error factor of 24.
> 3. **Confusing corrosion rate (mm/yr) with corrosion allowance (mm)** — they are not the same; allowance is rate × life.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per numerical answer without proper units.
> 5. **Skipping the design-life recommendation / NACE classification** — these interpretative comments fetch 1–2 marks and are almost always expected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- 📌 **Corrosion allowance** = CR × Design Life. Always quote units (mm).
- 📌 **Two key constants:** **534** (for mpy, area in **in²**) and **87.6** (for mm/yr, area in **cm²**).
- 📌 **Faraday's constant F = 96,485 C/mol** — use this in electrochemical CR derivations.
- 📌 **NACE Classification:** < 1 mpy Excellent; 1–5 Good; 5–10 Fair; 10–50 Poor; > 50 Severe.
- 📌 **Indirect corrosion cost** is often 3–5 times the direct cost — remember this for "economic aspects" theory questions.
- 📌 **Safety failures** caused by corrosion: boiler explosions, aircraft SCC (Aloha 1988), bridge collapses, pipeline blasts.
- 📌 **Corrosion allowance** is added to mechanical design thickness, along with mill tolerance.
- 📌 Always convert time to **hours** before applying the mpy/mm/yr formulas.
- 📌 The **3–4 % GDP** figure for global corrosion cost is a board-favourite stat.
- 📌 Functional aspects = **practical engineering impact**, NOT the electrochemical mechanism (that belongs to scientific aspects).
- 📌 State **units** in every numerical answer — losing 0.5 mark per slip is painful.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts in Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts in Corrosion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Corrosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion** is the **destructive and unintentional attack of a metal** by chemical or electrochemical reaction with its environment, leading to the deterioration of the metal's properties (mechanical, electrical, aesthetic) and its conversion into a more thermodynamically stable compound such as an oxide, hydroxide, sulfide, or carbonate.

According to **Fontana's classical definition (1986)**, corrosion is best understood as the **reverse process of extractive metallurgy**. While metallurgy extracts pure metals from ores using energy, corrosion returns the metal back to its original ore-like state, releasing the same amount of energy that was originally consumed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Corrosion is an **electrochemical phenomenon** in the presence of an electrolyte (moisture/aqueous solution) and a **chemical phenomenon** (oxidation/direct chemical attack) in dry/gaseous environments. Both mechanisms involve the **transfer of electrons** from the metal (anode) to a reducible species (cathode).

### 1.2 The Three Essentials of Corrosion

For any corrosion process to occur, **three fundamental components** must be present simultaneously:

| Component | Role | Common Example |
|-----------|------|----------------|
| **Anode** (Metal) | Site of **oxidation**; loses electrons | Iron surface (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) |
| **Cathode** | Site of **reduction**; gains electrons | Adjacent copper, rust, or impurity |
| **Electrolyte** | Ionic conduction medium for charge transport | Water, salt spray, humid air |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Rusting Bicycle Analogy:** Imagine you left your old steel bicycle out in the rain for a year. The shiny paint fades, ugly reddish-brown patches appear, and eventually holes form in the frame. The iron in the bicycle is essentially "trying to go back home" — back to the iron oxide (ore) state from which it was originally extracted. The rain acts as the **electrolyte** (a thin invisible conductive film), oxygen from the air plays the role of the **cathodic reactant**, and different regions of the steel surface (with slight compositional or stress differences) become tiny **anodes and cathodes**. Tiny galvanic cells form, and the metal gradually dissolves. This is corrosion in its simplest, most relatable form.

### 1.4 Why Should Engineers Care?

> [!WARNING]
> The **annual global cost of corrosion** is estimated to be **US $2.5 trillion**, which is roughly **3.4% of the world's GDP** (NACE International IMPACT Study). For a KTU engineer, this is not merely an academic chapter — it is a multi-billion dollar industrial problem affecting petroleum pipelines, ship hulls, chemical reactors, aircraft, reinforced concrete, and biomedical implants.

### 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Faraday's Constant (F):** $\mathbf{F = 96{,}485 \; C \; mol^{-1} \approx 96{,}500 \; C \; mol^{-1}}$
- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $\mathbf{R = 8.314 \; J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}}$
- **Standard Temperature:** $\mathbf{T = 298.15 \; K \; (25°C)}$
- **Standard Pressure:** $\mathbf{P° = 1 \; atm \; (101.325 \; kPa)}$
- **Corrosion Rate Unit (mils per year, mpy):** $1 \; mpy = 0.0254 \; mm/year$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electrochemical Nature of Corrosion

Corrosion in aqueous environments is fundamentally an **electrochemical process** comprising two simultaneous half-reactions occurring on the metal surface:

**At the Anode (Oxidation):**
$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^{-}$$

**At the Cathode (Reduction):** Depending on the environment, the most common cathodic reactions are:

| Cathodic Reaction | Equation | Environment |
|-------------------|----------|-------------|
| **Hydrogen Evolution** (Acidic) | $2H^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2$ | Low pH (acidic) |
| **Oxygen Reduction** (Neutral/Aerated) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$ | Neutral/alkaline, aerated |
| **Oxygen Reduction** (Acidic) | $O_2 + 4H^{+} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | Acidic, aerated |
| **Metal Ion Reduction** | $M^{n+} + ne^{-} \rightarrow M$ | Concentrated solutions |
| **Metal Deposition** | $M^{m+} + me^{-} \rightarrow M$ | Noble metal plating |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In the corrosion of iron in neutral aerated water (most common case), the **oxygen reduction reaction (ORR)** is the dominant cathodic process. The anodic product Fe²⁺ and cathodic product OH⁻ combine to form the characteristic rust (Fe(OH)₂/Fe₂O₃·H₂O).

### 2.2 Thermodynamic Driving Force

The spontaneity of corrosion is governed by the **Gibbs Free Energy change ($\Delta G$)**:

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

Where:
- $\Delta G$ = Change in Gibbs free energy (J/mol)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred
- $F$ = Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol)
- $E_{cell}$ = Cell potential (V)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The sign convention rule:** If $\Delta G < 0$ (negative), the corrosion reaction is **spontaneous**. Equivalently, if $E_{cell} > 0$ (positive), the reaction proceeds spontaneously in the forward direction.

### 2.3 The Nernst Equation

The **Nernst Equation** allows the calculation of the electrode potential under non-standard conditions:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{[Reduced]}{[Oxidized]}$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm (at 25°C, using 2.303 RT/F = 0.0592 V):

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[Reduced]}{[Oxidized]}$$

### 2.4 Faraday's Laws and Corrosion Rate Calculation

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated/deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

**Faraday's Second Law:** The mass of substance liberated is proportional to its equivalent weight.

**Corrosion Current Density → Corrosion Rate Conversion:**

$$CR \; (mpy) = \frac{0.129 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$

Where:
- $i_{corr}$ = Corrosion current density ($\mu A/cm^2$)
- $EW$ = Equivalent weight of metal (g/eq)
- $\rho$ = Density of metal (g/cm³)
- 0.129 = Unit conversion constant

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | Gibbs free energy-cell EMF relation | J/mol |
| 2 | $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log \frac{[Red]}{[Ox]}$ | Nernst Equation (25°C) | Volts |
| 3 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell EMF (reduction potentials) | Volts |
| 4 | $W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved (Faraday's Law) | grams |
| 5 | $CR = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mpy | mils/year |
| 6 | $CR = \frac{534 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho \cdot A}$ | Corrosion rate (general form) | mm/year |
| 7 | $E_{eq} = E^{\circ} + \frac{0.0592}{n} \log [M^{n+}]$ | Equilibrium potential of metal | Volts |
| 8 | $V_{corr} = \frac{i_{corr}}{nF} \cdot \frac{M}{\rho}$ | Volumetric corrosion rate | cm/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The corrosion rate formula is the **backbone of every cathodic protection and material selection design**. Industries use Tafel extrapolation of polarization curves to find $i_{corr}$, then convert it to mpy or mm/yr to predict the service life of pipelines, reactor vessels, and offshore structures.

### 2.6 The Mixed Potential Theory (Evans Diagram Concept)

When two dissimilar electrochemical reactions occur simultaneously on the same metal surface, the system settles at a **mixed (corrosion) potential** $E_{corr}$, where the anodic current equals the cathodic current. This is the foundation of:

- **Tafel Extrapolation Method** (used to find $i_{corr}$)
- **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** technique
- **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** analysis

The Stern-Geary equation (used in LPR):

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

Where $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ are the anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes, and $R_p$ is the polarization resistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gibbs Free Energy and Cell EMF

**Starting Point:** For a reversible electrochemical cell, the maximum non-expansion work obtainable equals the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$

**Step 1 — Relate charge transferred to moles of electrons:**

When $n$ moles of electrons flow per mole of reaction, the total charge $Q$ for 1 mole of reaction is:

$$Q = nF$$

where $F$ is Faraday's constant (96,485 C/mol).

**Step 2 — Electrical work done by the cell:**

Electrical work is the product of charge transported and the potential difference driving it:

$$W_{elec} = Q \cdot E_{cell} = nF \cdot E_{cell}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for maximum work:**

$$-\Delta G = nF \cdot E_{cell}$$

**Step 4 — Final Standard Result:**

$$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$

**Interpretation:** A **positive** $E_{cell}$ yields a **negative** $\Delta G$, indicating a **spontaneous** corrosion/galvanic reaction. This is the fundamental thermodynamic link that makes corrosion predictable from electrochemical measurements.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Nernst Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a generic reversible half-cell reaction:

$$aA + ne^{-} \rightleftharpoons bB$$

The Gibbs free energy change at any condition is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q = \frac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}$ is the reaction quotient.

**Step 1 — Substitute the cell potential relation** $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by $-nF$:**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Convert natural logarithm to base-10** using $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

**Step 4 — At standard temperature** (T = 298.15 K), evaluate the constant:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485} = 0.05916 \approx 0.0592 \; V$$

**Step 5 — Final Nernst Equation (25°C):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$$

**Interpretation:** Each 10-fold change in the ion concentration changes the electrode potential by $\frac{0.0592}{n}$ volts at 25°C.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law

**Problem:** A steel structure shows a corrosion current density of $i_{corr} = 50 \; \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate the corrosion rate in mpy. (Given: Fe atomic weight = 55.85 g/mol, n = 2, $\rho$ = 7.87 g/cm³)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing formula:**

$$CR = \frac{0.129 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Equivalent Weight of iron:**

For Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, valency change = 2:

$$EW = \frac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{\text{Valency}} = \frac{55.85}{2} = 27.925 \; g/eq$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$CR = \frac{0.129 \times 50 \times 27.925}{7.87}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication in the numerator:**

$$0.129 \times 50 = 6.45$$

$$6.45 \times 27.925 = 180.22$$

**Step 5 — Divide by density:**

$$CR = \frac{180.22}{7.87} = 22.90 \; mpy$$

**Final Answer:**

$$CR \approx 22.9 \; mpy \; \text{or} \; \approx 0.58 \; mm/year$$

**Interpretation:** This is considered a **moderate corrosion rate** in industrial service. A pipeline with this rate would lose roughly 5.8 mm wall thickness in 10 years — a critical figure for fitness-for-service assessment.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Standard EMF Series vs Galvanic Series

| Feature | Standard EMF Series | Galvanic Series |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Basis** | Pure metals in their standard state (1M ion, 25°C) | Actual metals/alloys in a specific environment (typically seawater) |
| **Measurement** | Thermodynamic (reversible equilibrium) | Empirical (practical observation) |
| **Scope** | Pure elements only | Includes commercial alloys |
| **Precision** | Exact numerical values | Lists in order of nobility |
| **Use in design** | Predicts reaction direction | Predicts practical corrosion behavior |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The EMF series tells you **whether** corrosion will occur (thermodynamics), but the Galvanic Series tells you **how fast** it will occur (kinetics in real environments). The Galvanic Series is therefore the **practitioner's tool**.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical: Tafel Extrapolation to Find Corrosion Rate

**Given:** A Tafel plot gives anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a = 0.12 \; V/decade$ and cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c = 0.15 \; V/decade$. The polarization resistance $R_p$ is measured as $1000 \; \Omega \cdot cm^2$. Calculate $i_{corr}$.

**Step 1 — Recall the Stern-Geary Equation:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator:**

$$\beta_a \cdot \beta_c = 0.12 \times 0.15 = 0.018 \; V^2/decade^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator factor $(\beta_a + \beta_c)$:**

$$0.12 + 0.15 = 0.27 \; V/decade$$

**Step 4 — Full denominator:**

$$2.303 \times 1000 \times 0.27 = 621.81$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.018}{621.81} = 2.895 \times 10^{-5} \; A/cm^2$$

**Step 6 — Convert to $\mu A/cm^2$:**

$$i_{corr} = 28.95 \; \mu A/cm^2$$

**Interpretation:** A low $i_{corr}$ value (< 50 $\mu A/cm^2$) generally indicates **good corrosion resistance** for many engineering alloys.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Fundamental Corrosion Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ANODE: Metal Surface] -- Oxidation: M to Mn+ plus ne --> B[Electrolyte: Aqueous Film]
    B -- Ionic conduction: Mn+ migrates --> C[CATHODE: Adjacent Surface]
    C -- Reduction: O2 plus H2O plus 4e to 4OH --> A
    D[External Metallic Path: Electron Flow] -- e flow from anode to cathode --> C
    A -- Metal dissolution --> E[Corrosion Product: Rust Scale or Deposit]
```

**Block Description:** The diagram shows the closed loop of a galvanic corrosion cell. The anode (left) loses electrons through oxidation, which travel through the metallic path (bottom) to the cathode (right), where they participate in the oxygen reduction reaction. Simultaneously, the metallic ions migrate through the electrolyte (top) to maintain electroneutrality. The combined anodic and cathodic products precipitate as the visible corrosion product (right output).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Corrosion Rate Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Identify Metal and Environment] --> P2[Step 2: Measure Ecorr via Reference Electrode]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Apply Polarization and Record Tafel Slopes]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Rp from Linear Polarization Region]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Apply Stern-Geary Equation to find icorr]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Convert icorr to CR in mpy or mm per year]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Compare with Standard Corrosion Resistance Tables]
    P7 --> P8{Acceptable Rate?}
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Design Approval]
    P8 -- No --> P10[Recommend Cathodic Protection or Material Change]
```

**Block Description:** This flowchart represents the standard KTU/industry workflow for evaluating the corrosion susceptibility of a metal. It begins with environmental identification, proceeds through electrochemical measurements (Ecorr, Tafel, Rp), and ends with a design-level decision (approve or apply protection).

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Corrosion Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Corrosion Classification] --> R1[Uniform or General Attack]
    R --> R2[Localized Corrosion]
    R --> R3[Galvanic or Two Metal]
    R --> R4[Environmental Cracking]
    R --> R5[Intergranular]
    R --> R6[Erosion Corrosion]
    R2 --> R2a[Pitting]
    R2 --> R2b[Crevice]
    R2 --> R2c[Filiform]
    R4 --> R4a[Stress Corrosion Cracking SCC]
    R4 --> R4b[Hydrogen Embrittlement]
    R4 --> R4c[Corrosion Fatigue]
```

**Block Description:** This classification map gives the major families of corrosion forms. KTU Module 1 covers the broad overview; localized forms (pitting, crevice) and SCC are addressed in detail in later modules.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Factors Influencing Corrosion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Corrosion Influencing Factors] --> F1[Metallurgical: Composition, Grain Size, Phases]
    F --> F2[Environmental: pH, Temperature, Velocity, Oxygen]
    F --> F3[Mechanical: Stress, Erosion, Fatigue]
    F --> F4[Electrical: Stray Currents, Galvanic Coupling]
    F1 --> X[Net Corrosion Rate]
    F2 --> X
    F3 --> X
    F4 --> X
```

**Block Description:** The four broad categories of factors that determine the actual rate observed in service. This is the framework used in failure analysis to identify root causes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define corrosion. List the three essentials required for an electrochemical corrosion process to occur.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Corrosion** is the deterioration of a metal caused by its chemical or electrochemical reaction with the surrounding environment, converting the metal into a more stable oxide, hydroxide, sulfide, or salt compound.

The **three essentials** of electrochemical corrosion are:
1. **Anode** — the site where the metal undergoes oxidation and dissolves.
2. **Cathode** — the site where a reduction reaction consumes the electrons released at the anode.
3. **Electrolyte** — an ionically conducting medium (usually an aqueous solution or moisture film) that completes the electrical circuit by carrying ions between the two electrodes.

If any one of these three is absent, electrochemical corrosion cannot proceed. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the three essentials)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between the Standard EMF Series and the Galvanic Series. Why is the Galvanic Series more useful in practical corrosion engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Standard EMF Series | Galvanic Series |
|--------|--------------------|---------------|
| Basis | Thermodynamic equilibrium of pure metals in 1M solutions at 25°C | Empirical ranking of metals/alloys in a specific environment (e.g., seawater) |
| Scope | Pure elements only | Includes commercial alloys |
| Values | Precise numerical potentials | Listed in order of nobility |
| Application | Predicts whether reaction is spontaneous | Predicts actual corrosion behavior in real environments |

**Why Galvanic Series is more useful in practice:**
The EMF series assumes **ideal, pure, equilibrium conditions** rarely encountered in real service. The Galvanic Series reflects **actual non-equilibrium behavior** in practical environments (e.g., seawater), accounting for passivity, surface films, and alloy effects. Therefore, when designing a structure or selecting materials, engineers rely on the **Galvanic Series** for predicting which metal will corrode when coupled. *(3 marks: 2 for the table/difference + 1 for the reason)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the **electrochemical mechanism of corrosion** of iron in neutral aerated water. Write the relevant anodic, cathodic, and overall cell reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Nernst Equation** starting from the Gibbs free energy relationship. State clearly the significance of each term.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Electrochemical Mechanism of Corrosion of Iron in Neutral Aerated Water:**

When iron is exposed to neutral aerated water, it forms a **galvanic micro-cell** on its surface. The process occurs as follows:

**Step 1 — Anodic Reaction (Oxidation of Iron):**
The iron surface acts as the anode and dissolves:

$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 2 — Cathodic Reaction (Oxygen Reduction):**
Dissolved oxygen in water is reduced at the cathode:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4OH^{-}$$

**Step 3 — Ionic Migration Through the Electrolyte:**
- $\,Fe^{2+}\,$ ions move from the anode through the water film.
- $\,OH^{-}\,$ ions move from the cathode through the water film.
- Electrons flow through the metal from anode to cathode.

**Step 4 — Formation of Corrosion Product:**
The Fe²⁺ and OH⁻ ions combine to form ferrous hydroxide, which is further oxidized by atmospheric oxygen to form hydrated ferric oxide (rust):

$$Fe^{2+} + 2OH^{-} \rightarrow Fe(OH)_2$$

$$4Fe(OH)_2 + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O \; (Rust)$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$2Fe + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe(OH)_2$$

The porous, flaky, non-protective rust layer continues to expose fresh metal to further attack, making corrosion **self-sustaining**. *(7 marks: 2 for anodic + 2 for cathodic + 2 for overall + 1 for explanation of rusting cycle)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of the Nernst Equation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the fundamental thermodynamic relation between Gibbs free energy and the reaction quotient $Q$:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cell-electrochemistry relation $\Delta G = -nFE$ at any condition and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$ at standard condition:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange by dividing by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 4:** Convert to base-10 logarithm using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the constant at T = 298.15 K:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298.15}{96{,}485} = 0.0592 \; V$$

**Step 6 — Final Nernst Equation:**

$$\boxed{E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q}$$

**Significance of Each Term:**
- $E$ = Electrode potential under actual conditions (V)
- $E^{\circ}$ = Standard electrode potential (V)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient (ratio of product to reactant activities)
- $0.0592$ = Temperature-dependent constant at 25°C

The equation predicts how the electrode potential shifts when ion concentration deviates from the standard 1 M value. *(7 marks: 1 for stating starting equation + 2 for substitution and rearrangement + 2 for conversion to log base + 1 for final form + 1 for significance)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**. A steel pipeline exhibits a corrosion current density of $85 \; \mu A/cm^2$. Calculate the **corrosion rate in mpy**. (Given: Atomic weight of Fe = 55.85, density = 7.87 g/cm³, valency = 2)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Mixed Potential Theory** with the help of an **Evans Diagram**. How is the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ determined from this diagram?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed through the electrolyte.

**Faraday's Second Law:** When the same quantity of electricity is passed through different electrolytes in series, the masses of substances liberated are proportional to their chemical equivalent weights.

**Step 1 — Identify the Corrosion Rate Formula:**

$$CR \; (mpy) = \frac{0.129 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Equivalent Weight of Iron:**

$$EW = \frac{55.85}{2} = 27.925 \; g/eq$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Equation:**

$$CR = \frac{0.129 \times 85 \times 27.925}{7.87}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply Numerator:**

$$0.129 \times 85 = 10.965$$

$$10.965 \times 27.925 = 306.20$$

**Step 5 — Divide by Density:**

$$CR = \frac{306.20}{7.87} = 38.91 \; mpy$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{CR \approx 38.9 \; mpy \;\; \text{(approximately } 0.99 \; \text{mm/year)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part a):** Many students forget to convert $i_{corr}$ from $\mu A/cm^2$ to $A/cm^2$ when using formulas that do not already absorb this conversion. The constant **0.129** in the mpy formula **assumes** $i_{corr}$ is given in $\mu A/cm^2$. Using 0.000085 instead of 85 will yield a wrong answer. Always cross-check the unit of the constant.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Mixed Potential Theory (Wagner-Traud, 1938):**

When a metal corrodes, two or more electrochemical reactions occur simultaneously on the same surface. The anodic (metal dissolution) and cathodic (reduction of O₂ or H⁺) half-reactions **polarize** each other until a steady-state **mixed potential** (also called the **corrosion potential** $E_{corr}$) is established. At this point, the **net anodic current = net cathodic current**, and the magnitude of this current is the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$**.

**Evans Diagram:**

The Evans Diagram is a plot of:
- **X-axis:** Electrode potential $E$ (V)
- **Y-axis:** Log of current density $\log \vert i \vert$ (A/cm²)
- **Anodic curve:** Metal dissolution reaction rising from $E^{\circ}_{M/M^{n+}}$
- **Cathodic curve:** Reduction reaction (O₂ or H⁺) falling from its equilibrium value

The **intersection** of the anodic Tafel line and the cathodic Tafel line gives:
- **X-coordinate:** $E_{corr}$ (corrosion potential)
- **Y-coordinate:** $\log(i_{corr})$ (logarithm of corrosion current density)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G[Evans Diagram Construction] --> G1[Plot anodic Tafel line of metal dissolution]
    G --> G2[Plot cathodic Tafel line of ORR or HER]
    G1 --> G3[Intersection gives Ecorr and log icorr]
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4[Read icorr from y-axis: corrosion rate indicator]
```

**Determination of $i_{corr}$:**

1. Apply small external polarizations (typically ±10 to ±20 mV around $E_{corr}$).
2. Record the current response to obtain the **linear polarization resistance** $R_p = \Delta E / \Delta i$.
3. Extrapolate the linear (Tafel) regions of the anodic and cathodic branches.
4. The intersection of these extrapolated Tafel lines yields $E_{corr}$ and $i_{corr}$.
5. Use the **Stern-Geary equation** to convert $R_p$ into $i_{corr}$:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

The $i_{corr}$ value is then converted to a corrosion rate (mpy or mm/yr) using Faraday's law. *(7 marks: 2 for theory + 2 for diagram/intersection concept + 2 for Stern-Geary application + 1 for rate conversion)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students often draw the Evans Diagram incorrectly by placing both curves in the same quadrant. Remember: the **anodic line slopes upward** (positive slope) and the **cathodic line slopes downward** (negative slope), and they **intersect in the anodic region of the cathodic curve**. Failure to mark $E_{corr}$ and $i_{corr}$ at the intersection will cost at least 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Concepts in Corrosion**

- ✅ **Corrosion** = reverse of extractive metallurgy; **destructive, unintentional** attack of a metal.
- ✅ Three essentials: **Anode + Cathode + Electrolyte**. Absence of any one halts corrosion.
- ✅ Anodic reaction always involves **metal oxidation**: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$.
- ✅ The **most common cathodic reaction** in natural waters is **oxygen reduction (ORR)**.
- ✅ **Thermodynamic spontaneity** is governed by $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$; negative $\Delta G$ ⟹ spontaneous corrosion.
- ✅ **Nernst Equation** (25°C): $E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$.
- ✅ **Faraday's Constant** = 96,485 C/mol. Master the **mpy formula**: $CR = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$.
- ✅ **EMF Series** is thermodynamic (pure metals); **Galvanic Series** is practical (alloys in real environment).
- ✅ **Mixed Potential Theory** is the foundation of modern electrochemical corrosion science (Wagner-Traud, 1938).
- ✅ **Corrosion current $i_{corr}$** is found at the **intersection of Tafel lines** in the Evans Diagram.
- ✅ **Stern-Geary Equation** converts **polarization resistance** into **corrosion rate**.
- ✅ The corrosion of iron in aerated water gives **rust** (Fe₂O₃·H₂O), a **non-protective, porous** product.
- ✅ Always specify the **environment** when discussing corrosion — corrosion rates are not intrinsic material properties.
- ✅ Common units: **mpy** (mils per year) and **mm/yr**; $1 \; mpy = 0.0254 \; mm/yr$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anodic and Cathodic Reactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Anodic and Cathodic Reactions # Module 1: Anodic and Cathodic Reactions — Core Foundations ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **electrochemical corrosion**, every corrosion process is fundamentally a **galvanic (electrochemical) cell** operating on a metal surface. This cell consists of two half-cell reactions occurring simultaneously at spatially distinct (or microscopically adjacent) sites on the metal: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anodic Reaction (Oxidation):** The half-cell reaction in which a metal atom **loses electrons** to the environment, dissolving as a positively charged ion into the electrolyte. It is the site where **metal destruction** occurs. > > **General Form:** $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^-$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cathodic Reaction (Reduction):** The half-cell reaction in which **species from the electrolyte gain the electrons** released by the anodic reaction. It is the site where **no metal loss** occurs, but supporting (often damaging) chemistry takes place. > > **General Form:** $Oxidized\,Species + n e^- \rightarrow Reduced\,Product$ The **sum** of these two half-cell reactions gives the **overall corrosion reaction**. Without these two reactions occurring in a **coupled, charge-balanced manner**, no corrosion current can flow, and the metal remains passive. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: A Leaky Water Tower Imagine a **water tower supplying a city**: - The **anode** is like the **water tank** that is leaking — water (metal ions) is leaving the system, and the water level (metal thickness) drops. - The **electrons** released are like the **pressure** that drives water (current) through the pipes. - The **cathode** is like the **city tap** — it does not lose water, but it *consumes* what is delivered. Different "cities" (different electrolytes — acidic, neutral, oxygenated) consume water differently. - The **pipe** connecting them is the **metal itself** (electronic conductor) and the **wetted surface** (ionic conductor / electrolyte). Just as the leak (anode) and the tap (cathode) **must be connected** for water to flow, anodic and cathodic sites **must be electrically connected** (through the metal) and **ionically connected** (through the electrolyte) for corrosion to occur. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Corrosion is a **two-sided coin**. You cannot have an anode without a cathode.
