The rate of metal loss at the anode **exactly equals** the rate of electron consumption at the cathode — this is enforced by **charge conservation** (Kirchhoff's Current Law for corrosion cells). --- ## 1.3 Critical Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | Symbol/Unit | |------|---------|-------------| | **Standard Electrode Potential** | Voltage of a half-cell vs. SHE at unit activity | $E^\circ$ (Volts) | | **Faraday's Constant** | Charge per mole of electrons | $F = \mathbf{96{,}485}$ C/mol | | **Corrosion Current** | Rate of electron flow in corrosion cell | $i_{corr}$ (A/cm²) | | **Anode Area** | Region undergoing oxidation | $A_a$ (cm²) | | **Cathode Area** | Region supporting reduction | $A_c$ (cm²) | # Module 1: Deep Theoretical Analysis — Anodic & Cathodic Reactions ## 2.1 The Anodic Reaction — Site of Metal Destruction The anodic reaction is always an **oxidation** process, characterized by: 1. **Loss of electrons** by the metal. 2. **Transition** of the metal from a **neutral atomic state** to a **positively charged ionic state**. 3. **Dissolution** of the metal ion into the adjacent electrolyte (or formation of an insoluble oxide/hydroxide if the local chemistry permits). 4. The metal ion is **solvated** (surrounded by polar water molecules), stabilizing it in solution. ### Common Anodic Reactions (KTU High-Yield List) | Metal | Anodic Reaction | Typical Context | |-------|-----------------|-----------------| | Iron | $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ | Most common corrosion reaction | | Zinc | $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$ | Galvanizing, sacrificial anodes | | Aluminum | $Al \rightarrow Al^{3+} + 3e^-$ | Atmospheric & marine corrosion | | Copper | $Cu \rightarrow Cu^{2+} + 2e^-$ | Aqueous, oxidizing environments | | Magnesium | $Mg \rightarrow Mg^{2+} + 2e^-$ | Highly active sacrificial anodes | > [!NOTE] > The **more negative** the standard reduction potential $E^\circ$ of the metal, the **more thermodynamically favorable** its oxidation becomes. This is the cornerstone of the **Galvanic Series**. --- ## 2.2 The Cathodic Reaction — Site of Supporting Chemistry The cathodic reaction **consumes** the electrons produced at the anode. The *type* of cathodic reaction depends on the **electrolyte chemistry**, particularly its **pH** and **dissolved oxygen content**. ### The Three Most Important Cathodic Reactions in Corrosion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reaction 1 — Hydrogen Evolution (in acidic, oxygen-free media):** > > $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2(g) \quad (E^\circ = 0.000\,V\,vs.\,SHE)$$ > > This dominates in **strong acid pickling baths, sour (H₂S-containing) environments, and reducing acid conditions**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reaction 2 — Oxygen Reduction in Acidic Solution:** > > $$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad (E^\circ = +1.229\,V\,vs.\,SHE)$$ > > This dominates in **aerated acidic environments** (e.g., atmospheric exposure with acid rain). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reaction 3 — Oxygen Reduction in Neutral/Alkaline Solution:** > > $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.401\,V\,vs.\,SHE)$$ > > This is **the most common cathodic reaction in practice** — it dominates in **seawater, atmospheric moisture, freshwater, and buried-soil conditions**. A fourth, less common cathodic reaction is **Metal Ion Reduction** (e.g., $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$), which is critical in **cementation** and **electrowinning**. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Law | Expression | Use / Application | |---------------|------------|-------------------| | **Faraday's First Law** | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal dissolved by current $I$ for time $t$ | | **Faraday's Second Law** | $\dfrac{m_1}{m_2} = \dfrac{EW_1}{EW_2}$ | Equivalent masses proportional to equivalent weights | | **Equivalent Weight** | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Mass per mole of electrons | | **Nernst Equation** | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log Q$ (at 25 °C) | Half-cell potential at non-standard conditions | | **General Nernst** | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Universal form | | **Corrosion Rate (Penetration)** | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mm/year | | **Mixed-Potential (Evans Diagram)** | $i_a = i_c$ at $E_{corr}$ | Corrosion potential and current from polarization | | **Charge Conservation** | $i_a \cdot A_a = i_c \cdot A_c$ | Current balance constraint in a galvanic cell | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constants to memorize:** > - $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant) > - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) (Universal gas constant) > - $T = 298.15$ K at standard conditions > - $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V at 25 °C > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by **failure analysts** at oil refineries, **cathodic protection designers** for pipelines, **ship hull protection engineers**, and **R&D teams in paint/coating industries** to predict service life and design protection schemes. --- ## 2.4 Why These Reactions Must Be Coupled The anodic and cathodic reactions are **not independent** — they are linked by two physical requirements: 1. **Electron Path (Electronic Conduction):** Electrons released at the anode must travel through the metal to the cathode. If the path is broken (e.g., a non-conductive coating), corrosion halts. 2. **Ion Path (Ionic Conduction):** Cations and anions must migrate through the electrolyte. The electrolyte completes the circuit. **Without either path, no current flows, and no corrosion occurs.** This is precisely why **drying a metal surface prevents corrosion** — the ionic path is broken. # Module 1: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions ## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis **Starting Postulate:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is **proportional to the quantity of electricity** passed through the electrolyte. **Step 1 — Set up proportionality:** $$m \propto Q$$ where $Q$ is the total charge in coulombs. **Step 2 — Introduce the proportionality constant (Electrochemical Equivalent):** $$m = Z \cdot Q$$ where $Z$ is the **electrochemical equivalent** (kg/C). **Step 3 — Express charge in terms of current and time:** $$Q = I \cdot t$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the proportionality:** $$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$ **Step 5 — Relate $Z$ to atomic mass and Faraday's constant:** For a metal with molar mass $M$ and valency $n$, **one mole of atoms** requires $nF$ coulombs to deposit/dissolve. $$Z = \frac{M}{nF}$$ **Step 6 — Final working equation (Faraday's First Law):** $$\boxed{m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}}$$ > This equation has **direct KTU board exam importance** — it appears in nearly every corrosion rate calculation question. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) **Problem:** A steel pipeline (Fe, $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³) is corroding uniformly with a corrosion current density $i_{corr} = 50\,\mu A/cm^2$. Calculate: 1. The mass of iron lost per day from **1 m²** of surface. 2. The corrosion rate in **mm/year** (penetration rate). **Given:** - $i_{corr} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,A/cm^2$ - Area $A = 1\,m^2 = 10^4\,cm^2$ - $n = 2$ (Fe²⁺) - $M = 55.85\,g/mol$ - $F = 96{,}485\,C/mol$ - $\rho = 7.87\,g/cm^3$ - Time $t = 1\,day = 86{,}400\,s$ ### Part (a) — Mass Loss Calculation **Step 1:** Total current $$I = i_{corr} \times A = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10^4 = 0.5\,A$$ **Step 2:** Total charge in 1 day $$Q = I \times t = 0.5 \times 86{,}400 = 43{,}200\,C$$ **Step 3:** Moles of electrons transferred $$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{43{,}200}{96{,}485} = 0.4477\,mol$$ **Step 4:** Moles of Fe dissolved (each Fe loses 2 electrons) $$n_{Fe} = \frac{0.4477}{2} = 0.2239\,mol$$ **Step 5:** Mass of Fe lost $$m = n_{Fe} \times M = 0.2239 \times 55.85 = \mathbf{12.50\,g/day}$$ ### Part (b) — Penetration Corrosion Rate **Step 1:** Volume of Fe lost $$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{12.50}{7.87} = 1.588\,cm^3/day$$ **Step 2:** Convert to uniform thickness lost (over $10{,}000\,cm^2$ area) $$\text{Thickness/day} = \frac{1.588}{10{,}000} = 1.588 \times 10^{-4}\,cm/day$$ **Step 3:** Convert to mm/year $$CR = 1.588 \times 10^{-4} \times 10\,(mm/cm) \times 365\,(days/yr)$$ $$CR = 1.588 \times 10^{-4} \times 3650 = \mathbf{0.580\,mm/yr}$$ **Step 4 (Short-cut verification using standard formula):** $$CR = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho} = \frac{0.00327 \times 50 \times (55.85/2)}{7.87} = \mathbf{0.580\,mm/yr} \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: The Nernst Equation (for Cathodic/Anodic Half-Cells) **Starting Postulate (Thermodynamic Basis):** For a general half-reaction $aA + ne^- \rightarrow bB$, the change in Gibbs free energy is: $$\Delta G = \Delta G^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ where $Q = \dfrac{[B]^b}{[A]^a}$. **Step 1:** Relate Gibbs energy to cell potential: $$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$$ **Step 2:** Substitute: $$-nFE = -nFE^\circ + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3:** Divide both sides by $-nF$: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 4:** Convert to base-10 logarithm ($\ln Q = 2.303 \log Q$) and evaluate at $T = 298\,K$: $$E = E^\circ - \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96{,}485} \log Q$$ **Step 5:** Simplify the constant: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96{,}485} = 0.0591$$ **Step 6 (Final Nernst Equation at 25 °C):** $$\boxed{E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n}\log Q}$$ > This equation lets us predict **whether** a metal will corrode in a given environment — KTU frequently tests this in 7-mark derivation problems. # Module 1: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — The Coupled Anode-Cathode Corrosion Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ANODE_REGION["ANODIC SITE (Metal Loss Zone)"] direction TB M1["METAL ATOM (M)"] --> M2["OXIDATION STEP"] M2 --> M3["METAL ION (M^n+)"] M2 --> M4["RELEASED ELECTRONS (n e^-)"] M3 --> M5["DISSOLVES INTO ELECTROLYTE"] end ELEC["ELECTROLYTE
(Ionic Conductor)"] subgraph CATHODE_REGION["CATHODIC SITE (No Metal Loss Zone)"] direction TB O1["OXIDANT (H+ or O2)"] --> O2["REDUCTION STEP"] O2 --> O3["REDUCED PRODUCT
(H2 or H2O or OH-)"] end M4 -- "ELECTRON FLOW
THROUGH METAL" --> O2 M5 -. "ION MIGRATION
THROUGH ELECTROLYTE" --> ELEC ELEC -. "ION MIGRATION" --> O1 style ANODE_REGION fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style CATHODE_REGION fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style ELEC fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#806600,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## 4.2 Decision Flow — Identifying the Cathodic Reaction in Practice ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Identify Corrosion Environment"] --> B{"Is the
electrolyte
strongly acidic?"} B -- "YES, low pH
and no O2" --> C["Cathodic Reaction:
2H+ + 2e- --> H2"] B -- "YES, low pH
and aerated" --> D["Cathodic Reaction:
O2 + 4H+ + 4e- --> 2H2O"] B -- "NO, neutral or
alkaline" --> E{"Is dissolved
oxygen present?"} E -- "YES" --> F["Cathodic Reaction:
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- --> 4OH-"] E -- "NO, deaerated" --> G["Cathodic Reaction:
2H2O + 2e- --> H2 + 2OH-"] style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366 style C fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style D fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style F fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 style G fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#cc0000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Architecture — Charge & Mass Balance in a Corrosion Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUTS["INPUT PARAMETERS"] ELEC_IN["Electrolyte pH
Dissolved O2
Temperature"] METAL_IN["Metal type
E_anode, E_cathode
Areas Aa and Ac"] end subgraph PROCESS["CORROSION CELL MODEL"] AN["ANODE:
M --> M^n+ + ne-"] CP["ELECTRONIC PATH
through metal"] CT["CATHODE:
Oxidant + ne- --> Product"] IP["IONIC PATH
through electrolyte"] end subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT METRICS"] ECORR["E_corr (Corrosion Potential)"] ICORR["i_corr (Corrosion Current Density)"] RATE["Corrosion Rate
mm/year"] MASS["Mass Loss
via Faraday's Law"] END INPUTS --> PROCESS AN --> CP CP --> CT CT --> IP IP --> AN PROCESS --> OUTPUTS style AN fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000 style CT fill:#d6e6ff,stroke:#003399 style OUTPUTS fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw a labeled diagram of a corrosion cell," always include — (1) the metal showing distinct anode and cathode regions, (2) the electrolyte, (3) arrows for **electron flow** (anode → cathode) and **ion flow** (cathode → anode in solution), and (4) the relevant half-reactions at each site. # Module 1: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between anodic and cathodic reactions in corrosion with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Anodic Reaction | Cathodic Reaction | |---------|----------------|-------------------| | Process | Oxidation (electron loss) | Reduction (electron gain) | | Effect on metal | Metal dissolves/loses mass | No mass loss | | Electron activity | Releases electrons | Consumes electrons | | Electrochemical nature | More negative potential | More positive potential | | **Example** | $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ | (3 marks — 1 for definition difference, 1 for mass-loss effect, 1 for examples) --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Write the three most common cathodic reactions encountered in corrosion with their standard potentials.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Hydrogen evolution in acid:** $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$, $E^\circ = 0.000\,V$ (vs. SHE) 2. **Oxygen reduction in acid:** $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$, $E^\circ = +1.229\,V$ (vs. SHE) 3. **Oxygen reduction in neutral/alkaline:** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$, $E^\circ = +0.401\,V$ (vs. SHE) (3 marks — 1 mark per correctly written reaction with $E^\circ$ value) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 Compiled]` **(a)** With neat sketches and relevant equations, explain the **anodic and cathodic reactions** that occur during the corrosion of **iron in a neutral, aerated aqueous environment**. Discuss the **role of dissolved oxygen** in determining the cathodic reaction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** from first principles. A mild steel tank of surface area $5\,m^2$ is observed to lose $250\,g$ of iron in $30$ days under uniform corrosion. Calculate the **corrosion current** and the **corrosion current density**. ($M_{Fe} = 55.85\,g/mol$, $n = 2$) **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** In neutral aerated water (e.g., freshwater, atmospheric moisture), iron undergoes the following: **Anodic Reaction (oxidation of iron):** $$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^- \quad (E^\circ = -0.440\,V\,vs.\,SHE)$$ **Cathodic Reaction (oxygen reduction in neutral medium):** $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.401\,V\,vs.\,SHE)$$ **Overall Cell Reaction** (multiply anodic by 2 to balance electrons): $$2Fe + O_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe^{2+} + 4OH^-$$ The $Fe^{2+}$ and $OH^-$ ions combine in solution to form **ferrous hydroxide**, which further oxidizes to **rust** (ferric oxide hydrate): $$2Fe(OH)_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O \rightarrow 2Fe(OH)_3 \rightarrow Fe_2O_3 \cdot H_2O$$ **Role of Dissolved Oxygen:** - It is the **oxidizing species** that accepts electrons at the cathode. - Without $O_2$, the cathodic reaction shifts to hydrogen evolution (slower in neutral solution). - Higher $O_2$ concentration → higher cathodic current → **higher corrosion rate**. - Differential aeration cells (crevices, water-line corrosion) exploit this — areas with **less** $O_2$ become **anodic**. (7 marks — 2 for reactions, 2 for overall, 3 for role of $O_2$) --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Derivation of Faraday's First Law:** The mass of a substance liberated at an electrode is proportional to the total charge passed: $$m \propto Q = I \cdot t$$ For a metal with molar mass $M$ and valency $n$, the charge required to liberate one mole is $nF$ coulombs. Therefore, the **electrochemical equivalent** $Z = M/(nF)$, giving: $$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ **Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $m = 250\,g$, $t = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2.592 \times 10^6\,s$, $A = 5\,m^2 = 5 \times 10^4\,cm^2$, $M = 55.85\,g/mol$, $n = 2$. **Step 1:** Rearrange Faraday's equation to solve for current: $$I = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{t \cdot M} = \frac{250 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{2.592 \times 10^6 \times 55.85}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate numerator: $$250 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 = 4.824 \times 10^7$$ **Step 3:** Calculate denominator: $$2.592 \times 10^6 \times 55.85 = 1.448 \times 10^8$$ **Step 4:** Solve: $$I = \frac{4.824 \times 10^7}{1.448 \times 10^8} = \mathbf{0.333\,A}$$ **Step 5:** Current density: $$i_{corr} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{0.333}{5 \times 10^4} = \mathbf{6.66 \times 10^{-6}\,A/cm^2} = 6.66\,\mu A/cm^2$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 Compiled]` **(a)** Discuss the **various cathodic reactions** possible during aqueous corrosion of metals. Explain how the **electrolyte pH** and **oxygen availability** influence the choice of cathodic reaction. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A buried cast iron pipe (Fe, $\rho = 7.87\,g/cm^3$, $M = 55.85\,g/mol, n = 2$) has been in service for 3 years and shows a uniform wall-thickness reduction of **2.4 mm**. Calculate: (i) the **corrosion current density**, and (ii) the **total mass of iron lost** from a $1\,m^2$ section. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** The cathodic reaction is the **reduction** of an oxidizing agent present in the electrolyte. Depending on pH and oxygen: **1. In Strongly Acidic Solutions (low pH, often deaerated):** $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \uparrow \quad (E^\circ = 0.000\,V)$$ **2. In Acidic, Aerated Solutions:** $$O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O \quad (E^\circ = +1.229\,V)$$ **3. In Neutral/Aerated Solutions (most common, e.g., seawater):** $$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.401\,V)$$ **4. In Neutral, Deaerated Solutions:** $$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^- \quad (E^\circ = -0.828\,V)$$ **5. Metal-Ion Reduction (special case, e.g., copper deposition):** $$Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu \quad (E^\circ = +0.337\,V)$$ **Influence of pH:** - **Low pH** → $H^+$ concentration is high → hydrogen evolution dominates. - **High pH** → $H^+$ is scarce → water reduction or oxygen reduction dominates. **Influence of Oxygen:** - **Aerated** → oxygen reduction has a much more positive $E^\circ$ → thermodynamically **preferred** and **faster**. - **Deaerated** → oxygen reduction is suppressed → hydrogen evolution or water reduction take over. (7 marks — 3 for listing reactions, 2 for pH influence, 2 for $O_2$ influence) --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Given:** Thickness loss $\Delta x = 2.4\,mm = 0.24\,cm$ over $t = 3\,years = 3 \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 9.461 \times 10^7\,s$, $A = 1\,m^2 = 10^4\,cm^2$, $\rho = 7.87\,g/cm^3$. **(i) Corrosion Current Density:** **Step 1:** Mass lost from $1\,m^2$: $$m = \rho \times V = \rho \times (A \times \Delta x) = 7.87 \times 10^4 \times 0.24 = 18{,}888\,g$$ **Step 2:** Apply Faraday's law to find current: $$I = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{t \cdot M} = \frac{18{,}888 \times 2 \times 96{,}485}{9.461 \times 10^7 \times 55.85}$$ **Step 3:** Numerator: $18{,}888 \times 2 \times 96{,}485 = 3.645 \times 10^9$ **Step 4:** Denominator: $9.461 \times 10^7 \times 55.85 = 5.284 \times 10^9$ **Step 5:** $I = 3.645 / 5.284 = 0.6898\,A$ **Step 6:** Current density: $$i_{corr} = \frac{0.6898}{10^4} = \mathbf{6.898 \times 10^{-5}\,A/cm^2} \approx 69\,\mu A/cm^2$$ **(ii) Total Mass Lost:** $$\boxed{m = 18{,}888\,g \approx 18.89\,kg}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is mixing seconds, hours, and days. Convert *all* times to seconds before substituting into Faraday's law. > 2. **Valency Confusion:** Students often write Fe³⁺ (from rust) instead of Fe²⁺ (the actual ion released at the anode). The **anode** reaction produces **Fe²⁺**; the subsequent oxidation to Fe³⁺ happens **in solution**, not at the metal surface. > 3. **Sign Convention:** Standard reduction potentials are for **reduction** reactions. For anodic reactions, the sign of $E^\circ$ **reverses** when you reverse the equation. > 4. **Oxygen Reaction Selection:** Many students write the wrong oxygen-reduction equation. Memorize: **acidic = uses H⁺**, **neutral/alkaline = uses H₂O**. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST - ✅ **Anode = Oxidation = Metal Loss** ($M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$) - ✅ **Cathode = Reduction = Electron Consumption** (no metal loss at this site) - ✅ **Three dominant cathodic reactions:** Hydrogen evolution, $O_2$ reduction (acidic), $O_2$ reduction (neutral) - ✅ **Memorize $E^\circ$ values** for all three cathodic reactions — they are frequently asked. - ✅ **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ — the workhorse equation for **all** corrosion rate numericals. - ✅ **Nernst Equation:** $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ — used to determine **direction** of corrosion at non-standard conditions. - ✅ **Corrosion Rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ in **mm/yr** — direct board-exam formula. - ✅ **Charge Conservation:** $i_a A_a = i_c A_c$ — the bridge connecting anode and cathode behavior. - ✅ **Coupling Requirement:** Anode and cathode must be **electrically** (metal) and **ionically** (electrolyte) connected. - ✅ **Role of Oxygen:** $O_2$ is the most common cathodic depolarizer in nature — its concentration directly controls corrosion rate in neutral water. - ✅ **Key Constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V at 25 °C. - ✅ **Discipline Trick:** In KTU numericals, always **carry units** through every line — partial credit is awarded for correct methodology even if the final answer has a slip.

topics.title: Anodic Reaction Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anodic Reaction Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**An anodic reaction** is the half-cell oxidation process occurring at the anode of a corroding electrochemical cell, in which a metal lattice atom loses one or more electrons and passes into the electrolyte as solvated cations (or forms an insoluble compound/oxide). The general stoichiometric form is:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **anodic reaction characteristics** refer to the complete electrochemical signature of this oxidation step — including its equilibrium potential, exchange current density, Tafel slope, polarization behavior, passivation tendency, critical current density, passive range, and transpassive breakdown — as obtained from a potentiostatic or potentiodynamic polarization experiment plotted as **E vs. log i** (Evans Diagram).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The student must be able to distinguish *active dissolution*, *passive*, and *transpassive* regions on a single anodic polarization curve, and must know the significance of the **Flade Potential (E_F)** and the **Critical Anodic Current Density (i_crit)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Leaking Bucket

Imagine a metal surface as a **bucket full of water (electrons)** standing on a table. The anodic reaction is essentially **drilling a hole in the side of the bucket** — the water (electrons) flows out, and the bucket itself (the metal) loses mass as the leaking water carries dissolved metal cations with it.

- If you drill a **small, slow-leaking hole** → the metal dissolves gently (active region, low current density).
- If you coat the hole with a **thin, tight plastic film (passive oxide)** → leakage nearly stops (passive region).
- If you **punch through or rupture the film** → leakage becomes catastrophic again (transpassive/pitting).
- The **position of the hole (potential)** and the **size of the hole (current density)** together define the *anodic reaction characteristics*.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants

- **Faraday's Constant, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (charge per mole of electrons)
- **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **Standard Temperature, T = 298.15 K** (reference for Nernst equation)
- **RT/F (at 25 °C) = 0.02569 V** (thermal voltage)
- **2.303 RT/F (at 25 °C) = 0.05916 V** (logarithmic form constant)
- **Standard reference electrode:** Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE), defined as **0.000 V** at all temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> Every anodic reaction characteristic discussed below is fundamentally a function of the **electrode potential (E)** and the **anodic current density (i_A)** — these two variables are the *coordinates of corrosion behavior*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — The Nernst Equation

The **driving force** for the anodic reaction is determined by the reversible electrode potential, given by the Nernst equation:

$$E_{M^{n+}/M} = E^{\circ}_{M^{n+}/M} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{M^{n+}}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Standard Potential (E°)** — The intrinsic nobility/activity of the metal referenced to SHE.
2. **Activity Term** — The actual ionic concentration (corrected by activity coefficient) in the electrolyte shifts the equilibrium potential.
3. **Concentration Polarization** — When the metal dissolves faster than ions can diffuse away, surface concentration rises, shifting E in the noble (positive) direction — this is the anodic concentration overpotential.
4. **Practical Consequence** — A more negative E° means a stronger thermodynamic driving force for the anodic reaction (e.g., Mg: −2.37 V vs. Cu: +0.34 V).

## 2.2 Kinetic Foundation — The Butler–Volmer & Tafel Framework

The **rate** of the anodic reaction is governed by activation polarization. For a single-step, single-electron transfer, the net anodic current density is:

$$i_A = i_o \left[ \exp\left(+\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

When the anodic overpotential (η_a = E − E_eq) is large (|η| > ~50 mV), the cathodic back-reaction becomes negligible, and the equation reduces to the **Tafel equation**:

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right)$$

Where the **anodic Tafel slope (β_a)** is related to the transfer coefficient:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha_a F} \approx \frac{0.0592}{\alpha_a} \text{ V/decade (at 25 °C)}$$

## 2.3 The Three Regimes of an Anodic Polarization Curve

A complete potentiodynamic scan from the corrosion potential (E_corr) in the **noble (positive) direction** reveals three distinct regimes:

### Regime I — Active Dissolution Region
- Metal dissolves uniformly; current rises exponentially with potential.
- Tafel behavior holds: **i_A ∝ 10^(η/β_a)**.
- Polarization resistance (R_p) is low → metal corrodes readily.

### Regime II — Active-to-Passive Transition
- At a critical potential (≈ passivation potential, **E_pp**), the current reaches a maximum — the **Critical Anodic Current Density (i_crit)**.
- Above E_pp, current **drops** sharply as a protective oxide/hydroxide film forms.
- A smaller i_crit indicates easier passivation (e.g., Cr, Al, Ti, stainless steels).

### Regime III — Passive Region
- Current is low and nearly **potential-independent** (typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ A/cm²).
- The passive film is stable, self-healing, and usually 1–10 nm thick.
- The film breakdown potential (upper limit) is the **Flade Potential (E_F)** or pitting potential (E_pit).
- Below E_F: passive; above E_F: transpassive.

### Regime IV — Transpassive Region
- Current rises again due to oxygen evolution on the passive film, oxidative dissolution of the alloying element (e.g., CrO₃ formation from Cr), or pitting/crevice breakdown.
- Useful for **anodic protection** (controlled potentiostat in passive range).

## 2.4 High-Yield KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Domain |
|---|----------|----------------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln a_{M^{n+}}$ | Nernst equilibrium potential | V (vs. SHE) | All anodic reactions |
| 2 | $i_A = i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{RT}\right)$ | Anodic activation current | A/cm² | High anodic η |
| 3 | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i_A}{i_o}\right)$ | Tafel form (anodic) | V | η_a > 50 mV |
| 4 | $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F} \approx \dfrac{0.0592}{\alpha_a}$ | Anodic Tafel slope | V/decade | 25 °C reference |
| 5 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern–Geary equation | A/cm² | Near E_corr (η < 10 mV) |
| 6 | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | Corrosion rate (mm/yr) | mm yr⁻¹ | mpy to mm/yr conversion |
| 7 | $E_F = E^{\circ}_{F} - 0.059\,\text{pH}$ | Flade potential (approx.) | V (SHE) | Passive film breakdown |
| 8 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's mass-loss law | grams (g) | Total metal dissolved |

**Critical KTU Symbols to Memorize:**

- **E_corr** — Corrosion (mixed) potential
- **i_corr** — Corrosion current density
- **i_crit** — Critical anodic current density (passivation peak)
- **i_pass** — Passive current density
- **E_pp** — Primary passivation potential
- **E_F** — Flade potential (passive film stability limit)
- **β_a, β_c** — Anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes
- **α_a** — Anodic transfer coefficient (typically 0.5)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Anodic reaction characteristics underpin several industrial practices:

1. **Anodic Protection (AP)** — A potentiostat holds a metal (e.g., carbon steel in H₂SO₄, stainless steel storage tanks) within its passive potential window, slashing corrosion rates by 10³–10⁶.
2. **Alloy Design** — Adding Cr, Al, or Ti to Fe lowers i_crit and broadens the passive range, producing stainless steels.
3. **Anodizing** — Deliberately forcing the metal deep into the transpassive regime to grow a thick, decorative, wear-resistant oxide (e.g., Type II anodized aluminum).
4. **Cathodic Protection Compatibility** — Knowing β_a helps engineers size the impressed-current anode and predict stray-current interference on neighboring structures.
5. **Failure Analysis** — Interpreting the anodic polarization shape (active vs. passive vs. transpassive) explains SCC, pitting, and crevice corrosion mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** A common 14-mark KTU question combines Evans diagram construction with the Stern–Geary equation. Always plot E on the y-axis (more noble upward) and log i on the x-axis — never invert them.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Butler–Volmer to the Tafel Slope (Anodic)

### Starting Point — The Full Butler–Volmer Equation

$$i = i_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

### Step 1 — Apply the "Large Anodic Overpotential" Approximation

For η ≫ 0 (i.e., the metal is polarized strongly in the noble direction), the **cathodic back-reaction** term is negligible:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \to 0$$

Therefore:

$$i_A \approx i_o \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{RT}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right) = \frac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{RT}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Natural Log to Base-10 Log

Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right) = \frac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{2.303\, RT}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Overpotential η_a

Rearranging the previous step by multiplying both sides by $\dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_a F}$:

$$\eta_a = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Tafel Slope

Define $\beta_a \equiv \dfrac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha_a F}$. At 25 °C with α_a = 0.5:

$$\beta_a = \frac{0.0592}{0.5} = 0.1184 \text{ V/decade}$$

This means **every tenfold increase in anodic current density requires a 118.4 mV noble shift in potential** — a result absolutely central to KTU numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a KTU problem gives you i₁, i₂, and the corresponding η_a1, η_a2, you can compute β_a directly from the experimental data without knowing i_o:
> $$\beta_a = \frac{\eta_{a2} - \eta_{a1}}{\log_{10}(i_{A2}/i_{A1})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stern–Geary Linear Polarization Resistance

### Step 1 — Butler–Volmer Expanded Near Equilibrium

Near E_corr (η < 10 mV), use the Taylor expansion $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for both exponentials:

$$i = i_o \left[\left(1 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right)\right]$$

### Step 2 — Simplify the Brackets

The constants cancel, leaving:

$$i = i_o \frac{F \eta}{RT}(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)$$

### Step 3 — Define the Polarization Resistance R_p

The slope of the E–i curve near the origin is the **polarization resistance**:

$$R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{RT}{i_o F(\alpha_a + \alpha_c)}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Tafel Form (Tafel Slopes Substitute the RT/αF Terms)

Substituting $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 RT}{\alpha_a F}$ and $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303 RT}{\alpha_c F}$:

$$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\, i_{corr}(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Corrosion Current Density

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}}$$

This is the **workhorse equation** of linear polarization resistance (LPR) corrosion rate monitoring.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Faraday's Law for Mass Loss

### Step 1 — Charge Passed During Corrosion

The total charge Q in coulombs passing through the anode is:

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

### Step 2 — Moles of Electrons

By definition of the Faraday constant, 1 mole of electrons = F coulombs. Therefore:

$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

### Step 3 — Moles of Metal Dissolved

For a metal M → M^(n+) + ne⁻, the stoichiometry gives:

$$n_M = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n F}$$

### Step 4 — Mass of Metal Lost

Multiply by the molar mass M_m (note: different from metal M):

$$m = n_M \cdot M_m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_m}{n F}$$

### Step 5 — Express as Corrosion Rate

Dividing by exposed area A and time t, and converting to mm/yr with the standard factor 0.00327:

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$

where **EW (Equivalent Weight) = M_m/n** and **ρ is density in g/cm³**.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor 0.00327 has units of (mm·g)/(yr·C·cm) and is derived from the identity 1 A·s = 1 C combined with 1 cm³/1000 mm³ and 8760 hr/yr ÷ 24 hr/day. **You will not be required to derive it in KTU**, but knowing its origin earns appreciation marks.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Typical KTU 14-Mark Numerical Pattern

**Problem:** For iron in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C, the anodic Tafel slope β_a = 0.060 V/decade and the corrosion current density i_corr = 10⁻⁴ A/cm². The anodic transfer coefficient α_a = 0.5. Calculate:
(a) The Tafel overpotential required to push the anodic current to 10⁻² A/cm².
(b) The corresponding polarization resistance R_p, assuming β_c = 0.120 V/decade.

### Solution (a) — Tafel Overpotential

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_{corr}}\right) = 0.060 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-4}}\right)$$

$$\eta_a = 0.060 \times \log_{10}(10^{2}) = 0.060 \times 2 = 0.120 \text{ V}$$

### Solution (b) — Polarization Resistance

$$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

$$R_p = \frac{(0.060)(0.120)}{2.303 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.060 + 0.120)}$$

$$R_p = \frac{0.00720}{2.303 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.180} = \frac{0.00720}{4.145 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$R_p \approx 173.7 \;\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pattern Tip:** KTU 14-mark questions almost always bundle a Tafel calculation with the Stern–Geary formula. Practice converting between log base 10 and natural log — this is a silent mark-killer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Regime-by-Regime Behavior Summary

| Parameter | Active | Passive | Transpassive |
|-----------|--------|---------|--------------|
| **Current trend** | Exponential rise (Tafel) | Low, nearly constant | Re-rises (O₂ evolution / pitting) |
| **i_A magnitude** | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻¹ A/cm² | 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁴ A/cm² | 10⁻⁴ to 10⁰ A/cm² |
| **Surface state** | Bare metal | Thin oxide film (1–10 nm) | Film breaks down, pits form |
| **Tafel valid?** | ✓ Yes | ✗ No (linear) | ✓ Yes (re-activated) |
| **Controlling step** | Charge transfer | Film dissolution/growth | Mass transport / O₂ evolution |
| **Protection strategy** | Cathodic protection | **Anodic protection** | Avoid! |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Complete Anodic Polarization Curve (Evans Diagram Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Sweep Potential from E_corr] --> B[E less than E_corr]
    B --> C[Cathodic Region - Hydrogen Evolution]
    A --> D[E greater than E_corr - Anodic Path]
    D --> E[Active Dissolution Region]
    E --> F{i_A reaches i_crit at E_pp}
    F -->|Yes| G[Active to Passive Transition]
    G --> H[Passive Plateau - Constant i_pass]
    H --> I{Reaches Flade Potential E_F}
    I -->|No| J[Stable Passivity Maintained]
    J --> K[Anodic Protection Operating Zone]
    I -->|Yes| L[Transpassive Region]
    L --> M[Oxygen Evolution OR Pitting OR Transpassive Dissolution]
    M --> N[Catastrophic Corrosion - Avoid]
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style E fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style G fill:#ed7d31,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    style H fill:#70ad47,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style K fill:#548235,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style L fill:#bf8f00,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style N fill:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style C fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style F fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style I fill:#7030a0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style J fill:#a9d18e,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    style M fill:#c55a11,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    style D fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Anodic Protection System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PS[Programmable Potentiostat] --> REF[Reference Electrode SHE or SCE]
    PS --> CE[Counter Electrode - Platinized Nb or Pb]
    PS --> WE[Work Electrode - Metal to be Protected]
    WE --> SEN[Surface Sensor]
    SEN -->|Feedback E_measured| CTRL[Controller Logic]
    CTRL -->|Compare with E_passive setpoint| PS
    REF -->|Measure E vs. Reference| CTRL
    
    style PS fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style REF fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style CE fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style WE fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style SEN fill:#ed7d31,color:#000000
    style CTRL fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Process Flow for Interpreting an Experimental Anodic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Prepare Working Electrode, polish to mirror finish] --> P2[Step 2: Immerse in deaerated electrolyte]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Measure Open Circuit Potential E_OCP for 1 hour]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Start Potentiodynamic sweep at 1 mV per second]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Record i vs. E response]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Identify Active Region and fit Tafel slope beta_a]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Locate Peak current i_crit and E_pp]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Identify Passive plateau i_pass]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Identify Flade Potential E_F]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Compute Corrosion Rate using Stern Geary equation]
    
    style P1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000000
    style P10 fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree for Selecting Anodic vs. Cathodic Protection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Metal exhibits active passive transition?} -->|Yes| Q2{Is electrolyte aggressive oxidizing?}
    Q1 -->|No| QC[Use Cathodic Protection]
    Q2 -->|Yes concentrated H2SO4| QA[Use Anodic Protection]
    Q2 -->|No dilute neutral| QB[Use Cathodic Protection or Coatings]
    QA --> R1[Set potentiostat to E_passive midpoint]
    R1 --> R2[Monitor via reference electrode]
    R2 --> R3[Maintain within passive window E_pp to E_F]
    
    style QA fill:#548235,color:#ffffff
    style QC fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style QB fill:#ed7d31,color:#000000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Critical Anodic Current Density (i_crit)" and explain its significance in determining the passivation behavior of a metal. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Critical Anodic Current Density (i_crit)** is defined as the maximum anodic current density observed on the polarization curve at the primary passivation potential (E_pp), representing the highest rate of active metal dissolution just before the protective oxide film begins to form.

**Significance (2 marks):**

- A **low i_crit** (e.g., 10⁻⁵ A/cm² for stainless steel in H₂SO₄) indicates **easy passivation** — the metal rapidly forms a stable film even at low applied potentials.
- A **high i_crit** (e.g., 10⁻² A/cm² for carbon steel) means the metal dissolves extensively before passivating, making anodic protection impractical.
- It governs the **current capacity** required by the potentiostat in an anodic protection system — lower i_crit means lower power consumption.

---

### Question 2: State the Tafel equation for the anodic reaction and define each term with units. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right)$$

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **η_a** — Anodic overpotential = E_applied − E_equilibrium, in volts (V).
- **β_a** — Anodic Tafel slope, in volts per decade of current (V/dec).
- **i_A** — Applied anodic current density, in A/cm².
- **i_o** — Exchange current density at equilibrium, in A/cm² (a kinetic property of the metal/electrolyte interface).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: (a) Draw a neat, fully labeled anodic polarization curve for a metal exhibiting active-passive behavior, clearly indicating the active region, i_crit, E_pp, passive region, and Flade potential. (b) With suitable equations, derive the Tafel slope from the Butler–Volmer equation and compute the anodic Tafel slope for a metal with α_a = 0.45 at 35 °C. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Sketch Description (Verbal since drawing is text-based):**

Plot a graph with:
- **Y-axis:** Electrode Potential E (V) — increasing upward, more noble on top.
- **X-axis:** log(i) in A/cm² — increasing to the right.

Label the following features:

1. **E_corr** — Mixed corrosion potential on the y-axis (no applied external current).
2. **Active Region** — Steep straight line rising from E_corr with slope β_a (Tafel line, ~60 mV/dec).
3. **i_crit** — Peak of the curve; mark the corresponding potential as **E_pp** (primary passivation potential).
4. **Active-to-Passive Transition** — Sharp current drop immediately after i_crit.
5. **Passive Region** — Nearly horizontal plateau at very low current density **i_pass** (typically 10⁻⁶ A/cm²).
6. **Flade Potential (E_F)** — Upper limit of the passive plateau; the potential at which the passive film begins to break down.
7. **Transpassive Region** — Current rises again beyond E_F.

*(Award full 7 marks for correctly labeled diagram with all 7 features identified.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from the Butler–Volmer equation for large anodic overpotential:

$$i_A = i_o \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{RT}\right)$$

Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right) = \frac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{RT}$$

Convert to base-10 logarithm using ln x = 2.303 log₁₀ x:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right) = \frac{\alpha_a F \eta_a}{2.303\, RT}$$

Rearrange to solve for η_a:

$$\eta_a = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha_a F} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i_A}{i_o}\right)$$

Therefore, the **Tafel slope** is:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha_a F}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: α_a = 0.45, T = 35 °C = 308.15 K, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹.

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 308.15}{0.45 \times 96485}$$

Compute numerator:

$$2.303 \times 8.314 = 19.147$$
$$19.147 \times 308.15 = 5900.45 \text{ J/mol}$$

Compute denominator:

$$0.45 \times 96485 = 43418.25 \text{ C/mol}$$

Therefore:

$$\beta_a = \frac{5900.45}{43418.25} = 0.1359 \text{ V/decade}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_a \approx 0.136 \text{ V/decade} = 136 \text{ mV/decade}}$$

---

### Question B: (a) Explain the concept of anodic protection with a neat schematic and discuss the conditions under which it is preferred over cathodic protection. (b) The polarization resistance of a corroding metal in 0.1 N HCl was measured as 250 Ω·cm². If the anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes are 0.060 V/dec and 0.120 V/dec respectively, calculate the corrosion current density and the corrosion rate in mm/yr (Take EW = 28 g/eq, ρ = 7.8 g/cm³). `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Anodic Protection — Concept (3 marks):**

Anodic protection (AP) is an electrochemical technique in which the metal to be protected is made the **anode** of an impressed-current electrochemical cell, and its potential is held within the **passive range** (between E_pp and E_F) using a **potentiostat**. Within this window, a thin, adherent, electronically insulating oxide film forms on the surface, reducing the corrosion current by factors of 10³ to 10⁶.

**Schematic (2 marks — described verbally):**

- A **potentiostat** with three electrodes immersed in the electrolyte:
  - **Working Electrode (WE):** The metal structure to be protected (e.g., sulfuric acid storage tank).
  - **Reference Electrode (RE):** Placed close to WE (e.g., SCE or platinum pseudo-reference).
  - **Counter Electrode (CE):** Inert cathode (Pt, platinized Nb, lead, or silicon cast iron).
- The potentiostat compares E_WE vs. E_RE and adjusts the auxiliary current through CE to keep E_WE at the **passive setpoint**, typically near the midpoint of the passive plateau.

**Conditions Favoring Anodic Protection (2 marks):**

1. The metal must exhibit a clear **active-passive transition** (e.g., stainless steels, Ti, Ni alloys, carbon steel in conc. H₂SO₄ or H₃PO₄).
2. The **electrolyte must be electrically conductive** to support current flow.
3. The **i_crit must be low** to keep the power supply rating economical.
4. The environment should be a **non-halide aqueous system** to avoid pitting within the passive range.
5. Preferred for **strong acids and concentrated alkalis** at elevated temperatures where coatings fail.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Corrosion Current Density using Stern–Geary (3 marks):**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

Substitute values:
- β_a = 0.060 V/dec
- β_c = 0.120 V/dec
- R_p = 250 Ω·cm²

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times 250 \times (0.060 + 0.120)}$$

Numerator:

$$0.060 \times 0.120 = 0.00720 \text{ V}^2/\text{dec}^2$$

Denominator:

$$2.303 \times 250 = 575.75$$
$$575.75 \times 0.180 = 103.635$$

Therefore:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{0.00720}{103.635} = 6.948 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{i_{corr} \approx 6.95 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2}$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion Rate in mm/yr (3 marks):**

$$\text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{corr} \times EW}{\rho}$$

Substitute:
- i_corr = 6.948 × 10⁻⁵ A/cm²
- EW = 28 g/eq
- ρ = 7.8 g/cm³

$$\text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \times 6.948 \times 10^{-5} \times 28}{7.8}$$

Numerator:

$$0.00327 \times 6.948 \times 10^{-5} = 2.272 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$2.272 \times 10^{-7} \times 28 = 6.362 \times 10^{-6}$$

Denominator:

$$7.8$$

Therefore:

$$\text{CR} = \frac{6.362 \times 10^{-6}}{7.8} = 8.156 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mm/yr}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CR} \approx 0.82 \;\mu\text{m/yr}}$$

**Step 3 — Practical Interpretation (1 mark):**

This is an **extremely low corrosion rate**, indicative of a metal that has likely entered the passive regime — consistent with a stainless steel in dilute acid. The system is in excellent condition; no remedial action is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Stern–Geary:** Forgetting that 2.303 converts the Tafel slopes from log₁₀ to natural log. Using β directly in a non-converted version loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing i_corr with i_crit:** i_corr is the corrosion current at E_corr (no external polarization); i_crit is the peak anodic current before passivation. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Sign Convention:** Anodic overpotential is **positive** (E > E_eq). Reversing the sign gives a negative Tafel slope, which is physically meaningless.
> 4. **Drawing the Evans Diagram Backwards:** The y-axis (E) must increase upward, and the x-axis (log i) must increase rightward. Students sometimes plot log i on the y-axis — full 7 marks lost in the diagram part.
> 5. **Forgetting the 0.00327 conversion factor** in CR calculations; some students write CR in mpy (mils per year) instead of mm/yr and the unit conversion is silently penalized.
> 6. **EW vs. Molar Mass:** EW = Atomic mass ÷ n (number of electrons transferred per atom dissolved). Using atomic mass directly instead of EW gives a wrong CR — losing 1 full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anodic Reaction Characteristics — 1-Page Mind Map**

- ⚡ **Anodic reaction = oxidation = electron release** → M → M^(n+) + ne⁻.
- 📐 **Tafel equation (anodic):** $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i_A / i_o)$.
- 🔢 **Tafel slope formula:** $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303\, RT}{\alpha_a F} \approx \dfrac{0.0592}{\alpha_a}$ V/dec at 25 °C.
- 📊 **Polarization curve has 4 regions:** Active → Active-Passive Transition → Passive → Transpassive.
- 🎯 **Key features to label:** E_corr, E_pp, i_crit, i_pass, E_F (Flade Potential).
- 🛡️ **Anodic Protection** = hold metal in passive window using a potentiostat (3-electrode system: WE, RE, CE).
- 🔁 **Stern–Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$.
- 🧪 **Faraday's Law:** $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M_m}{n F}$ and CR $= \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ in mm/yr.
- 🧠 **Memorize constants:** F = 96,485 C/mol, RT/F = 0.02569 V, 2.303 RT/F = 0.05916 V at 25 °C.
- 🚫 **Passive film fails in halide solutions** (Cl⁻, Br⁻) → pitting corrosion dominates.
- ✅ **Metals that passivate easily:** Al, Ti, Cr, Zr, stainless steels (Fe-Cr-Ni).
- ❌ **Metals that do NOT passivate well:** Mg, Zn, mild carbon steel in neutral aerated water.
- 📈 **α_a typically ≈ 0.5** for single-step single-electron transfer; assumed unless otherwise stated.
- 🔋 **Lower i_crit = easier passivation = lower power requirement for anodic protection.**
- 🧯 **Transpassive = dangerous** — current re-rises, oxygen evolves, pitting initiates.
- 🎨 **Anodizing (decorative/industrial)** uses the transpassive regime deliberately for thick oxide growth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cathodic Reaction Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cathodic Reaction Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In an electrochemical corrosion cell, the **cathodic reaction** is the *reduction half-reaction* that consumes the electrons liberated by the anodic (metal dissolution) reaction. In the absence of an externally applied current, the cathodic reaction is the *depolarizer* reaction whose kinetics control the rate of the entire corrosion process.

$$ \text{Oxidant} + n e^- \longrightarrow \text{Reductant} \quad (\text{Reduction at the Cathode}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim emphasis):** A *cathodic reaction* is a reduction process in which positively charged species (cations) or neutral molecules (O₂, H₂O) accept electrons at the metal/electrolyte interface. Because the cathodic reaction *consumes* electrons, its rate (in mol s⁻¹) **must equal** the anodic dissolution rate at the **corrosion potential (E_corr)** by the principle of charge conservation (mixed potential theory).

### 1.2 Common Cathodic Reactions Encountered in Corrosion

| Environment | Cathodic Reaction | Name | Standard Potential E° (V vs SHE) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acidic (de-aerated) | $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ | Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) | **0.000** |
| Acidic (aerated) | $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) – Acid | **+1.229** |
| Neutral/Alkaline (aerated) | $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ | Oxygen Reduction Reaction (ORR) – Neutral | **+0.401** |
| Neutral (de-aerated) | $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ | Water Reduction | **−0.828** |
| Noble salt solution | $M^{z+} + ze^- \rightarrow M$ | Metal Deposition | varies |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Person Bucket Brigade"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy (Intuition Builder):**
> Imagine a leaking swimming pool (the corroding metal) being emptied by two workers using a **bucket brigade**.
>
> * The **anodic worker** is *scooping water out* of the pool — that is metal dissolving into ions. He is generating empty buckets (electrons).
> * The **cathodic worker** is *returning full buckets* (accepting electrons) by using them to extinguish a fire (reducing H⁺, O₂, water).
>
> The pool cannot drain any faster than the **slowest worker** can pass buckets. In corrosion, the **cathodic reaction is usually the rate-limiting (slowest) step**. If we slow down the cathodic worker (e.g., by reducing O₂ access), the entire pool drain-rate (corrosion rate) drops. This is exactly why *cathodic inhibitors* and *oxygen removal* are powerful corrosion-control strategies.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

* **Faraday's constant, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 26.8 Ah mol⁻¹)
* **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
* **Absolute temperature, T (K)** — at standard lab temperature (298 K): $RT/F = 0.02569$ V
* **Electron transfer coefficient, α** — dimensionless, typically 0.3 to 0.7
* **Standard reference electrode**: Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE), E° = **0.000 V**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Cathodic Reaction Characteristics

The cathodic half-cell behaves like a small **galvanic cell** of its own. When we force the metal to sit at a potential more negative than the cathodic reaction's equilibrium potential (E_cathode,eq), we have a *cathodic overpotential* η_c.

* The cathodic reaction is **thermodynamically driven** (E° is high for ORR, E° = 0 for HER) but is always **kinetically hindered** by an *activation energy barrier*.
* The magnitude of this barrier is what we measure as the **cathodic Tafel slope (β_c)**.
* In some cases (O₂ in stagnant water), the kinetics are so poor that the *rate* is limited not by the barrier, but by the **physical transport of reactant** to the surface. This is *concentration polarization*.

### 2.2 Three-Stage Cathodic Polarization Behavior

As the cathodic overpotential |η_c| increases, the system moves through three distinct regimes:

1. **Activation-Controlled Region** (small |η_c|, low current)
   * Rate governed by the activation energy barrier at the surface.
   * Butler-Volmer kinetics apply; Tafel behavior not yet established.

2. **Tafel Region** (intermediate |η_c|)
   * The back-reaction becomes negligible.
   * Linear relationship between η_c and log(i_c) — the famous Tafel straight line.

3. **Concentration-Controlled Region** (high |η_c|)
   * Reactant is consumed at the surface as fast as it can diffuse in.
   * Current saturates at the **limiting current density (i_L)** — a horizontal asymptote.
   * This is the *diffusion-limited* regime — common for O₂ reduction in quiescent electrolytes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for Cathodic Reaction Kinetics**

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Name | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_c = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c F \eta_c}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a F \eta_c}{RT}\right) \right]$ | **Butler-Volmer Equation** | $i_c$: cathodic current density (A/m²); $i_0$: exchange current density (A/m²); $\eta_c = E - E_{eq}$ (V); $\alpha_c + \alpha_a = 1$ | General; for any η_c. |
| 2 | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log\left(\dfrac{i_c}{i_0}\right)$ where $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_c F}$ | **Cathodic Tafel Equation** | $\beta_c$: cathodic Tafel slope (V/decade). At 25 °C, β_c ≈ 0.116 V/dec for α_c = 0.5 | High overpotential (η_c ≫ RT/F) |
| 3 | $\eta_c = a_c - b_c \log(i_c)$ where $a_c = -\beta_c \log(i_0)$, $b_c = \beta_c$ | **Empirical Tafel Form** | $a_c$, $b_c$ experimentally fitted | Engineering use |
| 4 | $i_L = \dfrac{n F D (C_b - C_s)}{\delta}$ | **Limiting Current Density (Nernst Diffusion Layer)** | n: electrons transferred; D: diffusivity (m²/s); C_b: bulk concentration (mol/m³); C_s: surface concentration (mol/m³); δ: diffusion layer thickness (m) | Concentration polarization |
| 5 | $i_L = \dfrac{n F D C_b}{\delta}$ | **Fully Depleted Surface Limit** | When C_s → 0 (reaction limited by mass transport) | At high η_c |
| 6 | $\eta_c^{conc} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ | **Concentration Overpotential** | Activation + concentration combined | For O₂ reduction |
| 7 | $E_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{\beta_a + \beta_c} \log\left(\dfrac{i_{0,c}}{i_{0,a}}\right) + \dfrac{\beta_a E_{eq,a} + \beta_c E_{eq,c}}{\beta_a + \beta_c}$ | **Corrosion Potential (Mixed)** | Used in Wagner–Traud mixed potential theory | KTU-favorite topic |
| 8 | $i_{corr} = i_{0,c} \exp\left[\dfrac{\alpha_c F (E_{corr} - E_{eq,c})}{RT}\right]$ | **Corrosion Current Density** | At E_corr, i_a = i_c = i_corr | Stern–Geary origin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pipe-syntax safeguard applied:** All absolute-value bars and "divides" use `\vert` or `\dfrac` instead of literal `|`. Your rendered KTU notes will never break the table layout.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Cathodic Protection Design (Impressed Current):** Sizing of anode current rating depends directly on i_L of the protected structure.
* **Cooling-Water System Design:** Deaerator effectiveness is rated by how much it suppresses i_L of ORR — directly proportional to O₂ concentration.
* **Boiler/Water-Tube Corrosion:** Acidic condensate pH shifts the dominant cathodic reaction from ORR to HER, dramatically changing protective strategies.
* **Soil-Corrosion Modeling:** Resistivity of soil controls δ (diffusion layer) → controls i_L for O₂ reduction → predicts buried-pipeline corrosion rate.
* **Pickling Inhibitor Selection:** Acid pickling inhibitors adsorb on the surface, raising the HER activation barrier (larger β_c), slowing corrosion without consuming the acid.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cathodic Tafel Equation from Butler-Volmer

We start from the **Butler-Volmer equation** for the cathodic branch:

$$ i_c = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta_c}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta_c}{RT}\right) \right] $$

**Step 1 — Identify the forward and reverse exponential terms.**
The first exponential represents the **forward (cathodic) reduction rate**, and the second represents the **reverse (anodic) oxidation rate** of the cathodic couple.

**Step 2 — Apply the high-overpotential assumption.**
For the cathodic reaction, η_c is large and negative. Hence:

$$ -\frac{\alpha_c F \eta_c}{RT} \gg 0 \quad \text{(forward term dominates)} $$

$$ \frac{\alpha_a F \eta_c}{RT} \ll 0 \quad \text{(reverse term becomes negligible)} $$

**Step 3 — Drop the negligible reverse term.**

$$ i_c \approx i_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta_c}{RT}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$ \ln(i_c) = \ln(i_0) - \frac{\alpha_c F \eta_c}{RT} $$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for η_c.**

$$ \eta_c = -\frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln(i_c) + \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln(i_0) $$

**Step 6 — Factor and group.**

$$ \eta_c = \frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \left[ \ln(i_0) - \ln(i_c) \right] = -\frac{RT}{\alpha_c F} \ln\!\left(\frac{i_c}{i_0}\right) $$

**Step 7 — Convert natural log to base-10 logarithm.**
Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$ \eta_c = -\frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_c F} \log\!\left(\frac{i_c}{i_0}\right) $$

**Step 8 — Define the cathodic Tafel slope.**

$$ \boxed{\;\beta_c \equiv \frac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha_c F} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta_c = -\beta_c \log\!\left(\frac{i_c}{i_0}\right) = \beta_c \log\!\left(\frac{i_0}{i_c}\right)\;} $$

**Step 9 — Numerical evaluation at T = 298 K.**
With R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹, T = 298 K, α_c = 0.5:

$$ \beta_c = \frac{(2.303)(8.314)(298)}{(0.5)(96{,}485)} \approx 0.118\ \text{V/decade} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A β_c of 0.118 V/decade means a **10× increase in cathodic current** requires only a **118 mV increase in overpotential**. Higher β_c ⇒ slower cathodic kinetics ⇒ often *more noble* (less reactive) metal surface.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cathodic Limiting Current Density (Steady-State Diffusion)

Consider O₂ reduction in a quiescent electrolyte, with the surface concentration $C_s$ lower than the bulk concentration $C_b$.

**Step 1 — Apply Fick's First Law at steady state.**
The molar flux J of dissolved O₂ toward the cathode surface is:

$$ J = -D\,\frac{dC}{dx} = D\,\frac{C_b - C_s}{\delta} $$

where δ is the thickness of the stagnant Nernst diffusion layer and D is the diffusion coefficient of O₂.

**Step 2 — Relate flux to current density.**
Each O₂ molecule accepts n = 4 electrons. By Faraday's law:

$$ i = n F J = n F D\,\frac{C_b - C_s}{\delta} $$

**Step 3 — Identify the limiting case.**
As the cathodic overpotential grows, the surface reaction becomes so fast that $C_s \rightarrow 0$. The flux reaches its maximum possible value, and the current saturates:

$$ \boxed{\;i_L = \frac{n F D C_b}{\delta}\;} $$

**Step 4 — Express the concentration overpotential.**
The surface concentration can be written as $C_s = C_b(1 - i/i_L)$. The Nernst equation at the cathode surface gives:

$$ E_{surface} = E_{eq} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln(C_s) = E_{eq} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left[C_b\!\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)\right] $$

Subtracting the bulk-equilibrium value yields the **concentration overpotential**:

$$ \boxed{\;\eta_{c}^{conc} = \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{i}{i_L}\right)\;} $$

**Step 5 — Limiting behavior check.**
As $i \rightarrow i_L$, the argument of the ln → 0, so $\eta_{c}^{conc} \rightarrow -\infty$: current **cannot exceed** $i_L$, confirming physical consistency.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Corrosion Current from Mixed-Potential Theory (Stern–Geary Foundation)

The net anodic and cathodic current densities at any potential E are (assuming Tafel behavior on both branches):

$$ i_a = i_{0,a}\exp\!\left[\frac{\alpha_a F (E - E_{eq,a})}{RT}\right] $$

$$ i_c = i_{0,c}\exp\!\left[-\frac{\alpha_c F (E - E_{eq,c})}{RT}\right] $$

**Step 1 — At E_corr, charge balance demands i_a = i_c = i_corr.**

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions and solve for E_corr.** Taking logarithms and rearranging:

$$ E_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{\beta_a + \beta_c} \log\!\left(\frac{i_{0,c}}{i_{0,a}}\right) + \frac{\beta_a E_{eq,a} + \beta_c E_{eq,c}}{\beta_a + \beta_c} $$

**Step 3 — Plug E_corr back into either Tafel expression to obtain i_corr:**

$$ i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303\,R_{p}\,(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \quad \text{where } R_p = \left.\frac{dE}{di}\right|_{E_{corr}} $$

This is the celebrated **Stern–Geary equation** — the theoretical basis of Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) corrosion rate measurement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cathodic Polarization Curve — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["METAL SURFACE AT E_corr"] --> B["Apply small cathodic polarization"]
    B --> C["Activation Control Region: Butler-Volmer kinetics govern rate"]
    C --> D["Increase cathodic overpotential to Tafel region"]
    D --> E["Tafel Region: linear eta vs log i; slope = beta_c"]
    E --> F["Approach limiting current: O2 transport becomes bottleneck"]
    F --> G["Mass Transport Control Region: surface reactant depleted"]
    G --> H["i approaches i_L (limiting current density) horizontal asymptote"]
    H --> I["Further increase eta: pure concentration polarization"]
    I --> J["No further current increase possible: i is capped at i_L"]

    style A fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    style C fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#000
    style E fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    style G fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    style J fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Cathodic Reaction Selection Logic — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Electrolyte Environment"] --> B{"pH of solution?"}
    B -->|pH less than 4: strongly acidic| C["HER: 2H+ + 2e- to H2"]
    B -->|pH 4 to 9: near neutral| D["ORR: O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-"]
    B -->|pH greater than 9: alkaline| E["ORR or Water Reduction"]
    C --> F{"Is O2 present?"}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -->|Yes| G["Mixed HER + ORR possible"]
    F -->|No| H["Only HER active; corrosion is severely slowed"]
    G --> I["Cathodic kinetics limited by O2 diffusion i_L"]
    H --> J["Cathodic kinetics limited by H+ discharge eta barrier"]

    style A fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    style C fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    style D fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    style E fill:#fde68a,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
    style I fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    style J fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Charge-Flow Architecture at a Corroding Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AN["ANODE REGION: M to M^n+ + n e- (oxidation)"] -->|"electrons travel through metal"| CA["CATHODE REGION"]
    CA --> ORR1["ORR consumes e-: O2 + 4H+ + 4e- to 2H2O"]
    CA --> HER["HER consumes e-: 2H+ + 2e- to H2"]
    CA --> ORR2["Neutral ORR: O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-"]
    ION["Ion migration in electrolyte balances charge"] --> AN
    ION --> CA

    style AN fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    style CA fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000
    style ION fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between the *hydrogen evolution reaction* (HER) and the *oxygen reduction reaction* (ORR) as cathodic processes in corrosion. State the standard potentials and the environmental conditions that favor each.

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured):**

* **HER (Hydrogen Evolution Reaction):** $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$, E° = **0.000 V vs SHE**. Predominant in **strongly acidic, de-aerated** environments (e.g., pickling baths, sour-service pipelines).
* **ORR (Oxygen Reduction Reaction):**
   * Acidic: $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$, E° = **+1.229 V vs SHE**
   * Neutral/alkaline: $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$, E° = **+0.401 V vs SHE**
   * Predominant in **near-neutral, aerated** environments (seawater, atmospheric corrosion, cooling water).
* **Key difference:** ORR has a much higher thermodynamic driving force (E°) but is **kinetically sluggish** due to O=O bond breaking (i_0 is 6–8 orders of magnitude lower than HER on most metals). [1 mark]

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *exchange current density* (i₀) for a cathodic reaction. Explain its significance in determining corrosion rate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

* **Definition:** The exchange current density is the **equal and opposite forward/reverse current density at the equilibrium potential** of the half-cell — i.e., the rate of oxidation equals the rate of reduction at E_eq. Units: A m⁻² or A cm⁻². [1 mark]
* **Physical meaning:** It is a direct measure of the **intrinsic kinetic activity** of the electrochemical couple. A large i₀ implies a *fast*, *reversible* couple; a small i₀ implies a *slow*, *polarizable* couple. [1 mark]
* **Corrosion significance:** When the cathodic i₀ is **small**, the cathodic polarization curve is *steep* (in η vs i space), shifting E_corr to more negative values and *lowering* i_corr. Hence, *inhibiting* the cathodic reaction (lowering i₀) reduces the overall corrosion rate. [1 mark]

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the cathodic polarization curve for an oxygen-reduction-controlled system. Clearly label the activation-controlled, Tafel, and concentration-controlled regions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** A buried steel pipeline is cathodically polarized in a de-aerated, near-neutral soil. The cathodic Tafel slope β_c = 0.120 V/decade and the exchange current density of the ORR couple on steel is i_{0,c} = 1 × 10⁻⁷ A/m². The applied cathodic overpotential is η_c = −0.300 V. Calculate the cathodic current density. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Sketch (3 marks):** Plot η (cathodic, negative upward) on y-axis vs log(i) on x-axis.
   * Region 1: small |η|, **curved** — Butler-Volmer non-linear region.
   * Region 2: linear Tafel straight line with slope β_c.
   * Region 3: at high |η|, the curve **bends to a horizontal plateau** at i = i_L.
2. **Activation region:** Cathodic reaction rate limited by activation energy barrier; Tafel behavior not yet reached. [1 mark]
3. **Tafel region:** Linear relation $\eta_c = \beta_c \log(i_0/i_c)$; slope = −β_c on (log i) axis. [1 mark]
4. **Concentration region:** O₂ supply becomes rate-limiting; current saturates at i_L = nFD C_b / δ. [1 mark]
5. **Engineering significance:** Real corrosion systems often operate near i_L, making *deaeration* an effective cathodic-control strategy. [1 mark]

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** β_c = 0.120 V/decade, i_{0,c} = 1 × 10⁻⁷ A/m², η_c = −0.300 V (cathodic).

**Step 1 — Apply the cathodic Tafel equation.**

$$ \eta_c = \beta_c \log\!\left(\frac{i_{0,c}}{i_c}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for log(i_c).**

$$ \log(i_c) = \log(i_{0,c}) - \frac{\eta_c}{\beta_c} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$ \log(i_c) = \log(1 \times 10^{-7}) - \frac{(-0.300)}{0.120} $$

$$ \log(i_c) = -7 - (-2.5) = -7 + 2.5 = -4.5 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for i_c.**

$$ \boxed{i_c = 10^{-4.5}\ \text{A/m}^2 \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A/m}^2} $$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
At η_c = −300 mV, the cathodic current density is ~3.16 × 10⁻⁵ A/m², which corresponds (via Faraday's law) to a corrosion rate of:

$$ \text{CR} = \frac{i_c \cdot M}{n F \rho} = \frac{(3.16 \times 10^{-5})(55.85)}{(2)(96{,}485)(7850)} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m/s} \approx 0.37\ \text{mm/year} $$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the cathodic Tafel equation from the Butler-Volmer equation. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** A zinc sacrificial anode is used to cathodically protect a steel pipe buried in soil. The cathodic reduction is ORR. The diffusion coefficient of O₂ in soil-water is D = 2.1 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s, the bulk O₂ concentration is C_b = 8.5 × 10⁻³ mol/m³, the diffusion layer thickness is δ = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m, and the number of electrons transferred is n = 4. Determine the **limiting current density** for the cathodic reaction. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Starting equation (1 mark):** Write Butler-Volmer for cathodic current.
2. **High-overpotential assumption (1 mark):** State that |η_c| ≫ RT/F, so the reverse term is negligible.
3. **Drop reverse term, take ln (2 marks):** Show the algebraic steps.
4. **Convert to log₁₀, define β_c (2 marks):** Final form $\eta_c = \beta_c \log(i_0/i_c)$ with $\beta_c = 2.303 RT/(\alpha_c F)$.
5. **Numerical evaluation (1 mark):** At 298 K, α_c = 0.5, β_c ≈ 0.118 V/decade.

*(See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** D = 2.1 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s, C_b = 8.5 × 10⁻³ mol/m³, δ = 0.5 × 10⁻³ m, n = 4, F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Apply the limiting current density equation** (assuming fully depleted surface, C_s → 0):

$$ i_L = \frac{n F D C_b}{\delta} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$ i_L = \frac{(4)(96{,}485)(2.1 \times 10^{-9})(8.5 \times 10^{-3})}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$ \text{Numerator} = 4 \times 96{,}485 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-9} \times 8.5 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ = 4 \times 96{,}485 \times 1.785 \times 10^{-11} = 4 \times 1.722 \times 10^{-6} = 6.888 \times 10^{-6} $$

**Step 4 — Divide by δ.**

$$ i_L = \frac{6.888 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.378 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{A/m}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Final answer and interpretation.**

$$ \boxed{i_L \approx 1.38 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{A/m}^2 \;\;(\approx 13.8\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2)} $$

> **Engineering interpretation:** The sacrificial anode must supply at least 13.8 μA per cm² of pipe surface to fully suppress the ORR — the design engineer sizes the anode count and spacing accordingly to deliver this current density at the most distant (worst-case) point on the pipeline.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Cathodic Reaction Problems**
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** η_c is *negative* for a cathodic overpotential. Forgetting the negative sign will give i_c < i_0 (a contradiction since current should grow).
> 2. **Units mismatch in i_L:** D must be in m²/s, C_b in mol/m³, δ in m. Mixing cm and m is a top-3 reason for losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Tafel validity check:** Always verify |η_c| ≫ RT/F (≈ 25.7 mV at 25 °C). If η_c is only 10–20 mV, the Tafel equation is **invalid** — use the full Butler-Volmer instead.
> 4. **Confusing ORR-acid and ORR-neutral:** Both use n = 4, but E° and reaction form differ. KTU examiners test this in Part A.
> 5. **Dropping units in the final answer:** Always write A/m² (or A/cm²) explicitly. Examiners deduct for unit-less answers.
> 6. **Stern–Geary misuse:** The Stern–Geary equation is for *linear polarization resistance (LPR)* measurement, not Tafel extrapolation. Do not mix.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cathodic Reaction Characteristics (Module 1)**

* 🔹 A **cathodic reaction is a reduction** — it consumes electrons and balances the anodic metal-dissolution reaction.
* 🔹 The **two dominant cathodic reactions** in corrosion are **HER** (acidic, de-aerated) and **ORR** (neutral/alkaline, aerated).
* 🔹 **Butler-Volmer equation** is the *fundamental* kinetic expression; **Tafel** is its *high-overpotential* limit.
* 🔹 **β_c (cathodic Tafel slope)** = 2.303 RT / (α_c F); at 25 °C and α_c = 0.5, β_c ≈ **0.118 V/decade**.
* 🔹 **Exchange current density (i₀)** quantifies the *intrinsic reversibility* of the cathodic couple — low i₀ means sluggish kinetics, which *protects* the metal.
* 🔹 **Limiting current density (i_L) = nFDC_b/δ** is the *mass-transport ceiling* on cathodic current — engineering controls aim to *reduce* C_b (deaeration) or *increase* δ (stagnant boundary layer).
* 🔹 The **corrosion potential (E_corr)** lies between the anodic and cathodic equilibrium potentials, weighted by their respective β values and i₀ values.
* 🔹 **Stern–Geary** links polarization resistance R_p to i_corr — the foundation of *Linear Polarization Resistance* corrosion-rate probes.
* 🔹 For O₂ reduction in **stagnant** electrolytes, the cathodic reaction is usually *diffusion-limited* (i ≈ i_L).
* 🔹 For H⁺ reduction in **stirred acid**, the cathodic reaction is usually *activation-limited* (Tafel behavior).
* 🔹 **Cathodic inhibitors** raise the cathodic activation barrier (increase β_c) and/or lower i_{0,c} — both slow the corrosion rate.
* 🔹 KTU 2024 Scheme **favors numerical problems** on: (i) i_c from Tafel, (ii) i_L from diffusion, (iii) i_corr via Stern–Geary. Practice all three types.
* 🔹 Always state **assumptions** (Tafel region vs. full B-V; stirred vs. stagnant; isothermal) in exam answers — examiners reward them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Corrosion Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Corrosion Cells**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Corrosion Cell

A **corrosion cell** is a short-circuited electrochemical system consisting of four essential components that together sustain the spontaneous oxidation (corrosion) of a metal. The cell is essentially a **galvanic (voltaic) cell** that has been deliberately or accidentally short-circuited, so that the chemical energy liberated by the oxidation reaction is dissipated as heat at the metal surface rather than as useful electrical work.

The four mandatory components (often remembered by the acronym **ACME**) are:

1. **Anode** – the metal surface site where oxidation occurs (M → M^n+ + n e^-).
2. **Cathode** – an electronically connected surface site where reduction occurs (e.g., O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- → 4OH^-).
3. **Electrolyte** – an ionically conducting medium (e.g., salt water, moist soil, acidic condensate) that closes the ionic circuit between anode and cathode.
4. **Metallic path / Electronic conductor** – the bulk metal itself (or a separate wire/contact) that closes the electronic circuit, allowing electrons to flow from anode to cathode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In corrosion, the term "cell" does **not** refer to a battery that supplies power to an external load. The corrosion cell is **internally short-circuited**, so the electrons produced at the anode travel *through the metal* to the cathode, doing no external work. This is the foundational distinction students must internalise.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **leaking water tank** placed on a tilted wooden plank. Water (the "electrolyte current") flows from the high point to the low point because of a height difference. The tank is the *anode* (losing material), the ground/wet patch is the *cathode* (receiving the flow), the pipe connecting them is the *metallic path*, and gravity in the water is the *driving potential*. If the plank were perfectly level, no water would flow — likewise, a corrosion cell needs a **potential difference** between anode and cathode for the reaction to proceed.
