In corrosion, this "height difference" is the **electrode potential difference** between two dissimilar regions, and the "water" is the **ionic current** flowing through the electrolyte.

## 1.3 Why Corrosion Cells Are Important to Engineers

Every form of wet corrosion — from a rusty garden gate to pitting in a marine propeller to stress-corrosion cracking in a nuclear steam generator — can be traced back to one of a small number of identifiable *cell types*. Recognising the cell type is the first step in selecting the correct **control method** (coating, cathodic protection, alloying, inhibitor dosing, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric / Convention:** Electrode potentials in corrosion science are universally referenced to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**, assigned a value of **0.000 V** at 25 °C, 1 atm H_2, and unit H^+ activity. Practical measurements in the field use the **Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) = +0.242 V vs SHE** or **Copper/Copper Sulphate (CSE) = +0.318 V vs SHE** as more robust references.

---

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Corrosion Cells

Corrosion cells are classified by **what causes the anode and cathode to be at different potentials** on the metal surface. The KTU 2024 scheme recognises the following principal types:

| # | Cell Type | Driving Force (Source of EMF) |
|---|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | Dissimilar-Metal (Galvanic) Cell | Difference in composition / standard potential of two metals |
| 2 | Concentration Cell | Difference in ion concentration at the metal/electrolyte interface |
| 3 | Differential Aeration Cell | Difference in dissolved O_2 concentration |
| 4 | Differential Temperature Cell | Difference in metal/electrolyte temperature |
| 5 | Differential Salt-Concentration Cell | Difference in electrolyte salinity |
| 6 | Stress / Strain Cell | Difference in elastic strain energy between cold-worked and annealed regions |
| 7 | Active–Passive Cell | Difference between active (corroding) and passive (oxide-film-covered) zones on the *same* alloy |
| 8 | Combined / Composite Cells | Two or more of the above operating simultaneously |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **all** the above cell types, the *anode is the region of lower (more negative) electrode potential* and the *cathode is the region of higher (more positive) electrode potential*. This is the universal rule, regardless of what physical/chemical factor created the potential difference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Corrosion Cell Logic (Step-by-Step)

The thermodynamic driving force for any corrosion cell is the **Gibbs free energy change**, $\Delta G$, of the overall redox reaction. The chain of logic for *every* corrosion cell is:

1. Two regions on a metal (or two metals in contact) acquire **different electrode potentials** $E_a$ (anode) and $E_c$ (cathode).
2. The **EMF of the cell** is $E_{\text{cell}} = E_c - E_a$. If $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$, the reaction is spontaneous.
3. When the cell is short-circuited (i.e., the two regions are electronically connected through the metal), current flows: electrons drift from anode to cathode *inside* the metal; ions drift through the electrolyte.
4. At the **anode**, the metal dissolves (M → M^n+ + n e^-), generating electrons.
5. At the **cathode**, a reduction reaction consumes those electrons. The most common cathodic reactions in neutral/alkaline aerated water are:
   - **Oxygen reduction (alkaline):** O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- → 4OH^- $\quad (E^\circ = +0.401\ \text{V vs SHE})$
   - **Oxygen reduction (acidic):** O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- → 2H_2O $\quad (E^\circ = +1.229\ \text{V vs SHE})$
   - **Hydrogen evolution:** 2H^+ + 2e^- → H_2 $\quad (E^\circ = 0.000\ \text{V vs SHE})$
6. The magnitude of corrosion current $I_{\text{corr}}$ is set by **kinetic polarisation**, not by thermodynamics. The mixed-potential theory (Evans diagram) gives $I_{\text{corr}}$ at the intersection of the anodic and cathodic polarisation curves.

## 2.2 Type-by-Type Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Dissimilar-Metal (Galvanic) Cell

Two metals with different standard electrode potentials are placed in electrical contact in the **same electrolyte**. The more *active* (more negative $E^\circ$) metal becomes the **anode** and corrodes preferentially; the more *noble* metal becomes the **cathode** and is protected.

- Classic example: **Fe–Cu** in salt water. Fe ($E^\circ = -0.440$ V) corrodes; Cu ($E^\circ = +0.337$ V) is cathodically protected.
- The **Galvanic Series in seawater** (a practical, non-standard ordering) supersedes the standard EMF series for design, because it accounts for **passivation effects** of real alloys.

### 2.2.2 Concentration Cell (Ion-Concentration Cell)

The metal is **chemically identical** at both electrodes, but the **electrolyte concentration** differs. The Nernst equation dictates that the electrode in the **dilute** solution becomes the **anode** (more negative potential) and corrodes.

- Example: Cu rod in 0.01 M CuSO_4 vs same Cu rod in 1.0 M CuSO_4 — the dilute-side Cu dissolves.

### 2.2.3 Differential Aeration Cell

A special, *extremely common* form of concentration cell where the differing species is **dissolved oxygen**. The region with **lower O_2** (oxygen-starved, often under a crevice, dirt deposit, gasket, or waterline just below the meniscus) becomes the **anode** and corrodes.

- Example: **Crevice corrosion** under a rubber gasket on a stainless-steel flange; **waterline attack** on a partially immersed steel plate; **pitting under a barnacle** on a ship's hull.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why low-O_2 regions become anodic:** According to the Nernst equation, the reversible O_2/H_2O couple becomes *less positive* as [O_2] falls. A less positive cathodic reaction makes the local mixed potential shift negative, so the low-O_2 site must become more anodic to balance the system.

### 2.2.4 Differential Temperature Cell

Two identical electrodes in the same electrolyte but at **different temperatures**. The electrode in the **hotter** solution is the **anode** for most metals (Zn, Fe, Cu) in aerated near-neutral solutions. This is the principle behind **heat-affected corrosion** in heat exchangers and boilers.

### 2.2.5 Differential Salt-Concentration Cell

A special concentration cell where the differing species is the **bulk salt** (e.g., NaCl). The metal in the **more dilute** electrolyte is the anode. This drives the classic **"piling corrosion"** of steel sheet piles driven into soils of varying salinity.

### 2.2.6 Stress / Strain Cell

Cold-worked (or plastically deformed) regions of a metal have a higher **internal strain energy** and a more negative potential than the same metal in its annealed state. The strained region becomes the anode. Drives **stress-corrosion cracking (SCC)** and **corrosion-fatigue** initiation.

### 2.2.7 Active–Passive Cell

Highly relevant for **stainless steels, Al, Ti, and Ni alloys**. A small break in the passive oxide film (e.g., by Cl^- attack or mechanical scratch) creates a tiny active anode surrounded by a large passive cathode — the area ratio drives **pitting** to extreme intensities.

> [!WARNING]
> **Active-passive cells are uniquely dangerous** because the cathode-to-anode area ratio can easily exceed 1000:1. By Faraday's law, the corrosion current density at the tiny anode is therefore enormous, producing deep pits in days rather than years.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipe `|` symbols appear in this table.** Where mathematical magnitude is required, the LaTeX vertical bar is written as `\vert` so the markdown table is not broken.

| # | Equation / Law | LaTeX Form | Meaning / When to Use | Unit |
|---|----------------|------------|------------------------|------|
| 1 | Cell EMF (thermodynamic) | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_c - E_a$ | Driving voltage of any corrosion cell | V |
| 2 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | Electrode potential away from standard state | V |
| 3 | Nernst at 25 °C | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0592}{n}\log_{10} Q$ | Numerical short form (25 °C / 298 K) | V |
| 4 | Gibbs–EMF link | $\Delta G = -nFE_{\text{cell}}$ | Spontaneity criterion; $\Delta G < 0$ ⇒ corrosion proceeds | J mol^{-1} |
| 5 | Faraday's Law (mass loss) | $w = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal lost by anodic current $I$ over time $t$ | g |
| 6 | Faraday's Law (corrosion rate) | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot i \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate in mils per year from current density $i$ ($\mu$A/cm²) | mpy |
| 7 | Concentration cell EMF | $E = \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\dfrac{a_1}{a_2}$ | Two identical electrodes, two different ion activities | V |
| 8 | Differential aeration O_2 effect | $E_{\text{high O}_2} - E_{\text{low O}_2} = \dfrac{0.0592}{4}\log\dfrac{[O_2]_{\text{high}}}{[O_2]_{\text{low}}}$ | EMF between aerated and oxygen-starved regions on the same metal | V |
| 9 | Tafel Equation | $\eta = \pm \beta \log\dfrac{i}{i_0}$ | Activation overpotential; $\beta$ = Tafel slope, $i_0$ = exchange c.d. | V |
| 10 | Reference conversions | $E_{\text{SHE}} = E_{\text{SCE}} + 0.242\ \text{V}$ ; $\ E_{\text{SHE}} = E_{\text{CSE}} + 0.318\ \text{V}$ | Convert lab/field readings to SHE | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols key:** $R = 8.314$ J mol^{-1} K^{-1}, $F = 96\,485$ C mol^{-1} (≈ 96 500 C mol^{-1}), $T$ in K, $n$ = electrons transferred per atom, $M$ = molar mass (g mol^{-1}), $\rho$ = metal density (g cm^{-3}), $i$ = current density (A cm^{-2}).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recognising the cell type is the first step in applying the **correct countermeasure**:

| Cell Type | Typical Countermeasure |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Dissimilar-metal | Isolation gaskets, insulating sleeves, painting the *cathode* (never the anode), avoid small-anode/large-cathode geometry |
| Concentration | Agitation, flow design, eliminate stagnant zones |
| Differential aeration | Remove deposits, design full-immersed or fully-dry, use sealed crevice-free joints |
| Temperature | Equalise flow, use cooler inlet zones, avoid heat-stagnation pockets |
| Salt-concentration | Use homogeneous backfill, avoid soil-to-water transitions on buried steel |
| Stress | Stress-relief anneal, shot peening (puts surface in compression), avoid sustained tensile + corrosive environment |
| Active–passive | Use higher-Cr/Mo alloys, maintain cleanliness, control Cl^- and O_2, apply cathodic protection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives the **EMF driving force** for the two cell types most frequently asked in KTU Module-1 questions: the **concentration cell** and the **differential aeration cell**. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly so the student can reproduce it under examination conditions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: EMF of an Ion-Concentration Cell

**System:** A single metal M (valency $n$) immersed in two beakers of its own salt solution of activities $a_1$ and $a_2$, joined by a salt bridge, with the metal shorted externally. Let $a_1 > a_2$.

**Step 1 — Write the half-cell Nernst expression for the metal/metal-ion couple.**

$$
E_{\text{M}^{n+}/\text{M}} = E^\circ_{\text{M}^{n+}/\text{M}} - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{1}{a_{\text{M}^{n+}}}
$$

**Step 2 — State the EMF as the difference between the two half-cell potentials.**

For a cell in which the dilute side is the anode, the cell EMF is

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode (conc)}} - E_{\text{anode (dilute)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the two Nernst expressions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} &= \left(E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{1}{a_1}\right) - \left(E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{1}{a_2}\right) \\
&= -\frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{1}{a_1} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{1}{a_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Use $\ln(1/a) = -\ln a$ to simplify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} &= -\frac{RT}{nF}(-\ln a_1) + \frac{RT}{nF}(-\ln a_2) \\
&= \frac{RT}{nF}\ln a_1 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln a_2 \\
&= \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{a_1}{a_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to base-10 logarithm at 25 °C.**

Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$ and $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$, $F = 96\,485$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96\,485}\,\log_{10}\frac{a_1}{a_2} = \frac{0.0592}{n}\,\log_{10}\frac{a_1}{a_2}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical check with a worked example.**

A copper concentration cell with $[Cu^{2+}]_1 = 1.0$ M and $[Cu^{2+}]_2 = 0.01$ M, $n = 2$:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0592}{2}\log_{10}\frac{1.0}{0.01} = 0.0296 \times 2 = 0.0592\ \text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The cell voltage of 0.0592 V is small but sufficient to drive measurable corrosion currents when the cell is shorted by the metal itself. In real buried-pipeline situations, a few tens of millivolts of differential aeration across the pipe length can drive serious localised attack.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: EMF of a Differential Aeration Cell

**System:** The *same* metal M in *the same* electrolyte but with two different dissolved-oxygen concentrations $[O_2]_1$ and $[O_2]_2$, with $[O_2]_1 > [O_2]_2$.

**Step 1 — Identify the cathodic half-reaction in near-neutral aerated water.**

$$
\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \quad (n = 4)
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Nernst expression for this oxygen electrode.**

$$
E_{\text{cathode}} = E^\circ_{\text{O}_2/\text{OH}^-} - \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\frac{a_{\text{OH}^-}^4}{p_{\text{O}_2}\cdot a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}^2}
$$

Assuming $a_{\text{OH}^-}$, $a_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$, pH are **constant** at the two sites, the only variable is $p_{\text{O}_2}$ (proportional to dissolved [O_2]):

$$
E_{\text{cathode}} = E' + \frac{RT}{4F}\ln[\text{O}_2]
$$

where $E'$ bundles the constants. Thus a **higher** O_2 concentration gives a **more positive** (nobler) cathode potential.

**Step 3 — Subtract the potentials of the two regions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} &= E_{\text{high O}_2} - E_{\text{low O}_2} \\
&= \left(E' + \frac{RT}{4F}\ln[\text{O}_2]_1\right) - \left(E' + \frac{RT}{4F}\ln[\text{O}_2]_2\right) \\
&= \frac{RT}{4F}\ln\frac{[\text{O}_2]_1}{[\text{O}_2]_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical form at 25 °C.**

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0592}{4}\log_{10}\frac{[\text{O}_2]_1}{[\text{O}_2]_2} = 0.0148 \log_{10}\frac{[\text{O}_2]_1}{[\text{O}_2]_2}
$$

**Step 5 — Quick numerical example.** If the aerated region has 8 ppm dissolved O_2 and the crevice has 0.5 ppm:

$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 0.0148 \log_{10}\frac{8}{0.5} = 0.0148 \times \log_{10}(16) = 0.0148 \times 1.204 \approx 0.0178\ \text{V}
$$

This 17.8 mV is enough to drive pitting in stainless steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **EMF of a differential aeration cell is independent of the metal**; it depends only on the O_2 ratio. The metal merely provides the *kinetic pathway*. This is why differential aeration corrosion occurs on Fe, Cu, Ni, Zn, and stainless steel alike.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Predicting Anode and Cathode

This matrix should be memorised — it is a high-frequency KTU short-answer requirement.

| Cell Type | Which side becomes the ANODE? | Which side becomes the CATHODE? |
|-----------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Dissimilar-metal (galvanic) | The more active (lower $E^\circ$) metal | The more noble (higher $E^\circ$) metal |
| Ion-concentration | Dilute electrolyte side | Concentrated electrolyte side |
| Differential aeration | Low O_2 region (under deposit / crevice) | High O_2 region (open, aerated) |
| Differential temperature (most metals in aerated water) | Hot side | Cold side |
| Salt-concentration | Dilute-salt (low conductivity) side | Concentrated-salt side |
| Stress / strain | Plastically deformed (cold-worked) region | Annealed, stress-free region |
| Active–passive | Active site (film break, Cl^- pit) | Passive film-covered surroundings |
| Composite / combined | Determined by *sum* of all EMF contributions | Determined by the opposite of the above |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Practice Style)

**Problem:** A differential aeration cell is set up on a mild-steel plate immersed in 0.5 M NaCl at 25 °C. The aerated zone has 8 mg L^{-1} of dissolved O_2 and the shielded zone has 1 mg L^{-1}. Calculate the cell EMF and state which zone corrodes.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cell}} &= \frac{0.0592}{4}\log_{10}\frac{[\text{O}_2]_{\text{high}}}{[\text{O}_2]_{\text{low}}} \\
&= 0.0148 \log_{10}\frac{8}{1} \\
&= 0.0148 \times 0.9031 \\
&\approx 0.0134\ \text{V} = 13.4\ \text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the cell EMF is **positive**, the cell reaction is spontaneous. The **low-O_2 (shielded) zone** is the anode and will corrode, while the **high-O_2 (open) zone** is the cathode.

This 13 mV is the same order of magnitude as the breakdown potential of passive films on many alloys — sufficient to initiate and sustain crevice or pitting attack.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Corrosion Cells

The following Mermaid block gives a publication-quality classification of all cell types covered in Module 1, with the driving EMF source for each.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[CORROSION CELLS Module 1] --> rootB[Driven by EMF difference]
    rootB --> nodeA1[Dissimilar Metal Cell]
    rootB --> nodeA2[Concentration Cells]
    rootB --> nodeA3[Physical State Cells]
    rootB --> nodeA4[Mechanical State Cells]
    rootB --> nodeA5[Surface State Cells]

    nodeA1 --> nodeB1[Two different metals in same electrolyte]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1[Example: Fe and Cu in seawater]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[Fe is anode and corrodes]

    nodeA2 --> nodeB2[Ion Concentration]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB3[Differential Aeration]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB4[Salt Concentration]
    nodeA2 --> nodeB5[Differential Temperature]

    nodeB2 --> nodeC2[Same metal two different ion activities]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD2[Dilute side is anode]
    nodeB3 --> nodeC3[Same metal two different O2 levels]
    nodeC3 --> nodeD3[Low O2 side is anode]
    nodeB4 --> nodeC4[Two different bulk salinities]
    nodeC4 --> nodeD4[Lower salinity side is anode]
    nodeB5 --> nodeC5[Two different temperatures]
    nodeC5 --> nodeD5[Hotter side is anode in aerated water]

    nodeA3 --> nodeB6[Identical metal but different T or velocity or phase]
    nodeB6 --> nodeD6[Crevice deposit cell under gasket]

    nodeA4 --> nodeB7[Stress or strain difference on same metal]
    nodeB7 --> nodeC7[Anode is plastically deformed zone]
    nodeC7 --> nodeD7[Drives SCC and corrosion fatigue]

    nodeA5 --> nodeB8[Active versus passive regions on same alloy]
    nodeB8 --> nodeC8[Film break becomes tiny anode]
    nodeC8 --> nodeD8[Surrounding passive film is large cathode]
    nodeD8 --> nodeE8[Area ratio drives intense pitting]

    rootA --> nodeZ[Combined Cells]
    nodeZ --> nodeY[Two or more cell types act simultaneously]
    nodeY --> nodeX[Most engineering failures are combined cells]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic Corrosion Cell

The block diagram below shows the **four mandatory components** and the **two current loops** (ionic and electronic) that must close for any corrosion cell to operate.

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockA[ANODE: Metal M dissolves] -->|Electrons flow in metal| blockB[METALLIC PATH: Bulk metal or wire]
    blockB -->|Electrons arrive| blockC[CATHODE: Reduction reaction]
    blockC -->|Ions migrate through liquid| blockD[ELECTROLYTE: Ionic conductor]
    blockD -->|Metal ions and charge carriers| blockA

    blockX[External Driver of EMF] --> blockA
    blockX --> blockC
    blockX -.Examples.-> blockY[Composition / O2 / T / Stress / Salt / Ion activity]
```

## 4.3 Evans-Type Polarisation Diagram (Schematic Logic)

Although the actual Evans diagram is an $E$–$\log i$ plot, the following Mermaid flow conveys the **mixed-potential logic** by which the corrosion current is determined.

```mermaid
graph TD
    pA[Start: Two half reactions in same electrolyte] --> pB[Anodic polarisation curve of metal dissolution]
    pA --> pC[Cathodic polarisation curve of reduction reaction]
    pB --> pD[Anodic curve rises with positive overpotential]
    pC --> pE[Cathodic curve falls with negative overpotential]
    pD --> pF[Curves intersect at one point]
    pE --> pF
    pF --> pG[Intersection defines Ecorr and icorr]
    pG --> pH[Ecorr is the mixed potential of the cell]
    pG --> pI[icorr controls the rate of metal loss by Faraday law]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Evans diagram is **kinetic**, not thermodynamic. It explains *why* a corrosion cell with even a small EMF (a few millivolts, as in differential aeration) can nevertheless produce a substantial corrosion current — because the polarisation resistances of the two half-reactions at the intersection can be quite low.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a corrosion cell. List its four essential components and state the function of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A corrosion cell is a short-circuited electrochemical system in which the spontaneous oxidation of a metal takes place by coupled anodic and cathodic half-reactions occurring at different sites on the metal surface, with the current flowing internally.

The four essential components are:

1. **Anode** – the surface region where metal is oxidised: M → M^n+ + ne^-.
2. **Cathode** – the electronically connected region where a reduction half-reaction consumes the electrons.
3. **Electrolyte** – the ionically conducting medium that carries the ionic current between the two sites.
4. **Metallic path** – the bulk metal (or an external wire/contact) that carries the electrons from anode to cathode.

*(1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for naming and explaining the four components — examiner pattern: half-mark for the list, half-mark each for brief functional notes.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a *dissimilar-metal cell* and a *concentration cell*, citing one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Dissimilar-Metal Cell | Concentration Cell |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Electrode materials | Two *different* metals | The *same* metal at both electrodes |
| Source of EMF | Difference in standard electrode potential $E^\circ$ | Difference in ion activity at the metal/electrolyte interface (Nernst effect) |
| Which is the anode | The more active (lower $E^\circ$) metal | The electrode in the more dilute electrolyte |
| Example | Fe–Cu couple in salt water; brass impeller on a steel shaft | Crevice corrosion under a gasket (O_2 difference) or piling corrosion in soils of variable salinity |

*(Full 3 marks for a clear two-column distinction plus one example each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the mechanism of a **differential aeration cell**. State which region becomes the anode and justify the answer using the Nernst equation.
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** A steel pipe is buried in soil where the dissolved-oxygen concentration varies from 9 mg L^{-1} (aerated topsoil) to 1 mg L^{-1} (compacted clay underneath). Calculate the EMF that drives the differential aeration corrosion cell at 25 °C. The relevant cathodic reaction is O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- → 4OH^-. Indicate which part of the pipe will suffer corrosion.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Mechanism of a differential aeration cell [7 marks]**

1. **Sketch** (2 marks) – Show a metal plate partly covered by a deposit/gasket. Label the O_2-rich region and the O_2-starved region. Draw electron flow in the metal and ion flow in the electrolyte.

2. **Mechanism** (3 marks) – The Nernst potential of the O_2/OH^- couple becomes *more positive* with increasing [O_2]. The high-O_2 region therefore acts as the *cathode*, while the low-O_2 region (deprived of oxidant) is forced to be the *anode* and corrodes.

3. **Nernst justification** (2 marks) – 
$$
E = E^\circ + \frac{0.0592}{4}\log[\text{O}_2]
$$
A decrease in [O_2] lowers the local cathodic potential, making the area more anodic.

**(b) Numerical EMF calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 – Identify the cathodic reaction.** The O_2/OH^- couple with $n = 4$ (1 mark).

**Step 2 – Apply the differential aeration EMF formula** (2 marks):
$$
E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0592}{4}\log_{10}\frac{[\text{O}_2]_{\text{high}}}{[\text{O}_2]_{\text{low}}}
$$

**Step 3 – Substitute values** (2 marks):
$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 0.0148 \log_{10}\frac{9}{1} = 0.0148 \times 0.9542
$$

**Step 4 – Evaluate** (1 mark):
$$
E_{\text{cell}} = 0.01412\ \text{V} \approx 14.1\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 5 – Conclusion** (1 mark): The cell EMF is **positive (~14 mV)**, hence the reaction is spontaneous. The **low-O_2 region under the clay (1 mg L^{-1})** acts as the **anode** and will suffer corrosion. The aerated topsoil region is the cathode and is protected.

---

> **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] — OR alternative to Question A**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **dissimilar-metal (galvanic) corrosion cell** with reference to the **Galvanic Series in seawater**. Explain how the **cathode-to-anode area ratio** affects the severity of attack, with one industrial example.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A small brass (Cu–Zn) component is accidentally connected to a large mild-steel pipe carrying seawater. The standard potentials are $E^\circ_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.337$ V and $E^\circ_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.440$ V (both vs SHE). (i) Identify which metal corrodes. (ii) Using Faraday's law, estimate the corrosion current required to remove 5 g of metal per day from the corroding member, assuming $M_{\text{Fe}} = 55.85$ g mol^{-1}, $n = 2$, and $F = 96\,485$ C mol^{-1}.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dissimilar-metal (galvanic) cell [7 marks]**

1. **Definition and sketch** (2 marks) – Two different metals in electrical contact immersed in a common electrolyte. Show the two electrodes, the metallic short, and the ionic current loop.

2. **Galvanic Series in seawater** (2 marks) – The series is an *empirical* ordering of alloys by their actual corrosion potentials in seawater; it is preferred over the EMF series for design because it accounts for passivation. From active (anodic) to noble (cathodic): Mg → Zn → Al → Mild Steel → Cu-Ni → Cu → Ni-Cu Alloy → Ti → Graphite → Pt. (Any partial list earns credit.)

3. **Area ratio effect** (2 marks) – The corrosion current is distributed over the anode area. A small anode + large cathode (e.g., brass rivet in a steel plate) means very high anodic current density → severe pitting. Reverse geometry (large anode + small cathode) is much less damaging.

4. **Industrial example** (1 mark) – e.g., copper plumbing connected directly to a galvanised iron tank causes accelerated zinc dissolution; or a brass valve in a steel pipeline.

**(b) Numerical identification and Faraday calculation [7 marks]**

**(i) Which metal corrodes? [2 marks]**

The more negative standard potential corrodes. $E^\circ_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.440$ V is far more negative than $+0.337$ V for Cu. Therefore **iron (the steel pipe) is the anode and corrodes**; the brass component is the cathode. *(Plus a clear mention of the adverse area ratio: small brass cathode + large steel anode is *less* severe than the reverse.)*

**(ii) Corrosion current for 5 g/day of iron [5 marks]**

**Step 1 – State Faraday's law** (1 mark):
$$
w = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}
$$

**Step 2 – Convert time to seconds** (1 mark):
$$
t = 1\ \text{day} = 24 \times 3600 = 86\,400\ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 – Rearrange and substitute** (2 marks):
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{w \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 2 \times 96\,485}{55.85 \times 86\,400} \\
&= \frac{964\,850}{4\,825\,440} \\
&\approx 0.200\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – State the result with units and physical meaning** (1 mark):
$$
I_{\text{corr}} \approx 0.200\ \text{A} = 200\ \text{mA}
$$

This is the steady anodic current that must flow for the steel to lose 5 g of iron per day through galvanic coupling with the brass.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "galvanic" with "galvanised".** *Galvanic* refers to a corrosion mechanism between two dissimilar metals; *galvanised* refers to a zinc coating. Examiners deduct one full mark if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Writing $E_{\text{cell}} = E_a - E_c$.** This is the *opposite* sign convention from the standard. The correct thermodynamic convention is $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$. Mixing the two is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting units in the Nernst equation.** At 25 °C the numerical coefficient is $0.0592$ V (not $0.0592$ mol, not $0.0592$ in base $e$).
> 4. **Forgetting the factor $n$ in Faraday's law.** Many students write $w = MIT/F$ and lose marks when the metal has $n=2$ or $n=3$.
> 5. **Stating "oxygen attacks the metal".** Oxygen is *not* the attacking species; it is the **cathodic reactant** that is *reduced*. The metal is oxidised. Examiners in Module 1 specifically look for this distinction.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert days to seconds** in Faraday calculations. This is a 1-mark deduction in nearly every KTU board paper.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **corrosion cell** has **four** components: Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte, Metallic path.
- [ ] The cell is **internally short-circuited** — it does **not** deliver power to an external load.
- [ ] **Anode** = site of oxidation = site of metal loss = more negative potential.
- [ ] **Cathode** = site of reduction = protected site = more positive potential.
- [ ] The driving force is the **EMF** $E_{\text{cell}} = E_c - E_a$ (always take cathode minus anode).
- [ ] **Galvanic Series in seawater** supersedes the standard EMF series for design decisions.
- [ ] In a **concentration cell**, the **dilute** side is the **anode**.
- [ ] In a **differential aeration cell**, the **low-O_2** side is the **anode** — this is the most common cell type in practice (crevice, pitting, deposit, waterline).
- [ ] The differential aeration EMF depends *only* on the O_2 ratio, not on the metal: $E = \frac{0.0592}{4}\log([O_2]_1/[O_2]_2)$.
- [ ] In a **temperature cell** in aerated water, the **hot** side is the anode for most engineering metals.
- [ ] In **stress cells**, the **plastically deformed** region is the anode.
- [ ] In **active–passive cells** (stainless steels, Al, Ti), a small film break forms a tiny anode against a large cathode — extremely intense pitting.
- [ ] **Combined cells** are the rule, not the exception, in real engineering failures.
- [ ] **Faraday's law** for corrosion rate: $w = \frac{M I t}{n F}$ — remember to convert $t$ to seconds and include the factor $n$.
- [ ] **Reference electrodes**: SCE = +0.242 V vs SHE; CSE = +0.318 V vs SHE.
- [ ] A corrosion cell is **kinetically controlled** — the corrosion current is set by polarisation (Tafel / mixed potential), not by thermodynamics alone.
- [ ] A **small cathode : large anode** geometry is *safe*; a **large cathode : small anode** geometry is *dangerous* and must be avoided in design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanism of Corrosion of Iron
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanism of Corrosion of Iron

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion of Iron** is defined as the **electrochemical degradation** of iron and its alloys through an **oxidation-reduction (redox) process** occurring spontaneously in the presence of an **electrolyte** (typically moisture containing dissolved oxygen, salts, or acids). The phenomenon transforms metallic iron (Fe⁰) into thermodynamically more stable **iron oxide-hydroxide compounds** (commonly known as rust, Fe₂O₃·nH₂O), accompanied by an undesirable degradation of mechanical and structural properties.

The mechanism is governed by the **principle of mixed potential theory** (Evans–Hoar theory), wherein anodic and cathodic half-reactions proceed simultaneously on a metal surface, coupled by an ionic current through the electrolyte and an electronic current through the metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Mandate):**
> - **Anode:** Site of metal dissolution (oxidation): Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻
> - **Cathode:** Site of electron-consuming reduction (e.g., O₂ or H⁺ reduction)
> - **Electrolyte:** Ionic conductor (moisture, salt solution)
> - **Rust:** Non-protective, porous, hydrated ferric oxide layer

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **car battery** accidentally formed on the surface of an iron pipe. The pipe itself is one electrode; a droplet of salty water acts as the electrolyte. Tiny invisible **anodic zones** behave like the negative terminal (iron dissolves), while nearby **cathodic zones** behave like the positive terminal (oxygen gets reduced). Electrons flow through the iron "wire," ions flow through the water droplet, and the result is the same: current flows, and energy is released. Unlike a useful battery, however, this current **destroys the metal itself**, producing the familiar reddish-brown flaky rust.

This is precisely why iron rusts faster near the sea (salt acts as electrolyte) and in humid climates (water acts as electrolyte) — the corrosion "battery" becomes more efficient.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Faraday's constant | F | **96485 C mol⁻¹** | Charge per mole of electrons |
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** | Ideal gas constant |
| Standard temperature | T | **298.15 K (25 °C)** | Reference temperature |
| Molar mass of iron | M(Fe) | **55.845 g mol⁻¹** | Used for corrosion rate |
| Standard electrode potential of Fe²⁺/Fe | E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) | **−0.440 V vs SHE** | Indicates strong tendency to oxidize |
| Standard electrode potential of O₂/OH⁻ | E°(O₂/OH⁻) | **+0.401 V vs SHE** | Driving force for oxygen reduction |

> [!TIP]
> A negative standard potential (−0.44 V) for iron tells us thermodynamically that iron **wants** to give up electrons and become Fe²⁺ — corrosion is essentially "nature's way of returning refined metal to its ore state."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Logical Steps of Iron Corrosion

The corrosion mechanism of iron proceeds through **five interconnected stages**. Each stage must be understood for board-level questions on the topic.

**Step 1 — Electrolyte Adsorption on Metal Surface**
- Moisture, atmospheric water, or a thin film of salt solution deposits on iron.
- Dissolved O₂ and CO₂ dissolve in the water film, lowering pH slightly.

**Step 2 — Anodic Dissolution (Oxidation)**
- At energetically heterogeneous sites (grain boundaries, inclusions, stressed regions), iron atoms ionize:

  $$ \text{Fe}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} $$

- These Fe²⁺ ions migrate into the electrolyte film.

**Step 3 — Electron Conduction Through Metal**
- The liberated electrons travel through the metallic lattice from the anode to cathodic sites.
- Iron is an excellent electronic conductor, enabling efficient electron migration.

**Step 4 — Cathodic Reduction Reactions**
Two principal cathodic reactions dominate depending on the environment:

> **In neutral/alkaline aerated solutions (most common):**
>
> $$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} $$

> **In acidic/deaerated solutions:**
>
> $$ 2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2 $$

**Step 5 — Rust Formation (Solid Product Formation)**
- Fe²⁺ ions react with OH⁻ to form ferrous hydroxide:

  $$ \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 $$

- Further oxidation by dissolved O₂ converts it to hydrated ferric oxide (rust):

  $$ 4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

  (The rust formula is variably written as **Fe₂O₃·nH₂O**, with n ≈ 1–3.)

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Examiner's Logic)

| Step | Driving Force | Engineering Significance |
|------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1. Electrolyte adsorption | Surface energy & hygroscopic impurities | Explains why even "dry" air causes corrosion above 60% RH |
| 2. Anodic dissolution | Thermodynamic instability (E° = −0.44 V) | Justifies why iron corrodes spontaneously in nature |
| 3. Electron flow | Metallic bonding & band structure | Reason why cathodic protection works (electron injection) |
| 4. Cathodic reduction | High E° of O₂/H⁺ couple | Why aerated zones are cathodes (differential aeration cells) |
| 5. Rust formation | Hydration & valence change | Rust is **non-protective** — unlike Al₂O₃ on aluminum |

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU examiner pitfall: students confuse **rust (Fe₂O₃·nH₂O)** with **protective oxide (Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃)**. Rust has a molar volume **greater than iron**, so it spalls off, exposing fresh metal — corrosion never self-arrests on iron in ambient environments.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Equation | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Nernst Equation | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Half-cell potential under non-standard conditions |
| 2 | General Nernst | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | Thermodynamic derivation; used for any T |
| 3 | Cell EMF | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | Predicts spontaneity of corrosion |
| 4 | Gibbs free energy | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | Determines thermodynamic feasibility |
| 5 | Faraday's corrosion rate | $r = \dfrac{M \cdot i \cdot 3600}{n \cdot F} \; \text{(g/m}^2\text{·h)}$ | Converts current density to mass loss |
| 6 | Mils per year (mpy) | $\text{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Engineering corrosion rate unit |
| 7 | Pilling–Bedworth Ratio | $\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}}$ | Predicts oxide protectiveness (Fe: PBR ≈ 2.1, non-protective) |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the iron corrosion mechanism is **foundational** to multiple applied technologies:

- **Cathodic Protection (CP):** Impressed current CP systems force iron to act as a cathode, suppressing the anodic dissolution reaction.
- **Sacrificial Anodes (Galvanizing):** Zinc coatings (E° = −0.76 V) preferentially corrode, protecting iron beneath.
- **Alloying with Chromium:** Stainless steels form a passive Cr₂O₃ layer that arrests the anodic reaction.
- **Boiler & Pipeline Design:** Differential aeration cells are mitigated by eliminating stagnant water zones.
- **Rebar Corrosion in Concrete:** Chloride ingress destabilizes the passive Fe(OH)₃ layer, causing structural collapse.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation for the Fe²⁺/Fe Half-Cell

The Nernst equation is the **backbone of electrochemical corrosion analysis** and is a high-frequency derivation topic in KTU exams.

**Starting thermodynamic relationship between free energy and cell potential:**

For a general electrochemical reaction:
$$\text{Ox} + ne^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Red}$$

The Gibbs free energy change is related to the cell potential by:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

At standard state (1 M, 1 atm, 25 °C):

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

Under non-standard conditions, the change in Gibbs free energy is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. For the half-reaction
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}$$
the reaction quotient is

$$Q = \dfrac{a_{\text{Fe}}}{a_{\text{Fe}^{2+}}} \approx \dfrac{1}{[\text{Fe}^{2+}]}$$

(since $a_{\text{Fe}} = 1$ for a pure solid).

**Substituting and equating expressions:**

Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE$ and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Dividing throughout by −nF (the negative sign reverses the log term):**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

For the iron half-cell specifically:

$$E_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} - \dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln \dfrac{1}{[\text{Fe}^{2+}]}$$

$$E_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} + \dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln [\text{Fe}^{2+}]$$

**At standard temperature T = 298.15 K**, substituting $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ and $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹:

$$\dfrac{RT}{F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 298.15}{96485} = 0.02569 \; \text{V}$$

Converting to log₁₀ (since $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$):

$$\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} = 2.303 \times 0.02569 = 0.05916 \; \text{V} \approx 0.0592 \; \text{V}$$

Therefore, the **final Nernst equation for iron at 25 °C** is:

$$E_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} + \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \log [\text{Fe}^{2+}] \; \text{V vs SHE}$$

$$E_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.440 + 0.0296 \cdot \log [\text{Fe}^{2+}] \; \text{V vs SHE}$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Application: Standard EMF of Iron Corrosion Cell

**Problem:** Calculate the standard EMF and Gibbs free energy for an iron corrosion cell in aerated neutral water.

**Given data:**
- Anode (oxidation): Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻, with reduction potential $E^{\circ}_{\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}} = -0.440$ V
- Cathode (reduction): O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻, $E^{\circ}_{\text{O}_2/\text{OH}^{-}} = +0.401$ V
- n = 4 (electrons balanced)
- F = 96485 C mol⁻¹

**Step 1 — Cell EMF calculation:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = (+0.401) - (-0.440)$$

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = +0.841 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Thermodynamic spontaneity check (ΔG°):**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -4 \times 96485 \times 0.841$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -324{,}575 \; \text{J mol}^{-1} \approx -324.6 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = +0.841$ V confirms the corrosion is **thermodynamically spontaneous**.
- $\Delta G^{\circ} = -324.6$ kJ mol⁻¹ (strongly negative) means the reaction proceeds with a large driving force — this is why unprotected iron rusts readily.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Application: Effect of Fe²⁺ Concentration on Half-Cell Potential

**Problem:** An iron electrode is immersed in a solution with [Fe²⁺] = 10⁻⁴ M. Calculate the half-cell potential at 25 °C.

**Solution using the derived Nernst equation:**

$$E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \log [\text{Fe}^{2+}]$$

$$E = -0.440 + \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \log (10^{-4})$$

$$E = -0.440 + 0.0296 \times (-4)$$

$$E = -0.440 - 0.1184$$

$$E = -0.5584 \; \text{V vs SHE}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Lower Fe²⁺ concentration shifts the potential to a **more negative (active)** value, making iron even more prone to corrosion — relevant in freshwater systems where Fe²⁺ remains low.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Common Iron Corrosion Forms

| Corrosion Type | Anode Location | Cathode Location | Triggering Condition | Example |
|----------------|---------------|------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Uniform Corrosion | Distributed across surface | Distributed across surface | Homogeneous electrolyte | Atmospheric rusting |
| Galvanic Corrosion | More active metal (Fe) | Less active metal (Cu, Sn) | Contact with dissimilar metal | Cu pipe joined to Fe pipe |
| Pitting Corrosion | Localized at defects | Surrounding passive area | Cl⁻ ions, broken passive film | Stainless steel in seawater |
| Differential Aeration | O₂-depleted zone | O₂-rich zone | Uneven oxygen access | Waterline corrosion in tanks |
| Crevice Corrosion | Inside crevice | Outside crevice | Stagnant electrolyte | Bolt/rivet joints |
| Intergranular Corrosion | Grain boundaries | Grain interior | Sensitization (Cr-depletion) | Improperly heat-treated SS |
| Stress Corrosion Cracking | Crack tip | Crack walls | Tensile stress + corrosive medium | Bridges, boilers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Flow Diagram: Iron Corrosion Mechanism

The diagram below illustrates the **functional electrochemical architecture** of iron corrosion, showing the simultaneous flow of electrons (through metal) and ions (through electrolyte) that constitute the corrosion cell.

```mermaid
graph TD
    surface["IRON SURFACE EXPOSED TO MOIST ELECTROLYTE"]
    anode["ANODIC ZONE: METAL DISSOLUTION SITE"]
    cathode["CATHODIC ZONE: REDUCTION SITE"]
    feIon["Fe2+ IONS RELEASED INTO ELECTROLYTE"]
    eFlow["ELECTRON FLOW THROUGH METAL LATTICE"]
    oRed["OXYGEN REDUCTION REACTION"]
    ohIon["HYDROXIDE IONS GENERATED"]
    feohInt["INTERMEDIATE FERROUS HYDROXIDE FeOH2"]
    rustOut["HYDRATED FERRIC OXIDE RUST Fe2O3.nH2O"]
    cellNet["NET RESULT: ELECTROCHEMICAL CORROSION CELL ACTIVE"]

    surface --> anode
    surface --> cathode
    surface --> cellNet
    anode --> feIon
    anode --> eFlow
    eFlow --> cathode
    cathode --> oRed
    oRed --> ohIon
    feIon --> feohInt
    ohIon --> feohInt
    feohInt --> rustOut
    cellNet --> rustOut
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Differential Aeration Cell (Engineering Application)

```mermaid
graph TD
    waterline["WATERLINE OF STORAGE TANK"]
    aerated["AERATED ZONE ABOVE WATERLINE: HIGH O2"]
    deaerated["DEOXYGENATED ZONE BELOW WATERLINE: LOW O2"]
    catArea["CATHODIC AREA: O2 RICH"]
    anodArea["ANODIC AREA: O2 DEPLETED"]
    feLoss["PREFERENTIAL IRON LOSS AT ANODE"]
    eRoute["ELECTRON PATH: ANODE TO CATHODE VIA METAL"]
    ionRoute["IONIC PATH: Fe2+ AND OH- THROUGH WATER"]
    damage["LOCALIZED PITTING AT DEEP ZONE"]

    waterline --> aerated
    waterline --> deaerated
    aerated --> catArea
    deaerated --> anodArea
    anodArea --> feLoss
    catArea --> eRoute
    eRoute --> anodArea
    anodArea --> ionRoute
    ionRoute --> catArea
    feLoss --> damage
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic Description (Verbal)

Since physical drawings cannot be embedded directly, here is the **layered structure** of an iron corrosion site:

1. **Top layer (gas phase):** O₂ from atmosphere diffuses downward.
2. **Bulk electrolyte layer:** Thin water film with dissolved O₂, CO₂, Cl⁻ ions.
3. **Anode zone (at metal interface):** Fe atoms ionize; Fe²⁺ enters electrolyte.
4. **Cathode zone (at metal interface):** O₂ accepts electrons; OH⁻ forms.
5. **Rust layer (above metal):** Loose, porous Fe₂O₃·nH₂O — does NOT protect.
6. **Substrate (bulk iron):** Continually thinned by anodic dissolution.

> [!TIP]
> The **rust layer is non-passive** because its molar volume (≈ 2× iron's) creates internal stress, causing spallation. This is why rust always reveals fresh metal underneath — corrosion is a **continuous** process on iron.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain briefly why rust formed on iron is non-protective, unlike the oxide layer on aluminum.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Rust (Fe₂O₃·nH₂O) is **porous and non-adherent**; it has a molar volume approximately **2.1 times** that of iron, causing it to **spall off** easily and expose fresh metal to further attack. **(1 Mark)**
- In contrast, aluminum forms Al₂O₃, which has a molar volume **smaller than** aluminum (PBR ≈ 1.28) and is **dense, adherent, and self-healing**, blocking further oxygen access. **(1 Mark)**
- Additionally, rust contains Fe³⁺ which can act as an oxidizing agent, accelerating the cathodic reaction; aluminum oxide is chemically inert and electrically insulating. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between anodic and cathodic areas in the corrosion of iron with suitable reactions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Anodic Area | Cathodic Area |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Reaction type | Oxidation | Reduction |
| Reaction on iron | Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ |
| Electron flow | Electrons released | Electrons consumed |
| Metal behaviour | Dissolves / corrodes | Protected / unaffected |
| Surface potential | More negative | More positive |

- Anodic areas are typically regions of **higher energy** (grain boundaries, stressed zones, low O₂ regions). **(1 Mark)**
- Cathodic areas are regions of **lower energy** (high O₂, noble inclusions like Fe₃C in steel). **(1 Mark)**
- Both reactions are **simultaneous**; their rates are coupled and equal at steady state (charge conservation). **(1 Mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the Nernst equation starting from fundamental thermodynamic principles. Apply it to compute the half-cell potential of an iron electrode immersed in a 10⁻³ M Fe²⁺ solution at 25 °C. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the mechanism of atmospheric corrosion of iron with emphasis on (i) the role of differential aeration and (ii) the formation of rust. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question A (a)**

**Step 1 — Starting equation relating free energy and electrode potential:** **(1 Mark)**

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

**Step 2 — Standard state reference equation:** **(1 Mark)**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Non-standard free energy with reaction quotient:** **(1 Mark)**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 4 — Equating and solving:** **(1 Mark)**

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to base-10 logarithm at 298 K:** **(1 Mark)**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$$

**Step 6 — Application to Fe²⁺/Fe with [Fe²⁺] = 10⁻³ M:** **(2 Marks)**

For the reaction Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Fe, $Q = 1 / [\text{Fe}^{2+}]$ and $n = 2$.

$$E = -0.440 - \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \log \left(\dfrac{1}{10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$E = -0.440 - \dfrac{0.0592}{2} \times (+3)$$

$$E = -0.440 - 0.0888$$

$$\boxed{E = -0.5288 \; \text{V vs SHE}}$$

The more negative value (compared to −0.440 V standard) shows that **dilute Fe²⁺ solutions drive iron further toward oxidation**.

---

##### **Solution to Question A (b)**

**(i) Differential Aeration Mechanism:** **(3 Marks)**

When an iron surface is partially immersed in water or exposed to uneven oxygen, **zones with low O₂ become anodes** and **zones with high O₂ become cathodes**.

- At the **anode (oxygen-poor):**

  $$ \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-} $$

- At the **cathode (oxygen-rich):**

  $$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} $$

- Electrons flow through the metal from the low-O₂ to the high-O₂ region.
- A classic engineering example is **waterline corrosion in storage tanks**: damage concentrates just below the waterline.

**(ii) Rust Formation Mechanism:** **(4 Marks)**

- Fe²⁺ ions migrate from the anode and react with OH⁻ ions produced at the cathode:

  $$ \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 \; \text{(ferrous hydroxide, greenish)} $$

- Further oxidation by dissolved O₂ yields hydrated ferric oxide:

  $$ 4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot 3\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

- The final product, written generally as **Fe₂O₃·nH₂O**, is the familiar reddish-brown rust.
- Rust is **non-adherent and porous**, with a **Pilling–Bedworth ratio ≈ 2.1**, causing internal stresses and spallation. This exposes fresh iron, leading to **continuous atmospheric corrosion**.
- Atmospheric corrosion rate depends on relative humidity, presence of pollutants (SO₂, Cl⁻), and temperature.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the electrochemical mechanism of corrosion of iron in a neutral aerated aqueous medium. Write the relevant anodic and cathodic reactions and the overall corrosion reaction. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Pilling–Bedworth ratio**. Why is rust considered non-protective for iron based on this criterion? Calculate the PBR for iron given that the molar volume of Fe is 7.09 cm³/mol and that of Fe₂O₃ is 30.5 cm³/mol. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Question B (a)**

**Diagram description (3 Marks):** Refer to **SECTION_4.1 Mermaid block diagram** — schematic should depict:
- Iron surface with adjacent anodic and cathodic zones.
- Electron flow through metal (anode → cathode).
- Ionic flow through electrolyte (Fe²⁺ and OH⁻).
- Rust layer forming above the surface.

**Anodic reaction (oxidation):** **(1.5 Marks)**

$$\text{Fe}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-}$$

**Cathodic reaction (oxygen reduction in neutral solution):** **(1.5 Marks)**

$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}_{(aq)}$$

**Overall corrosion reaction (balanced):** **(1 Mark)**

Multiply the anodic reaction by 2 and add to the cathodic reaction:

$$2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{2+} + 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

The Fe²⁺ + 2OH⁻ further react to form **Fe(OH)₂**, then **Fe₂O₃·nH₂O (rust)**.

---

##### **Solution to Question B (b)**

**Concept of Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR):** **(2 Marks)**

The Pilling–Bedworth ratio is defined as:

$$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$$

where $V$ denotes the molar volume of the oxide formed per mole of metal consumed, $M$ is the molar mass, $\rho$ is density, and $n$ is the number of metal atoms per formula unit of oxide.

**Protective behavior criteria:** **(2 Marks)**

- If **PBR < 1:** The oxide is too thin to cover the metal surface fully — **non-protective**.
- If **1 < PBR < 2:** The oxide is **continuous, adherent, and protective** (e.g., Al₂O₃, Cr₂O₃).
- If **PBR > 2:** The oxide is too bulky, generates compressive stresses, **cracks and spalls** — **non-protective**.

**Numerical Calculation for Iron (Fe → Fe₂O₃):** **(3 Marks)**

For 2 Fe atoms combining with 3/2 O₂ to form 1 Fe₂O₃:

$$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{V_{\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3}}{2 \times V_{\text{Fe}}}$$

$$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{30.5}{2 \times 7.09}$$

$$\text{PBR} = \dfrac{30.5}{14.18}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PBR} \approx 2.15}$$

**Conclusion:** Since PBR ≈ 2.15 > 2, the oxide layer **exceeds the protective range** and is therefore **non-protective** — consistent with the observed rust-spalling behavior of iron.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Forgetting that E°(anode) must be subtracted (i.e., E°cathode − E°anode). Many students write E°cell = E°anode − E°cathode.
> 2. **Confusing Fe²⁺ vs Fe³⁺ potentials:** E°(Fe³⁺/Fe) = −0.04 V, E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) = −0.44 V, E°(Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺) = +0.77 V — three distinct values, often interchanged.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert ln to log:** The factor 2.303 (= ln 10) is frequently missed; students write 0.0256/n instead of 0.0592/n.
> 4. **Not balancing electrons:** When combining half-reactions, multiply anodic reaction by 2 and cathodic by 1 (or vice versa) to balance e⁻ — a common 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Molar volume in PBR calculation:** PBR uses **molar volume per metal atom**, not per formula unit. Dividing by 2 for Fe₂O₃ is mandatory.
> 6. **Writing rust as "Fe₂O₃" alone:** Should be written as **Fe₂O₃·nH₂O** to indicate hydrated form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanism of Corrosion of Iron**

- [x] Corrosion of iron is **electrochemical** in nature — requires an anode, cathode, electrolyte, and metallic path.
- [x] Anodic reaction (always): **Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻** (E° = −0.44 V).
- [x] Cathodic reaction in **neutral/alkaline aerated water**: O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻.
- [x] Cathodic reaction in **acidic/deaerated medium**: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.
- [x] Rust formula: **Fe₂O₃·nH₂O** — porous, non-adherent, **non-protective**.
- [x] Pilling–Bedworth ratio for Fe ≈ **2.15** (> 2 ⇒ non-protective).
- [x] Nernst equation at 25 °C: **E = E° − (0.0592/n) log Q**.
- [x] Corrosion cell EMF for Fe in aerated water: **E°cell = +0.841 V** (spontaneous).
- [x] ΔG° = −nFE°cell; negative ΔG° confirms spontaneous corrosion.
- [x] **Differential aeration:** O₂-poor zones = anode; O₂-rich zones = cathode.
- [x] **Galvanic corrosion:** Iron (anode) corrodes when coupled with Cu, Ag, or other nobler metals.
- [x] Faraday's constant: **F = 96485 C mol⁻¹**.
- [x] Atmospheric corrosion accelerates with **humidity > 60%**, **SO₂**, **Cl⁻**, and **temperature**.
- [x] Pure iron resists corrosion better than **carbon steel** (Fe₃C inclusions form galvanic microcells).
- [x] **Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)** is the reference for all potential measurements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Free Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Free Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Free Energy

In chemical thermodynamics, **Free Energy** is a state function that quantifies the maximum useful (non-expansion) work extractable from a closed thermodynamic system at constant temperature and pressure. For corrosion science — which is fundamentally driven by the **spontaneous** oxidation of metals — free energy provides the master criterion for predicting *whether* a corrosion reaction will occur in the forward direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEICT866, Module 1):**
> The concept of free energy underpins the *thermodynamic tendency* of a metal to corrode. A negative change in free energy ($\Delta G < 0$) is the **necessary (but not sufficient)** condition for spontaneous corrosion.

The two principal variants encountered in corrosion thermodynamics are:

### A. Gibbs Free Energy (G)

$$G \;=\; H \;-\; T \cdot S \;=\; U \;+\; P \cdot V \;-\; T \cdot S$$

where:
- $H$ = Enthalpy of the system (J or kJ)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (**K**, SI unit)
- $S$ = Entropy of the system (J/K or kJ/K)
- $U$ = Internal energy (J or kJ)
- $P$ = Pressure (**Pa**, SI unit)
- $V$ = Volume (m³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Gibbs Free Energy** applies under conditions of *constant temperature AND constant pressure* — the typical atmospheric/aqueous service condition experienced by engineering materials. Hence it is the **workhorse function** of corrosion thermodynamics.

### B. Helmholtz Free Energy (A or F)

$$A \;=\; U \;-\; T \cdot S$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Helmholtz Free Energy** applies at *constant temperature AND constant volume*. While less frequently cited in atmospheric corrosion, it becomes critical when analyzing **closed electrochemical cells** (e.g., fuel cells, autoclave corrosion tests, high-pressure boilers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Waterfall" Model

Imagine a waterfall cascading down a cliff:
- The **height** of the waterfall = the change in Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$). The greater the fall, the more *driving force* the system possesses.
- The **temperature of the surrounding air** = $T$ (which multiplies the entropy term).
- The **turbulence and disorder** of the splashing water = the entropy term ($S$).

A corrosion reaction is like water spontaneously flowing downhill. Just as water never flows *up* a cliff on its own, a metal never spontaneously *ungoes* corrosion to regenerate the pure metal and environment. The system seeks the *lowest possible* free energy state.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following constants are **exam-essential**:
> - **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}485 \; \text{C mol}^{-1}$ (often rounded to $96{,}500 \; \text{C mol}^{-1}$)
> - **Universal Gas Constant** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
> - **Standard Temperature** $T^{\circ} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$ (often written as $25^{\circ}\text{C}$)
> - **Standard Pressure** $P^{\circ} = 1 \; \text{atm} = 101{,}325 \; \text{Pa}$

The **Standard Gibbs Free Energy Change** ($\Delta G^{\circ}$) is conventionally referenced at $T = 298 \; \text{K}$ and unit activity of all species.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The First and Second Laws — Foundation of Free Energy

The entire concept of free energy is constructed from two pillars of thermodynamics:

### The First Law (Conservation of Energy)
$$dU \;=\; \delta Q \;-\; \delta W$$

For a system doing only $P \cdot V$ work: $\delta W = P \cdot dV$, and combining with the definition of enthalpy $H = U + P \cdot V$:

$$dH \;=\; dU \;+\; P \cdot dV \;+\; V \cdot dP \;=\; \delta Q \;+\; V \cdot dP$$

### The Second Law (Entropy Principle)
$$dS \;\geq\; \frac{\delta Q}{T}$$

For a *reversible* process, equality holds; for an *irreversible* (real) process, strict inequality applies. This leads directly to the **Clausius Inequality**.

## 2.2 Deriving the Gibbs Free Energy Change

Starting from the combined First and Second Law statement for a closed system at equilibrium:

$$dU \;=\; T \cdot dS \;-\; P \cdot dV$$

Adding $P \cdot dV + V \cdot dP - T \cdot dS - S \cdot dT$ to both sides:

$$dU \;+\; P \cdot dV \;+\; V \cdot dP \;-\; T \cdot dS \;-\; S \cdot dT \;=\; 0$$

By inspection, the left-hand side is the exact differential of $G = U + P \cdot V - T \cdot S$:

$$dG \;=\; V \cdot dP \;-\; S \cdot dT$$

This is the **fundamental Gibbs relation** — the cornerstone identity of corrosion thermodynamics.

## 2.3 Spontaneity Criteria for Corrosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Criterion (Memorize for KTU Examinations):**
> - If $\Delta G < 0$ → Reaction is **spontaneous** in the forward direction (corrosion occurs).
> - If $\Delta G > 0$ → Reaction is **non-spontaneous** in the forward direction (reverse reaction is favored; metal is *noble*).
> - If $\Delta G = 0$ → System is at **equilibrium** (no net corrosion driving force).

The temperature dependence of spontaneity is governed by the **Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation**:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \Delta H \;-\; T \cdot \Delta S$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name / Use | Critical Condition | Units |
|---|----------|------------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | $G = H - T \cdot S$ | Gibbs free energy definition | Constant $T$, $P$ | kJ |
| 2 | $A = U - T \cdot S$ | Helmholtz free energy definition | Constant $T$, $V$ | kJ |
| 3 | $dG = V \cdot dP - S \cdot dT$ | Fundamental Gibbs relation | Closed, reversible system | kJ |
| 4 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \cdot \Delta S$ | Spontaneity master equation | Constant $T$, $P$ | kJ mol$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -R \cdot T \cdot \ln K$ | Standard free energy / equilibrium | Standard state | kJ mol$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}$ | Electrochemical free energy | Electrochemical cell | kJ mol$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_P = -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz equation | Constant $P$ | kJ mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$ |
| 8 | $E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n \cdot F}$ | Cell EMF from free energy | Reversible cell | V (volts) |
| 9 | $\Delta S = -\left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial T}\right)_P$ | Entropy from free energy | Constant $P$ | kJ mol$^{-1}$ K$^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $\Delta V = \left(\frac{\partial \Delta G}{\partial P}\right)_T$ | Volume from free energy | Constant $T$ | m³ mol$^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In Equation 6 above, the absolute-value bars in $-n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}$ are rendered as $-n \, F \, E^{\circ}$ — never use the literal pipe character $\vert$ inside markdown tables.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Free Energy Concepts

1. **Prediction of Corrosion Tendency:** Using tabulated $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ values, engineers rank metals from most active (e.g., magnesium, $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ highly negative) to most noble (e.g., gold, $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ positive).
2. **Calculation of Cell Voltage:** Equation 6 connects thermodynamic data directly to measurable potentials used in **Pourbaix Diagrams**.
3. **Ellingham Diagrams (Module 2 preview):** Plots of $\Delta G^{\circ}$ vs. $T$ for oxide formation — built directly on the linear relation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \Delta S$.
4. **Design of Cathodic Protection Systems:** Free energy tells the engineer *how much driving force* must be countered.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles to $dG = V \, dP - S \, dT$

**Step 1:** Begin with the combined statement of the First and Second Laws for a reversible, closed system doing only $P$–$V$ work:

$$dU \;=\; T \cdot dS \;-\; P \cdot dV$$

**Step 2:** Recall the definition of enthalpy:

$$H \;\equiv\; U \;+\; P \cdot V$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $H$:

$$dH \;=\; dU \;+\; P \cdot dV \;+\; V \cdot dP$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expression for $dU$:

$$dH \;=\; \left(T \cdot dS \;-\; P \cdot dV\right) \;+\; P \cdot dV \;+\; V \cdot dP$$

**Step 5:** The $P \cdot dV$ terms cancel:

$$dH \;=\; T \cdot dS \;+\; V \cdot dP$$

**Step 6:** Define Gibbs free energy:

$$G \;\equiv\; H \;-\; T \cdot S$$

**Step 7:** Differentiate $G$:

$$dG \;=\; dH \;-\; T \cdot dS \;-\; S \cdot dT$$

**Step 8:** Substitute $dH = T \, dS + V \, dP$:

$$dG \;=\; \left(T \cdot dS \;+\; V \cdot dP\right) \;-\; T \cdot dS \;-\; S \cdot dT$$

**Step 9:** The $T \cdot dS$ terms cancel exactly, yielding the **fundamental relation**:

$$\boxed{\,dG \;=\; V \cdot dP \;-\; S \cdot dT\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation tells us that at constant pressure (corrosion in an open container), $dG = -S \, dT$, and at constant temperature (isothermal corrosion), $dG = V \, dP$. The two natural variables of $G$ are therefore $P$ and $T$ — *not* $S$ and $V$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Gibbs–Helmholtz Equation

**Step 1:** Begin with the differential form of $\Delta G$:

$$d(\Delta G) \;=\; -\Delta S \cdot dT \quad \text{(at constant } P\text{)}$$

This follows from $dG = V \, dP - S \, dT$ applied to a reaction where $\Delta$ denotes the *change* between products and reactants.

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $T$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G)}{T} \;=\; -\Delta S \cdot \frac{dT}{T}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $\Delta G / T$ with respect to $T$ using the quotient rule. Define $y = \Delta G / T$. Then:

$$\frac{dy}{dT} \;=\; \frac{T \cdot \frac{d(\Delta G)}{dT} - \Delta G}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $d(\Delta G)/dT = -\Delta S$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G / T)}{dT} \;=\; \frac{T \cdot (-\Delta S) - \Delta G}{T^{2}} \;=\; \frac{-T \cdot \Delta S - \Delta G}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 5:** Using $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \cdot \Delta S$, we have $-T \cdot \Delta S = \Delta G - \Delta H$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta G / T)}{dT} \;=\; \frac{(\Delta G - \Delta H) - \Delta G}{T^{2}} \;=\; \frac{-\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 6:** The **Gibbs–Helmholtz equation** is therefore:

$$\boxed{\,\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_P \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation is used to compute the *temperature variation* of $\Delta G$ when the enthalpy change of a reaction is known. It is the analytical engine behind the slope of any line in an Ellingham diagram.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — From $\Delta G^{\circ}$ to Cell EMF (Electrochemical Link)

**Step 1:** For a reversible electrochemical cell, the maximum electrical work obtainable equals the free energy decrease:

$$W_{max} \;=\; -\Delta G$$

**Step 2:** For $n$ moles of electrons transferred through a potential difference $E$:

$$W_{max} \;=\; n \cdot F \cdot E$$

where $F = 96{,}485 \; \text{C mol}^{-1}$.

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions:

$$-\Delta G \;=\; n \cdot F \cdot E$$

**Step 4:** Under standard conditions:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}\,}$$

**Step 5:** Numerically, with $\Delta G^{\circ}$ in kJ mol$^{-1}$ and $E^{\circ}$ in volts:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -96.5 \cdot n \cdot E^{\circ} \;\; \text{(kJ mol}^{-1}\text{)}$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the **single most important equation** linking *thermodynamic* tables (free energies of formation) to *electrochemical* measurements (half-cell potentials). It allows a corrosion engineer to construct Pourbaix diagrams from $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ data alone.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application to Common Metals

| Metal / Reaction | $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ (kJ mol$^{-1}$) | $n$ | Calculated $E^{\circ}$ (V) | Corrosion Tendency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mg + $\tfrac{1}{2}$O$_2$ → MgO | $-569.3$ | 2 | $+2.95$ | Very high (highly active) |
| Al + $\tfrac{3}{2}$O$_2$ → $\tfrac{1}{2}$Al$_2$O$_3$ | $-527.4$ (per $\tfrac{1}{2}$ Al$_2$O$_3$ basis) | 3 | $+1.82$ | High |
| Fe + $\tfrac{1}{2}$O$_2$ → FeO | $-244.0$ | 2 | $+1.27$ | Moderate |
| Cu + $\tfrac{1}{2}$O$_2$ → CuO | $-129.7$ | 2 | $+0.67$ | Low |
| Ag + $\tfrac{1}{2}$O$_2$ → Ag$_2$O | $-32.0$ (per $\tfrac{1}{2}$ Ag$_2$O basis) | 1 | $+0.33$ | Very low (noble) |
| Au + $\tfrac{3}{2}$O$_2$ → $\tfrac{1}{2}$Au$_2$O$_3$ | $+39.0$ (per $\tfrac{1}{2}$ Au$_2$O$_3$ basis) | 3 | $-0.13$ | Negligible (noble) |

> [!NOTE]
> Numerical values above are **approximate** tabulated $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ values for the most stable oxide at 298 K. They are sufficient for KTU-style calculations; in research-grade work consult the NBS thermodynamic tables.

**Observation from the matrix:** As $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ becomes *more negative*, the equivalent $E^{\circ}$ becomes *more positive*, and the metal becomes *more reactive*. The reverse is true for noble metals like gold, where $\Delta G^{\circ}_f > 0$ and $E^{\circ} < 0$ (oxide is thermodynamically unstable).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
