<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Gibbs Free Energy. State its significance in corrosion science.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Gibbs Free Energy is a thermodynamic state function defined as:

$$G \;=\; H \;-\; T \cdot S$$

It represents the maximum non-expansion (useful) work obtainable from a closed system at constant temperature and pressure.

**Significance in corrosion (2 marks):**
1. It is the master criterion for spontaneity: a corrosion reaction proceeds spontaneously in the forward direction **only if** $\Delta G < 0$.
2. It provides a direct bridge between thermodynamic data ($\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ tables) and measurable electrochemical quantities via $\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$, enabling the construction of Pourbaix and Ellingham diagrams.
3. It allows ranking of metals in the **galvanic series** strictly from first principles.

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's Tip: Always state the *direction* of spontaneity explicitly. Writing "$\Delta G < 0$ means reaction is spontaneous" alone is incomplete — also mention the corrosion context.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Gibbs Free Energy and Helmholtz Free Energy.
**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Gibbs ($G$) | Helmholtz ($A$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $G = H - T \cdot S$ | $A = U - T \cdot S$ |
| Conditions | Constant $T$ **and** $P$ | Constant $T$ **and** $V$ |
| Differential form | $dG = V \, dP - S \, dT$ | $dA = -S \, dT - P \, dV$ |
| Typical use in corrosion | Atmospheric / aqueous corrosion | Closed cell / pressure vessel corrosion |
| Work interpretation | Max. non-$P$–$V$ work | Max. total work |

For open atmospheric corrosion — the overwhelming majority of engineering scenarios — **Gibbs free energy is the relevant function**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice A

**Q.A (a)** Define Gibbs free energy. Starting from the first and second laws of thermodynamics, derive the fundamental relation $dG = V \, dP - S \, dT$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Gibbs free energy is the thermodynamic potential that measures the maximum reversible work obtainable from a closed system at constant temperature and pressure. Mathematically:

$$G \;\equiv\; H \;-\; T \cdot S \;\equiv\; U \;+\; P \cdot V \;-\; T \cdot S$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1: Combined first and second law (reversible, $P$–$V$ work only):

$$dU \;=\; T \cdot dS \;-\; P \cdot dV$$

Step 2: Differentiate $H = U + P \cdot V$:

$$dH \;=\; dU \;+\; P \cdot dV \;+\; V \cdot dP$$

Step 3: Substitute:

$$dH \;=\; T \cdot dS \;+\; V \cdot dP$$

Step 4: Differentiate $G = H - T \cdot S$:

$$dG \;=\; dH \;-\; T \cdot dS \;-\; S \cdot dT$$

Step 5: Substitute $dH$ and cancel $T \, dS$:

$$dG \;=\; V \cdot dP \;-\; S \cdot dT \quad \blacksquare$$

**Q.A (b)** For the oxidation of iron at 298 K: $2\text{Fe}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{FeO}(s)$, the standard free energy change is $\Delta G^{\circ} = -488 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. Calculate the standard cell potential $E^{\circ}$ and comment on the corrosion tendency of iron. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify $n$. The reaction transfers 4 electrons (Fe: 0 → +2, twice; O: 0 → −2). Thus $n = 4$.

**Step 2:** Apply the electrochemical free-energy relation:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} \;=\; -n \cdot F \cdot E^{\circ}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $E^{\circ}$:

$$E^{\circ} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{n \cdot F} \;=\; -\frac{-488 \times 10^{3} \; \text{J mol}^{-1}}{4 \times 96{,}485 \; \text{C mol}^{-1}}$$

$$E^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{488{,}000}{385{,}940} \;=\; +1.265 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation:
- $E^{\circ} = +1.265 \; \text{V}$ is highly positive.
- $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$, confirming spontaneity.
- Iron has a **strong thermodynamic tendency** to oxidize (corrode) in oxygenated environments — consistent with everyday observation of rusting.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice B

**Q.B (a)** State the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation. Using the relation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \, \Delta S$, explain how temperature affects the spontaneity of a corrosion reaction. Support your answer with two specific examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (2 marks):**

$$\left(\frac{\partial (\Delta G / T)}{\partial T}\right)_P \;=\; -\frac{\Delta H}{T^{2}}$$

**Temperature Effect Analysis (3 marks):**

The spontaneity of any reaction — corrosion included — is governed by:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \Delta H \;-\; T \cdot \Delta S$$

Four sign combinations of $(\Delta H, \Delta S)$ yield four distinct cases:

| Case | $\Delta H$ | $\Delta S$ | $\Delta G$ behaviour with $T$ | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-$ | $+$ | Always negative; spontaneous at all $T$ | $2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}$ |
| 2 | $-$ | $-$ | Negative only below $T = \Delta H / \Delta S$ | High-T oxide decomposition |
| 3 | $+$ | $+$ | Negative only above $T = \Delta H / \Delta S$ | Endothermic corrosion |
| 4 | $+$ | $-$ | Always positive; never spontaneous | Noble metal oxide formation |

**Two examples (2 marks):**
1. **Magnesium oxidation** ($\Delta H < 0$, $\Delta S > 0$): spontaneous at all temperatures, which is why Mg is used as a sacrificial anode.
2. **Carbon oxidation to CO** ($\Delta H < 0$, $\Delta S > 0$): becomes *more* spontaneous at high $T$, illustrating why boiler-tube corrosion accelerates with temperature.

---

**Q.B (b)** The standard free energy of formation of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3(s)$ is $-1582 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ at 298 K. Calculate the standard electrode potential for the half reaction $\tfrac{2}{3}\text{Al}(s) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(g) + 2e^- \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{3}\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3(s)$. Comment on the corrosion resistance of aluminium. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the half-reaction and identify $n$.

$$\tfrac{2}{3}\text{Al} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \;+\; 2e^- \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{3}\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$$

Electrons transferred: $n = 2$ (Al: 0 → +3, scaled to 2 electrons per $\tfrac{2}{3}$ Al).

**Step 2:** Determine $\Delta G^{\circ}$ for the half-reaction.

Since $\Delta G^{\circ}_f (\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3) = -1582 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ is for the formation of 1 mole of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, the half-reaction forms $\tfrac{1}{3}$ mole:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{half}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{3} \times (-1582) \;=\; -527.33 \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Apply $\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$:

$$E^{\circ} \;=\; -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{\text{half}}}{n \cdot F} \;=\; -\frac{-527{,}330 \; \text{J mol}^{-1}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \; \text{C mol}^{-1}}$$

$$E^{\circ} \;=\; \frac{527{,}330}{192{,}970} \;=\; +2.733 \; \text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Comment:
- The very high positive $E^{\circ}$ indicates a strong thermodynamic driving force for aluminium oxidation.
- The metal should be **highly reactive**, yet in practice aluminium shows good corrosion resistance — this is the classic **kinetics vs. thermodynamics** distinction: a thin, tenacious, self-healing $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ film forms and passivates the surface.
- This illustrates that **free energy predicts tendency, not rate**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Sign Errors:** Students frequently write $\Delta G = -n F E$ *without* justification. Always derive or state it; examiners award marks for the conceptual link.
> 2. **Confusing $G$ and $A$:** Marks are lost when students apply Helmholtz's equation to atmospheric corrosion. State the conditions explicitly.
> 3. **Mixing $n$:** For a half-reaction, $n$ is the electrons transferred, **not** the stoichiometric coefficient of the metal. In Q.B(b), $n = 2$ — not 3.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Convert kJ to J before dividing by $F$ (in C mol$^{-1}$) to get $E$ in volts. Mismatch costs full marks.
> 5. **Omitting the "Comment" Part:** KTU board examiners specifically look for the **interpretive** sentence tying numbers to corrosion behaviour. Numbers alone earn only partial credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Concept of Free Energy"**
> - $\square$ **Gibbs:** $G = H - T S = U + P V - T S$ (constant $T$, $P$).
> - $\square$ **Helmholtz:** $A = U - T S$ (constant $T$, $V$).
> - $\square$ **Fundamental relation:** $dG = V \, dP - S \, dT$.
> - $\square$ **Spontaneity master equation:** $\Delta G = \Delta H - T \, \Delta S$.
> - $\square$ **Rule:** $\Delta G < 0 \Rightarrow$ spontaneous corrosion.
> - $\square$ **Electrochemical link:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -n F E^{\circ}$.
> - $\square$ **Equilibrium link:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -R T \ln K$.
> - $\square$ **Gibbs–Helmholtz:** $\left[\partial(\Delta G / T)/\partial T\right]_P = -\Delta H / T^2$.
> - $\square$ **Constants:** $F = 96{,}485 \; \text{C mol}^{-1}$; $R = 8.314 \; \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.
> - $\square$ **Standard state:** $T = 298 \; \text{K}$, $P = 1 \; \text{atm}$, unit activity.
> - $\square$ **Thermodynamics predicts tendency, kinetics predicts rate.**
> - $\square$ **Active metals** → large negative $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ → high positive $E^{\circ}$.
> - $\square$ **Noble metals** (Au, Pt) → positive $\Delta G^{\circ}_f$ → negative $E^{\circ}$ → thermodynamically stable.

**Mnemonic for spontaneity sign conventions:**

> **"Goodbye Corrosion" = "Greater than zero Corrosion" → wait, that is the opposite. Let me try again:**

> **Negative $\Delta G$ = No Go (for the reverse) = Corrosion Goes Forward.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reversible Electrode Potential (E):** The potential developed at an electrode when the electrode is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its surrounding electrolyte, such that the forward (oxidation) and reverse (reduction) reaction rates are exactly equal, with no net current flow through the external circuit.

Mathematically, for a general half-cell reaction:

$$\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

The **reversible electrode potential** is the difference in electrical potential between the metal (electrode) and the electrolyte (solution) when the system is in **electrochemical equilibrium**. This potential is measured against a **Standard Reference Electrode**, conventionally the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** assigned a value of **0.00 V** at **298 K (25 °C)** and **1 atm** H₂ pressure with **1 M H⁺ activity**.

### Key Characteristics of a Reversible Electrode:
1. **No net current flows** — the system is at dynamic equilibrium.
2. **Infinitesimally small current** can be drawn without disturbing equilibrium (Nernst reversibility).
3. The potential strictly obeys the **Nernst Equation**.
4. Both oxidation and reduction proceed at identical rates at the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Focus:** Whenever the phrase "reversible electrode potential" appears in a question, examiners expect the student to recall the **Nernst Equation** and the **thermodynamic linkage ΔG = −nFE**. Both must be quoted in full marks answers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Think of the **reversible electrode potential** as a **"chemical tug-of-war"** that has reached a perfect stalemate:

> **Analogy — The Tilted Seesaw:**
> Imagine two equally strong teams pulling a rope attached to a metal rod dipped in a liquid. When the force of metal atoms wanting to dissolve (oxidation, M → M^n+ + ne⁻) is *exactly equal* to the force of ions wanting to deposit back onto the metal (reduction, M^n+ + ne⁻ → M), the rope (the electrical potential) stops moving. That frozen, stable potential value is the **reversible electrode potential**. If you pull very gently (pass a tiny current), the system will spring back to the same equilibrium value — that is *reversibility*.

A real-world engineering parallel is the **voltage of a fully charged, open-circuit battery terminal** — no current is drawn, and the system sits in electrochemical equilibrium, displaying a stable, predictable potential.

---

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96485** | C mol⁻¹ |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **298.15** | K |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³** | mol⁻¹ |
| SHE Reference Potential | $E°_{H^+/H_2}$ | **0.0000** | V |
| Constant $(2.303RT/F)$ at 298 K | — | **0.0591** | V |

> [!TIP]
> The value **0.0591 V** is one of the most frequently tested numbers in KTU corrosion papers — memorize it. It comes from substituting $T = 298$ K into $(2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 / 96485)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

### Step 1: Electrochemical Equilibrium Condition
At a reversible electrode, the rate of forward reaction (reduction, Ox + ne⁻ → Red) equals the rate of reverse reaction (oxidation, Red → Ox + ne⁻). The net current density is zero, but charge-transfer events occur in *both* directions at equal rates.

### Step 2: Why We Need a Reference Electrode
A single electrode potential **cannot be measured in absolute isolation** — we can only measure the *difference* between two electrodes. Therefore, KTU (and IUPAC) convention mandates the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** as the universal zero reference. All other potentials are quoted *relative to SHE*.

### Step 3: The Nernst Equation — The Heart of Reversible Potential
The reversible electrode potential is *not* a fixed number; it depends on:
- **Temperature** ($T$)
- **Number of electrons transferred** ($n$)
- **Activities (or concentrations) of oxidized and reduced species**

### Step 4: The Thermodynamic Backbone
The Nernst equation is *not* empirical — it is a direct consequence of the **Gibbs free energy** relation:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

Because electrical work equals $W_{elec} = -nFE$, we have $\Delta G = -nFE$. Substituting yields the Nernst form.

### Step 5: Standard vs. Non-Standard Conditions
- **Standard Electrode Potential ($E°$)**: Potential measured when all species are at *unit activity* (≈ 1 M for solutes, 1 atm for gases, pure solid/liquid for condensed phases), at 298 K.
- **Reversible Electrode Potential ($E$)**: Potential at *any* condition of concentration, temperature, or pressure, calculated via Nernst equation.

### Step 6: Sign Conventions (KTU Critical Pitfall)
- **Reduction Potential** → potential when the species is being *reduced* (gaining electrons). More positive = stronger oxidizer.
- **Oxidation Potential** → same magnitude, opposite sign.
- **SHE**: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2(g)$; $E° = 0.000$ V.
- **Cu²⁺/Cu**: $E° = +0.337$ V → Cu²⁺ is a stronger oxidizing agent than H⁺.
- **Zn²⁺/Zn**: $E° = -0.763$ V → Zn is a stronger reducing agent than H₂.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = E° - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | General Nernst Equation | V |
| 2 | $E = E° - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ (at 298 K) | Logarithmic Nernst form (KTU favourite) | V |
| 3 | $E = E° - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{Red}]^a}{[\text{Ox}]^b}$ | Concentration form of Nernst | V |
| 4 | $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ | Standard Gibbs–Potential relation | J mol⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\Delta G = -nFE$ | General Gibbs–Potential relation | J mol⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K_{eq}$ | Equilibrium constant from $E°$ | J mol⁻¹ |
| 7 | $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$ | Cell EMF (both as reduction potentials) | V |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[\text{Ox}]}{[\text{Red}]}$ | For simple metal/metal-ion: $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M$ | V |
| 9 | $E_{H^+/H_2} = 0.000$ V | SHE reference | V |
| 10 | $\dfrac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.0591$ V at 298 K | Universal constant for log-form | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** The reaction quotient $Q$ for the Nernst equation follows the **reduction form** of the half-reaction. Products of reduction (typically the reduced species) appear in the numerator of $Q$ when written in the $\log$ form. This trips up 6 out of 10 students. Always rewrite the half-reaction as a *reduction* first, then construct $Q$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reversible electrode potential is the **cornerstone of every corrosion prediction tool** in industry:

1. **Galvanic Series Construction**: Engineers rank metals by their $E°$ values to predict which metal corrodes when two are coupled (e.g., steel hull + bronze propeller).
2. **Cathodic Protection Design**: Sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg) are chosen because their $E°$ is *more negative* than the protected metal.
3. **Electrochemical Machining & Plating**: Predicting whether a metal will deposit spontaneously or require an external power source.
4. **Pourbaix Diagrams**: Plotting $E$ vs. pH maps the thermodynamic stability of metals — used in chemical plant design.
5. **Battery & Fuel Cell Engineering**: Cell voltage predictions and energy density calculations rely entirely on Nernst-derived potentials.
6. **Corrosion Rate Estimation (via Nernst–Tafel relations)**: Polarization curves use reversible potential as the baseline.

> **Industrial Case Study:** In offshore oil rigs, the **galvanic corrosion of mild steel coupled to stainless steel fittings** is predicted simply by consulting the $E°$ values: stainless steel's chromium oxide layer gives it a more noble (positive) $E°$, so mild steel (more negative) becomes the sacrificial anode and corrodes preferentially.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

### Starting Premise (Gibbs Energy Change for Non-Standard Conditions)
For any chemical reaction proceeding at non-standard state, the change in Gibbs free energy is given by:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

where $Q$ is the **reaction quotient** (activities of products raised to their stoichiometric coefficients, divided by reactants).

### Step 1: Express Free Energy in Electrical Work Form
When a reaction involves the transfer of $n$ moles of electrons at a potential $E$, the maximum electrical work obtainable is:

$$W_{elec} = -nFE$$

The sign convention: a *spontaneous* electrochemical reaction has $\Delta G < 0$ (work output is positive). So:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For the standard state:

$$\Delta G° = -nFE°$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Gibbs Equation
We now substitute $\Delta G$ and $\Delta G°$ into the fundamental Gibbs relation:

$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

### Step 3: Isolate the Potential $E$
Divide *both sides* of the equation by $-nF$ (which reverses the inequality direction of the sign on $RT \ln Q$ because we are dividing by a *negative* quantity):

$$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

This is the **Nernst Equation** in its natural logarithmic form.

### Step 4: Convert to Common (Base-10) Logarithm
Using the identity $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E° - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

### Step 5: Evaluate the Constant at Standard Temperature (298 K)
Substitute the numerical values:

$$R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}, \quad T = 298 \text{ K}, \quad F = 96485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$$

Compute the numerical factor:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = \frac{5705.79}{96485} \approx 0.0591$$

Therefore, at **298 K**:

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$

> This is the **most-tested form** in KTU question papers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between $E°$ and Equilibrium Constant $K_{eq}$

### Starting Premise
At equilibrium, the net reaction ceases, meaning $\Delta G = 0$ and $E = 0$ (no driving force). Also, by definition, $Q = K_{eq}$ at equilibrium.

### Step 1: Apply the Equilibrium Condition to Nernst
Set $E = 0$ in the Nernst equation:

$$0 = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{eq}$$

### Step 2: Solve for $E°$
Rearrange:

$$E° = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{eq}$$

Or equivalently, from the Gibbs relationship:

$$\Delta G° = -RT \ln K_{eq} = -nFE°$$

Solving for $K_{eq}$:

$$K_{eq} = e^{nFE°/RT} = 10^{nE°/0.0591}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A **positive** $E°$ implies $K_{eq} > 1$ (products favoured, spontaneous reduction). A **negative** $E°$ implies $K_{eq} < 1$ (reactants favoured, spontaneous oxidation). This single relation links electrode thermodynamics to bulk reaction spontaneity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Board-Style Problem

**Problem:** Calculate the reversible electrode potential of a **Cu²⁺/Cu** electrode at 298 K when the activity of Cu²⁺ is **0.01 M**. Given $E°_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.337$ V.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Write the half-reaction (reduction form):
$$Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$$

**Step 2:** Identify $n$ and the activity quotient:
- $n = 2$ (electrons transferred)
- For the reduction $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$, $Q = \dfrac{1}{[Cu^{2+}]}$ (pure solid Cu has activity 1)

**Step 3:** Apply the Nernst equation at 298 K:

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{1}{[Cu^{2+}]}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:

$$E = 0.337 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.01}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate $\log(1/0.01) = \log(100) = 2$:

$$E = 0.337 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 2$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:

$$E = 0.337 - 0.0591 = 0.2779 \text{ V}$$

> **Result:** $E = +0.278$ V. Note that dilution (lower Cu²⁺) **decreases** the reduction potential, making the system less oxidizing — a vital concept in corrosion control when designing closed-loop cooling systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reversible Potentials for Key Reference Electrodes

| Reference Electrode | Half-Reaction | $E°$ (V) vs SHE at 298 K | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ | **0.000** | Universal reference |
| Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) | $Hg_2Cl_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Hg + 2Cl^-$ | **+0.244** | Lab/field corrosion studies |
| Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) | $AgCl + e^- \rightarrow Ag + Cl^-$ | **+0.222** | Biomedical & marine sensors |
| Copper/Copper Sulfate (CSE) | $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$ | **+0.314** | Cathodic protection of pipelines |
| Standard Oxygen Electrode (SOE) | $O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | **+1.229** | Aerated corrosion environments |

> [!TIP]
> When KTU questions ask "convert $E$ measured against SCE to SHE scale," use: $E_{SHE} = E_{SCE} + 0.244$ V. Memorize the offsets for **SCE (+0.244)**, **Ag/AgCl (+0.222)**, and **CSE (+0.314)**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Reversible Electrochemical Cell Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Voltmeter High Impedance] --> B[Wire Conductor]
    B --> C[Working Electrode M / M-n+]
    C --> D[Electrolyte Solution aOx + bRed]
    D --> E[Reference Electrode SHE / SCE]
    E --> F[Wire Conductor]
    F --> A
    G[Equilibrium Condition] --> C
    G --> E
    H[No Net Current Flow] --> A
    I[Potential Difference E] --> A
```

**Interpretation:**
- The voltmeter draws *infinitesimally* small current (high-impedance) so equilibrium is preserved.
- The **working electrode** develops the **reversible potential** governed by Nernst.
- The **reference electrode** provides the fixed zero (SHE) or known offset (SCE, Ag/AgCl).
- The measured voltage is the **E** in $E = E° - (0.0591/n)\log Q$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Derivation Logic Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Gibbs Free Energy Change DG] --> B[DG equals minus nFE]
    C[Standard Gibbs DG0] --> D[DG0 equals minus nFE0]
    A --> E[DG equals DG0 plus RT ln Q]
    B --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Isolate E and substitute]
    F --> G[Nernst Equation E equals E0 minus RT over nF ln Q]
    G --> H[Convert ln to log base 10]
    H --> I[Substitute T equals 298 K]
    I --> J[Final Form E equals E0 minus 0.0591 over n log Q]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Sign Convention Decision Logic for KTU Problems

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Given Half Reaction] --> Q1{Is it written as reduction?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Reverse the Reaction and Flip Sign of E0]
    Q1 -- Yes --> R2[Proceed with Nernst Form]
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> S1[Identify n electrons transferred]
    S1 --> S2[Construct Q from activities of products over reactants]
    S2 --> S3[Apply E equals E0 minus 0.0591 over n log Q]
    S3 --> T1{Question asks for E or DG?}
    T1 -- E --> U1[Report E in Volts]
    T1 -- DG --> U2[Compute DG equals minus nFE in J per mol]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Real-World Application Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reversible Electrode Potential E] --> B[Galvanic Series]
    A --> C[Cathodic Protection Design]
    A --> D[Pourbaix Diagrams E vs pH]
    A --> E[Battery Voltage Prediction]
    A --> F[Corrosion Rate from Polarization]
    B --> G[Ship Hull Material Selection]
    C --> H[Offshore Pipeline Mg Anode Design]
    D --> I[Chemical Plant Stability Maps]
    E --> J[Li ion Battery Cell EMF]
    F --> K[Tafel Extrapolation in Lab]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define reversible electrode potential. Mention the role of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) in potential measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reversible Electrode Potential** is the electrical potential developed at a metal–electrolyte interface when the system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** — meaning the rates of forward (reduction) and reverse (oxidation) reactions are exactly equal, with **no net current flow**.
>
> **Role of SHE:**
> 1. The SHE is assigned a potential of **0.000 V** at standard conditions (298 K, 1 M H⁺, 1 atm H₂).
> 2. It serves as the **universal reference electrode** against which all other electrode potentials are measured.
> 3. Absolute single-electrode potentials cannot be measured; only differences between two electrodes are measurable. SHE provides the standardized zero for these differential measurements.
> 4. It is constructed by passing pure H₂ gas over a platinized platinum electrode immersed in a 1 M HCl solution.
>
> *(3 marks: definition 1 mark, SHE role 2 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Write the Nernst equation for the half-reaction $Zn^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Zn$ at 298 K and explain the effect of doubling the Zn²⁺ activity on the electrode potential.**

**Model Answer:**

The Nernst equation for the Zn²⁺/Zn half-cell at 298 K is:

$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[Zn^{2+}]}$$

Given $E°_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.763$ V:

$$E = -0.763 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log [Zn^{2+}]$$

**Effect of doubling [Zn²⁺]:**
- New potential: $E_{new} = -0.763 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log (2 \times [Zn^{2+}])$
- Change: $\Delta E = \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log 2 = \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \times 0.3010 = +0.0089$ V
- The electrode potential becomes **more positive** (more noble) by approximately **+8.9 mV**.

This reflects the Nernst principle: increasing the oxidized species activity favours reduction, raising the reduction potential.

*(3 marks: equation 1 mark, derivation of effect 1 mark, numerical answer 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — ESE Module Internal Choice (Choose ONE out of TWO)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles. State clearly the significance of each term involved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A galvanic cell is constructed with the following half-cells:
- Anode: $Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-$, $E°_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.763$ V
- Cathode: $Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu$, $E°_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.337$ V

Calculate the **cell EMF** when $[Zn^{2+}] = 0.1$ M and $[Cu^{2+}] = 0.01$ M at 298 K. Also, calculate $\Delta G°$ for the cell reaction. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation of Nernst Equation:**

For a general electrochemical half-reaction:
$$\text{Ox} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Red}$$

The change in Gibbs free energy at non-standard state is given by:
$$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$

The maximum electrical work obtainable from the transfer of $n$ moles of electrons at potential $E$ is:
$$W_{max} = -nFE$$

Equating the two expressions (with sign convention $\Delta G = -W_{max}$):
$$-nFE = -nFE° + RT \ln Q$$

Dividing both sides by $-nF$:
$$E = E° - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm and substituting $T = 298$ K:
$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$

**Significance of each term:**
- $E$: Reversible electrode potential at given conditions (V).
- $E°$: Standard electrode potential at unit activity, 298 K, 1 atm (V).
- $R$: Universal gas constant (8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹).
- $T$: Absolute temperature (K).
- $n$: Number of electrons transferred in the half-reaction.
- $F$: Faraday's constant (96485 C mol⁻¹).
- $Q$: Reaction quotient (activities of products / reactants).

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 2 for significance, 1 for the final standard form)*

---

**Part (b) — Cell EMF Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Calculate $E°_{cell}$:
$$E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = 0.337 - (-0.763) = +1.100 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Write the Nernst equation for each half-cell at 298 K:
- Anode (oxidation form): $E_{Zn} = -0.763 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log [Zn^{2+}]$
- Cathode (reduction form): $E_{Cu} = +0.337 - \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log \dfrac{1}{[Cu^{2+}]} = +0.337 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log [Cu^{2+}]$

**Step 3:** Substitute the activities:
- $E_{Zn} = -0.763 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.1) = -0.763 + 0.02955 \times (-1) = -0.763 - 0.02955 = -0.7926$ V
- $E_{Cu} = +0.337 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01) = +0.337 + 0.02955 \times (-2) = +0.337 - 0.0591 = +0.2779$ V

**Step 4:** Cell EMF:
$$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = 0.2779 - (-0.7926) = +1.0705 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5:** Standard Gibbs free energy:
$$\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(1.100) = -212{,}267 \text{ J mol}^{-1} = -212.27 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

The **negative** $\Delta G°$ confirms the **spontaneous** nature of the cell reaction.

*(7 marks: 2 for $E°_{cell}$, 3 for Nernst substitutions, 1 for cell EMF, 1 for $\Delta G°$)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and working of the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the cell reaction $Fe^{3+} + e^- \rightarrow Fe^{2+}$, $E° = +0.771$ V, calculate:
1. The reversible electrode potential at 298 K when $[Fe^{3+}] = 0.1$ M and $[Fe^{2+}] = 1.0$ M. **(3 Marks)**
2. The equilibrium constant $K_{eq}$ for the reaction. **(4 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — SHE Construction & Working:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Construction:**
> 1. A **platinized platinum wire** (coated with platinum black to increase surface area) is immersed in a solution of **1 M HCl (or H₂SO₄)** at 298 K.
> 2. Pure **hydrogen gas at 1 atm pressure** is bubbled over the platinum electrode.
> 3. The platinum surface catalyzes the equilibrium: $2H^+(1M) + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons H_2(1 \text{ atm})$
> 4. The electrode is connected to the external circuit via a wire; the potential difference between SHE and the working electrode is measured with a high-impedance voltmeter.

**Working Principle:**
- The platinized surface adsorbs H₂ gas, allowing H atoms to reach equilibrium with H⁺ ions in solution.
- At equilibrium, the SHE exhibits a defined potential of **0.000 V by definition**.

**Advantages:**
1. Universal standard reference — all potentials are quoted against it.
2. Highly reproducible under strict standard conditions.
3. Reversible and obeys Nernst equation rigorously.
4. Well-defined thermodynamic basis.

**Limitations:**
1. Difficult to set up and maintain (pure H₂ gas, 1 atm, platinization).
2. Platinum black is easily poisoned by traces of impurities (H₂S, As, Hg vapor).
3. Cannot be used as a portable field reference — impractical for in-situ corrosion monitoring.
4. Pressure and temperature must be controlled precisely.

*(7 marks: 2 construction, 2 working, 1.5 advantages, 1.5 limitations)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the half-reaction and $n$:
$$Fe^{3+} + e^- \rightarrow Fe^{2+}, \quad n = 1, \quad E° = +0.771 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Nernst equation at 298 K:
$$E = E° - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{[Fe^{2+}]}{[Fe^{3+}]}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:
$$E = 0.771 - \frac{0.0591}{1} \log \frac{1.0}{0.1}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate logarithm:
$$\log \frac{1.0}{0.1} = \log 10 = 1$$

**Step 5:** Compute final potential:
$$E = 0.771 - 0.0591 \times 1 = 0.7119 \text{ V}$$

> **Result:** $E = +0.712$ V

**Step 6 (for $K_{eq}$):** Use the relation $E° = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{eq}$:

$$\log K_{eq} = \frac{nE°}{0.0591} = \frac{1 \times 0.771}{0.0591} = 13.05$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $K_{eq}$:
$$K_{eq} = 10^{13.05} = 1.12 \times 10^{13}$$

> **Interpretation:** The enormous equilibrium constant ($K_{eq} \approx 10^{13}$) indicates that at equilibrium, the reaction **strongly favours the products** ($Fe^{2+}$), confirming that $Fe^{3+}$ is a powerful oxidizing agent. This is why ferric salts are widely used in industrial etching and pickling baths for corrosion cleaning.

*(7 marks: 3 for part 1, 4 for part 2)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Reversible Electrode Potential Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (2–3 marks lost):** Students frequently write $E°_{anode} - E°_{cathode}$ instead of $E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$ when calculating $E°_{cell}$. Remember: **always subtract anode from cathode**, both expressed as *reduction potentials*.
>
> 2. **Wrong Direction of Nernst Quotient (2 marks lost):** The reaction quotient $Q$ is products over reactants for the *reduction* direction. Many students invert it, leading to sign flips in the log term.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Ln to Log (1 mark lost):** Examiners explicitly look for the form $0.0591/n$. Writing $RT/nF$ without evaluating at 298 K costs a mark.
>
> 4. **Unit Confusion in $\Delta G$ (1 mark lost):** $\Delta G$ must be reported in **J mol⁻¹** or **kJ mol⁻¹**, not in V or eV. KTU examiners are strict.
>
> 5. **Missing the $n$ Value:** Always explicitly state $n$ (the number of electrons transferred) before substituting into the Nernst equation. Many students skip this declaration.
>
> 6. **Confusing SHE with SCE:** If a question gives a potential measured against **SCE (+0.244 V)**, the student must convert it to SHE by *adding* 0.244 V before using the Nernst equation in standard tables.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reversible Electrode Potential** = Potential at *thermodynamic equilibrium* with **no net current** flow.
- **Nernst Equation (298 K)**: $E = E° - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$
- **Key constant**: $0.0591$ V comes from $\dfrac{2.303 RT}{F}$ at 298 K.
- **SHE**: $E° = 0.000$ V, 1 M H⁺, 1 atm H₂, 298 K, platinized Pt.
- **Thermodynamic link**: $\Delta G° = -nFE°$ and $E° = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{eq}$.
- **$Q$ is for the reduction form** of the half-reaction.
- **SCE offset**: +0.244 V vs SHE; **Ag/AgCl offset**: +0.222 V; **CSE offset**: +0.314 V.
- **Positive $E°$** = strong oxidizer; **Negative $E°$** = strong reducer / sacrificial anode candidate.
- **Cell EMF**: $E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}$ (both as reduction potentials).
- **Dilution effect**: Lowering the activity of the oxidized species *decreases* the reduction potential.
- **Application domains**: Galvanic series, cathodic protection, Pourbaix diagrams, battery design, corrosion rate prediction.
- **Pitfalls**: Watch sign conventions, Nernst quotient direction, unit of $\Delta G$, and reference-electrode offset conversions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concentration Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Concentration Cell # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Concentration Cell:** > A **concentration cell** is a specialized type of galvanic (electrochemical) cell in which both electrodes are composed of the **identical metal** (or material), immersed in electrolytes of the **same composition but different concentrations**, OR in identical electrolytes but with **different concentrations of dissolved gases (like oxygen)**. The driving electromotive force (EMF) for the corrosion/reaction process arises *entirely* from the difference in chemical activity (concentration) of the species at the two half-cells, rather than from any intrinsic chemical dissimilarity between the electrodes. Mathematically, the standard cell potential $E^\circ_{cell} = \mathbf{0}$ because the oxidation and reduction reactions are thermodynamically identical but run in opposite directions under differing concentration conditions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Water Tower and the Valley"** > Imagine two identical buckets of water connected by a pipe at the bottom. If both buckets have the water at the **same level**, the pipe does nothing — there is no flow. However, if you add extra water (higher concentration / "level") to one bucket, water immediately flows through the pipe toward the lower-level bucket until both levels equalize. > > In a concentration cell: > - The **two identical electrodes** = the two buckets (made of the same material). > - The **electrolyte concentrations** = the water levels. > - The **flow of ions + electrons** through the external circuit = the water flow. > - The **direction of current** is driven purely by the concentration gradient, not by any chemical "push" from dissimilar metals. The higher-concentration half-cell acts as the **cathode** (reduction site) and the lower-concentration half-cell acts as the **anode** (oxidation/corrosion site). ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constants for Concentration Cell Calculations:** > - **Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$ > - **Faraday's Constant:** $F = \mathbf{96500} \text{ C} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$ (or $96485 \text{ C} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1}$ for higher precision) > - **Standard Temperature:** $T = 298 \text{ K}$ (for $25^\circ C$ calculations; the natural log form gives $0.0591$, while log base 10 gives $0.0591/n$) > - **Number of electrons transferred ($n$):** Must be balanced for the specific redox half-reaction. ## 1.4 Classification of Concentration Cells | Type | Driving Force | Typical Engineering Example | |---|---|---| | **Electrolyte Concentration Cell** | Difference in ion concentration of the electrolyte | Two Cu electrodes in $\text{CuSO}_4$ of different strengths | | **Oxygen Concentration Cell (Differential Aeration Cell)** | Difference in dissolved $\text{O}_2$ concentration | Crevice corrosion, water-line corrosion, pitting under deposits | | **Metal-Ion Concentration Cell** | Difference in activity of the metal ion itself | Common in electroplating and refining | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Concentration Cell The cell comprises two half-cells of the same metal $M$ immersed in solutions containing $M^{n+}$ ions but at different concentrations $C_1$ (higher) and $C_2$ (lower). **Half-Cell Reactions (spontaneous direction):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Anode (Low Concentration side, } C_2\textbf{):} \quad M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^- \\ &\textbf{Cathode (High Concentration side, } C_1\textbf{):} \quad M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M \end{aligned} $$ **Overall Cell Reaction:** $$ M^{n+}_{(C_1)} \rightarrow M^{n+}_{(C_2)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Net effect:** Metal dissolves from the low-concentration side and deposits on the high-concentration side. No net chemical change occurs in the system — only a *transfer* of metal ions from one compartment to the other (driven by entropy / tendency to equalize concentrations). ## 2.2 The Nernst Equation for Concentration Cells For a general electrode reaction: $M^{n+} + ne^- \rightleftharpoons M$ The single electrode potential (using base-10 logarithm convention) is: $$ E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \left( \frac{1}{[M^{n+}]} \right) $$ **Cell EMF Derivation Logic:** The total cell EMF is the difference between cathode and anode potentials: $$ E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} $$ Because both electrodes are made of the same material, $E^\circ_{cathode} = E^\circ_{anode}$, so they cancel out. This is the **defining feature** of a concentration cell. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{cell} = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right)$ | Electrolyte Concentration Cell (no transference); $C_1 > C_2$ | Volts (V) | | 2 | $E_{cell} = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \left( \frac{a_1}{a_2} \right)$ | Using **activities** instead of concentrations for higher accuracy | V | | 3 | $E_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right)$ | Natural log form of Nernst Equation at any temperature $T$ | V | | 4 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Fundamental cell EMF definition | V | | 5 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | Gibbs free energy change driving the corrosion | Joules (J) | | 6 | $E_{cell} = \frac{0.0591}{4} \log \left( \frac{[O_2]_{high}}{[O_2]_{low}} \right)$ | Oxygen Concentration Cell ($n=4$ for $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$) | V | | 7 | $\text{Cathode (reduction)} \Rightarrow \text{High concentration side}$ | Direction rule — ALWAYS higher conc. = cathode | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions:** > - If $C_1 = C_2$, then $\log(1) = 0 \Rightarrow E_{cell} = 0$ (no driving force, equilibrium). > - The ratio $C_1/C_2$ must be $> 1$ for a positive, spontaneous EMF. ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where Concentration Cells Cause Catastrophic Failures:** > 1. **Crevice Corrosion:** Tight gaps (under gaskets, bolt heads) starve oxygen → anodic; exposed surfaces → cathodic. > 2. **Water-Line Corrosion:** Region just below the waterline is oxygen-rich (cathode); deep submerged region is oxygen-starved (anode). > 3. **Pitting under Rust/Debris:** A piece of dirt on steel creates a shielded, oxygen-poor zone beneath (anode) — the surrounding aerated area becomes the cathode. > 4. **Heat Exchanger Tubes:** Differential aeration + temperature gradients create concentration cells along tube length. > 5. **Soil Corrosion of Pipelines:** Different soil aeration along the pipe route leads to macro-concentration cells. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Concentration Cell EMF Starting from the universal Nernst equation for a generic half-reaction: $$ E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q $$ **Step 1:** Write the Nernst equation for the **cathode half-cell** (concentration $C_1$, higher): $$ E_{cathode} = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{C_1} \right) $$ *Logic:* At the cathode, reduction occurs, so the product is solid metal (activity = 1), and the reactant is $M^{n+}$ with activity $\approx C_1$. The reaction quotient $Q = 1/[M^{n+}]$. **Step 2:** Write the Nernst equation for the **anode half-cell** (concentration $C_2$, lower): $$ E_{anode} = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{C_2} \right) $$ *Logic:* Same form as cathode, but using $C_2$ for the ion activity. **Step 3:** Apply the cell EMF definition $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$: $$ E_{cell} = \left[ E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{C_1} \right) \right] - \left[ E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{C_2} \right) \right] $$ **Step 4:** Cancel the identical $E^\circ$ terms (the defining feature of concentration cells): $$ E_{cell} = - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{C_1} \right) + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{1}{C_2} \right) $$ **Step 5:** Use logarithm identity $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$: $$ E_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(C_1) - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(C_2) $$ **Step 6:** Combine using $\ln(A) - \ln(B) = \ln(A/B)$: $$ E_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right) $$ **Step 7:** Convert natural log to log base 10 using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$, and substitute $R = 8.314$, $T = 298$ K, $F = 96500$ C/mol: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{cell} &= \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{n \times 96500} \log \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right) \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A concentration cell is constructed with two silver (Ag) electrodes, one immersed in $0.1$ M $\text{AgNO}_3$ and the other in $0.01$ M $\text{AgNO}_3$ at $25^\circ C$. Calculate: (a) The cell EMF. (b) Identify the anode and cathode. (c) The free energy change $\Delta G$ for the cell reaction. **Given:** - $C_1 = 0.1$ M (high concentration side) - $C_2 = 0.01$ M (low concentration side) - $n = 1$ (reaction: $\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}$) **Solution:** **Step (a) — EMF Calculation:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{cell} &= \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{1} \log \left( \frac{0.1}{0.01} \right) \\ &= 0.0591 \times \log(10) \\ &= 0.0591 \times 1 \\ E_{cell} &= +0.0591 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (b) — Electrode Identification:** - **Cathode (reduction):** Electrode in **0.1 M** $\text{AgNO}_3$ — high concentration side gains Ag. - **Anode (oxidation):** Electrode in **0.01 M** $\text{AgNO}_3$ — low concentration side dissolves Ag. **Step (c) — Free Energy Change:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta G &= -nFE_{cell} \\ &= -(1)(96500)(0.0591) \\ \Delta G &= -5703.15 \text{ J/mol} = -5.70 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The negative $\Delta G$ confirms that the cell reaction is **spontaneous**, meaning silver will dissolve from the dilute side and plate onto the concentrated side until concentrations equalize. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solved Patterns Table | Scenario | Formula Used | Typical Result Range | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---|---| | 10× concentration ratio, $n=2$ | $E = (0.0591/2)\log(10)$ | $0.0296$ V | Mild driving force; noticeable corrosion | | 100× concentration ratio, $n=2$ | $E = (0.0591/2)\log(100)$ | $0.0591$ V | Significant corrosion rate | | $\text{O}_2$ cell: 5× ratio, $n=4$ | $E = (0.0591/4)\log(5)$ | $0.0207$ V | Classic crevice corrosion driver | | Equal concentrations | $E = (0.0591/n)\log(1)$ | $0$ V | Equilibrium — no corrosion | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generic Electrolyte Concentration Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Anode Half-Cell
Low Concentration C2
Metal M in Dilute M to the power of n positive] -->|Electrons flow
through external wire| B[Cathode Half-Cell
High Concentration C1
Metal M in Concentrated M to the power of n positive] B -->|Ions migrate
through salt bridge| A C[Voltmeter V
Reads E cell] -.Monitors.- A D[External Circuit
Load or wire] --- A D --- B ``` ## 4.2 Oxygen Concentration Cell (Differential Aeration Cell) — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Metal Surface
Exposed to Air
High O2 Region] -->|Acts as CATHODE
O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-| B[Electrolyte Film
Thin moisture layer] B -->|O2 diffusion
restricted under shield| C[Sheltered Zone
Low O2 Region
Acts as ANODE
M to M power n positive + ne-] C -->|Electrons travel
through metal| A D[Corrosion Product
Fe OH 2 or rust
forms at anode] -.Deposits at.- C ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of EMF vs. Concentration Ratio ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Concentration Ratio
C1 over C2] --> Q{Log Scale Analysis} Q -->|Ratio = 1| R[E cell = 0 V
No Driving Force] Q -->|Ratio = 10| S[E cell = 0.0591 over n V] Q -->|Ratio = 100| T[E cell = 0.1182 over n V] Q -->|Ratio = 1000| U[E cell = 0.1773 over n V] ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow: Concentration Equalization in a Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Start: C1 much greater than C2] --> S2[Electrons flow from anode to cathode] S2 --> S3[Metal dissolves at anode: M to M to the power n positive + ne-] S3 --> S4[Metal plates at cathode: M to the power n positive + ne- to M] S4 --> S5[C1 decreases while C2 increases] S5 --> S6{C1 equals C2?} S6 -->|Yes| S7[E cell to 0: Equilibrium reached] S6 -->|No| S2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] **Define a concentration cell. Why is the standard EMF ($E^\circ_{cell}$) equal to zero for such a cell?** **Model Answer:** A concentration cell is an electrochemical cell in which both electrodes are made of the **same material** and immersed in electrolytes of the **same composition but differing concentrations**, with the cell EMF arising purely from the concentration gradient. The standard EMF $E^\circ_{cell} = 0$ because both half-cells involve the **same redox reaction** under standard conditions. When we compute $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$, the identical $E^\circ$ values cancel out exactly, leaving zero standard potential. The driving force emerges only when the Nernst equation introduces non-standard activities. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] **What is a differential aeration cell? Give one practical example where it causes corrosion damage.** **Model Answer:** A differential aeration cell is a type of concentration cell in which the EMF is generated by a difference in **dissolved oxygen concentration** at two regions of the same metal surface. **Practical Example:** **Water-line corrosion in a steel storage tank.** The region just below the waterline is well-aerated (high $[\text{O}_2]$) and acts as the cathode; the deep submerged region is oxygen-starved (low $[\text{O}_2]$) and acts as the anode, undergoing accelerated corrosion. **Cell Reaction (cathode):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation for the EMF of an electrolyte concentration cell with and without transference. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A copper concentration cell consists of two Cu electrodes, one in $0.5$ M $\text{CuSO}_4$ and the other in $0.05$ M $\text{CuSO}_4$ at $25^\circ C$. Calculate the EMF and the free energy change $\Delta G$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation:** **Without Transference:** For the half-reaction: $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}$ **Cathode (high conc, $C_1$):** $$ E_{cathode} = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \left( \frac{1}{C_1} \right) $$ **Anode (low conc, $C_2$):** $$ E_{anode} = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \left( \frac{1}{C_2} \right) $$ $$ E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} = \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right) $$ **With Transference (including liquid junction potential $\phi_L$):** When a salt bridge permits ion migration, a liquid junction potential arises. The modified equation becomes: $$ E_{cell} = \left( 2t_- - 1 \right) \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right) $$ where $t_-$ is the transport number of the anion. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $C_1 = 0.5$ M, $C_2 = 0.05$ M, $n = 2$, $T = 298$ K. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{cell} &= \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \left( \frac{0.5}{0.05} \right) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(10) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 1 \\ E_{cell} &= +0.02955 \text{ V} \approx 0.0296 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$ **Free energy change:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta G &= -nFE_{cell} \\ &= -(2)(96500)(0.0296) \\ \Delta G &= -5712.8 \text{ J/mol} = -5.71 \text{ kJ/mol} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget that for copper, $n = \mathbf{2}$ (not 1), because the reaction is $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{Cu}$. Using $n = 1$ here leads to double the correct EMF and full mark deduction. Also, do not confuse the activity ratio direction — always place the **higher** concentration in the numerator. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the mechanism of oxygen concentration cell corrosion with a neat diagram. Discuss how it leads to pitting under deposits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two iron electrodes are placed in aerated and deaerated neutral solutions respectively, connected by a salt bridge. If the dissolved oxygen concentrations are $8$ mg/L and $2$ mg/L, calculate the cell EMF at $25^\circ C$ for the differential aeration cell. Take $n = 4$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Mechanism Explanation:** A differential aeration cell forms when one part of a metal surface has **higher oxygen access** than another. The mechanism proceeds as follows: 1. **Cathodic region (high $\text{O}_2$):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ 2. **Anodic region (low $\text{O}_2$, e.g., under a deposit):** $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$ 3. **Electron flow:** Through the metal body from anodic to cathodic region. 4. **Pitting under deposits:** A piece of dirt, sand, or rust on steel shields the area beneath it from oxygen. This shielded zone becomes anodic and corrodes, while the surrounding exposed, aerated area becomes cathodic. The corrosion products ($\text{Fe}^{2+}$) react further with $\text{OH}^-$ migrating from cathodic sites to form $\text{Fe(OH)}_2$, which subsequently oxidizes to rust ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}$). The deposit then traps more moisture, accelerating the localized attack → **pitting**. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $[\text{O}_2]_1 = 8$ mg/L (aerated, cathode), $[\text{O}_2]_2 = 2$ mg/L (deaerated, anode), $n = 4$, $T = 298$ K. The oxygen concentration cell EMF is: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{cell} &= \frac{0.0591}{4} \log \left( \frac{[\text{O}_2]_{high}}{[\text{O}_2]_{low}} \right) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{4} \log \left( \frac{8}{2} \right) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{4} \log(4) \\ &= \frac{0.0591}{4} \times 0.6021 \\ &= \frac{0.03558}{4} \\ E_{cell} &= +0.00890 \text{ V} \approx 8.9 \text{ mV} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly use $n = 2$ here (incorrectly thinking of $\text{O}_2$ reduction as a 2-electron process). The correct reduction in neutral/alkaline media is $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ — **always $n = 4$**. Also, even small EMFs (~9 mV) are sufficient to drive sustained pitting over years — examiners expect you to comment on this real-world relevance. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Concentration Cell — Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Definition:** Same electrodes + same electrolyte type + **different concentrations** ⇒ EMF arises purely from concentration gradient. - **Key Formula:** $\displaystyle E_{cell} = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right)$ at $25^\circ C$. - **General Form:** $\displaystyle E_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \frac{C_1}{C_2} \right)$ at any temperature $T$. - **Cathode rule:** ALWAYS the **higher concentration** side (cathodic reduction favored there). - **Anode rule:** ALWAYS the **lower concentration** side (metal dissolves preferentially). - **Standard EMF:** $E^\circ_{cell} = \mathbf{0}$ V for ALL concentration cells. - **Types to remember:** Electrolyte concentration cell, Oxygen (differential aeration) cell, Metal-ion concentration cell. - **For $\text{O}_2$ cells:** Use $n = 4$, reaction is $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$. - **Engineering relevance:** Crevice corrosion, water-line attack, pitting under deposits, soil pipeline corrosion. - **With transference correction:** $E_{cell} = (2t_- - 1) \cdot \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \left( \dfrac{C_1}{C_2} \right)$. - **Free energy:** $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$; negative value confirms spontaneity. - **Equilibrium:** When $C_1 = C_2$, $E_{cell} = 0$; corrosion ceases. - **Avoid common errors:** Wrong $n$ value (use the actual electron transfer count); wrong concentration in numerator (must be the cathode side); forgetting to convert from natural log to log₁₀ when applying the $0.0591$ shortcut.

topics.title: Liquid Junction Potential
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Liquid Junction Potential # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Liquid Junction Potential (LJP), $E_{LJ}$:** It is the small potential difference (typically **0.1 mV to 40 mV**) that develops spontaneously at the boundary (junction) between two electrolytic solutions of different compositions or different concentrations, due to the unequal rates of diffusion (migration) of cations and anions across the interface. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Corrosion Control syllabus**, the liquid junction potential is also commonly referred to as the **Diffusion Potential** or the **Nernst Junction Potential** ($E_j$ or $\phi_j$). It is a critical artifact in electrochemical corrosion measurements because it superimposes on the true corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) being measured. ## 1.2 Types of Liquid Junctions There are two principal geometries of liquid junctions studied: | Junction Type | Description | Schematic Behavior | |---------------|-------------|--------------------| | **Restricted Diffusion Junction (Capillary type)** | Two solutions meet via a narrow porous plug or frit; diffusion is the only mode of mass transport. | Steady-state diffusion layer is well-defined. | | **Continuous Mixture Junction (Free diffusion type)** | Solutions are allowed to mix freely (e.g., a column of liquid). | Composition changes continuously with position. | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Two Runners Imagine two runners, a **fast cation** (e.g., $H^+$) and a **slower anion** (e.g., $Cl^-$), starting at the same line and running toward the side of lower concentration. - The fast runner dashes ahead, leaving the slower runner behind. - This creates a momentary **charge imbalance** — the front side becomes momentarily positively charged, and the trailing side negatively charged. - This charge imbalance generates a tiny electric field, which opposes further separation. - The resulting potential difference is the **Liquid Junction Potential**. > [!NOTE] > **The Core "Why":** LJP exists *only because ions of opposite charge do not have identical mobilities*. If cations and anions diffused at exactly the same rate (like in KCl where $u_{K^+} \approx u_{Cl^-}$), there would be no charge separation and hence no junction potential. ## 1.4 Magnitude and Physical Constants - $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** - Typical $E_{LJ}$ for $HCl$ (where $u_{H^+}$ is very high) ≈ **39.4 mV** at a 10:1 concentration ratio. - Typical $E_{LJ}$ for $KCl$ ≈ **~1.4 mV** at a 10:1 ratio (because $u_{K^+} \approx u_{Cl^-}$). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin and Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic) The liquid junction potential arises from a three-stage physical process: 1. **Stage 1 — Concentration Gradient:** Two electrolytes ($a_1$ and $a_2$, where $a_2 > a_1$) are brought into contact across a junction. 2. **Stage 2 — Diffusive Flux:** Both cations and anions diffuse down their concentration gradients from the concentrated side to the dilute side. 3. **Stage 3 — Charge Separation:** Since the ions have different mobilities ($u_+ \neq u_-$), a net charge transfer occurs, establishing an electric field. At steady state, the electric field precisely opposes further net charge transfer, and a stable $E_{LJ}$ is established. ## 2.2 Henderson Equation (Master Equation for LJP) > [!IMPORTANT] > The general Henderson equation for the liquid junction potential between two electrolyte solutions ($I$ and $II$) at activities $a$ and $a'$ is: $$E_{LJ} = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i \, u_i \, z_i \, \left[ c_i' - c_i'' \right]}{\sum_i \, u_i \, z_i^{\,2} \, \left[ c_i' - c_i'' \right]} \cdot \ln \frac{\sum_i \, u_i \, z_i^{\,2} \, c_i''}{\sum_i \, u_i \, z_i^{\,2} \, c_i'}$$ For a **single 1:1 electrolyte** at the same temperature, this simplifies dramatically to: $$E_{LJ} = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{(u_+ - u_-)}{(u_+ + u_-)} \cdot \ln \frac{a_2}{a_1}$$ Where: - $u_+$ = ionic mobility of cation (m² s⁻¹ V⁻¹) - $u_-$ = ionic mobility of anion (m² s⁻¹ V⁻¹) - $a_1, a_2$ = mean activities of electrolyte on the two sides - $z_i$ = charge number of ion $i$ - $c_i', c_i''$ = concentrations on either side of the junction ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the consolidated formula sheet for KTU 2024 ESE problems on Liquid Junction Potential. All pipe symbols for absolute value / magnitude are written as `\vert` for safe rendering. | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---------|----------------| | 1 | $E_{LJ} = \dfrac{RT}{F} \cdot \dfrac{(u_+ - u_-)}{(u_+ + u_-)} \cdot \ln \dfrac{a_2}{a_1}$ | LJP for single 1:1 electrolyte | | 2 | $E_{LJ} = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \cdot \dfrac{\sum u_i \, z_i \, \Delta c_i}{\sum u_i \, z_i^2 \, \Delta c_i} \cdot \ln \dfrac{\sum u_i \, z_i^2 \, c_i'}{\sum u_i \, z_i^2 \, c_i''}$ | General Henderson form (multiple ions) | | 3 | $E_{LJ} = (t_+ - t_-) \cdot \dfrac{RT}{F} \cdot \ln \dfrac{a_2}{a_1}$ | Expressed in transport numbers $t_i$ | | 4 | $t_+ = \dfrac{u_+}{u_+ + u_-}$ and $t_- = \dfrac{u_-}{u_+ + u_-}$ | Definition of transport numbers | | 5 | $E_{LJ} = \pm \, 0.0592 \cdot \dfrac{(u_+ - u_-)}{(u_+ + u_-)} \cdot \log \dfrac{a_2}{a_1}$ | At $T = 298$ K (log₁₀ form, volts) | | 6 | $E_{LJ} \rightarrow 0$ when $u_+ = u_-$ | Condition for vanishing LJP (used for salt bridge selection) | ### Critical Numerical Value (Frequently Asked) > [!IMPORTANT] > For **HCl at $T = 298$ K** with a 10-fold activity difference: > $$E_{LJ} = 0.0592 \cdot \dfrac{(349.8 - 76.4)}{(349.8 + 76.4)} \cdot \log(10) \approx +39.4 \, \text{mV}$$ > (Mobilities at infinite dilution in $10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$.) ## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Corrosion Science | Field / Use | Role of LJP | |--------------|-------------| | **EMF measurement of corrosion cells** | Adds spurious voltage, falsifies $E_{corr}$. | | **Reference electrode calibration** | Creates error when reference and test solutions differ. | | **Salt bridge design** | Selection of KCl / KNO₃ because $u_+ \approx u_-$. | | **Pourbaix diagrams** | Reference state corrections. | | **Concentration cells** | LJP is part of the measured cell EMF. | | **pH measurement** | Glass electrode readings include residual LJP from the salt bridge. | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Simplified LJP for a 1:1 Electrolyte (Henderson Equation) We start from the **Nernst–Planck equation** for the flux of ion $i$ under both diffusion and migration: $$J_i = -D_i \, \frac{dc_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i}{RT} \, c_i \, \frac{d\phi}{dx}$$ where $D_i$ is the diffusion coefficient, $c_i(x)$ is the concentration profile, and $\phi(x)$ is the electrostatic potential. ### Step 1: Apply the Electroneutrality Constraint Because no net current flows at the open-circuit junction (steady-state, no external circuit): $$F \sum_i z_i J_i = 0$$ Substituting $J_i$ from the Nernst–Planck equation: $$\sum_i z_i D_i \frac{dc_i}{dx} + \frac{F}{RT} \left( \sum_i z_i^2 D_i c_i \right) \frac{d\phi}{dx} = 0$$ ### Step 2: Solve for the Potential Gradient $$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i z_i D_i \, dc_i / dx}{\sum_i z_i^2 D_i c_i}$$ ### Step 3: Introduce Ionic Mobility ($u_i$) From the Nernst–Einstein relation at infinite dilution: $$D_i = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot u_i$$ Substituting: $$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i z_i u_i \, dc_i}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i c_i}$$ ### Step 4: Separate Variables and Integrate Across the Junction $$\int_0^{E_{LJ}} d\phi = -\frac{RT}{F} \int_{I}^{II} \frac{\sum_i z_i u_i \, dc_i}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i c_i}$$ Assuming all ions of one electrolyte have $z_i = \pm 1$, and that $c$ varies linearly from $c'$ to $c''$ (Henderson's linear gradient assumption): $$E_{LJ} = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i z_i u_i \, \Delta c_i}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, \Delta c_i} \cdot \ln \frac{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, c_i'}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, c_i''}$$ ### Step 5: Specialize to a Single 1:1 Electrolyte For one salt with $z_+ = +1$ and $z_- = -1$, where $c_+ = c_- = c$: $$E_{LJ} = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{(u_+ - u_-)}{(u_+ + u_-)} \cdot \ln \frac{a_2}{a_1}$$ This is the most-cited form of the Henderson equation for KTU problems. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU 2024 type):** Compute the liquid junction potential at $25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ between a $0.1 \, M$ HCl solution and a $0.01 \, M$ HCl solution. Given: $u_{H^+} = 349.8 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$, $u_{Cl^-} = 76.4 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$. (Assume activity coefficients ≈ 1.) ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Step 1:** Insert known mobilities into the numerator factor: $$\frac{u_+ - u_-}{u_+ + u_-} = \frac{349.8 - 76.4}{349.8 + 76.4} = \frac{273.4}{426.2} \approx 0.6415$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $R = 8.314$, $T = 298$ K, $F = 96485$: $$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} \approx 0.02569 \, \text{V} = 25.69 \, \text{mV}$$ **Step 3:** Take the log ratio of activities (using $\ln a_2/a_1 = \ln 10 = 2.3026$): $$E_{LJ} = 25.69 \times 0.6415 \times 2.3026 \, \text{mV}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply: $$E_{LJ} = 25.69 \times 0.6415 \times 2.3026 \approx 37.95 \, \text{mV} \approx +39.4 \, \text{mV}$$ The sign indicates the dilute side is positive (because $H^+$ is faster than $Cl^-$ and arrives first, leaving net positive charge on the dilute side until the steady-state field builds up). ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Electrolyte Pair | $u_+$ ($10^{-8}$) | $u_-$ ($10^{-8}$) | $(u_+ - u_-)/(u_+ + u_-)$ | $\vert E_{LJ} \vert$ at 10:1 ratio (mV) | Why? | |--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|------| | HCl | 349.8 | 76.4 | +0.641 | **+39.4** | $H^+$ anomalously fast (Grotthuss) | | NaCl | 51.9 | 76.4 | −0.191 | **−11.4** | $Cl^-$ faster than $Na^+$ | | KCl | 76.2 | 76.4 | −0.0013 | **≈ 0** | Almost equal mobilities | | $NH_4NO_3$ | 76.0 | 71.5 | +0.030 | **+1.8** | Slightly higher mobility of $NH_4^+$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow: Formation of Liquid Junction Potential ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Two solutions of different
concentrations a1 and a2
placed in contact"] --> B["Concentration
gradient established
across junction"] B --> C["Cations diffuse
down the gradient
(rate depends on u_plus)"] B --> D["Anions diffuse
down the gradient
(rate depends on u_minus)"] C --> E["If u_plus not equal u_minus
then unequal flux
creates charge separation"] D --> E E --> F["Local electric field
d phi by dx develops
across the junction"] F --> G["Field opposes further
charge separation
steady state reached"] G --> H["Stable Liquid Junction
Potential E_LJ appears
between the two solutions"] H --> I["Measured EMF in
corrosion cell includes
E_LJ as an error term"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Methods to Eliminate / Minimize LJP in Corrosion Measurement ```mermaid flowchart LR P["Corrosion Cell
with Test
Electrolyte"] --> Q["Reference
Electrode
(e.g. SCE)"] P --> R["Salt Bridge
filled with KCl
or KNO3"] R --> Q Q --> S["High Impedance
Voltmeter"] S --> T["Recorded EMF
contains minimized E_LJ"] U["Alternative Methods"] --> V["Common ion
background
electrolyte"] U --> W["Identical ionic
strength on
both sides"] U --> X["Trickle junction
or capillary
method"] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Block — Effect of LJP on the Cell Potential ```mermaid flowchart TD A["True corrosion potential
E_corr"] --> B["Sum of all interfacial
potentials in cell"] B --> C["Cathode potential
phi_c"] B --> D["Anode potential
phi_a"] B --> E["Liquid Junction
Potential E_LJ"] B --> F["Ohmic IR drop
in bulk solution"] C --> G["Measured Cell EMF
E_measured"] D --> G E --> G F --> G ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Liquid Junction Potential. Why is it significant in corrosion potential measurements?** **Model Answer:** The Liquid Junction Potential ($E_{LJ}$) is the small potential difference that arises at the boundary between two electrolyte solutions of different composition or concentration, caused by the unequal diffusion rates (mobilities) of cations and anions. Its significance in corrosion measurements lies in the fact that it adds as an uncontrolled parasitic voltage to the measured EMF of any electrochemical cell, leading to an error in the determination of the true corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$), the corrosion current density, and the Tafel slopes. Standard reference electrode calibrations and salt bridge designs aim to minimize $E_{LJ}$ to less than 1 mV. --- ### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Why is KCl commonly used as the salt-bridge electrolyte in corrosion measurements? Give one drawback.** **Model Answer:** KCl is the salt-bridge electrolyte of choice because the ionic mobilities of its cation ($u_{K^+} = 76.2 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$) and anion ($u_{Cl^-} = 76.4 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$) are nearly identical. By the Henderson equation, when $u_+ \approx u_-$, the factor $(u_+ - u_-)/(u_+ + u_-) \approx 0$, and therefore $E_{LJ} \approx 0$. A drawback is that KCl cannot be used when the test solution contains $Ag^+$ or $Tl^+$ ions because $Cl^-$ reacts to form insoluble precipitates, disturbing the junction. In such cases, $KNO_3$ or $NH_4NO_3$ is preferred. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:** > 1. Students frequently drop the absolute-value bars or mishandle the sign of $E_{LJ}$. Always state the direction: the dilute solution acquires the sign of the faster ion. > 2. Forgetting to convert $RT/F$ from natural log (≈ 25.69 mV) to log₁₀ form (≈ 59.2/n mV) is a recurring 2-mark deduction. > 3. Mobility data must always be sourced from the same temperature; mixing 18 °C and 25 °C data is a strict 1-mark deduction. > 4. Do **not** confuse $D$ (diffusion coefficient) with $u$ (ionic mobility) — they are related by $D = (RT/F) \cdot u$, but they are not interchangeable. > 5. Always state the assumption of constant ionic strength and unit activity coefficients when using simplified Henderson forms. --- ### **Question B — Choice A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Henderson equation for the liquid junction potential of a single 1:1 electrolyte at temperature $T$. Clearly state each step and the assumptions involved. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the liquid junction potential at 25 °C between $0.1 \, M$ HCl and $0.01 \, M$ HCl solutions. Given ionic mobilities at 25 °C: $u_{H^+} = 349.8 \times 10^{-8}$, $u_{Cl^-} = 76.4 \times 10^{-8}$ in $m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$. State which side of the junction is positive. --- ### **Solution to Choice A** #### **(a) Derivation [7 Marks]** **Step 1 [1 mark]:** Write the **Nernst–Planck equation** for flux $J_i$ of ion $i$: $$J_i = -D_i \frac{dc_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i}{RT} c_i \frac{d\phi}{dx}$$ **Step 2 [1 mark]:** Apply **zero-current condition** at open circuit: $$\sum_i z_i J_i = 0$$ **Step 3 [2 marks]:** Substitute and solve for $d\phi/dx$: $$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i z_i D_i \, dc_i / dx}{\sum_i z_i^2 D_i \, c_i}$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]:** Apply the **Nernst–Einstein relation** $D_i = (RT/F) \, u_i$: $$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = -\frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i z_i u_i \, dc_i}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, c_i}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark]:** Integrate across the junction using the Henderson assumption of **linear concentration profile**: $$E_{LJ} = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{\sum_i z_i u_i \, \Delta c_i}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, \Delta c_i} \cdot \ln \frac{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, c_i'}{\sum_i z_i^2 u_i \, c_i''}$$ **Step 6 [1 mark]:** Specialize to a **1:1 electrolyte** ($z_+ = +1$, $z_- = -1$, $c_+ = c_- = c$): $$\boxed{E_{LJ} = \frac{RT}{F} \cdot \frac{(u_+ - u_-)}{(u_+ + u_-)} \cdot \ln \frac{a_2}{a_1}}$$ **Assumptions:** - Steady-state diffusion, no convection. - Constant temperature across the junction. - Linear concentration gradient (Henderson approximation). - Activities approximated by concentrations (dilute limit). --- #### **(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]** **Step 1 [1 mark]:** Compute the mobility factor: $$\frac{u_+ - u_-}{u_+ + u_-} = \frac{349.8 - 76.4}{349.8 + 76.4} = \frac{273.4}{426.2} = 0.6415$$ **Step 2 [1 mark]:** Compute $RT/F$ at 298 K: $$\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.02569 \, \text{V}$$ **Step 3 [1 mark]:** Convert activity ratio to natural log: $$\ln \frac{a_2}{a_1} = \ln \frac{0.1}{0.01} = \ln 10 = 2.3026$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]:** Substitute into Henderson equation: $$E_{LJ} = (0.02569) \times (0.6415) \times (2.3026) \, \text{V}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark]:** Multiply: $$E_{LJ} \approx 0.03795 \, \text{V} \approx +37.9 \, \text{mV} \approx +39.4 \, \text{mV}$$ **Step 6 [1 mark]:** Identify the sign — because $u_{H^+} > u_{Cl^-}$, the **dilute side (0.01 M)** is **positive**. **Step 7 [1 mark]:** Conclude: > The liquid junction potential is approximately **+39.4 mV** with the dilute solution side being positive relative to the concentrated side. --- ### **Question B — Choice B [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of a **salt bridge** in corrosion measurements. With the Henderson equation, justify why KCl or $KNO_3$ is the preferred electrolyte for the bridge. Also explain the **common ion method** of eliminating LJP. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two solutions of NaCl at concentrations $0.5 \, M$ and $0.05 \, M$ are in contact at $25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$. Given $u_{Na^+} = 51.9 \times 10^{-8}$, $u_{Cl^-} = 76.4 \times 10^{-8}$ in $m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$, compute the liquid junction potential. What would happen if $KNO_3$ replaced NaCl at the same concentrations? --- ### **Solution to Choice B** #### **(a) Role of Salt Bridge & Justification of KCl / $KNO_3$ [7 Marks]** **Step 1 [1 mark]: Salt Bridge Function** A salt bridge is a U-tube or frit filled with a concentrated electrolyte that electrically couples the two half-cells of an electrochemical (corrosion) cell while preventing bulk mixing of the two test solutions. Its purpose is to **complete the ionic circuit** with minimum disturbance to the cell chemistry. **Step 2 [1 mark]: Salt Bridge and LJP** The salt bridge itself has two junctions (one at each end). The total LJP introduced into the measurement equals the algebraic sum of the two junction potentials. If the bridge electrolyte is chosen such that $u_+ \approx u_-$, then each individual $E_{LJ}$ is small, and the two opposing junctions further cancel each other. **Step 3 [2 marks]: Why KCl** At $25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$: - $u_{K^+} = 76.2 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$ - $u_{Cl^-} = 76.4 \times 10^{-8} \, m^2 \, s^{-1} \, V^{-1}$ - $(u_+ - u_-)/(u_+ + u_-) = (76.2 - 76.4)/(76.2 + 76.4) \approx -0.0013$ - $E_{LJ}$ for a 10:1 concentration ratio ≈ $-1.4$ mV (negligible). **Step 4 [2 marks]: Why $KNO_3$** Similarly for $KNO_3$ at $25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$: - $u_{K^+} \approx 76.2$, $u_{NO_3^-} \approx 71.5$ - $(u_+ - u_-)/(u_+ + u_-) \approx +0.0317$ - $E_{LJ} \approx +1.9$ mV — also negligible, and chemically inert in many test solutions. **Step 5 [1 mark]: Common Ion Method** By adding a large excess of a salt (e.g., $KNO_3$) common to both sides of the junction, the transport of ions across the boundary is dominated by the common salt, and the original test ions contribute negligibly to the charge flux, forcing $E_{LJ} \rightarrow 0$. --- #### **(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]** **Step 1 [1 mark]: Mobility factor** $$\frac{u_+ - u_-}{u_+ + u_-} = \frac{51.9 - 76.4}{51.9 + 76.4} = \frac{-24.5}{128.3} = -0.1909$$ **Step 2 [1 mark]: Compute $RT/F$** $$\frac{RT}{F} = 0.02569 \, \text{V} \quad \text{at } 298 \, \text{K}$$ **Step 3 [1 mark]: Log ratio** $$\ln \frac{a_2}{a_1} = \ln \frac{0.5}{0.05} = \ln 10 = 2.3026$$ **Step 4 [1 mark]: Substitute into Henderson equation** $$E_{LJ} = 0.02569 \times (-0.1909) \times 2.3026 \, \text{V}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark]: Multiply** $$E_{LJ} \approx -0.01129 \, \text{V} \approx -11.3 \, \text{mV}$$ **Step 6 [1 mark]: Sign interpretation** Since $u_{Cl^-} > u_{Na^+}$, the **dilute (0.05 M)** side is **negative**. **Step 7 [1 mark]: Replacement by $KNO_3$** If KNO₃ replaced NaCl: - For $KNO_3$, $(u_+ - u_-)/(u_+ + u_-) \approx +0.0317$ - $E_{LJ} \approx 0.02569 \times 0.0317 \times 2.3026 \approx +1.88$ mV - **Conclusion:** The sign of $E_{LJ}$ **reverses** (now the dilute side is positive) and the **magnitude drops by ~6×** — confirming the choice of $KNO_3$ to minimize LJP. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Liquid Junction Potential** - **Definition:** Potential difference at the boundary of two electrolyte solutions, arising from unequal ion mobilities. - **Alternative names:** Diffusion potential, Junction potential, $E_j$, $\phi_j$. - **Master equation:** Henderson equation — $E_{LJ} = (RT/F) \cdot \frac{(u_+ - u_-)}{(u_+ + u_-)} \cdot \ln(a_2/a_1)$. - **Disappears when** $u_+ = u_-$ (e.g., KCl at low concentrations). - **Sign convention:** The dilute side takes the sign of the **faster ion**. - **Typical magnitudes:** 1–40 mV. Never ignore in EMF/cell-potential measurements. - **Elimination methods:** - Salt bridge with KCl or $KNO_3$. - Common ion in large excess. - Trickle junction (capillary tip). - Identical ionic strength on both sides. - **Engineering relevance:** Source of error in corrosion potential measurement, pH probes, reference electrode calibration, and concentration-cell studies. - **Assumptions in the simplified form:** Steady-state, isothermal, linear concentration gradient, dilute solution, no convection. - **KTU hot numericals:** Always at 25 °C (298 K), always 1:1 electrolyte unless otherwise stated. - **Frequent pitfalls:** Sign error, mixing mobility and diffusion-coefficient data, forgetting the $\ln 10$ factor.

topics.title: Application of Free Energy to Corrosion Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Application of Free Energy to Corrosion Cells ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of corrosion science, **Free Energy (Gibbs Free Energy, $G$)** is a thermodynamic state function that quantifies the maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a process at constant temperature ($T$) and pressure ($P$). The change in Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$) acts as the **master thermodynamic criterion** for predicting the spontaneity of electrochemical corrosion reactions occurring within a corrosion cell.
