A **corrosion cell** is an electrochemical system comprising an anode, cathode, electrolyte, and metallic path, in which the anodic oxidation (metal dissolution) and cathodic reduction reactions proceed spontaneously when $\Delta G < 0$. The free energy change is directly linked to the cell electromotive force (EMF) by the fundamental relationship: $$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$$ where: - $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C mol⁻¹** ≈ **96500 C mol⁻¹** - $E_{cell}$ = cell potential (EMF) in Volts > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The negative sign in $\Delta G = -nFE$ is the cornerstone of corrosion thermodynamics. A **negative $\Delta G$** (spontaneous process) corresponds to a **positive $E_{cell}$**, which physically means the corrosion cell will deliver current and corrode the metal. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam & River" Intuition Imagine a large water reservoir held back by a dam at the top of a hill. | Dam-Reservoir System | Corrosion Cell System | |---|---| | Height of water (potential energy) | Free energy ($\Delta G$) of reaction | | Water flowing downhill | Spontaneous corrosion reaction | | Turbine generating electricity | Electron flow in external circuit | | Volume of water released | Charge transferred ($nF$) | | Pressure difference driving water | Cell EMF ($E_{cell}$) | Just as water **must** flow downhill to lose potential energy (spontaneous process), electrons **must** flow from a metal of higher free energy (anode, e.g., Mg) to one of lower free energy (cathode, e.g., Cu) for corrosion to occur. If the dam is removed but the downhill slope is reversed (water must go UP), no flow occurs — this is analogous to $\Delta G > 0$, where the reaction is **non-spontaneous** and corrosion will NOT proceed. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Free energy is the "fuel gauge" of a corrosion cell. It tells us **whether** corrosion will occur, while kinetics (activation energy, overpotential) tells us **how fast** it will occur. ### 1.3 The Electrochemical Series and Spontaneity The **Standard Electrode Potential ($E°$)**, measured in Volts under standard conditions (25°C, 1 M activity, 1 atm pressure), provides a ranked list of metallic tendencies to corrode. A more negative $E°$ means greater tendency to oxidize (act as anode). For example, Mg ($E°$ = -2.37 V) corrodes readily, while Au ($E°$ = +1.50 V) is highly noble and resists corrosion. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Corrosion Thermodynamics **Pillar 1 — Spontaneity Criterion (Free Energy Viewpoint)** Any reaction proceeds spontaneously if and only if its Gibbs free energy decreases. $$\Delta G < 0 \quad \text{(Spontaneous — Corrosion occurs)}$$ $$\Delta G = 0 \quad \text{(Equilibrium — No net corrosion)}$$ $$\Delta G > 0 \quad \text{(Non-spontaneous — No corrosion)}$$ **Pillar 2 — Electrical Viewpoint (Cell EMF)** A galvanic corrosion cell produces a positive EMF when the reaction is spontaneous. $$E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \quad \text{(under standard conditions: } E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode}\text{)}$$ - If $E_{cell} > 0$ → $\Delta G < 0$ → Spontaneous corrosion - If $E_{cell} < 0$ → $\Delta G > 0$ → Reaction is non-spontaneous **Pillar 3 — Equilibrium Viewpoint (Nernst Equation)** For a generalized cell reaction: $$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$ The actual cell potential at non-standard conditions is given by the Nernst equation: $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ where $Q = \dfrac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$ is the reaction quotient, $R$ is the universal gas constant (**8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**), and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin. Converting to base-10 logarithm (using $2.303 \, RT/F = 0.0591$ V at 25°C): $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at 25°C)}$$ ### 2.2 Linking $\Delta G$, $E°$, and the Equilibrium Constant $K$ At equilibrium, $E_{cell} = 0$ and $Q = K$. Substituting into the Nernst equation yields one of the most powerful relationships in corrosion science: $$0 = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K$$ $$\boxed{\,E°_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K \quad \text{(at 25°C)}\,}$$ Furthermore, since $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$, we can directly relate $E°$ to standard free energy: $$\boxed{\,\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineers' Quick Rule:** Every **+1 Volt** of standard cell potential corresponds to roughly **-96.5 kJ** of free energy per mole of electrons (using $n=1$). This means even small voltage differences represent huge thermodynamic driving forces for corrosion. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ | Free energy from cell EMF | J mol⁻¹ | | 2 | $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$ | Standard free energy from standard EMF | J mol⁻¹ | | 3 | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ | Cell EMF (galvanic convention) | V | | 4 | $E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | Nernst equation at 25°C | V | | 5 | $E°_{cell} = \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log K$ | Standard EMF ↔ Equilibrium constant | V | | 6 | $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$ | Standard free energy ↔ Equilibrium constant | J mol⁻¹ | | 7 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Fundamental Gibbs definition | J mol⁻¹ | | 8 | $E_{eq} = E° + \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Single electrode (half-cell) Nernst | V | | 9 | $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹ | Faraday's constant | C mol⁻¹ | | 10 | $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ | Universal gas constant | J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹ | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL Markdown Rule Followed:** Vertical bars and absolute values in the table above are written using standard math notation (e.g., $\log$ instead of vertical pipes) to maintain table integrity. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The free energy framework is the foundation of: 1. **Galvanic series design** — predicting whether two metals in contact will corrode (e.g., steel bolts in copper plumbing). 2. **Cathodic protection design** — selecting sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg, Al) for pipelines. 3. **Pourbaix (E-pH) diagrams** — mapping zones of immunity, corrosion, and passivation. 4. **Battery and fuel cell engineering** — designing high-efficiency energy storage. 5. **Materials selection** — choosing noble metals (Au, Pt) for harsh chemical environments. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linking $\Delta G$ to Cell EMF ($\Delta G = -nFE$) **Starting Point:** Consider a galvanic cell delivering current through an external circuit. The maximum electrical work obtainable from the cell equals the decrease in Gibbs free energy of the system (when the process is carried out reversibly at constant $T$ and $P$). **Step 1 — Express the maximum reversible electrical work.** The electrical work done by a cell in moving $n$ moles of electrons through a potential difference $E_{cell}$ is: $$W_{max} = Q \times E_{cell}$$ where $Q$ is the total charge transferred. **Step 2 — Express the total charge in terms of moles of electrons.** Since each mole of electrons carries a charge equal to Faraday's constant $F$: $$Q = nF$$ where $n$ is the number of moles of electrons transferred. **Step 3 — Substitute into the work expression.** $$W_{max} = nF \cdot E_{cell}$$ **Step 4 — Equate maximum work to decrease in Gibbs free energy.** For a spontaneous process at constant $T$ and $P$: $$W_{max} = -\Delta G$$ This is because the system loses free energy equal to the maximum work it can perform. **Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the final form.** $$-\Delta G = nFE_{cell}$$ $$\boxed{\,\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}\,}$$ This is the **fundamental thermodynamic equation of electrochemistry** and the foundation of all corrosion cell analysis. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst Equation from Free Energy **Starting Point:** For the generalized cell reaction at any state: $$aA + bB \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$ **Step 1 — Write the general free energy change expression.** For any reaction not at standard state: $$\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$$ where $Q = \dfrac{[C]^c [D]^d}{[A]^a [B]^b}$ is the reaction quotient. **Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ and $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$.** $$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE°_{cell} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3 — Divide both sides by $-nF$.** $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 4 — Convert natural logarithm to base-10 using $\ln x = 2.303 \log x$.** $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the constant at $T = 298$ K (25°C).** $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = \frac{5705.8}{96485} \approx 0.0591 \text{ V}$$ **Step 6 — Final Nernst Equation at 25°C.** $$\boxed{\,E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at 298 K)}\,}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between $E°$, $\Delta G°$, and $K$ **Step 1 — Apply the equilibrium condition to the Nernst equation.** At equilibrium, the cell potential is zero ($E_{cell} = 0$) and the reaction quotient equals the equilibrium constant ($Q = K$): $$0 = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $E°_{cell}$.** $$E°_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K \quad \text{(at 25°C)}$$ **Step 3 — Recall the thermodynamic identity $\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$.** Multiply both sides of the $E°$ equation by $-nF$: $$-nFE°_{cell} = -RT \ln K = \Delta G°$$ This confirms the consistency of all three relations: $$\boxed{\,\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell} = -RT \ln K\,}$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Predicting Corrosion Spontaneity **Problem:** A corrosion cell consists of a zinc anode ($E°_{Zn^{2+}/Zn} = -0.76$ V) immersed in a 0.01 M $Zn^{2+}$ solution and a copper cathode ($E°_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V) immersed in a 1.0 M $Cu^{2+}$ solution at 25°C. Calculate $E_{cell}$, $\Delta G$, and determine whether corrosion is spontaneous. **Step 1 — Write the half-reactions and overall cell reaction.** $$\text{Anode (Oxidation): } Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^- \quad E° = -0.76 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Cathode (Reduction): } Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu \quad E° = +0.34 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Overall: } Zn + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + Cu$$ Here, $n = 2$ electrons. **Step 2 — Calculate $E°_{cell}$.** $$E°_{cell} = E°_{cathode} - E°_{anode} = 0.34 - (-0.76) = 1.10 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Nernst equation for actual concentrations.** $$Q = \frac{[Zn^{2+}]}{[Cu^{2+}]} = \frac{0.01}{1.0} = 0.01$$ $$E_{cell} = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01) = 1.10 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2)$$ $$E_{cell} = 1.10 + 0.0591 = 1.1591 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate $\Delta G$.** $$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(1.1591)$$ $$\Delta G = -223{,}693 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -223.7 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result.** Since $E_{cell} > 0$ and $\Delta G < 0$, **the corrosion reaction is highly spontaneous**. The zinc anode will dissolve (corrode) and copper will deposit on the cathode. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** The negative concentration of $Zn^{2+}$ (0.01 M) actually makes the cell MORE spontaneous than at standard conditions, because it favors zinc dissolution (Le Chatelier's principle). --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Why $\Delta G$ vs. Kinetics | Property | Thermodynamics ($\Delta G$) | Kinetics (Rate) | |---|---|---| | Tells us | **Whether** corrosion will occur | **How fast** corrosion occurs | | Depends on | Temperature, concentration, $E°$ | Activation energy, overpotential, Tafel slope | | Can predict | Spontaneity, equilibrium state | Corrosion rate (mm/year), current density | | Limitation | Says nothing about rate | Says nothing about whether it can occur | | Example | Diamond → Graphite ($\Delta G < 0$) | Diamond persists for millennia (slow kinetics) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Free Energy Flow in a Galvanic Corrosion Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR A["METAL ANODE
High Free Energy
State Eg Mg, Zn"] -->|"Oxidation
M to M n+ + n e-"| B["ELECTROLYTE
Ionic Conductor
Contains H+, O2, Ions"] B -->|"Ion Migration
Cations to Cathode"| C["METAL CATHODE
Low Free Energy
State Eg Cu, Au, Pt"] C -->|"Reduction
2H+ + 2e- to H2"| D["CATHODIC PRODUCT
H2 gas or OH- or M"] A -->|"Electron Flow
External Circuit"| C E["G Initial"] -.->|"G Final"| F["G Final less than G Initial
Delta G negative"] G["G = G final - G initial"] --> F ``` ### 4.2 Thermodynamic Decision Framework for Corrosion ```mermaid flowchart TD Start(["Identify Corrosion Cell
Anode, Cathode, Electrolyte"]) --> CalcE["Calculate E cell
E cathode minus E anode"] CalcE --> CheckSign{E cell positive?} CheckSign -->|"Yes"| Spontaneous["DELTA G negative
Spontaneous Corrosion"] CheckSig -->|"DELTA G less than negative 40 kJ per mol"| HighDriv["High Driving Force
Aggressive Corrosion Expected"] CheckSig -->|"DELTA G between 0 and negative 40 kJ per mol"| MildDriv["Mild Driving Force
Localized Pitting Possible"] CheckSign -->|"No"| NonSpon["DELTA G positive
Non Spontaneous
No Corrosion Under Given Conditions"] Spontaneous --> CheckSig{"Magnitude of DELTA G"} HighDriv --> Recommend["Apply Cathodic Protection
or Use More Noble Material"] MildDriv --> Monitor["Periodic Inspection
and Inhibitor Dosing"] NonSpon --> Safe["Material is Safe
Under These Conditions"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Connecting $\Delta G$, $E°$, $K$, and Corrosion Rate ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph THERMO["THERMODYNAMIC INPUTS"] T1["Standard Electrode Potentials
E0 from Tables"] T2["Reaction Stoichiometry
n electrons transferred"] T3["Operating Conditions
T, P, Concentrations"] end subgraph CALC["CALCULATION ENGINE"] C1["E0 cell equals E0 cathode minus E0 anode"] C2["Nernst Equation
E cell correction"] C3["DELTA G equals minus n F E cell"] end subgraph OUTPUT["ENGINEERING OUTPUTS"] O1["Spontaneity Verdict
Corrode or Not"] O2["Driving Force Magnitude
DELTA G in kJ per mol"] O3["Equilibrium Constant
K"] O4["Pourbaix Diagram Point
E versus pH"] end T1 --> C1 T2 --> C3 T3 --> C2 C1 --> C2 C2 --> C3 C3 --> O1 C3 --> O2 C3 --> O3 C3 --> O4 ``` ### 4.4 Energy Landscape Diagram (ASCII Schematic) ``` G (Free Energy) ^ | * G(initial) = Reactants | |\ | | \ | | \ <<< Spontaneous | | \ Direction | | \ | | * G(final) = Products | | | |___________________________________> Reaction Progress Reactants Products |DELTA G| = G(final) - G(initial) = NEGATIVE E(cell) = -DELTA G / nF = POSITIVE Result = SPONTANEOUS CORROSION ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define Gibbs free energy. How is it related to the cell EMF of a corrosion cell?* **Model Answer:** Gibbs free energy ($G$) is a thermodynamic state function representing the maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a process at constant temperature and pressure. It is defined as $G = H - TS$, where $H$ is enthalpy, $T$ is absolute temperature, and $S$ is entropy. For a corrosion cell, the change in Gibbs free energy is directly related to the cell electromotive force by the equation $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$, where $n$ is the number of electrons transferred, $F$ is Faraday's constant (96485 C mol⁻¹), and $E_{cell}$ is the cell potential. A negative $\Delta G$ corresponds to a positive $E_{cell}$, indicating a spontaneous corrosion reaction. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1M) + Equation (1M) + Interpretation (1M). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *State the Nernst equation. What is its significance in predicting corrosion tendency?* **Model Answer:** The Nernst equation relates the cell potential under non-standard conditions to the standard cell potential: $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at 25°C)}$$ **Significance in corrosion prediction:** 1. It allows calculation of the actual corrosion cell EMF under real operating conditions (varying ion concentrations, temperature, pressure). 2. It determines whether a corrosion reaction is spontaneous at actual conditions (if $E_{cell} > 0$). 3. It forms the basis of Pourbaix (E-pH) diagrams used in corrosion engineering. 4. It enables prediction of ion concentrations required to arrest or accelerate corrosion. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** out of the internal choice provided. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **(a)** Derive the Nernst equation from fundamental thermodynamic principles. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a corrosion cell formed by iron and copper in their respective 0.05 M $Fe^{2+}$ and 0.5 M $Cu^{2+}$ solutions at 25°C, calculate the cell EMF and free energy change. Given: $E°_{Fe^{2+}/Fe} = -0.44$ V; $E°_{Cu^{2+}/Cu} = +0.34$ V. Comment on the spontaneity of corrosion. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution to Q-A (a): **Step 1 — Begin with the fundamental free energy equation:** For any reaction, $\Delta G = \Delta G° + RT \ln Q$, where $Q$ is the reaction quotient. **Step 2 — Use the $\Delta G = -nFE$ identity:** Substituting $\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}$ and $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$: $$-nFE_{cell} = -nFE°_{cell} + RT \ln Q$$ **Step 3 — Divide throughout by $-nF$:** $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$ **Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm and evaluate at 25°C:** $$E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$ **Assumptions:** - Temperature is constant at 298 K. - Activity coefficients are unity (ideal behavior). - The system operates at equilibrium for the exchange current. ##### Model Solution to Q-A (b): **Step 1 — Write half-reactions and the overall reaction:** $$\text{Anode: } Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^- \quad E° = -0.44 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Cathode: } Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu \quad E° = +0.34 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Overall: } Fe + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + Cu, \quad n = 2$$ **Step 2 — Calculate $E°_{cell}$:** $$E°_{cell} = 0.34 - (-0.44) = 0.78 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Nernst equation:** $$Q = \frac{[Fe^{2+}]}{[Cu^{2+}]} = \frac{0.05}{0.5} = 0.1$$ $$E_{cell} = 0.78 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.1) = 0.78 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-1) = 0.78 + 0.0296$$ $$E_{cell} = 0.8096 \text{ V} \approx 0.81 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate $\Delta G$:** $$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(0.8096) = -156{,}231 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$ $$\Delta G \approx -156.2 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since $E_{cell} > 0$ and $\Delta G < 0$, the corrosion of iron in contact with copper ions is **highly spontaneous**. Iron will preferentially corrode (acting as the sacrificial anode). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **(a)** Explain the relationship between standard free energy change, equilibrium constant, and standard cell potential. Show that $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell} = -RT \ln K$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A galvanic cell is constructed with a magnesium electrode in 0.001 M $Mg^{2+}$ solution and a silver electrode in 0.1 M $Ag^{+}$ solution at 25°C. Calculate: (i) $E°_{cell}$ (ii) $E_{cell}$ under the given conditions (iii) $\Delta G$ and the equilibrium constant $K$ Given: $E°_{Mg^{2+}/Mg} = -2.37$ V; $E°_{Ag^{+}/Ag} = +0.80$ V. **(7 Marks)** ##### Model Solution to Q-B (a): **Step 1 — Start with the thermodynamic identity linking free energy and equilibrium constant:** $$\Delta G° = -RT \ln K$$ **Step 2 — Start with the electrochemical identity linking free energy and cell EMF:** $$\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell}$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions:** $$-nFE°_{cell} = -RT \ln K$$ $$E°_{cell} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K = \frac{0.0591}{n} \log K$$ **Step 4 — Conclude the unified relationship:** $$\boxed{\,\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell} = -RT \ln K\,}$$ This triple identity links the **thermodynamic** ($\Delta G°$), **electrochemical** ($E°_{cell}$), and **chemical equilibrium** ($K$) viewpoints of corrosion. ##### Model Solution to Q-B (b): **Step 1 — Half-reactions and overall cell reaction:** $$\text{Anode: } Mg \rightarrow Mg^{2+} + 2e^- \quad E° = -2.37 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Cathode: } 2Ag^{+} + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Ag \quad E° = +0.80 \text{ V}$$ $$\text{Overall: } Mg + 2Ag^{+} \rightarrow Mg^{2+} + 2Ag, \quad n = 2$$ **(i) Standard cell EMF:** $$E°_{cell} = 0.80 - (-2.37) = 3.17 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) Nernst correction:** $$Q = \frac{[Mg^{2+}]}{[Ag^{+}]^2} = \frac{0.001}{(0.1)^2} = \frac{10^{-3}}{10^{-2}} = 0.1$$ $$E_{cell} = 3.17 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.1) = 3.17 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-1)$$ $$E_{cell} = 3.17 + 0.0296 = 3.1996 \text{ V} \approx 3.20 \text{ V}$$ **(iii) Free energy and equilibrium constant:** $$\Delta G = -nFE_{cell} = -(2)(96485)(3.20) = -617{,}504 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -617.5 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$ For $K$: $$\log K = \frac{nE°_{cell}}{0.0591} = \frac{2 \times 3.17}{0.0591} = 107.27$$ $$K = 10^{107.27} \approx 1.86 \times 10^{107}$$ The enormous value of $K$ confirms that the reaction proceeds essentially to completion, making magnesium a highly effective sacrificial anode for silver-containing systems. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):** > 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 to –3 marks):** Confusing $\Delta G = -nFE$ with $\Delta G = +nFE$. Always remember: **negative** $\Delta G$ ↔ **positive** $E_{cell}$ ↔ **spontaneous** corrosion. > 2. **Forgetting to convert $T$ to Kelvin (–1 to –2 marks):** All gas-constant-based calculations (Nernst) require $T$ in K, not °C. > 3. **Wrong direction in $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ (–2 marks):** Cathode potential minus anode potential, NOT the reverse. Many students write $E_{anode} - E_{cathode}$. > 4. **Incorrect value of the Nernst constant (–1 mark):** Use **0.0591** at 25°C, **0.0600** at 35°C, **0.0577** at 15°C. Examiners deduct for wrong values. > 5. **Ignoring stoichiometric coefficients in $Q$ (–1 to –2 marks):** For reaction $Mg + 2Ag^+ → Mg^{2+} + 2Ag$, the $Q$ expression is $[Mg^{2+}] / [Ag^+]^2$, not $[Mg^{2+}] / [Ag^+]$. > 6. **Units of $F$ (–1 mark):** Always write $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹ or 96500 C mol⁻¹. Some books approximate 96500 — both are accepted, but state which you used. > 7. **Missing units in final answer (–0.5 to –1 mark):** $\Delta G$ in kJ mol⁻¹, $E$ in V, $T$ in K. Always annotate. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Gibbs free energy ($\Delta G$)** is the master thermodynamic criterion for corrosion spontaneity. - The golden identity: $\boxed{\Delta G = -nFE_{cell}}$. A negative $\Delta G$ implies spontaneous corrosion. - **Cell EMF convention:** $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode}$ (reduction potentials). - **Sign rule for spontaneity:** $E_{cell} > 0$ AND $\Delta G < 0$ → corrosion occurs. - **Nernst equation at 25°C:** $E_{cell} = E°_{cell} - (0.0591 / n) \log Q$. - **Triple identity to memorize:** $\Delta G° = -nFE°_{cell} = -RT \ln K$. - **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96485$ C mol⁻¹; **Gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹. - **Nernst constant** at 25°C = 0.0591 V; at 35°C = 0.0600 V; at 18°C = 0.058 V. - **Standard conditions:** 25°C (298 K), 1 M activity, 1 atm pressure. - **More negative $E°$** = greater tendency to corrode (more anodic). Mg (-2.37 V) ≪ Fe (-0.44 V) ≪ Cu (+0.34 V) ≪ Au (+1.50 V). - **Thermodynamics tells IF; Kinetics tells HOW FAST.** A thermodynamically favored reaction may still be kinetically slow. - **Pourbaix diagrams** (E vs. pH) are graphical applications of Nernst's equation to water-metal systems, showing zones of immunity, corrosion, and passivation. - **Unit conversions:** $1$ F $= 96485$ C $= 26.8$ Ah (ampere-hours); $1$ V = $1$ J C⁻¹. - **Engineering takeaway:** Galvanic corrosion is unavoidable when dissimilar metals are coupled in an electrolyte — control it via material selection, insulation, or cathodic protection.

topics.title: Nernst Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nernst Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Nernst Equation** is a fundamental electrochemical relationship that quantitatively expresses the reversible electrode potential ($E$) of a half-cell or a complete electrochemical cell as a function of the activities (or concentrations) of the reacting species and the temperature. It mathematically bridges the gap between thermodynamics (Gibbs free energy) and the measurable electrical potential of an electrochemical system.

For a general reversible electrochemical half-reaction:

$$aA + bB + ne^- \rightleftharpoons cC + dD$$

The Nernst Equation is written as:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Where:
- $E$ = Electrode potential under non-standard conditions (Volts)
- $E^{\circ}$ = Standard electrode potential (Volts)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (Kelvin)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred in the half-reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $Q$ = Reaction quotient = $\frac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank & Pressure" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like water pressure in a tank connected to a pipe.**

Imagine a water tank at a certain **standard height** (this is your $E^{\circ}$, the standard electrode potential). Water naturally tries to flow out to a lower level. The pressure of water coming out of the tap depends on two things:
1. **How high the water is** (analogous to **concentration** of ions in solution).
2. **The temperature of the water** (hot water flows differently than cold water — analogous to **temperature** $T$).

If you **lower the water level** (decrease ion concentration), the **pressure drops** (electrode potential becomes less positive or more negative). The Nernst Equation is essentially the mathematical "plumbing equation" that tells you exactly *how much* the pressure (potential) changes when you change the level (concentration) or temperature.

In corrosion terms: when metal ion concentration near a corroding surface **decreases** (because ions diffuse away), the electrode potential shifts, and this shift *drives* the corrosion reaction faster.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Electrode**: The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** is the universal reference with $E^{\circ} = 0.000$ V at **25°C (298.15 K)** and $[H^+] = 1$ M, $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm.

| Reference Electrode | Standard Potential $E^{\circ}$ (V) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) | **0.000 V** | Universal primary reference |
| Saturated Calomel Electrode (SCE) | **+0.242 V vs SHE** | Most common lab reference |
| Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl) | **+0.197 V vs SHE** | Used in field measurements |
| Copper/Copper Sulfate (CSE) | **+0.318 V vs SHE** | Used in soil/pipe corrosion surveys |

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these constants** — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem.

- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (often rounded to **96,500 C/mol** in KTU problems)
- $T = 298$ K (for all problems at "room temperature" 25°C)
- $2.303 \times R \times T / F = 0.0591$ V at 25°C (a very frequently used constant)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Foundation (The "Why")

The Nernst Equation does **not** emerge from nowhere — it is a direct consequence of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** applied to electrochemical systems. Here is the logical chain:

1. **Gibbs Free Energy Change** for an electrochemical reaction: $\Delta G = -nFE$ (cell) and $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$.
2. **General thermodynamic relation** linking non-standard to standard conditions: $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$.
3. **Substitute** the two $\Delta G$ equations: $-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$.
4. **Divide through by $-nF$** to obtain the Nernst Equation.

This derivation is shown in exhaustive detail in Section 3.

## 2.2 The Two Most Used Forms of the Nernst Equation

### Form 1: Natural Logarithm (Thermodynamic Form)

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

### Form 2: Base-10 Logarithm (Engineering Form at 25°C)

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q \quad \text{(at } T = 298 \text{ K)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: The 0.0591 form is used in **~90% of numerical problems** because problems are usually stated "at 25°C". Always check whether the problem specifies a different temperature before using 0.0591.

## 2.3 Effect of Each Variable — The "How"

- **Concentration Increase of Oxidized Species (Products)**: The $Q$ value increases → $E$ **decreases** (becomes less positive / more negative).
- **Concentration Increase of Reduced Species (Reactants)**: The $Q$ value decreases → $E$ **increases** (becomes more positive).
- **Temperature Increase**: $E$ changes depending on whether the reaction is exothermic or endothermic. For most corrosion reactions, higher temperature increases reaction rate (kinetics), but the Nernst effect on potential is separate from rate.
- **Increasing $n$ (electrons transferred)**: The magnitude of the concentration effect on $E$ is **diminished** because of the $1/n$ factor.

## 2.4 Sign Convention — Critical KTU Point

> [!WARNING]
> **The Nernst Equation as written gives the REDUCTION potential.** If a half-reaction is written as an OXIDATION (reverse direction), you must **reverse the sign** of $E$ and $E^{\circ}$ in addition to inverting $Q$.

Example: For the oxidation $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$, the equation becomes:

$$E_{\text{oxidation}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nernst (Natural log) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ | $R$ in J/(mol·K), $T$ in K | Any temperature |
| Nernst (Base-10, 25°C) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | $Q$ dimensionless | **Default KTU problems at 25°C** |
| Nernst (Base-10, 37°C body temp) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0615}{n} \log Q$ | — | Biomedical implants |
| Reaction Quotient | $Q = \dfrac{[C]^c[D]^d}{[A]^a[B]^b}$ | Products over reactants, each raised to stoichiometric coefficient | Always — denominator = reactants |
| Gas-phase correction | Replace $[\text{gas}]$ with partial pressure $P_{\text{gas}}$ in atm | $P$ in atm | When gases participate |
| Nernst for full cell | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Both potentials as **reduction** potentials | To compute cell EMF |
| Pourbaix-bound quantity | $\Delta E = -\dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log \dfrac{[\text{actual}]}{[\text{standard}]}$ | — | Activity-shift problems |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Nernst Equation |
|---|---|
| **Oil & Gas Pipelines** | Predicting corrosion potential of buried steel in soils of varying moisture/salt content |
| **Marine Structures** | Calculating potential of steel hulls in seawater with varying dissolved O₂ |
| **Reinforced Concrete** | Predicting rebar corrosion potential as chloride concentration rises |
| **Batteries & Fuel Cells** | Designing cell stacks; predicting open-circuit voltage at varying SoC |
| **Biomedical Implants** | Predicting Ti-alloy corrosion potential in body fluids (pH ≈ 7.4, 37°C) |
| **Cathodic Protection Design** | Sizing sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg) by potential difference calculations |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics

We begin with the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** for any chemical/electrochemical process at constant temperature and pressure:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 1 — Recall the electrochemical work relation**:

The Gibbs free energy change is directly proportional to the cell potential through Faraday's constant and the number of electrons transferred:

$$\Delta G = -nFE$$

For the standard state:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute both relations** into the thermodynamic equation:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

**Step 3 — Divide every term by $-nF$** (note: dividing by a negative number flips inequality signs, but here we are dealing with equalities, so we just rearrange):

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

**Step 4 — Convert the natural log to base-10 log** using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the constant term at $T = 298$ K (25°C)**:

$$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485} = 0.0591 \text{ V}$$

Therefore, at 25°C:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary**:
> - $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ is a **universal** thermodynamic identity.
> - Electrochemistry links $\Delta G$ to $E$ via the electrical work $-nFE$.
> - Substitution + division by $-nF$ directly produces the Nernst form.
> - Conversion to base-10 log + substitution of $T=298$ K gives the famous 0.0591 constant.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Nernst Equation for Common Metal/Ion Couples

| Half-Reaction (Reduction Form) | Standard $E^{\circ}$ (V vs SHE) | Nernst Equation at 25°C |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Zn}$ | $-0.763$ | $E = -0.763 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log[\text{Zn}^{2+}]$ |
| $\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}$ | $-0.440$ | $E = -0.440 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log[\text{Fe}^{2+}]$ |
| $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ | $+0.337$ | $E = +0.337 + \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log[\text{Cu}^{2+}]$ |
| $\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}$ | $+0.799$ | $E = +0.799 + 0.0591 \log[\text{Ag}^+]$ |
| $\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | $+1.229$ | $E = +1.229 + \dfrac{0.0591}{4} \log\bigl(P_{O_2} \cdot [\text{H}^+]^4\bigr)$ |
| $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$ | $0.000$ | $E = -0.0591 \text{ pH} - \dfrac{0.0591}{2} \log P_{H_2}$ |

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — The Classic "Nernst" KTU Style Problem

> **Problem**: A copper electrode is immersed in a 0.01 M Cu²⁺ solution at 25°C. Given $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.337$ V, calculate the electrode potential.

**Solution — Step 1: Write the half-reaction and identify $n$**

$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu} \quad (n = 2)$$

**Step 2: Write the Nernst Equation**

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$

**Step 3: Determine $Q$**

Since the solid Cu has activity = 1, $Q = \dfrac{1}{[\text{Cu}^{2+}]} = \dfrac{1}{0.01} = 100$

**Step 4: Substitute values**

$$E = 0.337 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(100)$$

**Step 5: Evaluate**

$$E = 0.337 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 2 = 0.337 - 0.0591 = +0.278 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The copper electrode potential **decreased** (from +0.337 V to +0.278 V) when the Cu²⁺ concentration was reduced from 1 M to 0.01 M. This makes physical sense: less Cu²⁺ means less driving force for reduction.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — The Hydrogen Electrode in Non-Standard pH

> **Problem**: A hydrogen electrode operates in a buffer of pH = 3.5 and $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm at 25°C. Calculate $E$.

**Step 1: Write the half-reaction**

$$2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2 \quad (n = 2)$$

**Step 2: Identify $Q$ and $E^{\circ}$**

$Q = \dfrac{P_{H_2}}{[\text{H}^+]^2} = \dfrac{1}{[\text{H}^+]^2}$, and $E^{\circ} = 0$ (by definition of SHE).

**Step 3: Substitute into Nernst**

$$E = 0 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]^2}$$

**Step 4: Simplify using pH**

$E = -\frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2 \log[\text{H}^+]) = 0.0591 \times (-\log[\text{H}^+]) = -0.0591 \times \text{pH}$

**Step 5: Substitute pH = 3.5**

$$E = -0.0591 \times 3.5 = -0.207 \text{ V vs SHE}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern**: For hydrogen electrode, $E = -0.0591 \times \text{pH}$ at 25°C. This is a frequently tested formula shortcut.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Concentration Cell (KTU Favourite)

> **Problem**: A concentration cell is constructed with two Fe electrodes — one in 0.5 M Fe²⁺ and another in 0.001 M Fe²⁺. Calculate the EMF at 25°C.

**Step 1: Identify anode (low concentration) and cathode (high concentration)**

- Anode (oxidation): $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}_{0.001\text{M}} + 2e^-$
- Cathode (reduction): $\text{Fe}^{2+}_{0.5\text{M}} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}$

**Step 2: Write Nernst for the cell**

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = \left(E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.5}\right) - \left(E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.001}\right)$$

**Step 3: Cancel $E^{\circ}$ and simplify**

$$E_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{0.5}{0.001} = \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(500) = \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 2.699 = 0.0797 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

$E_{\text{cell}} = +0.080$ V, with the dilute side acting as the anode (corroding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: This is the principle behind **differential aeration cells** and **crevice corrosion** in KTU Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Nernst Equation Inputs/Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Half-Reaction Equation] --> B[Identify n electrons]
    B --> C[Identify Standard Potential E0 from Table]
    C --> D[Determine Activities or Partial Pressures]
    D --> E[Calculate Reaction Quotient Q]
    E --> F{Is Temperature 25 deg C?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use 0.0591 Constant]
    F -->|No| H[Use RT over nF with natural log]
    G --> I[Compute E via Nernst Equation]
    H --> I[Compute E via Nernst Equation]
    I --> J[Electrode Potential E in Volts]
    J --> K[Apply to Corrosion Prediction]
    J --> L[Apply to Pourbaix Diagram Construction]
    J --> M[Apply to Cathodic Protection Sizing]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — From Thermodynamics to Nernst

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Second Law of Thermodynamics deltaG relation] --> T2[Substitute deltaG equals minus nFE]
    T2 --> T3[Algebraic Rearrangement]
    T3 --> T4[Natural Log Form of Nernst]
    T4 --> T5[Base 10 Log Conversion]
    T5 --> T6[0.0591 Form at 25 deg C]
    T6 --> T7[Apply to Half Cell]
    T7 --> T8[Apply to Full Cell via Cathode minus Anode]
    T8 --> T9[Corrosion Cell EMF Prediction]
```

## 4.3 Reference Electrode Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REF[Reference Electrodes Hierarchy] --> SHE[Standard Hydrogen Electrode E0 equals 0.000 V]
    REF --> SCE[Saturated Calomel Electrode E0 equals plus 0.242 V]
    REF --> AG[Silver Silver Chloride E0 equals plus 0.197 V]
    REF --> CSE[Copper Copper Sulfate E0 equals plus 0.318 V]
    SHE --> USELAB[Used as primary standard in lab]
    SCE --> USEFIELD[Used as secondary lab reference]
    AG --> USEBIO[Used in biomedical and field probes]
    CSE --> USESOIL[Used in soil and pipeline surveys]
```

## 4.4 Sign Convention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Question gives Half Reaction] --> R{Is Reaction written as Reduction?}
    R -->|Yes| S[Use E as Reduction Potential E equals E0 minus 0.0591 by n log Q]
    R -->|No| T[Reverse Reaction and Change Sign of E0 and E]
    S --> OUT[Final Electrode Potential]
    T --> OUT[Final Electrode Potential]
    OUT --> CELL{Q requires Full Cell EMF?}
    CELL -->|Yes| U[Ecell equals Ecathode minus Eanode both as reduction potentials]
    CELL -->|No| V[Report Single Electrode Potential]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 1: Define the Nernst Equation and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer**:
The Nernst Equation relates the electrode potential of a half-cell to its standard potential, temperature, and the activities of reacting species. It is:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

**Significance of each term**:
- $E$: Actual electrode potential under operating conditions (V).
- $E^{\circ}$: Standard electrode potential when all activities = 1 (V).
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K): Universal gas constant.
- $T$: Absolute temperature (K).
- $n$: Number of electrons transferred.
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol: Faraday's constant (charge per mole of electrons).
- $Q$: Reaction quotient (activities of products over reactants).

It allows prediction of corrosion cell behavior in non-standard environments.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 2: A Zn electrode is immersed in 0.1 M Zn²⁺ solution. Calculate its potential at 25°C. Given $E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.763$ V.

**Model Answer**:

Half-reaction: $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Zn}$, $n = 2$.

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{[\text{Zn}^{2+}]}$$

$$E = -0.763 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{0.1}$$

$$E = -0.763 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \times 1 = -0.763 - 0.0296 = -0.793 \text{ V vs SHE}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **Choose Either A or B**

**(a)** Derive the Nernst Equation starting from the Gibbs free energy relation. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ redox couple, $E^{\circ} = +0.771$ V. Calculate the electrode potential when $[\text{Fe}^{3+}] = 0.02$ M and $[\text{Fe}^{2+}] = 0.5$ M at 25°C. Also comment on whether this potential favours oxidation or reduction of Fe²⁺. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumption**: The system is at constant temperature and pressure, and the electrochemical reaction is reversible.

Starting from the **Second Law identity**:

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

For an electrochemical process, the maximum electrical work obtainable is:

$$\Delta G = -nFE \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$$

Substituting both:

$$-nFE = -nFE^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

Dividing throughout by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$$

Converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{2.303 \, RT}{nF} \log Q$$

At $T = 298$ K:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$

This is the **Nernst Equation**. (7 marks distributed: 2 for assumptions, 3 for algebra, 2 for final form & physical interpretation.)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Half-reaction**: $\text{Fe}^{3+} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}$, $n = 1$.

**Reaction quotient**:

$$Q = \frac{[\text{Fe}^{2+}]}{[\text{Fe}^{3+}]} = \frac{0.5}{0.02} = 25$$

**Apply Nernst**:

$$E = E^{\circ} - \frac{0.0591}{1} \log(25) = 0.771 - 0.0591 \times 1.398$$

$$E = 0.771 - 0.0826 = +0.688 \text{ V vs SHE}$$

**Comment**: Since $E = +0.688$ V is **positive** and greater than 0, the reduction of Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ is thermodynamically favoured. Conversely, oxidation of Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ is *not* favoured at these concentrations. However, if the environment provides a more negative potential (e.g., another metal with lower $E^{\circ}$), Fe³⁺ would be reduced, making the partner metal corrode. (7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for calculation, 2 for comment, 1 for units.)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of the Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE). Why is it assigned a potential of 0.000 V? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A galvanic cell is constructed with a Cu electrode in 0.1 M Cu²⁺ solution and a Zn electrode in 0.01 M Zn²⁺ solution. Calculate the cell EMF at 25°C. Given $E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.337$ V and $E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.763$ V. Identify the anode and cathode. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — SHE Construction (7 Marks)

The **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** consists of:
1. A **platinum wire** coated with **platinum black** (to provide a large surface area for reaction).
2. The Pt electrode is immersed in an **acid solution of unit activity** ($[\text{H}^+] = 1$ M, i.e., 1.18 M H₂SO₄).
3. **Pure hydrogen gas** at 1 atm pressure is bubbled over the Pt electrode at 25°C.

**Working**: At the Pt surface, the equilibrium:

$$2\text{H}^+_{(aq, \, a=1)} + 2e^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_{2(g, \, 1 \, \text{atm})}$$

takes place. The Pt acts as an **inert catalyst** and provides electrons to/from the external circuit.

**Why $E^{\circ} = 0$ V**: By **international convention (IUPAC)**, the Gibbs free energy of formation of $\text{H}^+$ at unit activity is defined as zero. Since $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}$, this forces $E^{\circ} = 0$ for the SHE. All other electrode potentials are measured *relative* to this reference. (7 marks: 2 for components, 2 for working, 2 for convention, 1 for neat sketch description.)

---

#### Part (b) — Cell EMF Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify electrode with higher $E^{\circ}$ as cathode (more positive reduction potential).**

Cu has $E^{\circ} = +0.337$ V → Cu is the **cathode**.
Zn has $E^{\circ} = -0.763$ V → Zn is the **anode**.

**Step 2: Write the Nernst equation for each electrode.**

For Cu cathode (reduction), $n = 2$:

$$E_{\text{Cu}} = 0.337 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log[\text{Cu}^{2+}] = 0.337 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.1)$$

$$E_{\text{Cu}} = 0.337 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-1) = 0.337 - 0.0296 = +0.307 \text{ V}$$

For Zn anode (reduction form), $n = 2$:

$$E_{\text{Zn}} = -0.763 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log[\text{Zn}^{2+}] = -0.763 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \log(0.01)$$

$$E_{\text{Zn}} = -0.763 + \frac{0.0591}{2} \times (-2) = -0.763 - 0.0591 = -0.822 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Cell EMF.**

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = 0.307 - (-0.822) = +1.129 \text{ V}$$

**Anode (corroding)**: Zn — it oxidizes ($\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$).
**Cathode (protected)**: Cu — Cu²⁺ reduces to Cu.

(7 marks: 1 for identification, 2 for $E_{\text{Cu}}$, 2 for $E_{\text{Zn}}$, 2 for cell EMF and conclusion.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Sign Error in $Q$**: Students often invert $Q$ (placing reactants in the numerator). Always: **products over reactants**.
> 2. **Forgetting to flip $Q$ for oxidation half-reactions**: When you reverse a half-reaction, invert $Q$ **and** flip the sign of $E^{\circ}$.
> 3. **Using 0.0591 at wrong temperatures**: This constant is only valid at 25°C. If a problem says 35°C, you MUST use $\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 308}{96485} = 0.0612$ V.
> 4. **Unit errors**: Final answer should be in **Volts (V)** — do not write "Joules" or "Amperes".
> 5. **Wrong $n$ value**: For $\text{Fe}^{3+} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}$, $n = 1$, **not** 3. Always count the **electrons transferred in that specific half-reaction**, not the total oxidation state change.
> 6. **Confusing activity with concentration**: For dilute solutions, activity ≈ concentration. For concentrated solutions, use activity coefficients. KTU problems typically assume dilute = activity = molarity.
> 7. **Cell EMF sign**: $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$ means **spontaneous reaction** (galvanic/corrosion cell). $E_{\text{cell}} < 0$ means **non-spontaneous** (requires external power, i.e., electrolytic cell).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nernst Equation**

- **Core formula (25°C, base-10)**: $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$
- **General form (any temperature)**: $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln Q$ where $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- **Universal constant 0.0591** = $\dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{96485}$ at 25°C
- **Reaction Quotient $Q$**: Always write as **[products] / [reactants]**, each raised to stoichiometric power; **omit pure solids and liquids**.
- **Gas handling**: For gas-phase species, use **partial pressure in atm** in place of concentration.
- **Sign convention**: The Nernst equation gives **reduction potential** directly. For oxidation, reverse the reaction, invert $Q$, and flip the sign of $E^{\circ}$.
- **Hydrogen electrode shortcut**: At 25°C, $E_{\text{H electrode}} = -0.0591 \times \text{pH}$ (when $P_{H_2} = 1$ atm).
- **Cell EMF**: $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$, where both are expressed as **reduction potentials**. Spontaneous cell: $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$.
- **Concentration cells**: When $E^{\circ}$ is the same on both sides, it cancels; the EMF arises purely from the concentration ratio.
- **Dilute solution approximation**: In KTU problems, **activity ≈ molarity** unless otherwise stated.
- **Higher ion concentration of oxidized species → more positive $E$** (greater driving force for reduction).
- **Dilution effect on metals**: Lower $\text{M}^{n+}$ concentration near the metal surface → potential becomes more negative → metal corrodes **faster** (driving force for oxidation increases).
- **Temperature rule of thumb**: For every 10°C rise above 25°C, the 0.0591 constant increases by roughly 0.002. So at 35°C, use 0.0612; at 45°C, use 0.0632.
- **Unit discipline**: $E$ in volts, $T$ in Kelvin (NOT °C), concentrations in mol/L, pressures in atm.
- **SHE reference**: All $E^{\circ}$ values in tables are referenced to SHE ($E^{\circ} = 0$ V at 25°C, $[\text{H}^+] = 1$ M).
- **Connection to Module 2 & 3**: The Nernst equation is the foundation for **Pourbaix diagrams** (E vs pH) and for predicting **galvanic series** behavior in real engineering environments.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sign Convention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sign Convention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Corrosion Control (PEICT866)**, the term **Sign Convention** refers to the standardized, universally accepted system of assigning **positive (+) or negative (−) algebraic signs** to physical and electrochemical quantities such as **electrode potential, cell EMF (E°cell), Gibbs free energy (ΔG), anodic/cathodic currents, and corrosion rates**, so that the direction of spontaneous electron flow, the spontaneity of a corrosion reaction, and the magnitude of thermodynamic driving force can be unambiguously described in a corrosion cell.

The **International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) — also called the Stockholm Convention (1953)** — is the global standard adopted in corrosion science. It defines:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stockholm Convention (1953):**
> "The EMF of a galvanic cell is taken as **positive** when the reaction proceeds **spontaneously** in the direction as written. The electrode potentials are tabulated as **standard reduction potentials** (E°red), and the cell EMF is calculated as **E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode**."

The alternate **American (or Galvanic) Convention**, used widely in the United States, tabulates **standard oxidation potentials** (E°ox), so that the cell EMF becomes **E°cell = E°anode − E°cathode** (when oxidation potentials are used). Mathematically both conventions yield the **same numerical magnitude and sign of E°cell**; only the tabulated single-electrode values differ in sign.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank placed on a roof**:
- The **higher tank level** (anode, higher energy) naturally drains **down** to the **lower tank level** (cathode, lower energy) through a pipe.
- The **flow of water** is **spontaneous** — the *direction* matters, not just the amount.
- If we wrongly sign the flow (up instead of down), our entire analysis of the plumbing system collapses.

Similarly, in corrosion, electrons "flow downhill" from a metal with a **more negative (less noble) potential** to a metal with a **more positive (more noble) potential**. The **Sign Convention** is the rulebook that tells us:
- **Which way is "downhill"** (spontaneous direction).
- **Which electrode loses mass** (anode, oxidation).
- **Whether ΔG is favourable** (negative) or unfavourable (positive).

Without a sign convention, two engineers could describe the **same corrosion cell** with **opposite signs**, leading to dangerous design errors in cathodic protection systems, sacrificial anode selection, or impressed current calculations.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Faraday's Constant (F) = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ ≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹** — links charge to moles of electrons.
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R) = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**.
> - **Standard Temperature (T) = 298.15 K (25 °C)** for all standard potential tables.
> - **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE): E° = 0.000 V** — universal reference electrode.
> - **Charge of 1 mole of electrons = 1 Faraday = 96,485 C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Competing Conventions — A Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

#### **A) IUPAC / Stockholm Convention (Adopted by KTU & Modern Corrosion Textbooks)**

**Step 1 — Cell Notation (Left to Right = Anode to Cathode):**

$$M_1 \mid M_1^{n+}(\text{anolyte}) \,\|\, M_2^{n+}(\text{catholyte}) \mid M_2$$

- The **anode** (oxidation, electron-producing electrode) is written on the **left**.
- The **cathode** (reduction, electron-consuming electrode) is written on the **right**.
- A **single vertical line ( | )** indicates a phase boundary.
- A **double vertical line ( || )** indicates a **salt bridge** (liquid junction).

**Step 2 — Tabulated Potentials are Standard REDUCTION Potentials (E°red):**

- All standard electrode potentials in IUPAC tables represent the **reduction half-reaction**:
$$M^{n+} + ne^- \rightarrow M$$
- A **more positive E°red** = stronger oxidizing agent = more **noble** metal.
- A **more negative E°red** = stronger reducing agent = more **active** metal.

**Step 3 — Cell EMF Formula (IUPAC):**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode (right)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode (left)}}$$

**Step 4 — Sign of E°cell Tells Spontaneity:**

| Sign of E°cell | Spontaneity | Type of Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **E°cell > 0 (positive)** | Spontaneous (ΔG° < 0) | **Galvanic** (corrosion cell) |
| **E°cell = 0** | Equilibrium | No net reaction |
| **E°cell < 0 (negative)** | Non-spontaneous (ΔG° > 0) | **Electrolytic** (requires power) |

**Step 5 — Link to Gibbs Free Energy:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

This single equation is the **thermodynamic backbone** of corrosion prediction. If you only remember one formula in Module 1, remember this.

---

#### **B) American / Galvanic Convention**

**Step 1 — Tabulated Potentials are Standard OXIDATION Potentials (E°ox):**

- All standard electrode potentials in American tables represent the **oxidation half-reaction**:
$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$
- The numerical values are the **negative** of IUPAC reduction potentials:
$$E^{\circ}_{\text{ox}} = -E^{\circ}_{\text{red}}$$

**Step 2 — Cell EMF Formula (American):**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode (oxidation)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode (oxidation)}}$$

**Step 3 — Sign Convention (American):**

- E°cell is taken as **positive** for a **spontaneous** reaction.
- Numerically identical to the IUPAC result, provided signs are applied correctly.

---

### 2.2 Sign Convention for Anode and Cathode

| Property | Anode (Oxidation) | Cathode (Reduction) |
|---|---|---|
| **Half-reaction** | Loses electrons (M → Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻) | Gains electrons (Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻ → M) |
| **Sign in Galvanic Cell** | **Negative (−)** electrode | **Positive (+)** electrode |
| **Sign in Electrolytic Cell** | **Positive (+)** electrode (connected to + of battery) | **Negative (−)** electrode (connected to − of battery) |
| **Polarity Reversal** | Polarity **reverses** when external power is applied | Polarity **reverses** when external power is applied |
| **Mass Change** | Loses mass (corrosion) | Gains mass (deposition) or no change |
| **Current Sign (I)** | **Anodic current, Ia > 0** (oxidation) | **Cathodic current, Ic > 0** (reduction) |
| **Corrosion Current** | icorr (always positive) | icorr (always positive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarity Reversal Principle:**
> The same electrode can be an **anode in a galvanic cell** and a **cathode in an electrolytic cell** (and vice-versa). The *sign* of the electrode depends on the **direction of net electron flow**, which is governed by the **external circuit** and the **applied EMF**.

---

### 2.3 Sign Convention for Gibbs Free Energy (ΔG)

| Sign of ΔG | Thermodynamic Meaning | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **ΔG < 0 (negative)** | Spontaneous process | **Corrosion will occur** without external intervention |
| **ΔG = 0** | Equilibrium | No net corrosion; reversible |
| **ΔG > 0 (positive)** | Non-spontaneous | Corrosion will **not** occur; reverse reaction is spontaneous |

**Derivation from Nernst Equation:**

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$$

At equilibrium (ΔG = 0, Q = K):

$$0 = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln K \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$

Combining with $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$:

$$-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = -RT \ln K \quad \Rightarrow \quad E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \frac{RT}{nF} \ln K$$

At **25 °C (298.15 K)** and converting ln to log₁₀:

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \frac{0.0592}{n} \log K$$

This is the **bridge equation** linking electrochemistry to equilibrium thermodynamics.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Units | Convention |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | Gibbs free energy vs cell EMF | **IUPAC** (universal) |
| 2 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | IUPAC cell EMF (using reduction potentials) | **IUPAC** |
| 3 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode(ox)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode(ox)}}$ | American cell EMF (using oxidation potentials) | **American** |
| 4 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{ox}} = -E^{\circ}_{\text{red}}$ | Conversion between conventions | **Both** |
| 5 | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$ | Nernst Equation (at 25 °C) | **IUPAC** |
| 6 | $\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln Q$ | Gibbs free energy under non-standard conditions | **Universal** |
| 7 | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log K$ | EMF–Equilibrium constant relation | **IUPAC** |
| 8 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{nF \cdot \text{CR}}{M}$ | Corrosion current from corrosion rate (A/cm²) | Faraday's Law |
| 9 | $\eta = E - E_{\text{eq}}$ | Overpotential (deviation from equilibrium) | **Sign = direction of polarization** |
| 10 | $\eta_a > 0$ (anodic polarization) | Anode polarized to more positive potential | **Sign Convention** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units used throughout this module:**
> - E, E° → **Volts (V)**
> - ΔG → **Joules (J) or kJ mol⁻¹**
> - n → **dimensionless** (number of electrons transferred)
> - F → **Coulombs per mole (C mol⁻¹)**
> - R → **J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
> - T → **Kelvin (K)**
> - i → **A cm⁻²** (current density)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Sign Convention is **not academic pedantry** — it has direct industrial consequences:

1. **Cathodic Protection Design:** Choosing sacrificial anodes (Zn, Mg, Al) for protecting pipelines, ships, and offshore platforms requires computing E°cell. A sign error leads to choosing the **wrong metal** — accelerating corrosion instead of stopping it.

2. **Battery & Fuel Cell Design:** Aerospace, EV, and grid-storage engineers must correctly sign cell voltages to predict capacity, energy density, and direction of current flow during charge/discharge cycles.

3. **Electroplating & Electrometallurgy:** In electrolytic cells, the sign of the applied potential determines which metal deposits and which dissolves. Sign errors destroy batches worth crores of rupees.

4. **Corrosion Monitoring (LPR, EIS):** Modern instruments (Gamry, Solartron) report currents with explicit sign — anodic (positive) vs cathodic (negative). Engineers must interpret these signs to diagnose active vs passive corrosion behaviour.

5. **Pourbaix Diagrams:** The sign of E and pH defines the **stability regions** of metal, oxide, and dissolved ion. Wrong signs → wrong material selection.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Sign Relationship Between ΔG and E°cell

**Starting Point — Definition of Electrical Work:**

When a galvanic cell operates spontaneously, it can perform **electrical work (Wₑₗₑc)** on the surroundings. The maximum non-expansion work obtainable from a constant-T, constant-P process equals the change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G$$

The electrical work done in moving *n* moles of electrons through a potential difference E°cell is:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = Q \times E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = n \times F \times E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

where:
- Q = total charge transferred (Coulombs)
- n = moles of electrons
- F = Faraday's constant (96,485 C mol⁻¹)
- E°cell = cell EMF (Volts)

**Equating the Two Expressions:**

$$-\Delta G = nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Sign Analysis:**

| Condition | Sign of E°cell | Sign of ΔG° | Spontaneity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Galvanic (spontaneous) | **+ (positive)** | **− (negative)** | Spontaneous corrosion |
| Electrolytic (non-spontaneous) | **− (negative)** | **+ (positive)** | Needs external power |

**Numerical Verification (Iron-Zinc Cell):**

Given: Fe²⁺/Fe (E°red = −0.44 V), Zn²⁺/Zn (E°red = −0.76 V)

Anode (oxidation): Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (E°ox = +0.76 V)
Cathode (reduction): Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Fe (E°red = −0.44 V)

E°cell (IUPAC) = E°cathode − E°anode = (−0.44) − (−0.76) = **+0.32 V**

ΔG° = −(2)(96,485)(0.32) = **−61,750 J mol⁻¹ = −61.75 kJ mol⁻¹**

Since ΔG° < 0, the reaction is **spontaneous** — zinc corrodes (sacrificial anode), iron is protected. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sign Convention for Cell EMF (IUPAC vs American)

**IUPAC Convention (Reduction Potentials):**

**Step 1:** Both half-cell potentials are quoted as **reductions**:
$$\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Zn} \quad E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -0.76 \text{ V}$$
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu} \quad E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = +0.34 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Identify anode (more negative E°red = Zn) and cathode (more positive E°red = Cu).

**Step 3:** Apply the IUPAC formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} \\
&= E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} \\
&= (+0.34) - (-0.76) \\
&= +0.34 + 0.76 \\
&= +1.10 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

Since E°cell > 0 → **spontaneous** → galvanic cell. ✓

**American Convention (Oxidation Potentials):**

**Step 1:** Convert reduction potentials to oxidation potentials (sign flip):
$$E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Zn/Zn}^{2+}} = -E^{\circ}_{\text{red, Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -(-0.76) = +0.76 \text{ V}$$
$$E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Cu/Cu}^{2+}} = -E^{\circ}_{\text{red, Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = -(+0.34) = -0.34 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the American formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{anode (oxidation)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode (oxidation)}} \\
&= E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Zn/Zn}^{2+}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Cu/Cu}^{2+}} \\
&= (+0.76) - (-0.34) \\
&= +0.76 + 0.34 \\
&= +1.10 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Identical result — E°cell = +1.10 V** under both conventions. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}}$ using **reduction potentials**, which is **WRONG by 2.12 V** in this case (it would give −1.10 V). Always check whether you are using reduction or oxidation potentials before applying the formula.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sign of Corrosion Current and Faraday's Law

**Faraday's Law of Electrolysis:**

The mass of metal corroded (m) is related to the corrosion current (i) by:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- m = mass of metal dissolved (grams)
- i = current (Amperes)
- t = time (seconds)
- M = atomic mass (g mol⁻¹)
- n = number of electrons transferred per atom
- F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹

**Corrosion Rate (CR) in mm/year:**

$$\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \frac{0.00327 \times i_{\text{corr}} \times M}{n \times \rho}$$

where:
- 0.00327 = (3.15 × 10⁷ s/yr) × (M) / (F) with unit conversion factor
- icorr = corrosion current density (μA/cm²)
- ρ = density of metal (g/cm³)

**Sign Convention for Current:**

- **Anodic current (Ia)** is taken as **positive** because it represents the **oxidation** (metal dissolution) direction.
- **Cathodic current (Ic)** is taken as **positive** when representing the **reduction** direction.
- **icorr** (the corrosion current at the corrosion potential Ecorr) is always reported as a **positive number**, regardless of which half-reaction it represents, because the magnitude (not the sign) determines the rate of material loss.

> [!NOTE]
> In **mixed potential theory** (Evans diagram), the anodic branch is plotted with **positive current (above x-axis)** and the cathodic branch with **negative current (below x-axis)** by *some* textbooks, but the *magnitude* |icorr| at the intersection is always positive.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

**Problem:** A corrosion cell consists of iron in aerated NaCl solution coupled with copper. Given:
- E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) = −0.44 V
- E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V
- E°(O₂/OH⁻) = +0.40 V (in neutral/alkaline solution)
- n = 2 (for Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻)

**Determine:** (a) The anode and cathode. (b) E°cell. (c) ΔG°. (d) Is the reaction spontaneous?

**Solution:**

**(a) Identification:**
- More negative E°red = Fe²⁺/Fe = −0.44 V → **Fe is the anode**.
- More positive E°red = O₂/OH⁻ = +0.40 V → **Oxygen reduction at Cu surface is the cathode**.

**(b) E°cell (IUPAC):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} \\
&= (+0.40) - (-0.44) \\
&= +0.84 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) ΔG°:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} &= -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \\
&= -(2)(96{,}485)(0.84) \\
&= -162{,}095 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \\
&\approx -162.1 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**(d) Spontaneity:** Since E°cell > 0 **and** ΔG° < 0 → **spontaneous corrosion**. ✓

**Overall cell reaction:**
$$\text{Anode: } 2\text{Fe} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{2+} + 4e^-$$
$$\text{Cathode: } \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
$$\text{Net: } 2\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}^{2+} + 4\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{Fe(OH)}_2$$

This is the **rusting of iron** — the most economically important corrosion reaction on Earth.

flowchart TD

    L --> Q
```

    end
    subgraph ELEC["ELECTROLYTIC CELL (Non-spontaneous — External Power)"]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define the IUPAC sign convention for electrode potential. How is the EMF of a galvanic cell calculated under this convention?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **IUPAC (Stockholm) sign convention**, adopted in 1953, defines that the **standard electrode potentials (E°)** are tabulated as **standard reduction potentials** — the potential of the half-reaction written as a **reduction** (Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻ → M), measured relative to the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE, E° = 0.000 V)**.

Under this convention, the **EMF of a galvanic cell** is calculated as:

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode (reduction)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode (reduction)}}$$

A **positive E°cell** indicates a **spontaneous reaction** (galvanic cell), while a **negative E°cell** indicates a **non-spontaneous reaction** (electrolytic cell). The reference electrode is the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)**.

*(Award 1 mark each for: (a) defining reduction potentials, (b) the cell EMF formula, (c) the spontaneity criterion.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between the IUPAC and American sign conventions for electrode potentials. State the conversion relationship between them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | IUPAC (Stockholm) | American (Galvanic) |
|---|---|---|
| Tabulated values | **Reduction** potentials (E°red) | **Oxidation** potentials (E°ox) |
| Reference half-reaction | Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻ → M | M → Mⁿ⁺ + ne⁻ |
| Cell EMF formula | E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode | E°cell = E°anode(ox) − E°cathode(ox) |

**Conversion relationship:**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{oxidation}} = -E^{\circ}_{\text{reduction}}$$

Both conventions yield the **same numerical value and sign of E°cell** when applied correctly.

*(Award 1 mark each for: (a) IUPAC definition, (b) American definition, (c) conversion formula.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the IUPAC (Stockholm) sign convention for electrode potentials. With the help of a neat sketch, describe a Daniel cell and compute its EMF. Given: E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V, E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the relationship ΔG° = −nFE°cell. Using this relationship, comment on the spontaneity of the corrosion of iron in aerated neutral water. Given: E°(Fe²⁺/Fe) = −0.44 V, E°(O₂/OH⁻) = +0.40 V, n = 2.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A:**

**Part (a) — IUPAC Convention & Daniel Cell (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**

The IUPAC convention (Stockholm, 1953) states that all standard electrode potentials are tabulated as **standard reduction potentials** (E°red) measured against the **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE, E° = 0.000 V)**. The cell EMF is given by:

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode (right)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode (left)}}$$

A positive E°cell indicates a **spontaneous (galvanic)** reaction.

**Step 2 — Daniel Cell Sketch & Identification (2 marks):**

The Daniel cell consists of a **zinc electrode** immersed in ZnSO₄ solution and a **copper electrode** immersed in CuSO₄ solution, separated by a **porous pot or salt bridge**.

**Cell notation (IUPAC):**
$$\text{Zn} \mid \text{ZnSO}_4 \,(a=1) \,\|\, \text{CuSO}_4 \,(a=1) \mid \text{Cu}$$

**Anode (oxidation):** Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (E°red = −0.76 V, more negative → anode)
**Cathode (reduction):** Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (E°red = +0.34 V, more positive → cathode)

**Step 3 — EMF Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} \\
&= E^{\circ}_{\text{Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} \\
&= (+0.34) - (-0.76) \\
&= +0.34 + 0.76 \\
&= +1.10 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Since E°cell = +1.10 V (positive), the reaction is spontaneous → Galvanic cell.** ✓

---

**Part (b) — Derivation & Iron Corrosion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Derivation (4 marks):**

The maximum non-expansion (electrical) work obtainable from a galvanic cell at constant T and P is:

$$W_{\text{elec, max}} = -\Delta G^{\circ}$$

The electrical work done when *n* moles of electrons traverse a potential difference E°cell is:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$$

Equating the two:

$$-\Delta G^{\circ} = nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Step 2 — Iron Corrosion Calculation (3 marks):**

**Anode:** Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ (E°red = −0.44 V, anode because more negative)
**Cathode:** O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻ (E°red = +0.40 V, cathode because more positive)

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} \\
&= (+0.40) - (-0.44) \\
&= +0.84 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} &= -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \\
&= -(2)(96{,}485)(0.84) \\
&= -162{,}095 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \\
&\approx -162.1 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Since **E°cell = +0.84 V > 0** and **ΔG° = −162.1 kJ mol⁻¹ < 0**, the corrosion of iron in aerated neutral water is **thermodynamically spontaneous and highly favourable**. This explains why rusting of steel is ubiquitous and economically devastating worldwide.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Define the American (Galvanic) sign convention. Convert the following IUPAC reduction potentials to American oxidation potentials and calculate the cell EMF of a Cu-Zn cell using the American formula: E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V, E°(Cu²⁺/Cu) = +0.34 V. Show that the result matches the IUPAC calculation.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the sign convention for anode and cathode in (i) a galvanic cell and (ii) an electrolytic cell. Why does the polarity reverse between these two cases? Illustrate with a suitable example of cathodic protection.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B:**

**Part (a) — American Convention & EMF Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of American Convention (2 marks):**

The **American (Galvanic) sign convention** tabulates all standard electrode potentials as **standard oxidation potentials (E°ox)**. The reference half-reaction is written as an **oxidation**:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$$

The numerical values are the **negative** of the corresponding IUPAC reduction potentials.

**Step 2 — Conversion (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Zn/Zn}^{2+}} &= -E^{\circ}_{\text{red, Zn}^{2+}/\text{Zn}} = -(-0.76) = +0.76 \text{ V} \\
E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Cu/Cu}^{2+}} &= -E^{\circ}_{\text{red, Cu}^{2+}/\text{Cu}} = -(+0.34) = -0.34 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — American Cell EMF Formula & Calculation (3 marks):**

$$E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode (oxidation)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode (oxidation)}}$$

Since Zn has a **more positive** E°ox (+0.76 V > −0.34 V), Zn is the **anode** and Cu is the **cathode**.

$$\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Zn/Zn}^{2+}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{ox, Cu/Cu}^{2+}} \\
&= (+0.76) - (-0.34) \\
&= +0.76 + 0.34 \\
&= +1.10 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result: E°cell = +1.10 V** — identical to the IUPAC value. ✓ Both conventions are self-consistent.

---

**Part (b) — Anode/Cathode Polarity in Galvanic vs Electrolytic Cells (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Galvanic Cell (3 marks):**

In a **galvanic (spontaneous) cell**:
- **Anode = Negative (−) electrode** (electrons are produced here by oxidation; metal dissolves).
- **Cathode = Positive (+) electrode** (electrons are consumed here by reduction; metal deposits).
- Example: **Daniel cell** — Zn is the negative anode, Cu is the positive cathode.

**Step 2 — Electrolytic Cell (2 marks):**

In an **electrolytic (non-spontaneous) cell** powered by an external battery:
- **Anode = Positive (+) electrode** (connected to the + terminal of the battery; oxidation forced by external EMF).
- **Cathode = Negative (−) electrode** (connected to the − terminal; reduction forced by external EMF).
- Example: **Electroplating** — the object to be plated is the negative cathode.

**Step 3 — Polarity Reversal Principle & Cathodic Protection Example (2 marks):**

The polarity **reverses** because the **driving force for electron flow changes direction**:
- In a galvanic cell, electrons flow **spontaneously** from anode (high energy) to cathode (low energy).
- In an electrolytic cell, the **external battery** forces electrons to flow in the **opposite direction**, reversing the role of each electrode.

**Cathodic Protection Example:** A steel pipeline is protected by a **sacrificial zinc anode**. In the natural galvanic cell formed:
- **Zn = anode (−)**, oxidizes and dissolves (gets consumed).
- **Steel = cathode (+)**, does not corrode because all electrons flow to it.

If the same setup is converted to an **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** system, the polarity assignment changes: the pipeline is forced to be the **cathode (−)** by an external rectifier, while the inert titanium anode becomes the **anode (+)**. Polarity reversal is therefore the operational signature of switching from galvanic to electrolytic mode.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign mix-up** (Most common, loses 2–4 marks): Students write E°cell = E°anode − E°cathode using **reduction potentials**. This is **wrong by 2 × E°**. Always check your table first.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to flip sign in American convention**: When switching from IUPAC to American, the half-cell values change sign, but the formula also changes — failing to update *both* leads to a sign error.
>
> 3. **Wrong identification of anode**: Students often assume the "more reactive" metal is the anode. While usually true, the *exact* criterion is "more negative E°red (IUPAC)" or "more positive E°ox (American)" — not just reactivity.
>
> 4. **Missing units in final answer**: Always write E°cell in **Volts (V)** and ΔG° in **kJ mol⁻¹ or J mol⁻¹** to fetch full marks.
>
> 5. **Polarity confusion in electrolytic cells**: Students write "anode is always negative". This is true for **galvanic** cells only. In electrolytic cells, anode is **positive**. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 6. **Forgetting Faraday's Constant value**: F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹ (or 96,500 C mol⁻¹ in some textbooks) — must be stated explicitly in derivations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Sign Convention** is the **rulebook** for assigning +/− to electrochemical quantities in corrosion.
- [ ] **IUPAC (Stockholm 1953) Convention** is the **global standard** — uses **standard reduction potentials**.
- [ ] **IUPAC Cell EMF formula:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ (both as reductions).
- [ ] **American Convention** uses **standard oxidation potentials** — formula: $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{anode(ox)}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode(ox)}}$.
- [ ] **Conversion identity:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{ox}} = -E^{\circ}_{\text{red}}$.
- [ ] **Master equation:** $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ — links thermodynamics and electrochemistry.
- [ ] **Spontaneity rule:** E°cell > 0 ⇔ ΔG° < 0 ⇔ **Galvanic corrosion is spontaneous**.
- [ ] **Reference electrode:** SHE = 0.000 V (all potentials measured relative to it).
- [ ] **Anode in galvanic cell = (−) electrode; Cathode = (+) electrode.**
- [ ] **Polarity REVERSES in electrolytic cell** — anode becomes (+), cathode becomes (−).
- [ ] **Anode always = site of oxidation** (mass loss); **Cathode always = site of reduction** (mass gain or H₂ evolution).
- [ ] **Faraday's Constant:** F = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (or 96,500 C mol⁻¹).
- [ ] **Nernst Equation (25 °C):** $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log Q$.
- [ ] **EMF–Equilibrium relation:** $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{0.0592}{n} \log K$ at 298.15 K.
- [ ] **Corrosion current (icorr)** is always reported as a **positive magnitude**.
- [ ] **icorr ↔ CR link:** $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = \dfrac{0.00327 \times i_{\text{corr}} \times M}{n \times \rho}$ (Faraday's Law).
