The system settles at a **mixed (corrosion) potential $E_{corr}$** at which the **total rate of oxidation equals the total rate of reduction**, yielding a net external current of zero.

**Evans Diagram** — schematic description for the text-based response:
- **X-axis:** $\log i$ (current density, A/cm²)
- **Y-axis:** Electrode Potential $E$ (V vs. SHE)
- **Anodic branch:** A straight line with slope $\beta_a$, extrapolating to $E_{eq,a}$ at $i = i_{0,a}$.
- **Cathodic branch:** A straight line with slope $-\beta_c$, extrapolating to $E_{eq,c}$ at $i = i_{0,c}$.
- **Intersection point:** This point defines $E_{corr}$ (Y-coordinate) and $i_{corr}$ (X-coordinate).

### Solution to Question B (b):

**(i) Corrosion Potential $E_{corr}$:**

$$E_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c \log\left(\frac{i_{0,c}}{i_{0,a}}\right) + \beta_c E_{eq,a} + \beta_a E_{eq,c}}{\beta_a + \beta_c}$$

Numerator:
- $\beta_a \beta_c \log(i_{0,c}/i_{0,a}) = (0.060)(0.120) \log(10^{-10}/10^{-6}) = 0.0072 \times (-4) = -0.0288$ V
- $\beta_c E_{eq,a} = 0.120 \times (-0.440) = -0.0528$ V
- $\beta_a E_{eq,c} = 0.060 \times 0 = 0$ V
- Sum: $-0.0816$ V

Denominator: $0.060 + 0.120 = 0.180$ V

$$\boxed{E_{corr} = -0.453 \text{ V (SHE)}}$$

**(ii) Corrosion Current Density $i_{corr}$:**

$$i_{corr} = i_{0,a} \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{E_{corr} - E_{eq,a}}{\beta_a}\right)} = 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{-0.453 + 0.440}{0.060}\right)}$$

$$i_{corr} = 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{-0.2167} = 10^{-6} \times 0.608$$

$$\boxed{i_{corr} = 6.08 \times 10^{-7} \text{ A/cm}^2}$$

**(iii) Corrosion Rate in mm/yr:**

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho} = \frac{0.00327 \times 6.08 \times 10^{-7} \times 27.92}{7.87}$$

$$CR = \frac{5.55 \times 10^{-8}}{7.87} = 7.06 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mm/yr}$$

Converting to a more readable form:

$$\boxed{CR \approx 7.06 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mm/yr} = 7.06 \text{ nm/yr}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Mixed Potential Problems:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** $E_{eq,a}$ and $E_{eq,c}$ in the Stern formula. Anodic is the *metal* equilibrium; cathodic is the *reduction* couple.
> 2. **Always** check that $E_{corr}$ lies **between** $E_{eq,a}$ and $E_{eq,c}$. If it does not, you have a sign error.
> 3. **Unit consistency** in the CR formula: $K$ value must match the desired output unit (mm/yr vs. mpy vs. g/m²·day).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Departure from Equilibrium**

### Foundational Concepts
- **Equilibrium State:** $E = E_{eq}$, no net current, forward rate = reverse rate = $i_0$.
- **Departure from Equilibrium:** Quantified by **overpotential** $\eta = E_{app} - E_{eq}$.
- **Exchange Current Density ($i_0$):** A kinetic property of the electrode-electrolyte interface. High $i_0$ = low polarization (e.g., Cu/Cu²⁺); low $i_0$ = high polarization (e.g., Fe/Fe²⁺).

### Three Polarization Modes
- **Activation polarization** — controlled by **charge transfer kinetics**; governed by Tafel equation.
- **Concentration polarization** — controlled by **mass transport**; approaches $i_L$ at high rates.
- **Ohmic (resistance) polarization** — controlled by **electrolyte resistivity**; $\eta = iR$.

### Master Equations
- **Butler-Volmer:** General kinetic equation valid for all $\eta$.
- **Tafel Equation:** $\eta = \pm \beta \log(i/i_0)$ — high-overpotential limit.
- **Stern-Geary:** $i_{corr} = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ — for LPR measurements.
- **Faraday's Law:** Converts $i_{corr}$ to **mm/yr** or **mpy**.

### Numerical Constants to Memorize
- $F = 96{,}485$ C mol⁻¹
- $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹
- $2.303 RT/F = 0.0592$ V at 298 K (Nernst factor for $n=1$)
- $0 < \alpha < 1$, typically 0.4–0.6

### Common KTU Board Traps
- Mixing anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (sign and magnitude).
- Forgetting that $i_{corr}$ is a **scalar** (magnitude) — not a vector.
- Confusing $E_{corr}$ (mixed potential) with $E_{eq}$ (true equilibrium).
- Omitting units in the final numerical answer.

### Diagram & Graph Literacy
- An **Evans Diagram** = $E$ vs. $\log i$ plot with intersecting Tafel lines.
- **Tafel Slope** = slope of the linear region in $E$ vs. $\log i$ plot.
- **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR)** = slope of $E$ vs. $i$ near $E_{corr}$ (in the small-$\eta$ limit).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Corrosion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Corrosion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Corrosion measurement** is the quantitative and qualitative determination of the extent, rate, and nature of degradation of a material resulting from its electrochemical or chemical interaction with the surrounding environment. It encompasses a family of analytical techniques—chemical, electrochemical, gravimetric, and non-destructive—employed to quantify the **corrosion rate (CR)**, evaluate the efficacy of preventive measures, predict service life of components, and support failure analysis in engineering systems. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** > Under the NEP 2020 / 2024 OBE framework, students must be able to **describe, classify, and apply** various methods of corrosion rate determination. The expected outcome links to *CO2: "Apply standard methods to evaluate corrosion rate and select mitigation strategies."* Mathematically, corrosion measurement rests upon three fundamental pillars: $$\text{Corrosion Rate} = \frac{\text{Mass Loss (g)}}{\text{Exposed Area (cm}^2\text{)} \times \text{Time (h)}}$$ which is often expressed in **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimetres per year (mm/yr)** as a final, standardized engineering unit. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a block of ice placed in a warm room. You could measure its "melting rate" by: - **Weighing it every hour** → *Gravimetric (Weight Loss) Method* - **Measuring how fast the water pools on the floor** → *Hydrogen Evolution / Oxygen Consumption Method* - **Measuring the electrical current that flows through the puddle** (since ice-water interaction is electrochemical in nature, akin to metal-acid) → *Electrochemical Method (Tafel / LPR)* - **Looking at it with a magnifying glass once a week** → *Visual & Non-Destructive Inspection* - **Listening to the sound waves bouncing off it** as it shrinks → *Ultrasonic Thickness Gauging* In every case, the **goal is the same: convert an indirect observable into a rate of material loss per unit time.** Corrosion is essentially the *melting of metals in slow motion*, and the "thermometer" we use depends on what we can physically or chemically observe. > [!NOTE] **Standard Reporting Units (must memorize)** > - **mpy** = mils per year (1 mil = 0.001 inch = **25.4 µm**) > - **mm/yr** = SI preferred unit > - **g/m²·day** = common in atmospheric studies > - **µA/cm²** = current density (used before conversion to mass loss) ## 1.3 Classification of Measurement Techniques Corrosion measurement methods can be classified into **two major paradigms**: | Paradigm | Sub-Techniques | Principle | |---|---|---| | **Direct / Non-Electrochemical** | Weight loss, visual inspection, dimensional change, solution analysis | Measures physical/chemical consequence of corrosion | | **Electrochemical** | Tafel extrapolation, Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR), Potentiodynamic, EIS, Potentiostatic | Measures electrical signal from ongoing corrosion reaction | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Gravimetric (Weight Loss) Method — The Gold Standard The most reliable, frequently used, and historically the **first method** in corrosion engineering. A coupon of known mass, geometry, and surface area is exposed to the corrosive environment, removed after a specified duration, cleaned of corrosion product, re-weighed, and the mass loss is recorded. ### Operating Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Specimen preparation** — Surface polishing to a uniform finish (e.g., 600-grit emery), degreasing with acetone, drying, and accurate weighing to **±0.0001 g** precision. 2. **Exposure** — Coupon is mounted on a holder such that only a known surface area is exposed. 3. **Retrieval & Cleaning** — Removal of corrosion product using a **pickling solution** (e.g., Clarke's solution for steel: HCl + Sb₂O₃ + SnCl₂), re-weighing. 4. **Mass loss calculation** — Difference in mass = **W (g)**. 5. **Normalization** — Convert W to a rate per unit area per unit time. > [!IMPORTANT] **Why use this method in the lab?** > It is the **only** method that gives a *direct, unambiguous* measurement of actual metal loss. Every other method is benchmarked against the weight-loss result for validation. ### Faraday's Law — The Bridge Between Chemistry and Electricity The electrochemical foundation of corrosion measurement is **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis**, which states: $$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ where the symbols are precisely: - $W$ = mass of metal dissolved (g) - $I$ = corrosion current (A) - $t$ = time of exposure (s) - $M$ = atomic mass of the metal (g/mol) - $n$ = number of electrons transferred per metal atom - $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol in exam problems) By rearranging, we obtain the **corrosion current** from weight loss: $$I_{corr} = \frac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t}$$ ## 2.2 The Electrochemical Triad — Tafel, LPR, and Impedance ### 2.2.1 Tafel Extrapolation When a metal is polarized ±250 mV away from its open-circuit potential ($E_{corr}$), the **logarithm of current density** is linearly related to overpotential, as described by the **Tafel equation**: $$\eta = \pm \beta \log\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ where: - $\eta$ = overpotential = $E - E_{corr}$ (V) - $\beta$ = Tafel slope (V/decade) — $\beta_a$ for anodic, $\beta_c$ for cathodic - $i$ = applied current density (A/cm²) - $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/cm²) When we plot **$E$ vs $\log \vert i \vert$**, both the anodic and cathodic branches become straight lines, and extrapolating these lines back to $E_{corr}$ gives us $i_{corr}$. ### 2.2.2 Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR / Stern-Geary) Stern and Geary (1957) showed that within ±10 to ±20 mV of $E_{corr}$, the relationship between overpotential and current is **linear**, and the slope is the **polarization resistance $R_p$**: $$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ The simplified **Stern-Geary equation** for $i_{corr}$ is: $$i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}$$ where the **Stern-Geary constant**: $$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ Typical values of $B$: - For active metals (steel, zinc, aluminum): $B \approx$ **26 mV** - For passive metals (stainless steel, Ni-Cr alloys): $B \approx$ **52 mV** ### 2.2.3 Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) A small AC perturbation (±5 to ±10 mV) is applied over a wide frequency range (10⁵ Hz to 10⁻³ Hz). The impedance response yields a **Nyquist plot** (Z'' vs Z') or **Bode plot** (|Z| and phase vs frequency), from which charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$ is extracted, and $i_{corr}$ calculated. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Key Constants / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Weight loss → mpy | $W$ (mg), $D$ (g/cm³), $A$ (in²), $T$ (h); **534** = (10⁶/9.91) conversion | | 2 | $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Weight loss → mm/yr | $W$ (mg), $D$ (g/cm³), $A$ (cm²), $T$ (h); **87.6** = (10⁻¹ × 24 × 365)/10 | | 3 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t}$ | Mass loss → current | $F = 96,485$ C/mol | | 4 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law | $F = 96,485$ C/mol | | 5 | $\eta = \pm \beta \log\left(\dfrac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$ | Tafel equation | $\beta$ in V/decade | | 6 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | Stern-Geary equation | $R_p$ in $\Omega \cdot$cm² | | 7 | $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | Simplified LPR form | $B$ in V, $R_p$ in $\Omega \cdot$cm² | | 8 | $CR_{mpy} = 0.129 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot \dfrac{EW}{D}$ | Electrochem. CR in mpy | $i_{corr}$ in $\mu$A/cm², $D$ in g/cm³ | | 9 | $EW = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | Equivalent weight | dimensionless (g/eq) | | 10 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Nernst Equation | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K | > [!IMPORTANT] **About the vertical pipe rule** — in this formula sheet, all absolute value notations and ratio separators use $\vert$ or $\log\left(\right)$ instead of the bare `|`, so the table integrity is preserved. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Corrosion Measurement - **Service-life prediction** for bridges, pipelines, ship hulls, and storage tanks. - **Quality assurance** of protective coatings and inhibitors. - **Selection benchmarking** of alloys for specific environments. - **Real-time monitoring** of process plants via corrosion probes. - **Regulatory compliance** for oil & gas, chemical, and nuclear industries. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Corrosion Rate in mpy from Weight Loss We need to convert a measured weight loss (mg) into a linear penetration rate (mpy) — i.e., depth of metal removed per year. ### Step 1 — Express mass loss as volume The volume of metal dissolved is: $$V = \frac{W}{D}$$ where $W$ is the mass loss and $D$ is the metal density. ### Step 2 — Express volume as linear thickness If $V$ is the volume and $A$ is the exposed area, then the **thickness of metal removed** is: $$\Delta L = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A}$$ ### Step 3 — Normalize to time For exposure time $T$ (hours), the rate of thickness change is: $$\frac{\Delta L}{T} = \frac{W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert from inches/hour to mils/year We have: - $W$ in **milligrams (mg)** - $D$ in **g/cm³** - $A$ in **in²** - $T$ in **hours (h)** The unit conversion chain is: - mg → g: divide by 1000 - cm³ → in³: multiply by $(1/2.54)^3 = 1/16.387$ - in → mil: multiply by 1000 - hour → year: multiply by $(24 \times 365) = 8760$ So the numerical constant is: $$K = \frac{1000 \times 16.387}{1000 \times 8760} \times \frac{1}{2.54} \approx 534$$ Hence the final working formula: $$\boxed{CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}}$$ For the SI version (mm/yr) using $A$ in cm² and $T$ in hours: $$\boxed{CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}}$$ The derivation constant **87.6** comes from: $$\frac{(1/1000) \cdot (1/8760) \cdot 10}{(1/8) \cdot 100} = 87.6 \text{ mm/yr equivalent}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Corrosion Current from Faraday's Law ### Step 1 — Start with Faraday's First Law $$W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$ ### Step 2 — Solve for $I$ $$I = \frac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{t \cdot M}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to current density by dividing by area $A$ $$i_{corr} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot M}$$ ### Step 4 — Recognize that $M/n$ is the equivalent weight $EW$ $$i_{corr} = \frac{W \cdot F}{A \cdot t \cdot EW}$$ This is the form that allows the electrochemical corrosion rate to be computed directly from measured mass loss and the equivalent weight of the corroding metal. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stern-Geary Equation from Tafel Kinetics ### Step 1 — Butler-Volmer Equation (foundation) For a single corrosion couple, the net current density $i$ at potential $E$ is the difference between the anodic and cathodic partial currents: $$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$ where $\eta = E - E_{corr}$. ### Step 2 — Small overpotential limit ($\vert \eta/\beta \vert \ll 1$) Using the Taylor expansion $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$: $$i \approx i_{corr}\left[1 + \frac{\eta}{\beta_a} - 1 + \frac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right] = i_{corr} \cdot \eta \left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain $R_p$ $$R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{1}{i_{corr}\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right)} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{i_{corr}(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert from natural log to base-10 log Because Tafel constants are conventionally reported in base-10 logarithms, we multiply by the conversion factor $\ln(10) = 2.303$: $$R_p = \frac{2.303 \cdot \beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{i_{corr} \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $i_{corr}$ $$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Guide | Method | Speed | Accuracy | Cost | Application Domain | |---|---|---|---|---| | Weight loss | Days–months | Highest | Low | Lab benchmarks, long-term testing | | Tafel extrapolation | Minutes | High | Moderate | Lab, mechanistic studies | | Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) | Seconds | High | Moderate | Field probes, online monitoring | | Potentiodynamic Polarization | Minutes | High | High | Passivity studies, pitting | | Cyclic Polarization | Minutes | High | High | Pitting & repassivation assessment | | EIS | Minutes | High | High | Coating evaluation, inhibitor screening | | Hydrogen evolution | Real-time | Moderate | Low | Acid pickling, gas pipelines | | Electrical Resistance (ER) probe | Real-time | High | Moderate | Plant monitoring in non-electrolytes | | Visual / thickness | Variable | Low–Moderate | Low | Inspections, asset integrity | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three-Electrode Electrochemical Cell — Block-Level Architecture This is the standard laboratory configuration used for Tafel extrapolation, LPR, and Potentiodynamic polarization measurements. ```mermaid flowchart LR PS[Potentiostat / Galvanostat] -- applies E and measures i --> WE WE[Working Electrode
specimen under test] --- ENV[Corrosive Electrolyte] CE[Counter Electrode
platinum or graphite] --- ENV RE[Reference Electrode
SCE or Ag-AgCl] --- ENV PS --- CE PS --- RE ENV[Corrosive Electrolyte
bulk solution] --> WE ENV --> CE ENV --> RE PS --> PC[Data Acquisition PC
plot E vs log i] ``` > [!NOTE] **Working principle:** The potentiostat measures the current flowing between the Working Electrode (WE) and the Counter Electrode (CE) while controlling the potential of WE relative to the Reference Electrode (RE). This three-terminal arrangement eliminates the IR drop that would otherwise distort the polarization data. ## 4.2 Tafel Plot — Schematic for Anodic and Cathodic Extrapolation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SP["Semi-log Tafel plot E on Y axis log i on X axis"] direction TB P0((Log i corr at E corr)) --- AN[Anodic branch of anodic polarization] P0 --- CAT[Cathodic branch of cathodic polarization] end E0[E corr] --- P0 BA[slope beta a] --- AN BC[slope beta c] --- CAT EX1[extrapolate anodic to E corr] --- X1[read i corr on X axis] EX2[extrapolate cathodic to E corr] --- X1 X1 --> OUT[Corrosion current density i corr from intersection] ``` ## 4.3 Corrosion Measurement Strategy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify the corrosion problem] --> B{Environment type} B -->|Electrolyte present| C[Use electrochemical methods] B -->|Gas or organic fluid| D[Use weight loss or ER probe] C --> E{Time available} E -->|Seconds| F[Use Linear Polarization Resistance] E -->|Minutes| G[Use Tafel or Potentiodynamic Polarization] E -->|Detailed analysis| H[Use EIS and Cyclic Polarization] D --> I[Use mass loss coupon] F --> J[Convert i corr to CR mpy or mm per yr] G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[Corrosion rate quantified] ``` ## 4.4 Stern-Geary LPR Measurement Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SAM[Small perturbation signal 10 mV peak to peak] --> POT[Potentiostat] POT --> POL[Polarize WE around E corr] POL --> I_MEAS[Measure current response delta i] I_MEAS --> RCOMP[Compute R p = delta E by delta i] RCOMP --> ICOMP[Compute i corr = B by R p] ICOMP --> CRFIN[Convert to mpy using Faraday equivalent] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Q:** List any three direct methods of corrosion rate measurement and state the principle on which each is based. **Model Answer:** 1. **Weight loss (gravimetric) method** — Principle: the actual mass of metal dissolved from a coupon of known area over a known time is measured by weighing before and after exposure. The mass loss is converted to a corrosion rate using the formula $CR_{mpy} = (534 \cdot W)/(D \cdot A \cdot T)$. 2. **Hydrogen evolution method** — Principle: the volume of hydrogen gas liberated from a corroding metal immersed in an acid environment is collected and measured; this is stoichiometrically equivalent to the metal dissolved, so the gas volume gives a real-time estimate of corrosion rate. 3. **Solution analysis method** — Principle: the concentration of metal ions in the corrosive medium is determined by analytical chemistry (atomic absorption spectroscopy, spectrophotometry, or titration). The increase in metal-ion concentration over time is directly related to the mass of metal lost. > [!WARNING] **Common mistake to avoid** — Students often confuse "hydrogen evolution" with "oxygen consumption." In acidic solutions, hydrogen evolution is the cathodic reaction. In neutral or near-neutral aerated solutions, oxygen consumption is the relevant measurement. Writing both interchangeably will lose you the mark. --- ### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Q:** Define the polarization resistance $R_p$ and explain its significance in corrosion rate measurement. **Model Answer:** **Polarization resistance $R_p$** is the slope of the potential versus current density plot at the corrosion potential, defined as: $$R_p = \left(\frac{dE}{di}\right)_{E = E_{corr}}$$ **Significance:** - It is **inversely proportional to the corrosion current density** $i_{corr}$ through the **Stern-Geary equation** $i_{corr} = B/R_p$. - $R_p$ measurement requires only a **very small perturbation** (typically $\pm 10$ mV) about $E_{corr}$, which means the corrosion process is **not disturbed** during measurement. - It enables **rapid, non-destructive, and real-time** estimation of corrosion rate, making it ideal for in-plant monitoring using LPR probes. - A **higher $R_p$** indicates **lower corrosion rate** and vice versa. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a **Tafel extrapolation method** for the determination of corrosion rate. State the Tafel equation. **(b) [7 marks]** A steel specimen of area **15 cm²** is exposed to an aerated acid solution for **48 hours**. The mass loss is found to be **0.182 g**. Calculate: (i) the corrosion rate in **mpy**, (ii) the corrosion rate in **mm/yr**, (iii) the equivalent corrosion current density in **µA/cm²**. Given: density of steel = **7.86 g/cm³**, equivalent weight of Fe = **27.92 g/eq**. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** ### Part (a) — Tafel Extrapolation Method **Working Principle:** In the Tafel method, the corroding metal is polarized over a wide range (typically ±250 mV about $E_{corr}$) using a potentiostat. The anodic and cathodic branches of the polarization curve are plotted as $E$ vs $\log \vert i \vert$. In the high-overpotential region (Tafel region), the curves are linear and obey the Tafel equation. **Tafel equation:** $$\eta = E - E_{corr} = \pm \beta \log\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$$ **Procedure:** 1. The **Working Electrode** (steel specimen) is placed in the electrolyte with a **Reference Electrode** (SCE) and a **Counter Electrode** (platinum). 2. The **Open-Circuit Potential (OCP)** is measured for 1 hour to allow $E_{corr}$ to stabilize. 3. The potential is then scanned from about $-300$ mV to $+300$ mV relative to $E_{corr}$ at a slow rate (e.g., 0.5 mV/s). 4. The resulting $E$ vs $\log \vert i \vert$ plot shows two linear Tafel regions. 5. The anodic and cathodic Tafel lines are extrapolated to their **point of intersection at $E = E_{corr}$**. 6. The current density at this intersection is read off the x-axis as **$i_{corr}$**. 7. The corrosion rate is then obtained from $i_{corr}$ using the Faraday's-law conversion: $$CR_{mm/yr} = 0.00327 \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$$ **Sketch:** ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TAFEL["E versus log i plot"] direction TB Y[E on Y axis] --- ORIG ORIG((E corr)) --- ANL[Anodic Tafel line slope beta a] ORIG --- CTL[Cathodic Tafel line slope beta c] ANL --- R_AN((anodic branch)) CTL --- R_CA((cathodic branch)) R_AN --- IC[read off i corr at intersection] R_CA --- IC end X[log i on X axis] --- IC ``` > [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently plot $E$ on the x-axis and $\log \vert i \vert$ on the y-axis. The **correct** convention (per ASTM G3 and G59) is to plot **$E$ (potential) on the y-axis** and **$\log \vert i \vert$ on the x-axis** — exactly the reverse of a "y = f(x)" plot in algebra. Mixing this up loses a mark. ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given data:** - $W = 0.182$ g $= 182$ mg - $A = 15$ cm² $= 2.325$ in² (since $1$ in² $= 6.4516$ cm²) - $T = 48$ h - $D = 7.86$ g/cm³ - $EW = 27.92$ g/eq - $F = 96,485$ C/mol #### (i) Corrosion rate in mpy $$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$ **Step 1 — substitute:** $$CR_{mpy} = \frac{534 \times 182}{7.86 \times 2.325 \times 48}$$ **Step 2 — calculate the numerator:** $$534 \times 182 = 97,188$$ **Step 3 — calculate the denominator:** $$7.86 \times 2.325 = 18.275$$ $$18.275 \times 48 = 877.2$$ **Step 4 — final division:** $$CR_{mpy} = \frac{97,188}{877.2} \approx 110.8 \text{ mpy}$$ #### (ii) Corrosion rate in mm/yr $$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$$ **Step 1 — substitute (with A in cm², here A = 15 cm²):** $$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{87.6 \times 182}{7.86 \times 15 \times 48}$$ **Step 2 — numerator:** $$87.6 \times 182 = 15,943.2$$ **Step 3 — denominator:** $$7.86 \times 15 = 117.9$$ $$117.9 \times 48 = 5,659.2$$ **Step 4 — final division:** $$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{15,943.2}{5,659.2} \approx 2.82 \text{ mm/yr}$$ #### (iii) Corrosion current density **Step 1 — convert mass loss to coulombs using Faraday's law:** $$I = \frac{W \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t}$$ But we use the equivalent weight $EW = M/n$, so: $$I = \frac{W \cdot F}{EW \cdot t}$$ **Step 2 — substitute:** $$I = \frac{0.182 \times 96,485}{27.92 \times (48 \times 3600)}$$ **Step 3 — numerator:** $$0.182 \times 96,485 = 17,560.27 \text{ C}$$ **Step 4 — denominator:** $$27.92 \times 172,800 = 4,824,576 \text{ C/(A·s) = } 1 \text{ C/s for 1 A}$$ Wait — careful. The unit of $EW \cdot t$ in denominator is $(\text{g/eq}) \times \text{s}$. So: $$27.92 \times 172,800 = 4,824,576 \text{ g·s/eq}$$ **Step 5 — final division:** $$I = \frac{17,560.27}{4,824,576} = 3.639 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 3,639 \text{ µA}$$ **Step 6 — convert to current density:** $$i_{corr} = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{3,639 \text{ µA}}{15 \text{ cm}^2} \approx 242.6 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$ **Final Answers (3 s.f.):** - $CR_{mpy} \approx 111$ mpy - $CR_{mm/yr} \approx 2.82$ mm/yr - $i_{corr} \approx 243$ µA/cm² > [!WARNING] **Valuation Warning:** Most students make unit-conversion errors. The two most common mistakes are: > 1. **Forgetting to convert cm² to in²** when using the mpy formula (constant 534). The formula $CR = 534 W/(D A T)$ requires **A in in²** — many students keep A in cm² and get an answer 15× too small. > 2. **Using $W$ in grams instead of mg** with the 534 constant. The constant 534 was derived for $W$ in mg; using grams will give a 1000× error. > Always write down the units next to every number to be safe. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR) technique** for the determination of corrosion rate. Derive the **Stern-Geary equation**. **(b) [7 marks]** In a Tafel experiment on **zinc** in 0.5 M H₂SO₄, the cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c$ is **120 mV/decade** and the anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a$ is **90 mV/decade**. The polarization resistance $R_p$ is measured to be **250 $\Omega \cdot$cm²**. Calculate: (i) the Stern-Geary constant $B$, (ii) the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$, (iii) the corrosion rate in **mm/yr** for zinc. Given: atomic mass of Zn = 65.38 g/mol, $n = 2$, density of Zn = 7.13 g/cm³. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** ### Part (a) — Linear Polarization Resistance Technique **Principle:** When a metal is perturbed by a small potential (typically $\pm 10$ mV) about its $E_{corr}$, the resulting current response is essentially **linear** with the overpotential. The slope of this $\Delta E$ vs $\Delta i$ line is called the **polarization resistance** $R_p$. **Derivation of Stern-Geary Equation:** **Step 1 — Start with the Butler-Volmer equation:** $$i = i_{corr}\left[\exp\left(\frac{\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right)\right]$$ **Step 2 — Apply small-perturbation approximation** ($\vert \eta/\beta \vert \ll 1$): Using $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x + x^2/2 + \ldots$, and truncating to first order: $$i \approx i_{corr}\left[1 + \frac{\eta}{\beta_a} - 1 + \frac{\eta}{\beta_c}\right]$$ $$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \eta \left(\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Invert to get $R_p$:** $$R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{1}{i_{corr}\left(\dfrac{1}{\beta_a} + \dfrac{1}{\beta_c}\right)}$$ **Step 4 — Combine over a common denominator:** $$R_p = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{i_{corr} \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ **Step 5 — Convert from natural logarithm to base 10** (since $\beta$ values are usually reported per decade): Multiplying numerator and denominator by 2.303: $$\boxed{R_p = \frac{2.303 \cdot \beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{i_{corr} \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}}$$ **Step 6 — Rearranged form for $i_{corr}$:** $$\boxed{i_{corr} = \frac{2.303 \cdot \beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{R_p \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{B}{R_p}}$$ **Procedure for LPR measurement:** 1. Set up a **three-electrode cell** with the working electrode (test specimen), reference electrode (SCE or Ag/AgCl), and counter electrode (Pt). 2. Measure the **$E_{corr}$** (open-circuit potential) after stabilization. 3. Apply a **small linear potential sweep** of $\pm 10$ mV around $E_{corr}$ at a slow scan rate (0.1 mV/s). 4. Record the current response. 5. Calculate the slope of the $E$ vs $i$ plot — this is $R_p$. 6. Apply the Stern-Geary equation to obtain $i_{corr}$. 7. Convert $i_{corr}$ to CR (mm/yr or mpy). **Significance:** LPR is **fast, repeatable, and non-destructive** — it can be used for in-field monitoring. The small perturbation ensures the metal surface is not significantly altered. ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** - $\beta_c = 120$ mV/decade - $\beta_a = 90$ mV/decade - $R_p = 250$ $\Omega \cdot$cm² - $M = 65.38$ g/mol - $n = 2$ - $D = 7.13$ g/cm³ #### (i) Stern-Geary constant $B$ **Step 1 — write the formula:** $$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$ **Step 2 — substitute values (in volts for unit consistency):** $$B = \frac{0.090 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times (0.090 + 0.120)}$$ **Step 3 — numerator:** $$0.090 \times 0.120 = 0.0108 \text{ V}^2$$ **Step 4 — denominator:** $$0.090 + 0.120 = 0.210$$ $$2.303 \times 0.210 = 0.48363$$ **Step 5 — final division:** $$B = \frac{0.0108}{0.48363} \approx 0.0223 \text{ V} = 22.3 \text{ mV}$$ #### (ii) Corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ **Step 1 — use $i_{corr} = B/R_p$:** $$i_{corr} = \frac{0.0223}{250}$$ **Step 2 — compute:** $$i_{corr} = 8.93 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2 = 89.3 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$ #### (iii) Corrosion rate in mm/yr **Step 1 — use the electrochemical-to-CR conversion:** $$CR_{mm/yr} = 0.00327 \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$$ where the constant 0.00327 incorporates Faraday's constant and time-unit conversion. To derive it, recall that: $$CR_{cm/s} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot D} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F \cdot D}$$ Converting cm/s to mm/yr with the factor $10 \times 31,536,000 = 3.15 \times 10^{8}$ and then noting that we want µA/cm² as input gives the prefactor 0.00327. **Step 2 — substitute:** $$CR_{mm/yr} = 0.00327 \cdot \frac{(89.3 \times 10^{-6}) \times 65.38}{2 \times 7.13}$$ **Step 3 — numerator of the fraction:** $$89.3 \times 10^{-6} \times 65.38 = 5.838 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 4 — denominator of the fraction:** $$2 \times 7.13 = 14.26$$ **Step 5 — divide:** $$\frac{5.838 \times 10^{-3}}{14.26} = 4.094 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 6 — multiply by 0.00327:** $$CR_{mm/yr} = 0.00327 \times 4.094 \times 10^{-4} = 1.34 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm/yr}$$ This result seems extraordinarily small — let me re-verify the formula's prefactor. The correct, fully-derived conversion is: $$CR_{mm/yr} = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot D}$$ where $i_{corr}$ is in µA/cm², $M$ in g/mol, $D$ in g/cm³. Re-doing the arithmetic carefully: **Step 6 (corrected) —** Numerator: $3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 89.3 \times 65.38 = 3.27 \times 10^{-3} \times 5838 = 19.09$ **Step 7 —** Denominator: $2 \times 7.13 = 14.26$ **Step 8 —** Division: $19.09/14.26 \approx 1.34$ $$CR_{mm/yr} \approx 1.34 \text{ mm/yr}$$ > [!WARNING] **Valuation Warning:** The constant 0.00327 is dimensionally tricky. Some textbooks use 0.00327 mm/yr, others 0.129 mpy, others 3.27. If you write 0.129 thinking you want mpy, you will be **9× off** because the unit of the output matters. **Always show the substitution clearly** and let the examiner see your dimensional thinking. **Final Answers (3 s.f.):** - $B \approx 22.3$ mV - $i_{corr} \approx 89.3$ µA/cm² - $CR \approx 1.34$ mm/yr --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Measurement of Corrosion"** ### A. Concepts to Memorize - **Weight loss method** is the **primary benchmark** against which all other methods are validated. Formula: $CR_{mpy} = (534 W)/(D A T)$, with $W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in in², $T$ in h. - **Faraday's law** ($W = ItM/(nF)$) is the **bridge** between mass loss and corrosion current. **$F = 96,485$ C/mol** is the keystone constant. - **Tafel extrapolation** uses $\pm 250$ mV polarization and plots $E$ vs $\log \vert i \vert$. Extrapolation to $E_{corr}$ gives $i_{corr}$. **Convention: $E$ on y-axis, $\log \vert i \vert$ on x-axis.** - **LPR (Stern-Geary)** uses only $\pm 10$ mV and is **linear, fast, and non-destructive**. Equation: $i_{corr} = B/R_p$ where $B \approx 26$ mV (active) or $\approx 52$ mV (passive). - **Cyclic polarization** is used specifically for **pitting studies** — the hysteresis loop reveals repassivation behavior. - **EIS** uses small-amplitude AC perturbation and gives charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$ from a Nyquist semicircle. - **Equivalent weight** $EW = M/n$ — this is the quantity that must be plugged into Faraday-based rate conversions. - **Hydrogen evolution** dominates in **acid** systems; **oxygen reduction** dominates in **near-neutral aerated** systems. ### B. Numerical Constants to Memorize | Constant | Value | Use | |---|---|---| | Faraday $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** | Faraday's law | | $2.303$ | $\log_{10}(e) \times 2$ | Tafel / Stern-Geary | | $534$ | mpy constant | Weight loss → mpy | | $87.6$ | mm/yr constant | Weight loss → mm/yr | | $0.00327$ | mm/yr prefactor | $i_{corr}$ → mm/yr | | $0.129$ | mpy prefactor | $i_{corr}$ → mpy | | $B$ (active) | $\approx 26$ mV | Steel, Zn, Al | | $B$ (passive) | $\approx 52$ mV | Stainless steel, Ni-Cr | ### C. Common Pitfalls (Valuation Black Holes) - **Wrong axis convention** on Tafel plot (E on x-axis instead of y-axis) → lose 2 marks. - **Unit mismatch** in weight loss formula (using cm² instead of in² with constant 534) → answer off by ~6.45×. - **Forgetting the 2.303 factor** in Stern-Geary derivation → answer off by ~2.3×. - **Confusing $R_p$ and $R_{ct}$** — they are different in EIS but related in DC polarization. - **Mixing up anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes** — a single wrong sign on $\beta_a$ or $\beta_c$ propagates through the entire $i_{corr}$ value. - **Using EW where MW is needed** (or vice versa) in Faraday's law — when the corrosion reaction involves multiple electron transfer steps, always verify $n$. ### D. Cross-Reference Map (How Topics Link) ``` Measurement of Corrosion ├── Weight Loss → Empirical benchmark │ └── Uses Faraday's Law (Module 1 linkage) ├── Tafel → Activation-controlled kinetics │ └── Links to Mixed Potential Theory (Module 1) ├── LPR → Stern-Geary → Electrochemical theory │ └── Links to Polarization and Passivity (Module 3) ├── Cyclic Polarization → Pitting & Crevice Corrosion (Module 3) └── EIS → Coating evaluation → Protective Coatings (Module 5) ``` --- > [!TIP] **One-Line Exam Mnemonic:** "**W**e **M**easure **E**lectrochemically **T**o **L**earn **E**xchange **R**ates" → Weight-loss, Mixed-potential, Electrochemical, Tafel, LPR, EIS, Resistance-probe. ---

topics.title: Determination of Corrosion Rates by Electrochemical Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Corrosion Rates by Electrochemical Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrochemical corrosion rate determination** refers to a family of in-situ, non-destructive electrochemical techniques used to quantify the instantaneous rate at which a metal degrades in a corrosive electrolyte. Unlike mass-loss methods (which require days or weeks of exposure), these techniques yield corrosion rates within minutes by analyzing the current/voltage response of a corroding metal–electrolyte interface.

The most widely adopted KTU-relevant methods are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Electrochemical Corrosion Rate Measurement (KTU Module 2):**
> 1. **Tafel Extrapolation Method (Polarization Method)** – faradaic, high-overpotential region.
> 2. **Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR / Stern–Geary Method)** – low-overpotential region.
> 3. **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS)** – AC frequency-domain method.

All three rely on a standard **three-electrode electrochemical cell**:

| Electrode | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Working Electrode (WE)** | The corroding metal under study (the "victim"). |
| **Reference Electrode (RE)** | Maintains a stable, known potential (SCE, Ag/AgCl, SHE). |
| **Counter (Auxiliary) Electrode (CE)** | Completes the circuit; carries the polarizing current. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Leaking Bucket with a Pressure Gauge:**
> Imagine a bucket (the metal) full of water (electrons) that is leaking through a hole (corrosion). You cannot see the hole directly, but you can:
> - **Apply a small push** (a tiny voltage) and watch how easily water flows → this is the *Linear Polarization Resistance* idea (low overpotential).
> - **Apply a strong push** (a large voltage) and watch the flow pattern on a log-paper graph → this is the *Tafel Extrapolation* idea (high overpotential).
> - **Tap the bucket gently at different frequencies** and watch how it vibrates back (resistance) → this is the *EIS / Nyquist plot* idea.

In all cases, **the easier it is for current to flow for a given voltage, the faster the metal is corroding**. The measured "ease of current flow" is mathematically converted into a corrosion current, and Faraday's Law converts *current* into *mass loss per unit time*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Key-Insight (KTU Board Favourite):**
> All electrochemical corrosion-rate techniques ultimately aim to determine a single quantity — the **corrosion current density, $i_{corr}$** (in $\mu A/cm^2$) — at the **mixed (corrosion) potential $E_{corr}$**. Once $i_{corr}$ is known, Faraday's law converts it to a corrosion rate (mpy or mm/yr).

---

### 1.3 Standard Constants & Units Used Throughout

- **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \ C \cdot mol^{-1}$
- **Gas constant:** $R = 8.314 \ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$
- **Tafel slopes:** typically reported in **mV/decade**
- **Equivalent weight (EW):** dimensionless, in grams per equivalent
- **Density $\rho$:** in $g/cm^3$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Butler–Volmer Foundation (The Why Behind All Three Methods)

When a metal corrodes freely, anodic dissolution and cathodic reduction occur at the *same* surface at the *same* rate — the **net external current is zero**, but the *internal* anodic and cathodic currents are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign. This magnitude is the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$**.

The **Butler–Volmer equation** describes the net current density $i$ as a function of the overpotential $\eta = E - E_{corr}$:

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

Where:
- $i$ = net current density ($A/cm^2$)
- $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density ($A/cm^2$)
- $\eta$ = overpotential, $E - E_{corr}$ (V)
- $\beta_a, \beta_c$ = anodic & cathodic Tafel slopes (V/decade)

The Tafel slopes encode the *kinetics* of the metal dissolution and the cathodic reaction (often oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution).

### 2.2 Three Regimes of the Polarization Curve

| Region | Overpotential $\vert \eta \vert$ | Approximation | Technique Used |
|--------|----------------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| **Linear (small $\eta$)** | < 10 mV | $i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \dfrac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a+\beta_c} \cdot \dfrac{F}{RT}$ simplification → **Ohmic-like** | LPR / Stern–Geary |
| **Tafel (large $\eta$)** | > 50 mV | Exponential dominates, log-linear | Tafel Extrapolation |
| **AC frequency sweep** | small amplitude sinusoid | $Z(\omega)$ response | EIS |

### 2.3 The Stern–Geary Equation (The Bridge Equation)

Linearizing the Butler–Volmer equation at very small overpotentials gives the famous **Stern–Geary relation**:

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}\bigg\vert_{\eta \to 0} = \frac{B}{i_{corr}}$$

Where the **Stern–Geary constant $B$** is:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \, \beta_c}{2.303 \, (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $B$ is called the "constant" — KTU Board Trap:**
> $B$ is *experimentally constant for a given metal–environment system*, not a universal constant. Common accepted defaults (only when Tafel slopes are unknown) are $B = 26 \ mV$ (active systems) and $B = 52 \ mV$ (passive systems). Examiners award full marks **only if the student measures $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ first**.

### 2.4 Tafel Extrapolation Logic (Step-by-Step Intuition)

1. Polarize the working electrode away from $E_{corr}$ by at least ± 250 mV.
2. In the **Tafel region** ($\vert \eta \vert > 50$ mV), the Butler–Volmer equation reduces to a log-linear form.
3. **Anodic branch** ($\eta$ positive, large): the cathodic exponential is negligible, giving:
   $$\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\frac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right)$$
4. **Cathodic branch** ($\eta$ negative, large): the anodic exponential is negligible:
   $$\eta_c = -\beta_c \log\left(\frac{\vert i_c \vert}{i_{corr}}\right)$$
5. Plot $E$ (or $\eta$) vs $\log \vert i \vert$ on a semi-log graph. The anodic and cathodic Tafel lines are **straight lines** in the Tafel region.
6. **Extrapolate both straight-line portions back to $E_{corr}$**. The intersection gives $\log i_{corr}$ — read directly off the x-axis.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All equations you must memorize for Module 2 (KTU 2024 Scheme).**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i_a/i_{corr})$ | Anodic Tafel | $\eta$ in V, $i$ in $A/cm^2$ |
| 2 | $\eta_c = -\beta_c \log(\vert i_c \vert/i_{corr})$ | Cathodic Tafel | same |
| 3 | $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a+\beta_c)}$ | Stern–Geary constant | V (or mV) |
| 4 | $R_p = B / i_{corr}$ | Polarization resistance | $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ |
| 5 | $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ | Corrosion current density from LPR | $A/cm^2$ |
| 6 | $CR_{mpy} = \dfrac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho (g/cm^3)}$ | Corrosion rate (mils per year) | mpy |
| 7 | $CR_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho (g/cm^3)}$ | Corrosion rate | mm/yr |
| 8 | $EW = \dfrac{\text{Atomic Weight}}{n}$ (for pure metals) | Equivalent weight | g/eq |
| 9 | $EW = \dfrac{1}{\sum \frac{n_i f_i}{a_i}}$ (alloys) | Alloy equivalent weight | g/eq |
| 10 | $Z(\omega) = Z' + jZ''$ | EIS complex impedance | $\Omega$ |

**Where:**
- $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$ when using equations (6) and (7).
- $EW$ = equivalent weight (g/eq).
- $\rho$ = metal density ($g/cm^3$).
- 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU stresses this topic — Industrial Relevance:**
> - **Pipeline integrity management** — LPR probes are permanently installed in oil & gas pipelines for real-time monitoring.
> - **Reinforced concrete** — rebar corrosion rate is measured by LPR to predict service life.
> - **Pharma / food industry** — Tafel extrapolation validates inhibitor selection.
> - **Cathodic protection design** — current density requirements are derived from $i_{corr}$ measured by these methods.
> - **Coating evaluation** — EIS separates coating capacitance from charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$ (≈ $R_p$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stern–Geary Equation from Butler–Volmer

**Start with the Butler–Volmer equation:**

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the small-$\eta$ regime.** For $\eta \ll \beta_a$ and $\eta \ll \beta_c$, the arguments of the exponentials are small. Use the Taylor series approximation $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$:

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \left(1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Simplify by cancellation of the two "1"s:**

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a} + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $\eta$:**

$$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \left[ \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Combine the fractions:**

$$\frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} = \frac{\beta_c + \beta_a}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back:**

$$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \cdot \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to solve for $i_{corr}$ in terms of $i/\eta$:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{i}{\eta} \cdot \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the slope $\Delta E / \Delta i = R_p$ (polarization resistance):**

$$R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} \quad \text{(in the linear region near } E_{corr}\text{)}$$

**Step 8 — Final Stern–Geary form:**

$$\boxed{\,i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}, \quad B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Corrosion Current Density → Corrosion Rate (mpy)

**Start with Faraday's Law** — the mass $m$ dissolved by charge $Q$ is:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot EW}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$

**Step 1 — Convert to mass per unit area per unit time (rate form):**

$$\frac{m}{A \cdot t} = \frac{i \cdot EW}{F} \quad \text{where } i = I/A$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $i = i_{corr}$ to get the corrosion mass-loss rate:**

$$\text{Mass loss rate} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{F} \quad [g \cdot cm^{-2} \cdot s^{-1}]$$

**Step 3 — Convert units of $i_{corr}$ from $A/cm^2$ to $\mu A/cm^2$:**

$$i_{corr} [\mu A/cm^2] = i_{corr} [A/cm^2] \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4 — Convert time from seconds to years** (1 year = $3.1536 \times 10^7$ s):

$$\text{Mass loss rate} = \frac{i_{corr} [\mu A/cm^2] \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot EW}{96{,}485} \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 \ \frac{g}{cm^2 \cdot yr}$$

**Step 5 — Numerically simplify the constants:**

$$\frac{3.1536 \times 10^7 \times 10^{-6}}{96{,}485} \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-4}$$

So in **mm/yr** units:

$$CR_{mm/yr} = 3.27 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho}$$

**Step 6 — Convert mm → mils** (1 mil = 0.0254 mm), hence mpy (mils per year):

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{3.27 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0254} \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho} = 0.01288 \cdot \frac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$$

$$\boxed{\,CR_{mpy} \approx \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho(g/cm^3)}\,}$$

The factor **0.129** is the canonical KTU/ASM constant for mpy conversion.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Tafel Slopes from Charge-Transfer Kinetics

The Butler–Volmer equation in its most fundamental form is:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{\alpha_c F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

In the high-$\eta$ anodic Tafel region, the cathodic term is negligible:

$$i_a = i_0 \exp\left(\frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

Taking $\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$\log i_a = \log i_0 + \frac{\alpha_a F \eta}{2.303 \, RT}$$

Comparing to the Tafel empirical form $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i_a / i_{corr})$:

$$\beta_a = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a F}$$

At $T = 298$ K (25 °C):

$$\beta_a = \frac{0.0592}{\alpha_a} \ V \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{59.2}{\alpha_a} \ mV/decade$$

Similarly, $\beta_c = 0.0592 / \alpha_c$ V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning of $\alpha$:** the **charge-transfer coefficient** represents the fraction of the applied potential that promotes the anodic (or cathodic) reaction. Typical values: $\alpha_a, \alpha_c \in [0.3, 0.7]$, with $\alpha_a + \alpha_c \approx 1$ for a single elementary step.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Numerical-Type)

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU question):**
For iron corroding in aerated 0.5 M NaCl at 25 °C, the following data were obtained by linear polarization near $E_{corr}$:
- $\Delta E = \pm 10 \ mV$ produces $\Delta i = \pm 20 \ \mu A/cm^2$
- Tafel slopes (from a separate potentiodynamic scan): $\beta_a = 60 \ mV/dec$, $\beta_c = 120 \ mV/dec$
- Iron equivalent weight $EW = 27.92 \ g/eq$, density $\rho = 7.87 \ g/cm^3$

**Calculate (a) $R_p$, (b) $i_{corr}$ in $\mu A/cm^2$, and (c) the corrosion rate in mpy and mm/yr.**

---

**Solution (a) — Polarization resistance:**

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} = \frac{10 \ mV}{20 \ \mu A/cm^2} = \frac{0.010 \ V}{20 \times 10^{-6} \ A/cm^2} = 500 \ \Omega \cdot cm^2$$

**Solution (b) — Stern–Geary constant $B$:**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{(0.060)(0.120)}{2.303(0.060 + 0.120)}$$

$$B = \frac{0.00720}{2.303 \times 0.180} = \frac{0.00720}{0.4145} = 0.01737 \ V$$

**Convert to mV for cleanliness:** $B = 17.37 \ mV$.

**Corrosion current density:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.01737 \ V}{500 \ \Omega \cdot cm^2} = 3.474 \times 10^{-5} \ A/cm^2$$

$$\boxed{\,i_{corr} = 34.74 \ \mu A/cm^2\,}$$

**Solution (c) — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW}{\rho} = \frac{0.129 \times 34.74 \times 27.92}{7.87}$$

Numerator: $0.129 \times 34.74 = 4.4815$; $4.4815 \times 27.92 = 125.13$

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{125.13}{7.87} = 15.90 \ mpy$$

**Convert to mm/yr** (1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr):

$$CR_{mm/yr} = 15.90 \times 0.0254 = 0.404 \ mm/yr$$

$$\boxed{\,CR \approx 15.9 \ mpy \approx 0.404 \ mm/yr\,}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Electrode Electrochemical Cell (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Potentiostat["POTENTIOSTAT (Electronic Unit)"]
        P1[Control Amplifier]
        P2[Current-to-Voltage Converter]
        P3[Signal Generator / Frequency Response Analyzer]
    end

    subgraph Cell["Electrochemical Cell (Electrolyte)"]
        WE[Working Electrode: Sample Metal Under Test]
        CE[Counter Electrode: Pt or Graphite]
        RE[Reference Electrode: SCE or Ag-AgCl]
    end

    subgraph Output["Data Acquisition and Analysis"]
        D1[Polarization Curve E vs log i]
        D2[Linear Region Slope Rp]
        D3[Nyquist Plot Z'' vs Z']
        D4[Computed icorr and CR]
    end

    P1 -- measures potential between WE and RE --> RE
    P1 -- injects current --> WE
    P2 -- measures current through --> CE
    P3 -- drives the experiment --> P1

    WE --> D1
    RE --> D1
    CE --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
```

### 4.2 Typical Polarization Curve (E vs log i) — Schematic of Tafel Extrapolation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[E axis: E_corr is at the center]
    A1[Anodic Tafel Line: slope = beta_a]
    A2[Cathodic Tafel Line: slope = beta_c]
    A3[Linear Region: width about 10 mV on each side of E_corr]
    A4[Intersection of extrapolated lines gives log i_corr]

    A0 --- A1
    A0 --- A2
    A0 --- A3
    A0 --- A4

    B0["Axes: x = log i (A/cm2), y = E (V vs RE)"]
    B1["Anodic branch (right side) is straight line with slope beta_a in V per decade"]
    B2["Cathodic branch (left side) is straight line with slope beta_c in V per decade"]
    B3["Near E_corr the curve is approximately linear: this is the LPR region of slope Rp"]
    B4["Extend both Tafel lines backward; their crossing on the x-axis gives i_corr"]

    B0 ~~~ B1
    B1 ~~~ B2
    B2 ~~~ B3
    B3 ~~~ B4
```

### 4.3 Flow Chart — Decision Tree for Selecting the Right Electrochemical Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What information do you need?}
    Q1 -->|Instant corrosion rate| Q2{Is the system steady?}
    Q1 -->|Mechanism and double-layer info| Q3[EIS Method]
    Q1 -->|Passivation breakdown pitting tendency| Q4[Potentiodynamic scan]

    Q2 -->|Yes, near equilibrium| A1[LPR Stern-Geary Method]
    Q2 -->|Far from equilibrium or fast scan needed| A2[Tafel Extrapolation]

    A1 --> R1[Apply delta E = 10 to 20 mV around E_corr]
    A1 --> R2[Measure slope delta E by delta i equals Rp]
    A1 --> R3[Compute B from Tafel slopes if available, else assume 26 or 52 mV]
    A1 --> R4[icorr = B by Rp, then CR by Faraday Law]

    A2 --> S1[Polarize from E_corr minus 250 mV to E_corr plus 250 mV at slow scan]
    A2 --> S2[Plot E versus log absolute i]
    A2 --> S3[Identify linear Tafel regions on both branches]
    A2 --> S4[Extrapolate to E_corr, read i_corr at the intersection]

    Q3 --> T1[Apply small amplitude AC signal 5 to 10 mV]
    Q3 --> T2[Sweep frequency 100 kHz down to 0.01 Hz]
    Q3 --> T3[Measure complex impedance Z over frequency]
    Q3 --> T4[Fit to equivalent circuit: Rs, CPEdl, Rct, W etc]
```

### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit for a Corroding Metal (Randles Circuit — used in EIS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rs[Rs: Solution Resistance]
    Rct[Rct: Charge Transfer Resistance equals Rp]
    Cdl[Cdl: Double Layer Capacitance]
    Zw[Zw: Warburg Impedance for diffusion control]

    A((Terminal A)) --- Rs --- B((Node B))
    B --- Rct --- C((Terminal C))
    B --- Cdl --- C
    C --- Zw --- D((Terminal D))
```

**Interpretation of components:**
- $R_s$ — electrolyte resistance between RE and WE surface.
- $C_{dl}$ — capacitance of the electrical double layer at the metal/solution interface.
- $R_{ct}$ — resistance to charge transfer across the interface; **this is what we equate to $R_p$**.
- $Z_w$ — Warburg impedance; present only if the corrosion is *diffusion-controlled* (e.g., oxygen reduction in stagnant electrolyte).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define polarization resistance $R_p$ and write the Stern–Geary equation relating it to the corrosion current density.*

**Model Answer:**
**Polarization resistance $R_p$** is defined as the slope of the polarization curve (potential vs current density) at the corrosion potential, i.e.,

$$R_p = \left( \frac{dE}{di} \right)_{E = E_{corr}}$$

It represents the resistance of the metal–electrolyte interface to small perturbations around the open-circuit (corrosion) potential. The **Stern–Geary equation** relates $R_p$ to the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$:

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}, \quad B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

where $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ are the anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes and $B$ is the Stern–Geary constant (V). **(3 marks — full credit for both $R_p$ definition and Stern–Geary form.)**

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
*State Faraday's law as applied to corrosion rate determination and write the working formula to express corrosion rate in **mpy** from $i_{corr}$.*

**Model Answer:**
**Faraday's law** states that the mass $m$ of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ passed:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$$

For a corroding system, the corrosion current $i_{corr}$ represents a continuous Faraday process. The corrosion rate in **mils per year (mpy)** is:

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times EW}{\rho(g/cm^3)}$$

where $EW$ is the equivalent weight and $\rho$ is the metal density. **(3 marks — full credit for both the symbolic form and the numerical conversion.)**

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the **Stern–Geary equation** starting from the Butler–Volmer equation. Clearly state the assumptions and the physical meaning of the Stern–Geary constant $B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For mild steel in an aerated 3.5% NaCl solution, the following data were measured at 25 °C by the LPR technique:
- Applied perturbation: $\Delta E = \pm 12 \ mV$
- Resulting current density: $\Delta i = \pm 18 \ \mu A/cm^2$
- Tafel slopes: $\beta_a = 70 \ mV/dec$, $\beta_c = 150 \ mV/dec$
- Density of mild steel: $7.85 \ g/cm^3$, Equivalent weight: $27.92 \ g/eq$

Calculate (i) the polarization resistance $R_p$, (ii) the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$, and (iii) the corrosion rate in **both mpy and mm/yr**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Stern–Geary Equation:**

*Step 1 — Write the Butler–Volmer equation (1 mark):*

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \exp\left(-\frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

*Step 2 — Apply small-$\eta$ (Taylor) approximation (2 marks):* For $\eta \to 0$, $\exp(x) \approx 1 + x$, hence:

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \left(1 + \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_a}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{2.303\,\eta}{\beta_c}\right) \right]$$

*Step 3 — Simplify (1 mark):*

$$i = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\beta_a} + \frac{1}{\beta_c} \right] = i_{corr} \cdot 2.303\,\eta \cdot \frac{\beta_a + \beta_c}{\beta_a \beta_c}$$

*Step 4 — Define polarization resistance as $R_p = \eta / i$ (1 mark):*

$$R_p = \frac{\eta}{i} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

*Step 5 — Rearrange to the canonical Stern–Geary form (1 mark):*

$$\boxed{\,i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}, \quad B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}\,}$$

*Step 6 — Physical meaning of $B$ (1 mark):* $B$ has units of voltage and is *experimentally constant* for a given metal–environment system. It depends on the kinetics (Tafel slopes) of the anodic dissolution and cathodic reduction reactions. When Tafel slopes are not measured, $B$ is often assumed as 26 mV (active corrosion) or 52 mV (passive systems).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

*Step 1 — Polarization resistance (2 marks):*

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i} = \frac{12 \ mV}{18 \ \mu A/cm^2} = \frac{0.012}{18 \times 10^{-6}} = 666.67 \ \Omega \cdot cm^2$$

*Step 2 — Stern–Geary constant $B$ (2 marks):*

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{(0.070)(0.150)}{2.303(0.070 + 0.150)} = \frac{0.01050}{2.303 \times 0.220}$$

$$B = \frac{0.01050}{0.5067} = 0.02073 \ V = 20.73 \ mV$$

*Step 3 — Corrosion current density (1 mark):*

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.02073}{666.67} = 3.109 \times 10^{-5} \ A/cm^2 = 31.09 \ \mu A/cm^2$$

*Step 4 — Corrosion rate in mpy (1 mark):*

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \times 31.09 \times 27.92}{7.85} = \frac{111.94}{7.85} = 14.26 \ mpy$$

*Step 5 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr (1 mark):*

$$CR_{mm/yr} = 14.26 \times 0.0254 = 0.362 \ mm/yr$$

**Final Answer:** $R_p = 666.7 \ \Omega \cdot cm^2$, $i_{corr} = 31.09 \ \mu A/cm^2$, $CR = 14.26 \ mpy = 0.362 \ mm/yr$.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Set]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat **potential vs log current density diagram**, explain the **Tafel extrapolation method** for determining the corrosion current density. State the limitations of the method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A magnesium alloy (AZ91, $EW = 12.15 \ g/eq$, $\rho = 1.74 \ g/cm^3$) shows a Tafel plot with the following extrapolated parameters:
- Anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a = 50 \ mV/dec$
- Cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c = 130 \ mV/dec$
- $i_{corr}$ read at the intersection of Tafel lines = $80 \ \mu A/cm^2$

Calculate the corrosion rate in **mm/yr**. Comment on the suitability of the alloy for marine service. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Tafel Extrapolation Method:**

*Step 1 — Schematic (3 marks):*

The diagram has:
- x-axis: $\log i$ (A/cm²) or $\log \vert i \vert$
- y-axis: $E$ (V vs reference electrode)
- $E_{corr}$ marked on the y-axis where the net current is zero.
- A bell-shaped polarization curve with two straight Tafel regions on the anodic and cathodic sides.
- Slopes $\beta_a$ (anodic) and $\beta_c$ (cathodic) labelled.
- Extrapolated straight lines meeting at $E_{corr}$ and giving $\log i_{corr}$ on the x-axis.

*Step 2 — Procedure (2 marks):*
1. Polish and mount the working electrode.
2. Immerse in the electrolyte and wait for a stable $E_{corr}$ (open-circuit potential).
3. Apply a slow potentiodynamic scan from $E_{corr} - 250$ mV to $E_{corr} + 250$ mV (or vice versa), at a low scan rate (~0.5 mV/s).
4. Plot $E$ vs $\log \vert i \vert$ (semi-log plot).
5. Identify the linear Tafel regions (typically $\vert \eta \vert > 50$ mV).
6. Extrapolate the two straight lines back to $E_{corr}$ — the corresponding x-value is $\log i_{corr}$.

*Step 3 — Underlying Tafel equations (1 mark):*

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log\left(\frac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right) \quad ; \quad \eta_c = -\beta_c \log\left(\frac{\vert i_c \vert}{i_{corr}}\right)$$

*Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):*
- Requires large polarizations that may **alter the surface** (oxide formation, pitting, inhibitor breakdown).
- Slow scan may be needed; cannot be used for **rapid online monitoring**.
- Tafel region may be **obscured** by diffusion-limited cathodic current.
- Cannot be applied to systems with **strong passivation** (no straight-line region).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical + Interpretation:**

*Step 1 — Check the values (no Stern–Geary needed, $i_{corr}$ given directly).*

*Step 2 — Corrosion rate in mpy (2 marks):*

$$CR_{mpy} = \frac{0.129 \times 80 \times 12.15}{1.74} = \frac{125.39}{1.74} = 72.06 \ mpy$$

*Step 3 — Convert to mm/yr (1 mark):*

$$CR_{mm/yr} = 72.06 \times 0.0254 = 1.83 \ mm/yr$$

*Step 4 — Marine-service comment (4 marks):*

A corrosion rate of **~72 mpy (~1.83 mm/yr)** is **very high** for a structural alloy in marine service. Industry-acceptable limits for marine applications are typically:
- < 5 mpy (~0.13 mm/yr) → excellent
- 5–20 mpy → acceptable with monitoring
- 20–50 mpy → marginal, requires protection
- \> 50 mpy → **unsuitable without cathodic protection or coatings**

Therefore, **AZ91 in its bare, unprotected form is unsuitable for long-term marine immersion**. Recommended mitigation:
- Apply **cathodic protection** (zinc or aluminum sacrificial anodes).
- Use **epoxy or polyurethane coatings**.
- Consider **alloying refinement** (lower Fe, Ni, Cu impurities) or switch to more noble alloys.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit-mismatch error:** Using $i_{corr}$ in $A/cm^2$ in the 0.129 formula instead of $\mu A/cm^2$ → answer off by $10^6$. Always convert and explicitly state units.
> 2. **Forgetting density $\rho$:** Many students omit $\rho$ in the denominator, giving values 8–10× too high.
> 3. **Confusing $R_p$ with $R_{ct}$:** Strictly, $R_p$ is the *linear* slope at $E_{corr}$, while $R_{ct}$ is the *true* charge-transfer resistance (from EIS). They are equal **only when $Z_w = 0$** (no diffusion control).
> 4. **Assuming $B = 26$ mV blindly:** Without justification (active vs passive) this is considered a *partial* answer.
> 5. **Tafel extrapolation from semi-log plot:** Forgetting to plot $\log \vert i \vert$ (not $\log i$) on the cathodic side — examiners check this.
> 6. **EW for alloys:** For alloys like brass, stainless steel — students must use the *weighted* equivalent weight formula, not the atomic weight of the base metal.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 — High-Density Revision Checklist (Electrochemical Corrosion Rate Determination)**

- [ ] All three techniques (Tafel, LPR/Stern–Geary, EIS) ultimately yield the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$**.
- [ ] **Butler–Volmer equation** is the *master* equation from which both Tafel and Stern–Geary are derived.
- [ ] **Stern–Geary equation** — memorize as: $i_{corr} = B/R_p$, where $B$ has units of **V or mV**.
- [ ] **Stern–Geary constant $B$** = $\beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$.
- [ ] Default values (only if Tafel slopes unknown): **B = 26 mV (active), 52 mV (passive)**.
- [ ] **LPR uses small perturbation** $\Delta E = \pm 10$ to $\pm 20$ mV; nearly non-destructive.
- [ ] **Tafel extrapolation** uses large polarizations $\vert \eta \vert > 50$ mV; can alter the surface.
- [ ] **CR in mpy** formula: $0.129 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW / \rho$.
- [ ] **CR in mm/yr** formula: $0.00327 \cdot i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \cdot EW / \rho$.
- [ ] **1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr** — memorise this conversion.
- [ ] **EW of a pure metal** = atomic weight / valence $n$.
- [ ] **EW of an alloy** = $1 / \sum (n_i f_i / a_i)$ where $f_i$ is mass fraction and $a_i$ is atomic weight.
- [ ] **EIS** applies a small AC signal (5–10 mV) over a wide frequency range (100 kHz → 10 mHz) and fits to a **Randles equivalent circuit**.
- [ ] In Randles circuit: $R_s$ = solution resistance, $C_{dl}$ = double-layer capacitance, $R_{ct}$ = charge-transfer resistance ($\approx R_p$ in absence of Warburg), $Z_w$ = Warburg (diffusion).
- [ ] **Nyquist plot** of $Z''$ vs $Z'$ — a single semicircle indicates activation-controlled corrosion; a 45° tail indicates diffusion control.
- [ ] Three-electrode setup: **WE (sample), RE (SCE/Ag-AgCl/SHE), CE (Pt or graphite)**.
- [ ] Faradaic conversion of $i_{corr}$ to mass-loss rate relies on **Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol**.
- [ ] **Tafel slope at 25 °C** is $\beta = 0.0592 / \alpha$ V per decade; for $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta \approx 120$ mV/dec.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polarisation Linearization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Polarisation Linearization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarisation Linearization** is the mathematical simplification of the non-linear Butler–Volmer equation of electrochemical kinetics into tractable linear or semi-logarithmic forms, valid over specific ranges of overpotential (η), in order to extract the corrosion current density ($i_{corr}$), Tafel slopes ($\beta_a$, $\beta_c$), and polarisation resistance ($R_p$) from experimentally measured polarisation data.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT866 – Corrosion Control)**, polarisation linearization underpins the two most industrially important DC electrochemical techniques for corrosion rate monitoring:

1. **Tafel Extrapolation Method** (valid at large $|\eta|$ > 50 mV)
2. **Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR) Method** (valid at small $|\eta|$ < 10–20 mV)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hill and the Two Surveyors"**
>
> Imagine the activation-energy barrier of a corroding metal as a steep, curved hill. A **blindfolded surveyor** walking up this hill (the Butler–Volmer equation) cannot easily measure the hill's height from a few data points because the slope keeps changing.
>
> - One surveyor is sent **far from the peak** (large $\eta$) and uses a **ruler and a log-table** — he plots $\eta$ versus $\log i$ and gets a straight line. This is the **Tafel surveyor**.
> - The other surveyor stands **right at the peak** (very small $\eta$, near $E_{corr}$) and uses a **simple level** — the curved hill looks essentially flat, so $\eta$ versus $i$ is a straight line. This is the **LPR surveyor**.
>
> Both surveyors report back with the "hill's height" (i.e., $i_{corr}$), but each using a different linear approximation valid in their own region.

## 1.3 The Master Equation — Butler–Volmer

The exact kinetic current–overpotential relationship for a single corroding electrode is:

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

where the key symbols and their standard units are tabulated below. **Standard values to remember:**

- **F** = **96,485 C mol⁻¹** (Faraday's constant)
- **R** = **8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹** (Universal gas constant)
- **T** = **298 K** (taken as 25 °C in most KTU numericals)
- $\alpha + \beta \approx 1$ for a single-electron transfer step

The problem is that this equation is **transcendental** (cannot be solved algebraically for $i_{corr}$). Linearization is the engineering fix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Overpotential Regimes

The Butler–Volmer equation behaves very differently in three distinct regions of $\eta$:

| Regime | Magnitude of η | Dominant Term | Linearized Form | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Small overpotential** | $\vert \eta \vert \le 10\text{–}20$ mV | Both anodic & cathodic terms | $\eta = \dfrac{RT}{i_{corr}(\alpha+\beta)F}\, i$ | LPR / $R_p$ method |
| **Intermediate overpotential** | 20 mV $< \vert \eta \vert <$ 50 mV | Full Butler–Volmer | $i = i_{corr}\!\left[e^{a} - e^{-b}\right]$ | Curve-fitting only |
| **Large overpotential** | $\vert \eta \vert \ge 50\text{–}100$ mV | One exponential dominates | $\eta = \beta_a \log\!\dfrac{i}{i_{corr}}$ (anodic) | Tafel extrapolation |

## 2.2 Why Linearization Works (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Exponential behaviour dominates**: The two exponential terms in the Butler–Volmer equation grow/decay at vastly different rates when $\eta$ is large; the smaller one can be safely dropped.
2. **Taylor-series expansion at $E_{corr}$**: Near $E_{corr}$, $\eta \rightarrow 0$, so $e^{x} \approx 1 + x$, which is linear in $\eta$.
3. **Semi-log conversion**: A logarithm converts the surviving exponential into a straight line — this is the genius of the Tafel plot.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table — Print This for the Exam Hall**

| # | Equation (use $\vert$ for "absolute value" in notes, not in the table) | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Butler–Volmer: $i = i_{corr}\!\left[e^{\alpha F\eta / RT} - e^{-\beta F \eta / RT}\right]$ | $i$ | A m⁻² or A cm⁻² |
| 2 | Tafel (anodic): $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\dfrac{i}{i_{corr}}$ | $\eta_a$ | V (often mV) |
| 3 | Tafel (cathodic): $\eta_c = -\beta_c \log_{10}\!\dfrac{i}{i_{corr}}$ | $\eta_c$ | V (often mV) |
| 4 | Anodic Tafel slope: $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$ | $\beta_a$ | V decade⁻¹ |
| 5 | Cathodic Tafel slope: $\beta_c = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\beta F}$ | $\beta_c$ | V decade⁻¹ |
| 6 | Polarisation resistance: $R_p = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}\bigg\vert_{\eta \to 0}$ | $R_p$ | $\Omega$ cm² |
| 7 | **Stern–Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}$ | $i_{corr}$ | A cm⁻² |
| 8 | Stern–Geary constant: $B = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | $B$ | V |
| 9 | Corrosion rate (mpy): $CR = 0.13 \times i_{corr}\times \dfrac{EW}{\rho}$ | $CR$ | mpy |
| 10 | Corrosion rate (mm/yr): $CR_{mm/y} = 0.00327 \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | $CR$ | mm yr⁻¹ |

> *(EW = equivalent weight in g/eq; $\rho$ = density in g/cm³; $i_{corr}$ in µA/cm² for mpy formula)*

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Online corrosion monitoring** in oil & gas pipelines (LPR probes)
- **Inhibitor efficiency screening** in cooling-water circuits
- **Coating evaluation** in marine environments
- **Lifetime prediction** of reinforced concrete structures
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Tafel Approximation (Large Anodic Overpotential)

**Starting point (Butler–Volmer):**

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the two terms.**
The first exponential grows with $\eta$ (anodic); the second decays (cathodic). When $\eta$ is large and positive (e.g., 100 mV anodic), the **cathodic exponential becomes negligibly small** (less than 1 % of the anodic term).

**Step 2 — Drop the cathodic exponential.**

$$i \;\approx\; i_{corr} \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right) = \frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{\alpha F} \ln\!\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to base-10 logarithm using** $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$, **and define the anodic Tafel slope** $\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{\alpha F}$.

$$\boxed{\;\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{i}{i_{corr}}\right)\;}$$

**Step 6 — Rewrite in the classic "Tafel form"** $\eta = a + b \log i$:

$$\eta_a = -\beta_a \log_{10} i_{corr} + \beta_a \log_{10} i$$

A plot of $\eta_a$ vs $\log_{10} i$ is a **straight line of slope $\beta_a$**, and extrapolating back to $\eta = 0$ gives $\log_{10} i_{corr}$, hence $i_{corr}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Linear Polarization Resistance (Small Overpotential)

**Starting point (Butler–Volmer):**

$$i = i_{corr} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{\beta F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Taylor series expansion** $e^{x} = 1 + x + \dfrac{x^2}{2!} + \dots$ **for small x.** For $\vert \eta \vert$ up to ~10 mV, terms of order $\eta^2$ and higher are negligibly small (less than 0.2 %).

**Step 2 — Substitute the truncated expansion.**

$$i \approx i_{corr} \left[ \left(1 + \frac{\alpha F \eta}{RT}\right) - \left(1 - \frac{\beta F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the "1"s and combine the $\eta$ terms.**

$$i \approx i_{corr} \cdot \frac{(\alpha + \beta) F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the "Ohm's-law form"** $\eta = (R_p) \cdot i$:

$$\eta = \frac{RT}{i_{corr} (\alpha + \beta) F}\cdot i$$

**Step 5 — Define the Polarisation Resistance** $R_p$ **(the slope of the linear E–i plot at** $\eta \to 0$):

$$R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}\bigg\vert_{\eta \to 0} = \frac{RT}{i_{corr}(\alpha+\beta)F}$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of Tafel slopes** $\beta_a = 2.303RT/\alpha F$ and $\beta_c = 2.303RT/\beta F$:

After algebra (multiply numerator and denominator of $R_p$ by $2.303$), we obtain the **Stern–Geary constant**:

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)}$$

and the celebrated **Stern–Geary equation**:

$$\boxed{\;i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical meaning of $R_p$:** $R_p$ is the *electrode's resistance to being polarized away from $E_{corr}$*. A high $R_p$ ⇒ slow corrosion (small $i_{corr}$); a low $R_p$ ⇒ aggressive corrosion. This inverse relationship is the very heart of the LPR technique.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem (Module 2, typical 7-mark):**
A corroding steel sample in 1 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C shows an anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a = 0.060$ V/dec and a cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c = 0.120$ V/dec. The polarisation resistance measured by an LPR probe is **870 Ω·cm²**. Calculate the corrosion current density and the corrosion rate in mpy. (Given: EW of Fe = 27.92 g/eq, $\rho_{Fe}$ = 7.87 g/cm³.)

**Step 1 — Stern–Geary constant $B$:**

$$B = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \frac{0.060 \times 0.120}{2.303 \times 0.180} = \frac{0.0072}{0.41454} = 0.01737 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Corrosion current density $i_{corr}$:**

$$i_{corr} = \frac{B}{R_p} = \frac{0.01737}{870} = 1.996 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A/cm}^2 \approx 19.96\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Corrosion rate in mpy:**

$$CR = 0.13 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times \frac{EW}{\rho} = 0.13 \times 19.96 \times \frac{27.92}{7.87}$$

$$CR = 0.13 \times 19.96 \times 3.548 = 9.20 \text{ mpy}$$

**Answer:** $i_{corr} \approx 20\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$; **CR ≈ 9.2 mpy**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Linearization

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Overpotential Value] --> small{Small eta less than 10 mV}
    start --> inter{Intermediate 10 to 50 mV}
    start --> large{Large eta greater than 50 mV}
    small --> lin[Linear Polarization Rp]
    lin --> sg[Stern Geary Equation]
    sg --> icorr1[Corrosion Current Density]
    inter --> bv[Full Butler Volmer Fit]
    bv --> num1[Numerical Iteration Needed]
    large --> taf[Tafel Extrapolation]
    taf --> slope[Slope equals ba or bc]
    slope --> icorr2[Extrapolate to Ecorr gives log icorr]
    icorr1 --> cr1[Corrosion Rate]
    icorr2 --> cr2[Corrosion Rate]
    num1 --> cr3[Corrosion Rate]
```

## 4.2 Tafel Extrapolation Schematic (E vs. log i)

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisx[X axis log current density i]
    axisy[Y axis Potential E in V]
    ecor[Ecorr point on potential axis]
    ba[Anodic Tafel Line Slope ba V per decade]
    bc[Cathodic Tafel Line Slope bc V per decade]
    ba --> int[Intersection Point]
    bc --> int
    int --> ic[log icorr at Ecorr]
    axisy --> ecor
    ecor --> ic
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LPR Instrument

```mermaid
graph TD
    we[Working Electrode Metal Sample] --> pot[Potentiostat applies small delta E]
    ce[Counter Electrode Platinum] --> pot
    re[Reference Electrode SCE] --> pot
    pot --> cur[Measures Resulting Current delta i]
    cur --> div[Compute Rp equals delta E by delta i]
    div --> bk[Stern Geary Constant B from ba and bc]
    bk --> calc[Compute icorr equals B by Rp]
    calc --> rate[Corrosion Rate in mpy or mm per year]
    rate --> out[Display on Monitor or Data Logger]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU:** The E–log i plot with two straight Tafel lines intersecting at $E_{corr}$ is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in either Part A or Part B. Be prepared to label axes, indicate slopes, and show the extrapolation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
