Therefore its thickness $d_{ox}$ is inversely proportional to the measured capacitance per unit area $C/A$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation For an ideal parallel-plate capacitor, the capacitance $C$ is: $$ C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \,\varepsilon_0 \, A}{d_{ox}} $$ Solving for the thickness $d_{ox}$: $$ d_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \,\varepsilon_0 \, A}{C} $$ Rewriting in terms of capacitance per unit area, $C_d = C/A$: $$ d_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \,\varepsilon_0}{C_d} $$ **Example numerical evaluation** (KTU standard problem): Given for an Fe specimen passivated in 0.5 M H₂SO₄: - $\varepsilon_r = 12$ (for Fe₂O₃) - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m - $C_d = 2.0 \times 10^{-2}$ F/m² (measured at 1 kHz) $$ d_{ox} = \dfrac{12 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12})}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} $$ $$ d_{ox} = \dfrac{1.0625 \times 10^{-10}}{2.0 \times 10^{-2}} $$ $$ d_{ox} = 5.31 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} \;=\; \boxed{5.31\ \text{nm}} $$ > [!NOTE] > This thickness (≈ 5 nm) is consistent with experimental TEM/XPS measurements of passive Fe₂O₃ films — confirming the validity of the parallel-plate model. ## 3.2 Derivation — Nernstian pH Dependence of the Flade Potential The equilibrium at the passive film surface can be written as: $$ \text{M} + \tfrac{n}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{M}_2\text{O}_n + n\,\text{H}^+ + n\,e^- $$ Applying the Nernst equation at 25 °C: $$ E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\log\dfrac{[\text{H}^+]^n}{1} $$ Because $[\text{H}^+] = 10^{-\text{pH}}$: $$ E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\,\log\,(10^{-n\,\text{pH}}) $$ $$ E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.0591}{n}\,(-n\,\text{pH}) $$ $$ \boxed{E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.0591\,\text{pH}} $$ **Interpretation:** The Flade potential shifts in the **active (negative) direction** by ~59 mV per unit increase in pH. This is why **raising pH promotes passivation** of Fe, Cr, Ni, etc. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Passivation Technique | Situation | Recommended Control | Justification | |---|---|---| | Carbon-steel tank with 93 % H₂SO₄ (hot) | **Anodic Protection** | Alloying is too expensive; AP reduces CR from > 5 mm/yr to ≈ 0.05 mm/yr | | Closed cooling-water loop (mild steel) | **Nitrite or molybdate inhibitor** | Dissolved O₂ keeps E just above $E_F$; inhibitor buffers the passive film | | Marine stainless-steel pump (Cl⁻ present) | **Alloying (use 316L, 6 % Mo)** | Pure chemical passivation fails — Cl⁻ pits the film; high Cr/Mo stabilises it | | Titanium heat-exchanger (Cl⁻-free) | **Natural passivation by O₂** | TiO₂ forms spontaneously; no AP needed | | Reinforcing steel in concrete | **High pH (passive due to Ca(OH)₂)** | Concrete pore water has pH ≈ 12.5, well above the Fe passivation range | # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Active → Passive Transition This Mermaid diagram maps the **decision tree** that a metal/electrolyte system follows when potential is scanned in the anodic direction. It is the **single most examinable diagram** of Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Metal immersed in electrolyte]) --> ActStart[Active Region: M → M^n+ + n e−] ActStart --> ActGrow{Current rises with potential
Tafel region slope b_a} ActGrow --> Critical[Reaches Critical Current Density i_c
at Primary Passivation Potential E_pp] Critical --> Trans[Active to Passive Transition
Nucleation of oxide film] Trans --> Passive[Passive Region
Current drops to i_p
≈ 10−6 A/cm²] Passive --> PCheck{Potential still
below transpassive?} PCheck -- Yes --> Stable[Stable Passivity
Film self-heals if damaged] PCheck -- No --> Transpass[Transpassive Region
O2 evolution OR pitting] Stable --> Hold{Applied potential
removed?} Hold -- Yes --> Decay{Flade potential E_F
crossed on decay?} Decay -- No --> Stable Decay -- Yes --> ActStart Transpass --> Damage[Pitting / Oxygen Evolution
Loss of protection] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Four Industrial Passivity-Control Routes ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RouteA["Route 1: Anodic Protection"] A1[DC Rectifier] --> A2[Reference Electrode
e.g. Ag/AgCl] A2 --> A3[Counter Electrode
Pt or SS] A3 --> A4[Protected Metal
at E_passive] end subgraph RouteB["Route 2: Chemical Inhibitors"] B1[Oxidizing Anion] --> B2[Chromate / Nitrite
Molybdate / Tungstate] B2 --> B3[Raises E above E_pp] B3 --> B4[Forms or repairs
passive oxide] end subgraph RouteC["Route 3: Alloying"] C1[Base Metal Fe] --> C2[Add Cr ≥ 12 percent] C2 --> C3[Add Ni for austenite] C3 --> C4[Add Mo for Cl pit resistance] end subgraph RouteD["Route 4: Environmental Control"] D1[Dissolved O2] --> D2[Raises mixed potential] D2 --> D3[pH Adjustment Alkaline] D3 --> D4[Lowers E_F via Nernst] end RouteA --> Outcome[Stable Passive Film
i_corr falls by 10^4 to 10^6] RouteB --> Outcome RouteC --> Outcome RouteD --> Outcome ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Three-Stage Anodic Polarization Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["A: Active region
Tafel slope b_a
i rises with E"] --> P2["B: Critical point
i = i_c at E = E_pp
Film nucleates"] P2 --> P3["C: Passive plateau
i = i_p ≈ 10−6 A/cm²
constant over ΔE"] P3 --> P4["D: Transpassive
O2 evolution
OR pitting in Cl−"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's tip:** Always label **(i)** the four regions, **(ii)** the four characteristic points ($i_c$, $i_p$, $E_{pp}$, $E_{pit}$), and **(iii)** the potential axis in SHE. Any one missing ⇒ **1 mark lost**. # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define passivity. List the four conditions a metal–environment system must satisfy to exhibit passivity. **Model Answer:** > **Passivity** is the phenomenon in which a thermodynamically reactive metal exhibits a dramatically reduced corrosion rate because of a thin, adherent, protective oxide (or chemisorbed-oxygen) film on its surface. > > **Four conditions:** > 1. Highly negative free energy of oxide formation ($\Delta G_{f}^{\circ} \ll 0$). > 2. Open-circuit (or applied) potential lying **above** the Flade potential $E_F$ but below the transpassive potential. > 3. The film must be adherent, non-porous, and **self-healing**. > 4. Film must be chemically stable (insoluble) in the surrounding electrolyte (e.g., resistant to Cl⁻ breakdown). --- ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between the **oxide-film theory** and the **adsorption theory** of passivity. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Oxide-Film Theory (Faraday) | Adsorption Theory (Uhlig) | |---|---|---| | Nature of film | 3-D solid compound, several nm thick | Monolayer of chemisorbed O, ≈ 1 atom thick | | Detectability | Visible by XPS, TEM, ellipsometry | Detected only by surface-sensitive techniques | | Mechanical separation | Possible | Not possible | | Applicable metals | Al, Cr, Ti, stainless steel | Fe in HNO₃, Ni in alkali | | Modern acceptance | Long-term barrier film | Initial nucleation step | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** *Explain the construction, working and application of an **anodic protection** system with a neat sketch. Show how the protective potential window is identified from the anodic polarization curve.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A carbon-steel storage tank containing 93 % H₂SO₄ at 50 °C is to be protected by anodic protection. The following data are available: tank surface area = **40 m²**, critical current density $i_c$ = **2.5 × 10⁻³ A/cm²**, passive current density $i_p$ = **8 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²**, Flade potential $E_F$ = **−0.30 V (SHE)**, applied passive potential $E_{app}$ = **+0.50 V (SHE)**, cell resistance $R_{cell}$ = **1.2 Ω**. Compute (i) the initial DC current required to establish passivity, (ii) the steady-state DC current to maintain passivity, and (iii) the rectifier voltage rating. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** Anodic protection (AP) is an electrochemical technique in which the metal to be protected is connected to the **positive terminal** of a DC rectifier so that its potential is forced into the **passive region** of its anodic polarization curve. *Construction (3 components):* - **DC rectifier** (controlled-voltage, 0–10 V, 0–50 A typical). - **Reference electrode** (Ag/AgCl, Hg/HgSO₄ or Pt/air) inserted through the tank wall to sense the protected metal's potential. - **Cathode** (platinum, stainless steel or silicon-iron) immersed in the electrolyte near the tank wall. - A **potentiostat** or simple feedback circuit compares $E_{measured}$ with the set-point and adjusts the rectifier output. *Working:* The reference electrode feeds back the metal's potential. The control loop forces the metal to sit at $E_{app}$, typically **+100 to +300 mV above the Flade potential $E_F$** but **below** the pitting/oxygen-evolution potential. During start-up a high current ($i_c \times A$) flows briefly to build the oxide; once passivity is established the current collapses to $i_p \times A$. *Identification of protective window:* From the anodic polarization curve the protective window is the segment of the passive plateau between $E_{pp}$ (≈ Flade potential $E_F$) and the transpassive potential $E_{pit}$ or $E_{O_2}$. *Applications:* Storage tanks for 70–98 % H₂SO₄, oxalic and phosphoric acid reactors, fertilizer storage vessels. **Part (b):** *Given:* - $A = 40\ \text{m}^2 = 40 \times 10^4\ \text{cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^5\ \text{cm}^2$ - $i_c = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A/cm}^2$ - $i_p = 8 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A/cm}^2$ - $E_F = -0.30\ \text{V (SHE)}$ - $E_{app} = +0.50\ \text{V (SHE)}$ - $R_{cell} = 1.2\ \Omega$ **(i) Initial current for passivation:** $$ I_c = i_c \times A = (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (4 \times 10^5) $$ $$ I_c = 1000\ \text{A} $$ **(ii) Steady-state current to maintain passivity:** $$ I_p = i_p \times A = (8 \times 10^{-6}) \times (4 \times 10^5) $$ $$ I_p = 3.2\ \text{A} $$ **(iii) Rectifier voltage rating:** The ohmic drop across the electrolyte: $$ V_{drop} = I_p \times R_{cell} = 3.2 \times 1.2 = 3.84\ \text{V} $$ The required overpotential (polarization from $E_F$ to $E_{app}$): $$ \eta = E_{app} - E_F = 0.50 - (-0.30) = 0.80\ \text{V} $$ Total rectifier voltage: $$ V_{rect} = \eta + V_{drop} = 0.80 + 3.84 = 4.64\ \text{V} $$ > [!NOTE] > The rectifier must be rated for **1000 A initially** (for a few minutes) but the running load is only **3.2 A**. This 300-fold drop is the hallmark signature of anodic protection — KTU examiners love asking why the running cost is so low. **Final Answers:** - $I_c = 1000\ \text{A}$ - $I_p = 3.2\ \text{A}$ - $V_{rect} \approx 4.64\ \text{V}$ (use a 5 V rating) --- ### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** *Discuss the role of **alloying elements** (Cr, Ni, Mo, Al, Ti) in promoting and stabilizing passivity. Use a stainless-steel Pourbaix/E-pH diagram to support your answer.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A passive film on iron has a measured capacitance per unit area of $C_d = 5 \times 10^{-2}$ F/m². If the relative permittivity of Fe₂O₃ is **12**, calculate the film thickness. Comment on whether this is consistent with the literature value and explain how the presence of chloride ions can destroy this passive film.* **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a):** *Chromium (Cr):* The single most important passivator. Above **≈ 12 wt-%**, Cr forms a continuous, amorphous **Cr₂O₃** layer on the surface. Cr-oxide is **thermodynamically far more stable** than Fe-oxide (more negative $\Delta G_f^{\circ}$ per mole O) and has a lower ionic conductivity. The Pourbaix diagram of the Fe–Cr–H₂O system shows that the **passive region expands dramatically** above 12 % Cr, covering almost the entire neutral-to-alkaline pH range. *Nickel (Ni):* NiO/Ni(OH)₂ is also passive, but less protective than Cr₂O₃. Ni is added primarily to stabilize the **austenitic (FCC)** crystal structure, which is more ductile and formable than ferritic (BCC) stainless steel. *Molybdenum (Mo):* Mo (≥ 2–6 %) does **not** widen the passive range itself, but it forms **MoO₄²⁻ / MoO₃** at the film surface that **repels Cl⁻ adsorption**, dramatically raising the **pitting resistance equivalent number (PREN)**: $$ \text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N} $$ *Aluminium (Al):* Forms amorphous **Al₂O₃**, the basis of "aluminized" coatings. *Titanium (Ti):* Forms **TiO₂** — extremely stable, the basis of biomedical implants and condenser tubes. *Pourbaix support:* In the Fe–Cr–H₂O diagram, the **passive zone (Fe₂O₃/Cr₂O₃)** dominates between **pH 4 and pH 12** at potentials above **−0.2 V (SHE)** when Cr > 12 %, whereas pure Fe has only a narrow passive lobe near pH 9–12. **Part (b):** *Step 1 — apply parallel-plate formula:* $$ d_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \,\varepsilon_0}{C_d} = \dfrac{12 \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12})}{5 \times 10^{-2}} $$ $$ d_{ox} = \dfrac{1.0625 \times 10^{-10}}{5 \times 10^{-2}} = 2.125 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m} $$ $$ \boxed{d_{ox} \approx 2.1\ \text{nm}} $$ *Step 2 — consistency:* The literature TEM and ellipsometric values for passive Fe₂O₃ on iron lie between **1.5 and 5 nm**, so 2.1 nm is **perfectly consistent**. *Step 3 — Cl⁻ destruction of the passive film (pitting mechanism):* 1. Cl⁻ ions are small, highly mobile and specifically **adsorb** on the passive film, displacing O²⁻ / OH⁻ at defect sites. 2. The adsorbed Cl⁻ locally **thins** the oxide and forms a **metal–Cl complex** that is soluble, e.g. $\text{FeCl}_3$, which dissolves into the electrolyte. 3. Localised dissolution creates a **micropit**; once initiated the pit grows autocatalytically because the pit interior becomes acidic and oxygen-depleted (differential aeration cell). 4. The electrochemical signature is a sudden rise in current at the **pitting potential $E_{pit}$**, which lies **below the oxygen-evolution potential** only when Cl⁻ is present. 5. Prevention: (i) raise $E_{pit}$ by adding Mo or Cr, (ii) lower Cl⁻ activity, (iii) maintain the applied (or corrosion) potential **below $E_{pit}$**. ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in this topic — and where you will lose marks:** > 1. **Confusing AP with CP** — writing "metal is made the cathode" for anodic protection ⇒ **−2 marks instantly.** > 2. **Units mismatch** — converting tank area from m² to cm² is mandatory because $i_c$ is in A/cm². Forgetting ⇒ wrong current by factor of 10⁴ ⇒ **0/7 in part (b).** > 3. **Plotting the polarization curve incorrectly** — forgetting to label the **passive plateau** or the **transpassive boundary** ⇒ −1 to −2 marks. > 4. **Wrong Flade-potential sign** — using $+0.059\,\text{pH}$ instead of $-0.059\,\text{pH}$ is a *very common* sign-flip error. > 5. **Ignoring the rectifier running load** — students often report the *initial* 1000 A as the *running* current, ignoring the 300-fold drop to $i_p$. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. > 6. **Not specifying SHE** — potentials must be quoted against **SHE** unless an alternative reference (Ag/AgCl, SCE) is explicitly stated. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅ - ☐ **Passivity** = thin (1–10 nm), adherent, self-healing oxide/chemisorbed film → CR drops by **10⁴–10⁶**. - ☐ Two theories: **oxide-film (Faraday)** vs **adsorption (Uhlig)** — modern view: chemisorbed monolayer nucleates a 3-D oxide. - ☐ **Four conditions** for passivity: negative $\Delta G_f$, sufficient oxidizing power, film adherence & self-healing, chemical stability. - ☐ **Four control routes:** Anodic Protection, Chemical Inhibitors, Alloying, Environmental Modification. - ☐ Key polarization-curve points: $E_{pp}$ (≈ Flade $E_F$), $i_c$, $i_p$, $E_{pit}$ / transpassive $E_{O_2}$. - ☐ **Flade potential Nernstian dependence:** $E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059\,\text{pH}$. - ☐ **Film thickness from capacitance:** $d_{ox} = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 / C_d$. - ☐ **Alloying rule:** Cr ≥ 12 % → continuous Cr₂O₃ → stainless-steel passivity; Mo raises $E_{pit}$; Ni stabilises austenite. - ☐ **Anodic protection** essentials: metal = anode of rectifier; set-point = +100 to +300 mV above $E_F$; initial current = $i_c A$, running current = $i_p A$; rectifier V = $\eta + I R_{cell}$. - ☐ **Cl⁻** destroys passive films by specific adsorption → pit nucleation; **PREN = %Cr + 3.3 %Mo + 16 %N** predicts pitting resistance. - ☐ **Stainless steel & Ti are the classical passive metals**; Fe passivates only in strong oxidizers or high pH. - ☐ **Cathodic ≠ Anodic protection** — never confuse them in the exam. - ☐ Always report potentials vs **SHE** unless stated otherwise. > 🎯 **Final tip:** Practise drawing the **anodic polarization curve** with all four regions and four landmark points *from memory*. It is the single highest-payoff diagram for Module 2 of PEICT866 Corrosion Control.

topics.title: Effect of Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of electrochemical corrosion, the **Effect of Environment** refers to the systematic influence of external physical, chemical, electrochemical, and biological conditions surrounding a metal surface on the **rate, mechanism, and morphology of corrosion**. The environment acts as the **electrolyte medium** that enables ionic conduction, supplies reactants (oxidizers, protons, dissolved gases), removes products, and frequently dictates whether a metal will passivate, corrode uniformly, or undergo localized attack.

The principal environmental parameters that govern corrosion behavior are:

- **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) concentration**
- **pH of the electrolyte**
- **Temperature**
- **Velocity / Flow regime (hydrodynamic conditions)**
- **Oxidizing agents (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, H₂O₂, Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺)**
- **Electrical conductivity of the medium**
- **Galvanic coupling and stray currents**
- **Biological (microbiological) activity**
- **Corrosion product films**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Environmental factors are *external variables* — they cannot change the intrinsic thermodynamic tendency of a metal to corrode, but they **profoundly alter the kinetics** (corrosion rate). The Pourbaix diagram tells you *IF* a metal will corrode; the environment tells you *HOW FAST*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a metal surface as a **candle** sitting in a room.

- The **wick** is the metal itself (its intrinsic reactivity).
- The **oxygen in the air** is the dissolved oxygen.
- The **humidity (moisture)** is the electrolyte.
- The **draft (wind speed)** around the candle is the flow velocity.
- The **temperature** of the room is the electrolyte temperature.
- A **gentle, still breeze** lets the candle burn steadily.
- A **strong gust** can either blow it out (passivation/deaeration effect) or feed the flame violently (erosion-corrosion).
- A **closed room with no air** suffocates the flame (oxygen starvation).

Just as these ambient conditions control *how fast* the candle burns, environmental variables control the **corrosion current density (iₐᵢₚₚ)** in an electrochemical cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used Throughout Corrosion Science:** The **corrosion rate** is universally reported in **mils per year (mpy)** or **millimeters per year (mm/y)**. Conversion: 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/y.

## 1.3 Why Environment Matters in Engineering Design

A steel pipeline buried in **dry desert sand** may survive for **centuries**, while the *same* steel in **warm seawater** may perforate in **less than 1 mm/year**. Material selection, protective coating choice, and cathodic protection current requirements are *all* determined by environmental analysis. Hence, the Effect of Environment forms the bridge between **thermodynamic possibility** (will it corrode?) and **engineering reality** (how long will it last?).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parameter-by-Parameter Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Effect of Dissolved Oxygen (DO)

Oxygen is the **most common cathodic depolarizer** in near-neutral aqueous environments.

- **Cathodic reaction (neutral/alkaline, pH > 7):**
  $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
- **Cathodic reaction (acidic, pH < 4):**
  $$\text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Logic:** The concentration of DO controls the **limiting diffusion current density (i_L)** for the cathodic reaction. As DO rises, i_L rises, and the **corrosion current (i_corr)** shifts upward on the Evans diagram.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept — Differential Aeration Cell:** When one part of a metal surface is exposed to high DO and another part to low DO, a galvanic cell forms. The **low-DO region becomes the anode** (corrodes), and the **high-DO region becomes the cathode** (protected). This explains **pitting under deposits, water-line corrosion, and crevice corrosion.**

### 2.1.2 Effect of pH

The Nernst equation for the hydrogen evolution reaction gives:

$$E_{\text{H}^+/\text{H}_2} = E^0 - \frac{0.0592}{2}\log\frac{P_{\text{H}_2}}{[\text{H}^+]^2} = -0.0591 \cdot \text{pH} \quad (\text{at } 25^\circ\text{C}, P_{\text{H}_2}=1\text{ atm})$$

- **Low pH (acidic):** Hydrogen evolution is thermodynamically and kinetically easier → **higher corrosion rate** for active metals like Fe, Zn, Mg.
- **High pH (alkaline):** Amphoteric metals (Al, Zn, Pb) suffer **increased attack** because their protective oxide dissolves as aluminate, zincate, or plumbite ions. Example:
  $$\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow 2\text{AlO}_2^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- **Near-neutral pH (6–9):** Steel forms a stable γ-FeOOH / Fe₃O₄ film → **minimum corrosion rate** in fresh water.

### 2.1.3 Effect of Temperature

Temperature influences corrosion rate through the **Arrhenius relationship:**

$$i_{\text{corr}} = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $E_a$ = activation energy of the corrosion process (typically **40–80 kJ/mol** for activation-controlled systems)
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant
- $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

- For **activation-controlled** systems: corrosion rate *doubles* for every **10 °C** rise.
- For **diffusion-controlled** systems: rate increase is **sub-linear** and may *decrease* if DO solubility drops sharply with temperature (e.g., open hot water tanks corrode *less* than cold water tanks).

### 2.1.4 Effect of Velocity

Velocity governs the **mass-transport regime**:

- **Stagnant flow (low velocity):** Diffusion layer is thick → corrosion limited by **DO diffusion**.
- **Moderate flow:** Thinner diffusion layer → higher i_corr.
- **High velocity:** Erosion, cavitation, or **impingement attack** strips the protective film → a sharp, almost vertical rise in corrosion rate.
- **Very high velocity:** **Erosion-corrosion** dominates; the rate becomes nearly **independent of the metal's intrinsic corrosion resistance** and is governed purely by hydrodynamics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Velocity Rule:** Beyond a **critical fluid velocity** (≈ 1 m/s for carbon steel in seawater), the protective Fe₃O₄/γ-FeOOH film is mechanically removed, and the rate can increase by **10–100×** abruptly.

### 2.1.5 Effect of Oxidizing Agents & Aggressive Ions

| Species | Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Cl⁻** | Destroys passive films on stainless steel → pitting & crevice corrosion |
| **SO₄²⁻** | Mildly aggressive; supports SRB activity |
| **H₂O₂, Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺** | Strong oxidizers → may push potential into the **passive region** for Ni, Cr, stainless steel; or dramatically **increase i_corr** for Cu, Fe |
| **NO₃⁻** | Acts as a *cathodic inhibitor* (oxidizer) for iron |

The oxidizing power is quantified by the **oxidizing capacity (OC):**

$$\text{OC} = \sum_i \left( n_i \cdot F \cdot C_i \right)$$

Where $n_i$ is the electron transfer number and $C_i$ the molar concentration.

### 2.1.6 Effect of Electrical Conductivity (Resistivity)

The corrosion current flowing between anode and cathode through the electrolyte follows **Ohm's law:**

$$I = \frac{\Delta E}{R_e + R_m}$$

Where $R_e$ = electrolyte resistance and $R_m$ = metallic path resistance.

- **High conductivity (low resistivity) water** (e.g., seawater ~ 5×10⁻² S/m) → corrosion current can spread over **large cathodic areas** → macrocells are effective.
- **Low conductivity (high resistivity) water** (e.g., pure rainwater ~ 10⁻⁵ S/m) → corrosion is **localized** to the immediate vicinity of the anode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Soil Corrosion Insight:** Resistivity is the **single most important soil parameter**. ASTM G57 / Wenner 4-pin method is used to measure it. Soils with resistivity < 1000 Ω·cm are **"corrosive"**, while > 20,000 Ω·cm are **"mildly corrosive"** to ferrous metals.

### 2.1.7 Effect of Galvanic Coupling & Stray Currents

- **Galvanic coupling** occurs when two dissimilar metals are in electrical contact in an electrolyte. The **Galvanic Series in that environment** dictates the polarity.
- **Stray currents** from DC traction systems, cathodic protection of neighboring pipelines, or welding machines cause severe localized attack where the current **leaves** the metal (becomes the anode).

The damage is given by **Faraday's law:**

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

### 2.1.8 Effect of Biological Organisms (MIC)

**Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** is caused by:
- **Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB)** — *Desulfovibrio* spp. — produce H₂S, accelerate pitting.
- **Iron-Oxidizing Bacteria** — *Gallionella*, *Crenothrix* — form tubercles that create differential aeration cells.
- **Acid-Producing Bacteria** — *Thiobacillus* — lower pH drastically.

### 2.1.9 Effect of Corrosion Products

Sometimes corrosion products are **beneficial (protective)** — e.g., PbO₂, Fe₃O₄, Al₂O₃, SnO₂.
Sometimes they are **harmful** — e.g., porous rust (FeOOH), which allows further attack and supports differential aeration.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i_{\text{corr}} = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Arrhenius rate law | A/m² | Effect of T on rate |
| 2 | $E = -0.0591 \cdot \text{pH}$ | Hydrogen line | V (SHE) | pH effect |
| 3 | $\Delta m = \dfrac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law of mass loss | kg | Any current-driven loss |
| 4 | $\text{CR (mpy)} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Weight-loss corrosion rate | mpy | Lab testing |
| 5 | $i_L = \dfrac{n F D C_b}{\delta}$ | Limiting diffusion current (DO) | A/m² | Cathodic control |
| 6 | $\text{OC} = n F C$ | Oxidizing capacity | C/L | Effect of oxidizers |
| 7 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Electrolyte resistance | Ω | Stray current & soil |
| 8 | $P_{\text{O}_2} = k_H \cdot X_{\text{O}_2}$ | Henry's law for DO | atm or Pa | DO saturation |
| 9 | $k = k_0 \exp(-\Delta G^\ddagger / RT)$ | Eyring / transition-state | various | General rate theory |
| 10 | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Pitting resistance equiv. | – | Cl⁻ resistance ranking |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $E = -0.0591 \times \text{pH}$ forgetting that this is **only valid at 25 °C** and **1 atm H₂**. At other temperatures, the Nernst slope is $-RT/F \cdot \ln 10 / 2 = -0.1985 \cdot T \text{ (K)} \, \mu\text{V/pH}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore oil platforms:** Designers consult environmental data (DO, Cl⁻, T, velocity, sulfate-reducing bacteria count) before selecting **6Mo super-austenitic** or **super-duplex** stainless steel.
- **Underground pipelines:** Soil resistivity surveys, redox potential, and pH mapping dictate **cathodic protection anode spacing**.
- **Chemical process industry:** Velocity limits in piping are restricted below the **erosion-corrosion threshold** (e.g., 1.8 m/s for austenitic SS in seawater).
- **Power plant condensers:** Tube material (Ti, Cu-Ni 90/10, 70/30) is selected by **velocity–temperature envelope** charts.
- **Biocide dosing** in cooling towers is determined by SRB counts in recirculating water.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Corrosion Rate from Weight-Loss Data (Faraday → mpy)

The KTU lab and viva routinely test the conversion from **measured mass loss** to **mpy**. Below is the *complete* derivation the examiner expects.

### Step 1: State Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

The mass of metal dissolved by a current $I$ flowing for time $t$ is:

$$\Delta m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $M$ = atomic weight (g/mol)
- $n$ = valence of the dissolving metal (electrons per atom)
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)

### Step 2: Express I in terms of measured charge

Total charge passed:

$$Q = I \cdot t \quad (\text{Coulombs})$$

Substitute back:

$$\Delta m = \frac{M \cdot Q}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 3: Convert the rate to "per year"

Annual rate of mass loss per unit area:

$$R = \frac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t} = \frac{M \cdot I}{n \cdot F \cdot A} \quad \left[\frac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}\right]$$

To convert to **mpy**, multiply by appropriate constants.

**Empirical "534" formula** widely used in ASTM G1:

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight loss (mg)
- $D$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $A$ = exposed area (in²)
- $t$ = exposure time (hours)
- The constant **534** has units such that $W$ (mg) / $D$ (g/cm³) / $A$ (in²) / $t$ (h) gives mpy.

### Step 4: Derive the constant 534

We need: $\left[\dfrac{\text{mil}}{\text{year}}\right]$ = ?

$$\frac{1 \text{ mil}}{\text{year}} = \frac{0.001 \text{ in}}{8760 \text{ h}} = \frac{0.001 \times 2.54 \text{ cm}}{8760 \text{ h}} = 2.899 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm/h}$$

For uniform corrosion, the depth $d$ in time $t$ is:

$$d = \frac{\Delta m}{D \cdot A}$$

Set $d = 1$ mil $= 2.54 \times 10^{-3}$ cm in 1 year (8760 h):

$$\frac{\Delta m}{D \cdot A} = 2.54 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}$$

Now, the charge required to dissolve equivalent mass of metal is given by Faraday's law (1 Faraday = 96,485 C = 26.8 A·h):

$$\Delta m \text{ (g)} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot 8760}{n \cdot 26.8}$$

For **iron** as reference: $M = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$.

$$\Delta m = \frac{55.85 \cdot I \cdot 8760}{2 \cdot 26.8 \cdot 1000} = 9.13 \cdot I \quad (\text{g for }I \text{ in A, with A in cm}^2)$$

After unit juggling for mpy:

$$\boxed{\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot t}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A carbon-steel coupon (Fe, $M=55.85$, $n=2$, $D=7.86$ g/cm³) of area **10 in²** is immersed in seawater for **720 h** and loses **1200 mg** of mass due to uniform corrosion. Calculate the corrosion rate in:
1. **mpy**
2. **mm/year**
3. **g/(m²·day)**

### Solution

**Part (a): mpy**

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{534 \times 1200}{7.86 \times 10 \times 720}$$

Computing numerator: $534 \times 1200 = 640{,}800$

Computing denominator: $7.86 \times 10 \times 720 = 56{,}592$

$$\text{CR (mpy)} = \frac{640{,}800}{56{,}592} = 11.32 \text{ mpy}$$

**Part (b): mm/year**

$$1 \text{ mpy} = 0.0254 \text{ mm/y}$$

$$\text{CR} = 11.32 \times 0.0254 = 0.2875 \text{ mm/y}$$

**Part (c): g/(m²·day)**

First convert units:
- $W = 1200$ mg $= 1.2$ g
- $A = 10$ in² $= 10 \times 6.4516 = 64.516$ cm² $= 64.516 \times 10^{-4}$ m² $= 6.4516 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $t = 720$ h $= 30$ days

$$\text{CR} = \frac{1.2}{6.4516 \times 10^{-3} \times 30} = \frac{1.2}{0.19355} = 6.20 \;\text{g/(m}^2 \cdot \text{day)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Scheme:** Each part carries equal weight. Always show the unit conversion; the examiner will deduct for missing units.

## 3.3 Derivation: Effect of pH on Corrosion Potential (Nernst)

For the half-reaction:

$$\text{H}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}\text{H}_2 \quad (E^0 = 0 \text{ V vs SHE})$$

By Nernst:

$$E = E^0 - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{(P_{\text{H}_2})^{1/2}}{[\text{H}^+]}$$

At $T = 298$ K, $P_{\text{H}_2} = 1$ atm:

$$E = -\frac{0.0257}{1}\ln\frac{1}{[\text{H}^+]} = -0.0257 \times 2.303 \times \text{pH}$$

$$\boxed{E = -0.0591 \times \text{pH} \;\;(\text{V vs SHE, at 25 °C})}$$

This shows that as pH rises by one unit, the reversible hydrogen potential becomes **59.1 mV more negative**, shrinking the driving force ($\Delta E$) for active metals corroding with H⁺ reduction.

## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Velocity on Corrosion Rate

For a **rotating disc electrode** (RDE) or pipe flow, the **Levich equation** gives the mass-transport limited current density:

$$i_L = 0.62 \cdot n \cdot F \cdot D^{2/3} \cdot \omega^{1/2} \cdot \nu^{-1/6} \cdot C_b$$

Where:
- $D$ = diffusivity of O₂ (m²/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)
- $C_b$ = bulk O₂ concentration (mol/m³)

**Physical interpretation:**
- $i_L \propto \omega^{1/2}$ for RDE
- $i_L \propto U^{1/2}$ in fully developed turbulent pipe flow (where $U$ is mean velocity)
- $i_L \propto U^{0.8 \text{ to } 1.0}$ in highly turbulent flow

**Engineering takeaway:** The corrosion rate, in transport-limited regimes, scales with the **square root of velocity** — a useful design rule when extrapolating lab data to field flow conditions.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Differential Aeration Cell

**Problem:** A horizontal steel pipe of length 2 m lies half-buried in saturated soil. The top half is exposed to air (DO ≈ 8 mg/L). The bottom half is in waterlogged, anaerobic soil (DO ≈ 0.5 mg/L). Estimate qualitatively:
1. Which region acts as the cathode?
2. Which region corrodes?
3. Suggest one protection strategy.

### Solution

1. The **top (aerated) region** is the **cathode** — it supports the reduction of O₂.
2. The **bottom (oxygen-starved) region** is the **anode** — it dissolves as Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻.
3. **Protection strategies:** (a) Apply **cathodic protection** (sacrificial Mg anode or impressed current), (b) ensure uniform backfill and drainage, (c) coat the bottom half with epoxy before burial.
