# 5. 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 ✍️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define cathodic protection. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a sacrificial anode cathodic protection system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cathodic protection is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is forced to become the cathode of an electrochemical cell, thereby preventing its anodic dissolution.

**Working (GACP):**
- A metal **more electrochemically active** than the structure (e.g., Zn for steel) is electrically connected to the structure and immersed in a common electrolyte.
- Due to the potential difference (Zn $E^\circ = -0.76$ V, Fe $E^\circ = -0.44$ V vs SHE), zinc preferentially oxidizes:
  $$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$
- Electrons released at the Zn anode travel through the metallic bond to the steel structure.
- At the steel surface, dissolved oxygen is reduced:
  $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
- The steel is polarized cathodically and corrosion is suppressed. The zinc anode is *sacrificed* and must be replaced periodically.

**Sketch:** *(See Section 4.2 Mermaid block)* — Zn anode | soil electrolyte | steel structure with bond wire connecting both.

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the protection criteria used to confirm that a structure is adequately cathodically protected.**

**Model Answer:**

A structure is considered *cathodically protected* when **any one** of the following criteria (per NACE SP0169 / ISO 15589) is met:

| # | Criterion | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Negative potential criterion | $E \leq -850$ mV vs CSE (with IR-drop considered) |
| 2 | Instant-off potential | $E \leq -950$ mV vs CSE (IR-drop eliminated) |
| 3 | 300 mV polarization shift | A minimum of 300 mV shift from native $E_{corr}$ |
| 4 | Anodic Tafel slope evidence | $dE/d\log I$ is positive at the protection point |

The **most widely used** is the $-850$ mV criterion.

---

## 5.2 ✍️ Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Choose ONE

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **working principle of impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP)** with a neat block diagram. Compare it with sacrificial anode cathodic protection on the basis of (i) power source, (ii) anode type, (iii) soil-resistivity suitability, and (iv) over-protection risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A buried steel pipeline 800 m long, 50 cm outer diameter is to be protected by the impressed current method. The protective current density required is 15 mA/m². The rectifier delivers 24 V DC, and the circuit resistance (anode-bed + cable + structure-to-soil) is 0.8 Ω. Calculate: **(i)** total protective current, **(ii)** total annual energy consumed in kWh, and **(iii)** the annual cost of electricity at ₹8 per kWh. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Working of ICCP & Comparison

**Working Principle (ICCP):**
1. AC mains (230 V) is fed to a **rectifier unit**, which converts it to low-voltage DC (typically 5–50 V).
2. The **positive terminal** of the rectifier is connected to an **auxiliary (inert) anode** — usually graphite, high-silicon cast iron, or MMO-coated titanium — buried in a coke-breeze backfill at a remote location.
3. The **negative terminal** is connected to the **structure to be protected** (pipeline, tank, etc.).
4. The rectifier *forces* a direct current to flow from the auxiliary anode, through the soil electrolyte, to the structure.
5. At the **inert anode**, oxygen (or chlorine) is evolved:
   $$2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$$
6. At the **structure (cathode)**, dissolved oxygen is reduced, suppressing the anodic dissolution of iron:
   $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$$
7. The structure potential is continuously monitored using a **reference electrode (CSE)** and the rectifier output is adjusted to maintain the structure at or below **$-850$ mV vs CSE**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | GACP | ICCP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Power source | Galvanic (none) | AC mains + rectifier |
| (ii) Anode type | Active (Mg, Zn, Al) | Inert (MMO/Ti, graphite) |
| (iii) Soil-resistivity suitability | $\leq 5000\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}$ | Any, including high resistivity |
| (iv) Over-protection risk | Negligible | Significant (must be controlled) |

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Total Protective Current:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi D L = \pi \times 0.50 \times 800 = 1256.64\ \text{m}^2 \\
I_{total} &= i_{c,prot} \times A \\
&= 15 \times 10^{-3} \times 1256.64 \\
&= 18.85\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Annual Energy Consumption:**

Power drawn from the rectifier:
$$\begin{aligned}
P &= V \times I = 24 \times 18.85 = 452.4\ \text{W} = 0.4524\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

(Assuming 100 % rectifier efficiency for simplicity; in practice, efficiency is 60–80 %.)

Energy per year:
$$\begin{aligned}
E &= P \times 8760\ \text{h} = 0.4524 \times 8760 = 3963.0\ \text{kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Annual Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= 3963.0 \times 8 = ₹31\,704\ \text{per year}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Alternative Set]`

**(a)** Describe the **mechanism and components of sacrificial anode cathodic protection** for an underground pipeline. What factors govern the **selection of anode material**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 m long, 30 cm diameter steel pipeline in seawater is to be protected using **aluminium sacrificial anodes**. Anode capacity of Al = 2700 A·h/kg, utilization factor = 0.85, efficiency = 0.95. Each anode weighs 5 kg and delivers 0.25 A. Required current density = 0.2 A/m². Calculate: **(i)** total current required, **(ii)** number of anodes, **(iii)** expected anode life in years. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Mechanism, Components & Anode Selection

**Mechanism & Components:**
1. **Anode:** Made of a more active metal (Mg, Zn, or Al) — installed in a prepared pit and surrounded by a low-resistance backfill (gypsum + bentonite + sodium sulphate).
2. **Bond wire / Cable:** A heavy-gauge insulated copper wire connects the anode to the structure, completing the electrical circuit.
3. **Structure (Cathode):** The buried pipeline, after surface preparation and coating.
4. **Electrolyte:** The moist soil or seawater surrounding both the anode and the structure.
5. **Reference Electrode (CSE):** Used to periodically measure the structure-to-electrolyte potential.

**Working:** The potential difference between the active anode and the structure drives a galvanic current. Electrons released by the anode's oxidation flow through the wire to the steel, polarizing it cathodically. The anode is gradually consumed.

**Factors governing anode material selection:**
| Factor | Influence |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Electromotive force (driving voltage)** | Mg highest; required for high-resistivity soils |
| **Anode capacity (A·h/kg)** | Higher capacity → longer life per kg |
| **Electrolyte compatibility** | Mg for soil; Al & Zn for seawater |
| **Cost & availability** | Zn cheapest; Al best capacity; Mg most expensive |
| **Self-corrosion behaviour** | Al must be alloyed (with In, Hg, Sn) to prevent passivation |
| **Mechanical form** | Available as rods, plates, bracelet (for hulls) |

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Total Current Required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi D L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 500 = 471.24\ \text{m}^2 \\
I_{total} &= i_{c,prot} \times A = 0.2 \times 471.24 = 94.25\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Number of Anodes:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{I_{total}}{I_{per\,anode}} = \frac{94.25}{0.25} = 377\ \text{anodes}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Anode Life:**

Effective capacity:
$$C_{eff} = 2700 \times 0.95 \times 0.85 = 2180.25\ \text{A·h/kg}$$

Anode life per anode (delivering 0.25 A):
$$\begin{aligned}
T_{life} &= \frac{m \cdot C_{eff}}{I \cdot 8760} \\
&= \frac{5 \times 2180.25}{0.25 \times 8760} \\
&= \frac{10901.25}{2190} \\
&\approx 4.98\ \text{years}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Result:** Total **377 aluminium anodes**, each with a life of about **5 years**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in CP Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-up in Faraday's Law:** Forgetting to convert $t$ to *seconds* (or $F$ to A·h). Loss = **2 marks**.
> 2. **Neglecting Utilization & Efficiency factors:** Using full anode capacity and forgetting 0.85 / 0.90 corrections. Loss = **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $E_{corr}$ with protection potential:** The criterion is *not* $E_{corr} - 850$ mV; it is *absolute* $-850$ mV vs CSE. Loss = **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong anode type in sketch:** Drawing Zn as the cathode (students invert the polarity). Loss = **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the rectifier unit** in the ICCP diagram — examiner often allocates a separate sub-mark for it. Loss = **1 mark**.
> 6. **Molar mass wrong:** Using 65.4 g for Zn is correct; using 65 is also acceptable; using 56 (iron's!) is **NOT**.
> 7. **No mention of reference electrode in CP** → Loss of **1 mark** in any descriptive answer.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 / CP System Working**

- 🔹 **CP = make the protected metal the cathode** of an electrochemical cell.
- 🔹 **Two methods:** (1) Sacrificial / Galvanic (GACP) — uses active metals; (2) Impressed Current (ICCP) — uses rectifier + inert anode.
- 🔹 **Anode materials (GACP):** Mg (high-resistivity soil), Zn (seawater, low-ρ soil), Al (offshore, alloyed with In/Hg).
- 🔹 **Anode materials (ICCP):** Graphite, High-Silicon Cast Iron, MMO/Ti, Platinized Ti — all "inert" or near-inert.
- 🔹 **Cathodic reaction:** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$ (in neutral aerated media).
- 🔹 **Anode reaction (GACP):** Metal dissolution, e.g., $\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$.
- 🔹 **Anode reaction (ICCP):** $\text{O}_2$ evolution (or $\text{Cl}_2$ in seawater) at inert anode.
- 🔹 **Protection criteria:** $-850$ mV vs CSE; $-950$ mV instant-off; 300 mV shift.
- 🔹 **Key equation — Faraday's Law:** $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
- 🔹 **Key equation — Anode life (yr):** $T = \dfrac{m \cdot C \cdot U}{I \cdot 8760}$.
- 🔹 **Key equation — Anode-to-earth resistance (Dwight formula):** $R_v = \dfrac{0.0052 \cdot \rho}{L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$.
- 🔹 **Backfill** = gypsum + bentonite + Na₂SO₄ → lowers anode resistance, keeps anode moist.
- 🔹 **Reference electrodes:** CSE (field), SCE (lab), Ag/AgCl (seawater).
- 🔹 **IR drop** must be eliminated for accurate potential measurement (use *instant-off* technique or place RE close to structure).
- 🔹 **Over-protection** in ICCP → causes H₂ embrittlement of high-strength steel & disbonding of coatings.
- 🔹 **Stray currents** from ICCP can damage nearby unconnected metallic structures (interference bonds needed).
- 🔹 **Application Memory Map:** Pipelines → ICCP; Ships & offshore → GACP (Al/Zn bracelets); Water heaters → Mg rod; Concrete bridges → Ti-mesh ICCP.

> 🏁 **End of Module 3 — Working of Cathodic Protection System** 🏁

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Leading to Corrosion of Underground Metallic Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Factors Leading to Corrosion of Underground Metallic Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underground Corrosion** is defined as the *electrochemical degradation of buried metallic structures* (pipelines, storage tanks, cables, reinforcing bars, and well casings) caused by the **soil electrolyte** acting as an aggressive ionic medium that drives coupled anodic dissolution and cathodic reduction reactions on the metal surface.

In the framework of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT866 – Corrosion Control)**, underground corrosion is classified under *wet / electrolyte-mediated corrosion* in which the soil behaves as a **heterogeneous, aerated, moist, and biologically active electrolyte**. Unlike atmospheric corrosion (thin-film electrolyte), the metal in soil is fully immersed in a complex ionic medium whose aggressivity depends upon the synergy between chemical, electrochemical, microbiological, and stray-current variables.

The generic anodic and cathodic half-cell reactions are:

$$ \text{Fe}_{(s)} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2e^{-} \quad (\text{Anodic — metal dissolution}) $$

$$ \text{O}_{2(g)} + 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} + 4e^{-} \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}_{(aq)} \quad (\text{Cathodic — oxygen reduction in neutral/alkaline soil}) $$

In **anaerobic (waterlogged) soils**, the dominant cathodic reaction shifts to:

$$ 2\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} + 2e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{H}_{2(g)} + 2\text{OH}^{-}_{(aq)} $$

or, in the presence of **sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB)**:

$$ \text{SO}_{4}^{2-}_{(aq)} + 9\text{H}^{+} + 8e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{HS}^{-}_{(aq)} + 4\text{H}_{2}\text{O}_{(l)} $$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Invisible Acid Lake" Beneath Our Feet

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine burying a steel water pipe beneath your garden. The surrounding soil is not just inert dirt — it is a *living, breathing chemical soup* containing dissolved salts, trapped rainwater, air pockets, microbes, and stray electrical currents from nearby railways. Just as the human body slowly corrodes a metallic orthopedic implant, the **soil electrolyte gradually "eats" the metal** through microscopic electrochemical cells. Where oxygen is plentiful (loose, dry topsoil), the metal becomes *cathodic*; where oxygen is scarce (clay-rich, waterlogged subsoil), it becomes *anodic* and dissolves. The result is the familiar but devastating **pipeline pitting failure** that municipalities and oil & gas industries spend billions fighting every year.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics for Underground Corrosion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Units Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Soil Resistivity, ρ** — measured in **Ω·cm** (ohm-centimetres) or **Ω·m** (ohm-metres). Lower resistivity ⇒ more aggressive soil.
> - **Corrosion Rate, CR** — expressed in **mm/yr** (millimetres per year) or **mpy** (mils per year, where 1 mpy = 0.0254 mm/yr).
> - **Redox Potential, Eₕ** — measured in **mV (SHE)** — indicates aeration/anaeration of the soil.
> - **Soil pH** — dimensionless; corrosion aggressivity rises sharply when pH < 5.5.
> - **Moisture Content, w** — expressed as **% by dry weight** of soil.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Underground Corrosion Factors

For a **14-mark KTU essay question**, the examiner expects a clean **3-tier classification**:

#### **Tier 1 — Soil Physico-Chemical Factors**

1. **Soil Resistivity (ρ)** — The single most important indicator. The lower the resistivity, the higher the ionic conductivity, and the more aggressive the soil.
2. **Soil Moisture Content (w)** — Governs ionic mobility. Corrosion rate is maximum at an *optimum moisture* (typically 15–25 % by weight) — too dry ⇒ no electrolyte; too saturated ⇒ oxygen-starved.
3. **Soil pH** — Acidic soils (pH < 5.5) accelerate corrosion by supplying H⁺ ions and destabilising protective oxide films.
4. **Dissolved Salts** — Chlorides (Cl⁻), sulfates (SO₄²⁻), and bicarbonates (HCO₃⁻) increase conductivity and break down passive films.
5. **Soil Texture / Particle Size** — Clay (fine) holds water → low aeration → anaerobic cells. Sand (coarse) → high aeration → differential aeration.
6. **Organic Matter & Humus** — Generates organic acids (humic, fulvic) and supports microbial growth.

#### **Tier 2 — Environmental / Biological Factors**

7. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO)** — Drives differential aeration cells. Variations along a buried pipe produce macro-cells.
8. **Soil Temperature** — A 10 °C rise roughly **doubles** the corrosion rate (Arrhenius-type behaviour).
9. **Microbial Activity (MIC — Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion)** — *Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria* (SRB, e.g., *Desulfovibrio*) are the principal culprits; they catalyse cathodic depolarisation.
10. **Redox Potential (Eₕ)** — A direct qualitative measure of soil aeration:
    - Eₕ > +400 mV → aerobic
    - +100 to +400 mV → mildly reducing
    - 0 to +100 mV → moderately reducing
    - Eₕ < 0 mV → strongly reducing (anaerobic — favours SRB)

#### **Tier 3 — External / Stray-Current Factors**

11. **DC Stray Currents** — From electric railways, welding operations, impressed-current cathodic protection of neighbouring structures, and DC transmission systems.
12. **AC Interference** — From high-voltage AC transmission lines buried in parallel.
13. **Galvanic Coupling** — Dissimiliar metals in the same electrolyte (e.g., copper service line joined to a steel main).
14. **Proximity to Other Structures** — Concentric piping, reinforcing bars in concrete, grounding electrodes.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory for KTU Board Exam – Memorise the following equations and units**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law — fundamental relation between voltage, current, and resistance | $V$ = potential (V), $I$ = current (A), $R$ = resistance (Ω) |
| 2 | $\rho = 2 \pi a R$ | **Wenner 4-Pin Method** for soil resistivity | $\rho$ = resistivity (Ω·cm), $a$ = electrode spacing (cm), $R$ = measured resistance (Ω) |
| 3 | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln \dfrac{a_{\text{ox}}}{a_{\text{red}}}$ | **Nernst Equation** — equilibrium electrode potential | $E^{\circ}$ = standard potential (V), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K), $F$ = 96485 C/mol, $n$ = electrons transferred |
| 4 | $\eta = a + b \log i$ | **Tafel Equation** — overpotential vs. current density | $\eta$ = overpotential (V), $a, b$ = Tafel constants, $i$ = current density (A/cm²) |
| 5 | $\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i \cdot EW}{d}$ | **Corrosion Rate from Polarisation Current** | $i$ = current density (μA/cm²), $EW$ = equivalent weight (g/eq), $d$ = density (g/cm³) |
| 6 | $\text{CR}_{mpy} = \dfrac{534 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | **Corrosion Rate from Weight-Loss** | $W$ = weight loss (mg), $D$ = density (g/cm³), $A$ = area (in²), $T$ = time (hr) |
| 7 | $\text{CR}_{mm/yr} = \dfrac{87.6 \cdot W}{D \cdot A \cdot T}$ | Corrosion Rate (SI form) | $W$ (mg), $D$ (g/cm³), $A$ (cm²), $T$ (hr) |
| 8 | $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\beta_a \cdot \beta_c}{2.303 \cdot R_p \cdot (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | **Stern–Geary Equation** — corrosion current from polarisation resistance $R_p$ | $i_{\text{corr}}$ in A/cm², $R_p$ in Ω·cm², $\beta_a, \beta_c$ in V/decade |
| 9 | $\text{Biological Factor} \propto \dfrac{[\text{SO}_4^{2-}]}{[\text{O}_2]}$ | SRB activity criterion | Ratio governs microbial aggressivity |

### 2.3 Why and How — Mechanistic "Why" Behind Each Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Logic Chain** *(essential for KTU Part B 14-mark answers)*

- **Why low resistivity is dangerous:** Resistivity is inversely proportional to ionic concentration. Low ρ means high ionic conductivity, which closes the electrochemical circuit between anodic and cathodic regions, allowing large corrosion currents to flow with minimal IR drop.
- **Why chlorides are aggressive:** Cl⁻ ions penetrate the protective Fe₂O₃/Fe₃O₄ passive film, displacing oxygen in the lattice and forming soluble FeCl₂ complexes that do not re-passivate the surface.
- **Why SRB cause havoc in anaerobic soils:** SRB consume cathodic hydrogen (depolarisation) and produce H₂S, which reacts with Fe²⁺ to form black iron sulfide (FeS) — a non-protective, porous corrosion product.
- **Why stray currents are devastating:** A buried pipeline that intercepts a portion of return current from a DC traction system acts as a *low-resistance path*; current discharges at the pipe surface, causing intense localised anodic dissolution (often > 1 mm/yr, i.e., pipe perforation within months).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Wenner 4-Pin Soil Resistivity Method

The Wenner method uses **four equally-spaced electrodes** driven into the soil in a straight line. A current $I$ is passed between the outer two pins, and the potential difference $V$ is measured between the inner two pins.

**Step 1 — Model the soil as a homogeneous semi-infinite medium.**

The current $I$ injected at the outer electrode creates a hemispherical equipotential surface in the soil. The current density at a radial distance $r$ from the electrode is:

$$ J(r) = \frac{I}{2 \pi r^{2}} $$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law in differential form.**

The electric field at distance $r$ is:

$$ E(r) = \rho \cdot J(r) = \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi r^{2}} $$

**Step 3 — Integrate the field from the inner pin to the outer pin.**

The measured potential between the inner two pins (spaced at distance $a$ from each other and from the outer pins) is:

$$ V = \int_{a}^{2a} E(r) \, dr = \int_{a}^{2a} \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi r^{2}} \, dr $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$ V = \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \int_{a}^{2a} \frac{dr}{r^{2}} = \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left[ -\frac{1}{r} \right]_{a}^{2a} $$

$$ V = \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \left( -\frac{1}{2a} + \frac{1}{a} \right) = \frac{\rho I}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2a} $$

**Step 5 — Solve for resistivity.**

The measured resistance is $R = V/I$. Substituting and rearranging:

$$ R = \frac{\rho}{4 \pi a} $$

$$ \boxed{\;\rho = 4 \pi a R\;} \quad \text{(in Ω·m if } a \text{ in metres, R in Ω)} $$

$$ \boxed{\;\rho = 2 \pi a R\;} \quad \text{(in Ω·cm if } a \text{ in cm, R in Ω)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Numerical Example (often asked in KTU Part A):** If pin spacing $a = 1.5$ m and measured resistance $R = 12 \, \Omega$, the soil resistivity is $\rho = 4 \pi (1.5)(12) = 226.1 \, \Omega \cdot m$. By standard classification, this is **moderately corrosive**.

### 3.2 Derivation — Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law

**Step 1 — Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.**

The mass of metal dissolved is directly proportional to the total charge passed:

$$ W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{Q \cdot EW}{F} $$

where $EW = M/n$ is the equivalent weight (g/equivalent) and $Q$ is total charge (C).

**Step 2 — Express in corrosion rate (penetration depth per year).**

The penetration depth $d$ is related to mass loss by $W = d \cdot A \cdot D$, so:

$$ d = \frac{W}{A \cdot D} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot EW}{A \cdot D \cdot F} = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot EW}{D \cdot F} $$

where $i = I/A$ is the current density (A/cm²).

**Step 3 — Convert time to a year (used universally in CR).**

A year contains $t = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.154 \times 10^{7}$ s. Faraday's constant $F = 96485$ C/eq.

$$ \text{CR} = \frac{i \cdot (3.154 \times 10^{7}) \cdot EW}{D \cdot 96485} \text{ cm/s} \times \text{conversion} $$

Combining and converting to mm/yr:

$$ \boxed{\;\text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i \cdot EW}{D}\; \text{mm/yr}\;} $$

with $i$ in **μA/cm²**, $EW$ in g/eq, $D$ in g/cm³.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Severity of Soil Corrosion vs. Resistivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a board-exam favourite — memorise the cut-off values.**

| Soil Resistivity (Ω·cm) | Corrosivity Rating | Expected Service Life of Bare Steel |
|-------------------------|--------------------|--------------------------------------|
| $0$ – $1000$ | **Very High (Severe)** | < 5 years |
| $1000$ – $2000$ | **High** | 5 – 10 years |
| $2000$ – $5000$ | **Moderate** | 10 – 20 years |
| $5000$ – $10000$ | **Low (Mild)** | 20 – 30 years |
| $> 10000$ | **Negligible** | > 30 years |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Predicting Pipeline Life from Stray Current

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical:** A 30 cm diameter buried steel pipeline intercepts 2.5 A of DC stray current over a 200 m section. Estimate the **time to perforation** if pipe wall thickness is 8 mm and iron density is 7.87 g/cm³. Given: $EW_{\text{Fe}} = 27.92$ g/eq.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Total charge per second (current is constant): $I = 2.5$ A.

Step 2 — Corrosion rate in mm/yr:

$$ \text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \times i \times EW}{D} $$

Surface area of the 200 m section: $A = \pi (0.30)(200) = 188.5$ m² $= 1.885 \times 10^{6}$ cm². Current density:

$$ i = \frac{2.5}{1.885 \times 10^{6}} \text{ A/cm}^{2} = 1.326 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A/cm}^{2} = 1.326 \ \mu\text{A/cm}^{2} $$

Step 3 — Corrosion rate:

$$ \text{CR} = \frac{0.00327 \times 1.326 \times 27.92}{7.87} = 0.01538 \text{ mm/yr} $$

Step 4 — Time to perforation (8 mm wall):

$$ T = \frac{8}{0.01538} = 520 \ \text{years} $$

> [!WARNING]
> The result is deceptively large because current density is averaged over a huge area. **In reality, stray current discharges at localised "hot spots"** (coating defects), and localised rates can be **50–100× higher**, leading to perforation in **months to a few years**. KTU examiners expect you to mention this *localisation effect*.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Define soil resistivity. How does it influence the corrosivity of soil towards buried metallic structures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Soil resistivity (ρ), measured in Ω·cm, is the resistance of a unit cube of soil to the passage of electric current; it is the inverse of ionic conductivity. Soils with **low resistivity (< 1000 Ω·cm)** contain abundant dissolved ions (chlorides, sulfates, moisture) that readily conduct current, closing the electrochemical circuit between anodic and cathodic regions of the buried metal and producing **high corrosion rates**. Soils with ρ > 10 000 Ω·cm (dry, sandy, low-salt) are essentially non-corrosive because ionic conduction is too poor to sustain a significant corrosion current. Hence resistivity is the **single most important field indicator** of soil corrosivity, routinely measured using the **Wenner 4-pin method**.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **What is Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)? Name the dominant microbial species responsible and the cathodic depolarisation reaction involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
MIC is the acceleration of metal degradation caused by the metabolic activity of microorganisms on or near the metal surface. The dominant species in underground corrosion are **Sulfate-Reduccing Bacteria (SRB)**, principally *Desulfovibrio desulfuricans*, which thrive in **anaerobic, sulfate-rich, near-neutral pH soils** (typically below the water table). SRB catalyse the cathodic reaction by consuming the atomic hydrogen film on the metal surface, depolarising the cathode and allowing further dissolution of iron. The overall corrosion reaction produces **black iron sulfide (FeS)** tubercle deposits:
**Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻** (anodic); **SO₄²⁻ + 9H⁺ + 8e⁻ → HS⁻ + 4H₂O** (cathodic), with Fe²⁺ + HS⁻ → FeS + H⁺.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question is internally optional. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. The 14 marks are typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **A (a)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
> **Discuss in detail the various soil-physicochemical factors that influence the corrosion of underground metallic structures. How are these factors measured in the field? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The corrosion of a buried metal in soil is governed by a combination of chemical, electrochemical, and physical characteristics of the surrounding soil medium.

**(1) Soil Resistivity (ρ):** This is the dominant parameter. Resistivity is determined by the ionic concentration, moisture, and temperature of the soil. Resistivity less than **1000 Ω·cm** indicates severe corrosivity. It is measured by the **Wenner 4-Pin method** (ρ = 2πaR) or the **Soil-Box method** for laboratory samples.

**(2) Soil Moisture Content (w):** Moisture acts as the solvent for the electrolyte. A minimum threshold (~10–15 %) is required for ionic conduction. Corrosion rate is maximum in the range **15–25 %** and falls in completely dry or fully waterlogged soils (oxygen starvation). Seasonal fluctuation creates wet-dry cycling that intensifies attack.

**(3) Soil pH:** Acidic soils (pH < 5.5) accelerate corrosion by dissolving the protective oxide film and increasing H⁺ availability for the cathodic reaction. Alkaline soils (pH > 9) tend to form a protective ferric-oxide/hydroxide film that passivates the metal.

**(4) Dissolved Salts:** Chloride (Cl⁻) ions penetrate the passive Fe₂O₃ film and form soluble FeCl₂ complexes, causing pitting. Sulfate (SO₄²⁻) ions sustain SRB metabolism. Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) can form protective siderite (FeCO₃) scales.

**(5) Soil Texture and Aeration:** Coarse, sandy soils allow oxygen ingress (aerobic, high Eh). Clay-rich soils retain moisture but starve the metal of oxygen (anaerobic, low Eh). This gradient is the root cause of **differential aeration cells** along a single pipeline.

**(6) Organic Matter:** Humus decomposes to release organic acids (humic, fulvic) that lower pH and chelate Fe²⁺ ions, increasing solubility and corrosion rate. However, very high organic content may also form a slightly protective layer.

**Field Measurement Techniques:**
- Resistivity → Wenner 4-pin (ASTM G57)
- pH → 1:1 soil-water slurry + pH meter (ASTM D4972)
- Moisture → gravimetric oven-drying (ASTM D2216)
- Redox potential → platinum-calomel electrode (ASTM G200)
- Salinity → electrical conductivity of saturation extract (ECe)

---

> **A (b)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
> **Explain the mechanism of differential aeration corrosion in buried pipelines with suitable sketches. What design and operational measures can minimise it? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Differential aeration corrosion occurs when **two regions of the same buried metal surface are exposed to different oxygen concentrations** in the surrounding soil electrolyte.

**Mechanism:** Consider a long steel pipe passing through zones of sandy (well-aerated) backfill and clayey (waterlogged, oxygen-starved) backfill.
- In the **oxygen-rich zone**, the metal behaves as the **cathode**:
$$ \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \longrightarrow 4\text{OH}^- $$
- In the **oxygen-starved zone**, the metal behaves as the **anode**:
$$ \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- $$

Electrons flow through the metal from anode to cathode, while ionic current is completed through the soil electrolyte. Because anodic dissolution is concentrated in the oxygen-poor zone, **deep, narrow pits develop** there, eventually perforating the pipe wall.

**Contributing factors:** non-uniform backfill, partial coating damage, root intrusion that locally consumes O₂, and seasonal water-table fluctuations.

**Preventive Measures:**
1. **Uniform, well-compacted backfill** using a single, homogeneous low-clay material (e.g., washed sand) along the entire pipe run.
2. **Protective coatings** (3LPE, FBE, coal-tar enamel) to isolate the metal from the soil electrolyte.
3. **Cathodic protection** (sacrificial Mg/Zn anodes or impressed-current Ti/MMO anodes) to drive the entire pipe surface into the immune region (E < −0.85 V vs. Cu/CuSO₄).
4. **Avoiding dissimilar-metal joints** that would set up additional galvanic macro-cells.
5. **Drainage engineering** to keep the pipe trench above the water table.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **B (a)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **Describe the mechanism of stray-current corrosion in buried metallic structures. How is it detected and mitigated? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of Stray-Current Corrosion:**
Stray currents are DC currents that flow through paths other than the intended conductor — typically the soil — and are intercepted by buried metallic structures such as pipelines, cables, and storage tanks. Common sources include:
- DC traction systems (electric trains, trams)
- DC welding equipment on construction sites
- Impressed-current cathodic-protection systems of *neighbouring* structures
- HVDC transmission systems

The buried metallic structure acts as a **low-resistance parallel path**. The current enters the structure at a *cathodic pickup point* (where the structure is at a more negative potential than the surrounding soil) and **discharges at anodic points** where the structure is positive relative to the soil. At these discharge (anodic) points, intense localised metal dissolution follows **Faraday's Law**:

$$ \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- $$

The rate of metal loss is **directly proportional to the magnitude of the discharged stray current**, and because the current density at the discharge point is extremely high, perforation can occur within **weeks to months**, even though the overall pipe-to-soil potential may appear normal elsewhere.

**Detection Methods:**
- Continuous **pipe-to-soil potential monitoring** with reference electrodes (Cu/CuSO₄) and data loggers.
- **Current measurement** using clamp-on ammeters and IR drop techniques.
- **Soil-surface potential gradient surveys** to map the direction and magnitude of stray current flow.
- **CIPS / DCVG surveys** (Close-Interval Potential Survey / Direct Current Voltage Gradient) for coating-defect correlation.

**Mitigation Measures:**
1. **Electrically bonding** the affected structure to the source's negative return (drainage bonds) — most common remedy.
2. **Forced drainage** through a rheostat or DC diode.
3. **Insulating flanges/joints** to break the electrical continuity.
4. **Coatings and Cathodic Protection** (sacrificial or impressed-current).
5. **Revised routing** of the pipeline away from DC transit corridors.
6. **Sectionalising** the pipe into electrically isolated segments.

---

> **B (b)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **Explain Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC) in underground structures. Discuss the conditions favouring SRB growth and the methods to control it. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism of MIC (SRB-type):**
Sulfate-Reducing Bacteria (SRB) — chiefly *Desulfovibrio* and *Desulfotomaculum* — are **anaerobic microorganisms** that use sulfate as their terminal electron acceptor in the metabolism of organic substrates. In the corrosion process, the SRB colonise the metal surface beneath tubercles, deposits, or disbonded coatings, creating a localised anaerobic environment even in nominally aerobic soils.

**Cathodic Depolarisation Theory (von Wolzogen Kühr & van der Vlugt):**
SRB consume the atomic hydrogen that forms on the cathodic regions of the metal surface (H_ad) and use it to reduce sulfate, producing H₂S. This **depolarises the cathode** and allows the anodic dissolution of iron to continue unabated. The overall chemistry is:

$$ \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 9\text{H}^{+} + 8e^- \xrightarrow{\text{SRB}} \text{HS}^{-} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{HS}^{-} \longrightarrow \textbf{FeS} + \text{H}^{+} $$

The characteristic **black, smelly FeS tubercle** deposit is a diagnostic fingerprint of SRB-driven MIC.

**Conditions Favouring SRB Growth:**
- **Anaerobic environment** (Eₕ < +100 mV; typically waterlogged clay soils).
- **pH range 5.5 – 9.0** (optimum 7.0–7.5).
- **Temperature 25 – 40 °C** (mesophilic); thermophilic strains up to 70 °C.
- **Presence of sulfates** (> 50 ppm) and **organic nutrients** (short-chain fatty acids).
- **Stagnant electrolyte** — flowing water inhibits biofilm attachment.

**Control Methods:**
1. **Biocides** — glutaraldehyde, isothiazolones, THPS (tetrakis-hydroxymethyl phosphonium sulfate) injected periodically.
2. **Cathodic Protection** to potentials more negative than −0.95 V (vs. Cu/CuSO₄) to suppress SRB metabolism.
3. **Coatings and pigging** to prevent biofilm attachment.
4. **Soil drainage and backfill selection** — avoid water-retaining clays; use porous sand.
5. **Periodic monitoring** — BART tests, culture-based SRB counts, ATP bioluminescence assays, and molecular (qPCR) tests.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in KTU Board Exams on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing *soil resistivity* with *soil resistance*.** They are not the same. Resistivity is an *intrinsic property* (Ω·cm) independent of geometry; resistance depends on geometry. Use ρ = 2πaR carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of *oxygen* in differential aeration cells.** The cathode is the *oxygen-rich* zone, not the *oxygen-starved* zone — a classic error that costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up chloride-induced pitting (Cl⁻ attacks the passive film) with SRB-driven MIC (anaerobic, sulfate-based).** They are different mechanisms and require different prevention.
> 4. **Not mentioning the *localisation effect* in stray-current calculations.** A 2.5 A current averaged over a 200 m pipe gives a deceptively low CR; examiners want to see "localised hot spots" mentioned.
> 5. **Writing *Nernst equation* without the natural-log or with wrong units for R and F.** Always state R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) and F = 96 485 C/mol.
> 6. **Omitting a labelled sketch** in 7-mark questions. A 30-second diagram of the differential-aeration cell or stray-current path is worth 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Factors of Underground Corrosion (Module 3)**

- ✅ Underground corrosion is an **electrochemical phenomenon** driven by soil as a heterogeneous, moist, ionically-conductive electrolyte.
- ✅ **Soil resistivity (ρ)** is the master indicator — measured by **Wenner 4-pin method** (ρ = 2πaR); lower ρ ⇒ higher corrosivity.
- ✅ Classification: **ρ < 1000 Ω·cm = Severe**, **1000–2000 = High**, **2000–5000 = Moderate**, **5000–10000 = Low**, **> 10000 = Negligible**.
- ✅ **Optimum moisture** for maximum corrosion = **15–25 %**; below 10 % or above saturation, corrosion drops.
- ✅ **Acidic soils (pH < 5.5)** are highly aggressive; **alkaline soils (pH > 9)** can passivate carbon steel.
- ✅ **Chlorides** cause pitting; **sulfates** sustain SRB; **bicarbonates** can be protective (FeCO₃ scale).
- ✅ **Differential aeration** arises when O₂ access varies along a pipeline — oxygen-poor zones become anodic and pit.
- ✅ **SRB (Desulfovibrio)** are the dominant MIC agents; they **depolarise the cathode** by consuming atomic H and reduce SO₄²⁻ to HS⁻ → black FeS deposits.
- ✅ **Redox potential (Eₕ)** is a qualitative aeration index: **Eₕ > +400 mV = aerobic**, **Eₕ < 0 mV = strongly reducing (SRB zone)**.
- ✅ **DC stray currents** from traction systems and welding discharge at anodic points on buried pipelines — can perforate in months.
- ✅ **AC interference** from HV transmission lines induces alternating corrosion effects, but less severe than DC.
- ✅ **Galvanic coupling** between dissimilar metals in the same electrolyte forms macro-cells; the more active metal (e.g., Fe vs. Cu) corrodes preferentially.
- ✅ **Temperature**: every 10 °C rise approximately **doubles** the corrosion rate (Arrhenius behaviour).
- ✅ Prevention triad: **Coatings + Cathodic Protection + Proper Backfill** — the standard engineering prescription.
- ✅ **Faraday's Law** gives the link between corrosion current and metal loss: $W = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot EW}{F}$; **CR (mm/yr) = 0.00327 · i · EW / D**.
- ✅ **Stern–Geary equation** $i_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ enables rapid *in-situ* CR estimation from polarisation-resistance measurements.

> **End of Notes — PEICT866 Module 3 — Factors Leading to Corrosion of Underground Metallic Structures**

## ⚡ SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

**Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-control technique in which the metal (the structure) to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of an electrochemical cell, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation) half-reaction responsible for metal dissolution. The electrical basis of CP rests on the principle that **when the open-circuit potential of a metal–electrolyte interface is polarized sufficiently in the negative (cathodic) direction, the net anodic current density drops to zero**, halting corrosion.

Mathematically, the **protection criterion** requires that the polarized protective potential $(E_{prot})$ measured against a stable reference electrode satisfy:

$$E_{prot} \;\leq\; E_{corr} \;-\; \eta_{a}$$

where $E_{corr}$ is the natural corrosion potential and $\eta_{a}$ is the activation (Tafel) overpotential needed to suppress the local-action current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The *electrical basis* of CP is fundamentally different from coatings. Coatings are a **physical barrier**, while CP is an **electrochemical intervention** that uses current flow to nullify the thermodynamic driving force for oxidation.

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Battery That Heals"

Imagine a metal pipeline buried in moist soil as a **leaking bucket** — every drop of water (metal ions dissolving) leaks out through tiny holes (corrosion sites). You cannot plug every microscopic hole physically. Instead, CP does something clever:

- It connects an **auxiliary anode** (a more reactive metal, or an externally powered one) to the pipeline through a **wire**, and immerses both in the **soil electrolyte**.
- This creates a **man-made galvanic cell** where the pipeline becomes the *cathode* (the "receiver" of current) and the auxiliary anode becomes the sacrificial *anode* that dissolves instead.
- In the **impressed-current version**, a **DC rectifier** (like a battery charger) pumps electrons from an inert anode (e.g., graphite, MMO) into the pipeline, artificially forcing it cathodic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of CP as a *body-guard* electron-shield. Corrosion happens when metal atoms *lose* electrons. CP *pumps* extra electrons onto the metal so strongly that atoms have no incentive to leave as ions. The current flows through the **electrolyte (soil/water)** and through the **metallic path (wire)** — completing a DC electrical circuit.

---

### 🔑 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------------|
| Faraday's Constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** ($\approx$ **96,500 C/eq**) |
| Soil resistivity (typical range) | $\rho$ | **1,000 – 50,000 Ω·cm** |
| Protection potential for steel (vs Cu/CuSO₄) | $E_{prot}$ | **−850 mV** |
| Typical CP current density (bare steel) | $i_{prot}$ | **10 – 100 mA/m²** |
| Typical CP current density (coated steel) | $i_{prot}$ | **0.01 – 1 mA/m²** |

## 📚 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### ⚙️ The Electrical Circuit Model of Cathodic Protection

A CP system is best understood as a **DC electrical circuit** with four essential branches:

1. **DC Power Source** — either a galvanic couple (sacrificial anode) or an impressed-current rectifier.
2. **Anode** — the electrode where oxidation is *deliberately* driven (corrosion is concentrated here).
3. **Electrolyte** — the conductive medium (soil, seawater, concrete pore solution) that closes the ionic circuit.
4. **Cathode (Structure)** — the protected metal, whose surface receives electrons and supports only reduction reactions.

The **four-loop relationship** governing CP is:

$$\boxed{\,I_{CP} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta E_{polarization}}{R_{circuit}}\,}$$

where $\Delta E_{polarization}$ is the driving voltage between anode and cathode potentials, and $R_{circuit}$ is the total circuit resistance (anode–soil + soil–cathode + metallic conductor resistance).

---

### 🔬 Polarization: The Core Electrochemical Phenomenon

When CP current flows, the structure's potential shifts from $E_{corr}$ toward a more negative (cathodic) value. This shift is called **cathodic polarization**, and it occurs through three mechanisms — each important for KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions:

| Polarization Type | Origin | Magnitude | Governing Equation |
|------------------|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Activation Polarization** | Charge-transfer kinetics at the metal surface | Dominant at low current densities | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i / i_0)$ |
| **Concentration Polarization** | Depletion of dissolved O₂ or buildup of reaction products near surface | Dominant at high current densities | $\eta_c = \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ |
| **Resistance (Ohmic) Polarization** | Voltage drop through electrolyte & surface films | Linear in current | $\eta_R = iR$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **Tafel equation** is the mathematical backbone of activation polarization:
>
> $$\eta_a \;=\; \beta_a \,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$
>
> where $\beta_a$ is the **Tafel slope** (typically **50–120 mV/decade** for steel) and $i_0$ is the exchange current density.

---

### 🔌 Reference Electrodes (The "Voltmeter Reference")

CP potential measurements must be made against a **stable, reversible reference electrode**. KTU expects knowledge of these:

| Reference Electrode | Half-Cell Reaction | Standard Potential (V vs SHE) |
|--------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Copper/Copper Sulfate (CSE)** | $\text{Cu}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Cu}$ | **+0.316 V** |
| **Saturated Calomel (SCE)** | $\text{Hg}_2\text{Cl}_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{Hg} + 2\text{Cl}^-$ | **+0.244 V** |
| **Silver/Silver Chloride** | $\text{AgCl} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^-$ | **+0.222 V** |
| **Zinc (seawater)** | $\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Zn}$ | **−0.763 V** |

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Gold)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = IR$ | Ohm's law for CP circuit | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | $I_{req} = A_s \cdot i_{prot}$ | Total current required | A |
| 3 | $A_s = \pi D L$ | Surface area of buried pipe | m² |
| 4 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Dwight's formula: vertical anode resistance to earth | Ω |
| 5 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2h}{L}\right]$ | Sunde's formula: vertical anode (extended) | Ω |
| 6 | $W_{anode} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot K}{u}$ | Mass of sacrificial anode required | kg |
| 7 | $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{u}{K}$ | Anode utilization factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $R_{soil} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Soil resistance for DC circuit | Ω |
| 9 | $E_{cell} = E_c - E_a$ | Driving EMF of galvanic CP | V |
| 10 | $L_{life} = \dfrac{W \cdot u}{I \cdot K}$ | Design life of sacrificial anode | years |
| 11 | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | Wenner 4-pin soil resistivity | Ω·cm |
| 12 | $i_{prot} = \dfrac{I_{total}}{A_{cathode}}$ | Average protective current density | A/m² |

Where:
- $I$ = current (A), $V$ = potential (V), $R$ = resistance (Ω)
- $\rho$ = electrolyte/soil resistivity (Ω·m)
- $L$ = anode length, $d$ = anode diameter, $h$ = depth to top of anode
- $A_s$ = surface area of structure to protect (m²)
- $K$ = anode consumption rate (kg/A·yr)
- $u$ = utilization factor (typically 0.85 for zinc, 0.50 for magnesium)
- $a$ = Wenner probe spacing (m), $R$ = measured resistance (Ω)

---

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application | Why CP? |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Oil & Gas** | Cross-country pipelines (Burhan–Chavara, etc.) | Soil resistivity variations, MIC risk |
| **Marine** | Ship hulls, offshore platforms, jetties | Aggressive Cl⁻ environment |
| **Civil** | Reinforcing steel in concrete (bridge decks, parking garages) | Chloride-induced rebar corrosion |
| **Municipal** | Water storage tanks, ductile iron water mains | Soil & water-side corrosion |
| **Power** | Underground cable sheaths, grounding grids | Stray-current interference zones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Without CP, the *economic loss* due to corrosion in India is estimated at **3–4% of GDP annually**. CP, costing typically **2–5% of the asset value**, offers one of the highest ROI corrosion-mitigation strategies.

## 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔍 Derivation 1: Protection Current Requirement from Ohm's Law

**Problem Statement:** A buried steel pipeline is 2 km long, 0.30 m diameter, in soil of resistivity **ρ = 5,000 Ω·cm**. The required protective current density is **$i_{prot}$ = 30 mA/m²**. Determine the total current required, and the driving voltage needed if the anode-to-electrolyte resistance is **0.5 Ω** and the structure-to-electrolyte resistance is **0.3 Ω**.

**Step 1 — Compute the surface area of the pipeline**

$$A_s \;=\; \pi D L \;=\; \pi \times 0.30 \,\text{m} \times 2000 \,\text{m} \;=\; 1884.96 \,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total current required (Ohm's law form for CP)**

$$I_{total} \;=\; A_s \times i_{prot} \;=\; 1884.96 \,\text{m}^2 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{A/m}^2$$

$$I_{total} \;=\; 56.55 \,\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total circuit resistance**

$$R_{circuit} \;=\; R_{anode} + R_{structure} + R_{cable} \;=\; 0.5 \,\Omega + 0.3 \,\Omega + 0.05 \,\Omega \;=\; 0.85 \,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Compute the driving voltage (rectifier output)**

$$V_{driving} \;=\; I_{total} \times R_{circuit} \;=\; 56.55 \,\text{A} \times 0.85 \,\Omega$$

$$V_{driving} \;=\; 48.07 \,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Verification / sanity check**

Since impressed-current systems typically operate at **20–60 V DC**, our answer of **~48 V** is realistic. ✔

---

### 🔍 Derivation 2: Mass of Sacrificial Zinc Anode (Galvanic CP)

**Problem Statement:** A buried tank requires a galvanic CP system using zinc anodes. The total current needed is **$I = 2.5 \,\text{A}$**. The design life is **$t = 10 \,\text{years}$**. Zinc consumption rate is **$K = 11.0 \,\text{kg/A·yr}$** and utilization factor is **$u = 0.85$**.

**Step 1 — Recall the anode-mass formula**

$$W_{anode} \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot K}{u}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values**

$$W_{anode} \;=\; \dfrac{2.5 \,\text{A} \times 10 \,\text{yr} \times 11.0 \,\text{kg/A·yr}}{0.85}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10 \times 11.0 \;=\; 275 \,\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Final division**

$$W_{anode} \;=\; \dfrac{275}{0.85} \;=\; 323.53 \,\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Practical design choice**

Round up to **325 kg total mass** and distribute among (say) **13 anodes of 25 kg each**. ✔

---

### 🔍 Derivation 3: Vertical Anode Resistance Using Dwight's Formula

**Problem Statement:** A silicon iron anode of length **L = 3 m** and diameter **d = 0.05 m** is buried vertically in soil of resistivity **ρ = 3,000 Ω·cm**. Compute anode-to-earth resistance using Dwight's formula.

**Step 1 — Convert units**

$$\rho \;=\; 3{,}000 \,\Omega\cdot\text{cm} \;=\; 30 \,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Dwight's formula**

$$R_a \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithmic term**

$$\dfrac{4L}{d} \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 3}{0.05} \;=\; 240$$

$$\ln(240) \;=\; 5.481$$

**Step 4 — Substitute**

$$R_a \;=\; \dfrac{30}{2\pi \times 3}\left[5.481 - 1\right]$$

$$R_a \;=\; \dfrac{30}{18.850} \times 4.481 \;=\; 1.5916 \times 4.481 \;=\; 7.131 \,\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**

A single anode gives **7.13 Ω** to remote earth. For a target circuit resistance of, say, **1 Ω**, we would need **~7 anodes in parallel** (since parallel resistances add reciprocally). ✔

---

### 🔍 Derivation 4: Wenner 4-Pin Soil Resistivity

**Problem Statement:** Four equally-spaced pins are placed at **a = 2.0 m** apart in a straight line. Current $I = 5\,\text{A}$ is passed through outer pins and voltage $V = 1.8\,\text{V}$ measured across inner pins. Compute soil resistivity.

**Step 1 — Measure resistance**

$$R \;=\; \dfrac{V}{I} \;=\; \dfrac{1.8}{5} \;=\; 0.36 \,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Apply Wenner formula**

$$\rho \;=\; 2\pi a R$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$\rho \;=\; 2\pi \times 2.0 \times 0.36 \;=\; 4.524 \,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Ω·cm (KTU convention)**

$$\rho \;=\; 4.524 \times 100 \;=\; 452.4 \,\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$$

**Step 5 — Classification**

A resistivity of **~450 Ω·cm** is considered **moderately corrosive soil**; CP design will need elevated current densities or anode backfill. ✔

---

### 🧾 Analytical Matrix — Galvanic vs Impressed Current CP

| Parameter | Galvanic (Sacrificial) CP | Impressed Current CP (ICCP) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Power source** | Galvanic potential difference | AC → DC rectifier |
| **Driving voltage** | 0.2 – 0.5 V | 5 – 50 V |
| **Anode material** | Zn, Mg, Al | Graphite, MMO, Si-Fe, scrap |
| **Anode consumption** | High (sacrificial) | Low (inert) |
| **Current capacity** | Low (≤ 5 A typically) | High (50+ A possible) |
| **Soil resistivity range** | Best ≤ 5,000 Ω·cm | Suitable for any ρ |
| **Economic radius** | Short (< 1 km typically) | Long (cross-country pipelines) |
| **Maintenance** | Replace anode periodically | Monitor rectifier output |
| **Stray-current interference** | Negligible | Must be managed |
| **Cost (small system)** | Lower | Higher initial |
| **Cost (large system)** | Higher (many anodes) | Lower per ampere |

## 🗺️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔧 Diagram 1: Equivalent Electrical Circuit of a CP System

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeRect[DC Rectifier or Galvanic Couple]
    nodeWire1[Connecting Cable Positive Lead]
    nodeAnode[Anode in Soil Electrolyte]
    nodeElec[Soil Electrolyte Path Ionic Conduction]
    nodePipe[Buried Pipeline Protected Cathode]
    nodeWire2[Connecting Cable Negative Lead]
    nodeRef[Reference Electrode CSE For Potential Measurement]
    nodeVM[High Impedance Voltmeter]

    nodeRect --> nodeWire1
    nodeWire1 --> nodeAnode
    nodeAnode -. Ionic current through soil .-> nodeElec
    nodeElec --> nodePipe
    nodePipe --> nodeWire2
    nodeWire2 --> nodeRect
    nodePipe -. Potential tap .-> nodeRef
    nodeRef --> nodeVM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = electronic (metallic) current flow path. Dotted arrows = ionic current flow path through the electrolyte. This closed loop is what makes CP an *electrical* technique.

### 🔧 Diagram 3: Polarization Behavior — E vs Log(i) Evans-Type Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1[Anodic Polarization Curve Steel in Soil]
    nodeP2[Natural Corrosion Potential Ecorr]
    nodeP3[Cathodic Polarization Curve O2 Reduction]
    nodeP4[Protected Potential Eprot negative of Ecorr by 250 to 300 mV]
    nodeP5[Net Anodic Current Density reduced to zero]

    nodeP1 --> nodeP2
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5
```

**Interpretation:**
- At $E_{corr}$, anodic current = cathodic current = $i_{corr}$ → corrosion occurs.
- Applying CP shifts the structure's potential negatively to $E_{prot}$.
- The intersection point of the structure's anodic curve with the new potential shows that the net anodic current is suppressed to (near) zero.

### 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define cathodic protection. State and explain the protection criteria for cathodic protection of a buried steel pipeline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cathodic protection is an electrochemical technique of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is made the cathode of a galvanic or impressed current cell so that the anodic dissolution reaction is suppressed.

**Protection Criteria (any 2 for 3 marks):**

1. **Potential Criterion:** A protective potential of at least **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄** (CSE) with the cathodic polarization present.
2. **Polarized-Shift Criterion:** A minimum negative polarization shift of **300 mV** from the natural $E_{corr}$.
3. **Current-Criterion (Tafel slope method):** The protective current density must equal or exceed the anodic Tafel current — net anodic current → 0.
4. **Statistical Criterion (100 mV shift):** A polarization shift of **100 mV** at the most anodic location, accounting for IR drop.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Differentiate between galvanic (sacrificial anode) cathodic protection and impressed current cathodic protection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Galvanic CP | Impressed Current CP |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Driving force** | Galvanic potential difference between dissimilar metals | External DC rectifier |
| **Anode** | Active metal (Zn, Mg, Al) — gets consumed | Inert (graphite, MMO, Si-Fe) |
| **Voltage** | 0.2 – 0.5 V | 5 – 50 V DC |
| **Application** | Low-resistivity soils, small structures | Long pipelines, marine, large tanks |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic anode replacement | Rectifier monitoring |

### 📘 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **electrical basis of cathodic protection** with a neat sketch of the equivalent circuit. Discuss the role of **polarization** in achieving protection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Cathodic protection is an electrical technique of corrosion control wherein the metal structure to be protected is made the cathode of an electrochemical cell.

**Electrical Circuit Equivalence:**

The CP system behaves as a **closed DC circuit** with the following elements in series:
1. **EMF source** — galvanic couple ($E_a - E_c$) OR DC rectifier.
2. **Anode-to-electrolyte resistance** $R_a$ — governed by soil resistivity and anode geometry.
3. **Electrolyte (soil) resistance** $R_s$ — ionic conduction path.
4. **Cathode (structure)-to-electrolyte resistance** $R_c$ — polarization resistance.
5. **Metallic cable resistance** $R_m$ — usually negligible.

Ohm's law applied:

$$I_{CP} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{cell} - \eta_{polarization}}{R_a + R_s + R_c + R_m}$$

**Role of Polarization:**

When the structure's surface becomes a cathode, the surface potential shifts negatively. This negative shift (cathodic polarization) drives the local-action corrosion current $i_{corr}$ down exponentially according to the **Tafel equation**:

$$\eta_a \;=\; \beta_a \,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_0}\right)$$

At sufficiently negative potential, the anodic current density of the structure approaches zero → **corrosion stops**.

The polarization arises from three contributions:
- **Activation polarization** — slow charge-transfer kinetics at the metal surface.
- **Concentration polarization** — depletion of dissolved oxygen.
- **Resistance (ohmic) polarization** — voltage drop through electrolyte and surface films.

**Sketch:** (Refer to SECTION 4 Diagram 1 above)

---

**(b)** A buried steel pipeline has the following data:

- Length $L = 1500$ m
- Diameter $D = 0.40$ m
- Soil resistivity $\rho = 4500 \,\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$
- Protective current density $i_{prot} = 40 \,\text{mA/m}^2$

Compute (i) the total current required, and (ii) the anode resistance using Dwight's formula for a single 3 m long, 50 mm diameter anode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total current required:**

$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.40 \times 1500 = 1884.96 \,\text{m}^2$$

$$I_{total} = A_s \times i_{prot} = 1884.96 \times 40 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$I_{total} = 75.40 \,\text{A}$$

**(ii) Anode resistance (Dwight's formula):**

Convert units: $\rho = 4500 \,\Omega\cdot\text{cm} = 45 \,\Omega\cdot\text{m}$

$$R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$

$$\dfrac{4L}{d} = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{0.05} = 240 \;\Rightarrow\; \ln(240) = 5.481$$

$$R_a = \dfrac{45}{2\pi \times 3}\left[5.481 - 1\right] = \dfrac{45}{18.85} \times 4.481$$

$$R_a = 2.388 \times 4.481 = 10.70 \,\Omega$$

**Conclusion:** A single anode gives too high a resistance. Multiple anodes in parallel (e.g., 10 anodes → ~1.07 Ω) are needed for practical operation.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) system**. List its advantages over galvanic CP. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ICCP Schematic:**
(Refer to SECTION 4 Diagram 2 above)

**Components:**
1. **AC source** (mains supply).
2. **Step-down transformer** (230 V → 20–60 V).
3. **Silicon rectifier** (AC → DC).
4. **Anode bed** (graphite, MMO, Si-Fe, or scrap).
5. **Anode backfill** (coke breeze for graphite) to lower contact resistance.
6. **Cable network** (anode positive, structure negative).
7. **Reference electrode** at test station.
8. **Voltmeter / data logger** for monitoring.

**Operation:**
The rectifier delivers DC current that flows from the **positive terminal** through the **anode bed** into the **soil**, then onto the **structure**, and returns through the **negative cable** to the rectifier. The structure is held at a potential more negative than $-850$ mV vs CSE.

**Advantages over Galvanic CP:**
1. **High current output** (50+ A possible) — suitable for large structures.
2. **Works in high-resistivity soils** (up to 50,000 Ω·cm).
3. **Adjustable** — rectifier output can be tuned to seasonal soil variations.
4. **Long anode life** — inert anodes last 20–40 years.
5. **Economical for long-distance pipelines** — fewer anodes needed.
6. **Continuously monitorable** — modern rectifiers have remote telemetry.

---

**(b)** Design a **galvanic cathodic protection system** using zinc anodes for an underground tank. Data:

- Total current required: $I = 3.0$ A
- Design life: $t = 15$ years
- Zinc anode consumption rate: $K = 11.0$ kg/A·yr
- Zinc utilization factor: $u = 0.85$

Compute (i) total zinc mass, (ii) number of anodes if each anode weighs **25 kg**, and (iii) verify against KTU design guidelines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total zinc mass required:**

$$W_{anode} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot K}{u} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 15 \times 11.0}{0.85}$$

$$W_{anode} = \dfrac{495}{0.85} = 582.35 \,\text{kg}$$

**(ii) Number of anodes:**

$$n = \dfrac{582.35}{25} = 23.29 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{round up to } n = 24 \,\text{anodes}$$

**(iii) Verification:**

- Design margin: $(24 \times 25 - 582.35) / 582.35 = 3.05\%$ extra → acceptable (5–10% recommended).
- Anode spacing: For 24 anodes around a tank of (say) 12 m diameter, spacing = $\pi \times 12 / 24 = 1.57$ m → suitable.
- Total anode current capacity: Each 25-kg zinc anode typically delivers **~0.15 A**, so 24 anodes → **3.6 A** capacity → **sufficient** for 3.0 A demand. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Resistivity in Ω·cm vs Ω·m is the #1 mistake. KTU loses ~2 marks per question for this.
> 2. **Mixing up the protection potential sign** — For steel vs CSE, it is **−850 mV** (negative), not +850 mV.
> 3. **Confusing Tafel slope β with activation overpotential η** — β is the slope; η is the actual voltage shift.
> 4. **Neglecting anode backfill resistance** — In graphite ICCP systems, coke breeze backfill dramatically reduces $R_a$.
> 5. **Ignoring IR-drop in field measurements** — "Instant-off" potential measurement is mandatory; including IR-drop overestimates protection.
> 6. **Design life vs utilization factor** — Don't compute life with $u=1.0$; KTU expects realistic $u$ (0.50–0.90).

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **CP = making structure cathode** by **suppressing anodic current** via **negative polarization**.
- [ ] **Two methods:** Galvanic (sacrificial anode) and Impressed Current (rectifier-driven).
- [ ] **Protection potential for steel: −850 mV vs CSE.**
- [ ] **Tafel equation:** $\eta = \beta \log(i/i_0)$ — the electrochemical heart of CP.
- [ ] **Three polarizations:** Activation, Concentration, Resistance (Ohmic).
- [ ] **Faraday's constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/eq — used in all corrosion-rate calculations.
- [ ] **Dwight's formula** for vertical anode resistance is the most-tested equation in KTU Module 3.
- [ ] **Anode mass formula:** $W = I \cdot t \cdot K / u$ — applies to sacrificial anodes.
- [ ] **Wenner 4-pin method:** $\rho = 2\pi a R$ — used to measure soil resistivity before design.
- [ ] **Reference electrodes:** CSE (field), SCE (lab), Ag/AgCl (marine), Zn (seawater).
- [ ] **Typical current densities:** Bare steel 10–100 mA/m²; coated steel 0.01–1 mA/m².
- [ ] **ICCP anodes:** Graphite, MMO (Mixed Metal Oxide), Si-Fe, high-silicon cast iron.
- [ ] **Galvanic anodes:** Mg (most negative, dry soils), Zn (seawater, low-ρ soils), Al (seawater, preferred for offshore).
- [ ] **Stray currents** from DC transit systems or HV-DC lines can cause *interference* — must be mitigated with drainage bonds.
- [ ] **Coatings + CP** is the most economical combined approach — coatings reduce current demand by 90%+.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrochemical Theory of Cathodic Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrochemical Theory of Cathodic Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion control technique in which the metal to be protected is forced to become the **cathode** of a purposely engineered electrochemical cell. This is achieved either by (a) supplying an external direct current from an impressed current source (Impressed Current Cathodic Protection — **ICCP**), or (b) connecting the metal to a more active (more negative) sacrificial metal (Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection — **SACP** / Galvanic Anode Method).

**Electrochemical Theory of Cathodic Protection** describes, in terms of mixed potential theory (Wagner–Traud), polarization kinetics, and thermodynamics, *why* forcing the open-circuit corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) of a metal to a more negative (cathodic) value eliminates the net anodic dissolution reaction, thereby achieving *immunity* from corrosion.

### 1.2 Key Technical Terms (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Open-Circuit Potential ($E_{corr}$):** The natural mixed potential of a corroding metal in a given electrolyte when no external current flows.
> - **Protection Potential ($E_{prot}$):** The most negative potential at which the anodic dissolution rate of the protected metal becomes negligibly small (≈0).
> - **Reference Electrode:** A half-cell of fixed, known potential (e.g., Saturated Calomel Electrode, Copper/Copper Sulphate) used to measure the metal's potential.
> - **Polarization:** The deviation of an electrode's potential from its equilibrium value due to a net current flow.
> - **Current Density ($i$):** Current per unit area of electrode surface, expressed in **A/m²** or **mA/cm²**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of Cathodic Protection as a "Sacrificial Guardian" for your pipeline.** Imagine an underground iron pipeline surrounded by moist, aggressive soil (an electrolyte). The iron naturally tends to lose electrons and dissolve (corrode). To stop this, we connect a more reactive metal like **magnesium** or **zinc** to the pipeline. The magnesium, being more electrochemically "generous," sacrifices itself by giving up its electrons to the iron. The iron, now flooded with electrons, no longer needs to "steal" them from itself, so it stops corroding. The magnesium corrodes *in place of* the iron. This is exactly what is meant when we say the iron is forced to become the **cathode** — it receives electrons instead of donating them.

### 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants Used in CP Theory

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Relevance |
|----------|--------|-------|-----------|
| Faraday's constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** | Relates charge to moles of electrons |
| Gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Nernst and Tafel equations |
| Absolute temperature | $T$ | **298 K (standard)** | Reference for thermodynamic potentials |
| Potential of SHE | $E^\circ_{H^+/H_2}$ | **0.000 V (definition)** | Universal reference for all potentials |
| Potential of CSE | $E_{CSE}$ | **+0.241 V vs SHE** | Common lab reference electrode |
| Potential of Cu/CuSO₄ | $E_{CSE}$ | **+0.318 V vs SHE** | Common field reference electrode |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Thermodynamic Basis — Why CP Works

The thermodynamic feasibility of any electrochemical reaction is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}}$$

At standard conditions ($T = 298\,\text{K}$, $a = 1$), the Nernst equation reduces to:

$$E = E^\circ - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log \frac{a_{red}}{a_{ox}}$$

**The Core Logic (Why):**
- For iron in neutral aerated water, $E_{corr} \approx -0.4$ to $-0.6$ V vs SHE. At this potential, the **anodic reaction** $\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^-$ is thermodynamically spontaneous.
- The **Pourbaix Diagram** for Fe–H₂O at pH 7 shows that the iron is in the *corrosion* zone at this potential.
- If we polarize the iron *cathodically* to a potential **more negative than approximately −0.62 V vs SHE** (or **−0.85 V vs Cu/CuSO₄**), the system enters the **immunity zone** where Fe is thermodynamically stable and *cannot* dissolve.
- **CP therefore shifts the operating point of the metal from the "corrosion region" to the "immune region" of the Pourbaix diagram.**

### 2.2 The Kinetic Basis — Mixed Potential Theory (Wagner–Traud, 1938)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wagner–Traud Theorem:** Any electrochemical corrosion process can be decomposed into **two or more independent oxidation and reduction reactions** occurring simultaneously on the metal surface, each following its own kinetics.

When the metal is freely corroding at $E_{corr}$:
- The rate of metal dissolution (anodic) = rate of cathodic reduction (oxygen or hydrogen evolution).
- This is shown graphically on an **Evans Diagram** (E vs log i).

**Operational logic of CP (How):**

1. **Step 1 — Natural State:** At $E_{corr}$, anodic current $i_a$ = cathodic current $i_c$ = $i_{corr}$. Metal corrodes at rate $i_{corr}$.
2. **Step 2 — Apply External Cathodic Current:** A current $I_{applied}$ is forced into the metal, making it the cathode. The metal's potential is *pushed* in the negative direction.
3. **Step 3 — Polarization of Cathodic Reaction:** The cathodic reaction rate is enhanced (oxygen reduction becomes faster), while the anodic dissolution rate is suppressed.
4. **Step 4 — Complete Protection:** When the metal is polarized to $E_{prot}$, the anodic current $i_a \rightarrow 0$. **No net metal dissolution occurs.**

### 2.3 Tafel Kinetics and Polarization

The relationship between the overpotential ($\eta$) and the current density for an electrochemical reaction is given by the **Tafel Equation**:

$$\eta = \beta \log \frac{i}{i_0}$$

Where:
- $\eta = E - E_{eq}$ = overpotential (V)
- $\beta$ = Tafel slope (V/decade), typically **0.06 to 0.12 V** for most metal/electrolyte systems
- $i$ = applied current density (A/m²)
- $i_0$ = exchange current density (A/m²)

The **cathodic polarization** of the metal surface when a CP current $I_{applied}$ flows is:

$$\Delta E = E_{corr} - E_{prot} = \beta_c \log \frac{i_{applied}}{i_{corr}}$$

### 2.4 Protection Criteria (Practical Engineering Limits)

| Criterion | Value | Reference Electrode | Notes |
|-----------|-------|---------------------|-------|
| **−850 mV** | $E \leq -0.850$ V | Cu/CuSO₄ | Most widely used CP criterion (NACE SP0169) |
| **−770 mV** | $E \leq -0.770$ V | CSE (Saturated Calomel) | Lab/bench-scale criterion |
| **300 mV Shift** | $\Delta E \geq 300$ mV cathodic | Any reference | Alternative "polarization shift" criterion |
| **−950 mV** (with H₂ risk) | $E \geq -0.950$ V | Cu/CuSO₄ | Upper limit to prevent H₂ embrittlement on high-strength steel |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Expression | Units | Use in CP Problems |
|---|----------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Current from potential | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R}$ | A | Ohm's law for circuit design |
| 2 | Current density | $i = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | A/m² | Used in Tafel and Evans analysis |
| 3 | Cathodic polarization | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log \dfrac{i_c}{i_0}$ | V | Quantifies potential shift needed |
| 4 | Anodic polarization | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log \dfrac{i_a}{i_0}$ | V | For sacrificial anode design |
| 5 | Nernst equation (298 K) | $E = E^\circ - \dfrac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$ | V | Thermodynamic feasibility |
| 6 | Corrosion rate (Faraday) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | mm/yr | Convert $i_{corr}$ to engineering units |
| 7 | Anode life | $L = \dfrac{m \cdot u}{I \cdot k}$ | years | Life of sacrificial anode |
| 8 | Resistance (Atkinson) | $R = \dfrac{0.00521 \rho}{L}\left[\ln\dfrac{8L}{d} - 1\right]$ | Ω | Soil resistance for groundbed design |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by corrosion engineers to (a) size the rectifier and anode groundbed for ICCP systems, (b) select the number and mass of sacrificial anodes for SACP, (c) estimate the design life of a CP system, and (d) interpret field potential measurements during CP surveys of pipelines, ships, and offshore platforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cathodic Polarization Required to Stop Corrosion

**Given:** A buried steel structure in soil with $i_{corr} = 10^{-4}$ A/m², anodic Tafel slope $\beta_a = 0.06$ V/dec, cathodic Tafel slope $\beta_c = 0.12$ V/dec. The oxygen reduction cathodic reaction is under activation control.

**Find:** The magnitude of applied cathodic current density required to shift the steel potential from $E_{corr}$ to a protection potential $E_{prot} = E_{corr} - 0.300$ V.

**Step 1:** Express the shift in potential as the overpotential of the cathodic reaction.

$$\Delta E_c = E_{corr} - E_{prot} = 0.300\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Tafel equation for cathodic polarization.

$$\Delta E_c = \beta_c \log \frac{i_{applied}}{i_{corr}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$0.300 = 0.12 \cdot \log \frac{i_{applied}}{10^{-4}}$$

**Step 4:** Isolate the logarithmic term by dividing both sides by 0.12.

$$\log \frac{i_{applied}}{10^{-4}} = \frac{0.300}{0.12} = 2.50$$

**Step 5:** Convert from logarithm to antilogarithm.

$$\frac{i_{applied}}{10^{-4}} = 10^{2.50} = 316.23$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the applied cathodic current density.

$$i_{applied} = 10^{-4} \times 316.23 = 3.16 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{A/m}^2 = 31.6\,\text{mA/m}^2$$

**Result:** Approximately **31.6 mA/m²** of cathodic current must be applied to achieve a 300 mV cathodic shift. This is the *minimum* current requirement for cathodic protection of this steel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Anode Life Calculation for a Sacrificial Magnesium Anode

**Given:** A magnesium sacrificial anode of mass $m = 5\,\text{kg}$ is used to protect an underground pipeline. The anode delivers a current of $I = 0.5\,\text{A}$ continuously. The utilization factor $u = 0.85$, the electrochemical capacity of magnesium $k = 1230\,\text{A·h/kg}$, and the **Faraday constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Step 1:** State the anode life equation.

$$L = \frac{m \cdot u}{I \cdot k}$$

**Step 2:** Express the electrochemical capacity in consistent units. Convert A·h to A·yr.

$$k = 1230\,\frac{\text{A·h}}{\text{kg}} \times \frac{1\,\text{yr}}{8760\,\text{h}} = 0.1404\,\frac{\text{A·yr}}{\text{kg}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$L = \frac{5 \times 0.85}{0.5 \times 0.1404}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the numerator and denominator.

$$L = \frac{4.25}{0.0702}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the result.

$$L = 60.54\,\text{years}$$

**Result:** A **5 kg** magnesium anode delivering **0.5 A** will have an estimated design life of approximately **60 years**, indicating that the system is over-sized for typical pipeline protection applications.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CP Methods

| Parameter | Sacrificial Anode (SACP) | Impressed Current (ICCP) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Driving Force** | Galvanic potential difference (Mg, Zn, Al) | External DC rectifier |
| **Typical Current** | 0.1 – 5 A per anode | 5 – 500 A per rectifier |
| **Voltage** | 0.2 – 0.5 V (cell potential) | 5 – 50 V (adjustable) |
| **Anode Material** | Mg, Zn, Al alloys | Fe-silicon, graphite, MMO, scrap |
| **Soil Resistivity Suitability** | Low to medium (< 5000 Ω·cm) | High (> 5000 Ω·cm) |
| **Cost-Effective For** | Small, well-coated structures | Large/uncoated structures |
| **Controllability** | Limited (fixed by alloy) | Excellent (variable DC output) |
| **Interference Risk** | Low | High (stray currents) |
| **Design Life** | Limited by anode mass | Limited by power supply |
| **Application** | Ship hulls, short pipelines, water heaters | Cross-country pipelines, offshore platforms |

---

### 3.4 Derivation 3: Required Cathodic Current for Complete Protection (Mixed Potential Approach)

**Given:** A corroding metal has $i_{corr} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$ A/cm². The anodic reaction is under activation control with $\beta_a = 0.10$ V/dec.

**Find:** The applied cathodic current density needed to reduce the corrosion rate to 1% of its original value (i.e., $i_a^{new} = 10^{-7}$ A/cm²).

**Step 1:** Write the anodic Tafel equation for both states.

$$E_{corr} - E_{eq,a} = \beta_a \log \frac{i_{corr}}{i_{0,a}}$$

$$E_{prot} - E_{eq,a} = \beta_a \log \frac{i_a^{new}}{i_{0,a}}$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the two equations to eliminate $E_{eq,a}$ and $i_{0,a}$.

$$E_{corr} - E_{prot} = \beta_a \log \frac{i_{corr}}{i_a^{new}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$E_{corr} - E_{prot} = 0.10 \cdot \log \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-7}} = 0.10 \cdot \log 100 = 0.10 \times 2 = 0.20\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4:** The corrosion rate has been reduced to 1% of its original value with a cathodic polarization of only **0.20 V (200 mV)**.

**Step 5:** The corresponding applied current density is found from the cathodic Tafel equation. The net cathodic current must equal the original $i_{corr}$ plus the suppressed anodic current.

$$i_{applied} \approx i_{corr} - i_a^{new} = (1 \times 10^{-5}) - (1 \times 10^{-7}) = 9.9 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A/cm}^2$$

**Result:** A cathodic polarization of **200 mV** is sufficient to reduce the corrosion rate to 1%, requiring a current density of approximately **9.9 × 10⁻⁶ A/cm²**. This is the theoretical basis for the commonly adopted **"300 mV shift"** CP criterion — it ensures even tighter suppression.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
