## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Evans Diagram — Effect of Cathodic Protection on Corrosion Kinetics

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Metal Surface"] --> nodeB["Anodic Reaction Fe to Fe2+"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Cathodic Reaction O2 Reduction"]
    nodeD["External DC Source"] --> nodeE["Anode Groundbed"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Electrolyte Soil"]
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeG["Negative Terminal Cathode"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Positive Terminal"]
    nodeI["Log Current Density Axis"] --> nodeJ["E corr Natural Potential"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["E prot Protection Potential"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Complete Protection Region"]
```

**Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**
- The **DC source** injects electrons into the protected structure via the negative terminal.
- The structure is forced to behave as a **cathode** (electron-consuming site).
- Electrons travel through the **electrolyte** (soil/water) to the **anode groundbed**, where oxidation (anode dissolution) occurs.
- On the **E vs log i** axis, the operating point moves from $E_{corr}$ downward to $E_{prot}$, suppressing the anodic current to near zero.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ICCP System

```mermaid
graph TD
    sourceA["AC Power Supply 230 V Mains"] --> rectA["Step Down Transformer"]
    rectA --> rectB["Silicon Diode Rectifier Bridge"]
    rectB --> rectC["Filter and Smoothing Capacitor"]
    rectC --> rectD["Variable DC Output 0 to 50 V"]
    rectD --> cableA["Positive Cable Red"]
    rectD --> cableB["Negative Cable Black"]
    cableA --> anodeA["Impressed Current Anode Fe-Si MMO"]
    anodeA --> soilA["Soil Electrolyte"]
    soilA --> pipeA["Protected Pipeline Structure"]
    pipeA --> cableB
    refA["Reference Electrode Cu CuSO4"] --> potA["Potential Measurement"]
    pipeA --> potA
    potA --> ctrlA["Controller for Auto Adjustment"]
    ctrlA --> rectD
```

**Block Functionality:**
1. **AC Supply + Transformer** — Steps down mains voltage to rectifier-compatible level.
2. **Rectifier Bridge** — Converts AC to DC (single-phase full-wave or three-phase).
3. **Filter Circuit** — Smooths the DC ripple to produce a steady current.
4. **Variable Output Stage** — Allows the operator to adjust the DC voltage (5–50 V) and current (5–500 A) to maintain $E_{prot}$.
5. **Anode Groundbed** — Where the impressed current enters the electrolyte; designed for low resistance and long life.
6. **Reference Electrode Feedback** — Continuously measures $E_{structure}$ and feeds the controller to maintain the set potential.
7. **Controller** — Automatically adjusts rectifier output to compensate for seasonal soil resistance variations.

---

### 4.3 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    pipeB["Protected Steel Pipeline"] --> wireB["Connection Wire"]
    wireB --> sacA["Magnesium Anode Active"]
    sacA --> soilB["Soil Electrolyte"]
    soilB --> pipeB
    refB["Reference Electrode"] --> potB["Potential Monitoring"]
    pipeB --> potB
    sacA -->|"Electron flow"| pipeB
    pipeB -->|"No Fe2+ release"| resB["Pipeline Remains Immune"]
```

**Process Logic:** The **magnesium anode** (more active metal) spontaneously corrodes and releases electrons, which flow through the wire to the steel pipeline. The pipeline, now receiving excess electrons, cannot dissolve — it is *thermodynamically forced into the immune region* of its Pourbaix diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: Define Cathodic Protection. State the two methods of implementing it.**

**Model Answer:**
Cathodic Protection is an electrochemical technique used to control the corrosion of a metal surface by making it the cathode of an electrochemical cell. This is achieved by supplying electrons to the metal, thereby suppressing its anodic dissolution reaction. The two methods are: **(i) Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP / Galvanic Method)** — using a more active metal like zinc, magnesium, or aluminium as the anode; and **(ii) Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** — using an external DC power source (rectifier) to drive current from an inert anode to the protected structure.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a protection potential? State its value for steel with respect to a Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode.**

**Model Answer:**
The protection potential ($E_{prot}$) is the most negative potential to which a metal must be polarized for its corrosion rate to become negligibly small (anodic current $\rightarrow 0$). For carbon steel in soil/water, the standard protection potential is **−0.850 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode (as per NACE standard). At potentials more negative than this, the iron lies in the *immune region* of the Fe–H₂O Pourbaix diagram.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q (a)** Explain the **electrochemical theory** of cathodic protection with the help of a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of mixed potential theory in achieving protection. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A buried steel pipeline is to be protected using an impressed current cathodic protection system. The pipeline has a surface area of $5000\,\text{m}^2$ exposed to the soil. The required cathodic current density is $20\,\text{mA/m}^2$. The soil resistivity is $4000\,\Omega\cdot\text{cm}$. Calculate **(i)** the total current required, and **(ii)** the rectifier voltage if the circuit resistance is $2.5\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Electrochemical Theory of Cathodic Protection:**
Cathodic protection is based on **Wagner–Traud's mixed potential theory**, which states that the corrosion of a metal in an electrolyte results from at least two simultaneous electrochemical reactions: an anodic metal dissolution reaction and one or more cathodic reduction reactions (such as oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution). At the open-circuit corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$), the rate of the anodic reaction equals the rate of the cathodic reaction, and this common rate is the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$.

**Step 2 — Application of Cathodic Current:**
In cathodic protection, an external current is supplied to the metal, which polarizes it in the **negative (cathodic) direction**. This external current is added to the natural cathodic current, while the anodic current is correspondingly suppressed. As the metal potential is shifted more negative, the rate of the anodic dissolution reaction decreases exponentially according to the Tafel equation:

$$i_a = i_{corr} \cdot 10^{\frac{E_{prot} - E_{corr}}{\beta_a}}$$

**Step 3 — Achievement of Complete Protection:**
When the potential is reduced to the **protection potential** ($E_{prot}$), the anodic current density approaches zero, and the corrosion rate becomes negligible. Thermodynamically, the metal has been shifted from the *corrosion region* into the *immune region* of its Pourbaix diagram, where the metal is the stable species.

**Step 4 — Role of Mixed Potential Theory:**
Mixed potential theory provides the *mathematical and graphical framework* (Evans diagram) to predict:
- The natural $E_{corr}$ and $i_{corr}$ from the intersection of anodic and cathodic polarization curves.
- The required external current to achieve a desired $E_{prot}$.
- The current distribution and overpotential of both reactions.
- The interference effects from neighboring structures.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Thus, the electrochemical theory explains that cathodic protection works by *kinetically suppressing* the anodic reaction and *thermodynamically stabilizing* the metal through controlled polarization.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:**
- Surface area, $A = 5000\,\text{m}^2$
- Required current density, $i = 20\,\text{mA/m}^2 = 0.020\,\text{A/m}^2$
- Circuit resistance, $R = 2.5\,\Omega$

**Part (i): Total current required**

$$I_{total} = i \times A = 0.020 \times 5000 = 100\,\text{A}$$

**Part (ii): Rectifier voltage**

Using Ohm's law:

$$V = I \times R = 100 \times 2.5 = 250\,\text{V}$$

**Result:** A rectifier capable of delivering **100 A at 250 V DC** is required. In practice, an oversized rectifier (e.g., 150 A, 300 V) would be selected to allow margin for aging and seasonal variations.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** With the help of a **Pourbaix diagram for iron**, explain how cathodic protection shifts the iron from the corrosion zone to the immunity zone. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A magnesium sacrificial anode of mass **8 kg** is connected to a steel structure. The electrochemical capacity of magnesium is **1230 A·h/kg** and the utilization factor is **0.85**. If the anode delivers a steady current of **0.4 A**, estimate the **design life in years**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Introduction to Pourbaix Diagram:**
The Pourbaix diagram for Fe–H₂O at 25 °C plots the equilibrium potential (E in V vs SHE) on the y-axis versus pH on the x-axis. The diagram is divided into three principal regions: **immunity** (where Fe is thermodynamically stable), **corrosion** (where soluble Fe²⁺ or Fe³⁺ species are stable), and **passivity** (where insoluble Fe₂O₃ or Fe₃O₄ forms a protective film).

**Step 2 — Corrosion Zone:**
At neutral pH (≈7) and the natural corrosion potential of iron in aerated water ($E_{corr} \approx -0.4$ to $-0.5$ V vs SHE), iron lies in the **corrosion zone** of the Pourbaix diagram. The dominant species is Fe²⁺ (aq), and the metal continuously dissolves.

**Step 3 — Effect of Cathodic Polarization:**
When cathodic protection is applied, the iron's potential is shifted to a more negative value. At a pH of 7, if the potential is reduced below **approximately −0.62 V vs SHE** (the lower boundary of the H⁺/H₂ line at pH 7, calculated from the Nernst equation), the system enters the **immunity zone**. In this region, the thermodynamically stable form of iron is the **elemental metal (Fe°)**, which means the metal does not dissolve.

**Step 4 — Quantitative Verification via Nernst Equation:**
The boundary between immunity and corrosion at pH 7 corresponds to the equilibrium:

$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{Fe}, \quad E^\circ = -0.44\,\text{V vs SHE}$$

Applying the Nernst equation with $[\text{Fe}^{2+}] = 10^{-6}$ M (the conventional "zero corrosion" concentration):

$$E = -0.44 - \frac{0.0591}{2} \log \frac{1}{10^{-6}} = -0.44 - 0.177 = -0.617\,\text{V vs SHE}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
CP therefore forces the iron to a potential more negative than **−0.617 V vs SHE** (or **−0.850 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** in field practice), placing the metal in the immune zone. This is the **thermodynamic basis** of cathodic protection.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Given:**
- Mass of Mg anode, $m = 8\,\text{kg}$
- Electrochemical capacity, $k = 1230\,\text{A·h/kg}$
- Utilization factor, $u = 0.85$
- Current, $I = 0.4\,\text{A}$

**Step 1 — State the anode life formula.**

$$L = \frac{m \cdot u}{I \cdot k}$$

**Step 2 — Express the current in A·h per year.**

$$I_{annual} = 0.4\,\text{A} \times 8760\,\text{h/yr} = 3504\,\text{A·h/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total available capacity of the anode.**

$$\text{Total capacity} = m \times k = 8 \times 1230 = 9840\,\text{A·h}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the utilization factor.**

$$\text{Usable capacity} = 9840 \times 0.85 = 8364\,\text{A·h}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the design life.**

$$L = \frac{8364}{3504} = 2.387\,\text{years}$$

**Result:** The magnesium sacrificial anode will have a design life of approximately **2.4 years** under continuous operation at 0.4 A. Periodic anode replacement would be required at this interval.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in CP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing signs of potential:** CP requires polarization to a *more negative* potential. Students often write the criterion with the wrong sign (e.g., "more positive than −0.85 V") and lose full marks.
> 2. **Mixing reference electrodes:** Switching between SHE, CSE, and Cu/CuSO₄ without conversion factors is a major error. Remember: $E_{CSE} = E_{SHE} + 0.241$ V, and $E_{Cu/CuSO_4} = E_{SHE} + 0.318$ V.
> 3. **Wrong Tafel slope usage:** Using $\beta_a$ instead of $\beta_c$ in cathodic polarization problems, or vice versa. Always match the slope to the reaction being polarized.
> 4. **Forgetting units in the anode life calculation:** Converting current from A to A·h using 8760 h/yr is essential; using 24 (hours/day) or 365 (days/year) is a frequent error.
> 5. **Confusing "corrosion rate" and "corrosion current density":** They are related through Faraday's law, but the question must be read carefully to determine which is being asked.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrochemical Theory of Cathodic Protection:**
> - **CP shifts the metal from the corrosion zone to the immune zone of the Pourbaix diagram.**
> - The protection potential for steel is **−0.850 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** (most common field criterion).
> - Alternative criterion: a **300 mV cathodic shift** from the native $E_{corr}$.
> - **Two methods:** SACP (galvanic, e.g., Mg/Zn/Al anodes) and ICCP (rectifier-driven, inert anodes).
> - **Mixed potential theory** (Wagner–Traud) is the kinetic foundation of CP.
> - **Tafel equation** quantifies the polarization: $\eta = \beta \log(i/i_0)$.
> - **Sacrificial anodes** must be more active (more negative $E^\circ$) than the protected metal: e.g., Mg ($E^\circ = -2.37$ V), Zn ($E^\circ = -0.76$ V), Al ($E^\circ = -1.66$ V).
> - **Anode life formula:** $L = \dfrac{m \cdot u}{I \cdot k}$ — where $k$ for Mg is **1230 A·h/kg**, Zn is **820 A·h/kg**, and Al is **2980 A·h/kg**.
> - **SACP** is suited for low-resistivity soils (< 5000 Ω·cm) and small structures; **ICCP** is used for large structures and high-resistivity environments.
> - **Upper potential limit:** $E \geq -0.95$ V vs Cu/CuSO₄ for high-strength steel to avoid hydrogen embrittlement.
> - **Overprotection hazards:** excessive current can cause (a) hydrogen embrittlement, (b) coating disbondment, and (c) stray current interference on neighboring structures.
> - **Reference electrodes** used in CP: Cu/CuSO₄ (field), Ag/AgCl/seawater (marine), Saturated Calomel (lab), Zn (seawater permanent reference).
> - **Current requirement** depends on coating quality: bare steel ≈ 10–20 mA/m², well-coated steel ≈ 0.01–0.1 mA/m².
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anodic Polarisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anodic Polarisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anodic Polarisation** is defined as the displacement of an electrode's potential in the **positive (more noble / more oxidising) direction** from its equilibrium (open-circuit) value, caused by the passage of a net anodic (oxidation) current across the electrode–electrolyte interface.

In electrochemical corrosion terminology, anodic polarisation is the phenomenon where the **anode in a corrosion cell becomes more positive (nobler)** than its reversible potential $E_{M/M^{n+}}$ due to sluggishness (kinetic hindrance) of the metal dissolution reaction. This is one of the two fundamental half-cell polarisation phenomena studied under the **Evans Diagram / Mixed Potential Theory** (Wagner–Traud, 1938).

Mathematically, the anodic overpotential is expressed as:

$$\eta_a = E_{applied} - E_{eq(a)}$$

where:
- $E_{applied}$ = the actual (polarised) anodic potential, **V vs SHE**
- $E_{eq(a)}$ = the equilibrium anodic potential (Nernst potential), **V vs SHE**
- $\eta_a$ = anodic overpotential, **V (always positive for a true anodic process)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Polarisation (Polarization)** in corrosion science is the general term for any deviation of an electrode's potential from its equilibrium value under the influence of an external current. The term *"polarisation"* is the **British/Indian spelling** (IUPAC-preferred) and is used throughout the KTU 2024 syllabus. The American spelling *"polarization"* is the same concept.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a ball (representing an electron or a metal ion) sitting in a valley — this is its **equilibrium state**. To push the ball out of the valley, you must apply some force; the bigger the push you need, the more the system is "polarised."

> **Real-World Analogy — The Leaky Tap Analogy:**
> Think of a water tap that is partially clogged by rust. When you turn it ON, water (electrons / current) cannot flow freely. The pressure behind the blockage **builds up** — this "extra pressure" needed to force water through is exactly like anodic polarisation. The metal (anode) wants to dissolve (oxidise), but the sluggish kinetics create a "back-pressure" of potential, forcing the anode potential to drift in the **positive (noble) direction**.
>
> • If the tap (reaction) is sluggish → **high polarisation** → the system protects itself
> • If the tap is wide open → **low polarisation** → corrosion proceeds rapidly
> • If rust completely blocks the tap → **passivation** → corrosion almost stops

---

### Standard Physical Constants & Key Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Constants (always required in KTU derivations):**
> - Faraday constant: $F = 96{,}485 \ \text{C mol}^{-1}$
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \ \text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
> - Standard temperature: $T = 298 \ \text{K}$ (25 °C), giving $RT/F = 0.02569 \ \text{V}$ and $2.303 \, RT/F = 0.0592 \ \text{V}$

**Three canonical metrics** in any polarisation study:
1. **Exchange Current Density ($i_o$)** — A · cm⁻² — the equal forward/reverse current at equilibrium
2. **Tafel Slope ($\beta$)** — V / decade — the slope of the polarisation curve in the Tafel region
3. **Polarisation Resistance ($R_p$)** — Ω · cm² — the slope of the linear polarisation region near $E_{corr}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Principal Causes of Anodic Polarisation

When an anodic current is forced through a metal–electrolyte interface, the metal dissolution reaction

$$M \longrightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

encounters one or more kinetic barriers. Each barrier contributes a component to the total anodic polarisation:

**(i) Activation Polarisation (Charge-Transfer Control) — $\eta_{a,act}$**
- The slowest step is the **transfer of metal ions across the electrical double layer**.
- This is the most fundamental cause; it is always present to some extent.
- Governed by the **Butler–Volmer equation** at low overpotentials and **Tafel equation** at high overpotentials.
- Typical $\beta_a$ values: **0.03 V to 0.15 V per decade** of current.

**(ii) Concentration Polarisation (Mass-Transport Control) — $\eta_{a,conc}$**
- The metal ions that dissolve cannot diffuse away fast enough, so their concentration at the metal surface builds up.
- The electrode potential shifts positive (Nernstian shift) in response to this higher surface concentration.
- Dominant near the **limiting current density ($i_L$)**.

**(iii) Resistance (Ohmic) Polarisation — $\eta_{a,IR}$**
- Caused by **current flowing through a resistive surface film** (e.g., porous oxide, salt film, scale).
- Mathematically: $\eta_{IR} = i \cdot R_f$, where $R_f$ is the film resistance in Ω · cm².
- Important in **passive systems** with thick or semi-conducting films (e.g., $Al_2O_3$, $TiO_2$).

**(iv) Passivation Polarisation — $\eta_{a,pass}$**
- A special and technologically vital case: at a critical anodic current density ($i_{pass}$ or $i_{crit}$), a **thin, adherent, and electronically insulating oxide film** spontaneously forms on the surface.
- The anodic current **drops by 2–6 orders of magnitude** to a low passive current density ($i_{passive}$).
- This is the basis for **stainless steels, anodising of Al/Ti, and chemical passivation treatments**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total anodic overpotential is the SUM of all contributing overpotentials:**
> $$\eta_a = \eta_{a,act} + \eta_{a,conc} + \eta_{a,IR} + \eta_{a,pass}$$

---

### 2.2 Mixed Potential Theory & The Evans Diagram

When two dissimilar electrochemical reactions occur simultaneously on a single metal surface (the essence of corrosion):

1. **Anodic half-reaction:** $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ (oxidation of metal)
2. **Cathodic half-reaction:** e.g., $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ or $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ (reduction of an oxidiser)

According to **Wagner and Traud (1938)**, at the open-circuit corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$), the anodic current density must **exactly equal** the cathodic current density (charge conservation / no net external current):

$$i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$$

The plot of anodic and cathodic polarisation curves on the same **potential (E) vs log current density (log i)** axes is the famous **Evans Diagram** (named after Ulick R. Evans). The intersection point gives:
- **x-coordinate → $i_{corr}$** (corrosion current density, proportional to corrosion rate)
- **y-coordinate → $E_{corr}$** (corrosion / mixed potential)

---

### 2.3 The Three Classical Control Regimes

The *steepness* of each half-cell polarisation curve determines which reaction controls the rate:

| Control Type | Dominant Polarisation | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Anodic Control** | $\beta_a$ large; anodic curve is steep | Active metal (Zn, Mg) in aerated acid |
| **Cathodic Control** | $\beta_c$ large; cathodic curve is steep | Steel in deaerated acidic solution (H₂ evolution sluggish) |
| **Mixed / Resistance Control** | Both $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ comparable; or significant $R_f$ | Most engineering corrosion situations |

> **KTU Favourite Tip:** When a polarisation diagram question gives only ONE curve and asks the type of control — if the diagram shows the anodic curve being steeper (larger Tafel slope) than the cathodic curve, the system is under **anodic control**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities must be expressed in SI-consistent units. Marks are deducted for unit omissions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_a = E - E_{eq}$ | Definition of anodic overpotential | V |
| 2 | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i / i_o)$ | **Tafel equation (anodic)** | V |
| 3 | $\eta_a = (2.303 \, RT / \alpha n F) \log_{10}(i / i_o)$ | Tafel equation in fundamental form | V |
| 4 | $\beta_a = 2.303 \, RT / \alpha n F$ | Anodic Tafel slope definition | V / decade |
| 5 | $i = i_o [\exp(\alpha n F \eta_a / RT) - \exp(-(1-\alpha) n F \eta_a / RT)]$ | **Butler–Volmer equation** (general) | A · m⁻² |
| 6 | $i_L = n F D C / \delta$ | Limiting current density (concentration polarisation) | A · m⁻² |
| 7 | $\eta_{conc} = (2.303 \, RT / n F) \log_{10}[i_L / (i_L - i)]$ | Concentration overpotential | V |
| 8 | $\eta_{IR} = i \cdot R_f$ | Ohmic drop through surface film | V |
| 9 | $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$ | Mixed potential condition | A · m⁻² |
| 10 | $E_{corr} = E_{eq,a} + \beta_a \log(i_{corr}/i_{o,a}) = E_{eq,c} - \beta_c \log(i_{corr}/i_{o,c})$ | Evans diagram intersection equations | V |
| 11 | $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ (Stern–Geary) where $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ | Corrosion rate from linear polarisation | A · m⁻² |
| 12 | $\text{CR} = (K \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW) / \rho$ | Faraday's law of corrosion rate | mm/yr or mpy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEE Notation Convention (mandatory in KTU scripts):** Use *KEE* = Kinetic, Electrochemical, and Engineering (the three pillars of corrosion rate analysis). When writing $i_{corr}$, the subscripts $o,a$ and $o,c$ refer to the **exchange current densities of the anodic and cathodic half-reactions respectively** — never to a single value.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The study of anodic polarisation is the foundation of:
- **Cathodic & Anodic Protection design** (Sacrificial anode and ICCP systems)
- **Anodising of aluminium and titanium** (decorative + hard-anodic coatings)
- **Selection of inhibitor type** — anodic inhibitors (e.g., chromates, nitrites, molybdates) shift the anodic Tafel line
- **Design of corrosion-resistant alloys** (Cr, Mo additions push the alloy into the passive region)
- **Electrochemical machining & electrorefining** — where high anodic currents are used productively
- **Battery & fuel cell electrode design** — the same Tafel and Butler–Volmer laws govern kinetics
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Anodic Tafel Equation (Butler–Volmer → Tafel High-Field Limit)

**Starting Point — The Single-Step Electrode Kinetics:**

For the elementary charge-transfer reaction $M \rightleftharpoons M^{n+} + ne^-$, the **net anodic current density** is the difference between the forward (oxidation) and reverse (reduction) contributions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= i_{forward} - i_{reverse} \\
i &= i_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{+\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(1-\alpha) n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **transfer coefficient** (typically 0.5 for a symmetric energy barrier; dimensionless, $0 < \alpha < 1$)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred in the rate-determining step
- $i_o$ = exchange current density (A · m⁻²)

---

**Step 1 — Apply the High-Anodic-Overpotential Approximation**

When $\eta_a \gg RT/F$ (i.e., $\eta_a \gtrsim 50 \ \text{mV}$ at 25 °C), the second exponential term becomes negligibly small (the reverse reaction is effectively suppressed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp\!\left(\dfrac{+\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) \gg \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(1-\alpha) n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) \approx 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the Butler–Volmer equation simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &\approx i_o \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right) &= \dfrac{\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Solve for $\eta_a$ Explicitly**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Convert $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ Using $2.303 \log_{10}(x) = \ln(x)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha n F} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Define the Anodic Tafel Slope $\beta_a$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_a &\equiv \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha n F} \\
\therefore \ \eta_a &= \beta_a \, \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the famous Tafel Equation (1905),** the cornerstone of anodic polarisation analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check at 25 °C (T = 298 K):**
> For $\alpha = 0.5$ and $n = 1$:
> $\beta_a = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298 / (0.5 \times 1 \times 96485) = 0.118 \ \text{V/decade} = 118 \ \text{mV/decade}$
> This matches the typical measured Tafel slope of hydrogen on iron (~0.12 V) and is a value the KTU examiner loves to test.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stern–Geary Linear Polarisation Equation

**Starting Point — Total Polarisation Resistance $R_p$:**

Near $E_{corr}$, the polarisation curve is approximately linear. The slope $dE/di$ at $i = i_{corr}$ is the **polarisation resistance $R_p$**.

Differentiating the Tafel expressions for both anodic and cathodic branches and adding them (using $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$ at the mixed potential):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_p = \left.\dfrac{d\eta}{di}\right|_{i=i_{corr}} &= \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate $i_{corr}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{corr} &= \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Stern–Geary Constant $B$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\equiv \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 (\beta_a + \beta_c)} \quad \text{(typical range 0.013 to 0.052 V)} \\
\therefore \quad i_{corr} &= \dfrac{B}{R_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the Stern–Geary equation (1957)** — it allows rapid, non-destructive measurement of corrosion rate using only small (≤ 10 mV) polarisation perturbations, and is the theoretical basis of **LPR (Linear Polarisation Resistance) probes** used in oil-and-gas, marine, and chemical-plant corrosion monitoring.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Identifying the Dominant Polarisation from a Given $i_o$ and $\beta$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A common KTU 14-mark problem format:** *"Two metals M1 and M2 are corroding in the same electrolyte. Given $i_{o,1}$, $\beta_{a,1}$, $i_{o,2}$, $\beta_{a,2}$, determine which is more corrosion-resistant and quantify the anodic polarisation at a specified anodic current density."*

**Solution Framework (use these steps for full marks):**

1. **Identify $E_{eq}$** from Nernst equation (or given directly).
2. **Apply the Tafel equation** $\eta_a = \beta_a \log(i/i_o)$ for the chosen current density.
3. **Compute $E_{polarised} = E_{eq} + \eta_a$** (potential shift is positive for anodic).
4. **Compare across materials** — larger $\eta_a$ at the same $i$ means greater anodic kinetic hindrance and thus *lower* corrosion susceptibility.
5. **Cross-check using Evans diagram** — verify graphically that the anodic line is steeper (more polarisation) for the more resistant material.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Sub-Part)

> **Problem:** The anodic polarisation of mild steel in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C obeys the Tafel equation with $\beta_a = 0.10 \ \text{V/decade}$ and $i_o = 10^{-6} \ \text{A/cm}^2$. Find (a) the anodic overpotential at $i = 10^{-3} \ \text{A/cm}^2$, and (b) the polarised anodic potential if $E_{eq} = -0.44 \ \text{V vs SHE}$.

**Solution (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_a &= \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right) \\
&= 0.10 \times \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{10^{-3}}{10^{-6}}\right) \\
&= 0.10 \times \log_{10}(10^{3}) \\
&= 0.10 \times 3 \\
&= 0.30 \ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{applied} &= E_{eq} + \eta_a \\
&= -0.44 + 0.30 \\
&= -0.14 \ \text{V vs SHE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The mild-steel anode has shifted from its reversible potential of **−0.44 V** to a polarised value of **−0.14 V** under the anodic current of $10^{-3} \ \text{A/cm}^2$. This 0.30 V noble shift is the entire anodic polarisation — diagnostic of **activation-controlled** dissolution (no concentration or passivation effects are present in this case, since the problem gives only a single Tafel slope).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — The Anodic Polarisation Phenomenon (Master Mermaid Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Metal Anode M"]:::root --> B["Apply Anodic Current i_a"]:::input
    B --> C{"Which Barrier Dominates?"}:::decision

    C --> C1["Activation Polarisation\nCharge transfer across EDL"]:::barrier
    C --> C2["Concentration Polarisation\nM^n+ cannot diffuse away"]:::barrier
    C --> C3["Resistance Polarisation\nCurrent through oxide film"]:::barrier
    C --> C4["Passivation\nInsulating oxide film forms"]:::barrier

    C1 --> D1["Eta_a,act = (2.303 RT / alpha n F) log i/ i_o"]:::formula
    C2 --> D2["Eta_a,conc = (2.303 RT / n F) log i_L / (i_L - i)"]:::formula
    C3 --> D3["Eta_a,IR = i times R_f"]:::formula
    C4 --> D4["i drops to i_passive (orders of magnitude)"]:::formula

    D1 --> E["Total Anodic Overpotential Eta_a = Sum of components"]:::sum
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F["Polarised Anode Potential E_applied = E_eq + Eta_a"]:::result
    F --> G["Plot on Evans Diagram - intersection gives E_corr and i_corr"]:::outcome

    G --> H1["Anodic Control: large Eta_a, steep anodic Tafel line"]:::control
    G --> H2["Cathodic Control: anodic Tafel line is shallow"]:::control
    G --> H3["Mixed Control: both slopes comparable"]:::control

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef input fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    classDef barrier fill:#ef4444,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    classDef formula fill:#a855f7,stroke:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
    classDef sum fill:#14b8a6,stroke:#14b8a6,color:#ffffff
    classDef result fill:#22c55e,stroke:#22c55e,color:#ffffff
    classDef outcome fill:#eab308,stroke:#eab308,color:#000000
    classDef control fill:#64748b,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.2 Evans Diagram — Mixed Potential & Corrosion Current Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X["E V vs SHE vertical axis"]
        direction TB
    end
    subgraph Y["log i A cm2 horizontal axis"]
        direction TB
    end

    A1["E_eq,a = -0.76 V\nfor Zn anode"]:::anode --> B1["Anodic Tafel line\nslope Beta_a"]:::anode
    A2["E_eq,c = +0.40 V\nfor O2 / H+ cathode"]:::cathode --> B2["Cathodic Tafel line\nslope Beta_c"]:::cathode

    B1 --> P["Intersection Point\nE_corr and log i_corr"]:::intersect
    B2 --> P
    P --> R["E_corr approx -0.65 V\ni_corr approx 10-5 A cm2"]:::readout

    classDef anode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    classDef cathode fill:#2563eb,stroke:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef intersect fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    classDef readout fill:#facc15,stroke:#facc15,color:#000000
```

---

### 4.3 The Anodic Polarisation Curve (Activation → Concentration → Limiting Current)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Region I: Linear Activation\nEta_a = Beta_a log i / i_o\nSlope Beta_a on E vs log i"]:::region1
    B["Region II: Tafel Line\nPure charge-transfer control\nNo concentration effects"]:::region2
    C["Region III: Concentration Polarisation\nCurvature due to mass transport limit"]:::region3
    D["Region IV: Limiting Current Density i_L\nEta_a increases sharply\nM n+ cannot diffuse faster"]:::region4
    E["Region V: Passivation\nSudden drop to i_passive\nOxide film formed"]:::region5

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E

    classDef region1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000
    classDef region2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000
    classDef region3 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000
    classDef region4 fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#d97706,color:#000000
    classDef region5 fill:#10b981,stroke:#10b981,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.4 Decision Logic: Identifying the Type of Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Plot Anodic and Cathodic\nTafel Lines on Evans Diagram"]:::start --> Check1{"Which line is\nsteeper?"}:::decision

    Check1 -->|"Anodic steeper"| AnodicControl["ANODIC CONTROL\nAnode reaction is sluggish\nIncreasing anodic polarisation\nslows corrosion"]:::typeA
    Check1 -->|"Cathodic steeper"| CathodicControl["CATHODIC CONTROL\nCathode reaction is sluggish\nCathodic polarisation is the limit"]:::typeB
    Check1 -->|"Equal slopes"| MixedControl["MIXED CONTROL\nBoth reactions contribute\nMost common in practice"]:::typeC
    Check1 -->|"Both lines displaced\nupward by same amount"| ResistanceControl["RESISTANCE CONTROL\nIR drop through film or\nevaporating electrolyte"]:::typeD

    classDef start fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    classDef typeA fill:#ef4444,stroke:#ef4444,color:#ffffff
    classDef typeB fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    classDef typeC fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#8b5cf6,color:#ffffff
    classDef typeD fill:#64748b,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
> **Q1. Define anodic polarisation. What is the sign convention for anodic overpotential?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Anodic polarisation is the shift of an electrode's potential in the **positive (more noble) direction** from its equilibrium value due to the flow of an anodic current (1 mark). It arises from kinetic hindrances such as activation, concentration, and resistance barriers (1 mark). The anodic overpotential is defined as $\eta_a = E_{applied} - E_{eq}$, and is **always positive** when the electrode is the anode (1 mark).

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
> **Q2. State and explain the Tafel equation for anodic polarisation.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Tafel equation (1 mark) is $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i / i_o)$, where $\eta_a$ is the anodic overpotential, $\beta_a = 2.303 RT / \alpha n F$ is the anodic Tafel slope, $i$ is the applied anodic current density, and $i_o$ is the exchange current density (1 mark). It is valid in the **high-overpotential region** (typically $\eta_a > 50 \ \text{mV}$) where the reverse reduction reaction is negligible (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Tafel equation for anodic polarisation from the Butler–Volmer equation. State the underlying assumptions and the significance of the transfer coefficient $\alpha$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Butler–Volmer Starting Point (1 mark):** The net current density for a single-step charge-transfer reaction is

$$
i = i_o \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{+\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-(1-\alpha) n F \eta_a}{RT}\right) \right]
$$

2. **High-Overpotential Assumption (1 mark):** When $\eta_a \gg RT/F$ (i.e., $\eta_a > 50 \ \text{mV}$), the second term in the brackets becomes negligible ($\sim 10^{-5}$ times smaller), so

$$
i \approx i_o \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}\right)
$$

3. **Take Natural Logarithm (1 mark):**

$$
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right) = \dfrac{\alpha n F \eta_a}{RT}
$$

4. **Solve for $\eta_a$ (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_a = \dfrac{RT}{\alpha n F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right)
$$

5. **Convert to $\log_{10}$ and Define $\beta_a$ (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_a = \underbrace{\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha n F}}_{\beta_a} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right)
$$

6. **Assumptions (1 mark):** (i) Single-step, one-electron transfer; (ii) symmetric energy barrier; (iii) high overpotential so reverse reaction is suppressed; (iv) isothermal, isobaric conditions; (v) no mass-transport effects.

7. **Significance of $\alpha$ (1 mark):** $\alpha$ (transfer coefficient) is the fraction of the applied potential that *helps* the forward (anodic) reaction. It lies between 0 and 1; for a symmetric barrier $\alpha = 0.5$. Physically, it tells us *how the activation energy changes with potential* — a high $\alpha$ means the anodic reaction is highly sensitive to potential.

---

**(b)** With the help of a labelled Evans diagram, explain the concepts of **anodic control** and **cathodic control**. How does the exchange current density $i_o$ affect the corrosion rate? **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Evans Diagram Description (2 marks):** A plot of potential (E vs SHE, y-axis) versus $\log_{10}(i)$ (x-axis). The anodic Tafel line rises from $E_{eq,a}$ with slope $+\beta_a$, and the cathodic Tafel line falls from $E_{eq,c}$ with slope $-\beta_c$. Their intersection gives the **mixed (corrosion) potential $E_{corr}$** and the **corrosion current density $i_{corr}$**.

2. **Anodic Control (1.5 marks):** When the anodic Tafel line is **steeper** than the cathodic line, the corrosion rate is limited by the **anodic (metal-dissolution) kinetics**. Increasing the anodic overpotential is the only way to increase current. Examples: active metals (Zn, Mg) in aerated neutral solutions.

3. **Cathodic Control (1.5 marks):** When the cathodic Tafel line is **steeper**, the corrosion rate is limited by the **cathodic (oxygen-reduction or hydrogen-evolution) kinetics**. Examples: iron and steel in deaerated acids, copper in aerated pure water.

4. **Effect of $i_o$ (2 marks):** The exchange current density measures the **kinetic facility** of a half-reaction at equilibrium.
   - **High $i_o$** → facile reaction → small polarisation needed to sustain a given current → **higher $i_{corr}$** → higher corrosion rate.
   - **Low $i_o$** → sluggish reaction → large polarisation needed → **lower $i_{corr}$** → lower corrosion rate.
   - For noble metals (Pt, Au): $i_o$ is very high → reaction is kinetically effortless → the metal stays near its equilibrium potential.
   - For passive metals (Al, Ti, Cr in oxidising media): the *anodic* $i_o$ is very low → corrosion is kinetically inhibited.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B Marks Distribution):**
> - **1 mark lost** for not stating the *sign convention* of $\eta_a$ (must be positive for an anodic process).
> - **1 mark lost** for using $E - E_{eq}$ without explicitly defining which electrode is the reference.
> - **1 mark lost** for not converting $\ln$ to $\log_{10}$ and thereby failing to obtain the **2.303 factor**.
> - **1 mark lost** for not specifying the *validity range* of the Tafel equation (high-$\eta$ limit; $\eta > 50 \ \text{mV}$).
> - **1 mark lost** for describing Evans diagram in words but **omitting the intersection interpretation** ($i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$).
> - **Common trap:** Students confuse $\alpha$ (transfer coefficient, dimensionless) with $\beta$ (Tafel slope, in V/decade) — they are *not* the same and must be defined separately for full marks.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks)**

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Explain the **four types of anodic polarisation** with suitable diagrams and equations. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Activation Polarisation (1.5 marks):** Slowest step is the **charge transfer of metal ions across the electrical double layer**. Quantified by the **Butler–Volmer equation** (general) and the **Tafel equation** (high-$\eta$ limit):

$$
\eta_{a,act} = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_o}\right)
$$

2. **Concentration Polarisation (1.5 marks):** Arises when metal ions accumulate at the anode surface because the rate of **mass transport (diffusion + convection)** to the bulk is slower than the rate of dissolution. The anodic overpotential grows as the current approaches the **limiting current density $i_L$**:

$$
\eta_{a,conc} = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{n F} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i_L}{i_L - i}\right)
$$

3. **Resistance (Ohmic) Polarisation (1.5 marks):** Caused by current passing through a **resistive film** (porous oxide, scale, salt film, or even the bulk electrolyte). Modeled as a simple ohmic drop:

$$
\eta_{a,IR} = i \cdot R_f
$$

4. **Passivation (2.5 marks):** At a critical anodic potential (the **Flade potential**, $E_F$), a thin, adherent, electronically insulating oxide film (typically 1–10 nm thick) forms spontaneously. The current density drops by **3 to 6 orders of magnitude** from the **critical current density $i_{crit}$** to the **passive current density $i_{passive}$**. The anodic polarisation in this regime is the **Flade overpotential**:

$$
\eta_{a,pass} = E - E_F \quad \text{(for } E > E_F\text{)}
$$

Diagram: Show the anodic polarisation curve with characteristic peaks: active peak at $i_{crit}$, active–passive transition, passive plateau at $i_{passive}$, and transpassive breakdown at higher potentials. (A complete table or labelled sketch is expected for the 2.5 marks.)

---

**(b)** Derive the **Stern–Geary equation** and discuss its application in **Linear Polarisation Resistance (LPR) corrosion monitoring**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Differentiate Total Polarisation Curve Near $i_{corr}$ (2 marks):** Near $E_{corr}$, the total polarisation $\eta$ has contributions from both anodic and cathodic branches. The slope of the net $E$–$i$ curve at $i = i_{corr}$ is the **polarisation resistance**:

$$
R_p = \left.\dfrac{d\eta}{di}\right|_{i = i_{corr}}
$$

2. **Express $R_p$ in Terms of Tafel Slopes (2 marks):** Adding the anodic and cathodic contributions:

$$
R_p = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, i_{corr} (\beta_a + \beta_c)}
$$

3. **Rearrange to Solve for $i_{corr}$ (1 mark):**

$$
i_{corr} = \dfrac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 \, R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)} = \dfrac{B}{R_p}
$$

where $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303 (\beta_a + \beta_c)]$ is the **Stern–Geary constant** (typically **13 to 52 mV** for most metal–electrolyte systems).

4. **Application in LPR Monitoring (2 marks):** A small polarisation (typically $\pm 10 \ \text{mV}$) is applied around $E_{corr}$, and the resulting current is measured. The slope $\Delta E / \Delta i$ at zero current gives $R_p$ directly, and the corrosion rate is obtained non-destructively. LPR probes are widely used in **oil pipelines, chemical reactors, marine platforms, and rebar-in-concrete** monitoring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Confusion Alert:** Students often write $B = (\beta_a + \beta_c) / (2.303 \beta_a \beta_c)$ — this is the **reciprocal** of the correct expression. Memorise correctly.
> - **Unit Alert:** $\beta_a$ and $\beta_c$ are in **V/decade**, not V. If you forget the 2.303 factor, units break.
> - **Validity Range:** Stern–Geary is valid only in the **linear polarisation region** ($\eta \leq 20 \ \text{mV}$). It fails in the Tafel region.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Commit to memory before every KTU attempt:**

- **Definition (must write verbatim):** Anodic polarisation = displacement of electrode potential in the **positive (noble) direction** due to anodic current.
- **Sign convention:** $\eta_a = E - E_{eq}$ is **always positive** for an anodic process. (NEVER negative — that's a cathodic overpotential.)
- **Tafel Equation (commit):** $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i / i_o)$ and $\beta_a = 2.303 \, RT / (\alpha n F)$.
- **Validity range:** Tafel equation valid for $\eta > 50 \ \text{mV}$ (high-field approximation). For $\eta < 10 \ \text{mV}$, use **linear polarisation** and Stern–Geary.
- **Four causes of anodic polarisation** — memorise in order: **A**ctivation, **C**oncentration, **R**esistance, **P**assivation. Mnemonic: **"A-C-R-P → Anodic Current Resistance/Polarisation"**.
- **Evans Diagram construction:** Anodic line rises from $E_{eq,a}$ with slope $+\beta_a$; Cathodic line falls from $E_{eq,c}$ with slope $-\beta_c$. Intersection = $E_{corr}$ and $i_{corr}$.
- **Mixed potential condition:** $i_a = i_c = i_{corr}$ at $E_{corr}$ (charge conservation, Wagner–Traud postulate).
- **Stern–Geary:** $i_{corr} = B / R_p$ where $B = \beta_a \beta_c / [2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)]$. B is typically **13–52 mV**.
- **Exchange current density meaning:** Large $i_o$ ⇒ facile kinetics ⇒ more corrosion (or more electrode activity). Small $i_o$ ⇒ sluggish ⇒ less corrosion.
- **Control type mnemonic:** *Steeper line = the one doing the controlling*. Anodic steep ⇒ Anodic control. Cathodic steep ⇒ Cathodic control.
- **Total anodic overpotential is additive:** $\eta_a = \eta_{act} + \eta_{conc} + \eta_{IR} + \eta_{pass}$ (this additive rule is a frequent KTU short-answer favourite).
- **Passivation signature:** Critical current $i_{crit}$ → Flade potential $E_F$ → Passive plateau $i_{passive}$ → Transpassive breakdown. The fall from $i_{crit}$ to $i_{passive}$ spans **3 to 6 decades** of current.
- **Faraday's law (final conversion):** $\text{CR (mm/yr)} = 0.00327 \times i_{corr}(\mu A/cm^2) \times EW / \rho$ — used in numerical problems.
- **Always state units** — V for potentials, A/cm² for current densities, mV/decade for Tafel slopes. The KTU examiner deducts ½ mark per missing unit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cathodic Protection System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Cathodic Protection System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion control technique in which the metal structure to be protected is deliberately made the **cathode** of a controlled galvanic (or electrolytic) cell, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation/dissolution) half-reaction that constitutes metal loss. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Verbatim Concept):** Cathodic Protection is a method of corrosion control in which the potential of a metallic structure is shifted in the **active (negative) direction** with respect to a suitable reference electrode (e.g., Cu/CuSO₄, Ag/AgCl, Zn/ZnSO₄) so that the entire structure resides within the immunity region of the Pourbaix diagram. Mathematically, the principle can be represented as: $$\text{If } E_{\text{structure}} \leq E_{\text{protection}} \Rightarrow \text{Metal is thermodynamically immune}$$ ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: "The Sacrificial Lamb Principle"** > Imagine a herd of sheep (your metal pipeline) being attacked by wolves (corrosion). You tie a louder, more aggressive sheepdog (sacrificial anode) to the herd. The wolves attack the sheepdog first — and since the sheepdog is "tastier" (more active in the galvanic series), the herd survives. > > In **Impressed Current CP (ICCP)**, instead of a sheepdog, you install an electric fence powered by a battery. The fence pushes electrons INTO the herd, so the wolves (corrosion) cannot grab electrons from your sheep at all. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | | Faraday's Constant (F) | **96,485 C/mol** | | Protection Potential (Steel in soil) | **-850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄** | | Negative Shift Criterion (E-off) | **≥ 100 mV** | | Typical Current Density (Steel in soil) | **10–30 mA/m²** | | Standard Soil Resistivity | **1000–10000 Ω·cm** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principle (The Electrochemical Why) A corroding metal in an electrolyte has two simultaneous reactions: **Anodic (oxidation):** $$\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$$ **Cathodic (reduction):** $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^- \quad \text{(aerated)}$$ **The Mixed Potential (E_corr)** lies between E_a and E_c. By **injecting external electrons** into the metal, we: 1. Polarize it in the **cathodic direction** (E_shift becomes more negative). 2. Force the anodic current density to **zero** (no metal dissolution). 3. Push the system into the **immunity zone** of the E-pH diagram. ## 2.2 The Two Strategic Variants of Cathodic Protection ### A) Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) / Galvanic Anode CP - **Driver:** The natural potential difference between two dissimilar metals. - **Anode material** must be **more active (more negative)** than the structure. - **No external power** is needed. **Mechanism (Step-by-step):** 1. Sacrificial anode (e.g., Zn) has a more negative open-circuit potential than steel. 2. When both are electrically connected and immersed in an electrolyte, electrons flow spontaneously: **Anode → Steel**. 3. The protected steel becomes a cathode; oxidation is "transferred" to the zinc. 4. The Zn anode **gradually dissolves** and must be replaced periodically. **Common Anode Materials for SACP:** | Anode | Open-Circuit Potential vs CSE | Practical Capacity (A·h/kg) | Best For | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Magnesium (Mg) | -1.55 V | **1100** | High-resistivity soils, freshwater | | Zinc (Zn) | -1.10 V | **780** | Seawater, low-resistivity soils | | Aluminum (Al) | -1.05 V | **2300** | Seawater, offshore structures | ### B) Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) - **Driver:** An external DC power source (rectifier or solar panel). - **Anode material** is **inert** (does not dissolve; only conducts current into the electrolyte). - Used for **large structures** and **high-resistivity environments**. **Mechanism:** 1. A DC source forces current from an **inert auxiliary anode** → electrolyte → protected structure → back to source. 2. The protected structure receives electrons continuously, suppressing anodic dissolution. 3. Anode lifetime is **very long** (only the inert anode slowly degrades). **Common ICCP Anode Materials:** | Anode | Composition | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Graphite | Carbon | Cheap, brittle | | High-Silicon Cast Iron (HSCI) | Fe-14%Si-3%Mo | Most common in soil | | Mixed Metal Oxide (MMO/Ti) | Ti substrate + IrO₂/Ta₂O₅ coating | Long life, high current | | Platinized Titanium | Ti + Pt coating | Premium, marine use | ## 2.3 Cathodic Protection Criteria (KTU High-Yield) | Criterion | Value | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Absolute potential (steel) | $-850$ mV vs Cu/CuSO₄ | Industry standard | | 100 mV polarisation shift | $\geq 100$ mV decay | "Off" potential criterion | | 300 mV formation shift | $\geq 300$ mV | E-on minus E-corr | | Tafel slope limit | E-log i curve | Lab verification | ## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **master formula block** for Module 3 numerical problems. Memorise these. | S.No. | Formula | Description | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $I = i \times A$ | Total current = current density × area | A, mA/m², m² | | 2 | $I_a = \dfrac{\Delta E}{R_T}$ | Anode current output (Ohm's law) | A, V, Ω | | 3 | $R_T = R_a + R_c + R_w$ | Total circuit resistance | Ω | | 4 | $R_v = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$ | Dwight's formula — vertical anode | Ω, Ω·cm, cm | | 5 | $R_h = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2h}{L} \right]$ | Sunde's formula — horizontal anode | Ω | | 6 | $N = \dfrac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_{\text{anode}}}$ | Number of anodes required | dimensionless | | 7 | $L_a = \dfrac{W \times C \times U}{8760 \times I_a}$ | Anode life in years | years | | 8 | $W = \dfrac{I_a \times t \times M}{n \times F}$ | Mass of metal consumed (Faraday's law) | kg | | 9 | $C = \dfrac{26.8 \times n \times 1000}{M}$ | Theoretical electrochemical capacity | A·h/kg | | 10 | $V = \rho \times i$ | Ohm's law in electrolyte (voltage drop) | V | Where: - $\rho$ = electrolyte resistivity (Ω·cm) - $L$ = anode length (cm), $d$ = anode diameter (cm), $h$ = depth to centre (cm) - $U$ = anode utilisation factor (typically **0.75–0.90**) - $C$ = anode capacity (A·h/kg) - $W$ = anode mass (kg) - $M$ = atomic mass, $n$ = valency, $F$ = 96,485 C/mol - $\Delta E = E_a - E_{\text{protection}}$ (driving voltage) ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cross-country pipelines** (oil, gas, water) — ICCP with HSCI anodes. - **Marine structures** — jetties, offshore platforms (SACP with Al anodes). - **Storage tank bottoms** — SACP ribbon anodes in impressed mode. - **Reinforced concrete** — Ti-MMO ribbon anodes in bridge decks. - **Ship hulls** — ICCP with Pb or Pt/Ti anodes. > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule of Thumb:** Coating + CP is the **industry benchmark** for buried pipelines — coating reduces current demand by **>90%**, and CP handles coating defects (holidays). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Anode Life from Faraday's Law (KTU Frequently Asked) **Starting principle:** The mass of metal dissolved at an anode is directly proportional to the total charge passed. $$m = \frac{I \times t \times M}{n \times F}$$ Now we want the **lifetime** in years. A practical anode has an **efficiency / utilization factor** $U$ (e.g., 0.85 for Zn) — meaning only 85% of the anode mass is actually consumable before mechanical failure. The **consumable mass** is: $$W_{\text{eff}} = W \times U$$ The **charge delivered** over time $t$ (in hours) is: $$Q = I \times t = I \times 8760 \times L_a$$ where $L_a$ is the life in years and 8760 is hours per year. Equating: $$W \times U = \frac{I \times 8760 \times L_a \times M}{n \times F}$$ Solving for $L_a$: $$L_a = \frac{W \times U \times n \times F}{I \times 8760 \times M}$$ Or equivalently, using **theoretical capacity** $C = \frac{n \times F \times 1000}{M \times 3600}$ expressed in A·h/kg: $$L_a = \frac{W \times C \times U}{8760 \times I_a}$$ > [!NOTE] > The "**8760**" comes from the hours in a year: $24 \times 365 = 8760$ h/yr. This is a constant that KTU examiners love to test. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** A 5 km long buried steel pipeline (diameter 30 cm) requires cathodic protection. The soil resistivity is 5000 Ω·cm, the design current density is 20 mA/m², and the protection potential is -0.85 V (CSE). Zinc anodes (size: 5 cm × 5 cm × 60 cm; mass 10 kg; utilization 0.85; OCP -1.10 V) are to be used. Calculate: - (a) Total current required - (b) Total circuit resistance assuming anode-to-electrolyte is dominant - (c) Current output per anode - (d) Number of anodes required - (e) Anode life ### Part (a): Total Current Surface area of pipe: $$A = \pi \times D \times L = \pi \times 0.30 \times 5000 = 4712.4 \text{ m}^2$$ Total current: $$I = i \times A = 0.020 \times 4712.4 = 94.25 \text{ A}$$ ### Part (b): Anode-to-Electrolyte Resistance (Vertical Anode — Dwight) Given: $\rho = 5000$ Ω·cm, $L = 60$ cm, $d = \sqrt{5^2 + 5^2}/\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 5$ cm (equivalent diameter of square section). For a square cross-section, the **equivalent diameter** for current flow is taken as the side length or $1.13 \times$ side — we will use $d = 5$ cm for simplicity. $$R_v = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$ Substituting: $$\frac{8L}{d} = \frac{8 \times 60}{5} = 96$$ $$\ln(96) = 4.564$$ $$R_v = \frac{5000}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 60}\left[4.564 - 1\right]$$ $$R_v = \frac{5000}{376.99}\times 3.564 = 13.27 \times 3.564$$ $$R_v \approx 47.3 \text{ Ω}$$ ### Part (c): Current per Anode Driving voltage: $$\Delta E = E_{\text{anode}} - E_{\text{protection}} = 1.10 - 0.85 = 0.25 \text{ V}$$ (Note: All values vs CSE; we use the **magnitude** of the potential difference.) Current per anode: $$I_a = \frac{\Delta E}{R_v} = \frac{0.25}{47.3} = 5.29 \text{ mA}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** If the resistance R_v comes out in tens of Ω and ΔE is 0.25 V, the per-anode current is in **milliampere** range. This is the standard magnitude for SACP — students who get amperes have made a unit error. ### Part (d): Number of Anodes $$N = \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_a} = \frac{94.25}{0.00529} \approx 17{,}816 \text{ anodes}$$ > [!NOTE] > This huge number is precisely why SACP is **economically unviable** for long pipelines. **ICCP is the engineering answer** here, because inert anodes deliver **amperes**, not milliamperes. ### Part (e): Anode Life For zinc: theoretical capacity $C \approx 820$ A·h/kg, with utilization $U = 0.85$: $$L_a = \frac{W \times C \times U}{8760 \times I_a} = \frac{10 \times 820 \times 0.85}{8760 \times 0.00529}$$ $$L_a = \frac{6970}{46.34} \approx 150 \text{ years}$$ (Long life is expected because per-anode current is small.) ## 3.3 SACP vs ICCP — KTU Comparative Analysis Matrix | Parameter | SACP (Galvanic) | ICCP (Impressed) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | External power | **Not required** | **Required (rectifier)** | | Driving voltage | ~0.25 V (fixed) | Adjustable 0–50 V | | Current per anode | mA range | A range | | Anode life | Limited (consumable) | Very long (inert) | | Best for | Small, low-resistivity | Large, high-resistivity | | Cost per ampere | High | Low | | Stray current risk | None | **High** (must manage) | | Reversibility (cut off) | Automatic | Manual switch-off | | Monitoring | Easy (OCP) | Needs reference electrodes | | Typical application | Ship hulls, short pipes | Long pipelines, tanks | ## 3.4 Key Auxiliary Concepts (High-Yield Definitions) **Reference Electrodes (Half-Cells):** - **Copper/Copper Sulfate (CSE):** +0.316 V vs SHE — used in **soil**. - **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl):** +0.222 V vs SHE — used in **seawater**. - **Saturated Calomel (SCE):** +0.241 V vs SHE — laboratory. - **Zinc/Zinc Sulfate:** -0.762 V vs SHE — portable field use. **Backfill Material (for SACP in soil):** A moist, low-resistivity mixture placed around the anode to: 1. Lower anode-to-soil resistance. 2. Keep the anode moist and active. 3. Typical composition: **75% gypsum (CaSO₄) + 20% bentonite + 5% Na₂SO₄**. **Stray Current Corrosion:** Current from an ICCP system that escapes the intended path and accelerates corrosion on **unrelated** nearby structures (e.g., neighbouring pipelines, railway tracks). Mitigated by **drainage bonds** and **insulated flanges**. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) — Electron & Ion Flow ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Protected Steel Structure
CATHODE"] -- electrons flow in --> nodeE["Connecting Wire
cable"] nodeE -- electrons supplied from --> nodeB["Sacrificial Anode
Zn or Mg or Al
ANODE"] nodeB -- Zn dissolves and releases Zn2 plus --> nodeC["Electrolyte
Soil or Seawater
ionic conduction"] nodeC -- ions migrate towards cathode --> nodeA nodeA -- reduction reaction OH minus forms --> nodeC nodeStyle[Surface
coating]:::coating nodeA -.- nodeStyle classDef coating fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP) — System Architecture ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["AC Power Supply
230 V mains"] -- step down --> nodeB["Transformer"] nodeB -- low voltage AC --> nodeC["Rectifier Unit
converts AC to DC"] nodeC -- positive terminal --> nodeD["Auxiliary Inert Anode
HSCI or MMO or Pt Ti"] nodeD -- current injected into --> nodeE["Electrolyte
soil or water"] nodeE -- current collected by --> nodeF["Protected Structure
Pipeline or Tank
CATHODE"] nodeF -- negative cable return --> nodeC nodeG["Reference Electrode
Cu CuSO4"] -.- |"monitors potential"| nodeF classDef source fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px classDef anode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px classDef cathode fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px classDef monitor fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,stroke-width:1px class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC source class nodeD anode class nodeF cathode class nodeG monitor ``` ## 4.3 Cathodic Protection Decision Flowchart (Choosing SACP vs ICCP) ```mermaid graph TD nodeStart["Start
Identify structure to protect"] --> nodeQ1{"Structure size
and current demand?"} nodeQ1 -- "Small, low current
lt 5 A total" --> nodeQ2{"Electrolyte
resistivity?"} nodeQ1 -- "Large, high current
gt 5 A total" --> nodeICCP["Use ICCP
Rectifier driven"] nodeQ2 -- "Low resistivity
lt 3000 Ohm cm
seawater soil" --> nodeSACP1["Use SACP
Zn or Al anode"] nodeQ2 -- "High resistivity
gt 3000 Ohm cm" --> nodeQ3{"Available power
source?"} nodeQ3 -- "Yes" --> nodeICCP nodeQ3 -- "No remote site" --> nodeSACP2["Use SACP
Mg anode
high driving voltage"] nodeSACP1 --> nodeEnd["Final Design"] nodeSACP2 --> nodeEnd nodeICCP --> nodeEnd ``` ## 4.4 Hybrid System — Coating Plus Cathodic Protection (Industry Standard) ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Outer Protective Coating
Fusion Bonded Epoxy or 3LPE"] --> nodeB["Steel Pipe Surface"] nodeB --> nodeC["Coating Defect
Holiday
small bare area"] nodeC -- localised corrosion risk --> nodeD["Cathodic Protection System
supplies protective current"] nodeD -- current density at defect --> nodeE["Polarised to negative 0.85 V
corrosion arrested"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** > **Define cathodic protection. List the two main methods used to implement it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Cathodic protection is an electrochemical method of corrosion control in which the metal to be protected is made the cathode of a galvanic or electrolytic cell, so that the anodic dissolution reaction is suppressed. The two main methods are: 1. **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** — uses a more active metal like Zn, Mg, or Al as the anode. 2. **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** — uses an external DC source with an inert anode. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark each for the two methods.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** > **State the cathodic protection criterion for carbon steel in soil. Name the reference electrode used.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The cathodic protection criterion for carbon steel buried in soil is that the **polarised pipe-to-soil potential** must be **more negative than −850 mV** with respect to a **Copper/Copper Sulfate (Cu/CuSO₄) reference electrode**. An alternative acceptable criterion is a **negative polarisation shift of at least 100 mV** from the natural corrosion potential. *(Mark distribution: 2 marks for -850 mV + 1 mark for CSE reference.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]* > **Q A(a). [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)**. List the components used and state the function of each. **Model Answer:** **Working Principle:** In ICCP, a DC power source (rectifier) is used to force a continuous protective current from an **inert auxiliary anode**, through the **electrolyte (soil/water)**, to the **structure to be protected (cathode)**. The protected structure is connected to the **negative terminal** of the DC source, while the inert anode is connected to the **positive terminal**. This causes the structure's potential to shift in the negative direction, suppressing the anodic reaction. (4 marks) **Components:** (3 marks) | Component | Function | | :--- | :--- | | **DC Rectifier** | Converts AC mains to regulated DC output (0–50 V, 0–100 A). | | **Auxiliary Anode** (HSCI / MMO / Pt-Ti) | Inert; conducts current into the electrolyte. | | **Reference Electrode** (Cu/CuSO₄) | Senses pipe-to-soil potential for monitoring. | | **Cables** | Carry current; anode cable (+) and cathode cable (−). | | **Junction Box / Test Station** | Allows periodic potential and current measurement. | --- > **Q A(b). [7 marks]** A buried steel tank (diameter 10 m, length 20 m) is to be protected by ICCP. The design current density is 15 mA/m² and the soil resistivity is 4000 Ω·cm. The selected impressed current anodes are **silicon cast iron**, each of length 1.5 m and diameter 15 cm. If the protection potential is −0.85 V (CSE) and the anode operating potential is +1.50 V (CSE), calculate: **(i) total current required, (ii) resistance of one vertical anode, (iii) current output per anode, and (iv) number of anodes required.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Total Current Required** Surface area of the cylindrical tank (lateral): $$A = \pi \times D \times L = 3.1416 \times 10 \times 20 = 628.32 \text{ m}^2$$ Total current: $$I = i \times A = 0.015 \times 628.32 = 9.42 \text{ A} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 2: Resistance of One Vertical Anode (Dwight's Formula)** $$R_v = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$ Given: $\rho = 4000$ Ω·cm, $L = 150$ cm, $d = 15$ cm: $$\frac{8L}{d} = \frac{8 \times 150}{15} = 80$$ $$\ln(80) = 4.382$$ $$R_v = \frac{4000}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 150}\left[4.382 - 1\right] = \frac{4000}{942.48} \times 3.382$$ $$R_v = 4.244 \times 3.382 = 14.35 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$ **Step 3: Current Output per Anode** $$\Delta E = E_{\text{anode}} - E_{\text{protection}} = 1.50 - (-0.85) = 2.35 \text{ V}$$ (Anode potential is positive; structure is polarised to -0.85 V; the driving voltage is the difference.) $$I_a = \frac{\Delta E}{R_v} = \frac{2.35}{14.35} = 0.164 \text{ A} = 164 \text{ mA} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$ **Step 4: Number of Anodes Required** $$N = \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_a} = \frac{9.42}{0.164} \approx 58 \text{ anodes} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$ (Suggest providing a small safety margin, e.g., **60 anodes**.) --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]* > **Q B(a). [7 marks]** Compare **Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)** and **Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)** under the following heads: principle, anode material, driving voltage, current per anode, applications, and limitations. **Model Answer:** (Tabular comparison – 1 mark per head × 6 heads ≈ 6 marks + 1 mark for neatness/justification) | Head | SACP | ICCP | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Principle** | Uses natural galvanic potential difference | Uses external DC source | | **Anode material** | Active (Zn, Mg, Al alloys) | Inert (HSCI, MMO, Pt-Ti, graphite) | | **Driving voltage** | Fixed ~0.25 V | Adjustable 0–50 V | | **Current per anode** | mA range (low) | A range (high) | | **Applications** | Ship hulls, small tanks, low-resistivity soils | Long pipelines, large storage tanks, marine platforms | | **Limitations** | Low driving V, anode consumption, not for high-ρ soils | Requires power, risk of stray currents, higher maintenance | --- > **Q B(b). [7 marks]** A 2 km long, 25 cm diameter buried water pipeline in soil of resistivity 2500 Ω·cm requires CP. Magnesium alloy anodes (size 7.5 cm × 7.5 cm × 35 cm; mass 5 kg; utilisation 0.5; capacity 1230 A·h/kg) are available. If the design current density is 25 mA/m² and the protection potential is −0.85 V (CSE) with anode OCP of −1.55 V, calculate the **(i) total current**, **(ii) anode-to-soil resistance** (vertical), **(iii) current per anode**, **(iv) anode life** in years. **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Total Current** $$A = \pi \times D \times L = 3.1416 \times 0.25 \times 2000 = 1570.8 \text{ m}^2$$ $$I = 0.025 \times 1570.8 = 39.27 \text{ A} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$ **Step 2: Vertical Anode Resistance** Equivalent diameter $d \approx 7.5$ cm; $L = 35$ cm; $\rho = 2500$ Ω·cm. $$\frac{8L}{d} = \frac{8 \times 35}{7.5} = 37.33$$ $$\ln(37.33) = 3.619$$ $$R_v = \frac{2500}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 35}\left[3.619 - 1\right] = \frac{2500}{219.91} \times 2.619$$ $$R_v = 11.37 \times 2.619 = 29.78 \text{ Ω} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$ **Step 3: Current per Anode** $$\Delta E = 1.55 - 0.85 = 0.70 \text{ V}$$ $$I_a = \frac{0.70}{29.78} = 0.0235 \text{ A} = 23.5 \text{ mA} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$ **Step 4: Anode Life** $$L_a = \frac{W \times C \times U}{8760 \times I_a} = \frac{5 \times 1230 \times 0.5}{8760 \times 0.0235} = \frac{3075}{205.86}$$ $$L_a \approx 14.94 \text{ years} \approx 15 \text{ years} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 8760 factor** in anode life calculations — examiners **deduct 1 full mark**. > 2. **Confusing potential polarities**: Anode OCP is negative (e.g., Zn = -1.10 V), but in anode-resistance calculations, you use the **magnitude** of the difference. > 3. **Using the wrong Dwight formula** (horizontal vs vertical) — read the question carefully; "laid horizontally" → Sunde's formula. > 4. **Skipping the area calculation** for cylindrical structures — they often want $\pi D L$, not $\pi r^2$. > 5. **Mixing up CSE and SCE potentials** — convert if the question gives a different reference. > 6. **Forgetting the utilisation factor** $U$ — anode life without $U$ is 1 mark penalty. > 7. **Not converting units** — ρ in Ω·cm but L, d, h also in cm (consistent). Marks lost for mixing Ω·m and cm. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cathodic Protection — Rapid-Fire Revision:** > - ✅ CP shifts metal potential **negatively** into the **immunity region** of the E-pH diagram. > - ✅ Protection criterion for steel: **−850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄** (or 100 mV polarisation shift). > - ✅ **SACP** uses active anodes (Zn, Mg, Al); no external power; current in **mA**. > - ✅ **ICCP** uses inert anodes (HSCI, MMO, Pt-Ti) + DC rectifier; current in **A**. > - ✅ Driving voltage $\Delta E = E_a - E_c$ (magnitude); current $I = \Delta E / R$. > - ✅ **Dwight's formula** for vertical anode: $R_v = (\rho/2\pi L)[\ln(8L/d) - 1]$. > - ✅ **Sunde's formula** for horizontal anode: $R_h = (\rho/2\pi L)[\ln(4L/d) + \ln(L/h) - 2 + 2h/L]$. > - ✅ Faraday-derived anode life: $L_a = (W \times C \times U) / (8760 \times I_a)$. > - ✅ **8760** = hours per year (constant to remember). > - ✅ Anode capacity: Mg ≈ 1100, Zn ≈ 820, Al ≈ 2300 A·h/kg (theoretical, before $U$). > - ✅ Backfill for SACP anodes: **Gypsum + Bentonite + Na₂SO₄** (lowers soil ρ around anode). > - ✅ **Coating + CP** = industry-standard hybrid for buried pipelines. > - ✅ Stray currents from ICCP can damage **third-party structures** — control via drainage bonds. > - ✅ For long pipelines or high-resistivity soils, **ICCP is always preferred** economically. > - ✅ Reference electrodes: **CSE** (soil) | **Ag/AgCl** (seawater) | **SCE** (lab). > - ✅ Total circuit resistance $R_T = R_a + R_c + R_w$ (anode + cathode + wire).

topics.title: Components of Galvanic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components of Galvanic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ Module 3: Components of Galvanic Systems

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Galvanic System** (also called a **Galvanic Couple** or **Bimetallic Couple**) is an electrochemical configuration in which two dissimilar metallic conductors are placed in electrical contact and exposed to a common electrolyte, thereby forming a spontaneous short-circuited electrochemical cell. The resulting **electromotive force (EMF)** drives electrons from the more active metal (**anode**) through the external metallic path to the more noble metal (**cathode**), where a reduction reaction occurs. The consequent anodic dissolution of the active metal is termed **Galvanic Corrosion**, and it is one of the three principal forms of *wet* or *electrochemical* corrosion studied in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The term *Galvanic System* does NOT refer to a single component. It is a **system** comprising **four mandatory functional elements** — failure of any one element will arrest the corrosion process entirely.

## 1.2 The Four Mandatory Components

For a galvanic corrosion cell to function, all four of the following components must be present **simultaneously**:

| # | Component | Role | Engineering Example |
|---|-----------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Anode** | Site of oxidation (M → M^n+ + ne⁻); undergoes corrosion | Active metal (e.g., Zn) |
| 2 | **Cathode** | Site of reduction; protected from corrosion | Noble metal (e.g., Cu) |
| 3 | **Electrolyte** | Ionic conduction path between electrodes | Seawater, soil moisture, condensate |
| 4 | **Metallic (Electronic) Path** | Conducts electrons from anode to cathode | Direct metal-to-metal contact, weld, bolt |

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrolyte + Metallic path together close the circuit.** Without either, no sustained current flows — Faraday's continuity law demands closure of both loops.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Unwanted Battery

> 🪔 **Think of a galvanic couple as an unintentional battery:**
>
> Imagine a copper wire accidentally wrapped around an iron nail and both are dropped into a puddle of seawater. Without you doing anything, the system *spontaneously starts producing electricity*. The iron nail begins to rust rapidly (it is being **eaten away** to generate the electricity), while the copper wire remains completely untouched. The puddle acts as the conductive "highway" for ions, and the twisted wires act as the "wire" for electrons. The whole setup is effectively a tiny short-circuited battery whose fuel is the iron itself.
>
> **In a normal battery**, this is *useful* (we want the energy).
> **In a galvanic couple**, this is *destructive* (we want both metals to survive, but the anode is sacrificed).

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard Temperature** for tabulated potentials: **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)**
- **Faraday's Constant**: **F = 96,485 C mol⁻¹** (≈ 96,500 C mol⁻¹ for engineering use)
- **Universal Gas Constant**: **R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **Reference Electrode**: **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** with **E° = 0.000 V** by convention.
- **Standard Cell Potential**: $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$

> [!NOTE]
> A *positive* $E^\circ_{cell}$ indicates the reaction is **spontaneous** — the galvanic system will operate and corrosion will occur. This sign convention is critical in KTU numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component 1 — The Anode (Oxidation Site)

The anode is the **electrochemically more active** metal of the couple — i.e., the metal with the **more negative standard electrode potential** ($E^\circ$).

**Mechanism (Why it corrodes):**
- Metal atoms at the anode surface **lose electrons** (oxidation): $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + n e^-$
- These liberated electrons travel through the metallic path toward the cathode.
- The anode is **consumed**; its mass decreases with time.

**Typical Anode Materials (in seawater):** Mg, Zn, Al, mild steel, cast iron.

**KTU Board-Examiner Heuristic:** A metal sitting higher in the **Galvanic Series** (more active) becomes the anode.

## 2.2 Component 2 — The Cathode (Reduction Site)

The cathode is the **electrochemically more noble** metal — i.e., the metal with the **more positive standard electrode potential** ($E^\circ$).

**Mechanism (Why it survives):**
- Electrons arriving from the anode **consume** species in the electrolyte.
- Common cathodic reactions in aerated neutral water:
  - **Oxygen reduction (ORR):** $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^- \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^-$  (most common, neutral/alkaline)
  - **Hydrogen evolution:** $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$  (acidic media)
- The cathode surface is **not consumed** (it may even accrue calcareous deposits in seawater).

**Typical Cathode Materials:** Cu, Ni, stainless steel (passive), Ti, graphite, bronze.

## 2.3 Component 3 — The Electrolyte (Ionic Conductor)

The electrolyte is the **aqueous medium** (or moist solid like wet soil) that bridges the anode and cathode through **ionic conduction**.

- **Key Property:** Ionic conductivity (σ, S/m or Ω⁻¹·m⁻¹).
- **Higher conductivity → Higher galvanic current → Faster corrosion.**
- Common engineering electrolytes: seawater (~5 S/m), industrial cooling water, soil moisture, acid rain, condensate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Threshold Rule:** If the electrolyte's resistivity is *too high* (e.g., very dry soil, distilled water, dry concrete), the ionic current is negligible, and galvanic corrosion effectively **stops**. This is why the same bimetallic couple may fail rapidly offshore but survive in a desert environment.

## 2.4 Component 4 — The Metallic / Electronic Path

This is the **direct physical metallic contact** (or a conductor like a bolt, weld bead, brazing alloy) that allows electrons to flow from anode to cathode.
