## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Define the cathodic protection criterion of –850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄. Why is this potential value considered the threshold for full protection of steel in seawater?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **–850 mV vs Cu/CuSO₄ criterion** is the universally accepted protective potential (per **NACE SP0169 / ISO 15589**) at which the corrosion rate of carbon steel in aerated soil/water is reduced to a negligible level (≤ 0.01 mm/yr). It is **300 mV more negative** than the native free-corrosion potential of steel (~ –0.55 to –0.65 V in seawater).

At this potential, the anodic dissolution reaction is thermodynamically and kinetically suppressed. Furthermore, the environment becomes sufficiently alkaline at the metal surface (from oxygen and water reduction generating OH⁻), forming a stable calcareous Mg(OH)₂/CaCO₃ deposit that adds a further diffusion barrier.

The criterion is a **polarized potential** — meaning all IR-drop errors must be eliminated (use of close-spaced reference electrode or instant-off measurement).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]**
> **List the four corrosion zones encountered by a marine riser and pipeline from platform to seabed, identifying the dominant corrosion mechanism in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Zone | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|
| 1. Marine atmospheric (above splash) | Salt-spray deposition + wet-dry cycling |
| 2. Splash & tidal | Wetting–drying + mechanical impact + biofouling — **highest corrosion rate** |
| 3. Continuous immersion (seawater) | Uniform corrosion, pitting under deposits, **MIC under SRB biofilms** |
| 4. Seabed mud (buried) | Anaerobic MIC by **sulfate-reducing bacteria (SRB)** producing H₂S; concentration cells |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A **30 km** subsea crude pipeline, **0.4064 m OD**, is coated with **3LPE**. The protective current density for bare steel in seawater is **0.20 A/m²** and the coating breakdown factor is **0.01**. Design the cathodic protection system using **Al–Zn–In bracelet anodes** ($E_a$ = **–1.05 V vs Cu/CuSO₄**) for a design life of **30 years**. Take $\rho_{sw}$ = **0.25 Ω·m**. Anode dimensions: **1.5 m length, 0.04 m equivalent radius**, $u$ = **0.85**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **sacrificial anode CP** and **impressed current CP** for submarine pipelines, listing **three advantages and two limitations** of each. Justify which system is preferred for the pipeline in part (a).

---

**Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Surface area**
$$
A_s = \pi D L = 3.1416 \times 0.4064 \times 30{,}000 = 38{,}310 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Cathodic current demand**
$$
I_c = i_{bare} \times A_s \times f = 0.20 \times 38{,}310 \times 0.01 = 76.62 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3: Anode resistance (Dwight)**
$$
R_A = \frac{0.25}{2\pi \times 1.5}\left[\ln\left(\frac{4 \times 1.5}{0.04}\right) - 1\right] = 0.1064 \; \Omega
$$

**Step 4: Current per anode**
$$
I_A = \frac{|E_c - E_a|}{R_A} = \frac{0.20}{0.1064} = 1.880 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5: Number of anodes**
$$
N = \frac{I_c}{I_A} = \frac{76.62}{1.880} = 41
$$

**Step 6: Total Al mass (Faraday)**
$$
Q = 76.62 \times 30 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 7.573 \times 10^{10} \text{ C}
$$
$$
m = \frac{7.573 \times 10^{10} \times 26.98}{3 \times 96485 \times 0.85} = 8{,}308 \text{ kg} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 203 \text{ kg per anode}
$$

**Final Answer:** **41 bracelet anodes of 203 kg each**, evenly spaced at $S = 30{,}000/41 ≈ 732$ m.

---

**Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks**

| Feature | Sacrificial Anode CP | Impressed Current CP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None — self-driven | External rectifier required |
| Anode material | Al, Zn, Mg (consumable) | Ti/MMO, Si-Cr-Fe, graphite (inert) |
| Current/voltage adjustability | Fixed by ΔE of alloy | Continuously variable |
| Current output per anode | Low (1–5 A typical) | High (50–500 A) |
| Cable infrastructure | None | Required |
| Suitability for long pipelines | Short (≤ 20 km) | Long (>50 km) |
| Failure mode | Gradual anode consumption | Rectifier failure, cable damage |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Operating cost | Replacement of anodes | Electricity, maintenance |

**Advantages of Galvanic:** No external power; no cables; minimal maintenance; predictable performance; intrinsically safe; **no risk of overprotection**.

**Limitations of Galvanic:** Low driving voltage; limited current; anode mass becomes excessive for long/large structures; cannot be adjusted to compensate for coating degradation.

**Advantages of ICCP:** High current; adjustable; compact anode bed; suitable for long pipelines; lower long-term anode mass.

**Limitations of ICCP:** External power dependency; risk of overprotection (hydrogen embrittlement, coating disbondment); interference with neighbouring structures; higher capital cost.

**Justification for part (a):** With demand of only **76.6 A** on a 30 km pipeline (current density effectively 2.5 A/km), **galvanic bracelet anodes** are technically feasible and economically superior. ICCP would require long subsea HV cables and platform-based rectifier infrastructure, unjustified for such low current demand.

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **three-layer polyethylene (3LPE) coating system** used for submarine pipelines. State the function and typical thickness of each layer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **internal corrosion protection** of submarine pipelines carrying sour crude (containing H₂S and CO₂). Include the role of chemical inhibitors, biocides, and intelligent pigging.

---

**Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks**

The **3LPE coating system** is the global industry standard for offshore pipelines. It comprises three functionally distinct bonded layers:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  LAYER 3 — POLYETHYLENE (PE) TOP COAT        │
│  Thickness: 1.8 – 3.0 mm                     │
│  Function: Mechanical protection, abrasion   │
│            resistance, water barrier         │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 2 — COPOLYMER ADHESIVE (tie-layer)    │
│  Thickness: 0.2 – 0.4 mm                     │
│  Function: Bonds PE to epoxy; flexural layer │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LAYER 1 — FUSION BONDED EPOXY (FBE)         │
│  Thickness: 0.10 – 0.15 mm (100–150 µm)      │
│  Function: Primary anti-corrosion barrier    │
│            Excellent adhesion to steel       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
        ▲ Steel pipe substrate (white-metal blast)
```

**Functions:**
- **FBE:** Chemically bonded to blasted steel; provides primary corrosion barrier with excellent cathodic disbondment resistance at elevated temperatures (up to **110 °C** for high-T grades).
- **Adhesive:** Provides mechanical flexibility and bridges the different thermal expansion between rigid FBE and ductile PE.
- **PE:** Outer tough jacket — handles bending during lay, abrasion during trenching, impact from rocks and trawl gear, and UV during storage.

For **deep-water / hot service**, 3LPP (polypropylene) replaces PE, giving higher glass-transition temperature and impact resistance.

---

**Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Internal corrosion** of submarine pipelines arises from:
- **CO₂ (sweet corrosion):** $\text{Fe} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{FeCO}_3 + \text{H}_2$
- **H₂S (sour corrosion):** $\text{Fe} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS} + \text{H}_2$ — risk of **SSC (Sulfide Stress Cracking)** and **HIC (Hydrogen Induced Cracking)**.
- **SRB activity:** Anaerobic respiration producing H₂S and FeS tubercles.
- **Oxygen ingress** during transient operations, pig runs, or shutdowns.

**Protection Strategy (Layered Defense):**

1. **Material Selection:** Use **CRA (Corrosion Resistant Alloys)** like **13Cr / Super 13Cr / 22Cr duplex stainless steel** or **Inconel clad** for severe service. Otherwise, carbon steel API 5L X65 with controlled hardness (≤ 22 HRC for sour service per NACE MR0175).

2. **Chemical Inhibition:** Continuous injection of **film-forming inhibitors**:
   - **Imidazolines** (cationic surfactants that adsorb on steel surface).
   - **Quaternary ammonium salts.**
   - **Fatty amine ethoxylates.**
   Dose: 5–50 ppm; injection point at wellhead or platform manifold.

3. **Biocide Treatment:**
   - **Glutaraldehyde** (broad-spectrum, fast-acting).
   - **THPS (tetrakis-hydroxymethyl phosphonium sulfate)** — environmentally preferred.
   - **Isothiazolones** for periodic shock dosing.
   Rotation of biocides is essential to prevent SRB resistance.

4. **Oxygen Scavengers:**
   - **Sodium sulfite (Na₂SO₃)** or **ammonium bisulfite** in closed systems to consume any ingressed O₂.

5. **Intelligent Pigging:**
   - **MFL (Magnetic Flux Leakage) pigs** — detect wall loss ≥ 10%.
   - **UT (Ultrasonic) pigs** — measure remaining wall thickness directly.
   - **Geometry pigs** — detect dents, ovality.
   - **EMAT / UT-Wave pigs** — detect cracking, SCC, HIC.
   Frequency: **every 3–5 years** for critical trunk lines.

6. **Process Control:**
   - Maintain flow above **erosional velocity** to prevent water dropout.
   - Limit pressure/temperature transients to avoid CO₂ release.
   - Use **top-of-line corrosion (TLC) inhibitors** for multiphase flow lines.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the IR-drop:** Stating the protected potential as "–0.85 V" without clarifying it is the **polarized (IR-free)** value. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit mix-up in Dwight's equation:** Forgetting that $\rho$ must be in Ω·m and L, r in metres. Resulting R_A error cascades into entire design.
> 3. **Confusion between anode-to-electrolyte resistance and circuit resistance:** The cable resistance is often wrongly doubled.
> 4. **Forgetting utilization factor:** Faraday's law gives theoretical mass; examiners expect **practical mass = theoretical / u**.
> 5. **Neglecting the mud zone:** Submarine pipelines are partially **buried** in seabed sediment. The mud-zone current demand is lower (~ 0.02 A/m²) but the SRB activity is severe. Students often design for seawater only.
> 6. **Overprotection hazard:** For ICCP, students often forget that potentials more negative than **–1.10 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** cause **hydrogen embrittlement** and coating disbondment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Submarine pipelines** operate in four zones — atmospheric, splash, submerged, and mud — each with distinct dominant corrosion mechanisms.
- The **multi-barrier philosophy** (coating + CP + design + monitoring) is the only reliable 25–40 year protection strategy; coating alone OR CP alone is inadequate.
- **Cathodic protection criterion:** Polarized steel potential ≤ **–0.850 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** (–0.800 V vs Ag/AgCl).
- **Coating systems:** FBE (≤ 80 °C), 3LPE (≤ 80 °C), 3LPP (≤ 110 °C), polyurethane (≤ 130 °C).
- **Galvanic anode alloys for seawater:** Al–Zn–In (–1.05 V), Zn (–1.10 V), Mg only in fresh water.
- **Inert anodes for ICCP:** Ti with **MMO (Mixed Metal Oxide)** coating is current industry standard.
- **Current density benchmarks:** Bare steel in seawater ≈ 0.10–0.30 A/m²; in mud ≈ 0.02–0.05 A/m²; coated surface ≈ 0.001–0.01 A/m².
- **Key equations:** $I_c = i_c A$, Dwight's $R_A$, Faraday's $m = Q M/(nFu)$, attenuation $\alpha = \sqrt{R_p/R_c}$.
- **Attenuation constant** $\alpha$ determines the maximum distance from a drainage point; for ICCP on long pipelines, $L_{eff} = 1/\alpha$.
- **Mud zone** demands special attention — anaerobic SRB produce FeS and H₂S → SSC / HIC cracking.
- **Internal corrosion** controlled by CRA selection, chemical inhibition (imidazoline), biocides (glutaraldehyde, THPS), O₂ scavengers, and intelligent pigging (MFL, UT).
- **Monitoring:** Reference electrodes (Ag/AgCl, Cu/CuSO₄), coupons, ER probes, periodic ROV surveys, instant-off potential measurement to remove IR drop.
- **Overprotection limit:** Do **not** exceed **–1.10 V vs Cu/CuSO₄** on coated pipelines → hydrogen blistering, disbondment.
- **Indian standards:** Follow **NACE SP0176**, **ISO 15589**, **DNV-RP-F103**, and **ONGC HSE guidelines** for offshore pipelines.
- **Design life of bracelet anodes:** Typically 25–30 years; must be verified by accurate current-demand estimates.
- **Concrete Weight Coating (CWC):** Provides negative buoyancy for stability, mechanical impact protection — but it is **NOT** an anti-corrosion coating; it must be coupled with an underlying anti-corrosion layer (FBE/3LPE).
- **Field joints / girth welds:** The weakest link — applied manually on the lay-barge with **Field Joint Coating (FJC)** systems (e.g., **PP–PP**, **infrared-cured FBE**, **heat-shrink sleeves**).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Parameters in Cathodic Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Parameters in Cathodic Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cathodic Protection (CP)** is an electrochemical corrosion-mitigation technique in which the metal structure to be protected (the **cathode**) is forced to become the most electro-negative electrode in an electrolytic environment, thereby suppressing the anodic (oxidation / metal dissolution) half-reaction on the structure itself.

Mathematically, the protective condition is expressed by the Nernst criterion:

$$
E_{\text{protected}} \;\leq\; E_{\text{Fe}/\text{Fe}^{2+}}^{\circ} \;-\; \eta_{\text{cathodic}} \;-\; \eta_{\text{a}}
$$

where $\eta_{\text{cathodic}}$ is the polarization over-potential supplied by the external current and $\eta_{\text{a}}$ is the anodic over-potential of the protected structure (which is suppressed toward zero).

The **standard protection potentials** (referenced against Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode) adopted by KTU board examiners and by **NACE / ISO 15589** are:

| Structure | Protection Potential (vs CSE) |
|---|---|
| Steel (aerated soil / water) | **−850 mV** |
| Steel (anaerobic / SRB soil) | **−950 mV** |
| Aluminum | **−950 mV to −1100 mV** |
| Copper / Cu-alloy | **−500 mV** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize):** "Cathodic protection is the prevention of corrosion by making the entire surface of the metal structure cathodic, either by the use of a sacrificial anode (galvanic CP) or by an impressed direct current from an external DC source (impressed-current CP)."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Electron Pump"

Imagine a leaking water pipe (the corroding metal). Water leaks out where pressure is highest. Cathodic protection is like installing a **one-way electron pump** that floods the metal surface with electrons from outside, so that the internal "pressure" trying to push metal-ions into solution is completely overwhelmed.

- **Sacrificial (Galvanic) CP** = a friendlier metal (Zn, Mg, Al) is bolted on and *willingly* dissolves to donate electrons — like a *donor* organ.
- **Impressed-Current CP (ICCP)** = a rectifier plugs the metal into the negative terminal of a DC source (like a battery charger), forcing electrons in continuously — like an *external life-support machine*.

The metal under CP "wants" to corrode, but the protective current keeps suppressing the anodic reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Analogy:** Cathodic Protection is essentially "**corrosion by proxy**" — the auxiliary anode corrodes *instead* of the protected structure, because all anodic reactions are forced onto the sacrificial or impressed-current anode.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants Used in CP Design

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faraday's constant | **F** | **96,485 C mol⁻¹** | Faraday's Law mass calculations |
| Iron dissolution | Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ | n = **2** | Equivalent weight = 55.85/2 = **27.92 g/eq** |
| Zinc dissolution | Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ | n = **2** | Eq. wt. = 65.38/2 = **32.69 g/eq** |
| Magnesium dissolution | Mg → Mg²⁺ + 2e⁻ | n = **2** | Eq. wt. = 24.31/2 = **12.16 g/eq** |
| Standard H₂ electrode | SHE | **0.000 V** | Reference for all potential shifts |
| Cu/CuSO₄ electrode | CSE | **+0.316 V vs SHE** | Field reference electrode |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Primary Design Parameters

Every CP design must quantify **six interrelated engineering parameters**. KTU examiners routinely ask students to identify *which parameter* is being asked about.

### Parameter 1 — Required Protective Current Density ($i$)

The amount of DC current per unit surface area needed to polarize the metal to its protection potential.

$$
i \;=\; \dfrac{I_{\text{req}}}{A_{\text{surface}}} \quad [\text{mA/m}^2]
$$

Typical values (memorize for KTU viva):

| Environment | i (mA/m²) |
|---|---|
| Soil (bare steel, normal) | 10–110 |
| Soil (bare steel, aggressive) | 110–430 |
| Sea water (bare steel) | 80–270 |
| Fresh water (bare steel) | 20–55 |
| Concrete (rebar) | 0.1–2.0 |

### Parameter 2 — Soil / Electrolyte Resistivity ($\rho$)

Measured using the **Wenner 4-pin method**:

$$
\rho \;=\; 2\pi a R \quad [\Omega \cdot \text{cm}]
$$

where $a$ is the equal pin spacing and $R$ is the resistance measured between the inner pins.

### Parameter 3 — Coating Efficiency ($\eta_{\text{coat}}$)

Coatings drastically reduce current demand. The effective current becomes:

$$
I_{\text{actual}} \;=\; I_{\text{bare}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{coat}})
$$

Typical coating efficiencies: **Fusion-bonded epoxy (FBE) ≈ 99 %**, **Coal-tar enamel ≈ 95 %**, **3-layer PE ≈ 99.5 %**.

### Parameter 4 — Anode-to-Earth Resistance ($R_a$)

For a **single vertical anode**, **Dwight's Equation**:

$$
R_a \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1 + \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{S^2 + L^2}} - \dfrac{L}{S} \right]
$$

For a **single horizontal anode** at depth $d$:

$$
R_a \;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{d}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2d}{L} \right]
$$

### Parameter 5 — Number of Anodes ($N$)

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{R_a}{R_{\text{total}}} \quad \text{where} \quad R_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\Delta V}{I_{\text{req}}}
$$

### Parameter 6 — Anode Life / Mass (Faraday's Law)

$$
W_{\text{anode}} \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot \mu \cdot 1000} \quad [\text{kg}]
$$

where $C$ = anode electrochemical capacity (A·h/kg), $\mu$ = utilization factor (typically **0.75–0.85**), $T$ = design life in years.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR ESE & VIVA**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \pi D L$ | Pipe surface area | m² |
| 2 | $I_{\text{req}} = A \cdot i$ | Total protective current | A |
| 3 | $I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{bare}} (1 - \eta_{\text{coat}})$ | After coating credit | A |
| 4 | $W = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's Law (mass consumed) | kg |
| 5 | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | Wenner soil resistivity | $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ |
| 6 | $R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(8L/d) - 1\right]$ | Horizontal anode (Sunde) | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | $\alpha = \sqrt{R/C}$ | Attenuation constant | m⁻¹ |
| 8 | $I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}$ | Current decay along pipe | A |
| 9 | $\Delta V_{\text{IR}} = I \cdot R_{\text{structure}}$ | Ohmic drop along pipe | V |
| 10 | $N = \dfrac{I_{\text{req}}}{I_{\text{per anode}}}$ | Number of anodes | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These parameters are the backbone of every cross-country pipeline, offshore platform, ship hull, and storage-tank bottom CP design. Real-world engineering consultancies (e.g., **Mott MacDonald**, **Bechtel**, **Engineers India Ltd**) use this exact KTU-recommended methodology in tender specifications for ONGC, IOCL, and GAIL pipelines.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Mass of Anode Required from Faraday's First Law

Faraday's First Law states that the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electricity passed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &\;\propto\; Q \\
W &\;=\; Z \cdot Q \quad \text{(Z = electrochemical equivalent, kg/C)} \\
Q &\;=\; I \cdot t \\
W &\;=\; Z \cdot I \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

For any element with atomic mass $M$ and valency $n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &\;=\; \dfrac{M}{n \cdot F} \\
\therefore \quad W &\;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** $\mathbf{W = I \cdot t \cdot M / (n \cdot F)}$ — This is the *root formula* of every anode-life problem in the KTU paper.

**Industry-adapted form (using anode capacity $C$ in A·h/kg):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W \;[\text{kg}] &\;=\; \dfrac{I \,[\text{A}] \times T \,[\text{yr}] \times 8760 \,[\text{h/yr}]}{C \,[\text{A·h/kg}] \times \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor **8760** converts years to hours (24 × 365 = 8760). The utilization factor $\mu$ accounts for the fact that an anode is retired before 100 % of its theoretical mass is consumed (typically $\mu \approx 0.85$).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Full ICCP Design (KTU Exam Pattern)

### Problem Statement

> *Design an impressed-current cathodic protection system for a buried water pipeline. Use the following data:*
> - *Pipeline length $L$ = 5.0 km = 5000 m*
> - *Pipe diameter $D$ = 40 cm = 0.40 m*
> - *Soil resistivity $\rho$ = 3000 $\Omega \cdot$cm = 30 $\Omega \cdot$m*
> - *Required current density $i$ = 30 mA/m² (bare steel in mildly aggressive soil)*
> - *Anode material: high-silicon cast iron, capacity $C$ = 1100 A·h/kg, utilization factor $\mu$ = 0.85*
> - *Design life $T$ = 30 years*
> - *Coating efficiency = 90 % (3-layer PE)*
> - *Maximum rectifier output voltage allowed = 50 V*
> - *Number of anodes per ground-bed = 8*
> - *Pipe wall thickness = 12 mm (for resistance computation)*
> - *Steel resistivity $\rho_{\text{Fe}}$ = 1.72 × 10⁻⁷ $\Omega \cdot$m*

### Solution — Step 1: Surface Area of Pipeline

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\;=\; \pi \cdot D \cdot L \\
A &\;=\; \pi \times 0.40 \times 5000 \\
A &\;=\; 6283.19 \;\; \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step-1 logic:* For a buried cylindrical pipeline of length much greater than diameter, the end caps are neglected; only lateral surface is considered.

### Solution — Step 2: Bare Current Demand

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{bare}} &\;=\; A \cdot i \\
I_{\text{bare}} &\;=\; 6283.19 \times 30 \times 10^{-3} \\
I_{\text{bare}} &\;=\; 188.50 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Apply Coating Credit (90 % efficient)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{effective}} &\;=\; I_{\text{bare}} \times (1 - \eta_{\text{coat}}) \\
I_{\text{eff}} &\;=\; 188.50 \times (1 - 0.90) \\
I_{\text{eff}} &\;=\; 188.50 \times 0.10 \\
I_{\text{eff}} &\;=\; 18.85 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step-3 logic:* A 90 %-efficient coating means only 10 % of the bare current is needed — this is why coating + CP is the *industry standard* for buried pipelines.

### Solution — Step 4: Total Charge Over 30 Years

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &\;=\; I_{\text{eff}} \times T \times 8760 \\
Q &\;=\; 18.85 \times 30 \times 8760 \\
Q &\;=\; 4.953 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{A·h}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 5: Mass of Anode Material Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &\;=\; \dfrac{Q}{C \cdot \mu} \\
W &\;=\; \dfrac{4.953 \times 10^{6}}{1100 \times 0.85} \\
W &\;=\; 5298.0 \;\; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 6: Mass per Anode (per ground-bed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{per anode}} &\;=\; \dfrac{W}{N} \\
W_{\text{per anode}} &\;=\; \dfrac{5298.0}{8} \\
W_{\text{per anode}} &\;=\; 662.25 \;\; \text{kg/anode}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 7: Anode-to-Earth Resistance (Dwight for vertical anode, $L = 1.8$ m, $r = 0.075$ m)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a &\;=\; \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right] \\
R_a &\;=\; \dfrac{30}{2\pi \times 1.8}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 \times 1.8}{0.075}\right) - 1\right] \\
R_a &\;=\; \dfrac{30}{11.31}\left[\ln(96) - 1\right] \\
R_a &\;=\; 2.652 \times [4.564 - 1] \\
R_a &\;=\; 2.652 \times 3.564 \\
R_a &\;=\; 9.453 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 8: Parallel Resistance of 8 Anodes

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{ground bed}} &\;=\; \dfrac{R_a}{N} \\
R_{\text{ground bed}} &\;=\; \dfrac{9.453}{8} \\
R_{\text{ground bed}} &\;=\; 1.182 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 9: Rectifier DC Voltage Required

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{DC}} &\;=\; I_{\text{eff}} \times (R_{\text{ground bed}} + R_{\text{cable}}) + E_{\text{back-EMF}} \\
V_{\text{DC}} &\;\approx\; 18.85 \times 1.182 + 2.0 \\
V_{\text{DC}} &\;\approx\; 22.28 + 2.0 \\
V_{\text{DC}} &\;\approx\; 24.28 \;\; \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Step-9 logic:* Since $V_{\text{DC}}$ = 24.28 V < 50 V (specified maximum), the design is **technically feasible**. If the voltage had exceeded 50 V, the student would need to either (a) reduce anode spacing, (b) increase anode quantity, or (c) select a different backfill with lower resistivity.

### Solution — Step 10: Attenuation Check (Verifying Protection Along the Line)

Attenuation constant for steel pipe:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{pipe}} / \text{m} &\;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{\text{Fe}}}{\pi \cdot (D - t) \cdot t} \\
&\;=\; \dfrac{1.72 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 0.376 \times 0.012} \\
&\;=\; 1.214 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \Omega/\text{m} \\
C_{\text{coat}} &\;\approx\; 1.0 \;\; \mu\text{F/m}^2 \cdot 6283 \, \mu\text{F} \;=\; 6.28 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{F (distributed)} \\
\alpha &\;=\; \sqrt{R/C} \;=\; \sqrt{1.214 \times 10^{-5} / (6.28 \times 10^{-3})} \\
&\;\approx\; 0.044 \;\; \text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Current at the far end of the line (assuming single rectifier mid-point, distance = 2500 m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(2500) &\;=\; I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x} \\
&\;=\; 18.85 \times e^{-0.044 \times 2500} \\
&\;=\; 18.85 \times e^{-110} \\
&\;=\; 18.85 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-48} \\
&\;\approx\; 0 \;\; \text{A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion of Step-10:* Attenuation is far too aggressive — **multiple rectifier stations are mandatory** (one station every ~500–1000 m in practice). KTU students often miss this and assume a single station suffices.

---

## 3.3 Summary of the Design Workflow (Algorithmic Logic)

1. Compute pipe surface area → $A = \pi D L$
2. Compute bare current demand → $I_{\text{bare}} = A \cdot i$
3. Apply coating credit → $I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{bare}} (1 - \eta_{\text{coat}})$
4. Compute anode mass → $W = I \cdot T \cdot 8760 / (C \cdot \mu)$
5. Size anodes (length, diameter, count) → Dwight's equation
6. Verify rectifier voltage → $V_{\text{DC}} = I \times R + E_{\text{back-EMF}}$
7. Verify attenuation → multiple stations if needed
8. Specify cable cross-section, junction boxes, and reference electrodes

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CP System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 415V 50Hz] --> B[Transformer Steps Down to 0-60V AC]
    B --> C[Rectifier Bridge Converts AC to DC]
    C --> D[DC Output Filtered and Regulated]
    D --> E[Cable Positive Feeder to Ground Bed]
    D --> F[Cable Negative Feeder to Protected Structure]
    F --> G[Buried Pipeline as Cathode]
    E --> H[Impressed Current Anode Ground Bed]
    H --> I[Carbonaceous Backfill Coke Breeze]
    I --> J[Soil Electrolyte]
    J --> G
    K[Cu CuSO4 Reference Electrode CSE] --> L[Potential Measurement and Monitoring]
    G --> L
    L --> M[DC Voltmeter / Data Logger]
    M --> N[Verify 850 mV Protection Potential]
    H --> L
```

> **Diagram Reading Guide for Students:**
> - The **positive terminal** of the rectifier connects to the **anode ground-bed** (consumable in the corrosion reaction).
> - The **negative terminal** connects to the **structure** (protected cathode).
> - The **CSE (reference electrode)** taps the structure-to-soil potential — this is the **−850 mV criterion** verification.

## 4.2 Design Workflow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Site Survey and Soil Resistivity] --> P2[Step 2: Pipe Geometry and Coating Data]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Compute Bare Current Demand]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Apply Coating Efficiency]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute Anode Mass Using Faraday Law]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Size Anodes Using Dwight Equation]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Compute Rectifier Voltage]
    P7 --> P8{Step 8: Voltage Within Limit?}
    P8 -->|Yes| P9[Step 9: Check Attenuation Along Line]
    P8 -->|No| P10[Step 10: Increase Anode Count or Change Layout]
    P10 --> P6
    P9 --> P11{Step 11: Multiple Stations Needed?}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Step 12: Specify Multiple Rectifier Stations]
    P11 -->|No| P13[Step 13: Finalize BOM and Drawings]
    P12 --> P13
```

## 4.3 Sacrificial vs Impressed-Current CP — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SacrificialCP[Sacrificial CP System]
    SA1[Zinc Magnesium or Alloy Anode] -->|Electron Flow| SA2[Protected Steel Structure]
    SA2 -->|Ionic Path through Soil| SA3[Soil Electrolyte]
    SA3 --> SA1
    end
    subgraph ICCPSystem[Impressed Current CP System]
    IB1[AC Mains] --> IB2[Rectifier DC Source]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**List the six major design parameters involved in the design of a cathodic protection system for a buried pipeline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full marks require ALL six parameters):**

The six major design parameters in CP design are:

1. **Required protective current density ($i$)** — typically 10–110 mA/m² for bare steel in soil, expressed in mA/m².
2. **Soil resistivity ($\rho$)** — measured via the Wenner 4-pin method, expressed in $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$.
3. **Coating efficiency ($\eta_{\text{coat}}$)** — fraction of bare current saved by an organic or polymeric coating (typically 90–99 %).
4. **Anode-to-earth resistance ($R_a$)** — computed using **Dwight's** or **Sunde's** equation, expressed in ohms.
5. **Number of anodes ($N$)** — determined from the parallel-resistance current-sharing rule.
6. **Anode life / mass ($W$)** — calculated from **Faraday's First Law** of electrolysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly omit "attenuation" or "driving voltage / rectifier sizing" from this list. While these are derived quantities, listing them as parameters is **acceptable**. A common error is omitting **coating breakdown factor** which is *separate* from coating efficiency — boards award partial credit if these are conflated.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**State and explain Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis as applied to anode-mass calculation in cathodic protection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** *The mass of any substance liberated or dissolved at an electrode is directly proportional to the quantity of electric charge passed through the electrolyte.*

$$
W \;=\; Z \cdot Q \;=\; Z \cdot I \cdot t
$$

where $W$ = mass of metal dissolved (kg), $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (kg/C), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (s).

**CP Application (1 mark):** For anode life design, the law is rearranged using atomic mass $M$ and valency $n$:

$$
W \;=\; \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}
$$

For magnesium (M = 24.31 g/mol, n = 2), F = 96,485 C/mol, so $Z_{\text{Mg}}$ = **0.126 mg/C**. This allows designers to compute how many kilograms of sacrificial anode will be consumed over a 20–30 year design life.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various design parameters involved in the design of an impressed current cathodic protection (ICCP) system for a cross-country buried pipeline. Explain how each parameter is determined and its engineering significance.**

**Model Solution:**

The design of an ICCP system requires the precise quantification of the following six parameters:

**(1) Current Density Requirement ($i$)** — Determined empirically from field data and laboratory polarization studies. For a typical aerated neutral soil, $i = 30$ mA/m² is used. Engineering significance: directly fixes the total current demand and hence the rectifier rating.

**(2) Soil Resistivity ($\rho$)** — Determined by the Wenner four-pin method at multiple locations along the pipeline right-of-way. A high $\rho$ (>5000 $\Omega \cdot$cm) demands more anodes or chemical backfill (gypsum / bentonite) to lower the anode-to-earth resistance.

**(3) Coating Efficiency / Breakdown Factor** — Modern 3-layer polyethylene coatings deliver $\eta_{\text{coat}} \approx 99.5$ %, reducing current demand by a factor of ~200. Even with aging, the **coating breakdown factor** (typically 1–5 %) is used in 25-year projections.

**(4) Anode-to-Earth Resistance ($R_a$)** — Computed using **Dwight's equation** for vertical anodes or **Sunde's equation** for horizontal anodes. Determines how many anodes are required in parallel to meet the current demand at a given rectifier voltage.

**(5) Attenuation Constant ($\alpha$)** — Given by $\alpha = \sqrt{R/C}$, where $R$ is the longitudinal pipe resistance per unit length and $C$ is the coating capacitance per unit length. Determines the maximum permissible distance between two successive rectifier stations (typically 30–80 km).

**(6) Driving Voltage / Rectifier DC Output** — Sum of $I \times R_{\text{anode}} + I \times R_{\text{cable}} + \text{counter-EMF of anodic reaction} + \text{structure-to-soil back-potential}$. Typical ICCP rectifiers are rated **0–60 V DC, 0–50 A**.

> **Engineering Significance:** All six parameters must be evaluated to specify the rectifier rating, the number of ground-beds, anode size and material (high-silicon iron, mixed metal oxide, or graphite), cable cross-section, and the layout of test stations.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A buried steel pipeline of length 3 km and diameter 50 cm is to be protected by an ICCP system. Soil resistivity is 2500 $\Omega \cdot$cm. Required current density is 25 mA/m². Coating efficiency is 95 %. Design life is 25 years. Anode: high-silicon cast iron (capacity 1100 A·h/kg, utilization 0.85). Compute: (i) total protective current, (ii) total anode mass required, (iii) number of anodes if each anode weighs 50 kg.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Protective Current:**

Surface area:
$$
A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.50 \times 3000 = 4712.39 \;\; \text{m}^2
$$

Bare current demand:
$$
I_{\text{bare}} = A \cdot i = 4712.39 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 117.81 \;\; \text{A}
$$

Effective current (after 95 % coating credit):
$$
I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{bare}} \times (1 - 0.95) = 117.81 \times 0.05 = 5.89 \;\; \text{A}
$$

**(ii) Total Anode Mass:**

Total charge over 25 years:
$$
Q = I_{\text{eff}} \times T \times 8760 = 5.89 \times 25 \times 8760 = 1.290 \times 10^{6} \;\; \text{A·h}
$$

Mass of anode:
$$
W = \dfrac{Q}{C \cdot \mu} = \dfrac{1.290 \times 10^{6}}{1100 \times 0.85} = 1380.0 \;\; \text{kg}
$$

**(iii) Number of Anodes:**
$$
N = \dfrac{W}{W_{\text{per anode}}} = \dfrac{1380}{50} = 27.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{28 \text{ anodes}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Alternative Choice]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain with neat sketches the difference between sacrificial (galvanic) and impressed current cathodic protection systems. Compare their advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Sacrificial CP (Galvanic CP):**
- A more electro-negative metal (Zn, Mg, Al-alloy) is electrically connected to the structure.
- The sacrificial anode dissolves preferentially, providing electrons to the protected structure.
- No external power source required.
- Driving voltage is the natural potential difference between the two metals (e.g., Mg-steel ≈ 0.7 V).
- Limited current output; suitable for small structures, well-coated pipelines, marine hulls.

**Impressed Current CP (ICCP):**
- An external DC source (rectifier) drives current from an inert anode (high-Si iron, MMO, graphite) to the protected structure.
- Independent of natural galvanic potentials.
- Adjustable voltage (0–60 V) and current (0–50 A); suitable for large bare or poorly-coated structures.
- Requires AC mains, transformer-rectifier unit, and continuous monitoring.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Sacrificial CP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None | AC mains + rectifier |
| Anode material | Zn, Mg, Al | High-Si Fe, MMO, graphite |
| Driving voltage | 0.2–0.7 V | 0–60 V (adjustable) |
| Current range | mA level | A level |
| Anode life | Short (5–10 yr) | Long (20–30 yr) |
| Soil resistivity limit | < 3000 $\Omega \cdot$cm | Up to 50,000 $\Omega \cdot$cm |
| Cost | Low installation | High installation, low operating |
| Best suited for | Coated structures, short runs, marine | Long pipelines, bare structures, tanks |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive Dwight's equation for the resistance of a single vertical anode. A vertical anode of length 2.0 m and radius 0.075 m is installed in soil of resistivity 4000 $\Omega \cdot$cm. Compute the anode-to-earth resistance. If 12 such anodes are connected in parallel, what is the combined resistance?**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation Sketch:** Dwight's equation assumes the anode behaves as an equipotential prolate spheroid. Integrating the surface potential over the anode surface gives:

$$
R_a = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1 + \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{S^2 + L^2}} - \dfrac{L}{S}\right]
$$

For deep vertical anodes (depth $S \gg L$), the last two terms $\to 0$, simplifying to:

$$
R_a \approx \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{r}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $\rho = 4000 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} = 40 \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$, $L = 2.0 \, \text{m}$, $r = 0.075 \, \text{m}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a &\;=\; \dfrac{40}{2\pi \times 2.0}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 \times 2.0}{0.075}\right) - 1\right] \\
&\;=\; \dfrac{40}{12.566}\left[\ln(106.67) - 1\right] \\
&\;=\; 3.183 \times [4.670 - 1] \\
&\;=\; 3.183 \times 3.670 \\
&\;=\; 11.68 \;\; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combined resistance for 12 anodes in parallel:**
$$
R_{\text{combined}} = \dfrac{R_a}{N} = \dfrac{11.68}{12} = 0.973 \;\; \Omega
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors**: Forgetting to convert $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ to $\Omega \cdot \text{m}$ before applying Dwight's equation in SI units — *results are off by a factor of 100*.
> 2. **Confusing anode capacity ($C$) with current ($I$)**: Capacity is in **A·h/kg**, current is in **A**. Students frequently lose 2–3 marks here.
> 3. **Forgetting utilization factor ($\mu$)**: Anode is retired at ~85 % of theoretical life. Omitting $\mu$ overestimates anode life.
> 4. **Summing resistances in series instead of parallel** for multiple ground-beds.
> 5. **Using 365 days/yr instead of 8760 hr/yr** when applying the Faraday equation.
> 6. **Forgetting back-EMF (~2 V)** when computing rectifier voltage — gives an under-spec rectifier.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Last-Minute Revision Before KTU Exam**

- **Cathodic Protection = corrosion prevention by making the protected metal the cathode** of an electrochemical cell.
- **Two methods**: (1) **Galvanic / sacrificial** — uses Zn, Mg, Al anodes; (2) **Impressed-current** — uses rectifier + inert anode (Si-Fe, MMO, graphite).
- **Standard protection potentials** (vs CSE): Steel **−850 mV**, Steel in SRB soil **−950 mV**, Al **−950 to −1100 mV**.
- **Six core design parameters**: (1) current density $i$, (2) soil resistivity $\rho$, (3) coating efficiency $\eta_{\text{coat}}$, (4) anode resistance $R_a$, (5) number of anodes $N$, (6) anode mass $W$ (from Faraday).
- **Faraday's Law (master formula)**: $\mathbf{W = I \cdot t \cdot M / (n \cdot F)}$.
- **Industry form**: $\mathbf{W \,[\text{kg}] = I \,[\text{A}] \times T \,[\text{yr}] \times 8760 / (C \times \mu)}$.
- **Wenner 4-pin soil resistivity**: $\mathbf{\rho = 2\pi a R}$.
- **Dwight's equation (vertical anode, deep)**: $\mathbf{R_a = (\rho / 2\pi L)[\ln(4L/r) - 1]}$.
- **Sunde's equation (horizontal anode)**: $\mathbf{R_a = (\rho / 2\pi L)[\ln(8L/d) - 1]}$.
- **Current with coating credit**: $\mathbf{I_{\text{eff}} = I_{\text{bare}} (1 - \eta_{\text{coat}})}$.
- **Attenuation constant**: $\mathbf{\alpha = \sqrt{R/C}}$; current along pipe: $\mathbf{I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\alpha x}}$.
- **Typical current density** for steel in soil: **10–110 mA/m²** (bare); **1–5 mA/m²** (well-coated).
- **Typical anode capacity**: Zn = **780 A·h/kg**, Mg = **1230 A·h/kg**, Al-alloy = **2700 A·h/kg**, Si-Fe = **1100 A·h/kg**, MMO = **~6000 A·h/kg**.
- **Utilization factor** $\mu$ typically **0.75–0.90** depending on anode type and backfill.
- **Coating + CP** is **always cheaper than CP alone** — practical exam answer.
- **Attenuation forces multiple rectifier stations** for long cross-country pipelines (>50 km).
- **Reference electrode**: Cu/CuSO₄ (+316 mV vs SHE) used in field; SHE (0 V) used in lab.
- **Rectifier sizing**: $V_{\text{DC}} = I \cdot (R_{\text{anode}} + R_{\text{cable}}) + E_{\text{back-EMF}} + \text{cathode back-potential}$.
- **KTU exam-favourite mistake to avoid**: not converting units between $\Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ and $\Omega \cdot \text{m}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurements in Cathodic Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurements in Cathodic Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Measurements in Cathodic Protection (CP)** constitute the systematic quantification of electrochemical and electrical parameters that govern the protective efficacy of impressed current and galvanic (sacrificial) cathodic protection systems installed on buried, submerged, or embedded metallic structures (pipelines, storage tanks, ship hulls, reinforced concrete, offshore platforms). The discipline involves the precise determination of **structure-to-electrolyte potential**, **protective current density**, **soil/electrolyte resistivity**, **anode bed resistance**, and **coating conductance** using calibrated reference electrodes and high-impedance digital voltmeters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Cathodic Protection Monitoring* is the measurement science that confirms whether the protected structure is held within the immunity region of the Pourbaix diagram (typically more negative than **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** reference electrode), as codified by NACE/AMPP SP0169 and ISO 15589.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition Pump

Imagine you are protecting a **wooden house** from termites by spraying a chemical barrier. How do you *know* the barrier works? You don't just spray once — you **measure** the chemical concentration in the soil at various points, check for termite activity at sentinel locations, and continuously monitor the perimeter.

Cathodic protection is exactly this scenario — but instead of termites, we are fighting **electrochemical corrosion** (the spontaneous anodic dissolution of metal), and instead of chemical spray, we are injecting electrons into the structure via a **DC power source** or a **more active sacrificial metal**.

**The Three Things We Must Measure:**

1. **Are we "negatively enough" to stop corrosion?** → Structure-to-soil potential measurement with a reference electrode (the "thermometer" of CP).
2. **Are we delivering enough electron flow?** → Current measurement (the "flow rate" of protection).
3. **Is the environment (soil/water) cooperative?** → Resistivity measurement (the "resistance" of the electrolyte to current flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Benchmarks Used Throughout CP Measurement:**
> - **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE) potential:** **0.000 V** (reference origin)
> - **Protection criterion for steel:** **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** (= **−770 mV vs. SCE** = **−780 mV vs. Ag/AgCl/seawater**)
> - **Minimum polarization shift:** **100 mV** (NACE criterion)
> - **Soil resistivity typical range:** **10 Ω·cm (wet clay)** to **100,000 Ω·cm (dry rocky soil)**

## 1.3 Why Measurement is Non-Negotiable

Without quantitative measurement, cathodic protection degrades into "blind protection" — overprotection wastes power and may cause **hydrogen embrittlement** or **cathodic disbondment of coatings**, while underprotection allows **pitting corrosion** to silently propagate. KTU examiners consistently emphasize that measurement is the **verification arm** of CP, not an optional supplement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of CP Measurements

The measurements performed on operating CP systems fall into **four pillars**:

### Pillar A — Reference Electrode Measurements (Potential Mapping)

A **reference electrode** is a half-cell with a stable, reproducible, temperature-dependent but well-defined potential against which the unknown structure potential is measured. The reference electrode acts as the **zero-point** of the electrochemical yardstick.

| Reference Electrode | Half-Cell Reaction | Standard Potential vs. SHE | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Copper/Copper Sulphate (CSE)** | Cu → Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ | **+0.318 V** | Most common for **buried pipelines** (soil/land) |
| **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl)** | Ag → Ag⁺ + e⁻ | **+0.222 V** (seawater), **+0.199 V** (KCl sat.) | **Seawater** and **marine** structures |
| **Saturated Calomel (SCE)** | Hg₂Cl₂ + 2e⁻ → 2Hg + 2Cl⁻ | **+0.241 V** | Laboratory bench and **concrete** |
| **Zinc (Zn)** | Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ | **−0.763 V** | **Permanent buried reference** in remote pipelines |
| **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE)** | 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ | **0.000 V** | **Primary laboratory reference only** |

### Pillar B — Structure-to-Electrolyte Potential (Pipe-to-Soil Potential)

The measurement is performed by connecting the **positive terminal** of a high-impedance (≥ 10 MΩ) digital voltmeter to the **structure** (e.g., pipeline via a test station lead wire) and the **negative terminal** to the **reference electrode** placed in the electrolyte directly above the structure.

$$E_{measured} = E_{structure} - E_{reference}$$

The measured "OFF" potential (instantly after interrupting the CP current) eliminates **IR drop** errors caused by the protective current flowing through the soil resistance.

### Pillar C — Current Measurements

- **Total current output** of the rectifier (impressed current systems): measured by an **ammeter in series** with the anode bed or by the **shunt resistor** in the rectifier panel.
- **Current distribution along a pipeline**: measured via **pipe current mapping** using calibrated four-pin probes that sense the voltage drop across a known pipe segment.
- **Anode current output (galvanic anodes)**: computed from Ohm's law and the anode-to-electrolyte resistance.

### Pillar D — Soil/Electrolyte Resistivity Measurement

Soil resistivity ($\rho$, in **Ω·cm**) controls how deeply protective current penetrates from the anode to the structure. The most widely used field technique is the **Wenner Four-Pin Method**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units | Boundary/Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{pipe-soil} = E_{Cu/CuSO_4}$ | Pipe-to-soil potential | V or mV | Negative values indicate protection |
| 2 | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | **Wenner soil resistivity** | Ω·cm | $a \ll$ depth to water table |
| 3 | $\rho = 4\pi a R / (1 + 2a/\sqrt{a^2 + 4b^2} - 2a/\sqrt{a^2 + 4b^2})$ | Schlumberger-Palmer | Ω·cm | Deep probe spacing |
| 4 | $R_A = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | **Dwight's anode-to-soil resistance** (single vertical anode) | Ω | $L \gg d$, uniform soil |
| 5 | $R_A = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{2L}{r}\right) + \ln\left(\frac{L}{2t}\right) - 2 + \frac{2t}{L}\right]$ | Horizontal anode (Sunde's) | Ω | $L \gg r$ |
| 6 | $I = \frac{\Delta E \cdot A \cdot C}{1000}$ | **Current requirement** | A | $\Delta E$ in V, $A$ in m², $C$ in µS/m² |
| 7 | $E_{polarization} \geq 100 \text{ mV}$ | NACE polarization criterion | mV | Decay between depolarized and native state |
| 8 | $i_{prot} = \frac{1}{\rho_{soil}}\cdot k$ | Protective current density | A/m² | Empirical, function of coating quality |
| 9 | $V_{IR} = I \cdot R_{soil}$ | **IR drop error** in potential | V | Must be subtracted for true OFF potential |
| 10 | $E_{true} = E_{measured(ON)} - I \cdot R_{soil}$ | **IR-drop corrected potential** | V | Synonymous with "instant-off" potential |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU 2024 EXAM TIP:** Examiners often quote pipe-to-soil potentials using different reference electrodes to test conversion skills. **Always convert to a common reference (preferably Cu/CuSO₄)** before applying the −850 mV criterion. Use: $E_{vs.Cu/CuSO_4} = E_{vs.SCE} + (0.318 - 0.241) = E_{vs.SCE} + 0.077 \text{ V}$.

## 2.3 Why Each Measurement Matters in Real Engineering

| Measurement | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|
| Pipe-to-soil potential | Confirms NACE compliance for **Kerala Pipeline Corporation** cross-country gas pipelines |
| Soil resistivity | Decides **anode bed design** (deep well vs. shallow distributed) for ONGC Kochi refinery |
| IR drop correction | Prevents **false-pass** readings that mask active corrosion under disbonded coatings |
| Anode current output | Determines **replacement schedule** of magnesium anodes in ship hull protection |
| Close-Interval survey | Detects **hot spots** caused by coating holidays near railway and highway crossings |

## 2.4 Practical Diagnostic Surveys (Beyond Routine Measurements)

- **Close Interval Potential Survey (CIPS):** Measures pipe-to-soil potential every **1–3 m** along the pipeline using a trailing wire synchronized to a GPS-positioned operator.
- **Direct Current Voltage Gradient (DCVG):** Detects coating defects by measuring the **soil surface voltage gradient** caused by CP current leaking through coating holidays.
- **Pearson Survey:** Locates coating defects by injecting an AC signal (~1000 Hz) and tracing the signal along the pipe.
- **Cathodic Shielding / Attenuation Survey:** Maps the **IR drop attenuation** along coated pipelines to identify sections receiving inadequate current.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Wenner Soil Resistivity Formula

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Setup

The Wenner array places **four equally-spaced electrodes** in a straight line on the soil surface at spacing **a** (in cm). The two outer electrodes (C₁ and C₂) inject a current **I** into the soil; the two inner electrodes (P₁ and P₂) measure the voltage drop **V**.

### 3.1.2 Geometric Derivation

Consider a single **point current source** on the surface of a homogeneous, isotropic semi-infinite soil medium. The potential at distance **r** from a current source of magnitude **I** on the surface of a semi-infinite medium is given by the hemispherical spreading relation:

$$
V(r) = \frac{I \cdot \rho}{2 \pi r}
$$

**Why factor 2π and not 4π?** Because current spreads only into the lower half-space (a hemisphere), not a full sphere, doubling the resistance per unit radius.

The total potential at inner pin P₁ (at distance **a** from C₁ and **2a** from C₂) due to both current electrodes:

$$
V_{P_1} = \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi a} - \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi (2a)}
$$

The total potential at inner pin P₂ (at distance **2a** from C₁ and **a** from C₂):

$$
V_{P_2} = \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi (2a)} - \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi a}
$$

The voltage difference measured between the two inner pins:

$$
\Delta V = V_{P_1} - V_{P_2} = \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi a} - \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi (2a)} - \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi (2a)} + \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi a}
$$

Simplifying the four-term expression by collecting like coefficients:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{a} - \frac{1}{2a} - \frac{1}{2a}\right] = \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{2}{a} - \frac{1}{a}\right] = \frac{I \rho}{2 \pi a}
$$

The resistance measured by the voltmeter-ammeter pair is $R = \Delta V / I$. Substituting and isolating $\rho$:

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi a} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\rho = 2 \pi a R}
$$

**Step 3.1.3 — Final Equation:** $\rho = 2 \pi a R$ where $a$ is pin spacing (cm) and $R$ is the measured resistance (Ω).

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A Wenner four-pin test conducted on a site in Ernakulam, Kerala uses pin spacing **a = 2 m = 200 cm**. The voltmeter reads **V = 1.85 V** and the ammeter reads **I = 5 mA**. Calculate the soil resistivity and classify it.

**Step 1:** Convert units to SI-consistent values. Pin spacing $a = 200$ cm. Current $I = 0.005$ A. Voltage $V = 1.85$ V.

**Step 2:** Compute resistance $R = V/I$.

$$
R = \frac{1.85 \text{ V}}{0.005 \text{ A}} = 370 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 3:** Apply Wenner formula.

$$
\rho = 2 \pi a R = 2 \pi \times 200 \times 370
$$

$$
\rho = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 200 \times 370 = 464{,}955 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} \approx 4.65 \times 10^5 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the value using soil classification.

| Soil Resistivity (Ω·cm) | Corrosivity | Anode Bed Recommendation |
|---|---|---|
| < 1,000 | **Severely corrosive** | Distributed shallow anodes |
| 1,000 – 5,000 | **Corrosive** | Standard horizontal bed |
| 5,000 – 20,000 | **Moderately corrosive** | Deep well anodes preferred |
| **> 20,000** | **Mildly corrosive** (Kerala laterite often falls here) | Extended deep-well anodes with backfill |

**Result:** $\rho \approx 465{,}000 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$ indicates **mildly corrosive** lateritic soil typical of mid-Kerala, requiring deep-well anode beds with high-quality coke-breeze backfill.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Dwight's Vertical Anode Resistance Formula

### 3.2.1 Setup

A vertical cylindrical anode of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is buried vertically with its top at depth $t$ below the soil surface. The anode is connected to the structure via a header cable. The anode discharges current uniformly along its length (assuming the soil is homogeneous and the backfill column is well-formed).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Differential resistance of an elemental cylinder.**
For a vertical anode of length $L$ and equivalent radius $r = d/2$, consider a horizontal disk element of height $dz$ at depth $z$. This disk behaves like a tiny point source with hemispherical equipotential surfaces.

The differential resistance contribution $dR_A$ for that element, integrated over a vertical line source, yields Dwight's well-known expression:

$$
R_A = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Derivation logic (sketched for KTU clarity):**

(a) Start with the resistance of a hemispherical point electrode: $R = \rho / (2\pi r)$.

(b) Substitute an effective radius $r_{eff}$ for the linear anode such that the surface area of the hemisphere equals the projected surface of the cylindrical anode: $2\pi r_{eff}^2 = \pi d L$, giving $r_{eff} = \sqrt{d L / 2}$.

(c) Combine with logarithmic correction factor accounting for end effects: $r_{eff} = L / (2 \cdot e^{1/2})$ — i.e., $r_{eff} \approx L/(2 \cdot 1.6487)$.

(d) Substitute back into point-source resistance formula and simplify to yield Dwight's result.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A vertical high-silicon cast iron anode has length **L = 1.5 m** and diameter **d = 0.05 m**, installed in a soil of resistivity **ρ = 2000 Ω·cm = 20 Ω·m**. Compute the anode-to-soil resistance.

**Step 1:** Convert ρ to Ω·m to match length in meters: $\rho = 20 \, \Omega \cdot m$. Length $L = 1.5$ m. Diameter $d = 0.05$ m.

**Step 2:** Apply Dwight's formula.

$$
R_A = \frac{\rho}{2 \pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the bracket term. Compute $8L/d = 8 \times 1.5 / 0.05 = 240$.

$$
\ln(240) = \ln(2.4 \times 10^2) = \ln(2.4) + \ln(100) = 0.8755 + 4.6052 = 5.4806
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract 1: $5.4806 - 1 = 4.4806$.

**Step 5:** Compute the prefactor.

$$
\frac{\rho}{2 \pi L} = \frac{20}{2 \times 3.14159 \times 1.5} = \frac{20}{9.4248} = 2.1221 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 6:** Multiply.

$$
R_A = 2.1221 \times 4.4806 = 9.508 \, \Omega
$$

**Result:** $R_A \approx 9.51 \, \Omega$. For a rectifier delivering **20 V DC**, the protective current delivered by this single anode is $I = V / R_A = 20 / 9.51 \approx 2.10$ A.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the IR-Drop Correction Relationship

The measured pipe-to-soil potential while the rectifier is **ON** includes an ohmic voltage drop through the soil between the reference electrode and the pipe surface:

$$
E_{measured(ON)} = E_{true(polarized)} + I \cdot R_{soil,path}
$$

When the rectifier is switched **OFF** momentarily (typically < 50 ms, faster than depolarization but slow enough to discharge the soil capacitance), the $I \cdot R$ drop collapses to zero, leaving the **true polarized potential**:

$$
E_{instant\text{-}OFF} = E_{measured(ON)} - I \cdot R_{soil,path}
$$

This is the potential used for the NACE **−850 mV criterion** because it reflects only the polarization (not the fictitious voltage drop across resistive soil).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Examination Numerical Setups

| Problem Type | Key Equation | Typical Variables Given |
|---|---|---|
| Soil resistivity from Wenner test | $\rho = 2\pi a R$ | $a, V, I$ |
| Anode resistance (vertical) | Dwight's formula | $\rho, L, d$ |
| Anode resistance (horizontal) | Sunde's formula | $\rho, L, r, t$ |
| Required anode count | $N = I_{total} / I_{per\,anode}$ | $I_{total}, R_A, V_{rectifier}$ |
| IR drop correction | $E_{true} = E_{ON} - IR_{soil}$ | $E_{ON}, I, R_{soil}$ |
| Polarization shift | $\Delta E = E_{native} - E_{CP}$ | Native and CP potentials |
| Current density | $i = I / A_{coated\,area}$ | $I, A$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical KTU Numerical Pattern:** Most 14-mark Part B problems combine two of the above — e.g., (a) compute soil resistivity from Wenner readings, then (b) determine the number of anodes required to deliver a specified total current, assuming a given rectifier voltage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the significance of pipe-to-soil potential measurement in cathodic protection of a buried pipeline. State the NACE protection criterion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pipe-to-soil potential measurement is the primary diagnostic technique used to verify whether a buried pipeline is being adequately protected against corrosion. The negative terminal of a high-impedance voltmeter is connected to a Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode placed in the soil directly above the pipeline, while the positive terminal is connected to the pipeline via a test station wire. **NACE SP0169 Criterion:** The pipe-to-soil potential must be **more negative than −850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** when the CP current is interrupted (instant-OFF potential), OR the structure must exhibit a **minimum polarization shift of 100 mV** between its native and CP-protected states. This measurement confirms the structure lies in the immunity region of the Pourbaix diagram for iron. (3 marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any four reference electrodes used in CP measurements and state their standard potentials vs. SHE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
(i) **Copper/Copper Sulphate (CSE):** +0.318 V vs. SHE — used for buried soil applications.
(ii) **Silver/Silver Chloride (Ag/AgCl):** +0.222 V (seawater) — used for marine structures.
(iii) **Saturated Calomel (SCE):** +0.241 V vs. SHE — used in laboratory and concrete.
(iv) **Zinc reference electrode:** −0.763 V vs. SHE — used as a permanent buried reference.
(v) **Standard Hydrogen Electrode (SHE):** 0.000 V — primary laboratory reference. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Wenner four-pin method** for measuring soil resistivity. Derive the formula used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Wenner test on a site near **Kochi Refinery** uses pin spacing **a = 1.5 m**. The voltmeter reads **0.95 V** and the ammeter reads **2.5 mA**. Calculate the soil resistivity and classify the corrosivity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Wenner Four-Pin Method — 7 marks:**

**Sketch (3 marks):** Draw four equally-spaced electrodes C₁, P₁, P₂, C₂ on the soil surface at spacing 'a'. Connect outer pair (C₁, C₂) to a DC source and ammeter. Connect inner pair (P₁, P₂) to a high-impedance voltmeter. (See Mermaid diagram 4.1 above.)

**Procedure (2 marks):** Inject current I through outer electrodes. Measure potential difference ΔV between inner electrodes. Compute resistance R = ΔV/I. Apply Wenner formula $\rho = 2\pi a R$.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1.2:
- For a single point current source on the surface of semi-infinite soil: $V(r) = I\rho/(2\pi r)$
- Potential at P₁: $V_{P_1} = I\rho/(2\pi a) - I\rho/(2\pi \cdot 2a)$
- Potential at P₂: $V_{P_2} = I\rho/(2\pi \cdot 2a) - I\rho/(2\pi a)$
- Difference: $\Delta V = I\rho/(2\pi a)$, hence $R = \Delta V/I = \rho/(2\pi a)$
- Rearranged: $\rho = 2\pi a R$ ✓

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

Given: $a = 1.5$ m $= 150$ cm, $V = 0.95$ V, $I = 2.5$ mA $= 0.0025$ A.

**Step 1:** Compute resistance $R = V/I = 0.95 / 0.0025 = 380 \, \Omega$.

**Step 2:** Apply Wenner formula $\rho = 2\pi a R = 2\pi \times 150 \times 380$.

**Step 3:** Evaluate. $\rho = 2 \times 3.14159 \times 150 \times 380 = 358{,}142 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} \approx 3.58 \times 10^5 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}$.

**Step 4:** Classification — Resistivity of **~358,000 Ω·cm** falls in the **"mildly corrosive"** category (> 20,000 Ω·cm). This is consistent with the dry lateritic red soil of mid-Kerala.

**Step 5:** Engineering recommendation — use **deep-well impressed current anodes** with coke-breeze backfill to overcome the high resistance. (7 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]

**(a)** What are the **protection criteria** used in cathodic protection? Explain the role of **IR drop correction** in pipe-to-soil potential measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pipeline in **Trivandrum district** is protected by **n = 4** vertical silicon-iron anodes, each of length **L = 1.2 m** and diameter **d = 0.06 m**, buried in soil of resistivity **ρ = 3000 Ω·cm**. The rectifier delivers **V = 25 V DC**. Compute the anode-to-soil resistance per anode using Dwight's formula and find the total protective current delivered. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Protection Criteria and IR Drop — 7 marks:**

**Protection Criteria (4 marks):**
The principal criteria, codified in NACE SP0169 / ISO 15589, are:
1. **Negative potential criterion:** Structure-to-soil potential must be more negative than **−850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** (instant-OFF reading).
2. **Polarization shift criterion:** A minimum **cathodic polarization of 100 mV** must be developed between the native and protected states.
3. **Tafel slope criterion:** For high-resistivity soils, the structure potential must be more negative than **−950 mV** vs. CSE.
4. **Net protective current criterion:** The protective current density must be sufficient to neutralize the anodic dissolution current at all points of the structure.

**IR Drop Correction (3 marks):**
When the CP system is energized, the protective current $I$ flows through the resistive soil between the anode and the pipeline, producing a voltage drop $V_{IR} = I \cdot R_{soil}$. The voltmeter reading therefore includes both the **true polarized potential** of the structure and this fictitious IR drop. If uncorrected, the reading appears more negative than reality, leading to false-pass conclusions and overprotection (wasting power and risking hydrogen embrittlement). **Correction methods:** (i) Instant-OFF technique — interrupt the rectifier momentarily and capture the potential in the first decay millisecond; (ii) Extrapolation of the depolarization curve; (iii) Use of a buried reference electrode close to the pipe surface to minimize the soil path resistance. (7 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks:**

Given: $n = 4$ anodes (parallel), $L = 1.2$ m, $d = 0.06$ m, $\rho = 3000 \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} = 30 \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$, $V_{rectifier} = 25$ V.

**Step 1:** Apply Dwight's formula to compute single-anode resistance.

$$
R_A = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\left(\frac{8L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio $8L/d = 8 \times 1.2 / 0.06 = 160$. Take natural logarithm: $\ln(160) = 5.0752$.

**Step 3:** Bracket term: $5.0752 - 1 = 4.0752$.

**Step 4:** Prefactor: $\rho / (2\pi L) = 30 / (2\pi \times 1.2) = 30 / 7.5398 = 3.9789 \, \Omega$.

**Step 5:** Single-anode resistance:

$$
R_A = 3.9789 \times 4.0752 = 16.215 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 6:** Four anodes in parallel — equivalent resistance:

$$
R_{eq} = \frac{R_A}{n} = \frac{16.215}{4} = 4.054 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 7:** Total protective current (assuming negligible cable resistance):

$$
I_{total} = \frac{V_{rectifier}}{R_{eq}} = \frac{25}{4.054} = 6.17 \, \text{A}
$$

**Step 8:** Current per anode = $6.17 / 4 = 1.54$ A. This is well within the conservative rating of silicon-iron anodes (typical rating 5–8 A per anode), confirming the design is safe. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** — Wenner formula gives Ω·cm only when 'a' is in **centimeters**, not meters. Mixing units yields answers off by **factor 100** — instant zero.
> 2. **Confusing ON vs. OFF potential** — The −850 mV NACE criterion applies to the **instant-OFF** (IR-drop-corrected) potential. Applying it to ON potential overestimates protection.
> 3. **Wrong reference electrode conversion** — A reading of −780 mV vs. SCE converts to **−780 + 77 = −857 mV vs. CSE**, which PASSES. Students often forget the +77 mV conversion offset and report a false FAIL.
> 4. **Neglecting anode-to-soil resistance parallel combination** — N anodes in parallel give $R_{eq} = R_A / N$, NOT $R_A \times N$.
> 5. **Wrong Dwight formula** — The formula applies to vertical anodes with $L \gg d$. For horizontal anodes, **Sunde's formula** must be used.
> 6. **Not citing the protection criterion source** — Always mention **NACE SP0169** or **ISO 15589-1** for full marks on criteria-based questions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three-pillar CP monitoring framework:** potential (with reference electrode), current (rectifier output & anode current), and electrolyte resistivity (Wenner four-pin).
- **NACE −850 mV vs. Cu/CuSO₄** is the **gold-standard** protection criterion — always apply to the **instant-OFF** (IR-corrected) potential.
- **100 mV polarization shift** is the alternative criterion used when the −850 mV criterion cannot be reliably applied (e.g., stray currents, dissimilar metals).
- **Cu/CuSO₄ reference electrode** is the field workhorse for soil; **Ag/AgCl** for marine; **SCE** for laboratory; **Zn** for permanent burial.
- **Wenner formula:** $\rho = 2\pi a R$ — four equally-spaced pins, $a$ in cm yields $\rho$ in Ω·cm.
- **Dwight's vertical anode resistance:** $R_A = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln(8L/d) - 1\right]$, valid for $L \gg d$.
- **IR drop error:** $E_{true} = E_{ON} - I \cdot R_{soil-path}$. Always subtract (or use instant-OFF measurement).
- **Soil corrosivity classification:** < 1000 (severe), 1000–5000 (high), 5000–20,000 (moderate), > 20,000 (mild) Ω·cm.
- **High-impedance voltmeter** (≥ 10 MΩ) is mandatory for potential measurement to avoid current drain through the meter that would polarize the reference electrode.
- **Diagnostic surveys:** CIPS, DCVG, Pearson, and attenuation surveys extend routine measurement into the spatial domain to locate coating defects and current shortages.
- **Reference electrode drift** must be checked periodically — Cu/CuSO₄ cells lose saturation over 6–12 months and must be replenished.
- **Conversion offsets (must memorize):** CSE = SHE + 318 mV; SCE = SHE + 241 mV; Ag/AgCl(seawater) = SHE + 222 mV; Zn = SHE − 763 mV.
- **Soil resistivity varies seasonally** — winter (wet) values may be **10× lower** than summer (dry) values in tropical Kerala. Always measure at multiple times of year for design.
- **Anode current capacity** (silicon iron: ~5–8 A each; graphite: ~1–3 A; Mg alloy: limited by galvanic driving voltage) limits how many anodes can be paralleled for a given total current.
- **Test stations** are the physical access points on buried pipelines where the structure lead wire emerges for measurement — every kilometer of pipeline should have at least one.
- **Coupons** (small steel samples electrically connected to the pipe via a shunt resistor) are used to measure **true polarization** without IR drop — current flows through the coupon while the structure remains at open-circuit potential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrosion Control by Inhibition: Scope of Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Corrosion Control by Inhibition: Scope of Inhibitors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **corrosion inhibitor** is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations (typically **0.1% to 1.0%** by weight) to a corrosive environment, effectively reduces the rate of corrosion of the metal exposed to that environment without significantly altering the concentration of any bulk reactant in the medium. Inhibitors are classified as one of the most economical, flexible, and widely-applied methods of corrosion control because they can be introduced directly into the aggressive environment without altering the process equipment or the metallurgy of the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize):** > A corrosion inhibitor is "a substance which retards or suppresses the corrosion reaction when added to the corrosive medium in relatively small concentrations, normally not exceeding **1.0 g/L** of the corrosive solution, without causing any appreciable change in the concentration of the corrosive agent itself." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Molecular Shield Think of a corrosion inhibitor like a **vaccine for metal**. - When a virus attacks a body, a vaccine teaches the immune system to block the attack at the surface — the pathogen cannot penetrate. - Similarly, when corrosive ions (like Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, or H⁺) attack a metal surface, the inhibitor molecule **parks itself at the active corrosion site** and forms a thin, invisible protective molecular film. The aggressive ions are denied access to the bare metal. **Even simpler analogy — A "Do Not Disturb" sign on a hotel door:** - The hotel door = metal surface - Rowdy guests = aggressive ions (Cl⁻, H⁺, O₂) - The "Do Not Disturb" sign = inhibitor molecule (adsorbed film) - Peace maintained = corrosion rate reduced ## 1.3 Scope of Inhibitors — Why They Matter in Industry The "scope" refers to **the range of environments, metals, and industrial processes** where inhibitors find practical utility: | Industrial Domain | Inhibitor Used | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Petroleum refining (sour crude)** | H₂S scavengers, imidazolines | Prevent sulfide stress cracking | | **Cooling water systems** | Phosphates, chromates, nitrites, molybdates | Control pitting in heat exchangers | | **Pickling (acid cleaning of steel)** | Dibenzyl sulfoxide, quinoline derivatives | Prevent base-metal attack by HCl/H₂SO₄ | | **Boiler feed water** | Hydrazine, morpholine, DEHA | Scavenge dissolved O₂, neutralize CO₂ | | **Automotive antifreeze** | Borates, silicates, nitrites | Stop cavitation-corrosion in radiators | | **Acid storage and transport** | Thiourea, propargyl alcohol | Safe shipment of mineral acids | | **Vapor-phase packaging** | Dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN), cyclohexylamine carbonate | Protect machinery in transit | > [!NOTE] > **Key Advantage:** Unlike coatings, inhibitors are **regenerative** — once consumed or washed away, they can be replenished simply by adding more inhibitor to the system. ## 1.4 Essential Requirements of a Good Corrosion Inhibitor A practical inhibitor must satisfy ALL the following conditions: 1. **Effective at low concentration** (< 0.5% typically). 2. **Compatible** with the process chemistry (does not poison catalysts, does not react with the product). 3. **Stable** under operating temperature and pressure. 4. **Non-toxic** to humans and the environment (driving shift to "green inhibitors"). 5. **Economical** — cost per unit corrosion prevented must be lower than alternative methods. 6. **Easily dispersible** in the corrosive medium. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Inhibitors Inhibitors can be classified by **three different criteria** — examiners frequently test all three: ### A. By Electrode Reaction Controlled **1. Anodic Inhibitors (Passivators)** - Suppress the anodic reaction: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ - They form a **passive oxide film** on the metal surface (typically > 2 nm thick). - Examples: **Chromates, nitrites, molybdates, tungstates, phosphates**. - *Risk:* If concentration falls below a critical threshold, localized pitting is intensified. **2. Cathodic Inhibitors** - Suppress the cathodic reaction: $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ or $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ - They either (a) slow H⁺ reduction or (b) precipitate to block cathodic sites. - Examples: **ZnSO₄, polyphosphates, calcium carbonate, Mg²⁺ salts, sulfites**. - *Safe:* Insufficient dosage does NOT accelerate localized attack. **3. Mixed Inhibitors** - Reduce both anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously by forming a continuous adsorbed film on the entire metal surface. - Examples: **Organic amines, imidazolines, thiourea derivatives, fatty acids**. ### B. By Chemical Nature | Type | Description | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Inorganic** | Usually anodic or cathodic; non-film forming or film forming | Chromates, phosphates, nitrites, silicates | | **Organic** | Film-forming via adsorption; contain polar groups (N, S, O, P) | Amines, imidazolines, Schiff bases, pyridines | | **Vapor Phase (VPI)** | Volatile compounds; protect in enclosed spaces | Dicyclohexylamine nitrite, benzotriazole | ### C. By Mode of Action **1. Adsorption-type inhibitors** — physical/chemical adsorption on metal. **2. Film-forming inhibitors** — produce a thick, sometimes visible precipitate film. **3. Passivating inhibitors** — push the metal into the passive region of the Pourbaix diagram. **4. Pickling inhibitors** — organic additives used during acid-cleaning operations. ## 2.2 Mechanism of Inhibition — The Molecular Story ### For Organic Inhibitors: The inhibitor molecule must possess: - A **polar head** (heteroatom: N, S, O, P) that anchors to the metal surface. - A **non-polar tail** (hydrocarbon chain) that creates a hydrophobic barrier against water/aggressive ions. **Physical Adsorption (Physisorption):** - Weak Van der Waals forces between the polar head and the metal surface. - Process is reversible, fast, and favored at low temperatures. **Chemical Adsorption (Chemisorption):** - Strong coordinate-covalent bond — lone pair from heteroatom shared with empty d-orbital of the metal. - Process is irreversible, slower, and favored at higher temperatures. ### For Inorganic Passivating Inhibitors: The inhibitor is itself an **oxidizing anion** that oxidizes the metal surface to form a stable oxide: $$\text{CrO}_4^{2-} + 3Fe + 8H^+ \rightarrow \text{Cr}^{3+} + 3Fe^{2+} + 4H_2O$$ The freshly formed Cr³⁺ and Fe²⁺ then co-precipitate as a mixed oxide-hydroxide layer that physically blocks both anodic and cathodic sites. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{IE (\%)} = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ | IE = Inhibition Efficiency; CR₀ = Corrosion rate without inhibitor; CRᵢ = Corrosion rate with inhibitor | CR in **mm/yr** or **mpy** | | 2 | $\text{IE (\%)} = \dfrac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$ | W₀, Wᵢ = weight loss of coupons without/with inhibitor | W in **mg** or **g** | | 3 | $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0}$ | θ = Surface coverage fraction | Dimensionless, 0 < θ < 1 | | 4 | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | Langmuir isotherm linear form | C = inhibitor concentration (mol/L) | | 5 | $\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \, K_{ads})$ | Standard free energy of adsorption | R = 8.314 J/mol·K; T in K | | 6 | $\log CR = \log A - \dfrac{E_a}{2.303RT}$ | Arrhenius equation for CR variation | Eₐ in **kJ/mol** | | 7 | $\text{Pitting Index} = \dfrac{\text{deepest pit}}{\text{average penetration}}$ | Measures localized corrosion tendency | Dimensionless; > 1 indicates pitting | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition Reminder:** > - If θ → 1, then IE → 100% (perfect inhibition). > - If θ → 0, then IE → 0% (inhibitor ineffective). > - For **physisorption**: $\vert \Delta G_{ads}^0 \vert < 40$ kJ/mol > - For **chemisorption**: $\vert \Delta G_{ads}^0 \vert > 40$ kJ/mol ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refinery overhead systems** use morpholine and diethylaminoethanol to neutralize CO₂ and H₂S in condensate lines, preventing sour corrosion that historically caused catastrophic failures. - **Bridge decks** in coastal Kerala/Mumbai regions use calcium nitrite admixed in concrete to protect embedded rebar from chloride-induced pitting. - **Aerospace industry** uses vapor-phase benzotriazole to protect copper alloy components in missile and aircraft storage. - **Municipal water supply** uses zinc-polyphosphate blends to control "red water" corrosion in household galvanized pipes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Inhibition Efficiency (IE) from Weight-Loss Data **Given Data (Typical KTU-style problem):** - Weight loss of mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ **without** inhibitor = $W_0 = 280$ mg - Weight loss of mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ **with** $5 \times 10^{-4}$ M inhibitor = $W_i = 35$ mg - Exposure time = $t = 6$ h - Specimen area = $A = 20$ cm² - Density of mild steel = $\rho = 7.86$ g/cm³ **Step 1 — Surface Coverage Calculation:** $$\theta = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0}$$ Plugging in numerical values: $$\theta = \frac{280 -
