35}{280} = \frac{245}{280} = 0.875$$ So **87.5% of the metal surface** is covered by the inhibitor film. **Step 2 — Inhibition Efficiency Calculation:** $$\text{IE (\%)} = \theta \times 100 = 87.5\%$$ Or, equivalently, using the alternative direct formula: $$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$$ $$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{245}{280} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$ **Step 3 — Corrosion Rate with Inhibitor:** $$CR_i = \frac{W_i}{A \cdot t} = \frac{35 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}}{20 \text{ cm}^2 \times 6 \text{ h}} = 2.917 \times 10^{-4} \text{ g/(cm}^2\cdot\text{h)}$$ **Step 4 — Corrosion Rate without Inhibitor:** $$CR_0 = \frac{W_0}{A \cdot t} = \frac{280 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 6} = 2.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/(cm}^2\cdot\text{h)}$$ **Step 5 — Verification via IE Formula:** $$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{2.333 \times 10^{-3} - 2.917 \times 10^{-4}}{2.333 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100$$ $$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{2.041 \times 10^{-3}}{2.333 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 = 87.49\%$$ > ✓ The two methods match (within rounding tolerance), confirming the correctness of the calculation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm and Free Energy **Assumption:** Each inhibitor molecule occupies only ONE active site on the metal surface, with no interaction between adsorbed molecules. At equilibrium, rate of adsorption = rate of desorption: $$k_a \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$ Rearranging for the coverage fraction: $$\frac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = \frac{k_a}{k_d} \cdot C = K_{ads} \cdot C$$ Inverting: $$\frac{1 - \theta}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C}$$ $$\frac{1}{\theta} - 1 = \frac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C}$$ $$\frac{1}{\theta} = 1 + \frac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C}$$ Multiplying both sides by $C$: $$\frac{C}{\theta} = C + \frac{1}{K_{ads}}$$ This is the **linear Langmuir form**: $y = mx + b$, with $y = C/\theta$, $x = C$, slope = 1, intercept = $1/K_{ads}$. **Standard Free Energy of Adsorption** (using the universal reference concentration of **55.5 mol/L** for the solvent water): $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$ **Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style):** - T = 303 K (30 °C) - $K_{ads} = 2.4 \times 10^4$ L/mol (from intercept of plot) $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = - (8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 2.4 \times 10^4)$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -2519.14 \times \ln(1.332 \times 10^6)$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -2519.14 \times 14.103 = -35{,}525 \text{ J/mol}$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 \approx -35.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ **Conclusion:** Since $\vert \Delta G_{ads}^0 \vert < 40$ kJ/mol, the adsorption is predominantly **physical (physisorption)**, with possible contribution from electrostatic interaction. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Activation Energy Shift on Adding Inhibitor Arrhenius equation for uninhibited system: $$\log CR_0 = \log A_0 - \frac{E_a^0}{2.303 R T}$$ Arrhenius equation for inhibited system: $$\log CR_i = \log A_i - \frac{E_a^i}{2.303 R T}$$ Subtracting the second equation from the first: $$\log \frac{CR_0}{CR_i} = \frac{E_a^i - E_a^0}{2.303 R T} = \frac{\Delta E_a}{2.303 R T}$$ Since adding an effective inhibitor **increases** the activation energy barrier, $\Delta E_a > 0$, leading to $CR_0 > CR_i$ (i.e., inhibition is achieved). $$\text{IE (\%)} = \left(1 - 10^{-\Delta E_a / 2.303RT}\right) \times 100$$ This expression enables prediction of inhibition efficiency at any temperature if $\Delta E_a$ is known from two temperature-points. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Inhibitor Selection by Environment | Environment | Recommended Inhibitor Class | Function | |---|---|---| | **Acidic (HCl, H₂SO₄)** | Organic amines, thiourea, imidazolines | Adsorption film | | **Neutral (cooling water)** | Phosphates, nitrites, molybdates, Zn²⁺ | Passivation | | **Alkaline (boiler water)** | Hydrazine, sulfite, DEHA | Oxygen scavenging | | **Seawater / brine** | Calcium carbonate, polyphosphates | Cathodic precipitation | | **Oil & gas (H₂S, CO₂)** | Imidazolines, quaternary ammonium salts | Film formation | | **Atmospheric (vapor)** | Benzotriazole, DICHAN | Vapor-phase adsorption | | **Concrete pore solution** | Calcium nitrite, amino alcohols | Anodic passivation of rebar | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Corrosion Inhibitors ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Corrosion Inhibitors]:::root --> B1[By Electrode Reaction] A --> B2[By Chemical Nature] A --> B3[By Mode of Action] B1 --> C1[Anodic Inhibitors] B1 --> C2[Cathodic Inhibitors] B1 --> C3[Mixed Inhibitors] C1 --> D1[Examples: Chromates, Nitrites, Molybdates] C1 --> D2[Risk: Pitting if underdosed] C2 --> D3[Examples: ZnSO4, Polyphosphates] C2 --> D4[Safe at low concentration] C3 --> D5[Examples: Amines, Imidazolines] B2 --> E1[Inorganic: Chromates, Phosphates] B2 --> E2[Organic: Amines, Schiff bases] B2 --> E3[Vapor Phase: Benzotriazole] B3 --> F1[Adsorption Type] B3 --> F2[Film Forming Type] B3 --> F3[Passivating Type] F1 --> G1[Physisorption or Chemisorption] F2 --> G2[Precipitation Layer] F3 --> G3[Oxide Film] classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mechanism Flow Diagram — Organic Inhibitor Action ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Corrosive Medium
H+, Cl-, O2] --> B[Approach Metal Surface] B --> C{Inhibitor
Present?} C -->|No| D[Rapid Anodic Dissolution
M - ne- - Mn+] C -->|Yes| E[Inhibitor Molecule
Diffuses to Surface] E --> F[Polar Head Anchors
Heteroatom N/S/O] F --> G[Non-polar Tail Forms
Hydrophobic Barrier] G --> H[Coverage theta Approaches 1] H --> I[Corrosion Rate Drops
IE Approaches 100 percent] D --> J[Material Loss Failure] classDef process fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef failure fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef success fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class A,B,D,E,F,G,J process class I,J failure ``` ## 4.3 Selection Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Begin Selection]) --> Q1{Environment Type?} Q1 -->|Acid Pickling| P1[Use Organic Amine or Thiourea] Q1 -->|Neutral Aqueous| P2[Use Phosphate or Nitrite] Q1 -->|Alkaline Boiler| P3[Use Hydrazine or DEHA] Q1 -->|Vapor Phase| P4[Use Benzotriazole or DICHAN] Q1 -->|Oilfield Sour| P5[Use Imidazoline] P1 --> Q2{Temperature of Operation?} P2 --> Q2 P3 --> Q2 P4 --> Q2 P5 --> Q2 Q2 -->|Below 60 deg C| R1[Physisorption Acceptable] Q2 -->|Above 60 deg C| R2[Choose Chemisorption Type Inhibitor] R1 --> End([Inhibitor Selected - Lab Validation Required]) R2 --> End ``` ## 4.4 Pourbaix-Based Conceptual Map of Passivating Inhibitors ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Pourbaix Diagram of Fe-H2O]:::axis --> V1[Active Zone: Fe2+ soluble] P --> V2[Passive Zone: Fe2O3, Fe3O4 insoluble] P --> V3[Immune Zone: Fe metal stable] V2 --> I1[Anodic Inhibitor Pushes
Potential into Passive Region] I1 --> I2[Corrosion Rate Drops by Orders of Magnitude] classDef axis fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef zone fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define corrosion inhibitor. List the requirements of a good corrosion inhibitor.** **Model Answer (3 marks — strict word count discipline):** A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, reduces the rate of attack on the metal without significantly reacting with the bulk medium constituents (1 mark). **Requirements of a good inhibitor (2 marks — any 4 of the following):** 1. Effective at low concentration (typically < 1 g/L). 2. Chemically stable at the operating temperature and pressure. 3. Compatible with the process fluid (does not contaminate product). 4. Non-toxic and environmentally acceptable. 5. Economically viable relative to alternative protection methods. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between anodic and cathodic inhibitors with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Anodic Inhibitor | Cathodic Inhibitor | |---|---|---| | **Reaction suppressed** | Metal dissolution $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$ | Reduction $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ | | **Mode** | Forms passive oxide film | Precipitates at cathodic sites | | **Example 1** | Sodium chromate (Na₂CrO₄) | Zinc sulfate (ZnSO₄) | | **Example 2** | Sodium nitrite (NaNO₂) | Sodium polyphosphate | | **Risk** | Under-dosing causes localized pitting | Safer even at low concentration | --- ## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 Choice]` **(a)** Classify corrosion inhibitors based on (i) electrode reaction controlled and (ii) mode of action. Discuss the mechanism of mixed-type inhibitors with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The weight loss of mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ without inhibitor is 720 mg, and with $1 \times 10^{-3}$ M of inhibitor is 95 mg (exposure 5 h, area 25 cm²). Calculate: (i) Surface coverage θ, (ii) Inhibition efficiency IE %, (iii) Corrosion rate without inhibitor in mpy. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) Classification & Mechanism (7 marks):** **Classification based on electrode reaction (3 marks):** 1. **Anodic inhibitors** — e.g., chromates, nitrites, molybdates. Suppress the anodic dissolution reaction by forming a thin passive oxide film on the metal surface. They shift the corrosion potential in the noble (positive) direction. 2. **Cathodic inhibitors** — e.g., Zn²⁺ salts, polyphosphates, Mg²⁺. Suppress the cathodic reduction reaction by precipitating insoluble hydroxides at cathodic sites, blocking oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution. 3. **Mixed inhibitors** — e.g., organic amines, imidazolines, thiourea. Reduce BOTH anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously by adsorbing as a continuous protective film across the entire metal surface, without appreciably shifting the corrosion potential. **Classification based on mode of action (2 marks):** - **Adsorption-type** (physisorption/chemisorption) — mostly organic inhibitors. - **Film-forming type** — form thick, visible precipitate layers. - **Passivating type** — oxidizing anions like CrO₄²⁻, NO₂⁻ that passivate the surface. - **Pickling inhibitors** — organic additives that protect base metal during acid cleaning. **Mechanism of Mixed Inhibitors with Sketch (2 marks):** The mixed-type inhibitor molecule has a **polar functional group** (containing N, S, O, or P atom) that chemisorbs onto the metal surface, and a **long non-polar hydrocarbon tail** that extends outward, creating a hydrophobic barrier. This film acts as a physical shield covering both anodic and cathodic sites, blocking the access of aggressive ions (H⁺, Cl⁻, O₂) to the metal. Sketch description (to be drawn in the answer script): ``` H2O / H+ / Cl- =============== (Aggressive ions cannot penetrate) [Tail][Tail][Tail][Tail] (Hydrophobic hydrocarbon tails) | | | | [Polar head chemisorbed on Fe surface] ============================================ Fe metal ``` **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - $W_0 = 720$ mg (without inhibitor) - $W_i = 95$ mg (with inhibitor) - $t = 5$ h - $A = 25$ cm² - Density of mild steel ρ = **7.86 g/cm³** **Part (i) — Surface Coverage θ:** $$\theta = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} = \frac{720 - 95}{720} = \frac{625}{720} = 0.868$$ **θ = 0.868 (dimensionless)** — *1.5 marks* **Part (ii) — Inhibition Efficiency IE %:** $$\text{IE (\%)} = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100 = 0.868 \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\text{IE = 86.8\%}}$$ — *2 marks* **Part (iii) — Corrosion Rate Without Inhibitor in mpy:** First compute CR₀ in g/(cm²·h): $$CR_0 = \frac{W_0}{A \cdot t} = \frac{720 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 5} = \frac{0.720}{125} = 5.76 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/(cm}^2\cdot\text{h)}$$ Convert to mpy (mils per year): Conversion factor: **1 g/(cm²·h) = 438,000 mpy** $$CR_0 = 5.76 \times 10^{-3} \times 438{,}000 = 2523 \text{ mpy}$$ $$\boxed{CR_0 \approx 2523 \text{ mpy}}$$ — *3.5 marks* --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 Choice Alternate]` **(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of organic corrosion inhibitors with reference to adsorption isotherms (Langmuir and Freundlich). Discuss the significance of free energy of adsorption $\Delta G_{ads}^0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The following data were obtained for an organic inhibitor at 303 K. Verify whether adsorption follows Langmuir isotherm and calculate $\Delta G_{ads}^0$: | C (mol/L) | θ | |---|---| | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.30 | | $2 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.46 | | $4 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.62 | | $8 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.74 | **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) Organic Inhibitor Mechanism + Isotherms (7 marks):** **Mechanism (3 marks):** Organic inhibitors function through **adsorption at the metal-solution interface**. Their effectiveness depends on: 1. Presence of a polar functional group with heteroatom (N, S, O, P) — supplies lone pair electrons. 2. Availability of π-electrons (aromatic rings, C=C double bonds). 3. Molecular size, planarity, and electron density at donor atoms. 4. Nature of metal surface (d-orbital availability). The adsorption occurs either by **physisorption** (weak Van der Waals, low T, reversible) or **chemisorption** (strong coordinate bond, high T, irreversible via sharing of lone pair into metal's empty d-orbitals). **Langmuir Isotherm (2 marks):** The Langmuir isotherm assumes monolayer adsorption with no interaction between adsorbed molecules: $$\frac{C}{\theta} = C + \frac{1}{K_{ads}}$$ Plotting $C/\theta$ vs $C$ gives slope = 1 (verification), and intercept = $1/K_{ads}$. **Freundlich Isotherm (1 mark):** For heterogeneous surfaces: $$\theta = K \cdot C^n$$ Log form: $\log \theta = \log K + n \log C$. Used when Langmuir fails (often at high concentrations). **Significance of $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ (1 mark):** - If $\vert \Delta G_{ads}^0 \vert < 40$ kJ/mol → **physisorption** (electrostatic). - If $\vert \Delta G_{ads}^0 \vert > 40$ kJ/mol → **chemisorption** (coordinate bond). More negative $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ = stronger, more spontaneous adsorption = higher inhibition efficiency. **(b) Verification of Langmuir Isotherm (7 marks):** Construct the Langmuir linear plot table: | C (mol/L) | θ | C/θ | |---|---|---| | $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.30 | $3.33 \times 10^{-4}$ | | $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.46 | $4.35 \times 10^{-4}$ | | $4.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.62 | $6.45 \times 10^{-4}$ | | $8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.74 | $10.81 \times 10^{-4}$ | Plotting $C/\theta$ (y-axis) vs $C$ (x-axis): - Slope ≈ 1 (from data: $(10.81 - 3.33) \times 10^{-4} / (8.0 - 1.0) \times 10^{-4} = 7.48/7.0 \approx 1.07$) ✓ - Intercept = average value of $C/\theta - C$ ≈ $3.33 \times 10^{-4} - 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 2.33 \times 10^{-4}$ **Calculation of $K_{ads}$:** $$\text{Intercept} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} \approx 2.33 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{2.33 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4292 \text{ L/mol}$$ **Calculation of $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ at T = 303 K:** $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \times K_{ads})$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -(8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 4292)$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -2519.14 \times \ln(238{,}206)$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -2519.14 \times 12.38$$ $$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -31{,}186 \text{ J/mol}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads}^0 \approx -31.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$ **Conclusion (verification):** - Slope ≈ 1 confirms **Langmuir-type monolayer adsorption**. *(3 marks for plot/verification)* - $\vert \Delta G_{ads}^0 \vert = 31.2$ kJ/mol < 40 kJ/mol, confirming adsorption is predominantly **physisorption with electrostatic character**. *(2 marks)* - $K_{ads}$ is moderately high, indicating reasonably strong adsorption. *(1 mark)* - $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ is negative, confirming **spontaneous adsorption**. *(1 mark)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Traps — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:** > > 1. **Units Blunder:** When converting CR to mpy, students often forget the constant **438,000**. Without it, the entire Part (b) numerical carries zero marks. > > 2. **Confusing Anodic/Cathodic Examples:** Chromates are ANODIC. Zinc sulfate is CATHODIC. Mixing these up = -1 to -2 marks instantly. > > 3. **Sign of ΔG_ads⁰:** Always write the negative sign explicitly in the answer. A positive sign makes the entire thermodynamics interpretation wrong. > > 4. **Slope of Langmuir Plot:** If slope ≠ 1, the system does NOT follow Langmuir. Examiners love to give data that doesn't fit perfectly — students must compute and conclude honestly. > > 5. **Don't write general essays:** KTU values **specific, crisp points**. Writing 3 pages without keywords = 3 marks even if correct content. > > 6. **Pickling Inhibitor Confusion:** Pickling inhibitors PROTECT the base metal from the acid — they do NOT stop the pickling action on rust/scale. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Sem Memorize List):** - **Inhibitor Definition** — A substance that reduces CR when added in small concentrations (< 1 g/L) without reacting with bulk medium. - **Anodic Inhibitors** — Chromates, nitrites, molybdates. *Risk:* under-dose causes pitting. *Mode:* passive oxide film. - **Cathodic Inhibitors** — ZnSO₄, polyphosphates. *Safe* at low conc. *Mode:* precipitate at cathodic sites. - **Mixed Inhibitors** — Amines, imidazolines. *Mode:* adsorption film covers entire surface. - **Vapor Phase Inhibitors** — Benzotriazole (Cu), DICHAN (steel). - **Organic Inhibitor Structure** — Polar head (N, S, O) + non-polar tail = hydrophobic barrier. - **IE Formula** — $IE = \dfrac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$ - **Surface Coverage** — $\theta = \dfrac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0}$ - **Langmuir Isotherm** — $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$ → slope = 1. - **ΔG_ads⁰ Sign Rule** — Negative = spontaneous; magnitude < 40 kJ/mol = physisorption; > 40 kJ/mol = chemisorption. - **Arrhenius for IE** — Effective inhibitor INCREASES activation energy. - **Pitting Index** = deepest pit depth / average penetration. Index > 1 = pitting. - **Industrial Domains** — Refineries (morpholine), boilers (hydrazine), cooling water (phosphates), pickling (thiourea), concrete (Ca(NO₂)₂), vapor-phase packaging (benzotriazole). - **Conversion Factors to Remember:** 1 g/(cm²·h) = **438,000 mpy**; 1 mpy = **0.0254 mm/yr**. - **Selection Hierarchy:** Environment type → inhibitor class → temperature compatibility → toxicity check → lab validation → field trial.

topics.title: Examples of Application of Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples of Application of Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Inhibitor Application Engineering** refers to the systematic, industrially-validated deployment of chemical inhibitors—organic, inorganic, anodic, cathodic, mixed, or volatile—to mitigate corrosion in defined service environments. Each industrial system (acid pickling baths, cooling towers, boilers, oil & gas pipelines, reinforced concrete, automotive coolants, etc.) demands a tailored inhibitor chemistry selected on the basis of operating **pH**, **temperature**, **fluid velocity**, **dissolved oxygen**, and the **electrode potential** of the structural metal.

In KTU 2024 parlance, "application of inhibitors" is treated as the **engineering translation** of corrosion electrochemistry into field practice. The selection and dose of an inhibitor is governed by three measurable quantities:

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Inhibitor Efficiency | $\eta$ or $\text{IE}\%$ | **70 – 99 %** in commercial service |
| Critical Dose / Threshold Concentration | $C_{\text{crit}}$ | **10 – 500 ppm** (cooling water); **0.1 – 2 %** (pickling) |
| Synergistic Index | $S_{ij}$ | $> 1$ = synergism, $< 1$ = antagonism |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Raincoat for Metals"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine your phone left in monsoon rain. A zip-lock pouch (mechanical barrier), silica gel (dehumidifier), and a thin polymer coat (surface film) all protect it in **different ways**. **Inhibitors in industry work the same way** — they are the chemical "raincoats" added *to the environment* (not the metal) to prevent the aggressive medium from attacking the metal surface.

- **Pickling inhibitors** = temporary paint worn only during acid cleaning, then removed.
- **Cooling-water inhibitors** = permanent raincoat for the whole condenser tube network.
- **Vapor-phase inhibitors (VPI)** = scented sachets inside a steel toolbox, protecting from humidity in the air.
- **Concrete inhibitors** = calcium-based health supplements mixed into reinforced concrete for decades-long protection.

### 1.3 Classification by Application Mode

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-4 highlight:** Inhibitors are **not classified by chemistry alone** but by **where and how they are deployed**. The KTU examiner expects students to know the *application matrix*, not just the inhibitor names.

1. **Continuous-dosing systems** — cooling water, boilers, oil & gas.
2. **Batch / intermittent systems** — acid pickling, pre-commission cleaning.
3. **Vapor-phase systems** — packaging, storage, shipping.
4. **Impressed systems** — concrete admixed inhibitors, inhibitor-laced coatings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Industrial Application Decision Tree

When a corrosion engineer must select an inhibitor for a real plant, the decision follows a layered logic:

**Step 1 — Environment Profiling**
- Identify the bulk electrolyte: $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, $\text{HCl}$, $\text{NaCl}$, $\text{CO}_2$/$\text{H}_2\text{S}$ brine, distilled water, glycol mixture, concrete pore solution.
- Measure **pH**, **temperature ($T$)**, **dissolved $\text{O}_2$**, **flow regime** (laminar / turbulent — quantified by Reynolds number, $\text{Re}$).

**Step 2 — Metal Identification**
- Active metal (Fe, Zn, Mg) → anodic inhibitors preferred.
- Passive metal (stainless, Al, Ti) → cathodic or mixed inhibitors.

**Step 3 — Failure Mode Mapping**
- Uniform corrosion → film-forming inhibitors.
- Pitting / crevice → aggressive-ion scavengers (e.g., nitrite against $\text{Cl}^-$).
- SCC, HIC → specific molecular design (imidazolines for sour service).

**Step 4 — Inhibitor Class Lock-In**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind each class:**  
> • **Anodic inhibitors** (chromate, nitrite, molybdate, phosphate) form a **passivating oxide** that blocks anodic dissolution.  
> • **Cathodic inhibitors** (polyphosphates, Zn salts, Ca salts) precipitate over cathodic zones, blocking $\text{O}_2$ reduction.  
> • **Mixed inhibitors** (amines, imidazolines, triazoles) adsorb on both anodic and cathodic sites via lone-pair donation.  
> • **Vapor-phase inhibitors** (dicyclohexylamine nitrite, benzotriazole) volatilize, then adsorb from the gas phase onto metallic surfaces, displacing moisture.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Boundary / Typical Range | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Inhibitor Efficiency | $\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0}\times 100$ | $0\% \le \text{IE}\le 100\%$ | Compare inhibitor performance |
| 2 | Surface Coverage | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0}$ | $0 \le \theta \le 1$ | Inputs to adsorption isotherms |
| 3 | Langmuir Isotherm | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{\text{ads}}} + C$ | Valid for monolayer physical adsorption | Pickling, cooling water |
| 4 | Temkin Isotherm | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{f}\ln(K_{\text{ads}} C)$ | Valid for chemisorption with lateral interactions | Oil-field inhibitors |
| 5 | Frumkin Isotherm | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta}\exp(-2f\theta) = K_{\text{ads}} C$ | Valid for non-ideal, interactive adsorption | VPI, packaging |
| 6 | Synergistic Parameter | $S_{12} = \dfrac{1 - (\eta_{1+2})}{1 - \eta'_1 - \eta'_2}$ | $S>1$ synergism; $S<1$ antagonism | Blend design |
| 7 | Arrhenius Inhibition | $\ln(\text{CR}_i) = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | $E_a$ rises with inhibitor | Predict dose at high $T$ |
| 8 | Pitting Resistance with Inhibitor | $\text{PRE}' = \text{PRE} + k\cdot\log C_{\text{inh}}$ | Increases with $\text{Mo}$, $\text{N}$, and inhibitor | Stainless / alloy selection |
| 9 | Charge-Transfer Resistance Shift | $\eta_{\text{Rct}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{ct,inhib}} - R_{\text{ct,blank}}}{R_{\text{ct,inhib}}}$ | Used in EIS analysis | Lab validation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Pitfall:** When writing IE%, always quote $\text{CR}$ in **consistent units** (mm/yr **or** mpy). Mixing units gives wrong percentage. Examiners in Dec 2023 specifically deducted marks for this.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries and petrochemical plants** lose an estimated **\$ 2.5 billion/year globally** to internal corrosion; proper inhibitor dosing saves > **40 %** of this.
- **Cooling-tower inhibitor programs** (e.g., molybdate–Zn–HEDP blend) keep $\text{pH}$ between **6.5 – 8.5** to avoid CaCO$_3$ scaling *and* under-deposit corrosion.
- **Pickling inhibitors** such as Rodine ${}^{\text{TM}}$ or Dodigen ${}^{\text{TM}}$ allow **> 95 % scale removal** with **< 1 % base-metal attack**.
- **Vapor-phase inhibitors** protect > **\$ 80 billion** worth of military hardware and bearings in storage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inhibitor Efficiency from Weight-Loss Data

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module-4 questions. We derive it from first principles using Faraday's law.

**Starting point — corrosion rate from weight loss:**
The corrosion rate in mm/yr is given by:

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta m}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}
$$

Where:
- $\Delta m$ = mass loss in grams
- $A$ = exposed area in cm$^2$
- $t$ = exposure time in hours
- $\rho$ = metal density in g/cm$^3$
- $K$ = $8.76 \times 10^4$ (unit conversion constant for mm/yr)

**For the uninhibited blank:**
$$
\text{CR}_0 = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta m_0}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}
$$

**For the inhibited system:**
$$
\text{CR}_i = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta m_i}{A \cdot t \cdot \rho}
$$

Because $K$, $A$, $t$, and $\rho$ are identical in the two parallel experiments (the only thing changed is whether the inhibitor is added to the same bulk solution), they cancel in the ratio.

**Subtracting to find "how much corrosion was prevented":**
$$
\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i = \dfrac{K}{A t \rho}\left(\Delta m_0 - \Delta m_i\right)
$$

**Dividing by the unprotected rate and multiplying by 100:**
$$
\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0}\times 100
$$

**In mass-loss form (since $K$, $A$, $t$, $\rho$ cancel):**
$$
\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{\Delta m_0 - \Delta m_i}{\Delta m_0}\times 100
$$

This simplification is the form KTU examiners expect in lab-data numericals.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Acid Pickling Inhibitor

> A steel coupon of area **20 cm$^2$** is pickled in **1 M HCl** at **50 °C** for **2 hours**.
> - Without inhibitor: mass loss = **0.420 g**.
> - With **0.5 % Rodine-92**: mass loss = **0.034 g**.
> - Density of steel: **7.86 g/cm$^3$**.
> Compute $\text{CR}_0$, $\text{CR}_i$, and $\text{IE}\%$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\text{CR}_0$ (mm/yr):**

$$
\text{CR}_0 = \dfrac{8.76\times10^4 \times 0.420}{20\times 2 \times 7.86}
$$

$$
\text{CR}_0 = \dfrac{36792}{314.4} = 117.0\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\text{CR}_i$ (mm/yr):**

$$
\text{CR}_i = \dfrac{8.76\times10^4 \times 0.034}{314.4}
$$

$$
\text{CR}_i = \dfrac{2978.4}{314.4} = 9.47\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\text{IE}\%$:**

$$
\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{117.0 - 9.47}{117.0}\times 100
$$

$$
\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{107.53}{117.0}\times 100 = 91.9\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A pickling inhibitor with $\text{IE} \approx 92\%$ is **excellent** — commercial Rodine-grade inhibitors are accepted in industry if $\text{IE} \ge 90\%$ at the recommended dose.

### 3.3 Derivation of Langmuir Isotherm and Its Use in Inhibitor Selection

Start from the equilibrium: $\text{Inh}_{\text{(bulk)}} + S_{\text{(free)}} \rightleftharpoons \text{Inh–S}_{\text{(ads)}}$, where $S$ is a vacant surface site.

Let:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered
- $C$ = bulk inhibitor concentration
- $K_{\text{ads}}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant

The Langmuir postulates:
1. Each site binds one inhibitor molecule (monolayer).
2. All sites are energetically equivalent.
3. No lateral interactions.

Rate of adsorption $= k_a C (1-\theta)$; rate of desorption $= k_d \theta$.

At equilibrium, $k_a C (1-\theta) = k_d \theta$.

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = \dfrac{k_a}{k_d} C = K_{\text{ads}} C
$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \dfrac{K_{\text{ads}} C}{1 + K_{\text{ads}} C}
$$

Inverting gives the **linearized Langmuir form** that is plotted experimentally:

$$
\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{\text{ads}}} + C
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **How KTU examiners test this:** A table of $(C, \theta)$ values is given. Students must compute $C/\theta$, plot against $C$, and from intercept ($1/K_{\text{ads}}$) compute $\Delta G_{\text{ads}} = -RT \ln(55.5 K_{\text{ads}})$. $\Delta G_{\text{ads}} < -40$ kJ/mol ⇒ **chemisorption**; between $-20$ and $-40$ kJ/mol ⇒ **physi-sorption**.

### 3.4 Application Selection Matrix (Analytical Tool)

| Service Environment | Operating pH | Temperature (°C) | Preferred Inhibitor Class | Commercial Example | Typical Dose |
|---------------------|--------------|-------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Acid pickling ($\text{HCl}/\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) | < 2 | 40 – 90 | Mixed organic, N/S heterocyclic | Rodine-92A, Dodigen 448 | **0.1 – 2 %** |
| Cooling water (open recirculating) | 6.5 – 8.5 | 30 – 60 | Mixed (phosphate + Zn + triazole) | HEDP-Zn, BZT + TT, molybdate blends | **10 – 100 ppm** |
| Boiler water (high pressure) | 9 – 11 | 200 – 320 | Oxygen scavenger + film amine | Hydrazine, DEHA, morpholine | **0.1 – 3 ppm O}_2$ scavenger |
| Oil & gas — sweet ($\text{CO}_2$) | 4 – 6 | 40 – 120 | Imidazoline, fatty amine | C$_{17}$-imidazoline, quat-amine | **10 – 100 ppm** |
| Oil & gas — sour ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$) | 3 – 6 | 40 – 150 | Film-forming amine + H$_2$S scavenger | Filming-amine 350, mercaptan scavenger | **50 – 500 ppm** |
| Concrete rebar | 12 – 13.5 | ambient | Anodic (nitrite, amino-alcohol) | Calcium nitrite, DMEA | **2 – 5 %** by cement wt. |
| Automotive coolant | 7 – 9.5 | 80 – 110 | Mixed (silicate-phosphate-Triazole) | BASF Glysantin ${}^\circledR$, Shell Rotella | **40 – 60 %** glycol + inhibitor pkg |
| Storage / shipping | ambient | ambient | Vapor-phase inhibitor | DICHAN, benzotriazole, VPI papers | **0.1 – 5 g/m$^3$** air |
| Fuel storage tanks | 6 – 8 | ambient | Film + biocide | BIOBOR JF, DCI-4A | **100 – 300 ppm** |
| Aircraft de-icing fluid | 7 – 10 | 0 – 25 | Mixed toluidinate / phosphate | Tolyltriazole, NaNO$_2$ | **0.1 – 1 %** |

### 3.5 Derivation: Synergistic Parameter $S_{12}$

When two inhibitors ($1$ and $2$) are blended, the observed efficiency of the blend is $\eta_{1+2}$. The contribution of each inhibitor *if acting alone* at the same concentration in the blend is $\eta'_1$ and $\eta'_2$ (these differ from $\eta_1$, $\eta_2$ measured at their individual optimal concentrations).

Aramaki-Hackerman synergistic parameter:

$$
S_{12} = \dfrac{1 - \eta_{1+2}}{1 - \eta'_1 - \eta'_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision rule:**  
> • $S_{12} > 1$ → **synergism** (use the blend).  
> • $S_{12} = 1$ → **additive** (no benefit to blending).  
> • $S_{12} < 1$ → **antagonism** (avoid the blend).

**Why synergism works (the "How"):** One inhibitor often forms a *primary* protective film; the second fills pinholes in the primary film (lateral cooperativity). Example: $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ + HEDP — $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ precipitates as $\text{Zn(OH)}_2$ at cathodes, while HEDP chelates residual $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ and blocks anodes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Application Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Corrosion Problem] --> B{Environment pH?}
    B -- pH less than 3 --> C[Acid Pickling / Cleaning]
    B -- pH 6 to 9 --> D{Open System?}
    B -- pH greater than 9 --> E[Boiler / Concrete]
    D -- Yes --> F[Cooling Water Inhibitor]
    D -- No --> G[Closed Loop Glycol]
    C --> C1[Mixed organic N or S heterocyclic]
    C --> C2[Rodine 92A or Dodigen 448]
    C --> C3[Dose: 0.1 to 2 percent]
    F --> F1[Phosphate plus Zn plus Triazole]
    F --> F2[BZT plus Tolyltriazole]
    F --> F3[Dose: 10 to 100 ppm]
    E --> E1[Oxygen scavenger plus Filming amine]
    E --> E2[Hydrazine or DEHA]
    E --> E3[Dose: 1 to 5 ppm]
    G --> G1[Silicate plus Phosphate]
    G --> G2[Nitrite plus Molybdate]
    G --> G3[Dose: 0.5 to 2 percent]
    C1 --> H{Validate via Weight Loss or EIS}
    C2 --> H
    C3 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H -- IE greater than 90 percent --> I[Deploy]
    H -- IE less than 90 percent --> J[Re-formulate Blend]
    J --> H
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Acid Pickling Inhibitor Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MetalSurface[Mild Steel Surface]
        siteA[Anodic Site Fe to Fe2+] --- siteC[Cathodic Site H plus to H2]
    end
    subgraph BulkAcid[1 M HCl Bulk]
        acid[H plus and Cl minus attacking] --- inh[Inhibitor Molecule e.g. amine]
    end
    inh -- Adsorbs --> film[Protective Mono-layer]
    film -- Blocks --> siteA
    film -- Blocks --> siteC
    acid -- Weakened attack --> MetalSurface
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Cooling-Water Inhibitor Program (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CWSystem[Open Recirculating Cooling System]
        CT[Tower Top] --> FH[Falling Film]
        FH --> SC[Sump]
        SC --> P1[Pump]
        P1 --> HE[Heat Exchanger Tubes]
        HE --> SC
    end
    subgraph ChemFeed[Chemical Feed Skid]
        TBI[Tolyltriazole 5 ppm] --- ZN[Zn salt 3 ppm]
        ZN --- HEDP[HEDP 8 ppm]
        HEDP --- BIO[Biocide 20 ppm shock]
        TBI --> Pump1[Dosing Pump]
        ZN --> Pump1
        HEDP --> Pump1
        BIO --> Pump1
    end
    Pump1 -- Inject --> SC
    SC -- pH 7 to 8.5 --> CT
```

### 4.4 Process Map — Selection of VPI for Export Shipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Export-Ready Steel Components] --> S1{Humidity Exposure Risk}
    S1 -- Less than 60 percent RH --> S2[Use VPI Paper Only]
    S1 -- 60 to 80 percent RH --> S3[Use VPI Powder plus Polyethylene Wrap]
    S1 -- greater than 80 percent RH --> S4[Use DICHAN plus Desiccant plus Sealed Barrier]
    S2 --> S5[Pack and Ship]
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S5
```

### 4.5 Functional Block Diagram — Inhibitor Action Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INH[Inhibitor Molecule in Bulk] -- Diffusion --> BL[Boundary Layer]
    BL -- Adsorption --> SF[Surface Film]
    SF -- Barrier --> AN[Anodic Reaction Blocked]
    SF -- Barrier --> CA[Cathodic Reaction Blocked]
    AN -- Lower i_corr --> OUT[Reduced Corrosion Rate]
    CA -- Lower i_corr --> OUT
    SF -- Lateral Coverage --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
*List any **three** industrial applications of corrosion inhibitors with one specific inhibitor used in each case.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct pair):**

1. **Acid pickling of mild steel** in $\text{HCl}/\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ → organic amines, e.g., **Rodine-92A** (a phenyl-thiourea derivative).
2. **Open recirculating cooling water** → **HEDP (1-hydroxyethylidene-1,1-diphosphonic acid) + $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ + Tolyltriazole** blend.
3. **Vapor-phase packaging of machine tools** → **Dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN)** impregnated VPI paper.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's tip:** Naming only the inhibitor *class* without a *specific commercial compound* loses 0.5 mark. Always pair class + example.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Why is **hydrazine** preferred over sodium sulfite as an oxygen scavenger in high-pressure boilers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **No solid residue:** Hydrazine reacts as $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{N}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$, producing only volatile, non-scaling products. Sodium sulfite leaves $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$, a solid that increases TDS and risks caustic embrittlement. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Passivating side-effect:** Hydrazine decomposes catalytically on steel surfaces to form a thin magnetite / iron-oxide protective layer that buffers against residual $\text{O}_2$. *(1 mark)*
3. **High-temperature stability:** It is effective up to **~ 300 °C**, beyond which DEHA (diethylhydroxylamine) is substituted. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module-Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **application of inhibitors in acid pickling of steel**. Cover the chemistry of pickling, the role of inhibitors, the mechanism of inhibition, and any **two** commercial pickling inhibitors with their characteristic functional groups. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A carbon-steel coupon of area **30 cm$^2$** is exposed to **0.5 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$** at **40 °C** for **3 hours**.
- Mass loss without inhibitor: **0.560 g**
- Mass loss with **0.3 % Dodigen-448**: **0.045 g**
- Density of steel: **7.85 g/cm$^3$**

Calculate (i) the uninhibited corrosion rate in **mm/yr**, (ii) the inhibited corrosion rate in **mm/yr**, and (iii) the inhibitor efficiency. State whether the inhibitor is acceptable by industrial standards. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Acid-Pickling Inhibitors (7 marks):**

Pickling is the chemical cleaning of mill scale ($\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4$, $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$) from hot-rolled steel using **hot $\text{HCl}$** or **$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$**.

Reactions (write these — 1 mark):
- $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 + 8\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_3 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- $\text{Fe} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\uparrow$

**Role of the inhibitor:** Suppresses the *second* reaction — the wasteful attack on **base metal** rather than scale — without hindering the first. (1 mark)

**Mechanism:** Organic N/S-containing molecules (amines, thioureas, imidazolines, quinolines) adsorb via lone pairs on N or S onto the steel surface, forming a **chemisorbed monolayer** that blocks active sites. They are mixed-type inhibitors reducing both $i_a$ and $i_c$. (2 marks)

**Two commercial examples (1 mark each):**

1. **Rodine-92A** — a substituted **thiourea / phenyl-thiourea**; functional group $\text{–NH–C(=S)–NH–}$. Excellent in HCl at 50 – 70 °C. IE > 90 %.
2. **Dodigen-448** — an **imidazoline** (5-membered N-heterocycle with a long C$_{17}$ hydrocarbon tail); forms hydrophobic film in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$. Effective at 0.1 – 0.5 %.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Uninhibited rate:**

$$
\text{CR}_0 = \dfrac{8.76 \times 10^4 \times 0.560}{30 \times 3 \times 7.85}
$$

$$
\text{CR}_0 = \dfrac{49056}{706.5} = 69.4\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**(ii) Inhibited rate:**

$$
\text{CR}_i = \dfrac{8.76 \times 10^4 \times 0.045}{706.5}
$$

$$
\text{CR}_i = \dfrac{3942}{706.5} = 5.58\ \text{mm/yr}
$$

**(iii) Inhibitor efficiency:**

$$
\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{69.4 - 5.58}{69.4}\times 100 = \dfrac{63.82}{69.4}\times 100 = 91.96\%
$$

**Acceptability:** Since **IE > 90 %**, Dodigen-448 **passes** the industrial acceptance criterion and may be deployed at 0.3 % dosage for the given pickling operation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **application of inhibitors in oil and gas pipelines**, distinguishing between **sweet ($\text{CO}_2$)** and **sour ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$)** service conditions. Discuss the inhibitor types, mechanism, dose range, and at least **one** monitoring technique used in the field. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An open-recirculating cooling-water system treats **500 m$^3$** of water. The target dose of **HEDP** is **8 ppm**, and the available stock solution is **50 % w/w HEDP** (density **1.30 g/mL**). Calculate the **daily volume of stock solution** required if the system bleed/evaporation loss is **5 % per day** (assume the inhibitor is not lost in bleed — i.e., total hold-up must be restored). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Oil & Gas Inhibitor Application (7 marks):**

- **Sweet ($\text{CO}_2$) service:** Corrosion by carbonic acid ($\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$). **Imidazoline-based filmers** and **fatty amines** are continuously injected at **10 – 100 ppm**; they are cationic and adsorb on the negatively-charged pipe wall at the **iron carbonate / siderite** film, blocking anodic dissolution. Mechanism: mixed-type with cathodic dominance. *(3 marks)*
- **Sour ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$) service:** $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ causes pitting, HIC, SSC. Used: **filming amines + $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ scavengers (triazines, Zn-based)**. Continuous injection at **50 – 500 ppm**. Imidazolines with a **long alkyl tail (C$_{17}$ – C$_{21}$)** form a hydrophobic oleophilic film that prevents $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ access. *(2 marks)*
- **Monitoring technique:** **Field coupon retrieval + LPR probe + iron-count trend analysis + residual inhibitor ppm** by HPLC at the downstream sample port. *(1 mark)*
- **Application mode:** Continuous via **chemical injection quill** into the pipeline centerline; often paired with a **batch slug treatment** every 7 – 14 days at **1000 – 5000 ppm**. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mass of HEDP needed in the loop:**

$$
m_{\text{HEDP}} = 500\ \text{m}^3 \times 8\ \text{g/m}^3 = 4000\ \text{g} = 4\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of stock required (assuming density-corrected 50 % active):**

Mass of stock needed for 4 kg of active HEDP:

$$
m_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{4\ \text{kg}}{0.50} = 8\ \text{kg}
$$

Volume of stock:

$$
V_{\text{stock}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{stock}}}{\rho} = \dfrac{8000\ \text{g}}{1.30\ \text{g/mL}} = 6153.8\ \text{mL} = 6.15\ \text{L}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily make-up (5 % loss means 5 % of hold-up must be replenished per day):**

Wait — the problem states "the inhibitor is not lost in bleed." Therefore, **inhibitor make-up per day is only what is needed to dose 5 % fresh water**, i.e., to maintain the 8 ppm in the new water added (since the inhibitor itself stays in the loop).

**Volume of fresh water added per day:**

$$
V_{\text{fresh}} = 0.05 \times 500 = 25\ \text{m}^3 = 25{,}000\ \text{L}
$$

**Mass of HEDP for the new water:**

$$
m_{\text{new}} = 25\ \text{m}^3 \times 8\ \text{g/m}^3 = 200\ \text{g}
$$

**Volume of stock solution for the new water:**

$$
V_{\text{daily}} = \dfrac{200\ \text{g}}{0.50 \times 1.30\ \text{g/mL}} = \dfrac{200}{0.65} = 307.7\ \text{mL/day} \approx 0.31\ \text{L/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final answer:** Approximately **0.31 L of 50 % stock solution per day** to maintain 8 ppm HEDP in the loop.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts:**
> 1. **Unit-mismatch trap:** In inhibitor-numerical questions, students frequently confuse **ppm = mg/L = g/m$^3$** in dilute aqueous solutions and lose 1 mark. **Memorize: 1 ppm in water ≈ 1 g/m$^3$.**
> 2. **Commercial naming trap:** Naming only "amine" without specifying the *functional group* (e.g., imidazoline, thiourea) is treated as half-credit by examiners.
> 3. **Application-environment trap:** Many students apply an anodic inhibitor (e.g., nitrite) in *low-pH* service — this fails because anodic inhibitors need passivating $\text{pH} > 6$. Always state pH range.
> 4. **Synergism trap:** Do **not** blend two inhibitors from the **same class** (e.g., two anodic inhibitors) — they often antagonize. Pair anodic + cathodic or organic + inorganic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bulleted Summary — read this twice before the exam.**

- [ ] **Acid pickling:** Hot HCl / $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$; inhibitors are **organic N/S compounds** (thiourea, imidazoline, quinoline). Target IE > 90 %. Examples: Rodine-92A, Dodigen-448, Pyridine derivatives.
- [ ] **Cooling water:** **Mixed blend** — HEDP + $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ + Tolyltriazole (for Cu-alloy tubes). Maintain **pH 6.5 – 8.5**, dose **10 – 100 ppm**.
- [ ] **Boiler water:** **Oxygen scavenger** (Hydrazine / DEHA / Erythorbate) + **filming amine** (morpholine, octadecylamine). pH 9 – 11.
- [ ] **Oil & gas (sweet):** Imidazoline, fatty amine, quaternary ammonium. **10 – 100 ppm**. Continuous injection via chemical injection quill.
- [ ] **Oil & gas (sour):** Filming amine + $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ scavenger. **50 – 500 ppm**. Plus batch slug at 1000 – 5000 ppm.
- [ ] **Concrete:** **Calcium nitrite** (anodic, 2 – 5 % by cement wt.); DMEA, amino-alcohol-based. Protects against chloride-induced rebar corrosion.
- [ ] **Automotive coolant:** Silicate + phosphate + Tolyltriazole + nitrite-molybdate blend; pH 7 – 9.5.
- [ ] **VPI / Packaging:** DICHAN, benzotriazole, VPI paper, powder, tablets. **0.1 – 5 g/m$^3$ air**.
- [ ] **Fuel storage:** DCI-4A, BIOBOR JF. **100 – 300 ppm**.
- [ ] **De-icing fluid:** Tolyltriazole + NaNO$_2$.
- [ ] **Master formulae to memorize:** $\text{IE}\% = (\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i)/\text{CR}_0 \times 100$; Langmuir $C/\theta = 1/K + C$; Synergism $S_{12}$ rule.
- [ ] **Master rule:** **Anodic inhibitors need $\text{pH} > 6$; cathodic inhibitors work at any pH; mixed-type adsorb best at the PZC.**
- [ ] **Always validate** field performance with **weight-loss coupons**, **LPR probes**, **EIS**, and **residual-inhibitor HPLC** — not just lab IE%.

> **End of Notes — Module 4 / Topic: Examples of Application of Inhibitors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Important Considerations in Selection of Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Important Considerations in Selection of Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Important Considerations in Selection of Inhibitors — Core Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **corrosion inhibitor** is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, effectively **reduces, prevents, or stops the rate of metal deterioration** without significantly altering the concentration of any corrosive reagent. The process of **selecting an inhibitor** involves a systematic evaluation of several physicochemical, electrochemical, environmental, economic, and toxicological parameters to ensure optimal protection of the target metal in its specific service environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"The selection of a corrosion inhibitor is not a random choice but a scientifically guided decision based on the compatibility of the inhibitor with the metal, the nature of the corrodent, operating conditions (T, P, pH, flow), toxicity regulations, and the cost–performance ratio. An ideal inhibitor must be effective at low concentrations, chemically stable, environmentally benign, and should not adversely affect the mechanical, thermal, or aesthetic properties of the protected system."*

Mathematically, the **Inhibition Efficiency (I.E. %)** is expressed as:

$$\text{I.E. (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

Where:
- $CR_0$ = Corrosion rate in the **absence** of the inhibitor
- $CR_i$ = Corrosion rate in the **presence** of the inhibitor

A good inhibitor should give **I.E. > 90%** at concentrations typically between **0.1 mM to 10 mM** (or 50–500 ppm).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Raincoat for Metals"

Imagine you are going out on a rainy day (the rain = corrosive electrolyte like acid, salt water, or humid air). You have many options to protect yourself:

- A **thick rubber raincoat** (analogous to a barrier coating) — blocks everything.
- A **water-repellent spray** (analogous to a corrosion inhibitor) — chemically interacts with your skin/clothes so water rolls off without causing damage.
- An **umbrella** (analogous to cathodic protection using sacrificial anodes) — diverts the harm away.

A **corrosion inhibitor is the "water-repellent spray"** of metallurgy. Just as the spray:
- Must be **selected based on your clothing fabric** (cotton vs silk → analogous to *type of metal/alloy*),
- Must work in **different rain intensities** (light drizzle vs acid rain → *type and concentration of corrodent*),
- Must remain **stable at body temperature** (*operating temperature*),
- Must not **stain or damage your skin** (*compatibility & toxicity*),

…the selection of an inhibitor demands a **holistic evaluation** of metal, environment, operating parameters, and regulatory constraints.

## 1.3 Why "Selection" is a Critical Engineering Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Did you know?** A wrongly selected inhibitor can **accelerate corrosion** (a phenomenon called **inhibitor failure** or **negative inhibition**). For example, using a phosphate inhibitor in a low-pH (acidic) medium can actually destabilize the protective film and cause *pitting corrosion*.

Selection is therefore **not a "trial and error"** exercise — it follows a structured decision framework based on fundamental corrosion electrochemistry and surface chemistry.

## 1.4 Categories of Considerations — Overview

The major considerations can be grouped into **five functional clusters**, which are the basis of the KTU 2024 Module 4 question framing:

| # | Cluster | Typical Examples |
|---|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Metal-related** | Composition, microstructure, passivity |
| 2 | **Environment-related** | pH, electrolyte type, dissolved gases |
| 3 | **Operating Conditions** | Temperature, pressure, flow velocity |
| 4 | **Inhibitor Properties** | Solubility, stability, film formation |
| 5 | **Health, Safety & Economics** | Toxicity, biodegradability, cost |

Each cluster will be dissected in the next section with full theoretical depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Selection Considerations

### A. Metal-Related Considerations

1. **Nature of the Metal / Alloy**
   - Inhibitors are often **metal-specific**. For instance:
     - **Phosphates, chromates, molybdates** → effective for **steel and iron** (anodic inhibitors).
     - **Amines, imidazolines** → effective for **copper and copper alloys**.
     - **Azoles (benzotriazole)** → specifically tailored for **brass and copper** (form complex polymeric films).
   - Pure metals often respond differently from alloys because of **grain boundaries, secondary phases, and inclusions** that act as localized cathodes.

2. **Electrochemical Nature of the Metal**
   - **Active metals** (Fe, Zn, Mg) → require strong film-forming inhibitors.
   - **Passive metals** (Al, Ti, Cr, stainless steels) → require inhibitors that **stabilize the passive film** (e.g., nitrites for Al).

3. **Surface Condition**
   - Rough, scaled, or pre-corroded surfaces adsorb inhibitors differently from polished surfaces. Pre-cleaning may be required.

4. **Metallurgical Compatibility**
   - Inhibitor must not cause **hydrogen embrittlement** (e.g., sulfide-containing inhibitors can be dangerous for high-strength steels).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Selection of a chromate inhibitor for **aluminium** is *not recommended* in food/pharmaceutical industries due to the **hexavalent chromium toxicity** issue, even though it offers excellent corrosion protection.

### B. Environment-Related Considerations

1. **Type of Corrosive Medium**
   - **Acidic media (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃):** → *Organic inhibitors* (amines, imidazolines, Schiff bases) are preferred. They adsorb on the metal surface via electron lone pairs on N, S, O atoms.
   - **Neutral / Near-neutral media (cooling water, seawater):** → *Inorganic inhibitors* (phosphates, nitrites, molybdates, zinc salts) dominate.
   - **Alkaline media:** → Silicates, nitrites, and certain organic inhibitors (e.g., quaternary ammonium salts).
   - **Atmospheric:** → Vapor-phase inhibitors (VPIs) like dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN).

2. **pH of the Medium**
   - Most inhibitors are **pH-sensitive**. Chromates work best at pH 7–11; phosphates at 6.5–7.5; amines at pH < 7.

3. **Concentration of Aggressive Ions**
   - High $[Cl^-]$, $[SO_4^{2-}]$, or $[S^{2-}]$ can **break down passive films** and **inhibit inhibitor adsorption**.

4. **Dissolved Gases**
   - **Dissolved oxygen** accelerates corrosion and may oxidize certain inhibitors (e.g., sulfite → sulfate), depleting their protective function.

### C. Operating Conditions

1. **Temperature**
   - Higher temperature generally **reduces adsorption** of organic inhibitors (exothermic adsorption process).
   - Inorganic inhibitors (e.g., chromates) are often **more thermally stable** but may lose effectiveness above 80 °C.
   - The **Arrhenius equation** governs this:

$$k = A \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

2. **Pressure**
   - In **high-pressure CO₂/H₂S systems** (oil & gas), special inhibitors like **filming amines and imidazolines** are mandatory.

3. **Flow Velocity**
   - High flow → risk of **erosion-corrosion**. Inhibitor film must be **mechanically strong**.
   - Very low flow → risk of **microbiologically influenced corrosion (MIC)** → requires **biocidal inhibitors**.

### D. Inhibitor-Related Properties

1. **Solubility in the Medium**
   - Must be sufficient to deliver the active species to the metal surface (typical: 50–500 ppm).

2. **Chemical & Thermal Stability**
   - Must not decompose under operating T, P, pH, and oxidizing conditions.

3. **Film-Forming Ability**
   - The protective film must be:
     - **Adherent** (high bond strength)
     - **Compact and continuous** (no pores)
     - **Impervious to electrolyte** (low ionic conductivity)
     - **Self-healing** (in some cases)

4. **Synergistic / Antagonistic Effects**
   - Combining two or more inhibitors (e.g., **Zn²⁺ + phosphate**, or **KI + amine**) often gives **synergism** (1+1 > 2 effect).
   - Some combinations are **antagonistic** (one cancels the other).

5. **Electrochemical Behaviour**
   - **Anodic inhibitors** (chromates, nitrites, molybdates) → suppress anodic dissolution.
   - **Cathodic inhibitors** (Zn²⁺, polyphosphates) → suppress cathodic reduction.
   - **Mixed inhibitors** (amines, azoles) → suppress both reactions.

### E. Health, Safety, Environmental & Economic (HSE-E) Considerations

1. **Toxicity** (LD₅₀ values)
2. **Biodegradability** (OECD 301 test compliance)
3. **Regulatory Status** (REACH, EPA, OSHA, Central Pollution Control Board – India)
4. **Cost per unit protection**
5. **Ease of handling and dosing**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inhibition Efficiency | $\text{I.E. (\%)} = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ | $CR$ in mpy or mm/yr |
| 2 | Surface Coverage ($\theta$) | $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0}$ | $0 \le \theta \le 1$ |
| 3 | Corrosion Rate (Tafel) | $CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | $i_{corr}$ in A/cm² |
| 4 | Langmuir Isotherm | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = K \cdot C$ or $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K} + C$ | $K$ = adsorption constant |
| 5 | Freundlich Isotherm | $\theta = K \cdot C^n$ | Empirical, $n < 1$ |
| 6 | Temkin Isotherm | $\exp(2a\theta) = K \cdot C$ | $a$ = interaction parameter |
| 7 | Arrhenius (T effect) | $\ln k = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | $E_a$ in J/mol |
| 8 | Activation Energy Shift | $\Delta E_a = E_a^i - E_a^0$ | Positive = physical adsorption |
| 9 | Synergistic Parameter | $S = \dfrac{1 - I_{1+2}}{1 - I'_{1+2}}$ | $S > 1$ = synergism |
| 10 | Adsorption Free Energy | $\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K)$ | $\vert \Delta G \vert < 20$ = physisorption; $> 40$ kJ/mol = chemisorption |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Board Exam:** The value **55.5** in formula #10 represents the **molar concentration of water** (in mol/L) at 25 °C, and its use in the Gibbs free energy equation is a **standard convention** in corrosion science literature. Do not forget it!

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Application | Typical Inhibitor Used |
|----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Oil & Gas (Downhole) | CO₂/H₂S corrosion in pipelines | Imidazolines, fatty amines, quaternary ammonium salts |
| Power Plants (Cooling Water) | Recirculating water systems | Phosphates, zinc, molybdates, HEDP |
| Automotive | Radiators, antifreeze | Silicates, nitrites, phosphates |
| Marine | Ship hulls, ballast tanks | Tannins, modified imidazolines |
| Pickling (Steel Industry) | Acid cleaning of scale | Thiourea, propargyl alcohol, dibenzyl sulfoxide |
| Concrete Reinforcement | Rebar protection | Calcium nitrite, amino alcohols |
| Aerospace | Hydraulic fluids, de-icing | Azoles, sulfonates |

Selection in each case is a **multi-criteria decision problem**, where trade-offs are made between *protection level*, *cost*, *toxicity*, and *system compatibility*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inhibition Efficiency from Corrosion Rates

**Statement:** A carbon steel coupon is exposed to 1 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C. The corrosion rate without inhibitor is **$CR_0 = 8.42$ mpy** (mils per year). With the addition of **0.5 mM of an organic inhibitor (A)**, the corrosion rate drops to **$CR_i = 0.92$ mpy**. Calculate the inhibition efficiency.

**Step 1 — Write down the given data.**

$$CR_0 = 8.42 \text{ mpy}, \quad CR_i = 0.92 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the I.E. formula.**

$$\text{I.E. (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\text{I.E. (\%)} = \frac{8.42 - 0.92}{8.42} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$8.42 - 0.92 = 7.50 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $CR_0$.**

$$\frac{7.50}{8.42} = 0.8907$$

**Step 6 — Multiply by 100 to get percentage.**

$$\text{I.E. (\%)} = 0.8907 \times 100 = 89.07\%$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\text{I.E.} \approx 89.1\%}$$

> An inhibition efficiency above 85% is considered *commercially excellent* for acid-pickling inhibitors. The surface coverage is $\theta = 0.8907$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Determination of Adsorption Isotherm (Langmuir Fit)

**Statement:** The following data were obtained for the adsorption of inhibitor **B** on mild steel in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ at 303 K. Verify that the adsorption follows the **Langmuir isotherm** and determine the adsorption constant $K_{ads}$.

| Inhibitor Conc. $C$ (mM) | Surface Coverage $\theta$ |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| 0.10 | 0.420 |
| 0.25 | 0.650 |
| 0.50 | 0.798 |
| 1.00 | 0.886 |
| 2.00 | 0.940 |

**Step 1 — Recall the linear form of the Langmuir isotherm.**

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C/\theta$ for each data point.**

$$\frac{0.10}{0.420} = 0.2381 \text{ mM}$$
$$\frac{0.25}{0.650} = 0.3846 \text{ mM}$$
$$\frac{0.50}{0.798} = 0.6266 \text{ mM}$$
$$\frac{1.00}{0.886} = 1.1287 \text{ mM}$$
$$\frac{2.00}{0.940} = 2.1277 \text{ mM}$$

**Step 3 — Plot $C/\theta$ (Y-axis) vs $C$ (X-axis) and find the linear equation.**

Performing linear regression on the data:

$$\text{Slope} \approx 0.945, \quad \text{Intercept} \approx 0.200 \text{ mM}$$

(Intercept $\approx 1/K_{ads}$. The slope is close to 1, confirming the Langmuir monolayer behaviour.)

**Step 4 — Solve for $K_{ads}$.**

$$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{\text{Intercept}} = \frac{1}{0.200} = 5.0 \text{ mM}^{-1} = 5.0 \times 10^{3} \text{ L/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the standard free energy of adsorption.**

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = - (8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 5.0 \times 10^{3})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -2519.14 \cdot \ln(2.775 \times 10^{5})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -2519.14 \cdot 12.534$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads} \approx -31.58 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

$$\vert \Delta G_{ads} \vert \approx 31.6 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Since this lies in the range of **20–40 kJ/mol**, the adsorption is a **mixed type** — partly physical (physisorption, electrostatic) and partly chemical (chemisorption, coordinate bond formation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** Always show the **logarithm computation step** explicitly. A common student error is forgetting the 55.5 factor, which leads to an incorrect $\Delta G_{ads}$ by ~10 kJ/mol and changes the conclusion about adsorption type.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Activation Energy from Arrhenius Plot

**Statement:** The corrosion rate of zinc in 1 N HCl is measured at three temperatures, with and without inhibitor X. The data are:

| Temperature $T$ (K) | $CR_0$ (mpy) | $CR_i$ (mpy) |
|----------------------|--------------|--------------|
| 303 | 12.0 | 2.5 |
| 313 | 26.5 | 6.0 |
| 323 | 55.0 | 14.0 |

Calculate the activation energies $E_a^0$ and $E_a^i$, and comment on the mechanism.

**Step 1 — Use the logarithmic form of the Arrhenius equation.**

$$\ln(CR) = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $1/T$ and $\ln(CR_0)$, $\ln(CR_i)$.**

| $T$ (K) | $1/T$ (K⁻¹) | $\ln(CR_0)$ | $\ln(CR_i)$ |
|---------|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 303 | 0.003300 | 2.485 | 0.916 |
| 313 | 0.003195 | 3.277 | 1.792 |
| 323 | 0.003096 | 4.007 | 2.639 |

**Step 3 — Slope of $\ln(CR_0)$ vs $1/T$:**

$$\text{Slope}_0 = \frac{4.007 - 2.485}{0.003096 - 0.003300} = \frac{1.522}{-0.000204} \approx -7461 \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_a^0$.**

$$E_a^0 = -R \cdot \text{Slope}_0 = -8.314 \times (-7461) = 62.02 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Slope of $\ln(CR_i)$ vs $1/T$:**

$$\text{Slope}_i = \frac{2.639 - 0.916}{0.003096 - 0.003300} = \frac{1.723}{-0.000204} \approx -8446 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $E_a^i$.**

$$E_a^i = -R \cdot \text{Slope}_i = -8.314 \times (-8446) = 70.21 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the shift in activation energy.**

$$\Delta E_a = E_a^i - E_a^0 = 70.21 - 62.02 = 8.19 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\Delta E_a > 0}$$

A **positive shift in activation energy** in the presence of the inhibitor indicates that the inhibitor is acting via **physical adsorption (physisorption)** — it raises the energy barrier for the corrosion reaction, slowing it down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative case:** If $\Delta E_a < 0$, the inhibitor likely works by **chemisorption**, modifying the reaction pathway rather than simply blocking surface sites.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Synergistic Parameter Calculation

**Statement:** Inhibitor 1 alone gives I.E. = 70%. Inhibitor 2 alone gives I.E. = 60%. Their combination (at same individual concentrations) gives I.E. = 95%. Determine the synergistic parameter $S_1$.

**Step 1 — Recall the synergism formula (Aramaki method).**

$$S_1 = \frac{1 - I_{1+2}}{1 - I'_1}$$

Where $I_{1+2}$ is the inhibition efficiency of the **mixture**, and $I'_1$ is the inhibition efficiency of inhibitor 1 in the mixture (assumed equal to its standalone value, $I_1$).

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$S_1 = \frac{1 - 0.95}{1 - 0.70} = \frac{0.05}{0.30} = 0.167$$

Wait — this is a special form. A more common formulation used in KTU 2024 syllabus is:

$$S_ = \frac{1 - I_{1+2}}{(1 - I_1) + (1 - I_2) - (1 - I_1)(1 - I_2)}$$

**Step 2 (corrected) — Substitute values.**

$$S = \frac{1 - 0.95}{(1 - 0.70) + (1 - 0.60) - (1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.60)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term.**

$$1 - I_{1+2} = 0.05$$
$$1 - I_1 = 0.30$$
$$1 - I_2 = 0.40$$
$$(1 - I_1)(1 - I_2) = 0.30 \times 0.40 = 0.12$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator.**

$$0.30 + 0.40 - 0.12 = 0.58$$

**Step 5 — Compute $S$.**

$$S = \frac{0.05}{0.58} = 0.0862$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

$$\boxed{S \approx 0.086 < 1}$$

Since $S < 1$, the mixture exhibits **strong synergism** (the two inhibitors reinforce each other; the combined effect is far greater than the simple additive prediction).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Rule:** $S = 1$ → additive; $S < 1$ → synergism; $S > 1$ → antagonism.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Systematic Selection of Corrosion Inhibitor

The following **Mermaid block diagram** outlines the complete, step-by-step decision logic that a corrosion engineer must follow when selecting an inhibitor. It incorporates the **five functional clusters** identified in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: System requires corrosion protection]) --> q1{Step 1: Identify metal or alloy type}
    q1 -->|Ferrous: Fe, mild steel, SS| nodeA1[Consider anodic film formers: chromates, molybdates, nitrites, phosphates]
    q1 -->|Non-ferrous: Cu, Al, Zn| nodeA2[Consider azoles, amines, silicates, benzotriazoles]
    q1 -->|Mixed multi-metal system| nodeA3[Consider broad-spectrum organic inhibitors: imidazolines, Schiff bases]
    nodeA1 --> q2{Step 2: Identify corrodent medium}
    nodeA2 --> q2
    nodeA3 --> q2
    q2 -->|Acidic, pH less than 4| nodeB1[Select organic amine, imidazoline, thiourea, propargyl alcohol]
    q2 -->|Neutral to alkaline, pH 6 to 9| nodeB2[Select inorganic: phosphate, nitrite, molybdate, zinc salt]
    q2 -->|Vapor phase or atmospheric| nodeB3[Select VPI: dicyclohexylamine nitrite, benzoate salts]
    nodeB1 --> q3{Step 3: Operating temperature and pressure}
    nodeB2 --> q3
    nodeB3 --> q3
    q3 -->|Low T below 60 C, low P| nodeC1[Most inhibitors acceptable, perform bench screening]
    q3 -->|High T 60 to 200 C| nodeC2[Use thermally stable molecules: imidazolines, quaternary ammonium]
    q3 -->|High P with H2S or CO2| nodeC3[Use filming amines and tailored oilfield chemistries]
    nodeC1 --> q4{Step 4: HSE and regulatory check}
    nodeC2 --> q4
    nodeC3 --> q4
    q4 -->|Toxic or restricted, e.g. Cr6 plus| nodeD1[Reject; seek green alternative: molybdate, silicate, plant extract]
    q4 -->|Compliant and biodegradable| nodeD2[Proceed to dose optimization and field trial]
    nodeD1 --> q4
    nodeD2 --> q5{Step 5: Economic and performance check}
    q5 -->|I.E. above 90 percent at less than 200 ppm, cost acceptable| nodeE1[Approve and deploy]
    q5 -->|Performance below target or cost too high| nodeE2[Reformulate or re-screen candidate pool]
    nodeE2 --> q1
    nodeE1 --> stopNode([End: Inhibitor selected and qualified])
```

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Interaction of Five Selection Clusters

The diagram below illustrates how the **five functional clusters** (Metal, Environment, Operating, Inhibitor, HSE-E) interact as a **multi-criteria decision matrix**, the *core analytical framework* expected in KTU 2024 long-answer questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cluster1[Cluster A: Metal factors]
        m1[Metal type]
        m2[Surface condition]
        m3[Electrochemical nature]
    end
    subgraph cluster2[Cluster B: Environment factors]
        e1[Corrodent type]
        e2[Aggressive ion concentration]
        e3[Dissolved gas content]
    end
    subgraph cluster3[Cluster C: Operating factors]
        o1[Temperature]
        o2[Pressure]
        o3[Flow velocity]
    end
    subgraph cluster4[Cluster D: Inhibitor properties]
        i1[Solubility and stability]
        i2[Film formation ability]
        i3[Electrochemical type]
    end
    subgraph cluster5[Cluster E: HSE and economics]
        h1[Toxicity and regulation]
        h2[Biodegradability]
        h3[Cost and availability]
    end
    cluster1 --> decisionCore((Selection decision engine))
    cluster2 --> decisionCore
    cluster3 --> decisionCore
    cluster4 --> decisionCore
    cluster5 --> decisionCore
    decisionCore --> output1[Approved inhibitor]
    decisionCore --> output2[Rejected candidate]
    decisionCore --> output3[Field trial protocol]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Selection Matrix — Visual Aid

| Selection Criterion | Anodic Inhibitor (e.g., chromate) | Cathodic Inhibitor (e.g., Zn²⁺) | Mixed/Organic (e.g., imidazoline) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best for | Steel in neutral water | Steel in cooling water | Acid pickling, oil & gas |
| Risk if under-dosed | **Severe pitting** | Generally low | Low |
| Toxicity | **High (Cr⁶⁺ carcinogen)** | Medium | Low to medium |
| Temperature limit | ~80 °C | ~70 °C | ~180 °C |
| Typical dose | 50–500 ppm | 10–100 ppm | 5–100 ppm |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate to high |
| KTU 2024 recommendation | Avoid where possible | Use with care | **Preferred in modern practice** |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2024]**

> *"Define Inhibition Efficiency. An inhibitor reduces the corrosion rate of mild steel in 1 M HCl from 12.5 mpy to 1.85 mpy. Calculate the inhibition efficiency and surface coverage."*

**Model Answer:**

The **Inhibition Efficiency (I.E. %)** is the percentage reduction in the corrosion rate of a metal due to the addition of an inhibitor, defined as:

$$\text{I.E. (\%)} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$$

Given: $CR_0 = 12.5$ mpy, $CR_i = 1.85$ mpy.

$$\text{I.E. (\%)} = \frac{12.5 - 1.85}{12.5} \times 100 = \frac{10.65}{12.5} \times 100 = 85.2\%$$

**Surface coverage:**

$$\theta = \frac{I.E.}{100} = 0.852 \quad (\text{or } 85.2\%)$$

The inhibitor covers **85.2% of the metal surface**, leaving only ~14.8% exposed to the acidic corrodent. This high coverage explains the strong protective effect.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

> *"List any **six important considerations** in the selection of corrosion inhibitors for industrial applications."*

**Model Answer:**

The six most important considerations in inhibitor selection are:

1. **Type of metal / alloy** being protected (since inhibitors are often metal-specific).
2. **Nature and concentration of the corrodent** (acidic, neutral, alkaline, atmospheric).
3. **Operating temperature and pressure** (affects adsorption strength and inhibitor stability).
4. **Solubility and chemical stability** of the inhibitor in the service environment.
5. **Toxicity, biodegradability, and regulatory compliance** (e.g., REACH, EPA norms).
6. **Cost-effectiveness and availability** at industrial scale.

Additional considerations include flow velocity, presence of dissolved gases, and synergistic/antagonistic effects with other chemicals in the system.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 🅰️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Sub-parts a + b)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 + KTU Model Paper 2024 Synthesis]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **important considerations** that govern the selection of a corrosion inhibitor for an industrial system. Categorize them into metal-related, environment-related, operating-condition, inhibitor-property, and HSE-economic factors. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A mild steel pipeline in an oil refinery carries a sour crude (containing H₂S) at 95 °C. Recommend a suitable inhibitor class with full justification, and discuss the role of **synergistic additives** in enhancing its performance. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

The selection of a corrosion inhibitor is a **multi-parameter decision problem** governed by the following categories:

**1. Metal-Related Factors (≈2 marks)**
- The chemistry of the base metal (Fe, Cu, Al, Zn, alloys) dictates the choice.
- Ferrous metals → anodic inhibitors (chromates, molybdates, nitrites).
- Copper & alloys → azoles (benzotriazole, tolyltriazole).
- Aluminium → requires inhibitors that stabilize the amphoteric passive film (silicates, nitrites).
- Surface condition (rough, scaled, pre-corroded) affects adsorption kinetics.

**2. Environment-Related Factors (≈2 marks)**
- **Acidic media** → organic amines, imidazolines, Schiff bases (adsorb via N/S/O lone pairs).
- **Neutral/alkaline media** → inorganic inhibitors (phosphates, molybdates, zinc).
- **Atmospheric/Vapour phase** → VPIs (dicyclohexylamine nitrite, benzoates).
- pH, dissolved oxygen, $[Cl^-]$, $[S^{2-}]$, $[SO_4^{2-}]$ concentration must be known.

**3. Operating Condition Factors (≈1 mark)**
- Temperature (Arrhenius dependency), pressure, flow velocity.
- High T → prefers chemically adsorbed, thermally stable films (imidazolines).
- High flow → film must resist shear (mechanical strength of film).
- Stagnant conditions → risk of MIC, requires biocide.

**4. Inhibitor-Property Factors (≈1 mark)**
- Solubility, film compactness, ionic conductivity of film, self-healing ability, electrochemical type (anodic/cathodic/mixed), and synergy with co-inhibitors.

**5. HSE & Economic Factors (≈1 mark)**
- Toxicity (LD₅₀), biodegradability (OECD 301), REACH/EPA compliance, cost per unit protection, ease of dosing and monitoring.

A **decision matrix** (refer Section 4.3) is typically constructed to score each candidate and select the optimal inhibitor.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Recommendation: Filming Amine / Imidazoline-based inhibitor**, often combined with a **quaternary ammonium salt** and a small dose of **KI (potassium iodide)** as synergist.

**Justification:**

| Requirement | Justified Choice |
|-------------|------------------|
| H₂S environment (sour service) | Imidazolines form **tenacious chemisorbed films** that resist H₂S attack. |
| High temperature 95 °C | Imidazolines are thermally stable up to ~150–200 °C. |
| Mild steel pipeline | Film is mixed-type (protects both anodic and cathodic sites). |
| Continuous liquid phase | Imidazoline is oil-soluble and disperses well in hydrocarbon streams. |

**Role of Synergistic Additives:**

- **KI (5–50 ppm):** Iodide ions **co-adsorb** with the imidazoline on the metal surface, increasing surface coverage from ~70% to >95%. The synergistic parameter $S < 1$ confirms strong synergism.
- **Quaternary ammonium salts:** Improve film durability and provide **biocidal action** to counter SRB (sulfate-reducing bacteria) often present in sour systems.
- **Formaldehyde releasers** (controversial due to toxicity) or **glutaraldehyde** are sometimes co-injected for H₂S scavenging.

**Conclusion:** A blended formulation of **imidazoline (50–100 ppm) + KI (10–20 ppm) + quaternary ammonium biocide (20 ppm)** offers optimal protection for the given conditions, delivering I.E. > 95% with acceptable HSE profile.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With the help of the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm**, show how the equilibrium constant of adsorption ($K_{ads}$) and the **standard free energy of adsorption** ($\Delta G_{ads}$) are determined experimentally. What does the magnitude of $\Delta G_{ads}$ reveal about the nature of adsorption? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** The following data were obtained for an inhibitor on mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ at 30 °C. Verify the isotherm fit and calculate $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G_{ads}$. (7 marks)
>
> | $C$ (mM) | $\theta$ |
> |----------|----------|
> | 0.20 | 0.55 |
> | 0.50 | 0.78 |
> | 1.00 | 0.89 |
> | 2.00 | 0.95 |

#### Model Solution — (a)

The **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** assumes:
- Monolayer coverage
- Uniform, energetically equivalent adsorption sites
- No interaction between adsorbed molecules

The linear form is:

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

**Experimental Procedure:**
1. Measure corrosion rates ($CR_0$, $CR_i$) at various inhibitor concentrations $C$.
2. Compute $\theta = (CR_0 - CR_i)/CR_0$.
3. Plot $C/\theta$ (Y-axis) vs $C$ (X-axis).
4. The plot should be linear with **slope ≈ 1** and intercept = $1/K_{ads}$.

**Calculation of $\Delta G_{ads}$:**

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

**Interpretation:**

| $\vert \Delta G_{ads} \vert$ (kJ/mol) | Nature of Adsorption |
|---|---|
| $\le 20$ | **Physisorption** (electrostatic, weak) |
| 20 – 40 | **Mixed** (both physical and chemical) |
| $\ge 40$ | **Chemisorption** (covalent/coordinate bond) |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $C/\theta$:**

$$\frac{0.20}{0.55} = 0.3636$$
$$\frac{0.50}{0.78} = 0.6410$$
$$\frac{1.00}{0.89} = 1.1236$$
$$\frac{2.00}{0.95} = 2.1053$$

**Step 2 — Linear regression of $C/\theta$ vs $C$:**

| $C$ (mM) | $C/\theta$ (mM) |
|----------|-----------------|
| 0.20 | 0.3636 |
| 0.50 | 0.6410 |
| 1.00 | 1.1236 |
| 2.00 | 2.1053 |

The slope of the best-fit line is **0.954** (close to 1 → Langmuir confirmation).

The intercept (at $C = 0$) is approximately **0.200 mM**.

**Step 3 — Calculate $K_{ads}$:**

$$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{0.200} = 5.0 \text{ mM}^{-1} = 5.0 \times 10^{3} \text{ L mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\Delta G_{ads}$:**

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -(8.314)(303)\ln(55.5 \times 5.0 \times 10^3)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -2519.14 \times \ln(2.775 \times 10^5)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -2519.14 \times 12.534$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{ads} \approx -31.6 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

Since $\vert \Delta G_{ads} \vert \approx 31.6$ kJ/mol, the adsorption is of **mixed type** (both physisorption and chemisorption), which is typical of efficient organic inhibitors in acidic media.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 55.5** in the $\Delta G_{ads}$ formula — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in KTU board papers.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $K_{ads}$ must be expressed in **L/mol** (or mM⁻¹) consistently when plugging into the formula. Mixing mM⁻¹ with mol/L leads to errors.
> 3. **Plotting $\theta$ vs $C$** instead of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ for the Langmuir fit — the linear form is mandatory in KTU answers.
> 4. **Confusing physisorption vs chemisorption threshold:** 20 kJ/mol and 40 kJ/mol are the boundary values most KTU examiners use.
> 5. **Using inhibitor concentration in % instead of mol/L or mM** without conversion — always convert to a consistent unit before computation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Inhibition Efficiency (I.E. %):** $\dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ — a good inhibitor gives **> 90%**.
- **Five selection clusters:** Metal | Environment | Operating | Inhibitor Property | HSE-E.
- **Acid media → organic inhibitors** (amines, imidazolines, Schiff bases).
- **Neutral/alkaline media → inorganic inhibitors** (phosphates, nitrites, molybdates, Zn²⁺).
- **Atmospheric → VPIs** (dicyclohexylamine nitrite, benzoate salts).
- **Anodic inhibitors:** Chromate, nitrite, molybdate — risk of pitting if under-dosed.
- **Cathodic inhibitors:** Polyphosphate, Zn²⁺, Ca²⁺ — safer but slower acting.
- **Mixed inhibitors:** Azoles, amines, imidazolines — most preferred in modern industry.
- **Langmuir isotherm** is verified by plotting $C/\theta$ vs $C$ — slope ≈ 1 confirms monolayer.
