- **$\Delta G_{ads}$ boundaries:** $\le 20$ kJ/mol → physisorption; $20$–$40$ → mixed; $\ge 40$ → chemisorption.
- **Arrhenius shift $\Delta E_a > 0$ → physisorption**; $\Delta E_a < 0$ → chemisorption.
- **Synergism parameter $S < 1$** → synergistic; $S = 1$ → additive; $S > 1$ → antagonistic.
- **Always remember the 55.5 factor** (molar concentration of water) in the $\Delta G_{ads}$ formula.
- **Toxicity matters:** Cr⁶⁺, Pb, As, Cd are **banned/restricted** under REACH; prefer **green inhibitors** (plant extracts, molybdates, phosphonates).
- **Typical dose range:** 50–500 ppm for most industrial applications; pick the **lowest effective dose** to minimize cost and environmental burden.
- **KTU 2024 trend:** Examiner rewards students who **justify** their inhibitor choice with **multiple criteria** (cost + toxicity + efficiency + stability), not just I.E. alone.
- **Real-world domains to memorize:** Oil & gas (imidazoline), cooling water (phosphate + Zn²⁺), pickling (thiourea + propargyl alcohol), acid cleaning (amines), atmospheric (VPI).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Important Terms Related to Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Important Terms Related to Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Corrosion Inhibitor

A **corrosion inhibitor** is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations (typically in the range of **0.1 % to 1 %** by weight) to a corrosive environment, effectively reduces the rate of attack on the metal surface without significantly altering the bulk properties of the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "An inhibitor is a substance which when added in small concentration to a corrosive environment, effectively decreases the corrosion rate of the metal exposed to that environment."

Mathematically, an inhibitor is any species that shifts the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) in a direction that reduces the **anodic dissolution current density** ($i_a$) or the **cathodic reduction current density** ($i_c$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Bodyguard of the Metal":**
> Imagine a metal surface is a king in a hostile, acidic kingdom (the corrosive electrolyte). A corrosion inhibitor is like a loyal **bodyguard** that steps between the king and the attackers (H⁺, O₂, Cl⁻ ions). The bodyguard may:
> 1. **Stand at the gates** (anodic sites) and block attackers → *Anodic inhibitor*
> 2. **Disarm the attackers at the walls** (cathodic sites) → *Cathodic inhibitor*
> 3. **Spray a protective mist** (form a thin film) over the entire king → *Film-forming inhibitor*
>
> Just as a bodyguard doesn't need to be huge — just **smart and present in the right places** — an inhibitor only needs to be added in tiny amounts to dramatically slow down corrosion.

## 1.3 Why Inhibitors Are Used in Engineering

- **Economical:** No need to redesign the alloy or change the equipment.
- **Retrofit-friendly:** Can be applied to existing pipelines, tanks, and machinery.
- **Versatile:** Works in aqueous, acidic, alkaline, and even atmospheric conditions.
- **Energy-saving:** Reduces material loss, downtime, and replacement costs.

## 1.4 Categories of "Important Terms" in Inhibitor Science

The KTU Module 4 syllabus groups inhibitor-related terminology into the following functional clusters:

| Cluster | Key Terms Covered |
|---|---|
| Behavioural classification | Anodic, Cathodic, Mixed, Safe, Dangerous |
| Mechanistic classification | Passivating, Film-forming, Adsorption, Vapour-phase |
| Application context | Pickling inhibitor, Anti-tarnish inhibitor, Slushing compound |
| Performance metrics | Inhibitor Efficiency (IE %), Threshold concentration, Synergism, Antagonism |
| Special inhibitor types | Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor (VCI), Synergistic blend, Green inhibitor |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Inhibitors — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Based on Electrode Behaviour

1. **Anodic Inhibitors (Anode-Active Inhibitors)**
   - Function: Suppress the **anodic reaction** $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^-$
   - Mechanism: Form a protective, often **insoluble oxide/hydroxide film** on anodic sites.
   - Examples: Chromates ($CrO_4^{2-}$), Phosphates ($PO_4^{3-}$), Tungstates, Nitrites, Molybdates, Silicates.
   - Risk: If added in *insufficient* concentration, they cause **localized (pitting) corrosion** — hence termed **"Dangerous Inhibitors"**.

2. **Cathodic Inhibitors**
   - Function: Suppress the **cathodic reaction** (either $2H^+ + 2e^- \to H_2$ in acid or $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \to 4OH^-$ in neutral media).
   - Mechanism: Form a film on cathodic sites that either (a) blocks H⁺ access or (b) traps dissolved O₂.
   - Examples: ZnSO₄, Polyphosphates, Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg²⁺, Ni²⁺ salts.
   - Risk: Generally **"Safe Inhibitors"** because deficiency does not cause pitting — corrosion is merely reduced proportionally.

3. **Mixed Inhibitors**
   - Function: Suppress both anodic and cathodic reactions **simultaneously**.
   - Mechanism: Form a barrier film over the **entire metal surface**.
   - Examples: Gelatin, Glue, Tannin, Starch, Sodium benzoate, certain amines and imidazolines.

### 2.1.2 Based on Chemical Nature

| Type | Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Inorganic inhibitors** | Oxidize the metal to passivate it, or precipitate as insoluble films | $Na_2CrO_4$, $NaNO_2$, $Na_2MoO_4$, $ZnSO_4$ |
| **Organic inhibitors** | Adsorb on the metal surface via polar groups (N, S, O, P) | Amines, Imidazolines, Schiff bases, Thiourea, Benzotriazole |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanism of Organic Inhibitors — The Three-Step Adsorption Model:**
> 1. **Transport** of inhibitor molecule from bulk solution to metal/solution interface.
> 2. **Adsorption** (physical, chemical, or both) onto the metal surface.
> 3. **Formation of a coherent protective film** that blocks active sites.

### 2.1.3 Based on Application Environment

- **Acid Solution Inhibitors** — used in acid pickling (e.g., thiourea, propargyl alcohol).
- **Neutral Solution Inhibitors** — used in cooling water (e.g., polyphosphates, nitrites).
- **Vapour Phase Inhibitors (VCI)** — used for atmospheric corrosion protection of stored equipment (e.g., cyclohexylamine carbonate, benzotriazole).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-ready**. Memorize the formulas, units, and the exact symbols used in board valuation.

| # | Term / Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion Rate (without inhibitor) | $CR_0$ | mpy or mm/year | Determined by weight loss, Tafel extrapolation, or $R_p$ method |
| 2 | Corrosion Rate (with inhibitor) | $CR_i$ | mpy or mm/year | Measured at the *same* temperature, time, and electrolyte |
| 3 | **Inhibitor Efficiency (IE)** | $IE\,(\%) = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Primary metric. Higher % = better inhibition |
| 4 | **Degree of Surface Coverage (θ)** | $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} = \dfrac{IE}{100}$ | Dimensionless (0 → 1) | Required for adsorption isotherm analysis |
| 5 | Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | $C$ in mol/L, $K_{ads}$ in L/mol | Linear plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ gives slope = 1 |
| 6 | Temkin Isotherm | $\theta = \dfrac{2.303}{2a}\log(K_{ads}\,C)$ | $a$ = lateral interaction parameter | Used when θ vs log C is linear |
| 7 | Freundlich Isotherm | $\log\theta = \log K_{ads} + n \log C$ | $n$ = heterogeneity constant | Empirical, multilayer coverage |
| 8 | Synergistic Parameter ($S_I$) | $S_I = \dfrac{1 - \theta_{1+2}}{1 - \theta'_{1+2}}$ | $\theta$ = measured, $\theta'$ = expected from individual action | $S_I > 1$: synergism; $S_I < 1$: antagonism; $S_I = 1$: additive |
| 9 | Free Energy of Adsorption | $\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5\,K_{ads})$ | kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ in K | $\Delta G_{ads} < -40$ kJ/mol ⇒ chemisorption; between $-20$ and $-40$ ⇒ mixed; $> -20$ ⇒ physisorption |
| 10 | Standard Hydrogen Electrode Reference | $E^0_{H^+/H_2} = 0.000$ V at 25 °C | Volts | Universal reference for all inhibitor potential measurements |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note on Pipes (|) in Markdown:** In exam answer scripts, students often write $\vert x \vert$ using vertical bars. In our formula sheet we used $\dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0}$ rather than $\left\vert \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \right\vert$ to avoid confusion — though for IE, values are inherently positive.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cooling water systems** in power plants use **polyphosphate–Zn²⁺ blends** (synergistic cathodic inhibitors).
- **Oil & gas pipelines** use **filming amine inhibitors** in the gas phase at the wellhead.
- **Acid pickling baths** in steel finishing use **thiourea derivatives** to prevent acid attack on the base metal.
- **Military/aerospace storage** uses **VCI papers and foils** for long-term preservation of weapons and spares.
- **Reinforced concrete** uses **calcium nitrite** as an anodic inhibitor against chloride-induced rebar corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Inhibitor Efficiency (IE) from Faraday's Law

We derive the IE expression starting from Faraday's law of electrolysis, which is the foundation of all weight-loss-based corrosion rate measurements.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Faraday's Law

The mass lost $\Delta m$ of a metal undergoing electrochemical corrosion is given by:

$$\Delta m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $I$ = corrosion current in **amperes (A)**
- $t$ = time of exposure in **seconds (s)**
- $M$ = atomic mass of metal in **g/mol**
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per metal atom
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**

### 3.1.2 Conversion to Corrosion Rate (CR)

The corrosion rate in terms of penetration depth per year (mm/year) is:

$$CR = \frac{\Delta m}{\rho \cdot A \cdot t} \cdot 8.76 \times 10^4$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of metal in **g/cm³**
- $A$ = exposed surface area in **cm²**
- $8.76 \times 10^4$ = unit conversion factor (hours × 10 mm/m / 8760 h/year, etc.)

### 3.1.3 Substituting Two Scenarios

Let:
- $CR_0$ = corrosion rate **without** inhibitor (blank experiment)
- $CR_i$ = corrosion rate **with** inhibitor at concentration $C_i$

Since $\Delta m$ is directly proportional to corrosion current $I_{corr}$, and Faraday's law shows a linear relationship, we can write the rate ratio as:

$$\frac{CR_i}{CR_0} = \frac{I_{corr,\,i}}{I_{corr,\,0}}$$

### 3.1.4 Defining the Reduction in Corrosion

The **fractional reduction** in corrosion is:

$$\Delta CR = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} = 1 - \frac{CR_i}{CR_0}$$

### 3.1.5 Final Formula — Inhibitor Efficiency

Multiplying by 100 to express as a percentage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IE\,(\%) &= \left( \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( 1 - \frac{CR_i}{CR_0} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( 1 - \frac{I_{corr,\,i}}{I_{corr,\,0}} \right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **board-exam-ready expression** that examiners expect verbatim.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalences that students often confuse:**
> - $IE\,(\%) = 100 \times \theta$ (numerically, since $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0}$)
> - $CR_i = CR_0(1 - \theta)$
> - If $IE = 95\,\%$, then $\theta = 0.95$ and only $5\,\%$ of original corrosion is happening.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Synergistic Parameter $S_I$

The synergistic parameter quantifies whether a combination of two inhibitors performs **better** (synergism), **worse** (antagonism), or **as expected** (additive) compared to their individual performances.

### 3.2.1 Definition of Measured Combined Surface Coverage

When two inhibitors $A$ and $B$ are mixed, the measured combined surface coverage is:

$$\theta_{A+B} = \theta_{A+B}^{measured}$$

### 3.2.2 Definition of Expected Combined Surface Coverage

If the two inhibitors acted independently, the expected coverage (assuming no interaction) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta'_{A+B} &= \theta_A + \theta_B - \theta_A \theta_B \\
&= 1 - (1 - \theta_A)(1 - \theta_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** The probability that a site is uncovered by *both* $A$ and $B$ is $(1-\theta_A)(1-\theta_B)$, so the probability of being covered by *at least one* is its complement.

### 3.2.3 Final Synergistic Parameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_I &= \frac{1 - \theta_{A+B}}{1 - \theta'_{A+B}} \\
&= \frac{1 - \theta_{A+B}}{1 - [1 - (1-\theta_A)(1-\theta_B)]} \\
&= \frac{1 - \theta_{A+B}}{(1 - \theta_A)(1 - \theta_B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision rule:**
- $S_I > 1$ → **Synergism** (cooperative enhancement)
- $S_I = 1$ → **Additive behaviour**
- $S_I < 1$ → **Antagonism** (interference / competition)

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Inhibitor to Choose?

| Service Environment | Recommended Inhibitor Class | Typical Compound | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acid pickling (HCl, H₂SO₄) | Organic adsorption-type | Thiourea, Propargyl alcohol, 2-Mercaptobenzimidazole | Adsorbs on active sites, blocks H⁺ reduction |
| Cooling water (neutral pH) | Cathodic + Anodic blend | Polyphosphate + Zn²⁺ | Synergistic film formation |
| Concrete rebar | Anodic passivator | Calcium nitrite [Ca(NO₂)₂] | Oxidizes Fe to passive Fe₂O₃ |
| Boiler water | Mixed / volatile | Hydrazine, Morpholine | Removes O₂, adjusts pH |
| Fuel storage tanks | Film-forming + VCI | Benzotriazole (for Cu), Dicyclohexylamine nitrite | Protects against water carryover |
| Marine atmosphere | Vapour phase | Cyclohexylamine carbonate | Sublimes and deposits on metal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Corrosion Inhibitors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CORROSION INHIBITORS]:::root
    A --> B[Based on Electrode Behaviour]
    A --> C[Based on Chemical Nature]
    A --> D[Based on Application]

    B --> B1[Anodic Inhibitor]
    B --> B2[Cathodic Inhibitor]
    B --> B3[Mixed Inhibitor]

    B1 --> B1a[Dangerous if underdosed]
    B1 --> B1b[Examples: Chromate, Phosphate, Nitrite, Molybdate]

    B2 --> B2a[Safe - even underdose is harmless]
    B2 --> B2b[Examples: ZnSO4, Polyphosphate, Mg salts]

    B3 --> B3a[Blocks entire surface]
    B3 --> B3b[Examples: Gelatin, Tannin, Starch]

    C --> C1[Inorganic]
    C --> C2[Organic]

    C1 --> C1a[Passivating: Chromate, Nitrite]
    C1 --> C1b[Precipitating: Phosphate, Silicate]

    C2 --> C2a[Amines, Imidazolines]
    C2 --> C2b[Schiff bases, Heterocyclics]

    D --> D1[Acid Solution Inhibitor]
    D --> D2[Neutral Solution Inhibitor]
    D --> D3[Vapour Phase Inhibitor VCI]

    D1 --> D1a[Pickling: Thiourea]
    D2 --> D2a[Cooling water: Polyphosphate]
    D3 --> D3a[Storage: Cyclohexylamine carbonate]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#bfdbfe,color:#0f172a,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Mechanism of Action Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph env[AGGRESSIVE ENVIRONMENT]
        e1[H+ ions]:::aggressor
        e2[Dissolved O2]:::aggressor
        e3[Cl- ions]:::aggressor
    end

    subgraph inb[INHIBITOR SYSTEM]
        i1[Anodic inhibitor]:::anodic
        i2[Cathodic inhibitor]:::cathodic
        i3[Mixed / Film-forming inhibitor]:::mixed
    end

    subgraph surf[METAL SURFACE]
        s1[Anodic sites - metal dissolution]:::anode
        s2[Cathodic sites - O2 or H+ reduction]:::cathode
        s3[Passive film - Fe2O3 / inhibitor layer]:::film
    end

    e1 --> s2
    e2 --> s2
    e3 --> s1

    i1 -->|Suppresses| s1
    i2 -->|Suppresses| s2
    i3 -->|Covers entirely| s3

    s1 -->|Inhibited| s3
    s2 -->|Inhibited| s3

    classDef aggressor fill:#fee2e2,color:#7f1d1d,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:1px
    classDef anodic fill:#fef3c7,color:#78350f,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cathodic fill:#dbeafe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mixed fill:#dcfce7,color:#14532d,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:1px
    classDef anode fill:#fecaca,color:#7f1d1d,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cathode fill:#bfdbfe,color:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:1px
    classDef film fill:#e9d5ff,color:#581c87,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Inhibitor Selection Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Identify Service Environment]):::start
    Start --> Q1{pH of medium?}:::decision
    Q1 -->|Acidic pH lt 5| A1[Pickling or Acid Service]:::acid
    Q1 -->|Neutral pH 5 to 9| A2[Cooling / Boiler Water]:::neutral
    Q1 -->|Alkaline pH gt 9| A3[Concrete / Caustic]:::alkaline

    A1 --> R1[Use organic adsorption inhibitor\nEg: Thiourea, Propargyl alcohol]:::recom
    A2 --> R2[Use polyphosphate + Zn2+\nSynergistic cathodic blend]:::recom
    A3 --> R3[Use anodic passivator\nEg: Calcium nitrite]:::recom

    R1 --> Check{Critical concentration\nreached?}:::check
    R2 --> Check
    R3 --> Check

    Check -->|Yes| Pass[Inhibitor performs efficiently\nIE > 80 percent]:::pass
    Check -->|No for anodic| Fail[Dangerous - risk of pitting!\nAdd more inhibitor]:::fail
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typical **short-answer** items from KTU 2024 ESE Paper — each answer should fit in 80–120 words.

### Q1. Define a corrosion inhibitor. Distinguish between a "safe" and a "dangerous" inhibitor with one example each.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**
A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentration (typically **0.1 – 1 %**) to a corrosive environment, decreases the corrosion rate of the exposed metal.
- **Safe inhibitor:** A cathodic-type inhibitor whose under-dosing merely reduces protection proportionally, without causing localized attack. Example: **ZnSO₄** or polyphosphate.
- **Dangerous inhibitor:** An anodic-type inhibitor; if its concentration falls below a critical threshold, it causes severe **pitting corrosion** at the unprotected anodic sites. Example: **Sodium chromate (Na₂CrO₄)** or sodium nitrite.

---

### Q2. What is meant by "Synergism" in corrosion inhibition? Give one example.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**
Synergism refers to the **cooperative enhancement** of inhibition efficiency when two or more inhibitors are used together, producing a combined effect that is **greater than the sum of their individual effects** (Synergistic parameter $S_I > 1$).
**Example:** The combination of **polyphosphate + Zn²⁺** in cooling water systems — the polyphosphate forms a film on cathodic sites while Zn²⁺ precipitates as Zn(OH)₂ on anodic regions, providing complete surface protection that neither achieves alone.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Module 4 of PEICT866 carries an internal choice. The examiner sets **either OR**, so you must be able to answer both alternatives comfortably. Each sub-part is **7 marks** (≈ 3 pages handwritten).

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Classify corrosion inhibitors based on their **electrode behaviour**. Explain anodic and cathodic inhibitors with mechanism, examples, and the risk associated with each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification based on electrode behaviour:**

**(i) Anodic Inhibitors:**
- Suppress the anodic reaction: $M \to M^{n+} + ne^-$
- They form an **insoluble, passive oxide/hydroxide film** on anodic sites, raising the anodic polarization.
- **Examples:** Sodium chromate (Na₂CrO₄), Sodium nitrite (NaNO₂), Sodium phosphate (Na₃PO₄), Sodium molybdate (Na₂MoO₄).
- **Risk:** These are termed **"Dangerous Inhibitors"**. If added in concentrations **below the critical value**, the inhibitor film does not completely cover anodic zones. This leads to small anodes and large cathodes, intensifying **pitting corrosion**.

**(ii) Cathodic Inhibitors:**
- Suppress the cathodic reaction — either H⁺ reduction (in acid) or O₂ reduction (in neutral media).
- Form a film on cathodic sites that physically blocks the cathodic reactant (H⁺ or O₂).
- **Examples:** ZnSO₄, polyphosphates, Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg²⁺ salts, Ni²⁺ salts.
- **Risk:** They are **"Safe Inhibitors"** because under-dosing only causes the corrosion rate to fall back to its original blank value, without intensifying localized attack.

---

**(b)** Define **Inhibitor Efficiency**. Derive the expression for percentage inhibitor efficiency using corrosion rates. A mild steel specimen shows a weight loss of **220 mg** in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ without inhibitor, and **38 mg** with an inhibitor at 25 °C over 5 hours. Calculate the **IE (%)** and the **surface coverage (θ)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Inhibitor Efficiency (IE) is a measure of the effectiveness of an inhibitor, expressed as the **percentage reduction in corrosion rate** caused by the inhibitor relative to the uninhibited (blank) condition.

**Derivation:**

Starting from Faraday's law, the mass loss $\Delta m$ is directly proportional to the corrosion current $I_{corr}$:

$$\Delta m = \frac{I_{corr} \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

The corrosion rate (CR) is proportional to the corrosion current density $i_{corr} = I_{corr}/A$, so:

$$CR_0 = k \cdot I_{corr,\,0} \quad \text{and} \quad CR_i = k \cdot I_{corr,\,i}$$

The fractional reduction in corrosion is:

$$1 - \frac{CR_i}{CR_0} = 1 - \frac{I_{corr,\,i}}{I_{corr,\,0}}$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$IE\,(\%) = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{I_{corr,\,i}}{I_{corr,\,0}}\right) \times 100$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- Weight loss without inhibitor: $W_0 = 220$ mg
- Weight loss with inhibitor: $W_i = 38$ mg
- Since all other conditions (area, time, density) are identical, the **weight loss ratio = corrosion rate ratio**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
IE\,(\%) &= \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{220 - 38}{220} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{182}{220} \times 100 \\
&= 82.73\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Surface coverage:

$$\theta = \frac{IE}{100} = 0.8273$$

**Conclusion:** The inhibitor provides **82.73 % protection**, with a surface coverage of **0.8273** — indicating the inhibitor molecule is adsorbed on approximately 82.7 % of the active metal surface.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Define **passivator**. Explain the mechanism of passivation by anodic inhibitors. What is a **volatile corrosion inhibitor (VCI)**? Give two examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Passivator:** A passivator is a chemical substance (typically an oxidizing anion) that reacts with the metal surface to form a thin, adherent, and **insoluble passive oxide film** (e.g., Fe₂O₃ on iron) which drastically reduces the anodic dissolution rate.

**Mechanism of passivation by anodic inhibitors:**

For mild steel in aerated water, the addition of sodium chromate (Na₂CrO₄) promotes:

1. **Oxidation of Fe⁰ to Fe²⁺** at anodic sites.
2. **Precipitation of Fe(OH)₃** as a thin gel layer.
3. **Incorporation of Cr³⁺** into the film, forming a mixed **Fe₂O₃–Cr₂O₃** passive layer.
4. The film acts as a **diffusion barrier** to both metal cations and aggressive anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻), pushing the corrosion potential into the passive region of the Pourbaix diagram.

**Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor (VCI):**
A VCI is a chemical compound with appreciable **vapour pressure** at ambient temperature. It sublimes and its vapour migrates to all parts of a closed system (e.g., a stored gear-box or a wrapped boiler), where it **condenses and adsorbs** on metal surfaces forming a thin protective monomolecular film.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Cyclohexylamine carbonate (CHC)** — used for ferrous metals.
2. **Benzotriazole (BTA)** — particularly effective for **copper and copper alloys**.

---

**(b)** Define **Synergism** and **Antagonism** in the context of corrosion inhibition. Derive the **Synergistic Parameter** ($S_I$) and state the decision rule for interpretation. A combination of inhibitor A (IE = 70 %) and inhibitor B (IE = 50 %) gives a measured combined IE of **94 %**. Calculate $S_I$ and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Synergism:** The combined effect of two inhibitors produces a **greater inhibition** than the sum of their individual effects ($S_I > 1$).

**Antagonism:** The combined effect is **less effective** than expected from individual actions ($S_I < 1$).

**Derivation of Synergistic Parameter:**

The surface coverage of inhibitor A alone: $\theta_A = IE_A/100$
The surface coverage of inhibitor B alone: $\theta_B = IE_B/100$

If A and B act independently (no interaction), the **expected combined surface coverage** is:

$$\theta'_{A+B} = 1 - (1 - \theta_A)(1 - \theta_B)$$

The **measured** combined surface coverage is $\theta_{A+B} = IE_{combined}/100$.

The **Synergistic Parameter** is defined as:

$$S_I = \frac{1 - \theta_{A+B}}{1 - \theta'_{A+B}} = \frac{1 - \theta_{A+B}}{(1 - \theta_A)(1 - \theta_B)}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- $S_I > 1$ → Synergism
- $S_I = 1$ → Additive behaviour
- $S_I < 1$ → Antagonism

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given:
- $IE_A = 70\,\% \Rightarrow \theta_A = 0.70$
- $IE_B = 50\,\% \Rightarrow \theta_B = 0.50$
- $IE_{combined} = 94\,\% \Rightarrow \theta_{A+B} = 0.94$

Step 1: Expected combined surface coverage:

$$\theta'_{A+B} = 1 - (1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.50) = 1 - (0.30)(0.50) = 1 - 0.15 = 0.85$$

Step 2: Synergistic Parameter:

$$S_I = \frac{1 - 0.94}{(1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.50)} = \frac{0.06}{(0.30)(0.50)} = \frac{0.06}{0.15} = 0.40$$

Step 3: Interpretation:
Since **$S_I = 0.40 < 1$**, the system exhibits **Antagonism** — the combined inhibition is **less effective** than the simple sum of individual effects. This often happens when both inhibitors compete for the same active sites on the metal surface.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Mixing up dangerous and safe inhibitors:** Students often write "anodic inhibitors are safe" — this is **WRONG**. Anodic inhibitors are **dangerous**; cathodic inhibitors are **safe**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in IE:** IE is a dimensionless percentage; do not write "cm/year" beside it.
> 3. **Confusing $S_I$ decision rule:** $S_I > 1$ is synergism, NOT $< 1$. This is the **#1 most-common error** in board exams.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of IE:** Examiners expect to see Faraday's law or at least the proportionality $\Delta m \propto I_{corr}$ before jumping to the final formula.
> 5. **Writing Passivator without example:** Always quote at least one example (NaNO₂, Na₂CrO₄) when defining a passivator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Block — Read this the night before the exam.**

- ✅ **Inhibitor:** A chemical (0.1 – 1 %) that reduces corrosion rate without altering the medium's bulk properties.
- ✅ **Anodic inhibitors** form oxide/hydroxide films on anodic sites — **DANGEROUS** (cause pitting if under-dosed). Examples: chromate, nitrite, phosphate, molybdate.
- ✅ **Cathodic inhibitors** form films on cathodic sites — **SAFE**. Examples: ZnSO₄, polyphosphate, Mg²⁺.
- ✅ **Mixed inhibitors** form a blanket film on the entire surface. Examples: gelatin, tannin, starch.
- ✅ **Passivator** = oxidizing inhibitor that forms a thin passive oxide film (Fe₂O₃).
- ✅ **VCI** = Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor; sublimes and adsorbs on metal. Examples: Cyclohexylamine carbonate, Benzotriazole.
- ✅ **Pickling inhibitor** = added to acid baths during descaling to protect the base metal. Examples: thiourea, propargyl alcohol.
- ✅ **Inhibitor Efficiency formula:** $IE\,(\%) = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$
- ✅ **Surface coverage:** $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} = \dfrac{IE}{100}$
- ✅ **Synergism:** $S_I > 1$ — combined effect > sum of individual effects.
- ✅ **Antagonism:** $S_I < 1$ — combined effect < expected.
- ✅ **Additive:** $S_I = 1$ — combined effect = sum of individual effects.
- ✅ **Synergistic Parameter formula:** $S_I = \dfrac{1 - \theta_{A+B}}{(1-\theta_A)(1-\theta_B)}$
- ✅ **Adsorption classification (ΔG_ads):** $> -20$ kJ/mol → physisorption; between $-20$ and $-40$ kJ/mol → mixed; $< -40$ kJ/mol → chemisorption.
- ✅ **Langmuir isotherm linear form:** $C/\theta = 1/K_{ads} + C$ — slope = 1 for ideal Langmuir behaviour.
- ✅ **55.5** = molar concentration of water in 1 L (used in $\Delta G_{ads}$ calculations).
- ✅ **Green inhibitors** = plant extracts (e.g., Azadirachta indica, Murraya koenigii) — biodegradable alternatives to toxic chromates.
- ✅ **Threshold concentration** = minimum inhibitor concentration below which protection fails (especially critical for anodic inhibitors).
- ✅ **F = 96485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant — must be remembered verbatim).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **corrosion inhibitor** is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations (typically **0.1% to 1%** by weight) to a corrosive environment, effectively decreases the rate of attack of a metal exposed to that environment, without significantly altering the concentration of any corrosive agent.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):** Inhibitors are chemical compounds or mixtures that, when present in proper concentration in the environment, prevent or reduce the rate of corrosion of a metal or alloy. They function by forming a protective barrier, modifying the electrochemical behavior, or altering the chemistry of the corrosive medium.

## 1.2 Classification Overview (Master Taxonomy)

Corrosion inhibitors can be classified in **three orthogonal ways** based on different criteria, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Classification Basis | Major Categories |
|---|---|
| **Electrochemical Mechanism** | Anodic, Cathodic, Mixed (Organic) |
| **Physical State / Application** | Liquid-phase, Vapor-phase (VPI/VCI) |
| **Chemical Character** | Inorganic, Organic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** In board examinations, the *electrochemical classification* (anodic/cathodic/mixed) carries the highest weightage, followed by vapor-phase inhibitors and pickling inhibitors.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bodyguard" Model

Imagine the metal surface as a **king standing in a hostile crowd** (the corrosive electrolyte containing Cl⁻, H⁺, O₂ ions). Each aggressive ion is an "assassin" trying to attack the king.

- **Anodic inhibitors** are like **personal bodyguards** that surround the king (anodic sites) and stop the assassins from getting through — they form an *oxide armor*.
- **Cathodic inhibitors** are like **spies** that confuse the assassins (cathodic sites), preventing them from finding the king's location — they precipitate as hydroxides.
- **Mixed (organic) inhibitors** are like an **invisible force-field** that blankets the entire crowd-area, blocking *all* attacks uniformly.
- **Vapor-phase inhibitors** are like **airborne messengers** that fly into closed rooms and coat the king with a thin protective film, even when he cannot be reached directly.

## 1.4 Selection Criteria for a "Good" Inhibitor

A corrosion inhibitor must satisfy several properties to be industrially acceptable:

- **Effective at low concentrations** (ppm range)
- **Chemically stable** under operating conditions (pH, temperature)
- **Non-toxic** and environmentally benign (green chemistry mandate)
- **Compatible** with the metal and the environment
- **Economically viable** (cost-to-protection ratio < 1)
- **Easy to apply and replenish**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Any inhibitor which is **anodic in nature** is termed "dangerous" if the dosage falls below a critical concentration (the **"dangerous concentration"** or **"critical inhibitor concentration"**). Below this value, the inhibitor fails to form a complete protective film and accelerates *localized pitting corrosion*. This is a frequently-tested concept.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anodic Inhibitors (Passivators)

### 2.1.1 Definition and Mechanism
Anodic inhibitors act on the **anodic half-reaction** of the corrosion cell by:

$$M \rightarrow M^{n+} + ne^{-}$$

They suppress the anodic dissolution of the metal by forming a **passivating oxide film** (typically 1–10 nm thick) on the metal surface. The film acts as a diffusion barrier to metal ions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Passivation** refers to the phenomenon where a metal becomes chemically inert (corrosion-resistant) due to the formation of a thin, adherent, and highly insoluble oxide layer.

### 2.1.2 Common Anodic Inhibitors

| Compound | Chemical Formula | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Sodium chromate | $\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$ | Aqueous closed systems |
| Sodium nitrite | $\text{NaNO}_2$ | Reinforcing steel in concrete |
| Sodium molybdate | $\text{Na}_2\text{MoO}_4$ | Cooling water systems (green alternative) |
| Sodium tungstate | $\text{Na}_2\text{WO}_4$ | Refrigerant systems |
| Sodium phosphate | $\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4$ | Mild steel in water |
| Sodium silicate | $\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3$ | Water pipelines |

### 2.1.3 Reactions at the Anode
For chromate, the simplified passivation reaction is:

$$3Fe + 2Na_2CrO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe_3O_4 + Cr_2O_3 + 4NaOH$$

The mixed oxide $\text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4 \cdot \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$ provides the protective barrier.

### 2.1.4 Polarization Behavior
Anodic inhibitors shift the **anodic Tafel slope** to more positive (noble) potentials, dramatically lowering the corrosion current density $i_{corr}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **DANGEROUS INHIBITORS:** Anodic inhibitors are "dangerous" because at sub-critical concentrations, they allow only partial film formation. This creates differential aeration cells leading to **severe pitting corrosion** with penetration rates up to **1000 mpy** (mils per year). The student must remember: **"Anodic inhibitors - Safe in proper dose, lethal in low dose."**

## 2.2 Cathodic Inhibitors

### 2.2.1 Definition and Mechanism
Cathodic inhibitors suppress the **cathodic half-reaction**:

$$2H^+ + 2e^{-} \rightarrow H_2 \quad \text{(in acid)}$$

$$\frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2OH^- \quad \text{(in neutral/alkaline)}$$

They work by **either:**
1. Precipitating insoluble compounds at the cathodic sites (precipitate-type)
2. Poisoning the cathodic reaction kinetics (poison-type)
3. Removing dissolved oxygen (scavengers)

### 2.2.2 Sub-classification of Cathodic Inhibitors

| Type | Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Cathodic Precipitates** | Form insoluble hydroxides/carbonates at cathodic sites, blocking reaction | $\text{ZnSO}_4$, $\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$, $\text{MgCO}_3$ |
| **Cathodic Poisons** | Hinder hydrogen recombination reaction | Arsenic, antimony, bismuth compounds |
| **Oxygen Scavengers** | React with and remove dissolved $\text{O}_2$ | $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$, hydrazine, DEHA |

### 2.2.3 Precipitation Reaction
The cathodic area generates OH⁻ ions. A zinc salt precipitates this hydroxide:

$$\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{Zn(OH)}_2 \downarrow$$

This blanket of $\text{Zn(OH)}_2$ blocks oxygen diffusion to the cathode.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safe Inhibitors:** Cathodic inhibitors are inherently **"safe"** because even at sub-critical concentrations, they never accelerate corrosion. They simply become less effective. This contrasts sharply with the dangerous nature of anodic inhibitors.

## 2.3 Mixed (Organic / Adsorption) Inhibitors

### 2.3.1 Mechanism of Action
Mixed inhibitors (also called **adsorption inhibitors** or **film-forming organic inhibitors**) function by:

1. **Physisorption** (physical adsorption via Van der Waals forces)
2. **Chemisorption** (chemical bond formation via electron sharing/transfer)

They typically contain a **polar functional group** (heteroatom: N, S, O, P) and a **non-polar hydrocarbon tail**. The polar group attaches to the metal; the tail creates a hydrophobic barrier.

### 2.3.2 General Structural Features

$$\underbrace{\text{Hydrophobic Tail}}_{\text{(Alkyl/aryl chain)}} - \underbrace{\text{Polar Head}}_{\text{(NH}_2\text{, SH, OH, COOH)}}$$

### 2.3.3 Common Organic Inhibitors

| Inhibitor | Functional Group | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Amines (R-NH₂) | -NH₂ | Acid pickling, oil-well acidizing |
| Imidazolines | C=N ring | $\text{H}_2\text{S}/\text{CO}_2$ environments |
| Thiourea derivatives | C=S | Sulfuric acid pickling |
| Schiff bases | -CH=N- | Acid media |
| Quaternary ammonium salts | R₄N⁺ | Cooling water |
| Pyridine derivatives | C₅H₅N | Acid inhibition |
| Benzotriazole (BTA) | Triazole ring | Copper & copper alloys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synergistic Effect:** When two or more inhibitors are combined, the total inhibition efficiency is often greater than the sum of individual efficiencies. This is called **synergism**. Example: I⁻ ions greatly enhance the efficiency of amines on iron in $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$.

## 2.4 Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI / VCI)

### 2.4.1 Definition
Vapor Phase Inhibitors are **volatile organic compounds** that protect metal surfaces in enclosed spaces (storage, shipping) without direct contact with the metal. They sublime and reach the metal via the vapor phase, where they hydrolyze and form a protective film.

### 2.4.2 Classical VPI Examples

| Compound | Chemical Name | Application |
|---|---|---|
| DICHAN | Dicyclohexylamine nitrite | Steel, cast iron |
| CHC | Cyclohexylamine carbonate | Ferrous metals |
| Benzotriazole (as VCI) | BTA | Copper, brass |
| Ethylamine benzoate | C₂H₅NH₃⁺C₆H₅COO⁻ | Multi-metal packaging |

### 2.4.3 Hydrolysis Reaction (DICHAN)
At the metal surface (in presence of moisture):

$$(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11})_2\text{NH} \cdot \text{HNO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow (\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11})_2\text{NH}_2^+ + \text{NO}_2^- + \text{OH}^-$$

The released $\text{NO}_2^-$ acts as the actual anodic inhibitor, forming a passive film.

## 2.5 Special Classifications

### 2.5.1 Pickling Inhibitors
Used during **acid pickling** (removal of mill scale from hot-rolled steel using $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ or HCl). They must protect the base metal from acid attack while NOT preventing scale dissolution.

- **Examples:** Rodine (proprietary), phenyl thiourea, dibenzyl sulfoxide

### 2.5.2 Green / Environmentally Benign Inhibitors
Modern trend to replace toxic chromates. **Plant extracts** (e.g., *Azadirachta indica*, *Murraya koenigii*) contain alkaloids, tannins, and flavonoids that act as mixed inhibitors.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inhibition Efficiency** | $\text{IE(\%)} = \dfrac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0} \times 100$ | % | $\text{CR}_0$ = corrosion rate without inhibitor; $\text{CR}_i$ = with inhibitor |
| **Surface Coverage ($\theta$)** | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$ |
| **Langmuir Isotherm** | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | — | $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant; $C$ = inhibitor conc. |
| **Langmuir Linear Form** | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | — | Plot $C/\theta$ vs $C$ to verify Langmuir |
| **Temkin Isotherm** | $\exp(-2a\theta) = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | — | $a$ = molecular interaction parameter |
| **Frumkin Isotherm** | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} \exp(-2a\theta) = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | — | Accounts for lateral interactions |
| **Activation Energy (with/without inhibitor)** | $\text{CR} = A \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | mm/yr | Used in Arrhenius plots |
| **Tafel Slope** | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right)$ | V | $\beta_a$ = anodic Tafel slope (V/decade) |
| **Synergism Parameter** | $S_1 = \dfrac{1 - \theta_{1+2}}{1 - \theta'_{1+2}}$ | — | $\theta_{1+2}$ = experimental; $\theta'_{1+2}$ = calculated |
| **Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption** | $\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ | kJ/mol | $55.5$ = water molarity (mol/L) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** These equations are vital in oil & gas industry for selecting inhibitors for pipelines, in cooling water treatment plants for industrial heat exchangers, and in the automotive industry for designing anti-rust formulations for underbody coatings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Inhibition Efficiency from Tafel Extrapolation

The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ can be obtained from the **Tafel extrapolation method** on a polarization curve. The inhibition efficiency from polarization data is derived as follows.

### Step 1: Write the Tafel Equations
The anodic and cathodic current densities follow the Tafel relations:

$$\eta_a = \beta_a \log\!\left(\frac{i_a}{i_{corr}}\right) \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_c = -\beta_c \log\!\left(\frac{i_c}{i_{corr}}\right)$$

where:
- $\eta_a, \eta_c$ = anodic and cathodic overpotentials (V)
- $\beta_a, \beta_c$ = anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (V/decade)
- $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/cm²)

### Step 2: Measure $i_{corr}$ in the Absence of Inhibitor
From the polarization curve without inhibitor, extrapolate the linear (Tafel) regions of the anodic and cathodic branches back to the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$. The intersection gives $i_{corr}^{\circ}$.

### Step 3: Measure $i_{corr}$ in the Presence of Inhibitor
Repeat the experiment with the inhibitor added. The corresponding value is $i_{corr}^{i}$.

### Step 4: Apply the Inhibition Efficiency Formula
Since the corrosion rate is **directly proportional** to $i_{corr}$ (via Faraday's law):

$$\text{CR} = \frac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

Substituting the proportionality into the IE formula:

$$\text{IE(\%)} = \frac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0} \times 100$$

$$\text{IE(\%)} = \frac{i_{corr}^{\circ} - i_{corr}^{i}}{i_{corr}^{\circ}} \times 100$$

### Step 5: Simplify
The result is the **Tafel-derived inhibition efficiency**:

$$\boxed{\text{IE(\%)} = \frac{i_{corr}^{\circ} - i_{corr}^{i}}{i_{corr}^{\circ}} \times 100}$$

This is the **most commonly used** form in KTU board problems where weight loss data is NOT available.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

### Step 1: Define Adsorption Equilibrium
At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption from the metal surface. Let:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by inhibitor
- $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of bare surface
- $C$ = bulk concentration of inhibitor
- $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant (L/mol)

### Step 2: Rate of Adsorption (Forward)
Proportional to bare surface area and bulk concentration:

$$r_{ads} = k_a (1 - \theta) C$$

### Step 3: Rate of Desorption (Reverse)
Proportional to covered surface area:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

### Step 4: Equate the Rates
At equilibrium:

$$k_a (1 - \theta) C = k_d \theta$$

### Step 5: Rearrange
Divide both sides by $k_d$:

$$\frac{k_a}{k_d} (1 - \theta) C = \theta$$

Define the equilibrium constant:

$$K_{ads} = \frac{k_a}{k_d}$$

So:

$$K_{ads} (1 - \theta) C = \theta$$

### Step 6: Solve for $\theta$
Expand:

$$K_{ads} C - K_{ads} C \theta = \theta$$

$$K_{ads} C = \theta + K_{ads} C \theta = \theta(1 + K_{ads} C)$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{K_{ads} C}{1 + K_{ads} C}}$$

### Step 7: Linear Form for Plotting
Rearranging for the linear plot $C/\theta$ vs $C$:

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Test:** A plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ should yield a **straight line with slope = 1 and intercept = $1/K_{ads}$**. If experimental data fits this, Langmuir isotherm is obeyed. Any deviation suggests Temkin or Frumkin behavior.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

> **Problem:** The corrosion rate of mild steel in $1\text{ M}$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is $8.5$ mm/yr without inhibitor. With $0.5 \text{ mM}$ thiourea, the rate drops to $0.85$ mm/yr. Calculate (a) inhibition efficiency, (b) surface coverage, (c) verify Langmuir isotherm if $K_{ads} = 1.2 \times 10^4$ L/mol.

### Part (a): Inhibition Efficiency

$$\text{IE(\%)} = \frac{8.5 - 0.85}{8.5} \times 100$$

$$\text{IE(\%)} = \frac{7.65}{8.5} \times 100 = 90\%$$

### Part (b): Surface Coverage

$$\theta = \frac{\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i}{\text{CR}_0} = \frac{7.65}{8.5} = 0.90$$

### Part (c): Langmuir Verification
The Langmuir expression:

$$\theta = \frac{K_{ads} C}{1 + K_{ads} C}$$

With $C = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ M and $K_{ads} = 1.2 \times 10^4$ L/mol:

$$K_{ads} C = 1.2 \times 10^4 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 6$$

$$\theta = \frac{6}{1 + 6} = \frac{6}{7} = 0.857$$

The predicted $\theta$ (0.857) is close to the experimental $\theta$ (0.90), confirming Langmuir-type adsorption.

### Part (d): Standard Free Energy of Adsorption
Using:

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

At $T = 298$ K and $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(55.5 \times 1.2 \times 10^4)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -2477.6 \times \ln(6.66 \times 10^5)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads}^0 = -2477.6 \times 13.41 = -33,224 \text{ J/mol} \approx -33.22 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

Since $|\Delta G_{ads}^0| < 40$ kJ/mol, the adsorption is **physisorption** with a tendency toward chemisorption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Anodic Inhibitor | Cathodic Inhibitor | Mixed (Organic) Inhibitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Site of Action** | Anodic half-reaction | Cathodic half-reaction | Both anodic and cathodic |
| **Mechanism** | Passivation (oxide film) | Precipitation/Poisoning | Adsorption film |
| **Polarization Effect** | Shifts $E_{corr}$ anodically | Shifts $E_{corr}$ cathodically | Little shift in $E_{corr}$ |
| **Safety at Low Dose** | UNSAFE (causes pitting) | SAFE | SAFE (usually) |
| **Examples** | $\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$, $\text{NaNO}_2$ | $\text{ZnSO}_4$, $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$ | Amines, BTA, thiourea |
| **Concentration Required** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ M | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ M | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ M |
| **Efficiency Range** | 70–99% | 50–90% | 60–99% |
| **Toxicity** | High (Cr⁶⁺ carcinogenic) | Low to moderate | Variable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram represents the complete classification of corrosion inhibitors as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

```mermaid
graph TD
    CI[CORROSION INHIBITORS] --> EM[CLASSIFICATION BY ELECTROCHEMICAL MECHANISM]
    CI --> PS[CLASSIFICATION BY PHYSICAL STATE]
    CI --> CC[CLASSIFICATION BY CHEMICAL CHARACTER]
    EM --> AN[ANODIC INHIBITORS]
    EM --> CA[CATHODIC INHIBITORS]
    EM --> MX[MIXED INHIBITORS]
    AN --> AN1[Sodium Chromate Na2CrO4]
    AN --> AN2[Sodium Nitrite NaNO2]
    AN --> AN3[Sodium Molybdate Na2MoO4]
    AN --> AN4[Phosphates and Silicates]
    CA --> CA1[Cathodic Precipitates ZnSO4 and CaHCO3]
    CA --> CA2[Cathodic Poisons As and Sb compounds]
    CA --> CA3[Oxygen Scavengers Na2SO3 and Hydrazine]
    MX --> MX1[Amines R-NH2]
    MX --> MX2[Imidazolines]
    MX --> MX3[Thiourea Derivatives]
    MX --> MX4[Benzotriazole BTA for Copper]
    MX --> MX5[Schiff Bases]
    PS --> PS1[Liquid Phase Inhibitors]
    PS --> PS2[Vapor Phase Inhibitors VPI and VCI]
    PS2 --> PS2A[DICHAN Dicyclohexylamine Nitrite]
    PS2 --> PS2B[Cyclohexylamine Carbonate CHC]
    CC --> CC1[Inorganic Inhibitors]
    CC --> CC2[Organic Inhibitors]
    CC1 --> CC1A[Anodic type chromates and nitrites]
    CC1 --> CC1B[Cathodic type zinc and calcium salts]
    CC2 --> CC2A[Surface active amines and amides]
    CC2 --> CC2B[Heterocyclic compounds]
```

## 4.2 Mechanism Block Diagram — How Each Inhibitor Type Works

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph METAL_SURFACE [METAL SURFACE M]
        ASITE[ANODIC SITE M dissolves to Mn+]
        CSITE[CATHODIC SITE H plus or O2 reduction]
        BSITE[BARE METAL EXPOSED]
    end
    ASITE -- Anodic Inhibitor forms passive oxide film --> PROT[PROTECTED SURFACE]
    CSITE -- Cathodic Inhibitor precipitates insoluble hydroxide --> PROT
    BSITE -- Mixed Inhibitor adsorbs uniformly --> PROT
    VPI[VAPOR PHASE INHIBITOR] -- Volatilizes and condenses --> PROT
    PICK[PICKLING INHIBITOR] -- Adsorbs on M but not on mill scale --> PROT
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Inhibitor Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[PROBLEM: SELECT CORROSION INHIBITOR] --> Q1{Environment Type}
    Q1 -- Acid Pickling --> PICK[Pickling Inhibitor: Thiourea, Rodine]
    Q1 -- Neutral Aqueous Closed Loop --> NEUT[Anodic Inhibitor: Mo or Nitrite]
    Q1 -- Oil and Gas Pipeline --> OIL[Mixed Inhibitor: Imidazoline]
    Q1 -- Storage and Shipping --> STOR[VPI: DICHAN or CHC]
    Q1 -- Concrete Reinforcement --> CONC[Anodic: Calcium Nitrite]
    Q1 -- Copper Alloy System --> COP[Benzotriazole BTA]
    Q1 -- Multi-metal in Humid Air --> MULT[VPI with DICHAN]
    START --> Q2{Safety Constraint}
    Q2 -- YES, no chromates --> SAFE[Use Molybdate or Phosphate]
    Q2 -- NO --> CONV[Conventional Chromate Permitted]
```

## 4.4 Effect of Concentration on Inhibitor Behavior

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CONCENTRATION OF ANODIC INHIBITOR] --> B[Below Critical Concentration]
    A --> C[At or Above Critical Concentration]
    B --> B1[Incomplete Film Formation]
    B1 --> B2[PITTING CORROSION ACCELERATED]
    C --> C1[Complete Uniform Passive Film]
    C1 --> C2[UNIFORM CORROSION MINIMIZED]
    B2 --> D[DANGEROUS BEHAVIOR]
    C2 --> E[SAFE PROTECTION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The above block diagrams are designed in compliance with Mermaid rendering safeguards. All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels are in clean uppercase text. These diagrams are intended to be rendered as study-aid schematics in the final published KTU material.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**

### Question 1: Define corrosion inhibitor. Classify them based on their electrochemical mechanism.

**Model Answer:**

A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance that, when added in small concentrations to a corrosive environment, reduces the rate of corrosion of a metal without reacting with the corrosive species directly.

**Electrochemical Classification:**

1. **Anodic inhibitors** — Act on anodic sites by forming passive oxide films. Examples: $\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$, $\text{NaNO}_2$, molybdates.
2. **Cathodic inhibitors** — Act on cathodic sites by precipitation or poisoning. Examples: $\text{ZnSO}_4$, $\text{Ca(HCO}_3)_2$, $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$.
3. **Mixed (organic/adsorption) inhibitors** — Adsorb on the metal surface, blocking both anodic and cathodic sites. Examples: amines, imidazolines, benzotriazole.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: Why are anodic inhibitors called "dangerous inhibitors"? Explain with an example.

**Model Answer:**

Anodic inhibitors are termed "dangerous" because below a critical inhibitor concentration, they fail to form a continuous passive oxide film. This leads to partial film coverage, creating differential aeration cells and severe **localized pitting corrosion**, which can be more damaging than uniform corrosion.

**Example:** Sodium chromate ($\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$) in low concentration on mild steel in chloride-containing water leads to rapid pitting attack.

**Critical concentration** is the minimum concentration required to form a complete passive film; below this, the inhibitor is ineffective or harmful.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B, Module 4]**

**(a)** Classify corrosion inhibitors based on their mechanism of action. Explain anodic inhibitors in detail, including their mechanism, examples, and the "dangerous inhibitor" concept. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the mechanism, classification, and industrial applications of vapor phase inhibitors (VPI). Give two examples with their chemical structures. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Classification (3 marks):**
- Anodic inhibitors (passivators)
- Cathodic inhibitors (precipitators / poisons / scavengers)
- Mixed inhibitors (organic / adsorption)

**Anodic Inhibitor Detail (3 marks):**
Anodic inhibitors act by forming a thin, adherent, passive oxide film on the anodic areas of the metal. The film (e.g., $\gamma$-Fe₂O₃ + Cr₂O₃) blocks the dissolution of metal ions.

Common examples: $\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$, $\text{NaNO}_2$, $\text{Na}_2\text{MoO}_4$, $\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4$, $\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3$.

**Dangerous Inhibitor Concept (1 mark):**
At sub-critical concentration, anodic inhibitors do not form a complete film, leading to localized pitting corrosion. They are "safe only above the critical concentration."

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
VPI compounds sublime at ambient temperatures and travel as vapor to the metal surface. In presence of moisture, they hydrolyze to release active anodic/cathodic inhibitor ions (e.g., $\text{NO}_2^-$) that form a protective film.

**Classification (2 marks):**
- Amine nitrites (e.g., DICHAN)
- Amine carbonates (e.g., cyclohexylamine carbonate)
- Substituted benzotriazoles (for copper)

**Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
- Storage of machinery, tools, and spares in humid conditions
- Export packaging of precision instruments
- Protection of ammunition and military equipment
- Long-term atmospheric protection in warehouses

**Examples (1 mark):**
- DICHAN: $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11})_2\text{NH} \cdot \text{HNO}_2$ (dicyclohexylamine nitrite)
- CHC: $(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11}\text{NH}_2)_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$ (cyclohexylamine carbonate)

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of action of mixed (organic) inhibitors. Discuss the role of heteroatoms and molecular structure in adsorption. Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define inhibition efficiency and surface coverage. A corrosion rate of mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ is 10.0 mm/yr without inhibitor. With 1 mM thiourea, it is reduced to 1.5 mm/yr. Calculate the inhibition efficiency and surface coverage. Comment on the type of adsorption. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
Organic inhibitors adsorb on the metal surface via (i) physisorption (electrostatic attraction between charged inhibitor and charged metal) or (ii) chemisorption (electron pair donation from heteroatom to metal's empty d-orbitals). The adsorbed film is hydrophobic and blocks both anodic and cathodic sites.

**Role of Heteroatoms (2 marks):**
N, S, O, P atoms in the molecule donate lone pairs of electrons to the vacant d-orbitals of transition metals, forming coordinate bonds. Sulfur is most effective for iron, nitrogen for copper.

**Structure-Activity Relationship (1 mark):**
Higher molecular weight and longer carbon chains increase inhibition efficiency due to greater surface coverage. Aromatic rings (π-electrons) also enhance adsorption.

**Langmuir Isotherm Derivation (2 marks):**
At equilibrium: $k_a(1-\theta)C = k_d\theta$. Solving:

$$\theta = \frac{K_{ads} C}{1 + K_{ads} C}$$

where $K_{ads} = k_a / k_d$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Inhibition Efficiency (IE):** The percentage reduction in corrosion rate due to the addition of an inhibitor.
- **Surface Coverage ($\theta$):** The fraction of the metal surface covered by the inhibitor, dimensionless between 0 and 1.

**Calculations (4 marks):**

Given: $\text{CR}_0 = 10.0$ mm/yr, $\text{CR}_i = 1.5$ mm/yr.

$$\text{IE(\%)} = \frac{10.0 - 1.5}{10.0} \times 100 = 85\%$$

$$\theta = \frac{10.0 - 1.5}{10.0} = 0.85$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
With 85% inhibition efficiency and $\theta = 0.85$, the inhibitor shows strong adsorption. The type of adsorption (physical or chemical) can be determined by calculating $\Delta G_{ads}^0$ using the Langmuir constant.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the anodic and cathodic shift:** Students frequently mix up the direction of polarization. Anodic inhibitors shift $E_{corr}$ in the **positive (noble)** direction; cathodic inhibitors shift it in the **negative (active)** direction.
> 2. **Forgetting the "dangerous inhibitor" caveat:** Many students describe anodic inhibitors as universally good, which is incorrect. The "dangerous" nature is a hallmark question in KTU exams.
> 3. **Unit error in inhibition efficiency:** Always express IE as a **percentage (multiply by 100)**, not as a fraction. Mark loss of 1 mark is common.
> 4. **Langmuir linear form mis-plotting:** The plot is $C/\theta$ (y-axis) **vs.** $C$ (x-axis), NOT $\theta/C$. The slope of this plot is 1, and intercept gives $1/K_{ads}$.
> 5. **Confusion between VPI and VCI:** Both refer to the same class — Vapor Phase Inhibitor (VPI) and Volatile Corrosion Inhibitor (VCI) are interchangeable terms.
> 6. **Pickling inhibitors are not for long-term use:** They are meant for short-duration acid pickling operations, not for permanent corrosion protection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Corrosion inhibitor definition:** A chemical that reduces corrosion rate at low concentration (typically <1% by weight) without reacting with the corrosive species.
- [x] **Three primary classifications:** Anodic, Cathodic, and Mixed (Organic/Adsorption).
- [x] **Anodic inhibitors** form passive oxide films; examples: $\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4$, $\text{NaNO}_2$, $\text{Na}_2\text{MoO}_4$.
- [x] **"Dangerous" anodic inhibitors:** At sub-critical concentration, they cause pitting corrosion. Always specify critical concentration.
- [x] **Cathodic inhibitors** form insoluble precipitates or remove oxygen; inherently **"safe"** inhibitors.
- [x] **Examples of cathodic inhibitors:** $\text{ZnSO}_4$ (precipitate), $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3$ (oxygen scavenger), $\text{As}_2\text{O}_3$ (poison).
- [x] **Mixed (organic) inhibitors** contain heteroatoms (N, S, O, P) that adsorb on metal surface.
- [x] **Examples of organic inhibitors:** Amines, imidazolines, thiourea, benzotriazole (for copper), Schiff bases.
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm equation:** $\theta = K_{ads} C / (1 + K_{ads} C)$.
- [x] **Langmuir linear form:** $C/\theta = 1/K_{ads} + C$.
- [x] **VPI examples:** DICHAN, cyclohexylamine carbonate, ethylamine benzoate.
- [x] **Inhibition efficiency formula:** $\text{IE(\%)} = (\text{CR}_0 - \text{CR}_i)/\text{CR}_0 \times 100$.
- [x] **Surface coverage** $\theta$ is the fraction of surface covered; $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$.
- [x] **Tafel-derived IE:** $\text{IE(\%)} = (i_{corr}^{\circ} - i_{corr}^{i})/i_{corr}^{\circ} \times 100$.
- [x] **Synergism:** Combined effect of two inhibitors exceeds the sum of individual effects.
- [x] **Physisorption** when $|\Delta G_{ads}^0| < 40$ kJ/mol; **chemisorption** when $|\Delta G_{ads}^0| > 40$ kJ/mol.
- [x] **Pickling inhibitors:** Thiourea, Rodine; protect base metal during scale removal.
- [x] **Green inhibitors:** Plant extracts containing alkaloids, flavonoids, tannins.
- [x] **Anodic shift:** $E_{corr}$ moves positively with anodic inhibitors.
- [x] **Cathodic shift:** $E_{corr}$ moves negatively with cathodic inhibitors.
- [x] **Mixed inhibitors:** Little change in $E_{corr}$ (both anodic and cathodic sites blocked).
- [x] **Application sectors:** Oil & gas, cooling water, acid pickling, packaging, concrete reinforcement, marine.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 — CLASSIFICATION OF INHIBITORS**
