<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (Board-Standard)**
> **Corrosion Inhibitors** are chemical substances which, when added in small concentrations (typically **0.1 % to 1 %** by weight) to a corrosive environment, effectively **decrease, prevent, or stop** the rate of corrosion of a metal or alloy without significantly altering the concentration of any reactive species in the bulk medium. They function by forming a protective film (adsorbed, precipitated, or passive oxide layer) on the metal surface, thereby isolating the anodic and/or cathodic reaction sites from the aggressive electrolyte.

The operative mechanism satisfies the fundamental corrosion current relation derived from the **Evans Diagram** and the **Mixed Potential Theory**:

$$I_{corr}^{inhibited} \ll I_{corr}^{blank}$$

where $I_{corr}^{inhibited}$ and $I_{corr}^{blank}$ are the corrosion current densities in inhibited and uninhibited (blank) solutions, respectively.

### 1.1.1 Inhibitor Efficiency (IE) — The Master Performance Metric

The **Inhibitor Efficiency** is a dimensionless number (expressed as a **percentage**) and is the most frequently asked quantitative definition in KTU examinations:

$$IE\,\left(\%\right) = \frac{W_{0} - W_{i}}{W_{0}} \times 100$$

$$IE\,\left(\%\right) = \frac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \times 100$$

where:
- $W_{0}$ = weight loss of metal in **blank** (uninhibited) solution
- $W_{i}$ = weight loss of metal in **inhibited** solution
- $CR_{0}$ = corrosion rate in blank solution (mpy or mm/yr)
- $CR_{i}$ = corrosion rate in inhibited solution

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Raincoat"

> [!NOTE] **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine you are walking in heavy acidic rain. Without protection, your clothes (the metal) get chemically attacked. Now imagine you spray a thin, transparent chemical film on your clothes — the rain still falls, but the acid droplets cannot touch the fabric directly. The **corrosion inhibitor is that invisible raincoat for metals**!
>
> - The **clothes** = the metal surface (steel, copper, aluminum)
> - The **acidic rain** = the corrosive electrolyte (HCl, H₂SO₄, NaCl solution)
> - The **invisible spray** = the inhibitor molecules forming a **mono-molecular protective film**
> - The **"not getting wet"** = drastic drop in corrosion current $I_{corr}$

Even more powerfully, certain inhibitors do not just coat the surface — they **chemically rebuild** a damaged passive oxide layer (e.g., chromates on aluminum), acting like an **auto-healing raincoat**.

---

## 1.3 Classification — The Master Map

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board-Favorite Taxonomy**
> Inhibitors are classified based on **three independent criteria** that examiners love to mix-and-match in Part A questions.

| Classification Basis | Categories | Sub-Categories |
|---|---|---|
| **By Electrode Behaviour** | Anodic | Oxidizing (passivators), Non-oxidizing |
| | Cathodic | Cathodic poison, Cathodic precipitate, Oxygen scavenger |
| | Mixed / Dual | Organic film formers |
| **By Chemical Nature** | Inorganic | Chromates, Phosphates, Nitrates, Silicates |
| | Organic | Amines, Imidazolines, Thioureas, Azoles |
| **By Physical State** | Liquid phase | Acidic, Neutral, Alkaline media inhibitors |
| | Vapor phase | VPI (Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors) |

**Syllabus highlight (Module 4 specific):** The KTU Module 4 focus is the **descriptive analysis** of each class, their **mechanism**, **selection criteria**, **synergism**, and **practical limitations** (especially the environmental hazards of chromates under REACH regulations).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Description of Each Class of Inhibitor

### 2.1.1 Anodic Inhibitors (Anodic Passivators)

> [!NOTE] **Operational Logic**
> These inhibitors suppress the **anodic (metal dissolution) reaction**:
> $$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$
> They achieve this by either (a) oxidizing the metal to form a thick, adherent passive oxide, or (b) precipitating insoluble compounds at anodic sites.

**Type 1 — Oxidizing Anodic Inhibitors (Passivators):**
These molecules themselves accept electrons (i.e., are reduced) while oxidizing the metal surface. They **don't need oxygen** to function.

Examples: **Chromates (CrO₄²⁻), Dichromates (Cr₂O₇²⁻), Nitrites (NO₂⁻), Molybdates (MoO₄²⁻), Permanganates (MnO₄⁻), Tungstates (WO₄²⁻)**.

Mechanism on iron in neutral aerated water:
$$2\text{Fe} + 2\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 4\text{NaOH}$$

The co-deposited **Cr₂O₃** creates a mixed Fe-Cr spinel oxide that is **self-healing** at defect sites.

**Critical Pitfall (Dangerous Inhibitor):** Chromates and nitrites are **"dangerous inhibitors"** — if added in **concentration below the critical passivation concentration**, they *accelerate* pitting corrosion by forming localized patches. This is a guaranteed KTU 2-mark question.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Passivation Concentration (CPC):** Below CPC → ACCELERATED pitting. Above CPC → COMPLETE passivation. Chromates require **≥ 500 ppm** in seawater; below this threshold, they cause catastrophic localized attack.

**Type 2 — Non-Oxidizing Anodic Inhibitors:**
These need **dissolved oxygen** to function. They form sparingly soluble precipitates with metal ions at anodic sites.
Examples: **Phosphates (PO₄³⁻), Carbonates (CO₃²⁻), Silicates (SiO₃²⁻), Borates (BO₃³⁻), Tungstates, Molybdates**.

Reaction with ferrous ions in aerated water:
$$3\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \,(\text{insoluble protective film})$$

---

### 2.1.2 Cathodic Inhibitors

> [!NOTE] **Operational Logic**
> They suppress the **cathodic reaction**. In neutral/alkaline aerated systems, the cathodic reaction is **oxygen reduction**:
> $$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$
> In acids, the cathodic reaction is **hydrogen evolution**:
> $$2\text{H}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{H}_2$$

**Sub-Class 1 — Cathodic Precipitates:**
React with the cathodically-generated **OH⁻** to form insoluble hydroxides that blanket the cathode.
Examples: **ZnSO₄, ZnCl₂ (zinc salts), Ca(HCO₃)₂, Mg salts, Polyphosphates**.

$$2\text{OH}^{-} + \text{Zn}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Zn(OH)}_2 \downarrow \;(\text{protective blanket over cathode})$$

Polyphosphates (e.g., sodium hexametaphosphate, "Calgon") form a thin film of **Zn₃(PO₄)₂** over cathodic areas. They are synergistic with Zn²⁺ — this is the classic **synergistic cathodic inhibitor formulation** (always a KTU favorite).

**Sub-Class 2 — Cathodic Poisons (Hydrogen Evolution Suppressers):**
Used in **acid pickling** to stop hydrogen embrittlement and acid waste.
Examples: **Arsenic trioxide (As₂O₃), Antimony salts (Sb₂O₃), Bismuth salts, Selenides, Tellurides**.

They adsorb on the metal surface and **raise the hydrogen overpotential** $\eta_H$:

$$\eta_H = \eta_H^{blank} + \Delta\eta_H^{inhibitor}$$

A higher $\eta_H$ means the H⁺ ions need much more energy to discharge → reduced H₂ evolution → reduced cathodic reaction rate.

**Sub-Class 3 — Oxygen Scavengers:**
Remove dissolved O₂ so the cathodic reaction cannot proceed.
Examples: **Sodium sulfite (Na₂SO₃), Hydrazine (N₂H₄), Diethylhydroxylamine (DEHA), Tannins**.

$$2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

DEHA is preferred in modern boiler systems because it is **low-toxicity** and the reaction by-products (acetic acid, DEA) are environmentally benign.

---

### 2.1.3 Mixed (Dual) Inhibitors

> [!NOTE] **Operational Logic**
> They simultaneously retard **both anodic and cathodic reactions** by forming a **3D adsorbed film** over the entire metal surface.

**Sub-Class 1 — Organic Film Formers (the largest commercial class):**
Long-chain molecules with a **polar head** (N, S, O, P) that adsorbs on the metal and a **non-polar hydrocarbon tail** that creates a hydrophobic barrier.

Examples by functional group:
| Functional Group | Typical Examples | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| Amines (-NH₂) | Octadecylamine, Dicyclohexylamine | Condensate lines, boilers |
| Imidazolines | 2-Mercapto-1-methyl imidazoline | CO₂/H₂S pipelines (oil & gas) |
| Amides | Oleic acid amides | Storage tanks |
| Azoles | Benzotriazole, Tolyltriazole | Copper & brass |
| Thioureas | Diphenyl thiourea | Acid pickling |
| Phosphonates | HEDP, ATMP | Cooling water |
| Quaternary ammonium salts | CTAB, Cetylpyridinium chloride | Acid media |

**Adsorption isotherm (very important for derivation in KTU):**

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm is the most commonly tested:

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

where:
- $C$ = inhibitor concentration in bulk (mol/L)
- $\theta$ = surface coverage fraction = $IE/100$
- $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant (L/mol)

A plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ yields a **straight line with slope = 1** and intercept = $1/K_{ads}$, confirming Langmuir monolayer adsorption.

The free energy of adsorption $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ (a KTU-favorite quantity):

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \, K_{ads})$$

Interpretation:
- $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} < -40$ kJ/mol → **chemisorption** (covalent bond, irreversible)
- $-40 < \Delta G_{ads}^{0} < -20$ kJ/mol → **mixed physio-chemisorption**
- $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} > -20$ kJ/mol → **physisorption** (van der Waals, reversible)

---

### 2.1.4 Vapor Phase Inhibitors (VPI / VCI)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (KTU Syllabus Specific)**
> Volatile Corrosion Inhibitors are compounds with **sufficiently high vapor pressure** at ambient temperature so that they sublime and saturate the enclosed atmosphere. The vapor molecules then adsorb on the metal surface and form a protective monomolecular layer.

**Examples:** **Dicyclohexylammonium nitrite (DICHAN), Dicyclohexylamine carbonate (DICHAC), Cyclohexylamine carbonate (CHC), Benzotriazole vapor, Vapor phase amides**.

**Mechanism:** They are bipolar — the amine end is **cationic** (attacks cathodic sites) and the nitrite/carbonate end is **anionic** (attacks anodic sites), giving them **dual (mixed) character**. This is the reason VPIs are classified as mixed inhibitors in modern KTU textbooks.

**Applications:** Protection of **stored machinery, machine tools, ball bearings, weapons, electronic components, ship engine spares** during storage and export shipping.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master Reference Table — Print This!**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variables | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $IE(\%) = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$ | Inhibitor efficiency by weight loss | % | Most common |
| 2 | $\theta = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0}$ | Surface coverage fraction | dimensionless | Required for isotherms |
| 3 | $CR = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$ | Corrosion rate (mpy) | mpy | $W$ = mg, $D$ = g/cm³, $A$ = in², $t$ = hr | Essential |
| 4 | $IE(\%) = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ | IE by corrosion rate | % | Equivalent to (1) |
| 5 | $\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | Langmuir isotherm | — | For organic inhibitors |
| 6 | $\log\left(\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}\right) = \log K_{ads} + n \log C$ | Freundlich isotherm | — | For heterogeneous surfaces |
| 7 | $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \, K_{ads})$ | Free energy of adsorption | kJ/mol | Physisorption/chemisorption check |
| 8 | $\eta_H^{inh} = \eta_H^{blank} + \Delta\eta$ | Hydrogen overpotential shift | V | Cathodic poisons |
| 9 | $I_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303(\beta_a + \beta_c)} \times \frac{\Delta E}{R_p}$ | Stern-Geary equation | A/cm² | Tafel slopes, $R_p$ |
| 10 | $P\% = \frac{R_p^{inh} - R_p^{blank}}{R_p^{inh}} \times 100$ | Protection efficiency by Rp | % | EIS & LPR data |

---

## 2.3 Selection Criteria for Industrial Inhibitors (Why Each One is Chosen)

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility Mapping**

A real-world engineer selecting an inhibitor must balance **performance, cost, toxicity, and environment**. The modern KTU exam rewards this integrated view:

1. **Chemical compatibility** — must not react violently with the process fluid
2. **Thermal stability** — boilers operate at 200–300 °C; HEDP and ATMP are stable here
3. **Toxicity profile** — chromates are being phased out under REACH; replaced by **molybdates, tungstates, phosphonates, and rare-earth (Ce, La) salts**
4. **Cost-to-protect ratio** — tannins and molybdates are far cheaper than benzotriazole
5. **Synergistic potential** — binary mixtures (e.g., Zn²⁺ + polyphosphate, BTA + MBT) often outperform single inhibitors
6. **Environmental persistence** — short-life, biodegradable inhibitors preferred in open cooling water

---

## 2.4 Synergism vs. Antagonism — The Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT] **Synergism Definition (KTU-Popular)**
> When the combined effect of two or more inhibitors is **greater than the sum of their individual effects**, the system is said to exhibit **synergism**. The quantitative **Synergistic Parameter (S₁)** is:

$$S_1 = \frac{1 - \theta_{A} - \theta_{B} + \theta_A \theta_B}{1 - \theta_{mix}}$$

where:
- $\theta_A$, $\theta_B$ = surface coverages of inhibitors A and B **individually**
- $\theta_{mix}$ = surface coverage of the **A+B mixture** at the same total concentration

**Interpretation:**
- $S_1 > 1$ → **Synergism** (one inhibitor enhances the other, e.g., KI + N-oleyl-1,3-propanediamine)
- $S_1 = 1$ → **Additive behaviour** (no interaction)
- $S_1 < 1$ → **Antagonism** (one inhibitor neutralizes the other)

**Classic example:** **Zn²⁺ + polyphosphate** — Zn²⁺ precipitates at cathodic sites, while polyphosphate precipitates at anodic sites; the two films are geometrically non-overlapping → simultaneous anodic + cathodic protection → **synergistic efficiency up to 95%**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm for Inhibitor Films

This is the most commonly requested derivation in KTU 14-mark questions on inhibitors.

> **Start with the equilibrium between vacant surface sites (S), inhibitor in solution (Inh), and occupied sites (S–Inh):**
> $$\text{S} + \text{Inh (aq)} \rightleftharpoons \text{S–Inh (adsorbed)}$$

Let:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by inhibitor
- $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of vacant surface
- $C$ = equilibrium concentration of inhibitor in bulk solution
- $K_{ads}$ = equilibrium constant for adsorption
- $k_1$ = rate constant for adsorption (forward reaction)
- $k_{-1}$ = rate constant for desorption (backward reaction)

**Step 1: Write the rate of forward adsorption (proportional to vacant sites and bulk concentration):**

$$\text{Rate}_{fwd} = k_1 \cdot (1 - \theta) \cdot C$$

**Step 2: Write the rate of backward desorption (proportional to occupied sites only, no concentration dependence for desorption):**

$$\text{Rate}_{bwd} = k_{-1} \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3: At equilibrium, the forward and backward rates are equal:**

$$k_1 (1 - \theta) C = k_{-1} \theta$$

**Step 4: Define the equilibrium constant $K_{ads}$ as the ratio of forward to backward rate constants:**

$$K_{ads} = \frac{k_1}{k_{-1}}$$

Substitute and rearrange:

$$K_{ads} = \frac{\theta}{(1 - \theta) C}$$

**Step 5: Rearrange to express $\theta$ explicitly:**

$$\theta (1) = K_{ads} (1 - \theta) C$$
$$\theta = K_{ads} C - K_{ads} C \theta$$
$$\theta + K_{ads} C \theta = K_{ads} C$$
$$\theta (1 + K_{ads} C) = K_{ads} C$$

**Step 6: Solve for $\theta$ — this is the famous Langmuir expression:**

$$\theta = \frac{K_{ads} C}{1 + K_{ads} C}$$

**Step 7: Invert both sides to obtain the linearized form most often plotted:**

$$\frac{1}{\theta} = \frac{1 + K_{ads} C}{K_{ads} C} = \frac{1}{K_{ads} C} + 1$$

**Step 8: Multiply by $C$ to get the KTU standard linearized Langmuir form:**

$$\boxed{\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C}$$

**Step 9: Compute the free energy of adsorption using the standard state of 55.5 mol/L for water:**

$$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{C_{solvent}} \cdot e^{-\Delta G_{ads}^{0} / RT} \quad \text{(using 1 mol/L standard state in solution)}$$

But because the adsorbed phase is referenced to a surface standard state, the **dimensionally consistent** form uses the molar concentration of water (55.5 mol/L) as the solvent reference:

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

If $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -45$ kJ/mol (negative, large magnitude), the inhibitor is **chemisorbed** (irreversible, robust). If $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -15$ kJ/mol, the inhibitor is **physisorbed** (reversible, weaker, temperature-sensitive).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem:** A steel specimen (area = 10 cm²) was exposed to 1 M H₂SO₄ for 6 hours. The weight loss without inhibitor was **480 mg**, and with **2 × 10⁻⁴ M benzotriazole (BTA)** it dropped to **48 mg**. Calculate:
> (a) The inhibitor efficiency IE (%)
> (b) The surface coverage $\theta$
> (c) Verify whether Langmuir isotherm is obeyed if $K_{ads} = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ L/mol
> (d) Compute $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ at 298 K (R = 8.314 J/mol·K)

**Given Data Summary:**
- $W_0 = 480$ mg
- $W_i = 48$ mg
- $C = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L
- $K_{ads} = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ L/mol
- $T = 298$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K

**Part (a): Inhibitor Efficiency**

$$IE(\%) = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100$$

$$IE(\%) = \frac{480 - 48}{480} \times 100 = \frac{432}{480} \times 100 = 90.0\,\%$$

**Part (b): Surface Coverage**

$$\theta = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} = \frac{432}{480} = 0.900$$

**Part (c): Langmuir Verification**

Use the linear form: $C/\theta = (1/K_{ads}) + C$

$$\text{LHS: } \frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.900} = 2.222 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

$$\text{RHS: } \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{4}} + 2 \times 10^{-4} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} + 2 \times 10^{-4} = 2.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

The two values (2.22 × 10⁻⁴ vs. 2.67 × 10⁻⁴) are within ~20% of each other, which is acceptable experimental deviation. **Langmuir behaviour is approximately obeyed.** For perfect obedience, you would need to plot $C/\theta$ vs. $C$ for several inhibitor concentrations and confirm a unit slope.

**Part (d): Free Energy of Adsorption**

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

Compute the argument of the logarithm:

$$55.5 \cdot K_{ads} = 55.5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{4} = 8.325 \times 10^{5}$$

Compute the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(8.325 \times 10^{5}) = \ln(8.325) + \ln(10^{5}) = 2.119 + 11.513 = 13.632$$

Compute $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$:

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -8.314 \times 298 \times 13.632 = -33{,}764 \; \text{J/mol} \approx -33.76 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $-40 < \Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -33.76 < -20$ kJ/mol, the adsorption of BTA on steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ is a **mixed physisorption–chemisorption** mechanism (predominantly chemisorption with electrostatic contribution). The lone pair on the BTA nitrogen donates electrons to the vacant 3d orbitals of Fe, forming a coordinate covalent bond.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Inhibitor for Which Application?

> [!NOTE] **Industry-Ready Selection Chart (Frequently Asked)**

| Application Environment | Recommended Inhibitor Class | Specific Examples | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acid pickling (HCl, H₂SO₄)** | Mixed organic film formers | Thioureas, amines, imidazolines, propargyl alcohol | Adsorb on metal, block both reactions |
| **Neutral cooling water** | Anodic + cathodic combo | Molybdates + Zn²⁺ + phosphonates (HEDP) | Passivation + cathodic precipitation |
| **Boilers (high T, low O₂)** | Mixed + oxygen scavenger | Hydrazine, DEHA, cyclohexylamine | Removes O₂ + film formation |
| **Oil & gas pipelines (CO₂/H₂S)** | Mixed organic film formers | Imidazolines, quaternary amines, fatty amides | Hydrophobic adsorbed film |
| **Marine / seawater** | Anodic passivators (carefully dosed) | Sodium nitrite (in closed systems), molybdates | Passive oxide formation |
| **Concrete / rebar** | Anodic inhibitors | Calcium nitrite, organic amino alcohols | Maintains passive layer on steel |
| **Storage / shipping (VPI)** | Vapor phase mixed | DICHAN, DICHAC, cyclohexylamine carbonate | Sublimes and adsorbs |
| **Atmospheric corrosion** | Mixed organic | BTA (Cu), dicyclohexylamine (steel) | Monolayer film |

---

## 3.4 Failure Modes of Inhibitors — The Dark Side

A high-quality KTU answer always mentions the **limitations**:

1. **Concentration-dependent acceleration (chromates, nitrites):** below CPC → pitting
2. **Temperature degradation:** polyphosphates hydrolyze above 60 °C → revert to orthophosphate → lose effectiveness
3. **Biological fouling:** phosphates in open cooling towers promote algal growth → reduced heat transfer
4. **Toxicity & regulation:** Cr(VI) compounds are **Category 1A carcinogens**; restricted under REACH, OSHA, and Indian Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules
5. **Specificity:** a good acid inhibitor is often a poor neutral-water inhibitor — they are **system-specific**
6. **Incompatibility:** amines can react with H₂S to form insoluble sulfide sludge
7. **Cost-volatility:** rare-earth (Ce, La) inhibitors are effective but expensive for bulk use
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Classification of Corrosion Inhibitors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Corrosion Inhibitors] --> B[Anodic Inhibitors]
    A --> C[Cathodic Inhibitors]
    A --> D[Mixed Inhibitors]
    A --> E[Vapor Phase Inhibitors]

    B --> B1[Oxidizing Anodic<br/>Chromates Dichromates<br/>Nitrites Permanganates]
    B --> B2[Non-Oxidizing Anodic<br/>Phosphates Silicates<br/>Borates Molybdates Tungstates]

    C --> C1[Cathodic Precipitates<br/>ZnSO4 Ca HCO3<br/>Polyphosphates]
    C --> C2[Cathodic Poisons<br/>As2O3 Sb2O3<br/>Bi Se Te compounds]
    C --> C3[Oxygen Scavengers<br/>Na2SO3 Hydrazine<br/>DEHA Tannins]

    D --> D1[Organic Amines<br/>Octadecylamine<br/>Dicyclohexylamine]
    D --> D2[Heterocyclics<br/>Imidazolines Azoles<br/>Thioureas Pyridines]
    D --> D3[Surfactants<br/>Quaternary Ammonium Salts<br/>CTAB CPC]

    E --> E1[DICHAN<br/>Dicyclohexylammonium Nitrite]
    E --> E2[DICHAC<br/>Dicyclohexylamine Carbonate]
    E --> E3[Benzotriazole Vapor<br/>Cyclohexylamine Carbonate]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mechanism of Action on Metal Surface

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Metal Surface Fe] --> SA[Anodic Site Fe2+ dissolution]
    M --> SC[Cathodic Site O2 reduction or H+ discharge]

    SA --> PA[Anodic Inhibitor Arrives<br/>e.g. Chromate Molybdate]
    SC --> PC[Cathodic Inhibitor Arrives<br/>e.g. Zn2+ Polyphosphate]
    SA --> PM[Mixed Inhibitor Arrives<br/>e.g. Amine Imidazoline]

    PA --> FA[Forms Passive Oxide Film<br/>Fe2O3 plus Cr2O3]
    PC --> FB[Forms Precipitate Blanket<br/>Zn OH 2 on cathode]
    PM --> FC[Forms Adsorbed Hydrophobic Film<br/>Polar head on metal<br/>Nonpolar tail outward]

    FA --> R[Reduced Corrosion Current]
    FB --> R
    FC --> R
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Polarization Behaviour of Each Class

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Evans Diagram Polarization Curves] --> P1[Blank Solution<br/>Ecorr and Icorr reference]
    P --> P2[Anodic Inhibitor<br/>Anodic curve shifts LEFT<br/>Ecorr shifts POSITIVE]
    P --> P3[Cathodic Inhibitor<br/>Cathodic curve shifts LEFT<br/>Ecorr shifts NEGATIVE]
    P --> P4[Mixed Inhibitor<br/>BOTH curves shift LEFT<br/>Ecorr nearly unchanged<br/>Icorr drops sharply]

    P2 --> X1[Passivation of anode<br/>Critical current density lowered]
    P3 --> X2[Oxygen reduction blocked<br/>or H2 overpotential raised]
    P4 --> X3[Adsorption film blocks<br/>both electrode reactions]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Synergistic Zn²⁺ + Polyphosphate System

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Synergistic Cathodic Inhibition] --> Z[Zn2+ ions in solution]
    S --> P[Polyphosphate Hexametaphosphate]

    Z --> ZA[Zn2+ migrates to cathode]
    ZA --> ZB[Reacts with cathodic OH minus]
    ZB --> ZC[Precipitates Zn OH 2<br/>blankets cathode]

    P --> PA[Polyphosphate hydrolyzes slowly]
    PA --> PB[Forms thin film of Zn3 PO4 2<br/>at anodic sites]
    PB --> PC[Self healing on defect]

    ZC --> OUT[Synergistic Efficiency 90 to 95 percent<br/>Much greater than individual effects]
    PC --> OUT
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Selection Flowchart for Industrial Application

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Step 1 Identify Environment] --> Q2{pH of medium}
    Q2 -->|Acidic pH less than 4| A1[Use organic mixed inhibitors<br/>Thiourea Imidazoline Amines]
    Q2 -->|Neutral pH 6 to 9| A2[Use anodic plus cathodic combo<br/>Molybdate plus Zn2 plus HEDP]
    Q2 -->|Alkaline pH greater than 9| A3[Use silicate or phosphate<br/>plus tannin]

    A1 --> Q3{Temperature}
    A2 --> Q3
    A3 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Less than 60 C| T1[Standard organic inhibitor OK]
    Q3 -->|60 to 200 C| T2[Use thermally stable<br/>Phosphonates HEDP ATMP]
    Q3 -->|Greater than 200 C| T3[Use hydrazine DEHA<br/>plus filming amines]

    T1 --> Q4{Toxicity Concern}
    T2 --> Q4
    T3 --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes environment sensitive| E1[Use green inhibitors<br/>Tannins rare earth Ce La]
    Q4 -->|No industrial closed loop| E2[Cost optimized<br/>Chromate Nitrite Polyphosphate OK]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define corrosion inhibitor. Differentiate between anodic and cathodic inhibitors with one example each. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Corrosion Inhibitor:** A corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance which, when added in small concentrations (0.1–1%) to a corrosive environment, reduces the corrosion rate of a metal by forming a protective film (adsorbed, precipitated, or passive oxide) on the metal surface.

| Feature | Anodic Inhibitor | Cathodic Inhibitor |
|---|---|---|
| **Site blocked** | Anodic (metal dissolution) reaction | Cathodic (O₂ reduction / H⁺ discharge) reaction |
| **Mechanism** | Forms passive oxide OR insoluble anodic precipitate | Forms insoluble cathodic precipitate OR poisons H⁺ discharge OR scavenges O₂ |
| **Evans diagram shift** | Anodic polarization curve shifts left, $E_{corr}$ becomes more positive | Cathodic polarization curve shifts left, $E_{corr}$ becomes more negative |
| **Example** | Sodium chromate (Na₂CrO₄) for Fe in cooling water | Zinc sulphate (ZnSO₄) for Fe in cooling water |
| **Risk** | Dangerous below CPC (pitting) | Relatively safe at low concentration |

---

### **Question 2:** What are "dangerous inhibitors"? Why are chromates classified as such? **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Q1b]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Dangerous inhibitors** are inhibitors that **accelerate corrosion if added below a critical passivation concentration (CPC)**. They promote localized pitting instead of uniform passivation.
>
> **Chromates (Na₂CrO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇)** are dangerous because:
> 1. Below CPC (~500 ppm in seawater), they form **incomplete passive patches** on the metal, creating an unfavorable area ratio of small cathode (passive) to large anode (active) → intense pitting.
> 2. Above CPC, they form a **continuous, self-healing mixed Fe-Cr spinel oxide** → complete passivation.
> 3. They are also **highly toxic and carcinogenic** (Cr⁶⁺), restricted under REACH, BIS, and EPA norms.
>
> Hence chromates must be used with continuous **dosage monitoring and controlled dosing pumps** to maintain concentration above CPC.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive 14-Mark Question**

#### **Q. (a)** **[7 Marks]** Classify corrosion inhibitors based on their electrode behaviour. Describe the mechanism of anodic oxidizing inhibitors with a suitable example. Discuss why chromates are termed "dangerous inhibitors."

#### **Q. (b)** **[7 Marks]** Define inhibitor efficiency. A steel specimen (area 25 cm²) immersed in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ for 4 hours gave a weight loss of 250 mg without inhibitor and 25 mg with the addition of 1 × 10⁻³ M imidazoline. Calculate (i) the inhibitor efficiency, (ii) the surface coverage, and (iii) the corrosion rates in mpy (density of steel = 7.86 g/cm³). **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Classification of inhibitors by electrode behaviour:**

1. **Anodic inhibitors** — block the metal dissolution reaction
   - Oxidizing: chromates, dichromates, nitrites, permanganates
   - Non-oxidizing: phosphates, silicates, borates, tungstates, molybdates

2. **Cathodic inhibitors** — block the cathodic reaction
   - Cathodic precipitates: Zn²⁺, Ca²⁺, polyphosphates
   - Cathodic poisons: As₂O₃, Sb₂O₃
   - Oxygen scavengers: Na₂SO₃, hydrazine, DEHA

3. **Mixed (dual) inhibitors** — block both reactions
   - Organic amines, imidazolines, thioureas, azoles, phosphonates

**Mechanism of anodic oxidizing inhibitors (chromate example):**

Chromate ions (CrO₄²⁻) are strong oxidizers. In aerated neutral water containing dissolved O₂, they oxidize the iron surface directly to form a **mixed oxide film** of Fe₂O₃ and Cr₂O₃:

$$2\text{Fe} + 2\text{Na}_2\text{CrO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3 + 4\text{NaOH}$$

The Cr₂O₃ **dopes** the Fe₂O₃ layer, converting it into a compact **spinel-type (Fe,Cr)₃O₄** passive film only a few nanometers thick. This film:
- Has very low ionic and electronic conductivity
- Repels Cl⁻ ions (inhibits pitting)
- Is **self-healing** at scratches because Cr⁶⁺ migrates to the defect and re-passivates it

**Why chromates are "dangerous":**

If the chromate concentration falls below the **critical passivation concentration (CPC)** — about 500 ppm in seawater — the protective film does not form continuously. Instead, the inhibitor creates **discrete passive islands** surrounded by large active (unprotected) areas. This creates a highly unfavorable **cathode-to-anode area ratio** (small cathode on the passive islands, large anode on the active regions). The anodic current density becomes very high at the small unprotected sites, leading to **severe pitting corrosion** that can perforate a steel plate in days. Therefore, chromates must be dosed with continuous monitoring systems. **[7 Marks — Full]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Area $A = 25$ cm²
- Time $t = 4$ hr
- Density of steel $D = 7.86$ g/cm³
- $W_0 = 250$ mg (blank)
- $W_i = 25$ mg (inhibited)
- Inhibitor: 1 × 10⁻³ M imidazoline

**Step 1: Inhibitor Efficiency**

$$IE(\%) = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100 = \frac{250 - 25}{250} \times 100 = \frac{225}{250} \times 100 = 90.0\,\%$$

**Step 2: Surface Coverage**

$$\theta = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} = 0.900 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 3: Corrosion Rate (blank) in mpy**

The standard formula for corrosion rate in **mpy** (mils per year):

$$CR(\text{mpy}) = \frac{534 \times W(\text{mg})}{D(\text{g/cm}^3) \times A(\text{in}^2) \times t(\text{hr})}$$

**Unit conversion:** $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 \times (1 \text{ in} / 2.54 \text{ cm})^2 = 25/6.4516 = 3.875 \text{ in}^2$

**Blank corrosion rate:**

$$CR_0 = \frac{534 \times 250}{7.86 \times 3.875 \times 4} = \frac{133{,}500}{121.83} = 1095.8 \;\text{mpy}$$

**Inhibited corrosion rate:**

$$CR_i = \frac{534 \times 25}{7.86 \times 3.875 \times 4} = \frac{13{,}350}{121.83} = 109.6 \;\text{mpy}$$

**Verification using IE formula:**

$$IE = \frac{1095.8 - 109.6}{1095.8} \times 100 = 90.0\,\% \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **IE = 90.0 %**
- (ii) **θ = 0.900**
- (iii) **CR₀ = 1095.8 mpy, CRᵢ = 109.6 mpy** **[7 Marks — Full]**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): Alternative 14-Mark Question**

#### **Q. (a)** **[7 Marks]** What are vapor phase inhibitors (VPI)? Describe the mechanism, two examples, and industrial applications of VPI. Explain why DICHAN is considered a mixed inhibitor.

#### **Q. (b)** **[7 Marks]** State and derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm as applied to organic corrosion inhibitors. How is the free energy of adsorption $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ calculated, and what does its sign and magnitude indicate? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition:** Vapor phase inhibitors (VPI), also called **volatile corrosion inhibitors (VCI)**, are organic compounds that sublime at ambient temperature and saturate the enclosed atmosphere around a metal. The vapor molecules adsorb on the metal surface and form a protective monomolecular film, suppressing corrosion during **storage and transit**.

**Mechanism:** VPI molecules are typically salts of a **weakly basic amine (cationic part)** and a **weakly acidic anion (anionic part)**, e.g., dicyclohexylammonium nitrite (DICHAN) dissociates as:

$$(\text{C}_6\text{H}_{11})_2\text{NH}_2^+ + \text{NO}_2^-$$

The **cationic amine** portion preferentially adsorbs at **cathodic sites** (the protonated nitrogen carries a positive charge attracted to negatively charged cathodic regions), and the **nitrite anion** migrates to **anodic sites** where it passivates the metal:

$$2\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{NO}_2^- \rightarrow 2\text{FeO} + \text{N}_2 \uparrow$$

This dual behaviour makes DICHAN a **mixed inhibitor** — it blocks both anodic and cathodic reactions simultaneously.

**Two examples:**
1. **DICHAN** — Dicyclohexylammonium nitrite
2. **DICHAC** — Dicyclohexylamine carbonate (less toxic alternative)

**Industrial Applications:**
- Protection of machine tools, bearings, and spares during storage
- Export packaging of weapons, electronics, and precision instruments
- Protection of pipelines and boilers during shutdown periods
- Long-term preservation of automotive and aircraft parts

**Why DICHAN is a mixed inhibitor:** Because it dissociates into a cation that adsorbs at cathodes AND an anion that passivates anodes, blocking both half-cell reactions. **[7 Marks — Full]**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Statement of Langmuir Isotherm:**

> The Langmuir adsorption isotherm describes the equilibrium between a solute (inhibitor) in solution and the adsorbed layer on a homogeneous metal surface, assuming (i) monolayer coverage, (ii) identical adsorption sites, and (iii) no interaction between adsorbed molecules.

**Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete 9-step derivation.)*

**Final linearized form:**

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

A plot of $C/\theta$ vs. $C$ gives a straight line with **unit slope** and intercept $= 1/K_{ads}$.

**Calculation of $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$:**

$$\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

The factor **55.5 mol/L** is the molar concentration of water, the solvent reference state.

**Sign and magnitude interpretation:**

| $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ value | Mechanism | Type of bonding |
|---|---|---|
| $< -40$ kJ/mol | Chemisorption | Coordinate covalent bond (irreversible) |
| $-40$ to $-20$ kJ/mol | Mixed physisorption + chemisorption | Electrostatic + covalent |
| $> -20$ kJ/mol | Physisorption | Van der Waals (reversible) |

A **negative value** indicates the adsorption is **spontaneous** (favorable, increases entropy/lowers free energy). A **large negative value** (< -40 kJ/mol) implies strong, irreversible chemisorption — typical of N- or S-containing heterocycles (imidazoline, thiourea, BTA) on transition metals via lone-pair donation to the metal's empty d-orbitals. **[7 Marks — Full]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "55.5 factor"** in $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ calculation → lose 1 mark
> 2. **Unit confusion in mpy formula** (mg, g/cm³, in², hr — must be exact) → lose 2 marks
> 3. **Calling DICHAN "anodic"** — examiners look for the **mixed** classification
> 4. **Ignoring the "dangerous inhibitor" angle** of chromates/nitrites → lose 1 mark
> 5. **Not stating the CPC (Critical Passivation Concentration) concept** for anodic oxidizing inhibitors → lose 1 mark
> 6. **Failing to mention synergistic parameter $S_1$** when describing combinations → lose 1 mark
> 7. **Omitting self-healing mechanism** for chromate/BTA films → KTU specifically tests this

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Definition:** Inhibitor = small conc. (0.1–1%) chemical that reduces corrosion rate by forming a protective film
- [x] **Inhibitor Efficiency formula:** $IE = \frac{W_0 - W_i}{W_0} \times 100 = \frac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$
- [x] **Anodic inhibitors** shift $E_{corr}$ in the **positive** direction; **cathodic** shift $E_{corr}$ **negative**; **mixed** shift $E_{corr}$ **minimally** but $I_{corr}$ **sharply**
- [x] **Oxidizing anodic inhibitors** (chromates, nitrites) are **dangerous below CPC** — cause pitting
- [x] **Non-oxidizing anodic inhibitors** (phosphates, silicates) require **dissolved O₂** to work
- [x] **Cathodic precipitates** (Zn²⁺, polyphosphates) blanket the cathode as hydroxides
- [x] **Cathodic poisons** (As₂O₃, Sb₂O₃) raise hydrogen overpotential — used in acid pickling
- [x] **Oxygen scavengers** (Na₂SO₃, hydrazine, DEHA) remove dissolved O₂
- [x] **Organic mixed inhibitors** are **polar-head + nonpolar-tail** molecules that form hydrophobic films
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm** linear form: $C/\theta = 1/K_{ads} + C$; **unit slope** confirms validity
- [x] **Free energy of adsorption:** $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \, K_{ads})$
- [x] **Chemisorption:** $\Delta G < -40$ kJ/mol; **physisorption:** $\Delta G > -20$ kJ/mol
- [x] **Vapor phase inhibitors** (DICHAN, DICHAC) are mixed inhibitors with cationic + anionic split
- [x] **Synergism parameter** $S_1 > 1$ means inhibitors enhance each other; classic pair = Zn²⁺ + polyphosphate
- [x] **Corrosion rate (mpy):** $CR = \frac{534 \times W}{D \times A \times t}$ with $W$ in mg, $D$ in g/cm³, $A$ in in², $t$ in hr
- [x] **Green inhibitors:** tannins, plant extracts, rare-earth (Ce, La) salts — modern replacement for chromates
- [x] **Always mention** environmental & toxicity regulations (REACH, OSHA) when discussing chromates
- [x] **Self-healing films:** chromate-Fe₂O₃, BTA-Cu, imidazoline-FeCO₃ — all are KTU exam favorites
- [x] **For numerical problems:** show unit conversions, final answer in **2 decimal places** for IE %, **3 sig figs** for CR

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inorganic Anodic Inhibitors** are water-soluble inorganic chemical compounds that function by selectively suppressing the **anodic (oxidation) reaction** of a corroding metal. They achieve this by forming a thin, adherent, and often invisible **passive oxide/hydroxide film** directly over the **anodic sites** on the metal–electrolyte interface. This passive layer dramatically reduces the anodic current density and forces the open-circuit corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) to drift in the **noble (positive) direction**, thereby polarizing the metal deep into its passivity region.

They are also called **"Passivators"** or **"Safe Inhibitors"** (only when concentration is above the critical threshold), because they mechanistically push the metal into the passive state identified on the **Pourbaix** and **Evans** diagrams.

## 1.2 Common Examples in the KTU Syllabus

The most extensively studied inorganic anodic inhibitors include:

- **Chromates & Dichromates:** $Na_2CrO_4$, $K_2Cr_2O_7$ (historically the most effective, now restricted due to toxicity).
- **Nitrites:** $NaNO_2$ (widely used in reinforced concrete and antifreeze formulations).
- **Molybdates:** $Na_2MoO_4$ (environmentally benign "green" replacement for chromates).
- **Tungstates / Vanadates:** $Na_2WO_4$, $NaVO_3$.
- **Phosphates / Polyphosphates:** $Na_3PO_4$, $Na_5P_3O_{10}$ (used in cooling water treatment).
- **Borates, Silicates, Carbonates, Bicarbonates.**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Armored Lock" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a metal pipeline being eaten away by an aggressive acid river flowing through it. The acid attacks the metal at random spots — these spots are the **anodes**. Now, sprinkle some *inorganic anodic inhibitor* (say, sodium nitrite) into the river. The inhibitor molecules rush toward every vulnerable anodic spot and lay down a **microscopic invisible armor coat** (a passive oxide film like $\gamma-Fe_2O_3$ or $Fe_3O_4$). Once the armor is in place, the acid can no longer "bite" the metal. The pipeline becomes immune — *passive* — to further attack. The coating is **site-specific**: it preferentially seals the anodes, not the cathodes.

**Key intuitive takeaways:**
- They **coat the anode** → hence "an-odic" inhibitors.
- They work best in **near-neutral / alkaline** solutions where passive films are stable.
- **Concentration matters:** below a critical inhibitor concentration, the film is porous and incomplete, leading to **localized pitting corrosion** — a very dangerous failure mode.

## 1.4 Operational Risk Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Dangerous Inhibitor" Trap:** Inorganic anodic inhibitors are sometimes called *dangerous inhibitors* if the dosing falls below the **critical passivation concentration**. Under-dosing leaves a partial film → causes severe pitting. This is a **favourite KTU examiner's question** and a major industrial failure mode.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Action — Step-by-Step Logic

The protective action of inorganic anodic inhibitors proceeds through the following structured sequence:

1. **Migration of Inhibitor Anion to the Metal Surface** — The negatively charged inhibitor anion (e.g., $CrO_4^{2-}$, $NO_2^{-}$, $MoO_4^{2-}$) is electrostatically attracted to the local anodic sites where metal ions ($M^{n+}$) are being liberated by oxidation.
2. **Oxidation of the Inhibitor Anion at the Anode** — The anion itself is a strong oxidizer. It is **reduced** at the anode (gets consumed) while oxidizing the metal surface to a higher valence state oxide.

$$NO_2^{-} + 8H^{+} + 6e^{-} \rightarrow NH_4^{+} + 2H_2O$$

3. **Formation of a Passive Oxide/Hydroxide Layer** — The metal surface gets converted to a stable, electronically insulating oxide.

$$2Fe + 3Na_2CrO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe_2O_3 \text{ (passive film)} + 3Cr_2O_3 + 6NaOH$$

4. **Reduction of Anodic Current Density** — Because the passive film has a very high resistance, the anodic dissolution current ($i_a$) drops by 3–6 orders of magnitude.
5. **Shift of $E_{corr}$ to a Nobler Value** — The mixed corrosion potential moves to the right (positive) on the Evans diagram, deep into the passivation plateau.
6. **Self-Healing Behaviour** — If the film is mechanically damaged, the inhibitor in solution re-oxidizes the freshly exposed metal and re-forms the film.

## 2.2 Pourbaix Diagram Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a Pourbaix (potential–pH) diagram for iron, the **passive zone** is bounded by two horizontal lines:
> - The upper boundary marks the **oxygen evolution / transpassive breakdown** potential.
> - The lower boundary marks the **Flade potential** ($E_F$) — the potential below which the passive film is no longer stable.

An effective anodic inhibitor must **shift the Flade potential upward (more noble)** and **expand the passive region horizontally** (widen the stable pH range). Chromates and nitrites achieve this by adsorbing specifically onto the oxide surface and stabilizing the higher oxidation state ($Fe^{3+}$ rather than $Fe^{2+}$).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Tafel Equation (Anodic) | $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i_a}{i_0}\right)$ | Polarization overpotential at the anode | V (volts) |
| 2 | Tafel Equation (Cathodic) | $\eta_c = \beta_c \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i_c}{i_0}\right)$ | Polarization overpotential at the cathode | V (volts) |
| 3 | Anodic Inhibition Efficiency | $IE\% = \dfrac{i_{corr}^{0} - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^{0}} \times 100$ | Percentage drop in corrosion current | % |
| 4 | Critical Passivation Current Density | $i_{crit}$ (read from Evans diagram) | Peak current required to initiate passivation | $\mu A / cm^2$ |
| 5 | Flade Potential (Passivation Threshold) | $E_F = E_{F}^{\circ} - 0.059 \, pH$ (for Fe, approx) | pH-dependent passivation onset potential | V (vs SHE) |
| 6 | Corrosion Rate via Faraday's Law | $CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 3270}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Mass-loss corrosion rate (3270 = Faraday constant conversion) | mpy (mils per year) |
| 7 | Nernst Equation (for $CrO_4^{2-}/Cr^{3+}$) | $E = E^{\circ} - \dfrac{0.059}{3}\log\!\dfrac{[Cr^{3+}]^2}{[CrO_4^{2-}][H^+]^{8}}$ | Reversible potential of the chromate reduction couple | V (vs SHE) |
| 8 | Adsorption Isotherm (Frumkin) | $\log\!\left(\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta}\right) = \log K + n \log C$ | Surface coverage $\theta$ as a function of bulk concentration $C$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - Anodic inhibitors are **effective only above a critical concentration** $C_{crit}$ (often 50–500 ppm depending on $Cl^{-}$ and pH).
> - At $C < C_{crit}$ → pitting and underfilm corrosion occur.
> - The inhibition efficiency $IE\%$ typically ranges from **70% to 99%** for well-dosed systems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inorganic anodic inhibitors are the workhorse of:
- **Cooling water circuits** in thermal power plants and refineries (phosphates + molybdates).
- **Antifreeze formulations** for automotive engines (nitrites + borates + silicates).
- **Reinforced concrete structures** (calcium nitrite admixed into the concrete mix to protect rebar).
- **Temporary protective coatings** ("wash primers") in the aerospace industry.
- **Oil-well acidizing** — modern nitrite-based "intensifiers" protect tubular steel from HCl attack.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Anodic Tafel Slope from the Butler–Volmer Equation

The Butler–Volmer equation describes the net current density at an electrode as a function of the overpotential $\eta$:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Recognise the two exponential terms.**
The first term represents the **anodic** (oxidation) contribution; the second term represents the **cathodic** (reduction) contribution.

**Step 2 — Apply the high-anodic-polarization limit.**
When the anodic overpotential $\eta$ is large and positive (i.e., the metal is heavily polarized in the anodic direction), the cathodic term becomes negligible:

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln i = \ln i_0 + \dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (since Tafel plots are semi-log).**

$$\eta = \underbrace{\left(\dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a n F}\right)}_{\beta_a} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Identify the anodic Tafel slope.**

$$\boxed{\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a n F}}$$

At **298 K** (25 °C), with $\alpha_a = 0.5$ and $n = 2$:

$$\beta_a = \dfrac{0.0592}{0.5 \times 2} \approx 0.0592 \text{ V/decade}$$

This is the **theoretical anodic Tafel slope** commonly observed for iron in acidic media — a value examiners expect students to recognize.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Flade Potential's pH Dependence

The Flade potential $E_F$ marks the boundary between the active and passive states. For iron, the film formation reaction can be written as:

$$2Fe_3O_4 + H_2O \rightarrow 3\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3 + 2H^{+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Step 1 — Write the Nernst equation for this half-cell.**

$$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - \dfrac{RT}{2F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{[H^{+}]^2}{1}\right) = E_F^{\circ} + \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{F} \, pH$$

**Step 2 — At 25 °C, substitute numerical constants.**

$$E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.0592 \, pH \quad \text{(relative to SHE)}$$

**Step 3 — Use the experimentally measured standard value for iron.**

With $E_F^{\circ} \approx +0.580$ V (vs SHE) at pH = 0:

$$\boxed{E_F \, (\text{V vs SHE}) = 0.580 - 0.0592 \cdot pH}$$

This equation explains why **raising the pH** (making the solution more alkaline) automatically stabilizes the passive film — the Flade potential drops below the operating potential, so the metal remains passive.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Inhibition Efficiency Calculation

**Problem:** A carbon steel specimen in aerated 0.5 M NaCl has $i_{corr} = 1.20 \times 10^{-4}$ A/cm². After adding 300 ppm of $NaNO_2$, the corrosion current density drops to $9.5 \times 10^{-6}$ A/cm². Calculate the inhibitor efficiency and the corrosion rate (in mpy) after inhibition, given $M_{Fe} = 55.85$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $\rho = 7.87$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Compute the inhibition efficiency.**

$$IE\% = \dfrac{(1.20 \times 10^{-4}) - (9.5 \times 10^{-6})}{1.20 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100$$

$$IE\% = \dfrac{1.105 \times 10^{-4}}{1.20 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100 \approx 92.08\%$$

**Step 2 — Compute the corrosion rate using Faraday's Law.**

$$CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 3270}{n \cdot \rho}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{(9.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (55.85) \times (3270)}{2 \times 7.87}$$

$$CR = \dfrac{1.7365}{15.74} \approx 0.1103 \text{ mpy}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

The original uninhibited corrosion rate was:

$$CR_0 = \dfrac{(1.20 \times 10^{-4}) \times (55.85) \times (3270)}{2 \times 7.87} \approx 1.394 \text{ mpy}$$

The inhibitor has reduced the corrosion rate by **roughly 13×**, confirming that the 300 ppm nitrite dose is **above the critical passivation threshold** and the film is protective.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Anodic Inhibitors at a Glance

| Inhibitor | Formula | Anion Mechanism | Typical Dose (ppm) | Critical pH Range | Environmental Status |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Sodium Chromate | $Na_2CrO_4$ | $CrO_4^{2-} \to Cr_2O_3$ film | 50–500 | 6.0 – 9.5 | **Toxic, restricted (REACH)** |
| Sodium Nitrite | $NaNO_2$ | $NO_2^{-} \to NO \to NH_3$; forms $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ | 300–1000 | 7.0 – 10.0 | Carcinogen; regulated |
| Sodium Molybdate | $Na_2MoO_4$ | $MoO_4^{2-}$ adsorbs on Fe-oxide | 200–500 | 7.5 – 9.5 | **Green alternative** |
| Sodium Tungstate | $Na_2WO_4$ | $WO_4^{2-}$ stabilizes $Fe_2O_3$ | 100–500 | 7.0 – 9.0 | Green, expensive |
| Sodium Vanadate | $NaVO_3$ | $VO_3^{-}$ forms polymeric film | 100–1000 | 6.0 – 9.0 | Moderate toxicity |
| Trisodium Phosphate | $Na_3PO_4$ | $PO_4^{3-} \to FePO_4$ film | 50–200 | 7.0 – 9.0 | Food-grade safe |
| Sodium Borate | $Na_2B_4O_7$ | $B_4O_7^{2-}$ forms borate glass film | 500–2000 | 8.0 – 10.0 | Low toxicity |
| Sodium Silicate | $Na_2SiO_3$ | $SiO_3^{2-}$ forms silica gel film | 100–1000 | 8.0 – 11.0 | Non-toxic |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evans Diagram — Effect of an Anodic Inhibitor

The Evans diagram is the central visual tool for explaining how anodic inhibitors shift the polarization behaviour. The block-level flow below maps the electrode kinetics before and after inhibitor addition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Metal-Electrolyte Interface] --> B[Without Inhibitor]
    A --> C[With Anodic Inhibitor Above Ccrit]

    B --> B1["Anodic line: M to M_n+ + ne-"]
    B1 --> B2["Cathodic line: O2 + 2H2O + 4e- to 4OH-"]
    B2 --> B3["Intersection: Ecorr at high i_corr (active corrosion)"]

    C --> C1["Anodic line shifted to lower i_a (passive plateau)"]
    C1 --> C2["Cathodic line unchanged"]
    C2 --> C3["New intersection: Ecorr more noble, i_corr drastically reduced"]
    C3 --> C4["Metal is now in passive state"]
    C4 --> C5["Passivation film: gamma-Fe2O3 or Fe3O4"]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style C3 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Pourbaix Diagram — Passivation Region Expansion by Anodic Inhibitors

The Pourbaix (E vs pH) diagram visualizes thermodynamic zones of immunity, corrosion, and passivity. An effective anodic inhibitor **expands the passive zone** to the left (towards lower pH).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pourbaix Diagram for Iron in Pure Water] --> P2[Without Inhibitor]
    P1 --> P3[With Chromate or Nitrite Inhibitor]

    P2 --> Q1["Corrosion zone: Fe2+ dominates"]
    P2 --> Q2["Passive zone: gamma-Fe2O3 / Fe3O4 narrow band, pH 9 to 12"]
    P2 --> Q3["Immunity zone: Fe metal stable, low E region"]

    P3 --> R1["Passive zone EXPANDS to pH 5 to 13"]
    P3 --> R2["Inhibitor anion stabilizes Fe3+ oxide lattice"]
    P3 --> R3["Flade potential line shifts downward by 100 to 200 mV"]
    P3 --> R4["Operating point at neutral pH now lies inside passive zone"]

    style P2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style R4 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define inorganic anodic inhibitors. Why are they called "passivators"?**

**Model Answer:**
Inorganic anodic inhibitors are inorganic compounds (e.g., chromates, nitrites, molybdates, phosphates) that suppress the **anodic dissolution reaction** of a corroding metal by forming a thin, adherent, and electronically insulating **passive oxide film** (e.g., $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ or $Fe_3O_4$) over the anodic sites.
They are called **passivators** because they shift the corrosion potential ($E_{corr}$) to a more noble (positive) value, deep into the **passive region** of the Pourbaix diagram, thereby converting an actively corroding surface into a thermodynamically passive one.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why are inorganic anodic inhibitors sometimes called "dangerous inhibitors"?**

**Model Answer:**
They are called **dangerous inhibitors** because if the inhibitor concentration falls **below a critical threshold** ($C_{crit}$), the passive film formed is **incomplete, porous, and discontinuous**. This leads to **localized pitting and crevice corrosion**, which is far more aggressive and unpredictable than the uniform corrosion the inhibitor was meant to prevent. Hence, overdosing is safer than underdosing — a unique feature of anodic inhibitors.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Discuss the mechanism of action of **inorganic anodic inhibitors** with suitable examples. Explain the role of chromates and nitrites with relevant chemical equations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The mechanism of inorganic anodic inhibitors proceeds as follows:

1. **Electrostatic Migration:** The negatively charged inhibitor anion (e.g., $CrO_4^{2-}$, $NO_2^{-}$) migrates to the anodic sites on the metal surface where the local potential is most positive.
2. **Oxidation of the Metal Surface:** The anion acts as an oxidizing agent, taking up electrons released by the dissolving metal and raising the surface metal ions to a higher oxidation state.
3. **Formation of Passive Film:** The higher-oxide layer (e.g., $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$) is highly insoluble, electronically insulating, and self-healing.

**For Chromates (e.g., $Na_2CrO_4$):**
The chromate ion is reduced to chromic oxide, which becomes incorporated into the passive film:

$$2Fe + 2Na_2CrO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow Fe_2O_3 + Cr_2O_3 + 4NaOH$$

The film consists of a mixed oxide of $Fe_2O_3$ and $Cr_2O_3$, with a typical thickness of 3–10 nm. Chromium substitution in the lattice makes the film extremely corrosion-resistant.

**For Nitrites (e.g., $NaNO_2$):**
Nitrite is reduced to ammonia and water while oxidizing iron to ferric oxide:

$$6NaNO_2 + 8Fe + 3H_2O \rightarrow 4Fe_2O_3 + 6NaOH + 3NH_3$$

The protective film is essentially $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ (maghemite), which is insulating and adherent. Nitrites are particularly effective in **near-neutral and slightly alkaline** solutions, such as reinforced concrete and engine coolants.

**(b)** With the help of an **Evans diagram**, explain how an inorganic anodic inhibitor alters the corrosion kinetics of a metal. Mention any **two limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In an Evans diagram, the **anodic polarization curve** (metal dissolution) and the **cathodic polarization curve** (oxygen reduction or hydrogen evolution) intersect at the corrosion potential $E_{corr}$ with a corrosion current $i_{corr}$.

When an anodic inhibitor is added at a concentration **above $C_{crit}$**:

- The anodic Tafel line **bends sharply to the left** at the **Flade potential ($E_F$)**, creating a **passive plateau** where the current density is $i_{pass}$ (3–6 orders of magnitude lower than $i_{corr}$).
- The cathodic line remains essentially unchanged.
- The new operating point shifts to a **nobler $E_{corr}$** and a **much lower $i_{corr}$** — the metal is now in the passive state.

**Mathematically**, the inhibition efficiency is:

$$IE\% = \dfrac{i_{corr}^{0} - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^{0}} \times 100 \approx 90\text{ to }99\%$$

**Two Limitations:**

1. **Pitting risk at low concentration:** If the inhibitor dose falls below $C_{crit}$, only a partial film forms, leading to **severe pitting corrosion** (e.g., 100 ppm nitrite in 1000 ppm Cl⁻ solution causes pitting of carbon steel).
2. **pH and chloride sensitivity:** Anodic inhibitors are **ineffective in strongly acidic media** (the passive film dissolves) and are **poisoned by chloride ions** (Cl⁻ penetrates the passive film, causing localized breakdown).
3. **Environmental toxicity:** Chromates are carcinogenic and strictly regulated under REACH and OSHA; nitrites can form carcinogenic *N-nitrosamines* in service.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Compare the following anodic inhibitors — **chromates, nitrites, and molybdates** — with respect to their mechanism, passivation efficiency, and environmental impact. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Chromates ($CrO_4^{2-}$) | Nitrites ($NO_2^{-}$) | Molybdates ($MoO_4^{2-}$) |
|----------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| Mechanism | Oxidizes Fe to $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ and incorporates $Cr_2O_3$ into the film | Oxidizes Fe to $Fe_2O_3$ via $NO \to NH_3$ reduction pathway | Adsorbs onto $Fe_2O_3$ lattice, stabilizes $Fe^{3+}$ sites |
| Film Composition | Mixed $Fe_2O_3 / Cr_2O_3$ solid solution | $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ (maghemite) | $Fe_2O_3$ with adsorbed $MoO_4^{2-}$ anions |
| IE% (typical) | 95 – 99 % | 80 – 95 % | 75 – 90 % |
| Toxicity | **High** — carcinogenic, regulated | Moderate — can form *N-nitrosamines* | **Low** — considered "green" |
| Cost | Low | Low | High |
| Critical pH | 6.0 – 9.5 | 7.0 – 10.0 | 7.5 – 9.5 |

**Summary:** Chromates are the most effective but are being phased out globally. Molybdates are the leading "green" replacement. Nitrites remain popular in concrete and antifreeze.

**(b)** Derive the **Tafel equation for the anodic reaction** from the Butler–Volmer expression, and calculate the anodic Tafel slope for iron at 25 °C, taking $\alpha_a = 0.5$ and $n = 2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from Butler–Volmer:

$$i = i_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\alpha_c n F \eta}{RT}\right) \right]$$

For large anodic polarization, the cathodic term is negligible:

$$i \approx i_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}\right)$$

Take natural log:

$$\ln i = \ln i_0 + \dfrac{\alpha_a n F \eta}{RT}$$

Convert to log base 10 and solve for $\eta$:

$$\eta = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a n F} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{i}{i_0}\right)$$

The anodic Tafel slope is therefore:

$$\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \, RT}{\alpha_a n F}$$

At T = 298 K, R = 8.314 J/mol·K, F = 96485 C/mol:

$$\beta_a = \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298}{0.5 \times 2 \times 96485} = \dfrac{5706.0}{96485} \approx 0.0591 \text{ V/decade}$$

$$\boxed{\beta_a \approx 60 \text{ mV/decade}}$$

This is the standard anodic Tafel slope reported for iron in acidic de-aerated solutions — a value students should remember for the KTU exam.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "anodic" with "cathodic" inhibitors** — A 14-mark answer that lists nitrite as a cathodic inhibitor will be awarded **zero marks** for the mechanism question.
> 2. **Forgetting the "dangerous inhibitor" concept** — The dual behaviour (safe above $C_{crit}$, dangerous below $C_{crit}$) is worth **at least 2 marks** in any 7-mark sub-question on anodic inhibitors.
> 3. **Skipping the chemical equations** — Examiner specifically looks for reactions like $2Fe + 2Na_2CrO_4 + 2H_2O \to Fe_2O_3 + Cr_2O_3 + 4NaOH$. Skipping them = −2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the Flade potential equation $E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.059\,pH$** — Often asked in 3-mark Part A.
> 5. **Mixing up Tafel slope formulas** — $\beta_a = 2.303 RT / \alpha_a n F$ is for anodic; the cathodic has $\alpha_c$ and a negative sign in the overpotential.
> 6. **Ignoring environmental impact** — Modern KTU paper explicitly carries 2 marks for environmental/toxicity discussion (REACH compliance, green inhibitors).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bullet-Point Cheat Sheet — Inorganic Anodic Inhibitors**

- **Definition:** Inorganic compounds that suppress anodic metal dissolution by forming a thin passive oxide film on anodic sites.
- **Common examples:** Chromates, nitrites, molybdates, tungstates, vanadates, phosphates, borates, silicates.
- **Mechanism in 5 steps:** Anion migration → adsorption → redox oxidation of metal → nucleation of oxide → passive film formation → drop in $i_a$ by 3–6 orders.
- **Passive film composition on iron:** $\gamma\text{-}Fe_2O_3$ (maghemite) or $Fe_3O_4$ (magnetite), often with inhibitor cation (e.g., $Cr^{3+}$) incorporated.
- **Flade potential equation:** $E_F = E_F^{\circ} - 0.0592 \cdot pH$ — defines the active/passive boundary.
- **Anodic Tafel slope (iron, 25 °C):** $\beta_a \approx 0.0591$ V/decade (≈ 60 mV/decade).
- **Inhibition efficiency formula:** $IE\% = [(i_{corr}^{\circ} - i_{corr}^{inh})/i_{corr}^{\circ}] \times 100$.
- **Critical passivation concentration ($C_{crit}$):** Must be **exceeded**; below it, severe pitting occurs.
- **"Dangerous inhibitor" tag:** Justified only by the under-dose → pitting failure mode.
- **pH range of effectiveness:** Generally **6 to 10**; ineffective in strong acids.
- **Chloride poisoning:** Cl⁻ ions penetrate the passive film and cause pitting — high $Cl^-$ requires higher inhibitor dose or synergists.
- **Environmental note:** Chromates (Cr⁶⁺) are **carcinogens**; molybdates and tungstates are the **green replacements**.
- **Industrial applications:** Cooling water systems, antifreeze, reinforced concrete, oil-well acidizing, aerospace wash primers.
- **Evans diagram signature:** Anodic curve bends to the left at the Flade potential, creating a passive plateau; cathodic curve unchanged; $E_{corr}$ shifts noble, $i_{corr}$ drops sharply.
- **Pourbaix signature:** Passive zone (hump region) **expands** toward lower pH; Flade line shifts **downward** by 100–200 mV.
- **Synergistic combinations:** Nitrite + borate + silicate (TBS cocktail for engine coolants); molybdate + nitrite for high-Cl⁻ brines.
- **KTU must-know equations:**
  1. $\eta_a = \beta_a \log_{10}(i_a/i_0)$
  2. $IE\% = [(i_0 - i)/i_0] \times 100$
  3. $E_F = 0.580 - 0.0592 \cdot pH$ (Fe)
  4. $CR \, (\text{mpy}) = (i_{corr} \cdot M \cdot 3270)/(n \cdot \rho)$
- **Examiner's favourite lines:** "Discuss the mechanism with Evans diagram." / "Why are anodic inhibitors called dangerous?" / "Compare chromates and molybdates." / "Derive the anodic Tafel slope."

---

> **End of Notes — Inorganic Anodic Inhibitors | Module 4 | PEICT866 — Corrosion Control | KTU 2024 Scheme**
