topics.title: Organic Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organic Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Organic Inhibitors** are organic compounds (typically containing heteroatoms such as N, S, O, or P, and/or multiple bonds / aromatic rings) that reduce the rate of corrosion of a metal by **adsorbing onto the active sites of the metal surface**, thereby forming a protective hydrophobic film that displaces water and aggressive ions from the metal–electrolyte interface.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, organic inhibitors are classified under **adsorption-type (interface-type / film-forming) inhibitors** as opposed to the **electrochemical (interface) type** that interferes with the anodic or cathodic reaction directly. Their action is governed primarily by **physico-chemical adsorption** phenomena described by isotherms such as **Langmuir**, **Freundlich**, and **Temkin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Heteroatoms of Interest:** Nitrogen (N), Sulfur (S), Oxygen (O), Phosphorus (P). Their lone pairs and π-electrons are the "anchors" that bind to the metal surface.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a metal surface in an acid bath as a **magnetically charged dance floor** where water molecules, H⁺ ions, and Cl⁻ ions are constantly "stomping" on the metal, chipping pieces away (corrosion). An **organic inhibitor** is like introducing a group of well-dressed, polite guests (the inhibitor molecules) who gently but firmly **insert themselves between the rowdy dancers and the floor**, covering the surface with a thin, oily "carpet."

- The **heteroatoms (N, S, O, P)** in the inhibitor act like the **"sticky shoes"** of these guests — they bond (adsorb) to the floor (metal).
- The **hydrocarbon tail** of the molecule acts like a **water-repellent umbrella**, shielding the metal from the aggressive electrolyte.

The more the carpet covers (higher **surface coverage θ**), the less corrosion occurs.

> [!TIP]
> **Coverage (θ)** is the fraction of metal surface occupied by inhibitor molecules. **θ = 0** means no protection; **θ = 1** means fully protected. It is the single most important concept in this module.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics

- **Inhibition Efficiency (IE %)** = **(CR₀ − CRᵢ) / CR₀ × 100**
- **Surface Coverage (θ)** = **(CR₀ − CRᵢ) / CR₀**
- **Standard Reference Unit:** Corrosion Rate is typically expressed in **mm/yr (mpy)** or **g·m⁻²·h⁻¹**.

Where:
- **CR₀** = Corrosion rate without inhibitor (blank)
- **CRᵢ** = Corrosion rate with inhibitor

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Action of Organic Inhibitors

The protection offered by organic inhibitors is a **four-step process**:

1. **Transport of inhibitor molecules** from the bulk electrolyte to the metal–solution interface (mass transfer by diffusion/convection).
2. **Adsorption** of the inhibitor onto the metal surface at active sites (the heart of inhibition).
3. **Film formation** — the adsorbed layer reorganizes into a coherent, hydrophobic film.
4. **Blocking effect** — the film acts as a physical barrier that:
   - Reduces the anodic dissolution rate (metal → metalⁿ⁺ + ne⁻) by lowering the active area.
   - Reduces the cathodic reaction rate (e.g., 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂ or O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻) by blocking H⁺/O₂ access.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why heteroatoms?** Metals have vacant d-orbitals (especially Fe, Cu, Ni). Heteroatoms (N, S, O) donate their lone pair electrons into these vacant orbitals → forms a **coordinate (dative) bond**. Aromatic rings donate **π-electrons** similarly. **S and N are more effective than O** because they are more polarizable (their electron clouds are more "deformable," leading to stronger bonding).

### 2.2 Types of Organic Inhibitors (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

| Class | Example Compound | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amines & Aniline derivatives** | Aniline, *p*-toluidine, diphenylamine | N as anchoring atom | Acid pickling of mild steel |
| **Quinolines & Pyridines** | Quinoline, 2-mercaptobenzimidazole | N + aromatic ring | Acid cleaning, cooling water |
| **Thioureas & Thio-compounds** | Thiourea, phenylthiourea | S + N (very polarizable) | H₂SO₄ pickling |
| **Azoles** | Benzotriazole (BTA), Tolyltriazole | Multiple N atoms | **Copper & brass** (vapour-phase) |
| **Imidazolines** | 2-Imidazoline, oleic imidazoline | N–C=N | CO₂/H₂S oil-field corrosion |
| **Sulfonates & Fatty acids** | Sodium petroleum sulfonate | Long hydrophobic tail | Lubricants, fuels |
| **Aldehydes & Ketones** | Cinnamaldehyde, furfural | π-electrons | Acid pickling, vapour phase |

### 2.3 Factors Affecting Inhibition Efficiency

- **Molecular structure:** Larger molecules with more electron-donating groups (–CH₃, –OH, –NH₂) → higher IE.
- **π-electron density:** Aromatic rings > aliphatic chains.
- **Polarizability of heteroatom:** S > N > O (in that order of strength of adsorption).
- **Concentration of inhibitor:** Higher concentration → higher θ → higher IE (until saturation).
- **Temperature:** Adsorption is generally **exothermic**; IE typically decreases with temperature rise (physisorption) but can increase for chemisorption.
- **Electrolyte nature:** pH, presence of aggressive ions (Cl⁻).
- **Metal surface charge:** The "PZC" (Point of Zero Charge) of the metal dictates whether anions or cations adsorb preferentially.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Adsorption Isotherms)

| Isotherm | Linear Form | Equation | Constant Meaning | Typical Plot (Y vs X) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Langmuir** | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$ | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant | C/θ vs C (slope = 1) |
| **Freundlich** | $\log\theta = \log K_{ads} + n \log C$ | $\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C^{n}$ | n = heterogeneity factor (0 < n < 1) | log θ vs log C (linear) |
| **Temkin** | $\theta = \dfrac{1}{2a}\ln(K_{ads} \cdot C)$ | $\theta = \dfrac{RT}{2a}\ln(K_{ads} \cdot C)$ | a = lateral interaction parameter | θ vs ln C (linear) |
| **Frumkin** | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} \exp(-2a\theta) = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | (includes lateral interaction) | a = interaction factor | — |
| **El-Awady** | $\log\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = \log K' + y \log C$ | (modified Langmuir) | 1/y = number of active sites occupied | log(θ/1−θ) vs log C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** $K_{ads}$ is in **M⁻¹** (L·mol⁻¹). $C$ is inhibitor concentration in **mol·L⁻¹ (M)**. $\theta$ is dimensionless. Temperature is in **Kelvin (K)**.

### 2.5 Thermodynamic Parameters of Adsorption

The standard free energy of adsorption $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ is computed from:

$$K_{ads} = \dfrac{1}{55.5} \exp\left(\dfrac{-\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT}\right)$$

Where **55.5** is the molar concentration of water in mol·L⁻¹ (used as standard state for adsorbed species on the surface from aqueous solution).

- If $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \le -20$ kJ·mol⁻¹ → **Physisorption** (electrostatic, weak).
- If $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \le -40$ kJ·mol⁻¹ → **Chemisorption** (covalent/coordinate bond, strong).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Adsorption isotherms are used to design **dosage charts** for industrial inhibitor packages (e.g., in oil & gas pipelines, cooling towers, and acid pickling baths). The isotherm constants $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ are also used to **screen new inhibitor molecules** via computational chemistry (DFT).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

The Langmuir isotherm assumes:
- **Monolayer adsorption** on a homogeneous surface.
- Each active site holds **one adsorbate molecule** (no stacking).
- **No lateral interaction** between adjacent adsorbate molecules.

Consider the reversible adsorption reaction of inhibitor molecules (I) on surface sites (S):

$$I_{(aq)} + S_{(surface)} \rightleftharpoons IS_{(ads)}$$

The equilibrium constant $K_{ads}$ is:

$$K_{ads} = \dfrac{[IS]}{[I] \cdot [S]} = \dfrac{\theta}{C \cdot (1-\theta)}$$

Where:
- $\theta = [IS]/[S]_{total}$ = fraction of surface covered.
- $(1-\theta) = [S]/[S]_{total}$ = fraction of surface bare.
- $C = [I]$ = equilibrium concentration of inhibitor in bulk.

Rearranging:

$$K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot (1-\theta) = \theta$$

$$K_{ads} \cdot C - K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot \theta = \theta$$

$$K_{ads} \cdot C = \theta + K_{ads} \cdot C \cdot \theta = \theta (1 + K_{ads} \cdot C)$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{K_{ads} \cdot C}{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $(K_{ads} \cdot C)$:

$$\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

This is the **Langmuir form**. The **linear form** used for plotting is obtained by taking the reciprocal:

$$\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

A plot of $\dfrac{C}{\theta}$ (y-axis) vs. $C$ (x-axis) gives a **straight line of slope 1** and intercept $\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ from $K_{ads}$

Starting from the thermodynamic definition of the equilibrium constant for adsorption from aqueous solution:

$$K_{ads} = \dfrac{1}{C_{H_2O}} \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{-\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT}\right)$$

Where $C_{H_2O} = 55.5$ mol·L⁻¹.

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$\ln K_{ads} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{55.5}\right) - \dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT}$$

Rearranging for $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$:

$$-\dfrac{\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}}{RT} = \ln K_{ads} - \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{55.5}\right) = \ln K_{ads} + \ln(55.5) = \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit reminder:** $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T$ in K, $K_{ads}$ in L·mol⁻¹. The result is in **J·mol⁻¹** (convert to kJ·mol⁻¹ by dividing by 1000).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ and Classify Adsorption Type

**Problem:** The adsorption of an organic inhibitor on mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ at 298 K follows the Langmuir isotherm. From the linear plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$, the intercept is **100 L·mol⁻¹**. Compute $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ and identify whether the adsorption is physisorption or chemisorption.

**Step 1 — Extract $K_{ads}$ from intercept:**

The Langmuir linear equation is:

$$\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

So the intercept on the y-axis is $\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$.

$$\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} = 100 \text{ L·mol}^{-1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_{ads} = 0.01 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thermodynamic equation:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

Substitute the values:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = - (8.314) \cdot (298) \cdot \ln(55.5 \times 0.01)$$

Compute the argument of the log:

$$55.5 \times 0.01 = 0.555$$

Take the natural log:

$$\ln(0.555) = -0.5887$$

Multiply:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = - (8.314 \times 298) \times (-0.5887) = -2477.6 \times (-0.5887)$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = +1458.7 \text{ J·mol}^{-1} \approx +1.46 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

The value is **positive**, meaning adsorption is **not spontaneous** under these conditions. This is a physically unrealistic result, indicating that the experimental data do **not** fit the Langmuir model (likely the inhibitor is too dilute, or the system is too weakly adsorbed). In a *correct* experimental scenario, you would expect a **negative** value (typically between −20 and −40 kJ·mol⁻¹), indicating **spontaneous** adsorption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention trap:** Always remember that a **negative** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ is required for **spontaneous** adsorption. If your calculation gives a positive value, double-check units and the linearity of the Langmuir plot.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Inhibitor Behaviour Summary

| Inhibitor Type | Anchoring Atom | Adsorption Type | Acid Medium Behaviour | Neutral/Saline Medium |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Amines** | N | Physio-chemisorption | Excellent (H⁺ protonates N → cation adsorbs on negative metal) | Moderate |
| **Thiourea** | S + N | Chemisorption | Very good in H₂SO₄ | Limited |
| **Benzotriazole (BTA)** | 3 × N | Chemisorption | Poor (decomposes) | **Excellent for Cu** |
| **Imidazoline** | N–C=N | Chemisorption | Moderate | **Excellent in CO₂/H₂S brine** |
| **Sulfonates** | SO₃⁻ | Physiosorption | Limited | Used in oils, fuels |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Flowchart — How an Organic Inhibitor Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulk Electrolyte with Inhibitor Molecules] --> B[Diffusion to Metal Surface]
    B --> C[Approach to Metal Electrochemical Double Layer]
    C --> D{Type of Adsorption}
    D -->|Physical| E[Physisorption: Electrostatic or van der Waals]
    D -->|Chemical| F[Chemisorption: Coordinate bond via lone pair / pi-electrons]
    E --> G[Formation of Hydrophobic Monolayer]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Block Active Anodic and Cathodic Sites]
    H --> I[Reduced Metal Dissolution Rate]
    H --> J[Reduced Hydrogen Evolution or Oxygen Reduction]
    I --> K[Lower Corrosion Current Density icorr]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Higher Polarization Resistance Rp]
    L --> M[Effective Corrosion Protection Achieved]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Organic Inhibitors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Organic Inhibitors] --> B[Anchoring Group Type]
    A --> C[Functionality]
    A --> D[Application]
    B --> B1[N-based Amines, Azoles, Quinolines]
    B --> B2[S-based Thioureas, Sulfides, Thiols]
    B --> B3[O-based Alcohols, Aldehydes, Ketones]
    B --> B4[P-based Phosphonium, Phosphonates]
    C --> C1[Anodic Film Formers]
    C --> C2[Cathodic Film Formers]
    C --> C3[Mixed Type]
    D --> D1[Acid Pickling e.g. Aniline]
    D --> D2[Neutral Cooling Water e.g. BTA for Cu]
    D --> D3[Oil and Gas Pipelines e.g. Imidazoline]
    D --> D4[Vapour Phase e.g. Cinnamaldehyde]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Adsorption Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Bulk[Bulk Solution]
        IM[Inhibitor Molecule] --> DIFF[Diffusion Layer]
    end
    subgraph Interface[Metal Solution Interface]
        DIFF --> EDL[Electrical Double Layer]
        EDL --> ADSITE[Active Site on Metal]
        ADSITE -->|Coordinate Bond| CHEM[Chemisorbed Film]
        ADSITE -->|Electrostatic| PHYS[Physisorbed Layer]
    end
    subgraph Surface[Protected Metal Surface]
        CHEM --> SURF[Metal Surface coverage theta]
        PHYS --> SURF
        SURF --> ICORR[Reduced Corrosion Rate]
    end
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Adsorption Isotherm Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Experimental C vs theta Data] --> B{Choose Best Fit}
    B --> C[Langmuir: C by theta vs C is linear with slope 1]
    B --> D[Freundlich: log theta vs log C is linear]
    B --> E[Temkin: theta vs ln C is linear]
    C --> F[Extract Kads from intercept]
    D --> G[Extract Kads and n from intercept and slope]
    E --> H[Extract Kads and a from intercept and slope]
    F --> I[Compute Delta G ads]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define organic inhibitors. What are the typical heteroatoms responsible for adsorption on the metal surface?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Organic inhibitors are organic compounds that reduce the corrosion rate of a metal by adsorbing on the metal surface to form a protective film, thereby displacing water and aggressive ions from the metal–electrolyte interface **[1 mark]**.

The typical heteroatoms responsible for adsorption are **Nitrogen (N), Sulfur (S), Oxygen (O), and Phosphorus (P)** **[1 mark]**. These atoms possess lone pairs of electrons that can be donated to the vacant d-orbitals of the metal to form coordinate (covalent) bonds. In addition, **π-electrons of aromatic rings** also contribute to chemisorption **[1 mark]**.

The general order of effectiveness based on polarizability is: **S > N > O**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation in its linear form. What does the constant $K_{ads}$ represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Langmuir adsorption isotherm in linear form is:

$$\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

**[1 mark for equation, 1 mark for stating slope = 1]**

A plot of $\dfrac{C}{\theta}$ (y-axis) versus $C$ (x-axis) yields a straight line of unit slope and intercept $\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}$ **[1 mark]**.

The constant $K_{ads}$ (in L·mol⁻¹) is the **adsorption equilibrium constant**; its magnitude indicates the strength of adsorption — a higher $K_{ads}$ means stronger binding of the inhibitor to the metal surface.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the mechanism of inhibition of organic inhibitors on metal surfaces. Highlight the role of heteroatoms and aromatic rings. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The adsorption of an organic inhibitor on mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄ at 303 K was studied, and the following data were obtained:

| $C$ (mol·L⁻¹) | $\theta$ |
|---|---|
| $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.40 |
| $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.75 |
| $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.86 |
| $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.96 |

Verify whether the data fit the **Langmuir isotherm** and calculate $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A(a) — Mechanism of Inhibition (7 marks):**

The protection offered by organic inhibitors proceeds through the following sequential steps **[1 mark]**:

1. **Transport** — Inhibitor molecules diffuse from the bulk solution to the metal surface through the hydrodynamic and diffusion boundary layers.
2. **Adsorption** — This is the critical step. Inhibitor molecules adsorb at the **active anodic and cathodic sites** of the metal. Adsorption can be:
   - **Physisorption** (electrostatic, reversible, low $\Delta H$) **[1 mark]**
   - **Chemisorption** (coordinate bond, irreversible, high $\Delta H$) **[1 mark]**
3. **Film formation** — The adsorbed monolayer reorganises into a coherent, hydrophobic film that displaces water.
4. **Blocking action** — The film reduces the accessible metal area, suppressing both:
   - Anodic reaction: $\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^{n+} + ne^-$ **[1 mark]**
   - Cathodic reaction: $2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2$ (in acids)

**Role of heteroatoms (N, S, O, P) [2 marks]:**
The heteroatoms contain **lone pair electrons** that are donated into the **vacant d-orbitals** of transition metals (Fe, Cu, Ni), forming a **coordinate (dative) covalent bond**. The effectiveness of adsorption follows the polarizability order **S > N > O > P**, because more polarizable atoms form stronger bonds due to greater orbital overlap.

**Role of aromatic rings [1 mark]:**
Aromatic rings (e.g., benzene, pyridine) provide a delocalized **π-electron cloud** that can be donated to the metal's d-orbitals, supplementing the heteroatom bonding. Aromatic amines, azoles, and quinolines are therefore stronger inhibitors than their aliphatic analogues.

---

**Solution to Q-A(b) — Langmuir Verification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $C/\theta$ for each data point [2 marks]:**

| $C$ (M) | $\theta$ | $C/\theta$ |
|---|---|---|
| $1 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.40 | $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| $5 \times 10^{-5}$ | 0.75 | $6.67 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.86 | $1.16 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 0.96 | $5.21 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Step 2 — Verify linearity [2 marks]:**

Plotting $C/\theta$ vs $C$ (linear regression gives a slope of approximately **1.05** and intercept $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{-5}$ L·mol⁻¹, confirming the data fit the Langmuir isotherm with a near-unit slope).

**Step 3 — Extract $K_{ads}$ [1 mark]:**

$$\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} = 2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ L·mol}^{-1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_{ads} = 4.35 \times 10^{4} \text{ L·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ at 303 K [2 marks]:**

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = - (8.314) \cdot (303) \cdot \ln(55.5 \times 4.35 \times 10^{4})$$

$$\ln(55.5 \times 4.35 \times 10^{4}) = \ln(2.414 \times 10^{6}) = 14.696$$

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = - (8.314 \times 303) \times 14.696 = -2519.1 \times 14.696$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \approx -37.0 \text{ kJ·mol}^{-1}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** The value is **negative** (spontaneous adsorption) and lies in the range −20 to −40 kJ·mol⁻¹, indicating **mixed physisorption–chemisorption** with chemisorptive character dominant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme] — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Explain the classification of organic inhibitors based on their **anchoring group**, giving one industrial example for each class. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A corrosion study of mild steel in 0.5 M H₂SO₄ at 308 K with various concentrations of aniline gave the following corrosion rates:

| Inhibitor Concentration (M) | Corrosion Rate (mm·yr⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| 0 (blank) | 8.50 |
| $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5.20 |
| $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 3.10 |
| $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1.95 |
| $5 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.85 |

Calculate the **inhibition efficiency (IE %)** and **surface coverage (θ)** for each concentration. Plot $C/\theta$ vs $C$ to verify the Langmuir isotherm, and find $K_{ads}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B(a) — Classification by Anchoring Group (7 marks):**

Organic inhibitors can be classified by the heteroatom that acts as the **anchoring (adsorbing) centre** **[1 mark]**:

1. **Nitrogen-based inhibitors** — Amines, amides, imines, quinolines, pyridines, azoles. The lone pair on N is donated to metal d-orbitals. **Example:** Benzotriazole (BTA) for **copper and brass protection in cooling water systems** **[1.5 marks]**.

2. **Sulfur-based inhibitors** — Thioureas, mercaptans, sulfides, sulfoxides. Sulfur is more polarizable than N, leading to stronger chemisorption. **Example:** Phenylthiourea in **H₂SO₄ pickling of mild steel** **[1.5 marks]**.

3. **Oxygen-based inhibitors** — Alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, esters, carboxylic acids. O is least polarizable of the three. **Example:** Sodium petroleum sulfonate as a **fuel and lubricant additive** **[1 mark]**.

4. **Phosphorus-based inhibitors** — Phosphonates, phosphonium salts. **Example:** Aminotrimethylene phosphonic acid (ATMP) in **cooling-water scale/corrosion inhibition** **[1 mark]**.

5. **Mixed/N–S containing** — Thiadiazoles, imidazolines, mercaptobenzimidazoles. **Example:** 2-Mercaptobenzimidazole in **CO₂/H₂S oilfield corrosion** **[1 mark]**.

---

**Solution to Q-B(b) — IE %, θ, and Langmuir Fit (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate IE % and θ [2 marks]:**

Using $IE\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$ and $\theta = IE\%/100$:

| $C$ (M) | CR (mm·yr⁻¹) | IE % | $\theta$ | $C/\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (blank) | 8.50 | — | — | — |
| $1 \times 10^{-4}$ | 5.20 | 38.82 | 0.3882 | $2.58 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | 3.10 | 63.53 | 0.6353 | $7.87 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| $1 \times 10^{-3}$ | 1.95 | 77.06 | 0.7706 | $1.30 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| $5 \times 10^{-3}$ | 0.85 | 90.00 | 0.9000 | $5.56 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Step 2 — Verify Langmuir fit [2 marks]:**

A plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ yields a **straight line** with slope ≈ 1.08 and intercept ≈ $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ L·mol⁻¹. The near-unit slope confirms Langmuir behaviour.

**Step 3 — Find $K_{ads}$ [1 mark]:**

$$\dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ L·mol}^{-1} \quad\Rightarrow\quad K_{ads} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{3} \text{ L·mol}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation [2 marks]:**

- The IE increases with concentration and reaches **90 %** at $5 \times 10^{-3}$ M, indicating aniline is a **good inhibitor** in H₂SO₄.
- The protonated anilinium ion ($\text{PhNH}_3^+$) electrostatically adsorbs on the negatively charged mild steel surface in acid (since PZC of Fe is below 0), giving high coverage.
- The relatively modest $K_{ads}$ value suggests **physisorption-dominated** behaviour, consistent with the protonated-cation mechanism.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit errors** in $K_{ads}$ (must be L·mol⁻¹) and $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ (must be in kJ·mol⁻¹) cause **automatic ½ to 1 mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the 55.5 factor** in the $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ equation is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Mixing up θ with IE %** — remember $\theta = IE\%/100$ (both dimensionless but different).
> 4. **Skipping the "verify Langmuir" step** — KTU expects students to explicitly mention that the slope is ≈ 1; otherwise the answer is treated as incomplete.
> 5. **Plot not drawn** — In 14-mark problems, even a rough sketch of the linear plot with axes labelled is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Organic Inhibitors (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Organic inhibitors protect metal by **adsorbing** to form a hydrophobic film; they are **interface-type** (film-forming) inhibitors.
- ✅ **Anchoring atoms (in order of effectiveness):** **S > N > O > P** (polarizability order).
- ✅ **Aromatic rings** with π-electron clouds enhance adsorption.
- ✅ **Inhibition Efficiency (IE %)** = (CR₀ − CRᵢ)/CR₀ × 100; **θ = IE % / 100**.
- ✅ **Langmuir linear form:** $C/\theta = C + 1/K_{ads}$ — slope must be ≈ 1.
- ✅ **Freundlich:** $\log\theta = \log K_{ads} + n \log C$ — for heterogeneous surfaces.
- ✅ **Temkin:** $\theta = (1/2a)\ln(K_{ads} \cdot C)$ — accounts for lateral interactions.
- ✅ **$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$** — must use $R = 8.314$ J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T$ in K, $K_{ads}$ in L·mol⁻¹.
- ✅ **Physisorption:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} > -20$ kJ·mol⁻¹; **Chemisorption:** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} < -40$ kJ·mol⁻¹.
- ✅ **BTA (Benzotriazole)** is the gold-standard inhibitor for **copper & brass** in neutral/cooling water.
- ✅ **Imidazolines** are used in **CO₂/H₂S oil-field environments** for sweet/sour corrosion.
- ✅ **Synergistic combinations** (e.g., amines + halides) often outperform individual inhibitors — a possible higher-order KTU question.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Sign convention of $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ (must be **negative** for spontaneous adsorption).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synergistic Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Synergistic Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synergistic Inhibitors** are defined as binary or multi-component inhibitor formulations in which the combined corrosion-inhibitive performance of two or more inhibitors is **significantly greater than the sum of the inhibition efficiencies** offered by each individual inhibitor acting independently. This cooperative enhancement is termed the **Synergistic Effect** (or **Synergism**), and is mathematically characterized by the **Synergism Parameter (S)**.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme framework, synergism is quantified using the relationship proposed by **Aramaki and Hackerman**, where the synergism parameter $S_{1}$ for inhibitor 1 in the presence of inhibitor 2 is given by:

$$S_{1} = \frac{1 - \theta_{1} - \theta_{2} + \theta_{1}\theta_{2}}{1 - \theta'_{1}}$$

where $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ are the surface coverages of inhibitors 1 and 2 acting alone, and $\theta'_{1}$ is the experimentally observed surface coverage of inhibitor 1 when used in combination with inhibitor 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The interpretation of the Synergism Parameter is the most frequently tested concept in Module 4:
> - $S > 1$ → **Positive Synergism** (Cooperative, desirable)
> - $S = 1$ → **Additive Effect** (No interaction, just summation)
> - $S < 1$ → **Antagonism** (Negative synergism, one inhibitor counteracts the other)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer and the Backup" Model

Imagine a corrosion cell where aggressive ions (like Cl⁻) are attempting to "break into" a metal surface to initiate pitting attack. A single inhibitor (say, an organic amine molecule) is like a single **bouncer** standing at the door, blocking some of the attackers. However, certain aggressive anions are small and persistent.

Now, add a second inhibitor — a **halide ion** (like I⁻ or SCN⁻). This halide ion does not block the entire surface, but it *chemisorbs* first onto the metal, creating a negatively charged intermediate layer. This negatively charged layer then electrostatically attracts the protonated organic amine (which is positively charged) much more strongly than it would have adsorbed on a bare metal surface.

> **Result:** The bouncer (organic inhibitor) is now *reinforced* by the halide "backup" — the combined protection far exceeds the sum of each acting alone. This is the essence of **synergism**.

## 1.3 Classification of Synergistic Interactions

| Type | Effect on Corrosion Rate | Synergism Parameter | Practical Significance |
|------|--------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Positive Synergism** | Greatly reduced | $S > 1$ | Highly desirable in industry |
| **Additive Effect** | Sum of individual reductions | $S = 1$ | Neutral — no cooperative gain |
| **Negative Synergism (Antagonism)** | Less than sum of individual reductions | $S < 1$ | Undesirable — must be avoided |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Notation (KTU Convention)**
> Throughout Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme uses the following notations:
> - $CR_0$ → Corrosion rate in absence of inhibitor
> - $CR_{inh}$ → Corrosion rate in presence of inhibitor
> - $\eta$ or $IE$ → Inhibition Efficiency (in %)
> - $\theta$ → Surface coverage fraction ($0 \le \theta \le 1$)
> - $K_{ads}$ → Adsorption equilibrium constant
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanistic Foundations of Synergism

The phenomenon of synergism arises from **mutual interaction** between two inhibitors at the metal-solution interface. This interaction can be classified by the underlying mechanism:

### 2.1.1 Mechanistic Classification

1. **Cooperative Adsorption Synergism**
   - One inhibitor creates favorable surface conditions (charge, polarity) for the adsorption of the second.
   - *Example:* Halide ions (Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻) pre-adsorb on the metal surface, creating a negatively charged layer that attracts protonated organic cations.

2. **Film-Reinforcement Synergism**
   - Each inhibitor forms a partial protective film; together, the films interlock or coalesce to form a denser, more impermeable barrier.
   - *Example:* Zn²⁺ precipitates as Zn(OH)₂ at cathodic sites, while polyphosphate forms a film at anodic sites — together, they protect both half-cells.

3. **Complex-Formation Synergism**
   - The two inhibitors form a *protective complex in solution* that adsorbs more strongly than either species alone.
   - *Example:* 8-Hydroxyquinoline + Zn²⁺ form a hydrophobic Zn–oxine complex that deposits on the metal surface.

4. **Electrochemical Pairing Synergism**
   - One inhibitor functions predominantly as an anodic inhibitor; the other as a cathodic inhibitor. Their combined action suppresses both half-cell reactions.
   - *Example:* Chromate (anodic) + Zn²⁺ (cathodic).

5. **pH-Modification Synergism**
   - One inhibitor adjusts the local pH at the metal surface, creating conditions favorable for the second inhibitor to function optimally.
   - *Example:* Amines raise pH, promoting the formation of protective oxide layers.

## 2.2 The Adsorption Isotherm Framework

The behavior of synergistic mixtures is most rigorously described through **adsorption isotherms**. The most commonly used isotherms in KTU Module 4 are:

### 2.2.1 Langmuir Isotherm
$$\frac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

Rearranged:
$$\theta = \frac{K_{ads} \cdot C}{1 + K_{ads} \cdot C}$$

### 2.2.2 Freundlich Isotherm
$$\theta = K \cdot C^{n}, \quad 0 < n < 1$$

### 2.2.3 Temkin Isotherm
$$\theta = \frac{RT}{b} \ln(K_{ads} \cdot C)$$

### 2.2.4 Frumkin Isotherm
$$\frac{\theta}{1 - \theta} \exp(-2a\theta) = K_{ads} \cdot C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Langmuir Isotherm** is the most frequently tested in KTU university examinations, because it enables a *linear* plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ to extract $K_{ads}$ and the Gibbs free energy of adsorption $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in this table use `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) to prevent any conflict with markdown table syntax.

| # | Equation / Parameter | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning / Use |
|---|----------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Inhibition Efficiency ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0} \times 100$ | Percentage reduction in corrosion rate |
| 2 | Surface Coverage ($\theta$) | $\theta = \dfrac{\eta}{100}$ | Fractional coverage of metal surface by inhibitor |
| 3 | Synergism Parameter ($S_1$) | $S_1 = \dfrac{1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 - \theta'_1}$ | Measures cooperative effect for inhibitor 1 |
| 4 | Synergism Parameter ($S_2$) | $S_2 = \dfrac{1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 - \theta'_2}$ | Same as above for inhibitor 2 |
| 5 | Langmuir Isotherm | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ | Linear form — used to find $K_{ads}$ from slope/intercept |
| 6 | Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption | $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ | Indicator of spontaneity & type (physisorption vs chemisorption) |
| 7 | Arrhenius Equation | $\ln(CR) = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | Activation energy calculation |
| 8 | Transition State Equation | $\ln\dfrac{CR}{T} = \ln\dfrac{R}{Nh} + \dfrac{\Delta S^{\circ \neq}}{R} - \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ \neq}}{RT}$ | Enthalpy and entropy of activation |
| 9 | Corrosion Rate from Tafel | $CR = \dfrac{i_{corr} \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Where $i_{corr}$ is current density, $M$ molar mass, $\rho$ density |
| 10 | Additive Inhibition Sum | $\eta_{additive} = \eta_1 + \eta_2$ | Hypothetical sum — used for comparison with observed $\eta_{mix}$ |

## 2.4 Engineering Industrial Utility

Synergistic inhibitor formulations are **the workhorse of real-world corrosion engineering**. Some landmark industrial applications include:

- **Cooling water systems:** Zn²⁺ + Polyphosphate + Tolyltriazole (the "ZPT" cocktail for closed-loop cooling)
- **Oil & gas pipelines:** Imidazoline + KI for CO₂/H₂S environments
- **Pickling acid baths:** Thiourea + HCl synergism for steel pickling
- **Marine coatings:** Tannin + Zn²⁺ for seawater ballast tanks
- **Automotive antifreeze:** Silicate + Nitrite + Molybdate "NOAT" coolant formulations

> The advantage of synergism is **dosage economy**: very low concentrations of two inhibitors can replace a much higher concentration of a single inhibitor, achieving equivalent or superior protection.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Synergism Parameter Equation (Aramaki–Hackerman Model)

The synergism parameter is not given as an axiom — it is **derived** from the assumption that both inhibitors adsorb on the metal surface following Langmuir-type **competitive** adsorption, but with mutual enhancement.

### Step 1 — State the starting assumption (Competitive Langmuir Adsorption)

For two inhibitors (1 and 2) competing for the same surface sites, the fraction of sites *not occupied* by either inhibitor is:

$$(1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2)$$

The term $\theta_1 \theta_2$ represents the **probability that a site is doubly available to both inhibitors**, leading to cooperative adsorption — this is the **synergistic enhancement term**.

### Step 2 — Define the surface coverage of inhibitor 1 *in the mixture*

The experimentally observed surface coverage of inhibitor 1 *in the presence of* inhibitor 2 is $\theta'_1$. The unoccupied surface fraction when the mixture is applied is $(1 - \theta'_1)$.

### Step 3 — Equate the two unoccupied-fraction expressions

The *unoccupied surface* as predicted by independent competitive adsorption must equal the *observed unoccupied surface* from experiment. Therefore:

$$1 - \theta'_1 = 1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for the synergistic component

By algebraic rearrangement, isolate the cooperative adsorption term $\theta_1 \theta_2$:

$$\theta_1 \theta_2 = \theta'_1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + 1$$

### Step 5 — Normalize to obtain the dimensionless Synergism Parameter

Divide both sides of the equation by $(1 - \theta'_1)$, the experimentally observed unoccupied fraction:

$$S_1 = \frac{\theta_1 \theta_2}{1 - \theta'_1} = \frac{1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 - \theta'_1}$$

This dimensionless number is the **Synergism Parameter** $S_1$. By symmetry, $S_2$ follows identically.

### Step 6 — Interpretation of the result

| Numerical Result | Physical Meaning |
|------------------|------------------|
| $S_1 > 1$ | The numerator (theoretical cooperative term) is *larger* than the denominator (observed unoccupied surface) → cooperative adsorption dominates → **positive synergism** |
| $S_1 = 1$ | The two inhibitors adsorb *independently* → additive effect |
| $S_1 < 1$ | The cooperative term is *smaller* than the observed unoccupied surface → one inhibitor *displaces* the other → **antagonism** |

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculation of Synergism Parameter from Experimental Data

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)**
> A binary inhibitor system is tested. The following data are obtained experimentally:
> - Inhibitor A alone: $CR_0 = 1.20$ mm/yr, $CR_A = 0.40$ mm/yr
> - Inhibitor B alone: $CR_B = 0.60$ mm/yr
> - Mixture (A + B): $CR_{mix} = 0.15$ mm/yr
>
> Determine (a) individual inhibition efficiencies, (b) surface coverages, and (c) the synergism parameter.

### Step (a) — Calculate the individual inhibition efficiencies

$$\eta_A = \frac{CR_0 - CR_A}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{1.20 - 0.40}{1.20} \times 100$$

$$\eta_A = \frac{0.80}{1.20} \times 100 = 66.67\,\%$$

$$\eta_B = \frac{CR_0 - CR_B}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{1.20 - 0.60}{1.20} \times 100$$

$$\eta_B = \frac{0.60}{1.20} \times 100 = 50.00\,\%$$

### Step (b) — Calculate the surface coverages and the mixture inhibition efficiency

$$\theta_A = \frac{\eta_A}{100} = 0.6667$$

$$\theta_B = \frac{\eta_B}{100} = 0.5000$$

$$\eta_{mix} = \frac{CR_0 - CR_{mix}}{CR_0} \times 100 = \frac{1.20 - 0.15}{1.20} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{mix} = \frac{1.05}{1.20} \times 100 = 87.50\,\%$$

### Step (c) — Calculate the observed surface coverage of A in the mixture

The *effective* surface coverage in the mixture is derived from $\eta_{mix}$:

$$\theta'_{A} = \frac{\eta_{mix}}{100} = 0.8750$$

### Step (d) — Substitute into the Synergism Parameter equation

$$S_1 = \frac{1 - \theta_A - \theta_B + \theta_A \theta_B}{1 - \theta'_A}$$

First, compute the denominator:

$$1 - \theta'_A = 1 - 0.8750 = 0.1250$$

Next, compute the numerator term-by-term:

$$1 - \theta_A - \theta_B = 1 - 0.6667 - 0.5000 = -0.1667$$

$$\theta_A \theta_B = 0.6667 \times 0.5000 = 0.3334$$

$$\text{Numerator} = -0.1667 + 0.3334 = 0.1667$$

Now divide:

$$S_1 = \frac{0.1667}{0.1250} = 1.3336$$

### Step (e) — Interpretation

Since $S_1 \approx 1.33 > 1$, the system exhibits **strong positive synergism**. Note that the additive sum would have been $66.67\% + 50.00\% = 116.67\%$, which is *physically impossible* (capped at 100%) — but the *observed* mixture efficiency is 87.50%, and the synergism parameter confirms that the two inhibitors are *cooperating*, not merely being summed. The fact that 87.50% < 100% (and yet $S_1 > 1$) reflects that synergism is *not* about exceeding 100%, but about exceeding the *predictable independent contribution*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption (for Synergistic Mixtures)

To confirm the *type* of adsorption (physical or chemical) in synergistic mixtures, the Langmuir constant $K_{ads}$ is extracted and $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ is calculated.

### Step 1 — Linear form of Langmuir Isotherm

$$\frac{C}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$$

A plot of $C/\theta$ (y-axis) vs $C$ (x-axis) gives a straight line with:
- Slope = 1 (confirming Langmuir conformity)
- Intercept = $1/K_{ads}$

### Step 2 — Extract $K_{ads}$

For a given experimental data set with intercept $b$:

$$K_{ads} = \frac{1}{b}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$

The standard Gibbs free energy of adsorption is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$$

The factor **55.5** is the molar concentration of water in mol/L.

### Step 4 — Interpretation

| Value of $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ | Type of Adsorption | Synergistic Implication |
|----------------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \ge -20$ kJ/mol | **Physisorption** (electrostatic) | Weak, reversible; common with halide-mediated synergism |
| $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \le -40$ kJ/mol | **Chemisorption** (covalent/ionic bond) | Strong, often irreversible; common in mixed organic–inorganic films |
| $-40 < \Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} < -20$ kJ/mol | **Mixed mode** | Both types contribute; typical of synergistic mixtures |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Synergistic System

| Desired Protection Type | Recommended Pairing | Mechanism | Industrial Application |
|--------------------------|---------------------|-----------|------------------------|
| Mild steel in HCl | **Amine + KI** | Cooperative adsorption | Acid pickling |
| Copper in cooling water | **BTA + KI** | Halide-mediated chemisorption | Heat exchangers |
| Carbon steel in neutral water | **Zn²⁺ + Polyphosphate** | Anodic-cathodic pairing | Open recirculating cooling |
| Aluminum in NaCl | **8-HQ + Zn²⁺** | Complex-formation | Marine atmospheres |
| Fe in CO₂-saturated brine | **Imidazoline + KI** | Cooperative adsorption | Oil & gas pipelines |
| Galvanized steel | **Chromate + Polyphosphate** | Film-reinforcement | Automotive underbody |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Mechanism of Halide–Organic Synergism on a Metal Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Metal Surface M]:::baseNode
    A2[Aggressive Ions Cl- and H+]:::aggressive
    B1[Step 1: Halide I- Chemisorbs]:::halide
    B2[Step 2: Negatively Charged Layer Forms]:::halide
    B3[Step 3: Protonated Organic Amine R-NH3+ Attracted]:::organic
    B4[Step 4: Tight Ion-Pair Film Forms]:::organic
    B5[Step 5: Aggressive Ions Blocked]:::protected
    A1 --> A2
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
    classDef baseNode fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aggressive fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef halide fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef organic fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef protected fill:#FFFFB3,stroke:#997700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** The diagram traces the sequential mechanistic steps. The aggressive ions (red) attempt to attack the bare metal (grey). The halide ion (blue) is the **first defender** — it chemisorbs and creates a negative field. The protonated organic amine (green) is the **second defender** — it is electrostatically pulled in. Together, they form a tight film (yellow) that excludes aggressive ions.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Classification Tree of Inhibitor Interactions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Inhibitor Interactions]:::root
    P[Positive Synergism S greater than 1]:::pos
    A[Additive Effect S equals 1]:::add
    N[Negative Synergism S less than 1]:::neg
    S --> P
    S --> A
    S --> N
    P --> P1[Cooperative Adsorption]:::posSub
    P --> P2[Film Reinforcement]:::posSub
    P --> P3[Complex Formation]:::posSub
    P --> P4[Anodic-Cathodic Pairing]:::posSub
    A --> A1[Independent Action]:::addSub
    N --> N1[Competitive Displacement]:::negSub
    N --> N2[Complex Deactivation]:::negSub
    classDef root fill:#222,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef pos fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef add fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef neg fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef posSub fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef addSub fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef negSub fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** This hierarchical tree first splits interactions into three categories by the value of the Synergism Parameter, then sub-classifies *positive synergism* into the four mechanistic families described in Section 2.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Functional Architecture of an Industrial Synergistic Cooling Water Programme

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw Cooling Water]:::input
    SOFT[Softener Unit]:::process
    FEED[Chemical Feed System]:::process
    TANK[Day Tank with Mixer]:::process
    ZN[Zinc Salt Solution Cathodic Inhibitor]:::chem
    POLY[Polyphosphate Solution Anodic Inhibitor]:::chem
    TOL[Tolyltriazole Solution Copper Inhibitor]:::chem
    DISP[Dispersion Pump]:::process
    LOOP[Cooling Tower Loop]:::process
    COOL[Heat Exchanger Surface]:::output
    PROT[Protected Film]:::output
    RAW --> SOFT
    SOFT --> FEED
    FEED --> TANK
    ZN --> TANK
    POLY --> TANK
    TOL --> TANK
    TANK --> DISP
    DISP --> LOOP
    LOOP --> COOL
    COOL --> PROT
    classDef input fill:#DCDCDC,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef chem fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

**Reading Guide:** A real-world industrial cooling water treatment programme uses **three** inhibitors dosed into a day tank, then dispersed into the cooling loop. The three inhibitors — **Zn²⁺ (cathodic), polyphosphate (anodic), and tolyltriazole (copper-specific)** — act in synergy to form a multi-layered, multi-metal protective film on the heat exchanger surfaces.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

### **Q1. Define synergism in the context of corrosion inhibitors. How is the synergism parameter interpreted?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Synergism is the phenomenon in which the combined inhibition efficiency of two or more inhibitors is **significantly greater** than the sum of their individual efficiencies, due to cooperative action at the metal–solution interface. It is quantified by the **Synergism Parameter $S$** (Aramaki–Hackerman equation).

**Interpretation (the high-yield 3-line answer):**
- If $S > 1$ → **Positive Synergism** (cooperative adsorption)
- If $S = 1$ → **Additive effect** (no interaction)
- If $S < 1$ → **Antagonism** (negative synergism / competitive displacement)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. Explain the mechanism of halide-induced synergism with organic amine inhibitors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The mechanism involves **cooperative adsorption** in two steps:
1. **Halide ions (Cl⁻, Br⁻, I⁻)** preferentially chemisorb onto the metal surface, creating a **negatively charged** intermediate layer.
2. **Protonated organic amines (R-NH₃⁺)** in acidic media are electrostatically attracted to this layer, adsorbing much more strongly than they would on a bare metal surface.

The combined halide–amine film is denser and more stable than either inhibitor could form alone, producing **positive synergism**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 Internal Choice Question A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Define synergistic inhibition. Derive the Aramaki–Hackerman synergism parameter equation. **\[7 Marks\]**
**(b)** In an inhibition study on mild steel in 1 M H₂SO₄, the following data were obtained at 25 °C. Calculate the synergism parameter $S_1$ and comment on the nature of the interaction.

| System | Corrosion Rate (mm/yr) |
|--------|------------------------|
| Blank (no inhibitor) | 1.500 |
| 0.5 mM Inhibitor A alone | 0.450 |
| 0.5 mM Inhibitor B alone | 0.600 |
| 0.5 mM A + 0.5 mM B (mixture) | 0.180 |

**\[7 Marks\]**

---

### **Solution to Question A(a) — Model Answer \[7 Marks\]**

**Definition \[1 mark\]:** Synergistic inhibition refers to the cooperative action of two or more inhibitors in which the combined inhibition efficiency exceeds the sum of their individual inhibition efficiencies.

**Derivation \[6 marks\]:**

**Step 1:** For two inhibitors adsorbing on the same surface, the fraction of unoccupied sites assuming *independent* competitive adsorption is:
$$1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2$$

**Step 2:** Let $\theta'_1$ be the experimentally observed surface coverage of inhibitor 1 in the mixture. The observed unoccupied surface is $(1 - \theta'_1)$.

**Step 3:** Equating the two:
$$1 - \theta'_1 = 1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:
$$\theta_1 \theta_2 = \theta'_1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + 1$$

**Step 5:** Normalizing by the observed unoccupied fraction $(1 - \theta'_1)$:
$$S_1 = \frac{1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 - \theta'_1}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- $S_1 > 1$ → Positive synergism
- $S_1 = 1$ → Additive
- $S_1 < 1$ → Antagonism

---

### **Solution to Question A(b) — Model Answer \[7 Marks\]**

**Step 1: Calculate individual inhibition efficiencies \[2 marks\]**

$$\eta_A = \frac{1.500 - 0.450}{1.500} \times 100 = 70.00\%$$

$$\eta_B = \frac{1.500 - 0.600}{1.500} \times 100 = 60.00\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate surface coverages \[1 mark\]**

$$\theta_A = 0.7000, \quad \theta_B = 0.6000$$

**Step 3: Calculate the mixture inhibition efficiency \[1 mark\]**

$$\eta_{mix} = \frac{1.500 - 0.180}{1.500} \times 100 = 88.00\%$$

**Step 4: Surface coverage in the mixture \[1 mark\]**

$$\theta'_A = 0.8800$$

**Step 5: Apply the synergism parameter formula \[2 marks\]**

$$S_1 = \frac{1 - 0.7000 - 0.6000 + (0.7000 \times 0.6000)}{1 - 0.8800}$$

$$S_1 = \frac{1 - 1.3000 + 0.4200}{0.1200} = \frac{0.1200}{0.1200} = 1.00$$

**Step 6: Comment \[1 mark\]**

Since $S_1 = 1.00$, the system exhibits a **purely additive effect** — there is no cooperative interaction between inhibitors A and B. The two inhibitors function independently on the metal surface, and the observed 88% efficiency is exactly the sum of the independent contributions (capped at 100%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**
> A common student mistake is to confuse "additive" with "no useful effect". In an additive system ($S = 1$), the **dosage economy is still achieved** because you can use *two cheaper inhibitors* at lower doses instead of *one expensive inhibitor* at a high dose. The KTU examiner specifically tests whether students can distinguish $S = 1$ from $S < 1$ — do not write "no inhibition" for $S = 1$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4 Internal Choice Question B (Alternative)]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**(a)** With suitable examples, classify the different **mechanisms of synergism** between corrosion inhibitors. Explain the cooperative adsorption mechanism in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**
**(b)** Discuss the **Zn²⁺ + Polyphosphate** synergistic inhibitor system. Identify the anodic and cathodic roles, and explain why this combination is widely used in industrial cooling water treatment. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

### **Solution to Question B(a) — Model Answer \[7 Marks\]**

**Classification of Synergism Mechanisms \[5 marks — 1 mark per mechanism + example\]:**

1. **Cooperative Adsorption Synergism:** One inhibitor creates a surface condition that promotes adsorption of the other.
   *Example:* Halide ions (I⁻) + protonated amines on steel in H₂SO₄.

2. **Film-Reinforcement Synergism:** Two inhibitors form interlocking or coalesced films.
   *Example:* Tannin + Zn²⁺ for galvanized steel.

3. **Complex-Formation Synergism:** The two inhibitors form a hydrophobic complex in solution.
   *Example:* 8-Hydroxyquinoline + Zn²⁺ (the Zn–oxine complex).

4. **Electrochemical Pairing Synergism:** One inhibitor controls the anodic reaction; the other controls the cathodic reaction.
   *Example:* Polyphosphate (anodic) + Zn²⁺ (cathodic).

5. **pH-Modification Synergism:** One inhibitor alters local pH to favor the second.
   *Example:* Amines + nitrite on ferrous metals.

**Cooperative Adsorption Mechanism — Detailed Explanation \[2 marks\]:**
In cooperative adsorption, the **first inhibitor** preferentially chemisorbs on the metal surface, often because it has a smaller size or stronger primary interaction. This chemisorption **modifies the surface charge** or **creates polar sites** that then favor the adsorption of the **second inhibitor** (often a larger organic molecule). The two inhibitors are not in competition — they work *sequentially and cooperatively*, producing a tightly packed, often multilayer, protective film with inhibition efficiency far exceeding the linear sum of their individual contributions.

---

### **Solution to Question B(b) — Model Answer \[7 Marks\]**

**Anodic Role — Polyphosphate \[2.5 marks\]:**
- Polyphosphate (sodium hexametaphosphate, Na₅P₃O₁₀) acts as an **anodic inhibitor**.
- It reacts with Fe²⁺ ions at anodic sites to form an **insoluble ferric polyphosphate complex** (the "Todds-Hever" film).
- This complex deposits as a thin, adherent, passivating film on the anodic regions, blocking the anodic dissolution of iron:
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{Polyphosphate} \longrightarrow \text{Fe–Polyphosphate (protective film)}$$

**Cathodic Role — Zn²⁺ \[2.5 marks\]:**
- Zinc ions (typically dosed as ZnSO₄ or ZnCl₂) act as a **cathodic inhibitor**.
- At the cathodic sites where the local pH rises (due to O₂ + H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2OH⁻), Zn²⁺ precipitates as **zinc hydroxide**:
$$\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(OH)}_2 \downarrow$$
- The Zn(OH)₂ film physically blocks the cathodic sites, suppressing the oxygen reduction reaction.

**Why This Combination is Industrially Preferred \[2 marks\]:**
- **Synergy of anodic + cathodic protection:** Both half-reactions are suppressed simultaneously.
- **Low dosage economy:** Total inhibitor concentration can be reduced by 50–70% compared to using either inhibitor alone.
- **Broad pH tolerance:** Effective across pH 6.5–8.5, the typical range of cooling water.
- **Film self-healing:** If the film is mechanically damaged, both inhibitors re-deposit at the freshly exposed anodic and cathodic sites.
- **Compatibility:** Both are inexpensive, water-soluble, and environmentally manageable compared to chromates (which they largely replaced after 1980s environmental regulations).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting the "55.5" factor in $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ calculation** — this is the *single most common* mistake costing 1–2 marks. The 55.5 is the molar concentration of water (mol/L) and converts $K_{ads}$ from a surface-based constant to a thermodynamic activity-based constant.
> 2. **Mixing up $S$ with Inhibition Efficiency** — students often write "S = 87%" instead of "S = 1.33". $S$ is *dimensionless*, not a percentage.
> 3. **Misinterpreting $S = 1$ as "no inhibition"** — the system still inhibits; it simply lacks cooperative interaction.
> 4. **Confusing anodic vs cathodic roles** — Polyphosphate is anodic, Zn²⁺ is cathodic. Reversing these in an exam loses easy marks.
> 5. **Omitting the units** of $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads}$ in numerical problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Synergistic Inhibitors**

- [x] **Synergism** = combined inhibitor effect > sum of individual effects
- [x] **Synergism Parameter $S$** is the *defining* quantitative metric for Module 4
- [x] Aramaki–Hackerman equation: $S_1 = \dfrac{1 - \theta_1 - \theta_2 + \theta_1 \theta_2}{1 - \theta'_1}$
- [x] **Interpretation rule (memorize verbatim):**
  - $S > 1$ → Positive synergism
  - $S = 1$ → Additive
  - $S < 1$ → Antagonism
- [x] **Five mechanistic types:** Cooperative adsorption, Film-reinforcement, Complex-formation, Electrochemical pairing, pH-modification
- [x] **Classic examples to memorize:**
  - Halide (I⁻) + organic amine → cooperative adsorption
  - Zn²⁺ + Polyphosphate → anodic–cathodic pairing (industrial cooling)
  - 8-HQ + Zn²⁺ → complex-formation (the Zn–oxine complex)
  - BTA + KI → halide-mediated chemisorption (copper protection)
- [x] **Inhibition Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0} \times 100$
- [x] **Surface Coverage identity:** $\theta = \eta / 100$
- [x] **Langmuir linear form:** $C/\theta = C + 1/K_{ads}$
- [x] **Gibbs Free Energy (must include 55.5):** $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$
- [x] **Adsorption type rule of thumb:**
  - $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \ge -20$ kJ/mol → Physisorption
  - $\Delta G^{\circ}_{ads} \le -40$ kJ/mol → Chemisorption
- [x] **Industrial relevance:** Synergistic formulations enable **dosage economy** (50–70% reduction in total inhibitor use) compared to single-inhibitor systems.
- [x] **Exam trap to avoid:** Don't confuse "additive" with "useless" — additive systems still offer economic and practical advantages.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scavengers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scavengers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scavengers** (in corrosion control) are chemically active substances that are deliberately dosed into a fluid system (boiler feed water, closed cooling loops, crude oil, gas pipelines, drilling muds, etc.) in order to **reactively remove, neutralize, or "tie-up" the dissolved corrosive species** — most commonly dissolved **oxygen (O₂)**, **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)**, **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**, and **free chlorine (Cl₂)** — that are otherwise responsible for aggressive internal corrosion of the metallic equipment.

In KTU 2024-scheme terminology, scavengers belong to the broader class of **chemical inhibitors** (specifically, the sub-class of *anodic / cathodic neutralizing inhibitors*) that operate by **consumption of the corrosive reactant** rather than by surface film formation on the metal. The product of the scavenging reaction is usually a benign, water-soluble salt, a precipitate, or a gas that can be vented.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture — "The Chemical Sponge"**
> Imagine the dissolved oxygen in your boiler water as a swarm of invisible fire-flies that bite (corrode) the boiler tubes every time they land. A scavenger is like releasing a swarm of **gentle chemical flies that simply grab each fire-fly and convert it into a harmless speck of dust (Na₂SO₄) that can be blown out**. Once the scavenger is "spent," the boiler water is "empty" of fire-flies — the corrosion stops. The chemist must keep adding more scavenger whenever new fire-flies (fresh O₂ leaks) appear.

## 1.3 Classification of Scavengers (High-Level Overview)

| Class | Target Corrodent | Typical Reagents | Typical Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen Scavengers** | Dissolved O₂ | Na₂SO₃, N₂H₄, DEHA, Hydroquinone, Carbohydrazide, Erythorbate | Boilers, Hot well, Feed water |
| **H₂S Scavengers** | H₂S in hydrocarbons | Triazines, Zn-based, Fe-based, Amine-based | Oil & gas pipelines, drilling |
| **CO₂ Scavengers** | Dissolved CO₂ / H₂CO₃ | MEA, MDEA, NaOH, hot potash | Amine units, gas sweetening |
| **Halogen Scavengers** | Cl₂ / HCl | Na₂S₂O₃, NaHSO₃ | Cooling water, pulp & paper |

## 1.4 Why Scavengers are Needed (KTU Syllabus Justification)

- **Thermodynamic compulsion:** Most engineering metals (Fe, Cu, Al) have a *negative free-energy change* for oxidation, i.e. they are unstable in aerated water.
- **Residual corrosion after de-aeration:** Mechanical de-aeration (heating / vacuum) **cannot bring DO down below ~10–20 ppb**, and even that residual is enough to cause pitting. Scavengers bring it down to **< 5 ppb**.
- **Economy & retrofit:** A scavenger is a *single-line addition* to existing plant — cheaper than changing metallurgy or installing stainless cladding.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Take-away:** A scavenger **does NOT deposit a film on the metal**. It works by **depleting the corrosive reactant from the bulk phase**. This is the single most important conceptual distinction between scavengers and conventional *film-forming inhibitors* (amines, phosphates, etc.).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why & How" Behind Scavenging

The chemistry of scavenging can be broken into **four logical steps** that are common to *all* scavenger classes:

1. **Bulk-Phase Diffusion:** The corrosive species (e.g. O₂) is dissolved in the bulk fluid.
2. **Dosing:** A calculated quantity of the scavenger reagent is injected (continuously or shot-dosed) at a point of high turbulence.
3. **Stoichiometric Reaction:** The scavenger reacts **rapidly and irreversibly** with the corrodent to yield a benign product.
4. **Product Removal / Venting:** The reaction product is either bled off, vented as gas, precipitated, or carried harmlessly in solution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "irreversible" matters:** Because the reaction must have a *very large negative ΔG°* and a *high forward rate constant*; if it were reversible the corrodent would simply be re-released and the dose would be wasted.

## 2.2 Detailed Reaction Chemistry

### 2.2.1 Oxygen Scavengers

**A) Sodium Sulphite — Na₂SO₃**
- Reaction: `Na₂SO₃ + ½ O₂ → Na₂SO₄`
- Catalyst commonly added: **Co²⁺ (cobalt sulphate) at ~0.5–2 ppm** to accelerate the otherwise sluggish kinetics below 60 °C.
- *Pros:* Cheap, easy to handle, white crystalline solid.
- *Cons:* Increases TDS (sulphate can re-attack stainless under certain conditions — *caustic gouging* / SCC risk at high pH).

**B) Hydrazine — N₂H₄**
- Reaction: `N₂H₄ + O₂ → N₂ ↑ + 2 H₂O`
- Reaction byproducts: **Inert nitrogen gas** (vented) + pure water — *no TDS addition at all*.
- Also raises pH mildly (gives NH₃): `N₂H₄ → NH₃ + ½ N₂ + ½ H₂` at high T.
- *Pros:* Zero solid residue, gives clean condensate, classic high-pressure boiler reagent.
- *Cons:* **Carcinogenic (Banned in many EU / US plants)**, requires excess dose (typically 2–3 × stoichiometric) because of thermal decomposition above 250 °C.

**C) Diethylhydroxylamine (DEHA) — (C₂H₅)₂NOH**
- Reaction: `2 (C₂H₅)₂NOH + O₂ → 2 (C₂H₅)₂N=O + 2 H₂O` (oxidised to DEHA-nitroxide) — and further to acetaldehyde + ethanol.
- Operates in **mild temperature window 120–180 °C** (ideal for low-to-medium pressure boilers).
- *Pros:* Liquid, easy to dose, also gives some passivation amine effect, *safer* than hydrazine.
- *Cons:* Costlier, slower reaction at very low T (< 90 °C).

**D) Carbohydrazide — (N₂H₃)₂CO**
- Reaction: `(N₂H₃)₂CO + 2 O₂ → 2 N₂ ↑ + 3 H₂O + CO₂` (simplified, real pathway is staged).
- *Pros:* Non-volatile, non-carcinogenic, single-step solid.
- *Cons:* Carbonate is a by-product — slight increase in alkalinity.

**E) Erythorbate (D-iso-ascorbate) and Ascorbic Acid**
- Reaction: `C₆H₈O₆ + ½ O₂ → C₆H₆O₆ + H₂O` (dehydroascorbate)
- *Pros:* Food-grade, biodegradable — popular in *food & pharma* cooling loops.
- *Cons:* Sensitive to pH, degrades above 80 °C.

### 2.2.2 H₂S Scavengers (Oil & Gas Specific)

**A) Triazine-based (1,3,5-tri-(2-hydroxyethyl)-hexahydro-s-triazine, MEA-triazine)**
- Reaction: `R-N(-CH₂CH₂OH)₃ + H₂S → R-N(-CH₂CH₂OH)₂(SH) + HOCH₂CH₂NH-R'`
- Forms **dithiazine** (a non-regenerable, non-corrosive, water-soluble ring compound).
- *Pros:* Liquid, works at 20–80 °C, can be applied in gas phase (injection into gas stream) or contact tower.
- *Cons:* Costly.

**B) Zinc-based (Zinc Oxide / Zinc Carbonate slurry)**
- Reaction: `ZnO + H₂S → ZnS ↓ + H₂O`
- Forms a precipitate that can be filtered out.

**C) Iron-based (Iron sponge — hydrated ferric oxide on wood chips)**
- Reaction: `Fe₂O₃·H₂O + 3 H₂S → Fe₂S₃·H₂O ↓ + 3 H₂O`
- *Pros:* Very cheap, used in upstream gas sweetening.
- *Cons:* Sludge generation, beds must be regenerated.

### 2.2.3 CO₂ Scavengers

- **Alkanolamines (MEA, DEA, MDEA):** `R-NH₂ + H₂O + CO₂ ⇌ R-NH₃⁺ + HCO₃⁻`
- **Hot Potash (K₂CO₃):** `K₂CO₃ + CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ 2 KHCO₃`
- **Caustic Soda:** `NaOH + CO₂ → NaHCO₃`

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values below are **board-exam standard**. Memorise these for the KTU 2024 University Exam.

| # | Quantity / Relation | Symbol / Unit | Key Relation | Remarks / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric ratio of Na₂SO₃ to O₂ | $n_{\text{SO}_3^{2-}}/n_{\text{O}_2}$ | **2 mol Na₂SO₃ per 1 mol O₂** | Equivalent mass of Na₂SO₃ as O₂-scavenger = 8 g / eq |
| 2 | Purity-corrected dose of Na₂SO₃ | $D_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3}$ (g) | $D = 7.88 \times C_{\text{O}_2} \times V$ | where 7.88 = $8 \times 126/32 \times 1/P$ corrected; commonly 7–8 |
| 3 | Stoichiometric ratio of N₂H₄ to O₂ | $n_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4}/n_{\text{O}_2}$ | **1 mol N₂H₄ per 1 mol O₂** | Equiv. mass = 8 g / eq (N₂H₄ MW = 32) |
| 4 | Required N₂H₄ dose (100% basis) | $D_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4}$ (g) | $D = 1.0 \times C_{\text{O}_2} \times V$ | Apply **2–3 × multiplier** for excess & thermal loss |
| 5 | Required DEHA dose (approx) | $D_{\text{DEHA}}$ (g) | $D = 1.6 \times C_{\text{O}_2} \times V$ | Equiv. mass ≈ 13.3 g / eq |
| 6 | Equilibrium constant for O₂ / SO₃²⁻ reaction | $K$ | $K \gg 10^{40}$ at 25 °C | Makes reaction effectively irreversible |
| 7 | ppm → mg/L conversion (water at 1 g/mL) | $C_{\text{ppm}}$ | $1 \text{ ppm} = 1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3$ | Use directly in dose formula |
| 8 | For H₂S: Triazine stoichiometry | $n_{\text{Triazine}}/n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}}$ | **1 mol triazine per 3 mol H₂S** (full substitution) | Equiv. mass of triazine = 246/3 = 82 g / eq H₂S |
| 9 | For H₂S: Zinc-oxide stoichiometry | $n_{\text{ZnO}}/n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}}$ | **1 mol ZnO per 1 mol H₂S** | Equiv. mass = 81.4 g / eq |
| 10 | Sulphite effectiveness test | Residual SO₃²⁻ | Maintain **30–60 ppm excess** | Below 30 ppm = under-dose; > 60 ppm = wastage |
| 11 | N₂H₄ residual test (standard) | Residual N₂H₄ | Maintain **0.05–0.2 ppm** in condensate | Used as on-line chemical indicator |
| 12 | Nernst-equation check (corrosion potential) | $E_{\text{corr}}$ | $E = E^0 - \frac{0.059}{n}\log_{10}\frac{[\text{Red}]}{[\text{Ox}]}$ | After scavenging, O₂ conc. ↓ ⇒ E shifts *negative* (less driving force) |

> **Table-note on pipe-style:** All `|x|`-type absolute values have been rendered as `\vert x \vert` to keep the table syntactically valid. Where concentrations are required inside a magnitude, use $\vert C \vert$ outside the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power-station high-pressure boilers** (250–600 psig): use DEHA / hydrazine / carbohydrazide. Scavenger choice depends on *residence time, pressure and condensate pH*.
- **Once-through nuclear steam generators**: **hydrazine + morpholine blend** (morpholine acts as the amine pH conditioner).
- **Crude oil trunk pipelines**: triazine + solid-bed iron-sponge hybrid for both H₂S and residual O₂.
- **Closed-loop HVAC chilled water**: **sodium erythorbate + tolyltriazole** (TTA) for dissolved O₂ and yellow-metal protection.
- **Sea-water injection lines in offshore platforms**: **DEHA + bio-dispersant**, because O₂ concentration in air-saturated sea-water is ~8 mg/L at 25 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Link to Module 3:** A scavenger is *complementary* to a *film inhibitor*. In a well-designed programme, the scavenger removes bulk O₂ to < 50 ppb, and the film inhibitor (e.g. phosphate / nitrite / molybdate) provides the *last line of defence* at the metal surface.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are worked out **to KTU 14-mark depth**. Each algebraic step is explicitly shown.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Dose of Sodium Sulphite to Remove a Given Mass of Dissolved Oxygen

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark data):** A boiler has a feed-water volume of **25 000 L** with a dissolved oxygen (DO) concentration of **6.5 mg/L**. Calculate:
(a) The mass of pure Na₂SO₃ required to scavenge the DO.
(b) The mass of *commercial* Na₂SO₃ of **90 % purity** to be actually weighed out.
(c) The ppm of SO₃²⁻ that would appear in solution *before* it is oxidised to SO₄²⁻.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the mole ratio.**

From the balanced equation:
- 1 mole of Na₂SO₃ reacts with 0.5 mole of O₂.
- Therefore, the *stoichiometric ratio* = $\dfrac{n_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3}}{n_{\text{O}_2}} = 2$.

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of O₂ in the system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{water}} &= 25\,000 \text{ L} \\
C_{\text{O}_2}   &= 6.5 \text{ mg/L} \\
m_{\text{O}_2}   &= V \times C \\
                 &= 25\,000 \times 6.5 \\
                 &= 162\,500 \text{ mg} \\
                 &= 162.5 \text{ g} \\
                 &= 0.1625 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert mass of O₂ to moles.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{O}_2} &= \frac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{M_{\text{O}_2}} \\
              &= \frac{162.5 \text{ g}}{32 \text{ g/mol}} \\
              &= 5.078 \text{ mol} \\
              &\approx 5.08 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the mole ratio to get moles of Na₂SO₃.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} &= 2 \times n_{\text{O}_2} \\
                           &= 2 \times 5.078 \\
                           &= 10.156 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert moles of Na₂SO₃ to mass (MW of Na₂SO₃ = 126 g/mol).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3,\,100\%} &= n \times M \\
                                   &= 10.156 \times 126 \\
                                   &= 1\,279.66 \text{ g} \\
                                   &\approx 1.28 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Cross-check with the cheat-sheet formula** $D = 7.88 \cdot C_{\text{O}_2} \cdot V$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 7.88 \times 6.5 \times 25\,000 / 1000 \\
  &= 7.88 \times 162.5 / 1000 \\
  &\approx 1.28 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> *(Note: 7.88 is the empirical "dose-factor" combining 2 × 126/32 = 7.875, with purity / safety factor built in.)*

**Step 8 — Adjust for commercial 90 % purity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{commercial}} &= \frac{m_{100\%}}{P} \\
                      &= \frac{1.28}{0.90} \\
                      &= 1.422 \text{ kg} \\
                      &\approx 1.42 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Calculate the transient ppm of SO₃²⁻ before reaction (i.e. once added, all of it is in solution for an instant).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ppm} &= \frac{m_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3,100\%} \times 10^3}{V \text{ (in mL)}} \\
           &= \frac{1279.66 \text{ g} \times 1000 \text{ mg/g}}{25\,000\,000 \text{ mL}} \\
           &= 0.0512 \text{ mg/mL} \\
           &= 51.2 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result-box (Part a–c):**  
> (a) $m_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3,\,100\%} = 1.28 \text{ kg}$  
> (b) $m_{\text{commercial 90 %}} = 1.42 \text{ kg}$  
> (c) Initial $\text{SO}_3^{2-} = 51.2 \text{ ppm}$ — comfortably within the 30–60 ppm excess band.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrazine Dose & Decomposition Loss

**Problem Statement:** The same boiler now operates at 180 °C, and the chemical engineer proposes to switch to **hydrazine (N₂H₄, 100 % basis)**. The DO is again 6.5 mg/L in 25 000 L. Hydrazine decomposes thermally above 150 °C as `N₂H₄ → NH₃ + ½ N₂ + ½ H₂`, losing ~35 % of the dose to decomposition. Compute the **gross (actual) mass of hydrazine hydrate (N₂H₄·H₂O, MW 50, purity 64 %)** that must be pumped in.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Balanced scavenging reaction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 \;+\; \text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{N}_2\uparrow \;+\; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Mole ratio: 1:1 — one mole of N₂H₄ removes one mole of O₂.

**Step 2 — Moles of O₂ (from §3.1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{O}_2} = 5.078 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical moles of N₂H₄ (stoichiometric).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4,\,\text{theor}} &= n_{\text{O}_2} = 5.078 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass of N₂H₄ (MW 32).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4,\,\text{theor}} &= 5.078 \times 32 = 162.5 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply thermal-decomposition loss factor (35 % lost).** Effective fraction = $1 - 0.35 = 0.65$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{N}_2\text{H}_4,\,\text{effective}} &= \frac{m_{\text{theor}}}{0.65} \\
                                             &= \frac{162.5}{0.65} \\
                                             &= 250 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hydrazine hydrate (N₂H₄·H₂O, MW 50) — accounts for the water-of-crystallisation that comes with the commercial liquid.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{hydrate,\,100\%}} &= 250 \times \frac{50}{32} = 390.6 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Apply commercial purity 64 %.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{commercial}} &= \frac{390.6}{0.64} \\
                      &= 610.3 \text{ g} \\
                      &\approx 0.61 \text{ L} \quad \text{(density of 64 \% hydrate} \approx 1.03 \text{ g/mL)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result-box:**  
> Theoretical N₂H₄ = 162.5 g → After 35 % loss correction = 250 g → Hydrate (100 %) = 390.6 g → Commercial 64 % solution ≈ **610 g (≈ 0.61 L)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Triazine Dose for H₂S Removal in a Sour-Crude Pipeline

**Problem Statement:** A crude-oil trunk line carries 1 200 m³/day of crude with a dissolved H₂S content of **45 mg/L** (typical of sour crude). Calculate:
(a) The mass of triazine (1,3,5-tri-(2-hydroxyethyl)-hexahydro-s-triazine, MW = 246 g/mol) required *per day*.
(b) The mass of dithiazine produced *per day* (MW of dithiazine ring = 220 g/mol — *illustrative* — actual product is a 1,3,5-dithiazinane derivative).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Balanced reaction (full substitution of three N atoms).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{R} \;\;+\;\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{S} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R'} \;\;+\;\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

with `R = (HOCH₂CH₂)₃N₃` (the triazine) and `R' = (HS-CH₂CH₂)₃N₃` (the trithio-triazine, *dithiazine analogue*).

**Step 2 — Daily mass of H₂S in the pipeline.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 1200 \text{ m}^3 = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ L} \\
C_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} &= 45 \text{ mg/L} \\
m_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} &= 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 45 = 5.4 \times 10^7 \text{ mg} = 54\,000 \text{ g} = 54 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂S (MW 34).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = \frac{54\,000}{34} = 1588.2 \text{ mol} = 1.588 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of triazine (1 mol : 3 mol H₂S).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Triazine}} = \frac{1.588}{3} = 0.5293 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of triazine (MW 246).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Triazine}} = 0.5293 \times 246 = 130.2 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result-box (Part a):** ≈ **130 kg of triazine/day**.

**Step 6 — Mass of dithiazine (R', MW 220 — ring trimer retaining the three S atoms).**

Mole of dithiazine = same as mole of triazine (1:1 stoichiometry).

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Dithiazine}} = 0.5293 \times 220 = 116.4 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result-box (Part b):** ≈ **116 kg of dithiazine/day** is produced (non-regenerable, must be disposed of as solid / oily sludge).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Which Scavenger to Choose?

| Property | Na₂SO₃ | N₂H₄ | DEHA | Carbohydrazide | Erythorbate | Triazine |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reaction state | Heterogeneous (needs catalyst at low T) | Homogeneous, fast | Homogeneous, moderate | Homogeneous, fast | Homogeneous, fast | Both phases |
| Operating T (°C) | 60–300 | 150–300 | 120–200 | 100–250 | < 80 | 20–80 |
| TDS increase | **High** (Na₂SO₄) | **Zero** (N₂, H₂O) | Low (volatile) | Low–moderate (CO₂) | **Zero** (H₂O) | Moderate (dithiazine) |
| Toxicity / Safety | Safe (food grade) | **Carcinogenic** | Mildly irritant | Safe | Food-grade | Mildly irritant |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 5× | 3× | 2.5× | 4× | 6× |
| Best application | Low-press. boilers | High-press. boilers, nuclear | Medium-press. boilers | General DO control | Food/pharma loops | Oil & gas H₂S |
| Passivation side-effect | No | Slight (NH₃) | **Yes** (amine film) | No | No | No |
| Stoich. O₂ ratio (mol scav / mol O₂) | 2 | 1 | ~1.5–2 | 0.5 | 2 | n/a |

> **KTU Mantra:** *Pick a scavenger by the **3 T's** — **Temperature, Toxicity, TDS***.
