# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Questions are patterned on the KTU 2024 Scheme University Exam (ESE) pattern: **Part A (3 marks each) and Part B (14 marks each, with internal choice between A and B)**. All numerical data has been chosen to be solvable with a single A4 page of working.

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, PEICT866]`
**Define a scavenger as used in corrosion control. With a balanced chemical equation, explain how sodium sulphite functions as an oxygen scavenger in boiler feed water.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words / 3-mark depth):**

A scavenger is a chemical additive that **reactively removes the dissolved corrosive species** (typically O₂, H₂S, CO₂) from the bulk fluid, rather than forming a film on the metal. Sodium sulphite scavenges dissolved oxygen according to the following **balanced reaction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4
\end{aligned}
$$

The reaction is catalysed by **cobalt sulphate (Co²⁺ ~ 1 ppm)** to overcome its slow kinetics below 60 °C. The product, Na₂SO₄, increases TDS but is non-corrosive at the residual dose levels maintained (30–60 ppm excess SO₃²⁻). The reaction effectively reduces DO from 50–200 ppb down to **< 5 ppb**, preventing pitting attack on boiler tubes.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, PEICT866]`
**List any three advantages and any two limitations of using hydrazine as a high-pressure boiler oxygen scavenger. Why is it being phased out in modern plants?**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words / 3-mark depth):**

**Advantages of N₂H₄:**

1. **Zero solid residue** — products are N₂ gas and pure H₂O, so no TDS increase.
2. **Volatile — carries over with steam** — protects the *condensate* return lines as well.
3. **Mildly alkaline** — raises pH, providing supplementary corrosion protection.

**Limitations of N₂H₄:**

1. **Highly toxic and carcinogenic** (IARC Group 2A / 2B) — requires stringent PPE and handling protocols.
2. **Thermal decomposition above 250 °C** — needs 2–3 × stoichiometric excess dose; operating cost is high.
3. Slow reaction rate at low temperatures (< 120 °C) — must be pre-heated in storage.

**Why it is being phased out:** *Regulatory pressure* (REACH in EU, OSHA in US) plus the *availability of safer solid alternatives* (carbohydrazide, erythorbate, DEHA) that give comparable performance with much lower toxicity and handling risk.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, internal choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Module-level Part-B questions have **internal choice**: the student answers **either Question A or Question B** from the same module. The 14 marks are *usually split 7 + 7 across two sub-parts*.

---

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, PEICT866, Module 4]`

**(a)** With a **block-flow diagram**, describe the *functional architecture* of a typical boiler-feed-water treatment programme that combines mechanical de-aeration, oxygen scavenging, and a film-forming inhibitor. Clearly indicate the **DO specification at each stage** and **the role of the scavenger** versus the film inhibitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-pressure boiler has a feed-water tank of **18 000 L** with a dissolved oxygen content of **7.2 mg/L** after mechanical de-aeration. Compute:
1. The mass of **pure sodium sulphite** required to scavenge the DO.
2. The mass of **commercial Na₂SO₃ (92 % purity)** to be actually dosed.
3. The **concentration in ppm of the resulting Na₂SO₄** in the boiler water *after* the reaction is complete (assume uniform mixing, no blowdown).
4. Comment on whether this residual Na₂SO₄ (≈ TDS increase) is acceptable for a low-pressure (< 10 bar) boiler. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

A block-flow diagramme is drawn (use Diagram 4.2 above). The examiner expects the following text content:

- **Mechanical de-aeration stage** (vacuum / heating to 105 °C): removes ~80–90 % of inlet DO; **DO at outlet ≈ 50–200 ppb**.
- **Scavenger injection point** (typically at the *suction of the boiler-feed pump*): a calculated stoichiometric dose of Na₂SO₃ / N₂H₄ / DEHA is pumped. The reaction is **bulk-phase**, and the **role of the scavenger is to *deplete* the DO to < 5 ppb.**
- **Film-inhibitor dosing point** (downstream of the scavenger, sometimes combined): phosphate / nitrite / molybdate deposits a *thin passivating film* on the tube surface, providing a *second line of defence* for any residual DO that survives scavenging.
- **Residual test station** (analyser): maintains 30–60 ppm SO₃²⁻ excess (sulphite) or 0.05–0.2 ppm N₂H₄.
- **Blowdown**: removes concentrated Na₂SO₄ or volatile alkaline products.

> **Examiner's key words to use in answer:** *"bulk-phase consumption"* vs *"surface film formation"* — these phrases alone will earn 2 marks.

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Mass of O₂ in 18 000 L at 7.2 mg/L.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{O}_2} &= 18\,000 \times 7.2 = 129\,600 \text{ mg} = 129.6 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of O₂ (MW 32).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{O}_2} &= \frac{129.6}{32} = 4.05 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric moles of Na₂SO₃ (2 mol : 1 mol O₂).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3} = 2 \times 4.05 = 8.10 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of pure Na₂SO₃ (MW 126).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{pure}} = 8.10 \times 126 = 1\,020.6 \text{ g} \approx 1.02 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (1): $m_{\text{pure}} \approx 1.02 \text{ kg}$.**

**Step 5 — Adjust for 92 % commercial purity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{commercial}} = \frac{1.02}{0.92} = 1.109 \text{ kg} \approx 1.11 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (2): $m_{\text{commercial}} \approx 1.11 \text{ kg}$.**

**Step 6 — Concentration of Na₂SO₄ produced.**

Stoichiometry: 2 mol Na₂SO₃ → 2 mol Na₂SO₄, so moles of Na₂SO₄ = 8.10 mol. MW of Na₂SO₄ = 142 g/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4} &= 8.10 \times 142 = 1\,150.2 \text{ g} = 1.15 \text{ kg} \\
\text{ppm} &= \frac{1\,150.2 \text{ g} \times 1000 \text{ mg/g}}{18\,000\,000 \text{ mL}} = 0.0639 \text{ mg/mL} = 63.9 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (3): Residual Na₂SO₄ ≈ 64 ppm.**

**Step 7 — Engineering comment.**

64 ppm of Na₂SO₄ in a low-pressure boiler (< 10 bar) is **acceptable**, because:
- TDS tolerance for low-pressure boilers is typically 1 000–3 500 ppm.
- 64 ppm contributes < 2 % of TDS budget.
- However, in **high-pressure (> 100 bar) boilers**, this level of Na₂SO₄ (sodium salt) may cause **caustic gouging / SCC** at stressed tube bends, and a *volatile* scavenger (DEHA / N₂H₄) would be preferred.

> **Answer (4):** Acceptable for low-pressure; *not* for high-pressure — use DEHA / N₂H₄ / carbohydrazide.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, PEICT866, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **chemistry of H₂S scavenging using triazine** in oil and gas pipelines. With a balanced chemical equation, derive the **daily dose of triazine** required for a pipeline carrying 800 m³/day of crude with **65 mg/L dissolved H₂S**. (MW triazine = 246 g/mol; stoich 1 mol triazine : 3 mol H₂S). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **the limitations of triazine** as a scavenger and compare it with **iron-sponge and zinc-oxide** alternatives. Use a **comparison table** of at least four attributes. Comment on the **disposal challenge** of dithiazine. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Reaction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(HOCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_3\text{N}_3 \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{S} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{(HSCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{)}_3\text{N}_3 \;+\; 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

The reaction is a **nucleophilic substitution** of the three hydroxyl arms with H₂S, releasing water and producing the trithio-triazine (*dithiazine analogue*). The reaction is essentially **irreversible** in the temperature range 20–80 °C and is non-catalysed.

**Step 2 — Daily H₂S mass in the pipeline.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 800 \text{ m}^3 = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ L} \\
m_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} &= 8 \times 10^5 \times 65 = 5.2 \times 10^7 \text{ mg} = 52\,000 \text{ g} = 52 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of H₂S (MW 34).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = \frac{52\,000}{34} = 1529.4 \text{ mol} = 1.529 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Moles of triazine (1 : 3).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{Triazine}} = \frac{1.529}{3} = 0.5097 \text{ kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of triazine per day (MW 246).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Triazine}} = 0.5097 \times 246 = 125.4 \text{ kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **125 kg of triazine per day**.

> **Excess dose (industry practice):** Add **20 % margin** → $125.4 \times 1.2 \approx 150 \text{ kg/day}$ as a *realistic operating dose*.

---

##### Part (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table — Triazine vs Iron-Sponge vs ZnO:**

| Attribute | Triazine (MEEA-triazine) | Iron Sponge (Fe₂O₃·H₂O on chips) | Zinc Oxide Slurry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase applied | Liquid — injected | Solid bed in contactor | Solid slurry in contactor |
| Operating T | 20–80 °C | 20–50 °C | 30–100 °C |
| Regenerability | **Non-regenerable** | Yes — air-revivable | No |
| Capital cost | Low (pump + tank) | High (large bed) | Medium |
| Operating cost | High (reagent) | Low (Fe₂O₃ cheap) | Medium (ZnO cost) |
| Disposal challenge | High — dithiazine oily sludge | Low — re-usable bed | Medium — ZnS sludge |
| H₂S slip achieved | < 4 ppm (sweet spec) | 1–5 ppm | < 4 ppm |
| By-product toxicity | Mild | Fe₂S₃ is benign | ZnS is mildly toxic |

**Limitations of Triazine:**

1. *Expensive reagent* (≈ 6 × the cost of Na₂SO₃ on a per-kg-of-corrodent basis).
2. *Non-regenerable* — the dithiazine cannot be thermally or chemically regenerated; it must be sent for hazardous-waste disposal.
3. *Sludge disposal* — dithiazine is a viscous oily solid, classified as **hazardous waste** under most EPA / MoEFCC schedules. It contains residual hydrocarbons and un-reacted triazine, both of which can create leachate problems in landfills.
4. *Cannot handle very high H₂S* (> 5 000 ppm) on its own — must be paired with a *solid scavenger bed* upstream.

**Disposal challenge of dithiazine:**
- It is *not biodegradable* within the standard 28-day BOD test.
- It can leach H₂S under acidic landfill conditions, defeating the purpose of scavenging.
- Industry practice: **solidification with cement / fly-ash** followed by **secure landfilling**; research continues on **thermal oxidation at 800 °C** to convert it to SO₂ (sent to scrubber) and CO₂.

> **Examiner's bottom-line answer:** *Triazine is preferred when the H₂S load is moderate and the operator needs a clean liquid-injection scheme without solid-bed handling. For high-load, continuous-duty sour-gas service, the iron-sponge bed is still the workhorse despite its bulky size.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4 — Scavengers (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Forgetting the stoichiometric factor of 2 for Na₂SO₃.** It is the *single most common error* — students write 1 mol : 1 mol. The half-oxygen coefficient (½ O₂) is the cause.
> 2. **Mixing up "scavenger" with "film inhibitor."** If a question asks *"What is the role of a scavenger?"* and you write *"it forms a film on the metal,"* you will score **zero** for the conceptual part, even if the rest of your answer is perfect.
> 3. **Ignoring the catalyst.** The reaction Na₂SO₃ + ½O₂ is *slow* below 60 °C; Co²⁺ catalyst at 1 ppm is **mandatory** in any low-temperature design. Examiners test this in Q2/PART-A frequently.
> 4. **Purity of commercial reagent not applied.** When 90 % or 64 % commercial grade is mentioned, students often forget the *division by purity*. Loss of 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 5. **Unit confusion in ppm ↔ mg/L ↔ g/m³.** The three are numerically equal **only in dilute aqueous solutions** (density ≈ 1 g/mL). Always state the assumption.
> 6. **Disposal of by-products** — examiners now ask *environmental* follow-ups. A perfect chemistry answer that ignores the dithiazine disposal problem will be docked 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting the catalyst and T-dependence of hydrazine** — saying "hydrazine works at all temperatures" is wrong. Above 250 °C it decomposes; below 120 °C the kinetics are sluggish.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin to Wall):**

- **Definition:** Scavenger = chemical that *consumes the corrosive reactant* (O₂, H₂S, CO₂) from the bulk fluid.
- **Key distinction:** Scavenger = bulk-phase; Film inhibitor = surface phase. Never confuse the two in the exam.
- **Na₂SO₃ + ½O₂ → Na₂SO₄** (requires Co²⁺ catalyst < 60 °C; dose = 7.88 × C_O₂ × V).
- **N₂H₄ + O₂ → N₂ + 2H₂O** (zero TDS; volatile; carcinogenic — being phased out).
- **DEHA** = (C₂H₅)₂NOH; 120–180 °C window; *less toxic* alternative to hydrazine.
- **Carbohydrazide** (CHZ) — safer solid; 100–250 °C; produces some CO₂.
- **Erythorbate / Ascorbate** — food-grade, T < 80 °C only.
- **H₂S scavengers** — Triazine (1 : 3), Iron sponge (regenerable), ZnO (sludge).
- **CO₂ scavengers** — Alkanolamines (MEA, DEA, MDEA), Hot Potash (K₂CO₃), NaOH.
- **Residual tests:** SO₃²⁻ 30–60 ppm; N₂H₄ 0.05–0.2 ppm; H₂S slip < 4 ppm (sweet spec).
- **3 T's to pick a scavenger:** Temperature, Toxicity, TDS.
- **Stoich. ratios to memorise:** 2 (Na₂SO₃), 1 (N₂H₄), 1.6 (DEHA), 0.5 (Carbohydrazide), 2 (Erythorbate), 1/3 (Triazine per H₂S), 1 (ZnO per H₂S).
- **Environmental angle:** Dithiazine is hazardous; Na₂SO₄ increases TDS; carbohydrazide yields CO₂ → carbon-footprint.
- **Numerical magic numbers:** MW(Na₂SO₃)=126, MW(N₂H₄)=32, MW(O₂)=32, MW(H₂S)=34, MW(Triazine)=246, dose factor 7.88, 64 % commercial hydrazine hydrate.
- **Engineering practice:** Mechanical de-aeration (80–90 % removal) + Scavenger (50–200 ppb → < 5 ppb) + Film inhibitor (last-line barrier).
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting the ½ O₂ coefficient in the sulphite reaction — leads to *half* the correct answer (loss of 1–2 marks per question).
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing units — ppm ≠ mg/L in *all* fluids; it equals mg/L only in dilute aqueous solutions of density 1 g/mL.
- **Common pitfall:** "Reagent dose" is the *stoichiometric* value; "actual dose" must include *excess factor* (typically 1.2–3 ×).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neutralisers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neutralisers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛡️ Neutralisers in Corrosion Control — Module 4

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Neutralisers (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Neutralisers* are **alkaline chemical additives** deliberately dosed into corrosive aqueous media (such as boiler feed water, steam condensate lines, oil-field brine, and closed cooling loops) to **neutralise acidic corrosive species** (chiefly dissolved CO₂, H₂S, mineral acids, and acidic corrosion by-products), thereby shifting the bulk pH into a passive, corrosion-resistant regime (typically **pH 8.5 – 9.5**).

In KTU Board parlance, neutralisation is treated as the **first line of chemical defence** before any inhibitor film is established. The principle obeys the classical acid–base reaction:

$$\text{Acid} + \text{Base} \longrightarrow \text{Salt} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The choice of neutraliser is dictated by:
1. **Volatility** (whether it travels with steam).
2. **Basic strength** (pKa of conjugate acid).
3. **Thermal stability** inside boilers (up to **~300 °C**).
4. **Toxicity / handling safety**.

### 🔑 Real-World Analogy — The "Antacid Tablet" Metaphor

Imagine your stomach producing excess HCl (acid reflux). You chew an *Eno* or *ENO tablet* (a base) which chemically neutralises the HCl, generating harmless salt + water + CO₂, and you feel relief.

Exactly the same thing happens inside a **steam boiler**: dissolved **CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻** makes the condensate acidic (pH ≈ 4.5), which eats away carbon steel pipes. When we dose **morpholine** or **ammonia**, it acts like an "antacid tablet" travelling with the steam and neutralising the acid *exactly where it condenses* — saving the condensate return lines from pin-hole corrosion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight**
> Neutralisers are **NOT** inhibitors — they do not form a film. They merely **raise pH** so that the cathodic hydrogen-evolution reaction becomes thermodynamically sluggish and iron stays passive.

---

## 2. Classification of Neutralisers (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically lists the following families. Memorise the table — it appears in 2-mark questions almost every exam cycle.

| **Class** | **Example** | **State** | **Volatility with Steam** | **Typical Dose (ppm)** | **Best Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatile Alkali** | Ammonia (NH₃) | Gas / Aqueous | **High** | 0.5 – 2.0 | Low-pressure boilers, condensate |
| **Volatile Amine** | Morpholine (C₄H₉NO) | Liquid | **Moderate** | 1.0 – 5.0 | High-pressure boilers |
| **Volatile Amine** | Cyclohexylamine (C₆H₁₁NH₂) | Liquid | **Moderate** | 1.0 – 3.0 | Distribution steam lines |
| **Filming Amine** | Octadecylamine (ODA) | Solid (waxy) | **Low** | 1.0 – 10.0 | Storage tanks, condensate lines |
| **Non-volatile Alkali** | NaOH, Na₂CO₃, Ca(OH)₂ | Solid / Liquid | **Negligible** | 5 – 50 | Boiler drum water only |
| **Phosphate Buffer** | Na₃PO₄ | Solid | **Negligible** | 5 – 20 | High-pressure boiler drum |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** *"MOCA"* — **M**orpholine, **O**DA, **C**yclohexylamine, **A**mmonia — the four "volatile" neutralisers frequently asked in viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 3. The Chemistry Behind Neutralisation

### 3.1 The Corrosion Problem (Without Neutraliser)

In steam–condensate systems, the silent culprit is **carbonic acid** formed from dissolved CO₂:

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$$

$$\text{HCO}_3^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{CO}_3^{2-}$$

The liberated **H⁺ ions** drive the cathodic reaction at mild-steel surfaces:

$$2\text{H}^+ + \text{Fe} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow$$

This produces the classic **"grooving" corrosion** at the 6-o'clock position of horizontal condensate pipes.

### 3.2 How Neutralisers Work (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Dose injection:** Volatile amine / ammonia is metered into the feed-water line.
2. **Distribution:** Because it is volatile, it vaporises with steam and travels to every remote point in the system.
3. **Condensation point reaction:** At first condensation, the amine dissolves preferentially and elevates the local pH.
4. **Acid neutralisation:** It reacts with H₂CO₃ / H⁺ → preventing H⁺ from attacking Fe.
5. **Re-evaporation:** Any unreacted amine re-evaporates and continues to protect downstream pipework.

### 3.3 Stoichiometric Dose Calculation

The **theoretical dose** of any amine is given by:

$$D_{\text{amine}} = \frac{M_{\text{amine}} \times C_{\text{CO}_2}}{n \times 10^6} \quad \text{(kg per kg of water)}$$

Where:
- $M_{\text{amine}}$ = Molecular weight of the amine (kg/kmol)
- $C_{\text{CO}_2}}$ = Concentration of CO₂ in feed water (ppm)
- $n$ = Number of acidic protons neutralised per mole of amine (= 1 for mono-amines)

### 3.4 Distribution Ratio (K) — Selection Criterion

The **distribution ratio** $K$ defines how a volatile amine partitions between steam and water at operating temperature:

$$K = \frac{C_{\text{steam}}}{C_{\text{water}}}$$

| **Amine** | **pKa** | **K (≈100 °C)** | **Behaviour** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ammonia | **9.25** | ~10 | More in steam → uniform protection |
| Morpholine | **8.36** | ~0.4–0.6 | More in condensate → first-condensation protection |
| Cyclohexylamine | **10.66** | ~2–4 | Lean toward steam phase |
| Octadecylamine | — | <0.01 | Stays in liquid → film former |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Question on "Why morpholine is preferred over ammonia in high-pressure boilers?" — answer is that morpholine's **lower K** keeps it in the condensate phase longer, giving sustained protection.

---

## 📐 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print This)

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Variables / Units** | **Where Used** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acid–base stoichiometry | $N_a V_a = N_b V_b$ | Normality (eq/L), Volume (L) | Lab neutralisation |
| Theoretical amine dose | $D = \dfrac{M \cdot C}{n \cdot 10^6}$ | kg amine / kg water | Engineering design |
| pH from $[\text{H}^+]$ | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | mol/L | All neutralisation calcs |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\dfrac{[\text{Base}]}{[\text{Acid}]}$ | dimensionless | Buffer design |
| Henderson equation (corrosion rate) | $C_R = K \cdot \dfrac{[\text{H}^+]^n}{[\text{Inhibitor}]^m}$ | mpy | Inhibitor efficiency |
| Distribution ratio | $K = C_s / C_w$ | ppm/ppm | Amine selection |

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Neutralisers protect turbine blades and condensate lines from acid grooving.
- **Oil & Gas:** Methanol pipelines use morpholine to neutralise H₂S/CO₂ co-produced with crude.
- **Refrigeration:** Ammonia is used as a neutraliser *and* refrigerant — dual role.
- **Nuclear Steam Generators:** Morpholine + hydrazine combo prevents both acid corrosion and oxygen pitting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

## 4. Worked Numerical 1 (KTU 2024 Expected Pattern)

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A low-pressure boiler has feed water containing **12 ppm of dissolved CO₂**. Calculate the theoretical dose of **morpholine (C₄H₉NO)** required to neutralise the acid. Given: Molecular weight of morpholine = **87.12 g/mol**, and one mole of morpholine neutralises **one mole of H⁺**.

### 🛠️ Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert CO₂ to molar basis**

Assume 1 kg of feed water = 1 L (≈). 12 ppm = 12 mg CO₂ per kg water.

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}}{44 \text{ g/mol}} = 2.727 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of H⁺ produced**

From the equilibria, each mole of CO₂ ultimately yields **2 moles of H⁺** in the fully ionised limit (but for neutraliser design we conservatively use **n = 1** equivalence for weak acids — KTU convention).

$$n_{\text{H}^+} = 2.727 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol per kg water}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the dose formula**

$$D_{\text{morpholine}} = \frac{M_{\text{morpholine}} \times C_{\text{CO}_2}}{n \times 10^6}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{morpholine}} &= \frac{87.12 \text{ g/mol} \times 12 \text{ ppm}}{1 \times 10^6} \\
&= \frac{87.12 \times 12}{10^6} \\
&= 1.045 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g per kg water} \\
&= 1.045 \text{ mg per kg water} \\
&= 1.045 \text{ ppm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Practical dose with 1.5× safety factor**

$$D_{\text{actual}} = 1.5 \times 1.045 = 1.57 \text{ ppm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Theoretical dose ≈ **1.05 ppm morpholine**; Practical dose ≈ **1.57 ppm**.

---

## 5. Worked Numerical 2 — Selection of Neutraliser via Distribution Ratio

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A high-pressure boiler operates at **180 °C**. Compare the suitability of **ammonia** (K = 10) and **morpholine** (K = 0.5) for condensate-line protection if the condensate pH is to remain above **8.5**. State which is preferable and justify.

### 🛠️ Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the requirement**

A neutraliser that stays dissolved in condensate longer will keep pH elevated **at the point of attack** (i.e., where steam condenses).

**Step 2 — Interpret K**

$$K_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{C_{\text{steam}}}{C_{\text{water}}} = 10$$

This means ammonia is **10× more concentrated in steam than in water** → it largely escapes with the steam and is **lost** from condensate. Protection is short-lived.

$$K_{\text{morpholine}} = \frac{C_{\text{steam}}}{C_{\text{water}}} = 0.5$$

Morpholine is **2× more concentrated in water than in steam** → it **lingers** in condensate, giving prolonged alkaline protection.

**Step 3 — Decision**

$$\boxed{\text{Morpholine is preferred for high-pressure boilers.}}$$

**Reason:** Morpholine's low distribution ratio ensures it remains in the liquid phase at first condensation, neutralising H₂CO₃ exactly at the corrosion-prone pipe wall.

---

## 6. Worked Numerical 3 — pH Verification after Dosing

### 📋 Problem Statement
> A condensate line has $[\text{H}^+] = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ M (pH = 5). After dosing morpholine, the pH rises to **8.7**. Verify the new $[\text{H}^+]$ and comment on whether this pH is safe for carbon-steel condensate piping.

### 🛠️ Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert pH → H⁺**

$$[\text{H}^+]_{\text{new}} = 10^{-8.7} = 2.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Compare against safe limit**

KTU safe range for carbon steel in condensate: **pH 8.0 – 9.5**.

Since $8.0 \le 8.7 \le 9.5$, the system is in the **safe passive zone**.

**Step 3 — Reduction in H⁺ activity**

$$\text{Reduction factor} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{2.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 5000\times$$

$$\boxed{\text{System is safe. Final pH} = 8.7.}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Diagram — Neutraliser Selection Flow

## 7. Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Corrosive Aqueous Medium] --> B{Identify Acidic Species}
    B -- CO2 / H2CO3 --> C[Volatile Amine Required]
    B -- Mineral Acid --> D[Non-Volatile Alkali]
    B -- H2S --> E[Amine + Scavenger Combo]
    C --> F{Operating Pressure?}
    F -- Low < 10 bar --> G[Ammonia]
    F -- High > 40 bar --> H[Morpholine]
    F -- Distribution Network --> I[Cyclohexylamine]
    G --> J[Dose Calculation via Stoichiometry]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D --> J
    E --> J
    J --> K[pH Verification 8.5 to 9.5]
    K --> L{Passive Film Stable?}
    L -- Yes --> M[System Protected]
    L -- No --> N[Re-dose or Add Buffer]
    N --> J
```

## 8. Schematic — Mechanism of Volatile Neutralisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BOILER[Boiler Drum]
        S1[Steam + NH3 / Morpholine vapour]
    end
    subgraph PIPE[Steam Pipeline]
        S2[Volatile neutraliser travels]
    end
    subgraph COND[Condensate Formation Zone]
        S3[Neutraliser condenses FIRST]
        S4[Acid CO2 H2O also condenses]
    end
    subgraph RXN[Neutralisation Reaction]
        S5[Amine + H2CO3 to Salt + H2O]
    end
    subgraph PROT[Protected Metal Wall]
        S6[pH rises to 8.5 to 9.5]
        S7[Carbon steel remains PASSIVE]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3
    S4 --> RXN
    S3 --> RXN --> S6 --> S7
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read the second diagram in sequence:** Steam carries the amine → amine dissolves in the *first* droplet of condensate → pH jumps *before* the carbonic acid can attack the metal wall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 9. Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a neutraliser. How is it different from a corrosion inhibitor?**

**Model Answer:**
A neutraliser is an alkaline chemical (e.g., ammonia, morpholine, Na₂CO₃) added to a corrosive aqueous medium to neutralise acidic species (CO₂, H₂S, mineral acids) and raise the pH into the passive region (8.5–9.5).

**Difference from inhibitor:**
- *Neutraliser:* Acts by bulk **acid–base reaction**; raises pH; no film formation.
- *Inhibitor:* Acts by **adsorbing on metal surface** and forming a protective barrier; often pH-independent.

*(3 marks: 1 definition + 2 differences)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Why is morpholine preferred over ammonia for high-pressure boilers?**

**Model Answer:**
Morpholine has a **lower distribution ratio (K ≈ 0.5)** between steam and water compared to ammonia (K ≈ 10). This means morpholine **preferentially condenses into the liquid phase** with the first drop of condensate, providing sustained alkaline protection at the most corrosion-prone location. Ammonia, being highly volatile, escapes with steam and provides only transient protection.

*(3 marks: K-value reasoning + 1-line conclusion)*

---

## 10. Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

> **(a)** Classify neutralisers used in corrosion control. Discuss in detail the mechanism by which volatile amines protect condensate lines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (3 marks):**

| Class | Example |
|---|---|
| Volatile alkalis | Ammonia |
| Volatile amines | Morpholine, Cyclohexylamine |
| Filming amines | Octadecylamine |
| Non-volatile alkalis | NaOH, Na₂CO₃ |
| Phosphate buffers | Na₃PO₄ |

**Mechanism (4 marks):**
1. Amine is dosed into feed-water.
2. It vaporises with steam because of its volatility (vapour pressure > water at boiler T).
3. Travels with steam to every part of the system.
4. At first condensation, it dissolves preferentially due to its basic nature (lone pair on N).
5. Reacts with H₂CO₃ → salt + H₂O, raising condensate pH.
6. Cathodic reaction 2H⁺ + Fe → suppressed because [H⁺] is reduced by 3–4 orders of magnitude.
7. Carbon steel attains passivity.

---

> **(b)** A boiler feed water contains **20 ppm of CO₂**. Calculate the dose of **cyclohexylamine (M = 99.18 g/mol)** required for neutralisation. Assume **n = 1**. Also calculate the dose in **kg/day** if the boiler handles **50,000 L of water per day**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the dose formula**

$$D = \frac{M \cdot C_{\text{CO}_2}}{n \cdot 10^6} = \frac{99.18 \times 20}{1 \times 10^6} = 1.983 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to ppm**

$$D = 1.98 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 3 — Practical dose with safety factor 1.3×**

$$D_{\text{actual}} = 1.3 \times 1.98 = 2.575 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kg/day**

Mass of feed water per day = 50,000 kg (≈)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of amine per day} &= 2.575 \times 10^{-6} \times 50{,}000 \\
&= 0.129 \text{ kg/day} \\
&\approx 129 \text{ g/day}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{D_{\text{cyclohexylamine}} \approx 1.98 \text{ ppm}; \quad \text{Total} = 0.129 \text{ kg/day}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B `[KTU University Exam – June 2022 Model]`

> **(a)** Explain the role of **filming amines** such as octadecylamine in corrosion control. Compare their action with that of volatile amines. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of ODA (4 marks):**
- Long-chain (C₁₈) amine with polar –NH₂ head and non-polar hydrocarbon tail.
- Polar head adsorbs on metal surface; tail forms a hydrophobic film.
- Film is **water-repellent**, so corrosive ions cannot reach the metal.
- Effective in condensate storage tanks and once-through systems.

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Volatile amine | Filming amine |
|---|---|---|
| Action | Neutralises acid | Forms physical barrier |
| Mechanism | Acid–base chemistry | Adsorption + hydrophobic film |
| Dose | 0.5 – 5 ppm | 1 – 10 ppm |
| Volatility | High | Negligible |
| Application | Steam–condensate | Storage tanks, pipelines |

---

> **(b)** The condensate from a steam line has a pH of **5.2**. Calculate the $[\text{H}^+]$ concentration. If **10 ppm of ammonia (NH₃, M = 17 g/mol)** is added, estimate the new pH assuming all NH₃ converts to NH₄⁺. Discuss whether this pH is acceptable for carbon-steel piping. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Initial H⁺**

$$[\text{H}^+]_i = 10^{-5.2} = 6.31 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of NH₃ added per litre**

$$n_{\text{NH}_3} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g/L}}{17 \text{ g/mol}} = 5.88 \times 10^{-7} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 3 — Remaining H⁺ after stoichiometric neutralisation**

$$\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{NH}_4^+$$

$$[\text{H}^+]_f = 6.31 \times 10^{-6} - 5.88 \times 10^{-7} = 5.72 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 4 — Final pH**

$$\text{pH}_f = -\log(5.72 \times 10^{-6}) = 5.24$$

**Step 5 — Comment**

Since 5.24 is **far below** the safe passive range (8.0–9.5), the dose of 10 ppm NH₃ is **insufficient**. The required dose to achieve pH 8.5 would be much higher, or a stronger neutraliser (morpholine with buffer action) should be used.

$$\boxed{\text{Final pH} \approx 5.24 \text{ — UNSAFE; increase dose or switch to morpholine.}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** — Board expects 1.2× to 1.5× on theoretical dose; skipping it costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing K with volatility** — Many write "high K = high volatility = best"; actually **low K is best for condensate**.
> 3. **Mixing units** — ppm vs mg/L vs g/L conversions trip up 30% of students. Always state units explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring the practical safety range pH 8.0–9.5** — final answer must be *compared* against this KTU-specified band.
> 5. **Drawing chemical equations with no balancing** — KTU strict on mass/charge balance in stoichiometric derivations.

---

## 11. 🚀 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Neutraliser** = alkaline additive that **neutralises acid**, raises **pH to 8.5–9.5**.
- **NOT** the same as inhibitor — neutraliser = bulk chemistry; inhibitor = surface film.
- **Volatile neutralisers** (NH₃, morpholine, CHA, ODA) **travel with steam** → protect remote condensate lines.
- **Non-volatile neutralisers** (NaOH, Na₂CO₃, Na₃PO₄) **stay in boiler drum** → control boiler-water pH.
- **Distribution ratio K** = $C_{\text{steam}} / C_{\text{water}}$ — *low K* = better condensate protection (morpholine).
- **pKa trend:** CHA (10.66) > NH₃ (9.25) > Morpholine (8.36) → CHA strongest base.
- **Dose formula:** $D = \dfrac{M_{\text{amine}} \times C_{\text{CO}_2}}{n \times 10^6}$ — multiply by **1.3–1.5×** for practical dosing.
- **Filming amine ODA** = physical barrier, not pH chemistry; used in storage tanks.
- **KTU safe band:** pH **8.0 – 9.5** for carbon steel condensate.
- **Mnemonic "MOCA":** **M**orpholine, **O**DA, **C**yclohexylamine, **A**mmonia — the four flagship neutralisers.
- **Selection rule:** High-pressure boiler → morpholine; Low-pressure → ammonia; Distribution network → CHA; Storage → ODA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scale Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scale Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Scale Inhibitors** are specialty chemical additives — typically organic or organophosphorus compounds — dosed in trace concentrations (usually **1–20 mg/L**) into aqueous industrial systems (cooling water, boiler feed water, desalination plants, and oilfield water-handling systems) to prevent or disrupt the precipitation, crystallization, adhesion, and deposition of sparingly soluble inorganic salts (most commonly **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃), calcium sulfate (CaSO₄·2H₂O), magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)₂), calcium phosphate (Ca₃(PO₄)₂), barium/strontium sulfate (BaSO₄ / SrSO₄), and silica (SiO₂)**) onto heat-transfer surfaces, pipelines, and process equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Scale inhibitors operate via four principal mechanisms — (i) **Threshold Inhibition**, (ii) **Crystal Modification / Distortion**, (iii) **Chelation / Sequestration**, and (iv) **Dispersion**. A single inhibitor molecule may simultaneously act via more than one mechanism, making the chemistry highly synergistic in multi-component industrial water formulations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Bodyguard"

Imagine your boiler tubes as the tiled floor of a swimming pool. As hard water (rich in dissolved Ca²⁺ and CO₃²⁻ ions) passes through and is heated, the dissolved minerals "want" to settle out and harden into a chalky white crust — just like limescale in a domestic kettle.

Now imagine hiring a team of molecular **"bodyguards"** (the scale inhibitors) that patrol the pipe walls. These bodyguards:
1. **Whisper sweet nothings** to the calcium and carbonate ions (chelation/threshold effect) so they stay dissolved in the water instead of bonding together into a solid.
2. **Slap the forming crystals out of shape** (crystal modification), making them too soft and irregular to stick to the metal.
3. **Carry negative electrical charges** (anionic dispersants) so that any tiny crystal that *does* form gets a "repulsion jacket" and is flushed away with the flowing water.

The bodyguard works at astonishingly low concentrations — only **1 molecule of inhibitor is needed for every 10 to 1000 molecules of scale-forming ions** — which is why scale inhibitors are classified as **"threshold"** or **"sub-stoichiometric"** chemicals. This efficiency is the reason they dominate modern industrial water treatment economics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Scale vs. Corrosion Product:**
> - **Scale** = Deposits from **sparingly soluble salts** (CaCO₃, CaSO₄) precipitating from the bulk water.
> - **Corrosion product** = Deposits from **the metal itself** reacting (Fe₂O₃, Fe₃O₄) on the surface.
> Scale inhibitors target the **first** category. They do *not* directly stop corrosion — that role belongs to corrosion inhibitors (Module 3 content).

---

### 1.3 Why Scale is a Critical Engineering Problem

| Consequence of Scale | Industrial Impact |
|---|---|
| Reduced heat transfer | A **1 mm** CaCO₃ scale layer can reduce thermal efficiency by **~10–15 %** in heat exchangers |
| Hot-spot formation | Local overheating → tube rupture in boilers |
| Under-deposit corrosion | Differential aeration cells (O₂ access blocked) |
| Increased pumping cost | Rough surface → higher frictional pressure drop |
| Process downtime | Frequent acid cleaning / mechanical descaling required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Scale inhibitors deliver **economic value** not by stopping a chemical reaction outright (that would be stoichiometric) but by **raising the kinetic barrier** to crystallization and **electro-statically dispersing** incipient nuclei — saving millions of rupees annually in chemical, energy, and maintenance costs across refineries, power plants, and desalination units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Scale Formation — Step-by-Step Logic

Scale formation is fundamentally a **supersaturation → nucleation → crystal growth → aggregation → adhesion** sequence.

1. **Supersaturation (S):** When the ion activity product (IAP) of a salt exceeds its thermodynamic solubility product (Ksp), the solution becomes supersaturated.
   $$S = \frac{IAP}{K_{sp}} = \frac{[Ca^{2+}][CO_3^{2-}]}{K_{sp}(CaCO_3)}$$
   - When **S > 1**, precipitation is thermodynamically favoured.
   - When **S ≤ 1**, the solution is undersaturated or at equilibrium.

2. **Nucleation:** Dissolved ions collide to form **critical nuclei** (embryos of solid phase). The activation energy for nucleation decreases as S increases.

3. **Crystal Growth:** Once a stable nucleus exists, ions from the bulk water deposit onto its active growth sites, enlarging the crystal.

4. **Aggregation:** Crystals collide and stick together (perikinetic and orthokinetic flocculation), forming larger particulates.

5. **Adhesion / Deposition:** Aggregates reach the metal wall, where van der Waals forces, electrostatic attraction, and surface heterogeneity lock them in place.

> [!NOTE]
> A scale inhibitor must intervene at **any one** (or several) of these stages to be effective. The four classical mechanisms each attack a different stage.

---

### 2.2 The Four Mechanisms of Scale Inhibition

#### A. Threshold Inhibition (Sub-Stoichiometric Effect)
- Operates at inhibitor concentrations **far below** the stoichiometric ratio needed to complex *all* the scale-forming cations.
- Typical dosage: **0.5–10 mg/L** of phosphonate for water containing **100–500 mg/L Ca²⁺**.
- Phosphonates adsorb onto nascent crystal nuclei and **block active growth sites**, raising the critical supersaturation ratio at which nucleation occurs.
- Example: **1-hydroxyethane-1,1-diphosphonic acid (HEDP)**, **Amino Trimethylene Phosphonic acid (ATMP)**.

#### B. Crystal Modification / Distortion
- The inhibitor molecule incorporates *into* the growing crystal lattice, but its bulky side groups distort the orderly arrangement.
- The distorted crystal is **soft, irregular, non-adherent**, and easily swept away by flowing water.
- Example: Polyacrylic acid (PAA), Polymaleic acid (PMA), Hydrolyzed Polymaleic Anhydride (HPMA).

#### C. Chelation / Sequestration (Stoichiometric)
- The inhibitor forms **stable, water-soluble, ring-shaped coordination complexes** (chelates) with the scale-forming cation, holding it in solution.
- Operates at **near-stoichiometric ratios** (1:1 or 2:1 ligand-to-metal mole ratio).
- Example: **EDTA, NTA, Citric acid, Gluconic acid, Tartaric acid**.

#### D. Dispersion
- Long-chain anionic polymers adsorb on particle surfaces, imparting a strong **negative zeta potential** (typically **−30 mV to −50 mV**).
- Electrostatic repulsion keeps particles suspended and prevents aggregation/deposition.
- Example: Sulfonated polymers, Polyacrylates, Lignosulfonates.

---

### 2.3 Classification of Scale Inhibitors by Chemistry

| Class | Representative Compounds | Typical Dose (mg/L) | Best Against |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyphosphates** | Sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP), Sodium hexametaphosphate (SHMP) | 1–10 | CaCO₃ |
| **Phosphonates (organophosphonates)** | HEDP, ATMP, EDTMP, DTPMP, PBTC | 1–15 | CaCO₃, CaSO₄, BaSO₄ |
| **Polycarboxylates** | Polyacrylic acid (PAA), Polymaleic acid (PMA), HPMA | 2–20 | CaCO₃, Ca₃(PO₄)₂ |
| **Copolymers (sulfonated)** | AA/AMPS, MA/SS, Vinyl sulfonate copolymers | 5–25 | Ca₃(PO₄)₂, silica, iron oxide dispersion |
| **Chelants (stoichiometric)** | EDTA, NTA, Citric acid, Gluconate, Tartrate | 20–200 | CaCO₃, Mg(OH)₂ |
| **Natural / Green inhibitors** | Polyaspartic acid (PASP), Carboxymethyl inulin, Tannins | 5–30 | CaCO₃, CaSO₄ |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations — they are the most commonly tested items in KTU ESE questions on scale inhibitors.

| # | Equation / Relation | Significance | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_{sp}(CaCO_3) = [Ca^{2+}][CO_3^{2-}]$ | Solubility product governs precipitation onset | (mol/L)² |
| 2 | $S = \frac{IAP}{K_{sp}}$ | Supersaturation ratio; S > 1 ⇒ scaling tendency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{LSI} = pH_{actual} - pH_s$ | Langelier Saturation Index; LSI > 0 ⇒ scaling | dimensionless |
| 4 | $pH_s = (pK_2 - pK_s) + p[Ca^{2+}] + p[Alk]$ | pH at which water is saturated with CaCO₃ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{CaH} = \frac{[Ca^{2+}][HCO_3^-]}{K_{sp} \cdot \gamma}$ | Calcium Hardness × Alkalinity driving force | — |
| 6 | $\text{Mole ratio} = \frac{\text{moles inhibitor}}{\text{moles Ca}^{2+}}$ | Sub-stoichiometric if ≪ 1; stoichiometric ≈ 1 | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Dose (mg/L)} = \frac{C_{Ca} \times \text{MW}_{inh}}{1000 \times z \times \text{Purity}}$ | Stoichiometric dose calculation | mg/L |
| 8 | $\% \text{ Inhibition} = \frac{[\text{Scale}]_{blank} - [\text{Scale}]_{inh}}{[\text{Scale}]_{blank}} \times 100$ | Performance metric in lab jar tests | % |
| 9 | $\text{Cycl. of Conc.} = \frac{C_{Cl, \text{cyc}}}{C_{Cl, \text{makeup}}}$ | Cycles of concentration (controls inhibitor dose) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{RSI} = 2pH_s - pH_{actual}$ | Ryznar Stability Index; RSI < 6 ⇒ scaling | dimensionless |

> **Note on symbols:** $pK_2$ = second dissociation constant of carbonic acid; $pK_s$ = negative log of CaCO₃ solubility product; $\gamma$ = activity coefficient; $C_{Cl}$ = chloride concentration; $z$ = charge of metal ion.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Cooling water towers (CTI / NACE standard dosing):** Phosphonate + polyacrylate blend at **5–15 mg/L** with HEDP : PAA weight ratio of roughly **3 : 1** to balance CaCO₃ control with iron oxide dispersion.
- **Boiler systems (high pressure, > 60 bar):** Coordinated phosphate–chelant program with **Na₃PO₄ + EDTA** to keep residual PO₄ at **30–60 mg/L** and prevent both scale and caustic embrittlement.
- **RO / Desalination plants:** Antiscalants such as **SHMP or ATMP** dosed at **2–5 mg/L** upstream of the high-pressure pump to prevent BaSO₄, CaSO₄, and silica scaling on membranes.
- **Oil & gas (downhole squeeze treatments):** Polyphosphonate esters are squeezed into the reservoir formation, slowly releasing inhibitor over **6–18 months** to protect production tubing from BaSO₄/SrSO₄ scaling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Threshold Dose of Phosphonate for CaCO₃ Inhibition

**Problem Statement:** A cooling-water circuit has calcium hardness = **200 mg/L as CaCO₃**, M-alkalinity = **150 mg/L as CaCO₃**, pH = **8.0**, and temperature = **40 °C**. Estimate the **minimum threshold dose (mg/L)** of HEDP (MW = **206 g/mol**) required to keep CaCO₃ in solution at a **supersaturation margin of 0.5** (i.e., the inhibitor allows up to 50 % of the saturation level before precipitation begins).

**Step 1 — Convert hardness and alkalinity to molar Ca²⁺ and HCO₃⁻.**

Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50 g/eq**; thus Ca²⁺ (as CaCO₃) = 200 mg/L.

$$[Ca^{2+}] = \frac{200 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3 \times 50 \text{ g/eq}}{100 \text{ mg/eq} \times 50 \text{ g/mol}} = \frac{200}{100 \times 1000} \times 50 = 0.002 \text{ mol/L} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

Wait, redo cleanly:

$$[Ca^{2+}] = \frac{200 \text{ mg/L}}{100 \text{ g/mol (as CaCO}_3 \text{ equivalent)}} \times \frac{50 \text{ g/mol}}{50 \text{ g/mol}} \times \frac{1}{40 \text{ g/mol (Ca)}}$$

A cleaner route: 1 mol CaCO₃ ≡ 1 mol Ca²⁺ ≡ 100 g of CaCO₃-equivalent hardness.

$$n_{Ca^{2+}} = \frac{200 \text{ mg/L}}{100{,}000 \text{ mg/mol}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$

For alkalinity: 1 mol HCO₃⁻ ≡ 1 mol CaCO₃-equivalent alkalinity ≡ 100 g CaCO₃-equiv.

$$n_{HCO_3^-} = \frac{150 \text{ mg/L}}{100{,}000 \text{ mg/mol}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Ion Activity Product (IAP) at operating pH.**

At pH = 8.0, the carbonate speciation ratio (using $K_2 = 4.69 \times 10^{-11}$) is:

$$\frac{[CO_3^{2-}]}{[HCO_3^-]} = \frac{K_2}{[H^+]} = \frac{4.69 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-8}} = 4.69 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$[CO_3^{2-}] = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.69 \times 10^{-3} = 7.04 \times 10^{-6} \text{ M}$$

**Step 3 — Compute IAP and compare to Ksp.**

$K_{sp}(CaCO_3) = 4.8 \times 10^{-9}$ at 25 °C (approximately; rises to **~6 × 10⁻⁹** at 40 °C — take $K_{sp} = 6.0 \times 10^{-9}$).

$$\text{IAP} = [Ca^{2+}][CO_3^{2-}] = (2.0 \times 10^{-3})(7.04 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.41 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\text{Saturation ratio } S = \frac{\text{IAP}}{K_{sp}} = \frac{1.41 \times 10^{-8}}{6.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.35$$

The water is **2.35× supersaturated** — scaling will occur.

**Step 4 — Define the target post-inhibition saturation.**

With a margin of 0.5, the inhibitor must suppress the **effective** IAP to **1.5 × Ksp**:

$$\text{IAP}_{\text{target}} = 1.5 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-9} = 9.0 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate moles of Ca²⁺ that must be "complexed" by HEDP to lower free Ca²⁺.**

Assuming CO₃²⁻ remains essentially unchanged (alkalinity is buffered), the new free $[Ca^{2+}]_{free}$ required:

$$[Ca^{2+}]_{free} = \frac{\text{IAP}_{\text{target}}}{[CO_3^{2-}]} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-9}}{7.04 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.28 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

$$[Ca^{2+}]_{complexed} = [Ca^{2+}]_{total} - [Ca^{2+}]_{free} = (2.0 - 1.28) \times 10^{-3} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the 1 : 1 Ca : HEDP binding assumption for sub-stoichiometric threshold action.**

Threshold phosphonates complex Ca²⁺ in a **1 : 1 mole ratio** at the active adsorption site:

$$n_{HEDP} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to mg/L of HEDP (MW = 206 g/mol, assuming 100 % active).**

$$\text{Dose} = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L} \times 206 \text{ g/mol} \times 1000 \text{ mg/g} = 148 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 8 — Apply the "threshold rule of thumb" correction.**

Empirically, phosphonates are effective at **1/10 to 1/100** of the stoichiometric dose due to surface-blockage and crystal-poisoning effects. Apply a factor of **1/30** (typical for HEDP on CaCO₃):

$$\text{Dose}_{\text{field}} \approx \frac{148}{30} \approx 5 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** A field dose of **5 mg/L** HEDP is sufficient — exactly in the operating range of **1–15 mg/L** quoted in Section 2.3. This validates the sub-stoichiometric "threshold effect" experimentally.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic for Scale Inhibitor Selection

Use this decision matrix when designing a water-treatment program:

```
                    ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                    │  WHAT IS THE DOMINANT SCALE?  │
                    └───────────────┬───────────────┘
                                    │
        ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐
        ▼                           ▼                           ▼
   CaCO₃ (LSI > 0)            CaSO₄ / BaSO₄             Ca₃(PO₄)₂ / Silica
        │                           │                           │
   Phosphonates                Phosphonates              Sulfonated copolymers
   (HEDP, ATMP)             (ATMP, DTPMP)               (AA/AMPS, MA/SS)
   + Polyacrylates          + Polyacrylates              + Phosphonates blend
        │                           │                           │
        ▼                           ▼                           ▼
   pH 7.5–8.5                pH 6.5–7.5                  pH 7.0–8.0
   Ca-hard 50–500            High TDS brine               Phosphate dosing
                              cycles 3–5
```

**Auxiliary Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Preferred Choice |
|---|---|
| Environmental (phosphate ban) | Polyaspartic acid (PASP), Carboxymethyl inulin |
| High temperature (> 80 °C) | Phosphonates (heat-stable) — avoid polyacrylates (>60 °C degrades) |
| High silica (SiO₂ > 150 mg/L) | AA/AMPS copolymer + boric acid |
| Iron oxide fouling | Sulfonated copolymers (best dispersancy) |
| RO membrane compatibility | Polyacrylates, low-charge phosphonates |

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Chelation Stoichiometry

**Problem:** How many mg of EDTA (disodium salt, MW = **372.24 g/mol**) are needed to sequester **150 mg/L** of Ca²⁺ (MW = 40 g/mol)?

$$\text{Mole ratio Ca : EDTA} = 1 : 1$$
$$n_{Ca^{2+}} = \frac{150 \text{ mg/L}}{40 \text{ g/mol}} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$
$$\text{Mass EDTA} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 372.24 \times 1000 = 1396 \text{ mg/L}$$

Compare with HEDP threshold dose from §3.1: only **5 mg/L** — a **279-fold reduction**, which is the fundamental economic driver for using phosphonate **threshold** inhibitors instead of chelants in large-volume cooling systems.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Map — How a Phosphonate Scale Inhibitor Works

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hard water enters heat exchanger - Ca2+ and CO3 2- present] --> B[Solution supersaturation S greater than 1]
    B --> C[CaCO3 nuclei begin to form on metal wall]
    C --> D[Phosphonate molecule HEDP adsorbs onto active growth site of crystal]
    D --> E[Two effects simultaneously]
    E --> F1[Threshold effect - blocks nucleation site, raises critical S]
    E --> F2[Crystal distortion - distorts lattice, soft non-adherent crystals]
    F1 --> G[Crystal does not grow - remains dispersed]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Anionic polymer dispersant gives high negative zeta potential]
    H --> I[Particles swept away by flowing water]
    I --> J[Clean heat transfer surface maintained]
    J --> K[Thermal efficiency preserved]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Cooling-Water Inhibitor Dosing System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Makeup water supply] --> nodeB[Pre-treatment multimedia filter]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Softener cation exchange]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Storage tank with level sensor]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Recirculation pump]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Heat exchanger]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Cooling tower]
    nodeG --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeH[Process outlet hot stream]
    nodeI[Chemical dosing skid] -->|phosphonate pump 5 mg per L| nodeD
    nodeI -->|polyacrylate pump 3 mg per L| nodeD
    nodeI -->|biocide pump intermittent| nodeD
    nodeJ[On-line analyser pH conductivity ORP] --> nodeK[PLC controller]
    nodeK -->|feedback signal| nodeI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** The **PLC** receives continuous inputs from the **on-line analyser** (pH, conductivity, ORP) and modulates the **chemical dosing pumps** to maintain target inhibitor residual of **3–6 mg/L** as HEDP in the recirculating water — a classic closed-loop feedback strategy in modern chemical-treatment plants.

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Inhibitor Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{System temperature} -->|below 60 deg C| Q2{pH operating range}
    Q1 -->|above 60 deg C| Q3[Phosphonate only - heat stable]
    Q2 -->|pH 6.5 to 7.5| Q4[Phosphonate plus polyacrylate]
    Q2 -->|pH 7.5 to 9.0| Q5[Polyacrylate plus copolymer]
    Q4 --> R{Environmental discharge limit}
    Q5 --> R
    R -->|strict zero phosphate| S[Switch to polyaspartic acid PASP]
    R -->|relaxed standard| T[Conventional phosphonate blend OK]
    Q3 --> R
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Question, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define "scale" and list four common types of scale encountered in industrial water systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Scale is a hard, adherent, crystalline or amorphous deposit formed by the precipitation of sparingly soluble inorganic salts from aqueous solutions onto heat-transfer surfaces and pipe walls.
- **Four common types (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. **Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** — most common in cooling water, formed when Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) > 0.
  2. **Calcium sulfate (CaSO₄·2H₂O, gypsum)** — common in high-TDS cooling circuits and RO systems.
  3. **Magnesium hydroxide (Mg(OH)₂)** — forms at pH > 9 and high temperature.
  4. **Calcium phosphate (Ca₃(PO₄)₂)** — forms in phosphate-dosed boiler systems.
  5. *(Optional, for extra credit)* Barium/strontium sulfate in oilfield brines; silica (SiO₂) in geothermal and high-pressure boiler systems.

---

**Q2. What is the difference between a "threshold inhibitor" and a "chelating agent"? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Threshold inhibitor (1½ marks):** Operates at **sub-stoichiometric concentrations** (typically 1–20 mg/L), preventing scale by adsorbing onto nascent crystal nuclei and blocking growth sites, **without stoichiometrically binding all** the scale-forming cations. *Example:* HEDP (1-hydroxyethane-1,1-diphosphonic acid) or ATMP.
- **Chelating agent (1½ marks):** Operates at **stoichiometric or near-stoichiometric concentrations**, forming stable water-soluble ring complexes (chelates) with scale-forming cations in a 1 : 1 mole ratio. *Example:* EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) or NTA (nitrilotriacetic acid).

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern, ESE Module 4]**

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain in detail the **four principal mechanisms by which scale inhibitors function**, giving one example compound for each mechanism and the chemical interaction responsible for inhibition.

**(b) (7 marks)** A cooling water system has the following analysis: Ca²⁺ = 160 mg/L, M-alkalinity = 100 mg/L as CaCO₃, pH = 7.8, temperature = 35 °C. Calculate the **Langelier Saturation Index (LSI)** and comment on whether the water is scale-forming or corrosive. Given: at 35 °C, $pK_2 - pK_s = 1.99$, $p[Ca^{2+}] = -\log_{10}([Ca^{2+}])$ with Ca²⁺ in mol/L, and alkalinity in mol/L as HCO₃⁻.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Four Mechanisms (7 marks)

**1. Threshold Inhibition (2 marks):**
- Sub-stoichiometric dosing of organophosphonates (HEDP, ATMP, EDTMP) at 1–10 mg/L.
- Phosphonate groups (–PO(OH)₂) bind to active Ca²⁺ sites on nascent crystal nuclei through the **PO₃²⁻ functional group**, blocking further ion addition.
- Raises the critical supersaturation required for nucleation from S ≈ 1.5 (no inhibitor) to S ≈ 3–5 (with inhibitor).

**2. Crystal Modification / Distortion (2 marks):**
- Polycarboxylate polymers (PAA, PMA, HPMA) incorporate their –COO⁻ side chains into the growing CaCO₃ lattice during growth.
- The bulky polymer backbone **physically disrupts the regular calcite rhombohedral lattice**, producing soft, rounded aragonite or vaterite crystals that do not adhere to metal surfaces.

**3. Chelation / Sequestration (1½ marks):**
- Chelants like EDTA, NTA, citric acid, and gluconic acid form **5- or 6-membered water-soluble coordination rings** with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺.
- Operating mode is **stoichiometric** — dose directly proportional to hardness.

**4. Dispersion (1½ marks):**
- Long-chain anionic polymers (sulfonated copolymers, lignosulfonates) adsorb onto particle surfaces.
- Imparts **high negative zeta potential** (typically −30 to −50 mV), creating strong electrostatic repulsion between particles.
- Prevents aggregation and deposition; works synergistically with threshold inhibitors.

#### Part (b) — LSI Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert Ca²⁺ to mol/L (1 mark):**
$$[Ca^{2+}] = \frac{160 \text{ mg/L}}{40 \text{ g/mol} \times 1000 \text{ mg/g}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$
$$p[Ca^{2+}] = -\log(4.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.40$$

**Step 2 — Convert alkalinity to mol/L as HCO₃⁻ (1 mark):**
$$[\text{Alk}] = \frac{100 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3}{50 \text{ g/eq} \times 1000} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ eq/L}$$
$$p[\text{Alk}] = -\log(2.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.70$$

**Step 3 — Calculate pH_s (2 marks):**
$$pH_s = (pK_2 - pK_s) + p[Ca^{2+}] + p[\text{Alk}]$$
$$pH_s = 1.99 + 2.40 + 2.70 = 7.09$$

**Step 4 — Calculate LSI (1 mark):**
$$\text{LSI} = pH_{actual} - pH_s = 7.8 - 7.09 = +0.71$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **LSI = +0.71 → Scale-forming water.**
- LSI is positive → water is supersaturated with respect to CaCO₃ → CaCO₃ scale will deposit on heat-transfer surfaces.
- **Recommendation:** Apply a phosphonate (HEDP) at **3–6 mg/L** or adjust pH downward (acid feed) until LSI falls in the range **−0.5 to +0.5** for balanced operation.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) (7 marks)** Compare the action of **polyphosphates, phosphonates, and polyacrylates** as scale inhibitors, with respect to (i) hydrolysis stability, (ii) calcium tolerance, and (iii) typical dose level. State one industrial application for each.

**(b) (7 marks)** A **once-through boiler** uses feed water containing 50 mg/L Ca²⁺ and 200 mg/L SO₄²⁻. The solubility product of CaSO₄ is $K_{sp} = 4.93 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate (i) whether CaSO₄ will precipitate, and (ii) the minimum concentration of EDTA (MW = 372 g/mol, 1:1 Ca²⁺ binding) required to keep all the calcium in solution as Ca-EDTA complex.

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Property | Polyphosphates (SHMP, STPP) | Phosphonates (HEDP, ATMP) | Polyacrylates (PAA, PMA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrolysis stability** | Low — reverts to orthophosphate above 50 °C, losing scale inhibition and **promoting Ca₃(PO₄)₂ scale** | **Excellent** — stable to > 100 °C; C–P bond is hydrolytically robust | Good below 60 °C; above 80 °C, amide/imide groups hydrolyse |
| **Calcium tolerance** | Moderate — precipitates as Ca-polyphosphate at high Ca²⁺ (> 200 mg/L) | **High** — Ca-phosphonate soluble up to ~ 600 mg/L Ca²⁺ | Moderate–high depending on MW |
| **Typical dose (mg/L)** | 1–10 | 1–15 | 2–20 |
| **Industrial application (½ mark each)** | Low-pressure cooling water | High-pressure boilers, oilfield squeeze treatment | Cooling water + iron-oxide dispersancy |

#### Part (b) — CaSO₄ Solubility Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert concentrations to mol/L (2 marks):**
$$[Ca^{2+}] = \frac{50 \text{ mg/L}}{40 \text{ g/mol} \times 1000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$
$$[SO_4^{2-}] = \frac{200 \text{ mg/L}}{96 \text{ g/mol} \times 1000} = 2.08 \times 10^{-3} \text{ M}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate IAP and compare to Ksp (2 marks):**
$$\text{IAP} = (1.25 \times 10^{-3})(2.08 \times 10^{-3}) = 2.60 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\text{IAP} \ll K_{sp} = 4.93 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\text{IAP} / K_{sp} = 0.053 \ll 1$$
- **CaSO₄ will NOT precipitate** under these conditions (the solution is undersaturated).

**Step 3 — EDTA dose for stoichiometric Ca²⁺ sequestration (2 marks):**
$$n_{Ca^{2+}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L (same as above)}$$
$$\text{EDTA required (1 : 1)} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$
$$\text{Mass} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 372 \times 1000 = 465 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Practical recommendation (1 mark):**
- Since CaSO₄ is not actually precipitating (IAP ≪ Ksp), the chelant dose of **465 mg/L** is unnecessary in practice.
- A **threshold phosphonate** at **2–5 mg/L** would be sufficient as a safety margin against any concentration build-up in the boiler drum.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **LSI sign convention:** Many students compute LSI with the wrong sign and conclude that **negative LSI means scaling** — it is the *opposite*. **LSI > 0 ⇒ scaling; LSI < 0 ⇒ corrosive; LSI ≈ 0 ⇒ balanced.** (−1 mark typical)
> - **Unit conversions:** Mixing mg/L with mol/L without converting MW of CaCO₃ (100) vs Ca²⁺ (40) — examiners specifically check the **50 g/eq equivalence** of CaCO₃. (−1 mark)
> - **Threshold vs stoichiometric confusion:** Stating that HEDP forms a 1:1 stoichiometric complex **with all the calcium** is a misconception — it acts at **1/10 to 1/100 of stoichiometric** by surface poisoning. (−1 mark)
> - **Forgetting the activity coefficient γ:** For precise work, replace [Ca²⁺] with activity {Ca²⁺} = γ[Ca²⁺] where γ ≈ 0.5–0.7 at typical ionic strengths. (−1 mark if examiner demands rigor)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Scale** = crystalline deposit of sparingly soluble salts (CaCO₃, CaSO₄, Mg(OH)₂, Ca₃(PO₄)₂, BaSO₄, SiO₂) on industrial surfaces.
- ✅ **Four mechanisms of scale inhibition:** Threshold · Crystal modification · Chelation · Dispersion.
- ✅ **Threshold inhibitors** (HEDP, ATMP, EDTMP) act sub-stoichiometrically at **1–15 mg/L** — economically dominant in industry.
- ✅ **Polyphosphates** (SHMP) are unstable above ~50 °C — hydrolyse to orthophosphate and **promote** Ca₃(PO₄)₂ scale.
- ✅ **Polyacrylates (PAA, PMA, HPMA)** are excellent **crystal modifiers and dispersants** but degrade above ~80 °C.
- ✅ **Chelants (EDTA, NTA)** operate stoichiometrically at **20–200 mg/L** — used in boilers, not in large cooling systems.
- ✅ **LSI = pH − pH_s**; **RSI = 2pH_s − pH**; **LSI > 0 ⇒ scaling**, **RSI < 6 ⇒ scaling**.
- ✅ **LSI > +0.5 → severe scale risk; LSI < −0.5 → corrosion risk**; aim for **−0.5 ≤ LSI ≤ +0.5**.
- ✅ **Green inhibitors (PASP, carboxymethyl inulin)** are emerging biodegradable replacements for phosphonates under strict environmental discharge norms.
- ✅ For **RO membranes** → use **SHMP, ATMP, or AA/AMPS copolymer** at 2–5 mg/L upstream of HP pump.
- ✅ **Sulfonated copolymers (AA/AMPS)** are best for **silica and iron-oxide dispersion**.
- ✅ Always dose inhibitor **upstream of the heat exchanger** — never downstream.
- ✅ Monitor **inhibitor residual** (typically 3–6 mg/L as HEDP-PO₄) using standard photometric/HPLC methods for closed-loop control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rebar Inhibitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rebar Inhibitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rebar Inhibitors** are a specialized class of chemical admixtures—primarily mixed-in or migrating corrosion inhibitors—that are introduced into reinforced concrete to suppress or significantly retard the electrochemical corrosion of the embedded steel reinforcement (rebar). They function by stabilizing the passive ferric oxide (γ-Fe₂O₃) film on the steel surface, thereby shifting the corrosion potential (E_corr) to more noble (positive) values and reducing the corrosion current density (i_corr) to acceptable engineering thresholds (typically **< 0.1 μA/cm²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (PEICT866 - Module 4):** Rebar inhibitors form a critical sub-topic under "Inhibitors for Specific Applications." They are the **only** inhibitor class specifically engineered to protect steel embedded in an alkaline, heterogeneous, cementitious matrix—where conventional pickling/cooling-water inhibitors fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in Practice:**
> - **Critical Chloride Threshold (C_th):** ~0.4 % by weight of cement for plain carbon steel
> - **Acceptable i_corr limit:** **< 0.1 μA/cm²** (negligible corrosion)
> - **Tolerable i_corr limit:** **0.1 – 0.5 μA/cm²** (low corrosion)
> - **High corrosion limit:** **0.5 – 1.0 μA/cm²** (moderate-to-high)
> - **Critical i_corr:** **> 1.0 μA/cm²** (severe corrosion)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain-English Explanation:**
Imagine a steel rebar embedded in concrete as a **person standing in a crowded, slightly damp room (concrete)**. Over time, **chloride ions and moisture (the "sweat" of the room)** seep in and start "attacking" the person (corroding the steel), causing them to rust and weaken. A **rebar inhibitor acts like a special medicated cream applied to the person** that:

1. **Pre-emptively forms a protective invisible skin** (passive film enhancement) — so attackers (Cl⁻) bounce off.
2. **Neutralizes the attackers chemically** (anodic passivation) — turning chloride "bullets" into harmless byproducts.
3. **Continues to work even after being applied years later** (migrating inhibitors) — like a slow-release medicine.

In short: **Rebar inhibitors = chemical bodyguards for steel inside concrete.**

### 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Rebar inhibitors are classified by **mode of application** and by **electrochemical mechanism**:

| Classification Basis | Type | Common Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Mode of Application** | Admixed (Mixed-in) | Calcium nitrite, Sodium nitrite |
| | Migrating (Surface Applied) | Amino-alcohol based, Amine-ester |
| **Electrochemical Mechanism** | Anodic (Passivating) | Calcium nitrite (Ca(NO₂)₂) |
| | Cathodic | Amines (e.g., DMEA) |
| | Mixed / Film-forming | Amino-alcohols, Phosphate esters |
| **Chemical Family** | Nitrite-based | Ca(NO₂)₂, NaNO₂ |
| | Amine / Amino-alcohol | Monoethanolamine, Amino-propyl-silane |
| | Organic / Migrating | Alkanolamines, Imidazoline derivatives |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Rebar Corrosion is a Unique Problem

Reinforced concrete provides **two natural protections** to steel:

1. **Physical barrier** — concrete cover.
2. **Chemical passivation** — high pore-water pH (~12.5 – 13.5) from Ca(OH)₂, which forms a stable **γ-Fe₂O₃ passive film** on the steel.

These protections are **defeated** by two primary mechanisms:

- **Carbonation:** CO₂ diffuses in, lowering pH below ~9, dissolving the passive film.
  $$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$
- **Chloride ingress:** Cl⁻ ions penetrate concrete, locally depassivating steel above a critical threshold (C_th ≈ **0.4 %** by cement weight).

Once depassivation occurs, the classic **galvanic micro-cell** forms:

$$ \text{Anode (Fe → Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \text{)} \quad \vert \quad \text{Cathode (O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-} \text{)} $$

This produces expansive rust (Fe(OH)₂ → Fe₂O₃·nH₂O) whose volume is **2 to 6×** the parent steel — causing **spalling** and structural failure.

### 2.2 Mechanism of Action of Rebar Inhibitors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logic of Anodic Passivation (Calcium Nitrite, Ca(NO₂)₂):**

- **Step 1 — Dissociation:** Ca(NO₂)₂ releases NO₂⁻ ions into the concrete pore solution.
  $$ \text{Ca(NO}_2)_2 \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\,\text{NO}_2^{-} $$
- **Step 2 — Competitive adsorption:** NO₂⁻ ions compete with Cl⁻ ions for adsorption sites on the steel surface.
- **Step 3 — Passive film repair:** NO₂⁻ oxidizes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ at the metal/solution interface, reinforcing the γ-Fe₂O₃ layer.
  $$ 2\,\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{NO}_2^{-} + 2\,\text{H}^{+} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{NO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$
- **Step 4 — Stable, defect-free film:** The repaired passive film has lower ionic conductivity, blocking both Fe²+ egress and Cl⁻ ingress.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Typical Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Corrosion current density (acceptable) | $i_{corr} < 0.1$ | μA/cm² | Passivity criterion |
| 2 | Critical chloride threshold | $C_{th} = 0.4$ | % by wt of cement | Depassivation limit |
| 3 | Calcium nitrite dosage | $D = k \cdot [Cl^-]$ | L/m³ or gal/yd³ | Design specification |
| 4 | Inhibitor efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{i_{corr}^{0} - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^{0}} \times 100$ | % | Performance metric |
| 5 | Corrosion rate (Faraday's law) | $CR = \dfrac{0.00327 \cdot i_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mm/yr | Service-life prediction |
| 6 | [Cl⁻]/[OH⁻] threshold ratio | $\dfrac{[Cl^-]}{[OH^-]} \leq 0.61$ | dimensionless | Corrosion initiation |
| 7 | Nitrite-to-chloride molar ratio (recommended) | $\dfrac{[NO_2^-]}{[Cl^-]} \geq 1.0$ | mol/mol | Inhibitor sufficiency |
| 8 | Cover depth-time relation (Fick's 2nd Law) | $C(x,t) = C_s \cdot \left[1 - \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_a t}}\right)\right]$ | kg/m³ | Chloride ingress prediction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical nitrite/chloride ratio:** A minimum molar ratio **[NO₂⁻] / [Cl⁻] ≥ 1.0** is required for effective protection. Excess nitrite acts as a "chemical reserve" to outcompete chloride adsorption — a key design point in KTU problems.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Bridge decks** exposed to de-icing salts (NaCl, CaCl₂).
- **Marine sub-structures** (piers, jetties, off-shore platforms).
- **Parking garages** and tunnel linings.
- **Pre-stressed and post-tensioned concrete** (high safety margin required).
- **Repair of heritage / heritage-grade concrete** (compatible with existing structures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inhibitor Efficiency from Polarization Data

The performance of a rebar inhibitor is quantified by its **inhibition efficiency (η, %)**, derived directly from linear polarization resistance (LPR) measurements on rebar in concrete.

**Step 1:** Measure the polarization resistance $R_p$ in the absence of inhibitor:

$$ R_p^{0} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}\bigg|_{E=E_{corr}} \quad \text{(Stern–Geary relationship)} $$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Stern–Geary equation** to obtain the corrosion current density:

$$ i_{corr} = \dfrac{B}{R_p} $$

where $B$ is the Stern–Geary constant (typically **B = 26 mV** for active rebar, **B = 52 mV** for passive rebar).

**Step 3:** Evaluate $i_{corr}$ in the **uninhibited** system:
$$ i_{corr}^{0} = \dfrac{B}{R_p^{0}} $$

**Step 4:** Evaluate $i_{corr}$ in the **inhibited** system (with rebar inhibitor added):
$$ i_{corr}^{inh} = \dfrac{B}{R_p^{inh}} $$

**Step 5:** Define and compute the inhibition efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{i_{corr}^{0} - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^{0}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \left(1 - \frac{R_p^{0}}{R_p^{inh}}\right) \times 100 \quad [\%]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

> Suppose LPR measurements on a rebar specimen in chloride-contaminated concrete yield:
> $R_p^{0} = 5.2 \times 10^{4} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^{2}$ (no inhibitor)
> $R_p^{inh} = 2.1 \times 10^{6} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm}^{2}$ (with 4 % calcium nitrite admixed)
> with $B = 52$ mV.

**Computing the inhibited and uninhibited current densities:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{corr}^{0} &= \frac{52 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}}{5.2 \times 10^{4}\,\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^{2}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A/cm}^{2} = 1.0\;\mu\text{A/cm}^{2} \\
i_{corr}^{inh} &= \frac{52 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}}{2.1 \times 10^{6}\,\Omega\cdot\text{cm}^{2}} = 2.476 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{A/cm}^{2} \approx 0.0248\;\mu\text{A/cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Inhibition efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{1.0 - 0.0248}{1.0} \times 100 \\
&= 97.52\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The calcium nitrite admixture reduces the corrosion current density from the **"high" regime (1.0 μA/cm²)** to well within the **"negligible" regime (< 0.1 μA/cm²)**, achieving an inhibition efficiency of **≈ 97.5 %**. This is a typical industry-acceptable value.

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Calcium Nitrite Dosage

For a design chloride exposure of $[Cl^-]_{design}$ (kg/m³ in concrete), the nitrite dose must satisfy:

$$
\dfrac{[NO_2^-]}{[Cl^-]} \geq 1.0 \quad \text{(molar basis)}
$$

**Conversion to volumetric Ca(NO₂)₂ dose:**

**Step 1:** Convert $[Cl^-]_{design}$ to moles per m³ of concrete:

$$ n_{Cl^-} = \dfrac{[Cl^-]_{design} \times 1000}{M_{Cl}} $$

where $M_{Cl} = 35.45$ g/mol.

**Step 2:** Required moles of nitrite (1:1 ratio):
$$ n_{NO_2^-} = n_{Cl^-} $$

**Step 3:** Convert to mass of Ca(NO₂)₂ (M = 132.09 g/mol, providing 2 mol NO₂⁻ per mol):

$$ m_{Ca(NO_2)_2} = \dfrac{n_{NO_2^-}}{2} \times 132.09 \quad [\text{g}] $$

**Step 4:** Express as % of cement weight (assuming cement content = 400 kg/m³):

$$ \%\,\text{dose} = \dfrac{m_{Ca(NO_2)_2}}{400 \times 1000} \times 100 $$

**Step 5 (Realistic industrial design):** Commercial Ca(NO₂)₂ solutions (typically 30 % active) are dosed at **10–30 L/m³** of concrete, depending on chloride exposure severity. KTU problems typically use:

| Chloride Exposure Severity | % Ca(NO₂)₂ by cement wt |
|---|---|
| Low (urban, no de-icing salts) | 1 – 2 % |
| Moderate (coastal atmosphere) | 2 – 3 % |
| High (marine splash / de-icing salts) | 3 – 5 % |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Rebar Inhibitor Classes

| Property | Calcium Nitrite (Anodic) | Amino-alcohols (Mixed) | Migrating Amine Esters | Sodium Monofluorophosphate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Anodic passivator | Mixed (anodic + cathodic) | Adsorption film | Surface phosphating |
| **Typical dosage** | 2 – 5 % cement | 1 – 3 % cement | Surface spray, 200–500 g/m² | 2 – 4 % cement |
| **Effect on concrete strength** | Slight acceleration | Negligible | None (surface) | Negligible |
| **Chloride tolerance** | High | Moderate–High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Long-term data** | 30+ years | 15+ years | 10+ years | 5+ years |
| **Risk** | Alkali-silica aggravation at > 5 % | Volatility losses | Inconsistent penetration | pH drop concerns |
| **Best application** | New construction (admixed) | New / repair | Repair / retrofit | New construction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `stepA`, `node1`). Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only, no markdown formatting, no `end` / `subgraph` / `graph` reserved keywords used as standalone nodes.

### 4.2 Schematic A: Functional Architecture of a Rebar Inhibition System in Concrete

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[CONCRETE MATRIX PHASE] --> node2[PORE SOLUTION PH 12.5 TO 13.5]
    node1 --> node3[CAPILLARY PORES WITH MOISTURE]
    node1 --> node4[REBAR EMBEDDED IN MATRIX]
    node3 --> node5[CHLORIDE INGRESS FROM EXTERNAL ENVIRONMENT]
    node3 --> node6[CARBONATION CO2 DIFFUSION]
    node2 --> nodeA[REBAR INHIBITOR ADDED ADMIXED OR MIGRATING]
    nodeA --> nodeB[CALCIUM NITRITE DISSOCIATION NO2 MINUS IONS]
    nodeA --> nodeC[AMINE ADSORPTION ON STEEL]
    node5 --> nodeD[CHLORIDE ATTACK ON PASSIVE FILM]
    node6 --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE[COMPETITIVE ADSORPTION NO2 VS CL]
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF[STABLE GAMMA FE2O3 PASSIVE FILM MAINTAINED]
    nodeD --> nodeG[BREAKDOWN OF PASSIVE FILM]
    nodeG --> nodeH[ACTIVE CORROSION MICRO CELL]
    nodeH --> nodeI[RUST EXPANSION 2X TO 6X VOLUME]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[CONCRETE SPALLING AND CRACKING]
    nodeF --> nodeK[REBAR PROTECTED STATE ICORR BELOW 0.1 MICRO A PER CM2]
    nodeK --> nodeL[SERVICE LIFE EXTENSION 30 PLUS YEARS]
```

### 4.3 Schematic B: Decision Flow for Selecting Rebar Inhibitor Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[START NEW CONSTRUCTION OR REPAIR] --> q1{IS STRUCTURE NEW OR EXISTING}
    q1 -->|NEW| q2{CHLORIDE EXPOSURE LEVEL}
    q1 -->|EXISTING REPAIR| q3[USE MIGRATING SURFACE APPLIED INHIBITOR]
    q2 -->|LOW| r1[CALCIUM NITRITE 1 TO 2 PERCENT]
    q2 -->|MODERATE| r2[CALCIUM NITRITE 2 TO 3 PERCENT]
    q2 -->|HIGH MARINE OR DE ICING| r3[CALCIUM NITRITE 3 TO 5 PERCENT PLUS EPOXY COATED REBAR]
    q2 -->|VERY HIGH| r4[CATHODIC PROTECTION PLUS CALCIUM NITRITE]
    q3 --> r5[AMINO ALCOHOL OR AMINE ESTER SURFACE SPRAY]
    r1 --> endok[DESIGN VERIFIED NO2 TO CL RATIO AT LEAST 1.0]
    r2 --> endok
    r3 --> endok
    r4 --> endok
    r5 --> endok
```

### 4.4 Schematic C: Electrochemical Action at the Steel–Concrete Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CONCRETE PORE WATER PH 12.5] -->|PROVIDES OH MINUS| B[STEEL SURFACE GAMMA FE2O3 FILM]
    C[NO2 MINUS FROM CA NO2 2 ADMIXED] -->|OXIDIZES FE2 PLUS TO FE3 PLUS| B
    D[CL MINUS INGRESS] -->|COMPETES WITH NO2 MINUS| B
    B -->|INTACT| E[REBAR PASSIVE ICORR BELOW 0.1 MICRO A]
    B -->|BREAKS DOWN CL ABOVE 0.4 PERCENT| F[ACTIVE CORROSION]
    F -->|FE GOES TO FE2 PLUS + 2E| G[ANODIC DISSOLUTION]
    F -->|O2 + 2H2O + 4E GOES TO 4OH MINUS| H[CATHODIC OXYGEN REDUCTION]
    G --> I[FE2 PLUS MIGRATES INTO PORE WATER]
    H --> I
    I --> J[FE2 PLUS + 2OH MINUS GOES TO FE OH 2]
    J --> K[FE2O3 N H2O EXPANSIVE RUST]
    K --> L[CONCRETE SPALLING]
    C -->|SHIFTS REACTION LEFT| B
```

### 4.5 Diagram Fallback Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid schematics above function as **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mappings. They replace physical drawings of the rebar-in-concrete cross-section, which Mermaid cannot render, and they map:
> - the **chemical / electrochemical interactions** between inhibitor, pore solution, and rebar;
> - the **decision logic** an engineer applies in inhibitor selection;
> - the **failure mode progression** when inhibition is absent or insufficient.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, PEICT866]**
