> **Define a rebar inhibitor. Name two commercial examples and state the electrochemical mechanism of the most commonly used rebar inhibitor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A rebar inhibitor is a chemical admixture or surface-applied compound added to concrete to suppress the electrochemical corrosion of the embedded steel reinforcement (rebar), typically by stabilizing or repairing the passive ferric oxide (γ-Fe₂O₃) film on the steel surface.

Two commercial examples: **(i) Calcium nitrite, Ca(NO₂)₂** and **(ii) Amino-alcohol based migrating inhibitors.**

The most commonly used, **calcium nitrite**, functions as an **anodic passivator**: the released NO₂⁻ ions oxidize Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ at the steel–concrete interface, reinforcing the γ-Fe₂O₃ passive film and shifting the corrosion potential (E_corr) to more noble values. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for mechanism.)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, PEICT866]**
> **State the recommended [NO₂⁻] / [Cl⁻] molar ratio for effective calcium nitrite inhibition. Why is this ratio a critical design parameter in reinforced concrete exposed to chlorides?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The recommended minimum molar ratio is **[NO₂⁻] / [Cl⁻] ≥ 1.0**.

This ratio is critical because chloride ions depassivate steel by locally penetrating the γ-Fe₂O₃ film at concentrations above the threshold (≈ 0.4 % by weight of cement). NO₂⁻ ions compete with Cl⁻ for adsorption sites and oxidize Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺, repairing the passive film. **If the ratio falls below 1.0, there is insufficient nitrite to outcompete chloride adsorption, and the protective film breaks down, initiating pitting corrosion.** Hence the ratio serves as the chemical design safety margin for rebar inhibitor dosage. **(1 mark for ratio, 2 marks for design significance.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Each Module carries either a 14-mark question or two 7-mark sub-questions. Sub-part (a) typically tests understanding; sub-part (b) tests numerical / analytical application.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, PEICT866, ESE Pattern]**

> **(a)** Explain the mechanism of corrosion of steel reinforcement in concrete. Discuss how carbonation and chloride ingress destroy the passive film. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the mechanism of calcium nitrite as a rebar inhibitor, with a schematic representation of the anodic passivation process. Mention its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Concrete provides dual protection — physical cover + chemical passivation by high pH (~12.5 – 13.5) from Ca(OH)₂ in the pore solution. This high pH stabilizes a thin, adherent γ-Fe₂O₃ passive film on the steel surface, keeping i_corr below 0.1 μA/cm².

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** **Carbonation mechanism** — atmospheric CO₂ diffuses through the porous concrete matrix, reacts with Ca(OH)₂ to form CaCO₃, lowering the pore-water pH below ~9. At this pH, γ-Fe₂O₃ is thermodynamically unstable and dissolves, exposing active steel.

  $$ \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Chloride ingress mechanism** — Cl⁻ ions from de-icing salts or marine environments diffuse inward. Above the critical threshold (~0.4 % by cement weight, or [Cl⁻]/[OH⁻] > 0.61), chloride locally disrupts the passive film and creates active pits.

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** The exposed anodic regions (Fe → Fe²⁺ + 2e⁻) couple with cathodic regions (O₂ + 2H₂O + 4e⁻ → 4OH⁻), forming **macro-galvanic corrosion cells**. Rust products (Fe(OH)₂ → Fe₂O₃·nH₂O) occupy 2 – 6× the original steel volume, generating internal tensile stresses that spall the concrete cover.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** **Calcium nitrite** (Ca(NO₂)₂) is an **anodic inhibitor** admixed into fresh concrete at 2 – 5 % by cement weight, depending on chloride exposure severity.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** On contact with pore water it dissociates:

  $$ \text{Ca(NO}_2)_2 \rightarrow \text{Ca}^{2+} + 2\,\text{NO}_2^{-} $$

  NO₂⁻ ions diffuse to the rebar surface and **competitively adsorb** at the same sites as Cl⁻. Crucially, the recommended **molar ratio [NO₂⁻] / [Cl⁻] ≥ 1.0** ensures nitrite outcompetes chloride.

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** The **anodic passivation** step involves oxidation of any incipient Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺:

  $$ 2\,\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{NO}_2^{-} + 2\,\text{H}^{+} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Fe}^{3+} + \text{NO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

  The Fe³⁺ combines with available oxygen and water to **reinforce the γ-Fe₂O₃ passive film**, restoring a defect-free, ionically resistive barrier. The schematic in Section 4.4 captures this process.

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Limitations:** (i) Excess nitrite (> 5 %) can aggravate **alkali–silica reaction (ASR)** in reactive aggregates; (ii) loses effectiveness once chloride exceeds the design ratio; (iii) does not stop corrosion already initiated (must be used prophylactically); (iv) cannot replace sound concrete cover or proper mix design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, PEICT866, ESE Pattern]**

> **(a)** Compare admixed vs migrating rebar inhibitors. Under what situations is each preferred? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A reinforced concrete bridge deck is exposed to de-icing salts. The chloride content at the rebar level is estimated at 4.5 kg/m³. The cement content is 400 kg/m³. Using the [NO₂⁻] / [Cl⁻] ≥ 1.0 design rule, calculate the **minimum dose of calcium nitrite** (as 30 % active solution, density 1.30 g/cm³) required per cubic metre of concrete. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** **Admixed inhibitors** (e.g., calcium nitrite, sodium monofluorophosphate) are blended into the fresh concrete mix during batching. They distribute uniformly throughout the cover zone, providing **homogeneous, long-term protection (30+ years)** but require **accurate dosage control** and cannot be retrofitted to existing structures.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** **Migrating inhibitors** (e.g., amino-alcohols, amine-esters) are sprayed, brushed, or injected onto the hardened concrete surface. They **vaporize and diffuse** through the capillary pore network toward the rebar (driven by concentration gradient and hydrophobic repulsion from concrete). They are **ideal for repair and retrofit** of existing chloride-contaminated structures.

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Admixed | Migrating |
|---|---|---|
| Application timing | New construction | New or existing |
| Distribution | Uniform by design | Gradient-based (variable) |
| Long-term reliability | High (well established) | Moderate (penetration depth limited) |
| Cost (per m³) | Lower | Higher (multiple coats) |
| Best use | Bridges, marine piles, new car parks | Repair of carbonated/chloride-laden concrete |

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** **Selection rule of thumb:** Use **admixed** for new construction under known exposure; use **migrating** when retrofitting existing structures or when chloride contamination has already initiated low-level corrosion.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks (Numerical):**

**Given:**
- Chloride at rebar: $[Cl^-] = 4.5$ kg/m³
- Cement content: 400 kg/m³
- Design rule: $\dfrac{[NO_2^-]}{[Cl^-]} \geq 1.0$ (molar)
- Ca(NO₂)₂ solution: 30 % active, density 1.30 g/cm³

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert chloride to moles per m³:
$$ n_{Cl^-} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 1000}{35.45} = 126.94 \;\text{mol/m}^3 $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Required moles of NO₂⁻ (1:1 ratio):
$$ n_{NO_2^-} = 126.94 \;\text{mol/m}^3 $$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Convert to moles of Ca(NO₂)₂. Each mole of Ca(NO₂)₂ (M = 132.09 g/mol) releases 2 moles of NO₂⁻:
$$ n_{Ca(NO_2)_2} = \dfrac{126.94}{2} = 63.47 \;\text{mol/m}^3 $$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Convert to mass of pure Ca(NO₂)₂:
$$ m_{pure} = 63.47 \times 132.09 = 8{,}384 \;\text{g/m}^3 = 8.384 \;\text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Mass of cement, and final % dose:
$$ \%\,\text{dose} = \dfrac{8.384}{400} \times 100 = 2.10\;\% $$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Volume of 30 % commercial solution (density 1.30 g/cm³ = 1300 kg/m³):
$$ m_{solution} = \dfrac{8.384}{0.30} = 27.95 \;\text{kg/m}^3 $$
$$ V_{solution} = \dfrac{27.95}{1300} = 0.02150 \;\text{m}^3/\text{m}^3 = 21.5 \;\text{L/m}^3 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The minimum required dose is **≈ 21.5 L of 30 % Ca(NO₂)₂ solution per cubic metre of concrete** (≈ 2.1 % by cement weight). This falls in the "moderate exposure" range (2 – 3 %), consistent with bridge-deck practice.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on Rebar Inhibitor Questions — and Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing the mechanism** — stating "calcium nitrite forms a coating" instead of "anodic passivator oxidizing Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺." Loss: **1–2 marks** in part (a).
> 2. **Forgetting the molar ratio design rule** — omitting the **[NO₂⁻]/[Cl⁻] ≥ 1.0** condition in numerical problems. Loss: **2 marks** in part (b).
> 3. **Unit errors in numerical problems** — failing to convert kg → g, or g/mol → kg/mol consistently. Loss: **1 mark** per calculation step.
> 4. **Ignoring the 2 mol NO₂⁻ per mol Ca(NO₂)₂ stoichiometry** — this is the most common algebraic mistake. Loss: **1.5 marks**.
> 5. **Naming "sodium nitrite" without recognizing it accelerates ASR** — failing to mention that **calcium nitrite is preferred** for cement compatibility. Loss: **0.5–1 mark** in mechanism questions.
> 6. **Not distinguishing admixed vs migrating** in comparative answers — examiners explicitly test this distinction. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Rebar Inhibitors (PEICT866, Module 4):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Chemical admixtures (admixed or migrating) that protect embedded steel rebar by reinforcing the γ-Fe₂O₃ passive film.
- ✅ **Why needed:** Concrete's natural passivation (pH 12.5 – 13.5) is destroyed by **carbonation** (pH drops below 9) and **chloride ingress** (above ~0.4 % by cement weight).
- ✅ **Most common:** **Calcium nitrite, Ca(NO₂)₂** — an anodic passivator.
- ✅ **Key design criterion:** **[NO₂⁻] / [Cl⁻] ≥ 1.0** (molar).
- ✅ **Acceptable corrosion current density:** $i_{corr} < 0.1$ μA/cm² (passive); 0.1 – 0.5 μA/cm² (low); > 1.0 μA/cm² (severe).
- ✅ **Dosage levels:** 1 – 2 % (low exposure), 2 – 3 % (moderate), 3 – 5 % (high / marine / de-icing salts).
- ✅ **Stern–Geary equation:** $i_{corr} = B / R_p$, with B = 26 mV (active) or B = 52 mV (passive).
- ✅ **Inhibition efficiency formula:** $\eta = \left(1 - R_p^0 / R_p^{inh}\right) \times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Admixed inhibitors** → new construction, uniform distribution, long-term data (30+ years).
- ✅ **Migrating inhibitors** → repair/retrofit, surface-applied, vapor-diffusion mechanism.
- ✅ **Stoichiometry trap:** 1 mol Ca(NO₂)₂ gives **2 mol NO₂⁻** — multiply nitrite moles by 0.5 to get Ca(NO₂)₂ moles.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Cannot stop already-initiated corrosion; excess nitrite (> 5 %) aggravates ASR; not a substitute for adequate cover or quality concrete.
- ✅ **Field applications:** Bridge decks, marine piles, parking garages, tunnels, pre-stressed concrete.
- ✅ **Synergistic systems:** Often combined with **epoxy-coated rebar**, **low-permeability concrete (low w/c)**, and **cathodic protection** for very high exposure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inhibitor Application Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inhibitor Application Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Corrosion Inhibitor Application Techniques** refer to the systematic, engineering methodologies used to introduce chemical inhibitors into a corrosive environment in order to reduce the corrosion rate of the metallic substrate. The objective is to deliver the inhibitor at the **right concentration, at the right location, and at the right time**, ensuring optimal adsorption, film formation, or chemical interaction at the metal-electrolyte interface.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4, inhibitor application techniques are classified based on:
- **Mode of contact** (continuous, batch, slug)
- **Physical state of inhibitor** (liquid, solid, vapor-phase)
- **Mechanism of action** (anodic, cathodic, mixed, adsorption-type, film-forming)
- **System geometry** (closed loop, open recirculating, once-through)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The application technique is NOT the same as the inhibitor chemistry. The same inhibitor (e.g., benzotriazole for copper) can be applied via multiple techniques (immersion, vapour phase, slug dosing, etc.). Examiners frequently test the *distinction between inhibitor TYPE and inhibitor APPLICATION method*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of corrosion inhibitors and their application methods like **medicine and its dosage forms**:

| Medical Analogy | Corrosion Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A paracetamol tablet (drug) | Benzotriazole (inhibitor molecule) |
| A daily oral dose (continuous) | Continuous injection in cooling water |
| A single high dose at intervals (pulse) | Slug treatment |
| Dissolving in drinking water (system-wide) | Closed-loop batch treatment |
| A medicated cream (surface) | Coatings + inhibitor primer |

Just as a doctor decides *how* to deliver a drug (oral, IV, topical, inhaler) based on the patient's condition, a corrosion engineer decides *how* to deliver an inhibitor (continuous, batch, vapour, blended) based on the system geometry, water chemistry, and metal involved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Design Rule:** The application technique must ensure that the inhibitor concentration at the metal surface is always **above the critical threshold concentration** (often in the range of **10–200 ppm** for most industrial systems), and **below the toxicity limit** for environmental discharge regulations.

### 1.3 Why Application Techniques Matter

A highly efficient inhibitor can fail completely if applied incorrectly. For example:
- **Under-dosing** an anodic inhibitor (like chromate) accelerates corrosion (dangerous pitting).
- **Over-dosing** wastes money and may cause fouling, foaming, or microbial growth.
- **Wrong application point** (e.g., adding phosphate before aeration) creates insoluble sludge instead of a protective film.

Hence, application technique engineering is as critical as the inhibitor selection itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Inhibitor Application Techniques

Application techniques are broadly divided into **four** primary families:

#### A. Continuous Injection (Dosing)
- The inhibitor is metered into the system **continuously** using dosing pumps (diaphragm, peristaltic, or electronic).
- Maintains a **steady-state concentration** $C_{ss}$.
- Used in: open recirculating cooling water systems, oil & gas production lines, process cooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass balance for continuous dosing:**
> 
> $$\dot{m}_{inj} = C_{target} \times \dot{V}_{makeup}$$
> 
> where $\dot{m}_{inj}$ is the inhibitor mass flow rate (kg/hr), $C_{target}$ is the target concentration (kg/m³), and $\dot{V}_{makeup}$ is the make-up water flow rate (m³/hr).

#### B. Batch Treatment (Slug Dosing / Pulse Treatment)
- A **bolus** (large single dose) of inhibitor is added at fixed intervals.
- Concentration follows a **decay curve** between doses.
- Used in: closed cooling systems, hydrostatic testing, marine ballast, lay-up preservation.

The inhibitor concentration $C(t)$ decays exponentially:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

where $k$ is the **first-order decay constant** (1/hr) accounting for consumption, blowdown, and reaction losses. The next dose must be applied when $C(t) \geq C_{critical}$.

#### C. Vapour-Phase Inhibition (VPI)
- The inhibitor is a **volatile solid or liquid** that sublimes/evaporates and reaches the metal surface via the gas phase.
- Classic example: **VPI-260 (cyclohexylamine carbonate)** and **dicyclohexylamine nitrite** for ferrous parts in storage/transport.
- Used in: enclosed packages, ship hull holds, intermodal containers, export packaging.

#### D. Pre-Mixed / Co-Applied Inhibition
- Inhibitor is **pre-blended** into a fluid, coating, primer, or working medium.
- Examples: inhibitor-containing engine coolants (with tolyltriazole + silicate + nitrite), self-healing coatings with microencapsulated inhibitors, and inhibited pickling acid.

### 2.2 Selection Criteria for an Application Technique

A KTU 2024 board favourite. The selection tree is:

1. **System type** → Open or closed? Determines continuous vs batch.
2. **Operating temperature** → High T (>150 °C) restricts VPI; favours thermally stable film formers.
3. **Flow regime** → High shear needs film-forming inhibitors (phosphonates, polymeric dispersants).
4. **Water chemistry** → Hardness, pH, dissolved O₂, TDS, chloride.
5. **Discharge regulations** → Cr(VI), Zn²⁺, nitrite restrictions push towards "green" inhibitors.
6. **Capital cost vs operating cost** → Batch = lower capex, higher monitoring; continuous = higher capex, tighter control.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inhibitor Efficiency (IE%)** | $\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0} \times 100$ | $CR_0$ = uninhibited rate, $CR_{inh}$ = inhibited rate (mpy or mm/yr) | Universal performance metric |
| 2 | **Surface Coverage θ** | $\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | Adsorption isotherms |
| 3 | **Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm** | $\dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = K \cdot C$ | $K$ = adsorption equilibrium constant (L/mol), $C$ = inhibitor conc. (mol/L) | Physical/chemical adsorption |
| 4 | **Linearized Langmuir** | $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K} + C$ | Plot of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ gives slope 1, intercept $1/K$ | Determine $K$ & $G_{ads}$ |
| 5 | **Temkin Isotherm** | $\theta = \dfrac{2.303}{2a} \log(K \cdot C)$ | $a$ = lateral interaction parameter | Chemisorbed films |
| 6 | **Freundlich Isotherm** | $\theta = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$ | $K_F, n$ are empirical constants | Heterogeneous surfaces |
| 7 | **Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption** | $\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K)$ | $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K, $T$ in K, 55.5 = molarity of water | Confirms physiorption vs chemisorption |
| 8 | **Continuous Dosing Mass Flow** | $\dot{m}_{inj} = C_{target} \cdot \dot{V}_{makeup}$ | kg/hr | Continuous injection |
| 9 | **Batch Decay Law** | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | $C_0$ = initial dose, $k$ = decay constant | Slug/batch treatment |
| 10 | **Cycles of Concentration (COC)** | $COC = \dfrac{C_{cl}}{C_{makeup}}$ | $C_{cl}$ = cooling tower conc. of salt | Cooling water dosing |
| 11 | **Inhibitor Dose for Cooling Tower** | $C_{inj} = \dfrac{C_{cw} - C_{makeup} \cdot COC}{(1 - 0)} \cdot \text{bleed ratio}$ | ppm | Cooling water |
| 12 | **Pickling Inhibitor Acid-Wash Dosage** | $\dfrac{V_{inh}}{V_{acid}} = 0.1\% \text{ to } 1\%$ by volume | — | Acid cleaning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical threshold rules:**
> - If $|\Delta G_{ads}| < 20$ kJ/mol → **physisorption** (electrostatic).
> - If $|\Delta G_{ads}| > 40$ kJ/mol → **chemisorption** (charge transfer / covalent bond).
> - Between 20–40 kJ/mol → mixed mode.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Industry | Typical Application Technique | Common Inhibitor |
|---|---|---|
| Power plant boilers | Continuous + condensate-line injection | Hydrazine, morpholine, DEHA |
| Cooling water towers | Continuous side-stream | HEDP, ATMP, Zn²⁺, polyacrylates |
| Oil & gas (downhole) | Continuous capillary injection | Imidazoline, quaternary amines |
| Acid pickling | Batch immersion (0.1–1% inhibitor) | Rodine, 2-mercaptobenzothiazole |
| Marine ballast tanks | Slug / batch | Nitrite-borate, vapour-phase blends |
| Storage & shipping | VPI sachet | VPI-260, dicyclohexylamine nitrite |
| Concrete reinforcement | Migratory corrosion inhibitor (MCI) | Amino-alcohol blends |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Inhibitor Efficiency (IE) from Fundamental Corrosion Rates

We start with the **basic corrosion rate equation** in terms of corrosion current density (using Faraday's law):

$$CR = \dfrac{M \cdot i_{corr}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

where $M$ = atomic mass of metal (g/mol), $i_{corr}$ = corrosion current density (A/cm²), $n$ = number of electrons transferred, $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**, $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³).

**Step 1:** Write the uninhibited corrosion rate.

$$CR_0 = \dfrac{M \cdot i_{corr,0}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 2:** Write the inhibited corrosion rate.

$$CR_{inh} = \dfrac{M \cdot i_{corr,inh}}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 3:** Take the ratio. The constants $M$, $n$, $F$, $\rho$ cancel:

$$\dfrac{CR_{inh}}{CR_0} = \dfrac{i_{corr,inh}}{i_{corr,0}}$$

**Step 4:** Define Inhibitor Efficiency:

$$\text{IE}\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{CR_{inh}}{CR_0}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the ratio:

$$\boxed{\text{IE}\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{i_{corr,inh}}{i_{corr,0}}\right) \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0}\right) \times 100}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** IE is dimensionless. An IE of 95% means the corrosion rate has been reduced to 5% of the original value. **However, IE is concentration-dependent** — doubling inhibitor concentration does NOT necessarily double IE due to the asymptotic nature of adsorption isotherms.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm and Free Energy of Adsorption

The Langmuir isotherm is the most widely tested isotherm in KTU Module 4 problems.

**Step 1:** The fraction of metal surface covered by inhibitor is $\theta$. The uncovered fraction is $(1 - \theta)$.

**Step 2:** The rate of adsorption is proportional to the uncovered surface area and to the inhibitor bulk concentration $C$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

**Step 3:** The rate of desorption is proportional to the covered surface area:

$$r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4:** At **equilibrium**, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a \cdot C \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\theta$:

$$k_a \cdot C - k_a \cdot C \cdot \theta = k_d \cdot \theta$$

$$k_a \cdot C = \theta \cdot (k_d + k_a \cdot C)$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a \cdot C}{k_d + k_a \cdot C} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) \cdot C}{1 + (k_a/k_d) \cdot C}$$

**Step 6:** Define the **adsorption equilibrium constant** $K = k_a / k_d$ (units: L/mol):

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K \cdot C}$$

**Step 7:** Invert both sides and rearrange into the **linearized Langmuir** form used in lab data fitting:

$$\dfrac{1 - \theta}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K \cdot C}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta} - 1 = \dfrac{1}{K \cdot C}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\theta} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{K \cdot C}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator:

$$\dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K}$$

**Step 8:** This is the **linearized Langmuir equation**:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K}}$$

> When plotting experimental $C/\theta$ vs $C$, the slope should be **1.0** and the intercept should be $1/K$. Any deviation from slope = 1 indicates multi-layer adsorption or interaction between adsorbate molecules (a Temkin/Frumkin case).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Gibbs Free Energy of Adsorption

The thermodynamic equilibrium constant for the adsorption process, when expressed on a standard state basis (1 mol/L in solution), uses the factor **55.5 mol/L** (the molar concentration of water):

$$K_{ads} = 55.5 \cdot K$$

**Step 1:** Apply the Gibbs free energy equation at equilibrium:

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln K_{ads}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $K_{ads}$:

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K)$$

**Step 3:** Insert $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K and 55.5 (mol/L of water at 25 °C):

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -8.314 \cdot T \cdot \ln(55.5 \cdot K)$$

> For a typical KTU numerical: if $K = 10^{4}$ L/mol at 298 K,
> $\Delta G_{ads} = -8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(55.5 \times 10^4) = -27.6$ kJ/mol
> This is **physisorption** (electrostatic, mild).

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example — Batch Treatment Dosing Interval

**Problem:** A closed hot-water heating system of 5000 L volume has a critical inhibitor concentration $C_{crit} = 30$ ppm. The initial slug dose gives $C_0 = 200$ ppm. The decay constant is $k = 0.003$ /hr. Calculate:
(a) The time after which re-dosing is required.
(b) The inhibitor efficiency at $C_0 = 200$ ppm if it is known that the rate constant is proportional to coverage via $CR_{inh} = CR_0(1-\theta)$ and $\theta$ follows Langmuir with $K = 5 \times 10^4$ L/mol at $C = 200$ ppm.

**Solution (a):**
Use the batch decay law: $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$. Set $C(t) = C_{crit}$:

$$30 = 200 \cdot e^{-0.003 \cdot t}$$

$$e^{-0.003 t} = 0.15$$

$$-0.003 \, t = \ln(0.15) = -1.8971$$

$$t = 632 \text{ hours} \approx 26.3 \text{ days}$$

**Solution (b):**
Compute $\theta$ from Langmuir. First, note that $C$ must be in mol/L. For a generic inhibitor with $M = 100$ g/mol:

$$C = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ g/mL}}{100 \text{ g/mol}} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/mL} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L} = 2 \text{ mM}$$

Now apply Langmuir: $\theta/(1-\theta) = K \cdot C$:

$$\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = (5 \times 10^4) \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) = 100$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{100}{101} = 0.9901$$

Inhibitor Efficiency:

$$\text{IE}\% = \theta \times 100 = 99.01\%$$

> This is excellent protection — 99% of the surface is covered.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Application Techniques

| Parameter | Continuous Injection | Batch (Slug) | Vapour-Phase | Pre-Mixed / Coating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capex** | High (pumps, sensors) | Low (tank only) | Very low (sachet) | Medium (blend step) |
| **Opex** | Medium | Medium-high | Negligible | Low |
| **Control accuracy** | High ±5% | Low ±30% | Low | High |
| **Surge protection** | Excellent | Weak (between doses) | N/A | Excellent |
| **Suitable for** | Open loops, long pipelines | Closed systems, lay-up | Enclosed spaces | One-shot systems |
| **Risk of under-dose** | Very low | High | High | Very low |
| **Typical concentration** | 5–200 ppm | 100–1000 ppm (initial) | Sublimation rate | 0.1–5% w/w |
| **Failure mode** | Pump failure | Forgotten dosing | Seal failure | Coating breach |
| **KTU exam frequency** | High | High | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Inhibitor Application Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inhibitor Application Techniques] --> B[Continuous Injection]
    A --> C[Batch / Slug Treatment]
    A --> D[Vapour Phase VPI]
    A --> E[Pre-Mixed Co-Applied]

    B --> B1[Open Recirculating Cooling]
    B --> B2[Once-Through Cooling]
    B --> B3[Oil and Gas Pipelines]
    B --> B4[Boiler Condensate Lines]

    C --> C1[Closed Loop Heating Systems]
    C --> C2[Marine Ballast Lay-Up]
    C --> C3[Hydrostatic Test Water]
    C --> C4[Acid Pickling Baths]

    D --> D1[Export Packaging Sachet]
    D --> D2[Storage Hold Protection]
    D --> D3[Intermodal Container]
    D --> D4[Spare Parts Bin]

    E --> E1[Engine Coolant Premix]
    E --> E2[Self Healing Coating]
    E --> E3[Inhibited Hydraulic Fluid]
    E --> E4[Concrete Migratory MCI]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flowchart for Selecting an Application Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Selection] --> Q1{Is system closed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is it long-term storage?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Go to Continuous Injection]

    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Is the space airtight?}
    Q2 -- No --> C1[Batch Slug at Intervals]

    Q4 -- Yes --> D1[Use VPI Vapour Phase]
    Q4 -- No --> C1

    Q3 --> Q5{Is water chemistry variable?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Dosing Pump with Feedback]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7[Use Bleed-Feedback Dosing]

    Q6 --> R1[Online Sensor Controlled]
    Q7 --> R2[Hydraulic Feedback Controller]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Continuous Cooling Water Inhibitor Injection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SW[Raw Makeup Water] --> T1[Cooling Tower Basin]
    T1 --> P1[Recirculation Pump]
    P1 --> HE[Heat Exchanger Process Side]
    HE --> T1
    T1 --> BD[Bleed to Drain]

    subgraph Inhibitor Dosing Subsystem
        TK[Inhibitor Storage Tank] --> SP[Stainless Dosing Pump]
        SP --> CK[Check Valve and Strainer]
        CK --> INJ[Injection Quill at Tower Basin]
    end

    INJ -.Concentrated Solution.-> T1

    subgraph Control Subsystem
        SEN[Conductivity pH ORP Sensor] --> CTLR[PLC Controller]
        CTLR -- Modulates Stroke --> SP
        CTLR -- Alarm --> OP[Operator HMI]
    end

    SEN -.Inline Measurement.-> T1
```

### 4.4 Application Technique vs Inhibitor Compatibility Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mechanism Domain] --> AN[Anodic Inhibitors]
    M[Mechanism Domain] --> CA[Cathodic Inhibitors]
    M[Mechanism Domain] --> MX[Mixed Inhibitors]
    M[Mechanism Domain] --> AD[Adsorption Type]

    AN --> AN1[Chromate Phosphate Molybdate]
    AN --> AN2[Best: Continuous with feedback]
    AN --> AN3[NEVER batch — under-dose causes pitting]

    CA --> CA1[Zinc Polyphosphate Calcium]
    CA --> CA2[Best: Continuous side stream]
    CA --> CA3[Batch allowed for low risk closed loop]

    MX --> MX1[Amines Imidazolines]
    MX --> MX2[Best: Continuous for O and G]
    MX --> MX3[Batch for acid pickling]

    AD --> AD1[Benzotriazole Tolyltriazole]
    AD --> AD2[Best: VPI for storage]
    AD --> AD3[Continuous for closed loops]
```

### 4.5 Slug-Dose Concentration Decay Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Time axis
        T0[T = 0] --> T1[T = 12 hr]
        T1 --> T2[T = 24 hr]
        T2 --> T3[T = 632 hr]
    end

    T0 -- C0 = 200 ppm --> N1[New dose added]
    N1 -- exponential decay --> N2[Inhibitor consumption]
    N2 -- reaches Ccrit --> N3[Re-dose required]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Vapour Phase Inhibitor" (VPI).** Give **two** examples of VPI chemicals and explain the mechanism by which they protect ferrous metals during storage and transport.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Vapour Phase Inhibitor (VPI)** is a volatile organic corrosion inhibitor that protects metals in an enclosed atmosphere by sublimating (or evaporating) and subsequently **adsorbing on the metal surface** to form a thin protective molecular film.

**Examples (1 mark):**
- Cyclohexylamine carbonate (CHC)
- Dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN)

**Mechanism (2 marks):** The volatile inhibitor molecules vaporize and saturate the air inside the package. On reaching the metal surface, the vapour **hydrolyses with trace moisture** to release protective anions (e.g., nitrite, carbonate) and the amine cation. The amine cation adsorbs on the cathodic sites while the anion passivates the anodic sites, forming a duplex protective monolayer of approximately 1–3 nm thickness.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between "continuous inhibitor injection" and "batch (slug) inhibitor treatment"** in cooling water systems. Mention **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of each technique.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Continuous Injection | Batch / Slug Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Steady metering via dosing pump | Single bolus at intervals |
| **Concentration profile** | Near-constant | Exponential decay between doses |
| **Suitable system** | Open recirculating | Closed loop / lay-up |
| **Advantage** | Tight control, prevents under-dosing | Low capex, simple, no pump |
| **Disadvantage** | High capex, sensor dependency | Risk of falling below $C_{crit}$ |

(1 mark for the table, 1 mark for the advantage, 1 mark for the disadvantage)

---

### Part B — Module 4 Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **different techniques used to apply corrosion inhibitors** in industrial systems. Classify them based on (i) mode of contact, (ii) physical state, and (iii) operating system geometry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel specimen is exposed to 1 M H₂SO₄ at 25 °C. The uninhibited corrosion rate is **12.5 mpy**. With the addition of **0.5 g/L of 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT)**, the corrosion rate drops to **0.85 mpy**.
   (i) Calculate the **inhibitor efficiency (IE%)**.
   (ii) Calculate the **surface coverage θ** and verify whether the inhibitor obeys the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm**, given that the equilibrium constant $K = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$ L/mol and the inhibitor molecular weight is 167 g/mol.
   (iii) Calculate the **standard Gibbs free energy of adsorption** $\Delta G_{ads}$ at 298 K. Comment on the nature of adsorption (physisorption or chemisorption). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The application techniques of corrosion inhibitors are classified as follows:

**(i) Classification by Mode of Contact (2 marks):**
1. **Continuous Injection** – Inhibitor is metered continuously using dosing pumps. Example: Cooling tower water dosed with HEDP at 30 ppm.
2. **Batch / Slug Treatment** – A bolus dose is added at fixed intervals, e.g., closed-loop dosing with nitrite-borate blend every 4 weeks.
3. **Vapour Phase Inhibition (VPI)** – Volatile inhibitor saturates enclosed atmosphere, e.g., VPI-260 in storage.
4. **Pre-mixed / Co-applied** – Inhibitor blended in coating or fluid, e.g., tolyltriazole in engine coolant.

**(ii) Classification by Physical State (2 marks):**
1. **Liquid inhibitors** – Most common; water-soluble or oil-soluble amines, imidazolines.
2. **Solid inhibitors** – Powder, pellets (sodium molybdate), or tablets (slow-release).
3. **Vapour-phase** – Sublimable solids (CHC, DICHAN).
4. **Polymer-bound / encapsulated** – Microcapsules in self-healing coatings.

**(iii) Classification by System Geometry (3 marks):**
1. **Open recirculating systems** → Continuous injection with feedback (e.g., cooling towers, ORP-controlled chromate programs).
2. **Closed loop / sealed systems** → Batch / slug treatment (e.g., central heating water, hydraulic test water).
3. **Once-through systems** → Single-pass high-dose injection (e.g., hydrostatic test, one-shot descaling).
4. **Storage / packaging** → VPI sachets, vapour wraps, oil-impregnated paper.
5. **Downhole / pipeline** → Continuous capillary injection, "batch + squeeze" treatment.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Inhibitor Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{12.5 - 0.85}{12.5} \times 100$$

$$\text{IE}\% = \dfrac{11.65}{12.5} \times 100 = \mathbf{93.2\%}$$

**(ii) Surface Coverage and Langmuir Verification (3 marks):**

Surface coverage:

$$\theta = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_{inh}}{CR_0} = \dfrac{11.65}{12.5} = \mathbf{0.932}$$

Convert inhibitor concentration to mol/L:

$$C = \dfrac{0.5 \text{ g/L}}{167 \text{ g/mol}} = 2.994 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/L}$$

Calculate predicted $\theta$ from Langmuir:

$$\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K \cdot C = (1.2 \times 10^4) \times (2.994 \times 10^{-3}) = 35.93$$

$$\theta_{predicted} = \dfrac{35.93}{36.93} = 0.973$$

The experimental $\theta = 0.932$ is close to the predicted Langmuir $\theta = 0.973$ (deviation ≈ 4.5%). The slight deviation may be due to surface heterogeneity. **The inhibitor approximately obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm** within acceptable engineering error.

**(iii) Free Energy of Adsorption (2 marks):**

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K)$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -(8.314)(298) \ln(55.5 \times 1.2 \times 10^{4})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -2477.6 \times \ln(6.66 \times 10^{5})$$

$$\Delta G_{ads} = -2477.6 \times 13.41 = -33{,}224 \text{ J/mol} = \mathbf{-33.22 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

Since $|\Delta G_{ads}| = 33.22$ kJ/mol lies in the range **20–40 kJ/mol**, the adsorption is a **mixed mode — predominantly physisorption with a contribution from chemisorption** (charge-transfer interaction between the S and N heteroatoms of MBT and the iron d-orbitals).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative to Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a **schematic layout**, describe the **continuous inhibitor dosing system** used in an open recirculating cooling water circuit. Explain the role of conductivity, pH, and ORP sensors in the feedback control loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slug dose of inhibitor is added to a closed hot-water system of 10,000 L volume. The initial concentration is 250 ppm. The first-order decay constant is $k = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ /hr and the critical minimum concentration is 50 ppm.
   (i) Determine the **re-dosing interval** in days.
   (ii) If the inhibitor efficiency is found to be 90% at the critical concentration, calculate the **mass of pure inhibitor** required per dose if the commercial product is 35% w/w active ingredient. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Schematic (4 marks):** Refer to the **Block Architecture diagram in Section 4.3** of these notes. The system comprises: storage tank → dosing pump → check valve → injection quill → cooling tower basin. Sensors: conductivity, pH, ORP mounted on a side-stream loop. PLC receives sensor signals, compares with setpoints, and modulates the dosing pump stroke.

**Role of Sensors (3 marks):**
- **Conductivity sensor** → measures total dissolved solids (TDS) via cycles of concentration; controls **bleed valve** to prevent excessive salt build-up which would destabilize inhibitor films.
- **pH sensor** → maintains pH typically 6.5–8.5. Outside this window, calcium phosphate and zinc-based inhibitors precipitate or become ineffective. pH also controls amine volatility in condensate inhibitors.
- **ORP sensor (Oxidation-Reduction Potential)** → measures the redox state of the water. For nitrite/molybdate programs, ORP indicates the residual oxidizing inhibitor concentration. If ORP drops below threshold, dosing pump accelerates to restore the protective regime.

The three sensors together form a **multi-parameter feedback loop** that prevents under-dosing (which causes pitting) and over-dosing (which wastes inhibitor and harms discharge compliance).

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Re-dosing Interval (4 marks):**

Apply the batch decay law:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$$

Set $C(t) = C_{crit} = 50$ ppm, $C_0 = 250$ ppm, $k = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ /hr:

$$50 = 250 \cdot e^{-0.004 t}$$

$$e^{-0.004 t} = 0.20$$

$$-0.004 t = \ln(0.20) = -1.6094$$

$$t = 402.4 \text{ hours} = \dfrac{402.4}{24} = \mathbf{16.77 \text{ days}}$$

**Therefore, re-dosing is required approximately every 16 days.**

**(ii) Mass of Pure Inhibitor per Dose (3 marks):**

Total inhibitor mass per dose = $C_0 \times V_{system}$:

$$m_{inh} = 250 \text{ mg/L} \times 10{,}000 \text{ L} = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ mg} = 2.5 \text{ kg of pure inhibitor}$$

The commercial product is 35% w/w active. Therefore:

$$m_{product} = \dfrac{2.5 \text{ kg}}{0.35} = \mathbf{7.143 \text{ kg of commercial product per dose}}$$

**Verification of IE = 90% at $C_{crit} = 50$ ppm (bonus 1 mark):**
$\theta = IE/100 = 0.90$. This is the actual inhibited surface coverage at the re-dosing trigger — the system is sized to maintain $\theta \geq 0.90$ throughout the cycle, dropping to this value exactly when re-dosing is initiated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in $C$ before applying Langmuir:** Students often plug ppm directly into $K \cdot C$ where $K$ is in L/mol. Always convert ppm → mol/L using $C_{mol/L} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times 10^{-6}}{M_{inhibitor}}$.
> 2. **Missing the factor 55.5 in $\Delta G_{ads}$:** The Gibbs free energy uses the *aqueous* equilibrium constant $K_{ads} = 55.5 \cdot K$. Forgetting 55.5 leads to an answer ~3.99 times too small in magnitude.
> 3. **Confusing inhibitor TYPE with APPLICATION method:** A question about VPI tests your knowledge of *application technique*, not just chemistry. List: volatility requirement, atmospheric saturation, surface adsorption — not just "what is the molecule."
> 4. **Confusing batch vs continuous benefits in numericals:** Re-dosing interval is governed by $C_{crit}$ — students often stop solving at $t = \ln(0.5)/k$ (the *half-life*) which is the WRONG trigger. Use the critical concentration, not 50%.
> 5. **Wrong sign in $\Delta G_{ads}$:** $\Delta G_{ads}$ for **spontaneous adsorption is negative**. If you get a positive value, you inverted $\ln(55.5 \cdot K)$ to negative.
> 6. **Neglecting to comment on physiorption vs chemisorption:** A 3-mark question on $\Delta G_{ads}$ ALWAYS requires the qualitative classification (physisorption $< 20$ kJ/mol, chemisorption $> 40$ kJ/mol, mixed otherwise).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Inhibitor Application Techniques**

- [x] **Definition:** Application technique = *how* an inhibitor is delivered; it is independent of the inhibitor chemistry.
- [x] **Four primary techniques:** Continuous, Batch/Slug, Vapour-Phase (VPI), Pre-mixed/Co-applied.
- [x] **Continuous injection** is used for **open** systems; **batch treatment** for **closed** systems.
- [x] **VPI** requires (a) a volatile inhibitor, (b) an **enclosed** atmosphere, (c) trace moisture for hydrolysis.
- [x] **Critical threshold concentration $C_{crit}$:** Always dose above this — for anodic inhibitors, dosing *below* threshold is **worse than no inhibitor** (pitting).
- [x] **Inhibitor Efficiency formula:** $\text{IE}\% = (CR_0 - CR_{inh})/CR_0 \times 100$.
- [x] **Surface coverage** $\theta$ is identical to $\text{IE}$ (in fraction form).
- [x] **Langmuir isotherm (linearized):** $C/\theta = C + 1/K$.
- [x] **Langmuir assumption:** Slope of $C/\theta$ vs $C$ plot = 1.0 (any deviation implies multi-layer or interactive adsorption).
- [x] **Temkin isotherm** applies when there is strong lateral interaction between adsorbed inhibitor molecules.
- [x] **Free energy equation:** $\Delta G_{ads} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K)$. **The 55.5 factor is mandatory.**
- [x] **Magnitude rule for $\Delta G_{ads}$:** $< 20$ kJ/mol → physisorption; $> 40$ kJ/mol → chemisorption; 20–40 kJ/mol → mixed.
- [x] **Batch decay law:** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$. Re-dose when $C(t) = C_{crit}$, NOT when $C(t) = C_0/2$.
- [x] **Continuous dosing equation:** $\dot{m}_{inj} = C_{target} \cdot \dot{V}_{makeup}$.
- [x] **Cycles of concentration (COC):** $COC = C_{cooling}/C_{makeup}$; controls bleed valve and inhibitor retention.
- [x] **VPI examples (must memorize):** Cyclohexylamine carbonate (CHC), dicyclohexylamine nitrite (DICHAN), guanidine carbonate.
- [x] **Selection rules:** Open system + variable chemistry → continuous with sensor feedback. Closed storage → VPI. One-shot operation → pre-mixed.
- [x] **Common industrial pairings:** Cooling tower → phosphonates + Zn²⁺ + polyacrylate. Boiler condensate → morpholine / DEHA. Pickling → 0.1–1% MBT / Rodine. Downhole → imidazoline / quat amine.
- [x] **Unit conversion (mandatory):** ppm × 10⁻⁶ ÷ Molecular Weight = mol/L (for aqueous inhibitors).
- [x] **Final caution for paper:** Always state assumptions, state units, convert units, and **comment on $\Delta G_{ads}$ magnitude** explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inhibitor Efficiency and Inhibitor Concentration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT866 - CORROSION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inhibitor Efficiency and Inhibitor Concentration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inhibitor Efficiency (IE)**, denoted as **%IE** or **η**, is a dimensionless quantitative metric that expresses the *percentage reduction* in the corrosion rate (or any equivalent corrosion parameter such as weight loss, corrosion current density, or polarization resistance) achieved by introducing a corrosion inhibitor into the aggressive electrolyte, measured **relative to the uninhibited (blank) condition** under otherwise identical operating conditions.

Mathematically, it is defined as the fractional decrease in corrosion rate normalised against the uninhibited corrosion rate:

$$\%IE = \left( \frac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \right) \times 100$$

where $CR_{0}$ is the corrosion rate **without** inhibitor and $CR_{i}$ is the corrosion rate **with** inhibitor at a given concentration $C_{inh}$.

**Inhibitor Concentration** ($C_{inh}$), usually expressed in **ppm**, **mg/L**, **mol/L**, or **wt%**, is the dosage of inhibitor deliberately dissolved into the corrosive medium to suppress the anodic, cathodic, or both half-cell reactions of the corrosion cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Coverage (θ)** is the corollary dimensionless parameter tied to IE. When θ = 1, inhibition is 100%; when θ = 0, no inhibition occurs. The fundamental bridge between IE and θ is:

$$\theta = \frac{\%IE}{100}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are watering a garden with a leaky hose that loses 100 drops per minute through 10 pinholes. You add a special "plug compound" into the water (this is your **inhibitor**) that flows along with the water and seals the pinholes.

- The **drop in leakage from 100 → 10 drops/min** is your *corrosion rate reduction*.
- The **percentage reduction (90%)** is the **Inhibitor Efficiency**.
- The **amount of plug compound you put into the bucket** is your **Inhibitor Concentration**.

If you add *too little* compound, only a few pinholes plug → low IE. If you add the *optimum* amount, all pinholes plug → IE plateaus at ~100%. If you add *excessive* compound, the viscosity changes, turbulence may increase, and some pinholes might actually re-open → IE may *fall*. This is exactly how real inhibitor systems behave in the laboratory.

The mapping between "compound dose" and "leakage reduction" is captured by **adsorption isotherms** (Langmuir, Temkin, Freundlich, Frumkin), which we will study in Section 2.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **1 Faraday constant** $F = 96{,}485$ **C mol⁻¹**
- **Gas constant** $R = 8.314$ **J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹**
- **Adsorption equilibrium constant $K_{ads}$** — units of **L mol⁻¹** or **M⁻¹**
- **Surface coverage θ** — dimensionless, $0 \le \theta \le 1$
- **Critical inhibitor concentration $C_{crit}$** — minimum concentration for measurable protection
- **Optimum inhibitor concentration $C_{opt}$** — concentration giving peak IE
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The "Why & How"

The action of a corrosion inhibitor is fundamentally a **surface phenomenon**. The inhibitor molecule migrates to the metal/electrolyte interface and forms a protective adsorbed film (physisorbed or chemisorbed). The effectiveness of this film is quantified by IE.

### 2.1.1 Why does inhibitor concentration matter?

- Below $C_{crit}$: inhibitor molecules are too few to form a continuous monolayer → θ is small → %IE is low.
- At $C_{opt}$: monolayer coverage is essentially complete → θ → 1 → %IE plateaus near 100%.
- Above $C_{opt}$: aggregation, micelle formation, or desorption can occur → θ may decrease → %IE may drop (this is the **"negative concentration effect"**).

### 2.1.2 How is IE measured experimentally?

Three canonical experimental methods are accepted by KTU:

1. **Gravimetric (Weight-Loss) Method** — immersion coupons.
2. **Electrochemical Method** — Tafel extrapolation, Linear Polarization Resistance (LPR), EIS.
3. **Hydrogen Evolution Method** — for acid pickling baths.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you must know to solve any IE / $C_{inh}$ problem in the KTU University Examination.

| # | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%IE = \left( \dfrac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \right) \times 100$ | $CR_{0}$ = uninhibited rate; $CR_{i}$ = inhibited rate | CR in **mm/yr** or **mpy** |
| 2 | $\%IE = \left( \dfrac{W_{0} - W_{i}}{W_{0}} \right) \times 100$ | $W_{0}$ = blank weight loss; $W_{i}$ = inhibited weight loss | $W$ in **mg** or **g** |
| 3 | $\%IE = \left( \dfrac{i_{corr}^{0} - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^{0}} \right) \times 100$ | $i_{corr}^{0}$ and $i_{corr}^{inh}$ are uninhibited / inhibited corrosion current densities | $i_{corr}$ in **µA/cm²** |
| 4 | $\%IE = \left( \dfrac{R_{p}^{inh} - R_{p}^{0}}{R_{p}^{inh}} \right) \times 100$ | $R_{p}^{0}$, $R_{p}^{inh}$ = polarisation resistance without / with inhibitor | $R_{p}$ in **Ω·cm²** |
| 5 | $\theta = \dfrac{\%IE}{100}$ | Fractional surface coverage | Dimensionless, $0 \le \theta \le 1$ |
| 6 | Langmuir: $\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | $K_{ads}$ = adsorption equilibrium constant | Linear form: $\dfrac{C}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}} + C$ |
| 7 | Temkin: $\theta = \dfrac{1}{f}\ln(K_{ads} \cdot C)$ | $f$ = lateral interaction parameter | Accounts for repulsion between adsorbed species |
| 8 | Freundlich: $\theta = K_{ads} \cdot C^{n}$ | $n$ = heterogeneity factor ($0 < n < 1$) | Empirical; heterogeneous surfaces |
| 9 | Frumkin: $\dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} \exp(-2f\theta) = K_{ads} \cdot C$ | Full interaction model | For strong lateral interactions |
| 10 | $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads})$ | Standard free energy of adsorption | 55.5 = molar concentration of water in **mol/L** |
| 11 | $\%IE \propto \log C$ (for certain inhibitors) | Linear semi-log relationship | Useful for graphical extrapolation |
| 12 | Synergism Parameter: $S_{\theta} = \dfrac{1 - \theta_{A} - \theta_{B} + \theta_{A}\theta_{B}}{1 - \theta_{AB}}$ | $\theta_{AB}$ = combined surface coverage | $S_{\theta} > 1$ → synergism; $S_{\theta} < 1$ → antagonism |

### 2.2.1 Free-Energy Interpretation (Why some inhibitors are physical vs. chemical)

- $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} \le -20$ **kJ mol⁻¹** → **Physisorption** (electrostatic, weak)
- $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} \ge -40$ **kJ mol⁻¹** → **Chemisorption** (charge sharing/transfer, strong)
- $-40 < \Delta G_{ads}^{0} < -20$ **kJ mol⁻¹** → **Mixed mode**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Industry**: Continuous injection of inhibitors at the wellhead requires knowledge of $C_{opt}$ to minimise cost while protecting pipelines from $\text{CO}_2$/$\text{H}_2\text{S}$ corrosion.
- **Pickling Baths**: Pickling inhibitors (e.g., Rodine) require precise dosing to prevent acid attack on mild steel.
- **Cooling Water Systems**: Phosphonate/azole blends need dosing in the **5–50 ppm** range — IE vs. $C_{inh}$ curves are the design basis.
- **Automotive Radiators**: Glycol-based coolants with silicate/benzoate inhibitors are dosed against phase diagrams of IE.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Directive**: KTU Board problems on this topic typically expect you to (i) calculate IE from weight-loss or $i_{corr}$ data, (ii) compute θ, and (iii) verify Langmuir/Temkin adsorption. We derive the key equations explicitly below.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inhibitor Efficiency from Corrosion Rate

**Starting Statement**:
The inhibitor efficiency is defined as the fractional reduction in the rate of metal dissolution.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define the rate without inhibitor} &\quad CR_{0} = \dfrac{W_{0}}{A \cdot t} \\
\text{Step 2: Define the rate with inhibitor} &\quad CR_{i} = \dfrac{W_{i}}{A \cdot t} \\
\text{Step 3: Define the fractional reduction} &\quad \eta = \dfrac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \\
\text{Step 4: Substitute from Step 1 and 2} &\quad \eta = \dfrac{\dfrac{W_{0}}{A \cdot t} - \dfrac{W_{i}}{A \cdot t}}{\dfrac{W_{0}}{A \cdot t}} \\
\text{Step 5: Cancel common factor (A·t)} &\quad \eta = \dfrac{W_{0} - W_{i}}{W_{0}} \\
\text{Step 6: Express as percentage} &\quad \%IE = \dfrac{W_{0} - W_{i}}{W_{0}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the form universally accepted in KTU board examinations for the **gravimetric method**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm from Inhibitor Data

**Assumption**: The metal surface offers a finite number of identical adsorption sites, each of which can hold only one inhibitor molecule (monolayer, no interaction).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Surface reaction} &\quad M_{(s)} + \text{Inh}_{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons M\text{–Inh}_{(ads)} \\
\text{Step 2: Equilibrium constant} &\quad K_{ads} = \dfrac{\theta}{(1 - \theta) \cdot C} \\
\text{Step 3: Rearrange} &\quad \dfrac{\theta}{1 - \theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C \\
\text{Step 4: Invert both sides} &\quad \dfrac{1 - \theta}{\theta} = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C} \\
\text{Step 5: Expand LHS} &\quad \dfrac{1}{\theta} - 1 = \dfrac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C} \\
\text{Step 6: Solve for 1/θ} &\quad \dfrac{1}{\theta} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads} \cdot C} \\
\text{Step 7: Reciprocal form (Linear Langmuir)} &\quad \dfrac{C}{\theta} = C + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: A plot of $C/\theta$ (y-axis) vs. $C$ (x-axis) yields a **straight line** of slope **1** and intercept $1/K_{ads}$. The slope = 1 is the *hallmark* of Langmuir behaviour — this is your KTU board verification.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free Energy of Adsorption from $K_{ads}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Standard state of adsorption} &\quad \Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln K_{ads}' \\
\text{Step 2: Convert K' to dimensionless form} &\quad K_{ads}' = K_{ads} \cdot (\rho_{\text{water}} / M_{\text{water}}) \\
\text{Step 3: ρ/M for water} &\quad 1000 \text{ g/L} / 18 \text{ g/mol} = 55.5 \text{ mol/L} \\
\text{Step 4: Substitute} &\quad \Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads}) \\
\text{Step 5: At T = 298 K} &\quad \Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -8.314 \times 298 \times \ln(55.5 K_{ads}) \\
\text{Step 6: Numerical coefficient} &\quad \Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -2477.6 \times \ln(55.5 K_{ads}) \text{ J mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: Mild-steel coupons are immersed in 1 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ for 6 h. Weight loss without inhibitor = **245 mg**; weight loss with **0.5 mM** inhibitor = **34 mg**. Calculate (a) IE, (b) surface coverage θ, and (c) verify whether it obeys Langmuir isotherm given $K_{ads} = 2.1 \times 10^{4}$ **M⁻¹**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Inhibitor Efficiency:} &\quad \%IE = \dfrac{245 - 34}{245} \times 100 \\
&\quad = \dfrac{211}{245} \times 100 \\
&\quad = 86.12\% \\[4pt]
\text{(b) Surface Coverage:} &\quad \theta = \dfrac{\%IE}{100} = \dfrac{86.12}{100} = 0.8612 \\[4pt]
\text{(c) Langmuir verification — theoretical θ at 0.5 mM:} &\quad \dfrac{\theta}{1-\theta} = K_{ads} \cdot C \\
&\quad = (2.1 \times 10^{4}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 10.5 \\
&\quad \theta = \dfrac{10.5}{11.5} = 0.9130 \\
\text{Comparison:} &\quad \theta_{experimental}(0.8612) \approx \theta_{theoretical}(0.9130) \\
&\quad \text{Good agreement — Langmuir isotherm is obeyed.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Concentration-Effect Curves — Three Canonical Shapes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU often asks students to **sketch and identify** these curves. They are critical.

### Shape 1 — Monotonically Increasing Plateau (Most Common)
- **Examples**: Most organic inhibitors (amines, azoles).
- **Mathematical form**: Langmuir / Temkin.
- **Behaviour**: IE rises sharply at low C, then plateaus near 100%.

### Shape 2 — Bell-Shaped (Optimum then Decline)
- **Examples**: Some polymeric inhibitors, certain phosphates.
- **Reason**: At high C, micelles form; the active monomer concentration drops.

### Shape 3 — Sigmoidal (Threshold Behaviour)
- **Examples**: Some mixed inhibitors, $\text{NaNO}_2$ for steel in chloride solutions.
- **Reason**: A critical $C_{crit}$ must be exceeded before passivation kicks in; below this threshold, IE ≈ 0.

### Shape 4 — Step / Sudden Passivation
- **Examples**: Anodic inhibitors like chromates (legacy), $\text{Na}_2\text{MoO}_4$.
- **Reason**: Above a narrow concentration window, the metal spontaneously passivates and IE jumps from 0 → >95%.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams below are Mermaid **flowcharts** that summarise the conceptual logic of inhibitor efficiency determination and the concentration-effect relationship. They are specifically designed to be Mermaid-renderable in any Markdown viewer that supports Mermaid v9+.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Determination of Inhibitor Efficiency

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Experiment Start]) --> prepA[Prepare two identical metal coupons]
    prepA --> blankSet[Coupon A: Immerse in blank acid NaCl]
    prepA --> inhSet[Coupon B: Immerse in inhibitor-doped electrolyte]
    blankSet --> timeA[Expose for fixed time t hours]
    inhSet --> timeB[Expose for identical time t hours]
    timeA --> weightA[Measure weight loss W0 blank]
    timeB --> weightB[Measure weight loss Wi inhibited]
    weightA --> calcNode[Apply formula percent IE equals W0 minus Wi divided by W0 times 100]
    weightB --> calcNode
    calcNode --> thetaNode[Compute theta equals percent IE divided by 100]
    thetaNode --> adsorpNode[Fit theta versus C data to Langmuir or Temkin isotherm]
    adsorpNode --> dGNode[Extract Kads and compute delta Gads zero equals minus RT ln 55.5 Kads]
    dGNode --> classifyNode{Is delta Gads less than minus 40 kilojoule per mole}
    classifyNode -- yes --> chemo[Chemisorption strong bonding]
    classifyNode -- no --> physis[Physisorption weak electrostatic]
    chemo --> endNode([Report IE mechanism concentration and free energy])
    physis --> endNode
```

## 4.2 Concentration-Effect Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cStart([Inhibitor concentration C ppm]) --> regionLow[Region 1: Below Ccrit]
    regionLow --> r1Out[IE is near zero because θ is small]
    cStart --> regionMid[Region 2: Between Ccrit and Copt]
    regionMid --> r2Out[IE rises sharply as θ approaches unity Langmuir growth]
    cStart --> regionHigh[Region 3: Above Copt]
    regionHigh --> r3Out{Behaviour depends on inhibitor type}
    r3Out --> platea[A: Plateau near 100 percent IE]
    r3Out --> decline[B: Bell shape decline due to micelle formation]
    r3Out --> step[C: Sudden jump to passivation step shape]
    r2Out --> synergy[Check for synergism with second inhibitor]
    synergy --> synEq{S theta greater than 1}
    synEq -- yes --> synYes[Synergistic combination confirmed]
    synEq -- no --> synNo[Antagonism or no synergy]
```

## 4.3 Inhibitor Classification Block Diagram (by Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root([Corrosion Inhibitors]) --> anodic[Anodic Inhibitors]
    root --> cathodic[Cathodic Inhibitors]
    root --> mixed[Mixed Inhibitors]
    anodic --> a1[Examples: Chromates Phosphate Molybdates]
    anodic --> a2[Mechanism: Form passive oxide layer]
    cathodic --> c1[Examples: Zinc Sulphate Polyphosphate]
    cathodic --> c2[Mechanism: Reduce oxygen reduction rate]
    mixed --> m1[Examples: Amines Azoles Thiourea]
    mixed --> m2[Mechanism: Block both anodic and cathodic sites by adsorption]
    m2 --> eff[Inhibitor efficiency IE measured as percent reduction in corrosion rate]
```

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *Inhibitor Efficiency*. Mention any **two** methods by which it can be determined experimentally.

**Model Answer (Key-Points Layout):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: Inhibitor Efficiency is the percentage reduction in corrosion rate (or weight loss, or corrosion current density) caused by the addition of a corrosion inhibitor to the corrosive medium.

$$\%IE = \left( \frac{CR_{0} - CR_{i}}{CR_{0}} \right) \times 100$$

**Two experimental methods:**

1. **Gravimetric (Weight-Loss) Method** — Two identical metal coupons are exposed to the blank and inhibitor-containing electrolytes for a fixed duration. The percentage decrease in weight loss gives %IE.
2. **Electrochemical Method (Tafel Extrapolation / LPR)** — The corrosion current density $i_{corr}$ is obtained from polarisation curves. %IE is calculated using the relation:

$$\%IE = \left( \frac{i_{corr}^{0} - i_{corr}^{inh}}{i_{corr}^{0}} \right) \times 100$$

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is meant by **critical inhibitor concentration**? How is it determined from a plot of %IE vs. $C_{inh}$?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concentration ($C_{crit}$)** is the **minimum** concentration of inhibitor below which the inhibition efficiency is negligible (typically %IE < 5%). It marks the onset of measurable protective action.

**Determination**:
- Plot %IE (y-axis) vs. $C_{inh}$ (x-axis).
- The point on the curve where %IE just begins to rise appreciably above the baseline (zero) corresponds to $C_{crit}$.
- Mathematically, $C_{crit}$ is the concentration at which the second derivative $d^{2}(\%IE)/dC^{2}$ changes sign (inflection onset).

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Past Pattern]`

**Q:** Mild-steel coupons of area **15 cm²** were immersed in **1 N $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$** for **5 hours** at 30 °C, both with and without an organic inhibitor at four different concentrations. The weight-loss data are tabulated:

| Inhibitor Concentration $C$ (mM) | Weight Loss $W$ (mg) |
|---|---|
| 0 (blank) | 412 |
| 0.2 | 305 |
| 0.5 | 178 |
| 1.0 | 84 |
| 2.0 | 42 |

Calculate:
**(a)** The inhibitor efficiency at each concentration and surface coverage θ. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** Verify whether the system obeys the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, and determine $K_{ads}$ and $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ at 303 K. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a): IE and θ Calculation** — *(7 Marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } C = 0.2 \text{ mM:} \quad \%IE &= \dfrac{412 - 305}{412} \times 100 = \dfrac{107}{412} \times 100 = 25.97\% \\
\theta_{0.2} &= 0.2597 \\[4pt]
\text{At } C = 0.5 \text{ mM:} \quad \%IE &= \dfrac{412 - 178}{412} \times 100 = \dfrac{234}{412} \times 100 = 56.80\% \\
\theta_{0.5} &= 0.5680 \\[4pt]
\text{At } C = 1.0 \text{ mM:} \quad \%IE &= \dfrac{412 - 84}{412} \times 100 = \dfrac{328}{412} \times 100 = 79.61\% \\
\theta_{1.0} &= 0.7961 \\[4pt]
\text{At } C = 2.0 \text{ mM:} \quad \%IE &= \dfrac{412 - 42}{412} \times 100 = \dfrac{370}{412} \times 100 = 89.81\% \\
\theta_{2.0} &= 0.8981
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Tabulated Result (carry into Part b):**

| $C$ (mM) | $\theta$ | $C/\theta$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.2 | 0.2597 | 0.7702 |
| 0.5 | 0.5680 | 0.8803 |
| 1.0 | 0.7961 | 1.2561 |
| 2.0 | 0.8981 | 2.2271 |

**Part (b): Langmuir Verification and $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$** — *(7 Marks)*

Langmuir linear form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{C}{\theta} &= C + \dfrac{1}{K_{ads}}
\end{aligned}
$$

A plot of $C/\theta$ vs. $C$ should be a straight line of slope **1**.

**Linear Regression Check** (manual two-point slope using extreme points):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope from (0.2, 0.7702) and (2.0, 2.2271):} \quad m &= \dfrac{2.2271 - 0.7702}{2.0 - 0.2} = \dfrac{1.4569}{1.8} = 0.8094 \\
\text{Since } m \approx 0.81 &\approx 1 \quad \text{(within experimental error, Langmuir is obeyed)} \\[6pt]
\text{Intercept } &= 0.7702 - 0.8094 \times 0.2 = 0.6083 \text{ mM} \\
K_{ads} &= \dfrac{1}{0.6083 \text{ mM}} = 1.644 \text{ mM}^{-1} = 1644 \text{ M}^{-1} \\[6pt]
\Delta G_{ads}^{0} &= -RT \ln(55.5 \cdot K_{ads}) \\
&= -(8.314)(303) \ln(55.5 \times 1644) \\
&= -2519.1 \times \ln(91242) \\
&= -2519.1 \times 11.42 \\
&= -28{,}768 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \approx -28.77 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation**: $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -28.77$ **kJ mol⁻¹** lies in the range $(-40, -20)$ → **mixed-type adsorption** (both physisorption and chemisorption contribute).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Module 4, Alt Choice]`

**Q:** **(a)** Define synergism and antagonism in the context of inhibitor combinations. How is the **synergism parameter $S_{\theta}$** evaluated, and what values indicate positive or negative synergy? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The polarisation resistance of mild steel in **0.5 M $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$** was found to be **18 Ω·cm²** for the blank and **230 Ω·cm²** with **5 mM** of inhibitor at 298 K. Calculate the inhibitor efficiency. If the activation energy for the corrosion process in presence of the inhibitor is **48 kJ/mol** and in absence is **28 kJ/mol**, comment on the nature of adsorption. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a): Synergism and Antagonism** — *(7 Marks)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synergism** is the phenomenon where the combined protective effect of two inhibitors (A + B) is **greater than the sum** of their individual efficiencies. **Antagonism** is the opposite — the combination performs *worse* than the individual components.

**Synergism Parameter $S_{\theta}$** (Aramaki-Hackerman formula):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\theta} &= \dfrac{1 - \theta_{A} - \theta_{B} + \theta_{A}\theta_{B}}{1 - \theta_{AB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $\theta_{A}$ = surface coverage of inhibitor A alone
- $\theta_{B}$ = surface coverage of inhibitor B alone
- $\theta_{AB}$ = surface coverage of combined A + B

**Decision Criteria:**
- $S_{\theta} > 1$ → **Synergism** (cooperative adsorption)
- $S_{\theta} = 1$ → **Additive behaviour** (no interaction)
- $S_{\theta} < 1$ → **Antagonism** (competitive adsorption)

**Typical synergism examples**: KI + amine inhibitors; $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ + phosphonates in cooling water.

**Part (b): Inhibitor Efficiency from $R_p$ and Activation Energy Analysis** — *(7 Marks)*

**Inhibitor Efficiency Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%IE &= \left( \dfrac{R_{p}^{inh} - R_{p}^{0}}{R_{p}^{inh}} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \left( \dfrac{230 - 18}{230} \right) \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{212}{230} \times 100 = 92.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activation Energy Comment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{a}^{inh} &= 48 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
E_{a}^{0} &= 28 \text{ kJ/mol} \\
\Delta E_{a} &= E_{a}^{inh} - E_{a}^{0} = +20 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $E_{a}^{inh} > E_{a}^{0}$, the inhibitor **increases** the energy barrier for the corrosion reaction. This is characteristic of **chemisorption** (the inhibitor molecules are firmly bonded to the metal surface, raising the activation energy for metal dissolution).

If $E_{a}^{inh} < E_{a}^{0}$, the inhibition would have been due to **physical adsorption** (lower barrier because of pre-adsorbed film reducing activation energy).

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in IE problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $K_{ads}$**: $K_{ads}$ from a Langmuir plot of $C/\theta$ vs. $C$ is in the **same units as $C^{-1}$**. Converting **mM⁻¹ → M⁻¹** requires multiplying by 1000. Forgetting this gives $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ errors of $\sim 17$ kJ.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 55.5** in $\Delta G_{ads}^{0} = -RT\ln(55.5 K_{ads})$. This converts $K_{ads}$ to a dimensionless standard state. Missing it loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $\theta$ and %IE**: $\theta$ is dimensionless (0–1), %IE is in % (0–100). Use θ in isotherm equations; use %IE in efficiency statements.
> 4. **Mixing up the $R_p$ formula sign**: The correct form is $(R_{p}^{inh} - R_{p}^{0})/R_{p}^{inh}$, NOT $(R_{p}^{0} - R_{p}^{inh})/R_{p}^{0}$. The latter is wrong by inversion.
> 5. **Declaring "Langmuir obeyed" without checking slope = 1**: Examiners want explicit verification — compute the slope or state that the regression coefficient $R^{2} > 0.99$ and slope ≈ 1.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Inhibitor Efficiency & Inhibitor Concentration**

- ✅ **IE formula**: $\%IE = \dfrac{CR_0 - CR_i}{CR_0} \times 100$; equivalent forms for $W$, $i_{corr}$, $R_p$ must be memorised.
- ✅ **θ definition**: $\theta = \%IE/100$, range $[0, 1]$.
- ✅ **Langmuir linear form**: $C/\theta = C + 1/K_{ads}$ → slope = **1**.
- ✅ **Free-energy rule of thumb**: $|\Delta G_{ads}^{0}| < 20$ kJ/mol → physisorption; $>40$ kJ/mol → chemisorption; in between → mixed.
- ✅ **Standard-state conversion**: Multiply $K_{ads}$ by **55.5** before taking natural log.
- ✅ **Synergism parameter**: $S_{\theta} > 1$ → synergy; $< 1$ → antagonism.
- ✅ **Four concentration-effect curve shapes**: Monotonic-plateau, bell-shaped, sigmoidal, step-passivation.
- ✅ **Activation-energy diagnosis**: $E_a^{inh} > E_a^{0}$ → chemisorption; $E_a^{inh} < E_a^{0}$ → physisorption.
- ✅ **Common inhibitor families**: Anodic (chromate, phosphate, nitrite), Cathodic (Zn²⁺, polyphosphate), Mixed (amines, azoles, thiourea, imidazoline).
- ✅ **Critical Concentration $C_{crit}$**: Minimum dose for measurable protection; read off the %IE vs. $C$ curve at the inflection onset.
- ✅ **Optimum Concentration $C_{opt}$**: Dose corresponding to peak %IE (before any decline).
- ✅ **Real-world dosage ranges**: Cooling water 5–50 ppm; oil & gas 10–200 ppm; pickling baths 0.1–1.0 wt%.
- ✅ **Always express $K_{ads}$ in standard SI units** (M⁻¹) before plugging into $\Delta G_{ads}^{0}$ formula.
